On the right side of the footer, aligned vertically, are three compact text links in a small monospaced font: “Ethics & Consent”, “Open Materials”, and “Colophon”. Each looks like tiny labels printed on the panel, with faint underlines that appear more engraved than drawn. Underneath all of this, centered at the very bottom, is a slim line of text in micro-typography saying: “© 2025 Cognitive Canvas Lab. Researching the interfaces that research us.”, in very muted gray. The entire page holds together with this consistent neo-skeuomorphic philosophy: everything appears like a modern, subtly three-dimensional control surface, with careful lighting (top-left light source), soft shadows, etched lines, and tactile affordances, but restrained and minimal so it still feels contemporary and calm. There are no animations, no moving parts, no videos, and no charts or data visualizations; instead, the richness comes from the interplay of depth, texture, and carefully crafted research-focused text and imagery.

instruction: Planning the generation details for the following website:
input:
Design a high-contrast, visually bold landing page for a boutique culinary school homepage called “Midnight Mise En Place,” using a dark, nearly-black background (#050507) contrasted with sharp white (#FFFFFF), electric orange (#FF6A3C), and acid chartreuse (#C6FF3F) accents. The overall layout is set on a strict multi-column grid (12 columns) with obvious vertical rhythm and generous gutters, but the composition uses creative asymmetry: overlapping cards, skewed blocks, and offset images that break the grid while still feeling cohesive. The entire page should use a single sans-serif font family reminiscent of “Space Grotesk” or “Suisse Int’l,” with tight, modern kerning, and clear typographic hierarchy: ultra-bold, all-caps headlines; medium-weight subheadings; and light-body text. The landing page fills the full viewport width (no soft rounded edges; everything is sharp and angular) and uses subtle, long-drop shadows in soft gray to make elements float over the dark background.

At the very top, a slim, full-width utility bar (about 60px height) sits against the pure black background, containing small, uppercase text on a 12-column grid. On the far left, aligned to the first column, in tiny white text it reads “Los Angeles · Night Classes · 12-Week Intensives.” On the far right, aligned to the last two columns, there are two links, evenly spaced: “Student Login” in white and “Book a Tour” in acid chartreuse, both in tiny all-caps with increased letter spacing; when hovered (described visually, not interactive), they invert: white text on a chartreuse pill-like background (still with sharp corners, not rounded).

Directly below, a prominent header/nav area is about 100px tall, spanning full width. On the left, occupying three grid columns, is the logo lockup: the name “Midnight Mise En Place” arranged in two lines. The word “MIDNIGHT” is large, all caps, in bright white, set in a condensed, bold sans-serif; beneath it “mise en place” is smaller, lowercase, in thin letters, in acid chartreuse. To the left of the text is a minimal line-art icon in electric orange: a flat, abstract chef’s knife silhouette with a slightly exaggerated, angular blade, drawn using a single continuous stroke. On the right side of the header, spanning about five grid columns, a horizontal navigation menu is right-aligned: “Programs,” “Workshops,” “Chefs,” “Kitchen Lab,” and “Visit.” Each nav item is set in white, uppercase, with generous letterspacing, spaced evenly with clear separation; the currently active link “Programs” has a thin chartreuse underline that is slightly offset below the text, as if it were a precise laser line running under the word from left to right.

The hero section below is full-height above the fold, structured as an asymmetric, two-column layout within the same 12-column grid. The left half (about 6–7 columns) contains the main hero text block, while the right half (5–6 columns) is dominated by a bold, high-contrast photographic hero image set inside a sharp rectangular container with slightly inset borders. The hero background remains #050507, but with a subtle diagonal gradient overlay—from pure black at the top left corner to an almost imperceptible dark charcoal at the bottom right—to create depth without softness.

The hero image is a photorealistic, high-contrast composition of a stainless-steel kitchen countertop in a dark room, lit dramatically from the top left by a single cool-white spotlight. On the counter is a black slate board with precisely arranged ingredients: a sliced blood orange with vivid red flesh, a small pile of sea salt crystals, fresh green herbs (like chives and thyme), and a single egg with a cracked shell revealing golden yolk. Behind them, slightly out of focus, are sleek, modern knives with black handles in a magnetic strip, and reflective steel mixing bowls catching sharp highlights. The focus is shallow, with the foreground ingredients sharply detailed and the background gently blurred. The overall color palette of the image makes use of deep shadows, stark highlights on the metal surfaces, and saturated color pops of orange and green, reinforcing the high-contrast theme. The image is bordered by a 2px solid white outline, and the entire image container casts a long, soft shadow diagonally down and to the right, in a muted charcoal tone, giving the impression that the image floats above the black page.

On the left, the hero text starts mid-height of the viewport, vertically centered. At the top of this text block is a small label “BOUTIQUE CULINARY SCHOOL” in tiny, white, all-caps letters with heavy letter spacing and a thin solid chartreuse line extending about 80px to the right of the label. Below it, the main headline spans three lines: “MASTER THE ART / OF AFTER-HOURS / COOKING.” Each line is set in huge, bold, all-caps white letters, aligned left, with tight leading so that the lines stack compactly. The words “AFTER-HOURS” are in electric orange, making them stand out against the rest of the white headline. Below the headline, a short subheading in medium-weight white text reads: “Immersive night classes for chefs, creatives, and insomniac food lovers in a fully lit pro kitchen.” It is a single paragraph, spanning roughly 60% of the width of the left hero column, with comfortable line spacing and slightly smaller size than the nav items.

Beneath the subheading, there are two primary call-to-action buttons placed side by side, with a small gap between them, aligning to the grid. The left button is the primary: a rectangular button with sharp corners, filled in electric orange (#FF6A3C) with white, all-caps text reading “VIEW PROGRAMS.” The button text is vertically centered and uses a bold sans-serif. The right button is outlined: transparent background with a thin 2px chartreuse border and chartreuse text reading “JOIN A MIDNIGHT TOUR.” The two buttons are the same height, and when visualized as hovered, the primary button flips to black background with orange border and orange text, while the secondary flips to chartreuse background with black text. Underneath the buttons, aligned to the left, is a small line of white microcopy, all caps but very small: “Next cohort starts March 10.” A tiny bright dot (a small circular accent) in chartreuse sits immediately before the text, like a status indicator.

Below the hero section, a sharp horizontal divider runs full width: a 1px solid line in a dark gray (#181A1F), visually dividing hero from content. The next section is a three-column grid titled “Our Signature Tracks,” occupying a dark background slightly lighter than the main (e.g., #080A0D). At the top of this section, left-aligned, is the section label “PROGRAMS” in tiny chartreuse uppercase text, followed by a mild white heading “Choose your midnight discipline” in medium-large white mixed-case text. Below, three program cards aligned horizontally, each occupying four grid columns, are laid out with equal spacing. Each card has a rectangular shape with a statically flat, matte-black background, a thin white border, and an internal 20–24px padding. At the top of each card is a small, square static image aligned left, with text wrapping beneath rather than beside. The first image is a flat-lay shot of neon-lit ingredients on a black surface: vibrant red chili peppers, glossy basil leaves, and glistening droplets of oil forming small lens-like reflections, photographed from above with hard directional light casting crisp shadows. The second image is a dramatic angled shot of a plated dish—a minimalist white plate with a small, artfully arranged stack of seared scallops and microgreens, illuminated by a focused overhead spotlight against a dark background, high contrast between plate and environment. The third image is a close-up of hands in black nitrile gloves delicately plating dessert on a black slate, with only the hands, the dessert, and a narrow strip of the slate in focus, everything else fading into darkness. Under each image is the program title in bold, white uppercase: “KNIFE WORK & FLAME,” “LATE-NIGHT PASTA LAB,” and “PLATING & SHADOW.” Below each title is a short description in smaller white body text: “Sharpen technique, speed, and control with pure fire and steel,” “Handmade pasta from flour to finish in the quiet hours,” and “Design plates that pop under low light and on camera.” At the bottom of each card is a thin line of electric orange text “Learn more →” aligned left, acting as a subtle link, with the arrow character included.

Next, separated by generous vertical spacing and another subtle horizontal divider line, there is a visually striking, full-width “Inside the Kitchen Lab” section that stretches edge-to-edge but maintains the inner content grid. The section background is a dark gradient from black on the left to deep bluish charcoal (#0C1016) on the right, emphasizing depth. The left half of this section is occupied by a tall, vertical image strip (spanning about four grid columns) featuring a high-contrast, black-and-white photograph of a commercial kitchen at night. In the image, stainless steel countertops and shelves recede into the background, with overhead industrial pendant lights hanging in a row, each shimmering with reflections. Only a few highlighted areas have subtle color accents: a single bright acid chartreuse post-it note stuck to a cabinet, and the glow of an under-counter strip light in warm orange—everything else is monochrome. The photo is shot from a slightly low angle, looking down the length of the kitchen to convey depth. The image has a thick white border on its left and right edges, but the top and bottom edges bleed to the section background, creating a vertical panel effect.

On the right side of this section, occupying the remaining grid columns, there is a two-column layout of text and feature highlights. At the top, the heading “Inside the Kitchen Lab” is in large, white sentence-case text; beneath it, a multi-line paragraph in smaller white text describes the lab: “Our classes happen in a real, working kitchen—chrome, concrete, and the hum of the walk-in. Late-night sessions let you focus, experiment, and plate under the same lighting conditions you’ll see in restaurants and on camera.” Below this paragraph is a three-item feature list, arranged in a staggered zig-zag pattern across two conceptual columns. Each feature is a small horizontal row with an icon and text. The icons are flat, minimalist outlines in acid chartreuse: a stylized burner flame for “Gas, induction, and open flame stations,” a minimalist camera aperture symbol for “Low-light plate photography corners,” and a simple knife-and-spoon crossed for “Professional tools, zero fluff.” The headings of each feature are in white bold text, with a single line of smaller white explanatory text beneath, aligned left. A subtle faint vertical grid line in semi-transparent gray runs behind these features, reinforcing the layout’s structure.

Below this, a strong visual break introduces a student stories section titled “After-Hours Stories.” The background switches back to pure black. This section features an asymmetric layout: on the left, a block of text and on the right, a stacked, staggered pair of testimonial cards. The section label “STUDENT STORIES” appears in tiny chartreuse uppercase, followed by the main heading “After-Hours Stories” in bold white, then a thin orange line underlining the heading’s width. Below the heading is a short two-line white paragraph describing the kind of students: “From pastry chefs to motion designers, our students learn to build meals that look as bold as they taste.” On the right side, two testimonial cards overlap slightly like physical printed cards scattered on a table, with one slightly higher and left, the other lower and right. The upper card has a dark charcoal background with a thin chartreuse border and contains a black-and-white portrait of a student—a close-up photographic image of a young person in a chef jacket, looking down intently at a dish, lit from one side, creating high-contrast shadows. Beneath the image, in white text, is the quote “Midnight prep changed how I plate everything.” in slightly larger italic type, with the name “— Avery, Freelance Food Stylist” in smaller white text below. The lower card has a similar style but with a thin orange border; it contains a portrait of another student—this time a person with tattoos on their forearms, captured in the act of tossing pasta in a pan, motion frozen mid-air, against a dark backdrop. The quote reads “The night kitchen is where my ideas finally make sense.” followed by “— Marco, Line Cook Turned Photographer.” The overlapping cards cast subtle, realistic drop shadows onto the black background.

Moving further down, a “Workshop Calendar Snapshot” section appears, maintaining the dark color scheme but introducing a grid-like visual reminiscent of a calendar without being a full chart. The section heading “Night Workshops & Pop-Ups” is in bold white, with a small subheading in grayish white: “Small-batch classes you can actually get into.” Below, there is a three-column layout of workshop tiles, each tile representing a specific upcoming workshop. Each tile is a narrow horizontal rectangle with a two-tone background: the left third is filled with electric orange or acid chartreuse, the right two-thirds remain matte black. In the colored left slot, the date is shown in bold white: for example, “MAR 12,” “MAR 21,” “APR 03.” The right side contains the workshop title in white and a single-line descriptor below in muted gray, e.g., “No-Recipe Sauces (Sauce Jam Session),” “Moonlight Pastry Crash Course,” and “Charred & Glazed: Vegetables at 11PM.” To the far right of each tile, aligned to the grid, is a tiny right-pointing chevron icon in chartreuse or orange, suggesting a link. Thin horizontal separators of 1px dark gray line divide each row.

Near the bottom, a bold “Apply Now” callout section breaks the page with a high-contrast inversion: the background becomes pure white, while most foreground elements turn black or dark. This section spans full width but visually feels like a crisp strip cutting through the dark site. Within this white band, the content is centered within the same grid. On the left, in large, black, all-caps text is the heading “APPLY FOR THE NEXT COHORT.” Beneath it in smaller black mixed-case type: “Limited seats. Portfolio optional. Curiosity required.” On the right, there is a prominent button filled in solid black with white uppercase text reading “START THE APPLICATION.” The button casts a subtle, cool-gray shadow downward. Under the button, a short line of chartreuse microcopy reads “We’ll reply within 72 hours.” As a subtle touch, behind the text and button, faint geometric linework—thin black outlines of kitchen tools like knives, whisks, and tongs—form an abstract pattern in extremely light gray, barely visible, adding texture without clutter.

Finally, the footer returns to the dark background, with a more compressed but still well-structured layout. The footer background is a slightly lighter black (#090B0F) with a top border of 1px dark gray. Within the footer, content is divided into three columns. The left column repeats the logo in a smaller size, with “MIDNIGHT” in white and “mise en place” in chartreuse. Below it, a short white sentence reads: “Culinary nightschool for the visually obsessed.” In the middle column, a small list of links, stacked vertically in white text: “Programs,” “Admissions,” “FAQ,” and “Contact.” Each link line has a tiny, flat line-art icon in electric orange to the left: a small grid for Programs, an open door for Admissions, a question mark in a circle for FAQ, and an envelope outline for Contact. The right column displays the physical address and email in small white text, e.g., “Downtown Los Angeles · 1123 Industrial Row · Studio 4C” and “hello@midnightmise.com” with the email in chartreuse. At the very bottom, centered, a thin, faint line separates the main footer from a final line of microtext: “© 2025 Midnight Mise En Place. All rights reserved.” in tiny gray text. The entire page feels rigorously structured by the grid but visually playful in its overlapping cards, high-contrast imagery, and strong angular shapes, with no animations or videos—just static images and solid, graphic elements that embody a bold, nocturnal culinary identity.

instruction: Planning the generation details for the following website:
input: Design a single-page research website in a stark, brutalist aesthetic, loosely inspired by a “handcrafted furniture methods archive” but presented as a serious design-research hub. The page has a strong single-column flow centered on the screen, with deliberate, slightly jarring asymmetries in spacing and alignment to enhance the brutalist feeling. The background of the entire page is a solid, slightly off-white color (#f5f5f0) that feels like aged paper. The primary text color is pure black (#000000). There are no rounded corners anywhere: all elements use hard, sharp, rectangular edges. No video, animation, charts, or data visualizations appear anywhere—everything is static. The typography uses two contrasting web-safe fonts: the main body text and section labels are set in "Courier New", monospace, giving a utilitarian, machine-typed feel, while occasional loud headings use "Impact, Haettenschweiler, 'Arial Narrow Bold', sans-serif" for raw, compressed emphasis. The overall width of the central column is narrow (about 760px) and centered on large desktop screens, with wide, empty margins of background color on each side; on smaller screens, content hugs the left edge in a more brutalist, unpolished manner, with no fancy responsive reflow beyond simple stacking. At the very top, a rigid, full-width horizontal strip forms the site header. This header has a solid, almost-clinical grey background (#e0e0e0) extending from edge to edge. On the far left, slightly inset with a 24px left padding, the site title is displayed in massive Impact font, all caps: "BRUTALIST CRAFT RESEARCH". The title is aligned flush left, almost uncomfortably close to the top edge, with minimal vertical padding. Below it, still in the header but aligned under the title with a slight 4px gap, appears a smaller subtitle in Courier New, all lowercase, stating: "an archive of methods in handcrafted furniture design". On the far right side of the header, aligned top-right with 24px right padding, is a simple text-only navigation list stacked vertically, each item separated by a 2px vertical gap. Each nav item is in uppercase Courier New at a small size, with letters spaced out: "INDEX", "METHODOLOGIES", "FIELD NOTES", "ABOUT". Each nav link is simply black text, and when hovered in the imagined design, the background behind each word would fill with a flat bright yellow (#ffe800), but the description shows them as static. The header itself casts a subtle, harsh, 1px solid black underline across its full width, emphasizing a separation from the rest of the page. Directly under the header, centered within the main column, is the hero section. It starts with a jarringly large headline in Impact, all caps, centered: "UNPOLISHED METHODS IN HANDMADE FURNITURE". The font size is oversized, stretching almost the full width of the content column, and the text is slightly misaligned vertically so that the first line sits closer to the top boundary than the bottom. Underneath is a short, typewriter-style paragraph in Courier New, left-aligned, with no justification: "This research site documents the processes, tools, failures, and micro-decisions that shape handcrafted furniture. No glossy finishes. No marketing. Just raw practice, documented." The line height is tight, almost cramped. Beneath this introductory paragraph, offset slightly to the left so it breaks the center alignment, is a prominent black-bordered rectangular hero image that feels like a rough scan placed on the page. The image is 3:2 ratio, with a thin, 2px black border. The image is a high-contrast, desaturated, photorealistic overhead shot of a wooden workbench in a small studio. The camera angle is directly above, looking down. The bench surface is scarred, with visible cut marks and stains. Scattered across the bench are hand tools: a metal hand plane, a wooden mallet, a chisel, and a measuring tape. The lighting is harsh and directional from the top-left, casting sharp shadows of the tools and accentuating the textures of the wood grain and metal surfaces. In the top left of the image, there is a partially assembled chair leg clamped in place, showing pencil marks and rough edges. The background outside the bench is dark and indistinct, with only faint hints of concrete floor texture. The image style is gritty and documentary rather than polished; colors are muted browns and greys with subtle cold highlights on the metal. Below the hero image, aligned flush left with its left edge, a caption in small Courier New reads: "Plate 01: Bench surface, failures included." The next section, "RESEARCH STATEMENT", begins after a large block of whitespace to create a sense of vertical austerity. A thin, all-caps heading in Courier New appears, left-aligned and preceded with a bracket to feel catalog-like: "[ RESEARCH STATEMENT ]". Under this, in a narrow column that does not stretch the full width (creating extra right-hand white space), is a dense paragraph in smaller Courier New. The text reads: "This site does not sell furniture. It does not tell you what to buy. It investigates the conditions under which craft decisions are made: which tool is picked up first, when a joint is considered 'good enough', how a maker reacts to a mistaken cut. Through annotated photographs, process transcripts, and workshop floor diagrams, this research maps the invisible logic of craft." The text is intentionally not justified and contains visible ragged right edges. About halfway down this paragraph, a brutalist design element intrudes: a solid black vertical bar (6px wide) appears to the right of the text block, starting at the mid-height of the paragraph and running down past its end, slightly extending into the margin below. This black bar is purely graphic, creating a sense of constraint. Next, the "INDEX OF PROJECTS" section acts as a rough catalog. The heading is typeset in Impact, left-aligned and slightly too large for the space: "INDEX OF DOCUMENTED PIECES". Beneath it is a vertical stack of three project blocks, each occupying the same width but each designed with slightly different internal alignment to suggest a manual layout. Each project block is separated by a 1px black horizontal rule that spans the column width. The first block is titled in Courier New, small caps: "Study 01 — Ladder-Back Chair in Ash". Below the title, the layout splits into a two-column asymmetry: on the left, taking about 60% width, a text description; on the right, occupying the remaining 40%, a small greyscale image. The description reads: "A series of three ladder-back chairs constructed with minimal jigs and non-uniform rungs. Documentation focuses on how the maker compensates for warped stock and imperfect joints." The right-side image is a stark black-and-white photograph with high contrast and slight film grain, showing a single ladder-back chair against a white wall. The camera angle is slightly low and off-center, making the chair loom awkwardly. The chair's back slats are slightly uneven, with faint pencil marks visible. Shadows from a side light fall diagonally across the wall, emphasizing the chair's silhouette. The floor is a raw concrete surface with visible cracks and dusty patches. The second project block, "Study 02 — Modular Bench with Visible Repairs", places its image on the left and text on the right, reversing the layout. The left image is a desaturated, photorealistic close-up of a bench corner where a cracked leg has been repaired with a visible butterfly key and an external metal brace. The perspective is close and shallow, focusing on the joinery details, with the background blurred—a scattered arrangement of shavings and a blurred tool rack. Light is cold and studio-like from the right-hand side, highlighting the fracture lines and textures. On the right, a text block in Courier New describes: "This bench is intentionally overloaded beyond design norms, stressing joints until failure begins. The study tracks how repairs accumulate as layered decisions over time, converting a singular design into a visible history of breaks and fixes." The third block takes the full width as text only, no image, heightening the brutalist sparseness. Its title: "Study 03 — Tables Built from Off-Cuts". The description: "Tables assembled entirely from leftover stock, each piece misaligned, each edge negotiated in real time. Process notes document when the maker chooses to hide misfits, and when they are left exposed as structure." There is a narrow, tall, empty rectangle outlined in black to the right of this text, like a missing image placeholder, with centered text inside in Courier New: "no image filed". This reinforces that the archive is incomplete and research-driven. Below the index, another section emerges with a slightly jarring design: "METHODOLOGIES". This heading is set in Impact, but this time, centered and underlined with a thick 4px black bar that overshoots the text width on both ends. Under the heading, three "method cards" are stacked vertically, but each card is left-aligned at a slightly different horizontal offset so the left edges form a staggered staircase. Each card is a rectangular block with a very light grey (#f0f0f0) fill and a thin black border, containing monospaced text. The first card is titled "01. TOOL PATH MAPPING" in uppercase Courier New. Inside, short bullet-like sentences (but written as raw text lines with hyphens) explain: "- trace the sequence of tools used on a single piece, minute by minute. - record when tools are set down and picked back up. - identify 'hesitation points' where decisions pause." The second card, offset slightly to the right, is titled "02. ERROR ANNOTATION". Its text: "- treat every mistake as data, not waste. - photograph each error from above, with scale. - annotate the image with cause, reaction, and outcome." The third card, offset further, is titled "03. MATERIAL INVENTORY DRIFT". Its text: "- document how off-cuts migrate across the workshop. - map their positions daily. - correlate movement with active projects." These cards feature no icons or decorative images, only text. To the left of the first card, intruding into the margin, is a small fixed-size black square (20x20px) acting as a stark, meaningless graphic element. Below this methods section, the "FIELD NOTES" area mimics a raw, semi-structured log. The heading "[ FIELD NOTES / TRANSCRIPTS ]" appears in Courier New, all caps, left aligned, followed by three distinct text blocks that look like scanned notebook pages but are rendered purely as text. Each block is set off by a thin border and a subtle drop shadow (a single 1px solid grey edge on the bottom and right) to evoke index cards taped onto the page. The first block contains time-stamped notes like: "09:13 — first cut on ash plank. slightly warped. correction planned but postponed. 09:27 — decision to keep warp as feature; plan shifted mid-cut." The second block reads: "11:05 — chisel slips, surface gouged. instinct: discard. decision: keep. annotation: 'acceptable damage'." The third block is more reflective: "13:42 — discussion with maker about 'perfection'. statement recorded: 'the chair is done when I stop noticing what’s wrong'." The monospaced font and tight line spacing make these notes feel dense and analytical. Slight misalignment between the borders adds to the handmade, brutalist layout. Near the bottom of the page, an "ABOUT THIS RESEARCH" section appears. The heading uses Impact, all caps: "ABOUT THIS SITE". The body text in Courier New explains: "This project is maintained by an independent researcher documenting manual craft practices. All studies are conducted in working workshops, not controlled labs." Below this paragraph, there is a small, left-aligned portrait image in stark black-and-white, passport-like, with a thick black border. The image shows the researcher: a mid-30s person wearing a plain dark shirt, photographed in flat, frontal light against a blank wall. The face is expressionless, with direct eye contact. The photo is high contrast, with clear shadows along the jawline and under the nose, and the background is a solid near-white. No identifiable branding, just a human presence. To the right of this portrait, aligned vertically with its midline, is a short list of text-only links in uppercase Courier New: "CONTACT", "ETHICS", "BIBLIOGRAPHY". Each link appears as black text; below them is a single horizontal rule. Near the very bottom of the single column, the footer spans full width like the header, but shorter. It has a flat, dark grey background (#333333) and small, all-caps Courier New text in white (#ffffff). On the left, text reads: "© 2025 BRUTALIST CRAFT RESEARCH". In the center, slightly misaligned, is the phrase: "NO COOKIES. NO TRACKING.". On the right, in small, compressed text: "PRINT THIS PAGE" and "PLAIN TEXT EXPORT" separated by a vertical bar. The footer uses no logos or icons. Overall, the page feels like a printed research dossier forced into a browser: sparse color, harsh lines, monospaced text, slightly uncomfortable asymmetries, and raw documentary images of handmade furniture process, with zero decorative animation or data visualization, emphasizing a brutalist, research-first aesthetic.

instruction: Planning the generation details for the following website:
input:
Design a hand-crafted, organic dashboard homepage for a freelance illustrator’s portfolio and client project tracker, with a gentle two-column layout that feels like a sketchbook spread laid flat on a wooden desk. The entire page should have a warm, tactile atmosphere with no video, no animation, and no charts or data visualizations—everything is static and serene. The background of the page is a subtle, high-resolution texture resembling off-white recycled paper with faint, irregular fibers and slightly darker specks; its color is a very soft warm cream (#F7F2E9). The edges of the viewport softly vignette into a slightly deeper beige, as if the page has been sun-kissed over time. Over this paper-like background, the main content is centered in a max-width container that feels like a physical notebook laid on a desk, with generous whitespace and soft shadows that make it float slightly above the background. At the very top is a hand-drawn-style header bar that spans the width of the content area, with an irregular, watercolor-like wash of pale blush pink (#FCE7E2) blending into muted sage green (#D6E5D0) from left to right, as if someone brushed watercolor across the page. This header has no hard edges—its bottom border is a gently wavy, slightly darker pencil line running horizontally across, and the wash extends beyond the normal boundaries in a loose, artistic way. Inside this header, left-aligned, is the site title in a hand-lettered script: “Studio Mossline” written in a dark inky charcoal (#2F2A28). The font style appears like a neat brush script, with slightly varying stroke thickness and imperfections to simulate a hand-drawn feel. Beneath it, in a tiny all-caps sans serif, slightly letter-spaced and in muted brown (#6D5243), is the subtitle “Illustration • Journaling • Client Dashboard”. On the right side of the header, arranged horizontally, is a simple navigation with the links “Dashboard”, “Portfolio”, “Commissions”, “Resources”, and “Profile”, each in a small, rounded sans serif font that looks handwritten but legible, in dark brown. Each nav item has a very subtle underline that looks like a pencil stroke—not perfectly straight, with slight wobble—and when describing the hover state, note that the underline thickens into a colored pencil stroke of muted terracotta (#C97A5A) but remains static (no animation). Just below the header, the main content is divided into a loose two-column layout: a wider left column (about 60% width) and a narrower right column (about 40% width), but their vertical sections are staggered to create natural asymmetry, like overlapping sheets of paper on a desk.

In the left column at the very top is a large hero section that feels like the cover of a sketchbook. It’s a slightly off-rectangular card with rounded corners and a soft drop shadow, on a very light, warm gray paper texture. At the top-left of this hero card, in medium-large handwritten-style serif, the heading reads: “Welcome back, Moss.” in dark charcoal, with a period at the end and slightly slanted baselines to evoke handwriting. Below this, smaller subtext in a clean, friendly serif says: “Here’s your creative snapshot for today.” in muted brown. To the right of the heading, taking up the upper-right quadrant of this hero card, is a static illustration image: a flat, painterly scene of a wooden desk from a slightly angled top-down perspective. On the desk are scattered art tools—a cup of paintbrushes, a fountain pen, a small watercolor palette with pastel colors, and a sketchbook open to a page showing an unfinished botanical illustration of ferns and wildflowers. The colors are soft greens, dusty rose, and warm browns. The style is semi-flat and slightly textured, like gouache; brush strokes are visible, edges are soft, and shadows are gentle and diffused. The surface of the desk is warm honey-toned wood with visible grain lines running horizontally. Light seems to come from the top-left, casting subtle, soft shadows. At the bottom of this hero card is a horizontal strip sectioned into three “today’s overview” metrics, each separated by faint vertical pencil lines. Each mini-block has a tiny hand-drawn icon on the left: a miniature sketchbook icon for the first, a small envelope with a tiny sprouting leaf for the second, and a minimalist clock face for the third. The first block’s label is “Projects in Focus” in tiny uppercase serif, with the number “3” in a larger, brush-script numeral in forest green (#3D5B47). The second block says “New Messages” with the number “5” in muted terracotta, and the third says “Deadlines This Week” with the number “2” in a darker slate blue (#2B3F51). Each number is slightly misaligned vertically, as if stamped by hand, reinforcing the organic aesthetic. Beneath this hero card is a slightly torn, semi-transparent strip of washi tape graphic anchored in the top-left corner of the card, with a pattern of tiny olive green leaves on a cream background, adding a crafted feel.

Directly under this hero section, still in the left column, are two stacked cards: “Current Projects” and “Sketchbook Queue”, each looking like separate sheets of paper partially overlapping. The “Current Projects” card sits on top and slightly overlaps the hero’s bottom edge, as if tucked under it. It has a subtle rough-cut paper edge at the top. The header text, “Current Projects”, is handwritten-style in dark brown with a small, hand-drawn underline that starts thick on the left and tapers to a thinner pencil line on the right. Within this card is a vertical list of three projects, each in its own lightly outlined row with rounded corners, separated by faint dashed lines that mimic stitching. Each row has, on the left, a small thumbnail image representing the project: for the first project, titled “Herbal Diary Cover”, the thumbnail is a square illustration of a dark green notebook with gold foil plant motifs and a ribbon bookmark, lying on a linen cloth. The style is photorealistic but softly lit, with the notebook slightly angled and the cloth background blurred. The second project, “Indie Film Poster: ‘Quiet Rivers’”, shows a minimalist illustrated poster thumbnail: a winding river in pale blue slicing through dark forest silhouettes under a starlit sky, executed in flat vector art with grainy texture. The third, “Wedding Invitations: Finch & Marigold”, shows a delicate flat-lay: cream invitation cards with botanical line art of finches and marigold flowers, gold ink pens, and pressed flower petals arranged around them, in a soft, lifestyle-photography style. To the right of each thumbnail, the project title is in a moderately bold serif font, dark charcoal, with a subtitle underneath in a smaller, lighter serif giving a brief status, like “Sketch phase • Due in 4 days”, “Color exploration • Due in 9 days”, and “Final review • Due tomorrow” respectively. On the far right of each row are two pill-shaped tags that appear like pieces of colored label paper: one with a status label (“In Progress”, “Concepting”, “Review”) and one with a priority label (“High”, “Medium”, etc.). These labels are muted, earthy solid colors—sage, dusty rose, clay—with rounded corners and slightly uneven edges as if cut with scissors. The text on these labels is tiny uppercase white lettering. At the bottom-right of this card is a small, outlined button that looks like it’s stamped onto the paper: “View all projects” in small serif text, encased in a faint pencil rectangle whose corners don’t quite connect, reinforcing the hand-drawn style.

Beneath “Current Projects” is the “Sketchbook Queue” card, slightly offset to the right so that its left edge doesn’t align perfectly, creating a casual stacked-page effect. Its title is “Sketchbook Queue” in the same handwritten style, followed by a small doodle of a pencil and tiny sparkles drawn next to it in a sketchy line art. Inside are three horizontal “idea chips” resembling sticky notes pinned across the card. Each chip is a pale pastel sticky note—one soft yellow, one pale mint, one powder blue—with a subtle simulated curl on one corner and a tiny piece of illustrated washi tape holding it at the top. On each sticky note is hand-written style text describing an idea: “Botanical border studies”, “Character poses: café regulars”, and “Urban sketch: twilight street market”. These are written in dark brown, slightly slanted script, with a faint ghosted line behind them to indicate writing guides on the sticky notes. The sticky notes slightly overlap each other but remain readable. Between the second and third sticky notes, in the negative space on the card, is a small hand-sketched ink drawing of a fern leaf in muted green, adding another crafted detail.

In the right column at the top is a vertical “Today’s Palette & Mood” section, which is a tall, narrow card that feels like a pinned mood board. The card background is light cream with a subtle pattern of faint, almost invisible dots like bullet journal pages. The heading, “Today’s Palette & Mood”, is written in a small, uppercase serif font in charcoal, with a tiny paint drop icon beside “Palette” colored in one of the palette colors. Across the center of the card is a horizontal strip of five circular color swatches, each about the size of a coin, arranged from left to right: “Moss Green (#5A7A63)”, “Terracotta Clay (#C8825D)”, “Sun-warmed Linen (#E5D3B3)”, “Ink Charcoal (#2E2B2A)”, and “Soft Sky (#AFC8D9)”. Under each circle is the color name in tiny handwritten-style text; one of the swatches (the terracotta) has a small, hand-drawn star next to its label, as if marked as a favorite. Above this palette strip, pinned diagonally with a drawn paperclip icon, is a small polaroid-style image: a square photograph with a white border showing a ceramic mug of coffee, an open sketchbook with a partially painted plant illustration, and a cluster of dried flowers on a rustic wooden table. The photo is warm-toned, softly lit, with shallow depth of field; the focus is on the sketchbook and mug, while the background fades into blur. Below the palette strip, aligned left, are three short descriptive lines in a very small serif font, with leading and line height giving plenty of breathing room: “Mood: quiet, grounded, botanical”, “Textures: rough cotton, recycled paper, dried petals”, and “Focus: narrative illustrations with muted contrasts”.

Directly under this mood card, still in the right column, is another card titled “Client Notes”. This card resembles a slightly thicker piece of notecard paper with a faint horizontal ruling like a notebook. At the top-left, the heading “Client Notes” is written in a neat, uppercase serif in dark brown. To the right of the heading is a tiny icon of an open envelope with a sprig sticking out. Inside this card are two sections separated by a softly-drawn horizontal line. The first section is labeled “Pinned” in tiny uppercase letters, with a small colored dot in terracotta. Under “Pinned” is a short note block with a hand-drawn checkbox icon (unchecked) followed by text in a small, slightly italic serif: “Send revised color thumbnails to Finch & Marigold before Friday.” The line wraps naturally, and each line of text aligns under the first letter of the sentence, giving the feel of a carefully written note. The second section below is labeled “Recent Messages” with a little doodle of two overlapping speech bubbles. Underneath are three single-line message summaries, each preceded by a tiny dash that looks like a pencil dash: “Herbal Diary: client approved updated cover typography.”, “Quiet Rivers: waiting on final tagline copy from festival team.”, and “New inquiry: children’s book concept (forest theme).” The last line has a subtle highlight effect, like someone lightly marked it with a pale yellow highlighter, not a solid block, but uneven and translucent. At the bottom-right of the “Client Notes” card is a tiny, understated link styled like hand-written marginalia: “Open inbox →” in a slightly darker brown, with the arrow hand-drawn and a bit curved.

Further down the page, spanning both columns but staggered visually, is a section that feels like a pinned corkboard of links and resources, titled “Studio Resources & Tutorials”. This section’s background is slightly darker beige, with a subtle cork-like texture and scattered speckles. The title “Studio Resources & Tutorials” is centered at the top, in a medium-large serif font, dark charcoal, with a hand-drawn double underline: the first line is slightly straight, the second is more wavy and playful. Below this, the content is arranged in a loose two-column grid of resource cards, each card like a pinned index card or small sheet of paper, but they’re not perfectly aligned; they sit at slightly varied vertical positions and slight rotations (only a few degrees) to mimic casually pinned notes. The top-left resource card, slightly tilted, is labeled “Tutorial: Building a Cohesive Portfolio Story” in a bold serif heading, with smaller descriptive text below: “A step-by-step guide to weaving narrative through your illustration portfolio.” At the top edge of this card is a drawn piece of tape in pale blue, and at the bottom-left corner a tiny sketch of a nib pen. To its right is another card with a small rectangular thumbnail image at the top: a flat illustration of a hand flipping through a stack of prints with various colorful illustrations, laid out on a white table; the style is flat and graphic with light shading and visible texture. Below the thumbnail, the card’s heading reads: “Tutorial: Photographing Your Art at Home” and the body text: “Lighting tips, DIY setups, and editing basics for showcasing your work.” Below these, two more resource cards fill the width, one in each column; the left one shows a small, flat illustration of a laptop with open art programs and color palettes on the screen, accompanied by text “Guide: Client Onboarding Flow for Freelancers”, while the right one has no image but includes a bulleted list of scribble-style bullet points (drawn as tiny, imperfect circles) with three bullets: “Pricing worksheets”, “Email templates”, “Project timeline printable”. Each card has subtle pencil lines around the edges, not perfectly parallel, reinforcing the hand-drawn aesthetic. The headings on these cards are all in small, serif font, while body text is in an even smaller but readable serif with generous line spacing.

At the very bottom of the main content area, spanning the width but visually aligned more to the left column, is a “Personal Notes” area that looks like a tear-off page from a notebook. The top edge of this section has a perforated line graphic—tiny repeating dashes—and a row of three illustrated binder ring holes, lightly shaded to show depth. The heading “Personal Notes” is in a relaxed script font in dark charcoal. Below, the content is a large, blank, lined area with faint gray horizontal lines like a journal page, and in the top-left of the lined area is a faint placeholder hint text in light brown: “Jot down ideas, reminders, or sketches…”. Near the bottom-right of this lined area is a small doodle of overlapping circles and a tiny leaf, as if the illustrator absentmindedly scribbled. Just beneath the lined area, aligned to the right, is a small button that looks like a rubber stamp impression; it’s an irregular oval outline in muted green with slightly ink-bleed edges, and inside it says “Save note” in tiny uppercase letters.

The footer of the page returns to a simple, minimal form. It spans the width of the content container and sits against the same off-white paper texture. A thin, hand-drawn horizontal line separates it from the main content above. In the center of the footer, small serif text reads: “© 2025 Studio Mossline. Crafted by hand, shared with care.” in dark brown. Below that, in even smaller text, are two subtle links: “Privacy notes” and “Contact”, separated by a tiny bullet point drawn as a small, uneven dot. Throughout the entire page, the typography maintains a consistent palette of hand-crafted feeling but always legible: headings in a slightly imperfect serif or script, body and labels in a clean serif, with sizes clearly hierarchical (large hero title, medium section titles, smaller card headings, smallest for notes). Colors remain grounded in earthy, muted tones—greens, clays, creams, charcoals—with occasional soft blues, and all edges, borders, and dividers lean into pencil lines, brush strokes, or torn paper illusions instead of crisp geometric shapes. There are no animations or moving parts; every interactive element (like buttons or links) is indicated only by slight changes in color and thickness described as hover states, purely conceptual. The resulting dashboard should feel like a calm, physical workspace translated into a digital view, rich with hand-made details and organic asymmetries while preserving a clear two-column structure for functional navigation and daily overview.

instruction: Planning the generation details for the following website:
input:
Design a single-page, vertically scrolling, single-column-leaning brutalist dashboard homepage for a fictional science museum called “ION CORE LABS,” serving as an internal control panel for exhibitions, ticketing, and staff alerts. The overall aesthetic is raw and utilitarian, with deliberate visual noise and asymmetry. The background of the whole page is a flat, almost glaring off‑white (#F8F8F8), with no gradients. The page uses only three colors for content: pure black (#000000), toxic neon green (#7BFF00), and safety orange (#FF4E00). Text is rendered in all caps for main headings and navigation, using a monospaced typeface like “IBM Plex Mono” in bold, and body text is in a harsh, geometric sans-serif, reminiscent of early web defaults. All elements sit within a central column that is roughly 900px wide, centered on large desktop screens, but with some blocks purposely breaking out of the column to the left or right to create asymmetry. Margins are generous vertically, with 40–60px of space between major sections, and sharp 1px black borders are heavily used, often enclosing content in boxy frames that resemble terminal windows.

At the very top, spanning the full width of the viewport, there is a rigid, full-width header bar with a solid neon green background (#7BFF00). Inside this bar, aligned left with a 40px left margin, is the museum’s wordmark in black, monospaced, bold, about 24px: “ION CORE LABS / SYSTEM DASHBOARD”. To the right side of this header bar, aligned to the far right with a 40px right margin, are three text-based navigation links in black, evenly spaced horizontally: “OVERVIEW”, “EXHIBITS”, and “ACCESS CONTROL”. Each link is in uppercase, monospaced, 14px, and separated by thin 1px vertical black lines that extend slightly above and below the text, giving a crude, hand‑built modular feel. The header has no drop shadow; the separation from the page background is purely via the color change and a bottom 2px thick black border.

Directly under the header, centered in the main column, is a hero “dashboard status” block that looks like a primitive system console. This block is a large rectangle with a white background, enclosed in a thick 3px black border, with slightly rounded but still sharp corners. Inside, at the top‑left, in small uppercase monospaced 12px text in neon green, it says: “STATUS: LIVE SYSTEM FEED”. Beneath this label, taking up the upper half of the block, is a large, static hero image styled as flat, brutalist vector art: a front‑facing, schematic cutaway of a multi-level science museum interior. The illustration is extremely geometric and simplified, using only black outlines on the off‑white base with solid neon green and safety orange rectangles to highlight sections. The museum interior is divided into three horizontal tiers: at the bottom, silhouettes of small human figures rendered as minimal black stick-like shapes stand in a row, facing exhibits that are just large outlined rectangles; in the middle tier, there are three large, overlapping circles representing “particle colliders,” filled in neon green with thick black outlines, with jagged orange zigzag lines around them to suggest energy; at the top tier, a tall, rectangular glass cylinder is drawn with stacked horizontal lines to imply a containment chamber, with a thin orange triangle at the top pointing upwards. The perspective is flat, straight-on, with no shading or gradients, giving a blueprint‑meets‑infographic look. The image background is transparent off‑white so it blends into the page, but contained within the console block’s border.

Below this hero image, still inside the same console block, the lower half is divided into two columns by a thick vertical black line. On the left, there is a vertical stack of three small, rectangular status tiles, each separated by 1px black lines. Each tile has a neon green background with black text. The top tile displays “TICKET QUEUE: 128 WAITING” in bold uppercase, center-aligned. The middle tile shows “CURRENT CAPACITY: 74%” in the same style. The bottom tile reads “SECURITY ALERTS: 2 ACTIVE” in safety orange text on neon green. On the right side of this console block, in a white background area, there is a list of recent events laid out as a text-only “log” with a typewriter feel. It is a vertical list of five lines, each line preceded by a black square bullet. The lines read in uppercase, monospaced, 12px black: “09:14 — QUANTUM GARDEN EXHIBIT: OPENED”, “09:32 — COSMIC THEATER: SOLD OUT”, “09:47 — LASER LAB: MAINTENANCE MODE”, “10:03 — SCHOOL GROUP ARRIVAL: 45 STUDENTS”, “10:18 — BIOSPHERE DOME: AIRLOCK TEST PASSED”. Lines are left-aligned, with generous line spacing, and the entire log block is inset with 20px padding.

Under this hero console, the main column continues with an asymmetrical layout of functional “cards” that represent different dashboard modules, stacked vertically but with some cards intentionally shifted left or right. The first such section is labeled in a brutal, loud way with a header that spans the width of the central column: a line of text in black, all caps, monospaced, 18px, left-aligned, with a raw underline consisting of a solid 3px safety orange line extending the full width. The text reads: “TODAY’S EXHIBIT CONTROL”. Beneath this header, three tall, narrow blocks are stacked. Each block spans the width of the central column on mobile but on desktop, the first is aligned flush left, the second is centered, and the third is offset slightly to the right so part of its border breaks past the column boundary, reinforcing the brutalist asymmetry.

The first block, “EXHIBIT: QUANTUM GARDEN,” has a white background with a thick 2px black border and a neon green title bar at the top. Inside the title bar, left-aligned in black monospaced 14px, is the label “EXHIBIT: QUANTUM GARDEN”. On the right of the title bar is a tiny, square, flat icon: a black outline of a plant with leaves composed of small triangles, placed inside a neon green square, reminiscent of a pixel icon. Inside the block, the content is divided horizontally. At the top half is a static image: a flat, top-down plan of a garden rendered as a grid of rectangles and circles in black outlines, with scattered neon green circular “nodes” connected by thin straight black lines, suggesting a network of “quantum plants.” The background is off‑white and the shapes are crisp, with no shading. Below this image, in black uppercase sans-serif 12px text, there is a short description, left-aligned: “INTERACTIVE LIGHT FIELD EXHIBIT. VISITOR FLOW IS STABLE.” Underneath, three horizontal toggle bars indicate system switches; each toggle is a narrow rectangular outline with “ON” and “OFF” text inside. The first toggle’s left half (“ON”) is filled with neon green and labeled “LIGHT FIELD”, with “OFF” unfilled. The second toggle is filled on “OFF” side in orange and labeled “SOUND ENVIRONMENT”. The third is filled on “ON” in green and labeled “SENSOR LOGGING”. The toggles are static graphics, not real switches, but their brutalist, flat design suggests functionality.

The second block, centered, is “EXHIBIT: COSMIC THEATER”. Its title bar is safety orange with black text. Inside, the main graphic is a flat, minimalist representation of a dome theater from the side: a large black semicircle on an off‑white background, with a series of straight neon green lines radiating from a central point near the bottom, as if projecting stars. The seating is implied by a row of small black squares along the base of the semicircle. Under this, uppercase black text describes: “IMMERSIVE SPACE PROJECTION. SESSION STATUS: FULL.” Under the text, instead of toggles, there are three rectangular “schedule” blocks side by side with thin borders: “10:00 SESSION — CLOSED”, “11:00 SESSION — BOARDING”, “12:00 SESSION — OPEN”. The middle block “11:00 SESSION — BOARDING” has a neon green background, others white.

The third block, offset to the right, is “EXHIBIT: LASER LAB”. Its title bar is neon green, with a small icon on the right showing a simple black triangle emitting three thin straight lines in orange to represent beams. Inside, the main graphic is a flat, isometric-ish arrangement of rectangular prisms (viewed from a shallow angle) drawn only with black outlines, representing lab benches, with diagonal neon green lines crossing between them as “lasers.” The angle is minimal but suggests depth, giving a slightly more dynamic look while still being harsh and 2D. Below, text reads: “HANDS-ON LASER OPTICS. CURRENT MODE: MAINTENANCE.” Beneath this text is a horizontally aligned row of three labeled status badges, each a small rectangle with 1px border: “OPEN” (white), “LIMITED ACCESS” (orange background, black text), “MAINTENANCE” (neon green background, black text). Only the “MAINTENANCE” badge is filled green, the other two stay white.

Continuing down the page, the next section is “TICKETING & ACCESS SNAPSHOT”, signaled by a blocky header similar to before but this time the underline is neon green. Below it, occupying the full column width, is a dense, table-like panel that looks intentionally crude and unrefined. The panel has a white background with a thick black border and inside is a manually drawn grid with thin 1px black lines creating rows and columns of text. At the top left of the grid in bold uppercase black text: “ADMISSION LINES”. Three rows under it read: “GENERAL ADMISSION — OPEN — EST. WAIT: 15 MIN”, “MEMBERS — OPEN — EST. WAIT: 5 MIN”, “SCHOOL GROUPS — OPEN — EST. WAIT: 20 MIN”. To the right side of this same panel, taking up roughly one-third of the width, is another block labeled “PASS STATUS”, with three stacked rows: “DAY PASSES: AVAILABLE”, “NIGHT LAB TOUR: LIMITED”, “VIP LAB ACCESS: SOLD OUT”. Each phrase “AVAILABLE”, “LIMITED”, “SOLD OUT” is highlighted by a rectangular background: neon green for “AVAILABLE”, orange for “LIMITED”, and a dense black for “SOLD OUT” with white text. The typography is monospaced and slightly larger than body text for readability, with a feeling of old ticket queue screens.

Below this, still within the same “TICKETING & ACCESS SNAPSHOT” area but separated by a thick horizontal black rule, is a narrower subpanel titled in small uppercase neon green text: “ENTRY GATES”. Under this, three horizontal bars are shown stacked, each like a crude progress bar made of 20 small adjacent rectangles outlined in black. The first bar, labeled “NORTH GATE”, has 10 filled with neon green and 10 left empty. The second bar, “EAST GATE”, has 15 filled neon green and 5 empty. The third bar, “WEST GATE”, has 5 filled neon green and 15 empty. Each label is left-aligned beside its bar. These bars are static, not animated, but clearly show capacity in a deliberately manual way.

Further down, the layout introduces a staff-focused area titled “INTERNAL NOTES & ALERTS”. The header text is in black, monospaced all caps, with no underline, but the section itself is enclosed in a large, rough‑looking box with a safety orange border that has slightly uneven thickness, as if drawn with a marker. Inside this box, the top-left quadrant is reserved for “ALERTS” and consists of two tall, narrow warning tiles side-by-side, but within the single column they are visually split by a central vertical border line. The first tile has a neon green background with a black border and a large uppercase headline in black: “MINOR ALERT”. Beneath, smaller text reads: “ACOUSTIC SENSOR GLITCH IN HALL B. REBOOT SCHEDULED FOR 11:30.” The second tile has a safety orange background and black text, headline “PRIORITY ALERT”, and body text: “SPILL REPORTED NEAR ‘BIO INTERFACE’ EXHIBIT. CLEANUP IN PROGRESS.” Above each alert tile, aligned to the top-right corner, is a small black triangle icon with an exclamation mark inside, flat-filled, functioning as a generic warning symbol.

In the top-right of the orange-bordered “INTERNAL NOTES & ALERTS” box is a small, intentionally ugly-looking, faux-textarea element for quick notes. It is a simple white rectangle with a thin black border and, in grey uppercase placeholder text, the words: “STAFF NOTE INPUT (READ-ONLY MOCKUP)”. Horizontal, irregular black lines mimic handwritten text lines inside, but they’re just graphical scribbles. Directly underneath this faux-textarea is a row of three brutally simple, rectangular, text-only “buttons”, each outlined in black: “MARK ALL AS READ”, “PRINT LOG”, and “EXPORT”. All three have white backgrounds by default, but the middle “PRINT LOG” button is filled with solid neon green to suggest primary action, creating visual hierarchy.

As the user scrolls further, they reach a section titled “FLOOR MAP SNAPSHOTS”. The header runs the width of the column and uses safety orange text on a white background, with a thin 1px neon green underline. Below is a vertical stack of two static map panels, each with a slightly different composition but sharing a similar brutalist design language. The first panel, “LEVEL 1 — PUBLIC FLOOR”, is a large rectangle with a thick black border. Inside is a schematic plan view made entirely with straight black lines forming rooms and corridors. Each room is labeled in uppercase, while the spaces themselves are filled with no color to retain minimalism. Major zones are marked by short, thick, fluorescent blocks: “LOBBY” has a neon green strip along its top edge; “GIFT SHOP” has a safety orange square in its center; “CAFÉ” has a small black coffee cup icon made of simple lines (a rectangle with a curved line suggesting steam). Small, blocky arrows in neon green point along corridors indicating visitor flow. The second panel, “LEVEL 2 — LAB & THEATER”, is similar but asymmetrically arranged, with a large rectangular “COSMIC THEATER” space occupying the left half, filled with a faint black dot pattern to differentiate it from other rooms, and the “LASER LAB” space on the right, outlined with slightly thicker lines and containing four parallel neon green line segments inside to suggest lab benches.

Near the bottom of the page, a last functional area appears: “STAFF ROSTER — TODAY”. The section header is bold, black, with a neon green underline. Inside is a deliberately crude list of staff names and roles in a single column as if printed on a receipt. The list is enclosed by a narrow, tall, off‑white rectangle with a black border and no internal grid lines. Each staff entry occupies a single line with name, role, and status separated by double slashes (“ // ”). For instance, lines read: “R. CARTER // FLOOR SUPERVISOR // ON DUTY”, “M. LEE // LAB TECH // IN LASER LAB”, “S. GARCIA // EDUCATOR // LEADING GROUP”, “J. PATEL // TICKETING // FRONT DESK”, “K. NAKAMURA // SECURITY // PATROL”. The typography is monospaced, 12px, all uppercase, and the text is left-aligned, giving a utilitarian, log-like feel.

Finally, the footer is a squat full-width strip along the bottom of the page with a solid black background. It is about 80px tall. Centered in this footer, in small neon green monospaced text, is the line: “ION CORE LABS — INTERNAL DASHBOARD PROTOTYPE — BUILD 0.4.2”. Beneath that, in even smaller uppercase grey text, it says: “ACCESS RESTRICTED. THIS INTERFACE IS FOR MUSEUM STAFF USE ONLY.” There are no logos or icons here, just the raw text. The footer’s top edge has a jagged, “sawtooth” 8-bit pattern separating it from the main page: a row of small black and white alternating rectangles like pixel teeth. Throughout the entire page, there are no hover states, animations, charts, or dynamic visualizations—every element is static and flat. Dividers between sections are made from thick horizontal black lines or full-width color bands, whitespace is robust and often abrupt, and the overall feel is of a harsh, brutally clear, experimental internal UI that fuses science museum functionality with an unapologetically raw design.

instruction: Planning the generation details for the following website:
input:
Design a single, vertically scrolling event webpage for a one-day creative technology festival called “GLITCH MUSEUM NIGHT” in a brutalist aesthetic, using a mostly single-column layout with deliberate asymmetries and jarring spatial choices. The entire page sits on a harsh, almost blinding #fdfdfd background with no gradients, only flat color blocks, thick borders, and stark, high-contrast typography. The primary colors throughout are pure black (#000000), electric cyan (#00f6ff), acid yellow (#fffd00), and a muted gray (#e0e0e0), with occasional bold red (#ff0033) highlights. Typography is brutalist and unapologetic: all headings are in an all-caps, condensed grotesk font that looks like “Impact” crossed with a Swiss sans-serif (you can imagine using something like “Anton” or “League Spartan”), body text is in a monospaced font similar to “IBM Plex Mono”, and small labels use a standard sans-serif like “Arial” in all caps. Text is usually left-aligned, sometimes jarringly right-aligned in boxed sections to create tension; there is generous padding but often uneven margins, like blocks nudged slightly off-center. Overall, the layout is a tall stack of rectangular sections, each full-width but with content constrained to a central column of about 900px, though several sections deliberately break this column with blocks that jut to the left or right, creating a sense of asymmetry without abandoning the single-column flow.

At the very top, a fixed-height, full-width header bar about 80px tall spans the screen, with a thick 4px black bottom border. The background of the header is solid acid yellow (#fffd00). On the left, there is a deliberately oversized black wordmark: “SCIENCE AFTER DARK_” in all caps, condensed grotesk, size ~26px, slightly vertically centered. Underneath the wordmark, hugging its baseline but smaller, in monospaced font, 10px above the bottom of the bar, is a tagline in black: “A ONE-NIGHT TECHNOLOGY EXHIBITION AT THE CITY SCIENCE MUSEUM”. On the right side of the header, aligned to the top-right corner with too-tight padding to feel slightly uncomfortable, there is a minimalist navigation list in monospaced all caps, black text: “ABOUT / SCHEDULE / SPEAKERS / TICKETS / FAQ”. Each item is separated by a forward slash, with no hover effects described; the nav wraps to two lines on small screens, adding to the brutalist, cramped feeling. The cursor area is invisible, but each word is clearly clickable. The header remains visually simple, but the tension comes from the heavy border and cramped upper-right nav vs. loose left logo.

Immediately below the header, the hero section fills most of the first viewport height with a stark, poster-like composition. The hero has a split background: the left two-thirds of the section (full height) is pure white, the right one-third is vertical stripes: alternating 40px bands of electric cyan and black, forming an aggressive barcode-like pattern. In the white portion, aligned left with large whitespace, is the title text: “GLITCH MUSEUM NIGHT” in gigantic all-caps condensed grotesk, roughly 72–80px, black, tight line-height so the words stack vertically as three lines. Underneath, in monospaced text, smaller (~16px), is a blunt description: “AN IMPOSSIBLY LOUD, ONE-NIGHT EVENT WHERE SCIENCE, GAMES, AND 3D PRINTING COLLIDE.” Below that, a single line in bold monospaced uppercase red: “LIVE IN THE CITY SCIENCE MUSEUM — OCTOBER 12, 2025 — 7PM → 2AM”. All text is left-aligned with a rigid 20px margin from the central column edge. On the right striped third of the hero, slightly centered vertically, sits a large, brutalist image framed by a thick 4px black border and a shadowless, flat appearance. The image is a high-contrast, posterized scene: a photorealistic but heavily thresholded black-and-white crowd of visitors in a dark science museum hall, facing an enormous glowing double-helix sculpture of neon cyan and yellow light. The double-helix stands in the center with stark directional lighting from below, creating harsh shadows on the floor and silhouettes of people; in the background, out-of-focus exhibits suggest abstract shapes of planets and geometric structures. The image has a gritty, halftone texture overlay, as if printed in a zine. In the bottom-right corner of the hero (still within the section but overlapping slightly onto the striped area), there is an oversized rectangular call-to-action button. The button background is pure black, with a thick cyan outline. Inside, text in acid yellow, monospaced uppercase: “GET TICKETS (LIMITED)”. Below it, in tiny monospaced uppercase gray: “EARLY-BIRD PRICING ENDS SEPT 01”. The button looks slightly misaligned, sitting closer to the right stripe edge than the main text column, giving an intentionally “off” balance.

Scrolling down, the next section is a single-column “About” block, taking on a stark, boxed-in design. The section background is pure white, but the content sits inside a central rectangular container with a thick 3px black border and a subtle, repeated dot-matrix pattern faintly visible behind the text in light gray (#f3f3f3), mimicking printed paper. At the top-left inside the container, there is a small label in a black box: black background, white monospaced uppercase text reading “SECTION // ABOUT THE EVENT”. Below, the section title is massive and brash: “SCIENCE, GAMES, PRINTING, NO SLEEP.” in all caps, condensed grotesk, black, size ~40px, left-aligned, with a period after “SLEEP.” Below this title, the body content is in monospaced font (~16px), black, with tight line spacing. The text describes the event in short, blunt paragraphs: “This is not a polite lecture series. ‘GLITCH MUSEUM NIGHT’ turns the City Science Museum into a noisy, neon laboratory for one night only. Play experimental science-based video games, step into messy maker labs, and print impossible objects in our 3D fabrication studio. No velvet ropes. No whispered voices. Just raw discovery.” Then a second paragraph: “We’ve invited indie game studios, rebellious researchers, and 3D printing artists to take over the galleries. Expect playable prototypes, hacked instruments, unstable simulations, and pop-up talks that feel like punk shows.” To the right inside this same bordered container, breaking the single-column feel, there is a narrow vertical block aligned to the right edge, with a pale cyan background and black border, about 220px wide, extending from mid-height of the text to slightly below it. This block is right-aligned so that it sticks out of the main 900px column slightly, causing asymmetry. Inside it, upper-right aligned, is a mini facts list, each line in uppercase monospaced, black: “DATE: OCT 12, 2025”, “TIME: 19:00 → 02:00”, “LOCATION: CITY SCIENCE MUSEUM”, “AGE: 16+ RECOMMENDED”. Each line is separated by a horizontal 1px black line, creating a stacked brutalist card.

Further down, a “What’s Happening” section lists the core experiences. The section background is a stark electric cyan that spans full width, with text and cards sitting on top. At the very top of this cyan field, there is a huge centered heading in black condensed grotesk, all caps: “INSIDE THE GLITCH”. Underneath, a short line of intro text is left-aligned in monospaced font, black, sitting with a lot of whitespace around it: “Choose your own chaos: labs, lounges, and late-night experiments.” Below, the content is arranged in a single-column vertical stack of three brutalist “experience cards,” each filling most of the width but separated by large vertical gaps (like signboards). Each card is a white rectangle with a 4px black border, slightly misaligned horizontally to create a sense of disorder: the first card is nudged 30px to the left, the second is centered, and the third is nudged 30px to the right; on small screens they all line up. The first card is titled “PLAYABLE LAB // GAME ARCADE” in a black rectangular label at the top-left, uppercase monospaced white text. Inside, on the left, is the description text in monospaced black: “Test-drive science-inspired games from underground studios and students. Navigate particle mazes, simulate alien ecosystems, and push physics engines until they break.” On the right side of the card, taking about one-third width, is a small rectangular static illustration framed by a black border. This image shows a flat-design arcade cabinet in impossible perspective: the cabinet is bright yellow with a cyan screen, on which an abstract wireframe molecule is displayed in electric cyan on black. In the background of the illustration, blocky pixel-art shapes of atoms and planets float in a neon-purple void. The style is flat and graphic, with thick outlines and no gradients, but fake screen glow implied via lighter cyan rectangles.

The second card is titled “PRINT LAB // 3D CHAOS STATION”. Inside, left side text in monospaced black: “Walk into a workshop of humming machines and hypnotic layers. Watch 3D printers spit out generative sculptures, impossible gears, and tiny glitch-city skylines.” On the right, another illustration in flat brutalist style: a side view of three 3D printers on a stark black tabletop. The printers are drawn as semi-abstract boxes—one cyan, one yellow, one white—all with open fronts. Inside each, a geometric object is mid-print: a jagged polyhedron, a spiraling staircase, and a warped ring. Background is off-white with diagonal black lines suggesting depth. The objects cast thick, sharp black shadows backward.

The third card is “TALK ZONE // NOISY LECTURES”. Its description: “Forget seated lectures. These are fast, unpolished lightning talks in the middle of the galleries. Hear from scientists, indie devs, and artists about failure, glitching data, and why the future should look weirder.” Its illustration features a stylized podium in black with a cyan microphone, in front of an audience rendered as simple blocky silhouettes (rectangles with small circles for heads), all in black. Behind them, a gigantic projected slide shows a jagged waveform and a pixelated DNA strand in neon red on a white background. Everything has thick outlines and no shading.

Next, a brutalist “Schedule” section appears, emphasizing grid lines and precision that is ironically messy. The background returns to white. At the top, aligned left, is the heading in black condensed grotesk: “ONE NIGHT, MANY MICRO-EVENTS”. Below, a narrow block of explanatory text in monospaced black: “Screens blink. Printers whir. Talks erupt. Pick your path through the night.” Under this is a visual schedule represented as an oversized, single-column time table that still hints at a grid. A tall rectangular container with no fill color but thick black outer border holds the schedule. Inside, horizontal 1px lines create rows, with each row representing a time slot. On the left edge of each row, a vertical strip about 80px wide has a light gray background; in it, the time is printed in uppercase monospaced black, like “19:00”, “20:00”, etc. Next to each time strip, the main area contains event information. Example rows: at “19:00” the text reads “DOORS OPEN + LOBBY INSTALLATION: ‘NEBULA ENTRANCE’” in uppercase monospaced font; a smaller subline beneath it in lighter gray: “The main hall becomes a swirling projected starfield, built from live visitor data.” At “20:00” the row text: “TALK: ‘WHY GLITCHES MATTER’ — DR. ALMA REYES (MAIN THEATER)”, with a subline in gray: “A 20-minute thunder talk with zero bullet points.” At “21:00”: “PLAY SESSION: ‘PARTICLE PANIC’ MULTIPLAYER GAME — GAME ARCADE”; 22:00: “LIVE 3D PRINT DEMO: GENERATIVE CITYSCAPE (PRINT LAB)”; 23:00: “NOISE LAB: HACKED INSTRUMENTS + DATA SONIFICATION (GALLERY B)”; 00:30: “MIDNIGHT PANEL: ‘IS SCIENCE A GAME?’ (TALK ZONE)”; 01:30: “SLOW FADE-OUT: LAST PRINTS, LAST RUNS, LAST GLITCHES.” Some rows are left blank except for the time label, emphasizing emptiness. Certain key events have a small black rectangle tag next to them, with uppercase text in white reading either “LOUD”, “QUIET”, or “EXPERIMENTAL”, acting as brutalist badges.

Below the schedule, a distinct section highlights featured studios and speakers using a raw collage style. The section background is solid black, and the overall width still respects the main column. The heading, centered and massive in acid yellow condensed grotesk: “WHO’S BREAKING THINGS”. Underneath, in uneven grids, are three vertically stacked “speaker strips”, each a horizontal band divided into two halves. Each strip has a left half containing a small, grainy, black-and-white portrait image and a right half containing the text on a colored background. The portraits are high-contrast photographs of people, cropped just above the shoulders, with harsh side lighting, and then posterized into just black and white with a halftone dot effect. For the first strip, the left image shows a person with short hair, facing slightly to the left, wearing round glasses reflecting neon cyan lights. The background of the portrait is blurry, with hints of lab equipment. The right half has a cyan background and black monospaced text: “DR. ALMA REYES” on the first line (big and in all caps), “CHAOTIC PHYSICS RESEARCHER / UNIVERSITY LAB” beneath, and then a block of smaller descriptive text: “Designs experiments that deliberately fail in order to find new behavior. Thinks every exhibit should be slightly dangerous.” The second strip has a portrait of a person with long curly hair and headphones, facing the camera, mid-laugh, in front of a collage of game pixel art pinned to a wall. The right half has an acid yellow background, with text: “LOOPFAULT STUDIO” and description: “Indie game collective making science-inspired games that punch you in the brain.” The third strip shows a person’s profile illuminated by the glow of a 3D printer in the background, with spools of filament visible. The text block is red background, white monospaced text: “PRINT//ERROR LAB”, “3D PRINTING ARTISTS TURNING FAILED PRINTS INTO SCULPTURES”.

After this comes a “Space Layout” section that uses a textual, brutalist floorplan concept without actual interactive maps. The background is pale gray (#f5f5f5), and the content sits inside a large, asymmetric box with uneven margins (more space on the left than right). The heading at the top-left reads: “MAP? WE BROKE IT.” in black condensed grotesk. Below, three tall, bordered rectangles are stacked vertically, each representing a zone. These rectangles are outlined in thick black and filled with white. Inside each rectangle, the zone name is stamped diagonally across in large, faint, uppercase outline letters (e.g., “ARCADE”, “PRINT LAB”, “TALK ZONE”), like a watermark. On top of each watermark, concise monospaced descriptions are placed, left-aligned: “GAME ARCADE // Main Hall — glowing screens, arcade cabinets, multiplayer chaos. If you hear cheering, you’re here.” for the first; “PRINT LAB // Workshop Wing — rows of 3D printers, filament spools, staff with safety goggles. Smells like warm plastic and caffeine.” for the second; “TALK ZONE // Side Theater — small stage, mismatched seating, no stage lights. Bring your questions, not your slides.” In the margins outside these rectangles, small brutalist arrows (simple black triangles with text labels) point outward with notes like “SNACKS THIS WAY” and “ROOFTOP VIEW (IF YOU FIND IT)”.

Towards the bottom, the ticketing section appears as a harsh, attention-grabbing block. The background flips to solid red (#ff0033). Centered near the top in giant, white, all-caps condensed grotesk: “TICKETS ARE NUMBERS. YOU ARE NOT.” Beneath it, the content returns to the main column but maintains center alignment. Three pricing options are presented as three vertically stacked, full-width but short cards — no side-by-side layout, in keeping with the single-column feel.
