Each card has a white background with black border and is separated by generous vertical spacing. The first card, labeled with a small black tag at the top-left reading “STANDARD” in white monospaced uppercase, includes large price text: “€25” in black, condensed grotesk, and below it in monospaced small text: “General entry, all-night access, most experiences.” The second card, labeled “STUDENT / UNDER 25” with a cyan tag, shows “€15” and the text: “Show valid ID. Science students encouraged. Philosophy students too.” The third card, labeled “SUPPORTER” in a yellow tag, displays “€50” and text: “You pay more. We break more things (safely). Includes a glitch poster.” Below the cards, a large, black, full-width button spans nearly the whole column, with acid yellow uppercase monospaced text: “RESERVE YOUR SLOT NOW”. Right below the button, in very small white monospaced text and left aligned: “No physical tickets. Your name goes on the door list. Bring ID.”

Near the very bottom of the page, an FAQ section appears as a brutalist Q&A column with obvious structural lines. The background goes back to pure white. The section title, left-aligned: “QUESTIONS, RESISTANCE, AND RULES.” in black condensed grotesk. Under it, a vertical list of question blocks is arranged. Each question is in uppercase monospaced black, prefixed by “Q: ”, and each answer, below, is prefixed by “A: ” in gray. Question examples: “Q: IS THIS FAMILY-FRIENDLY?” with answer “A: There is loud audio, low lighting, and weird visuals. Teens will love it. Younger kids might find it overwhelming.” Another: “Q: DO I NEED TO KNOW ANY SCIENCE?” / “A: No. Curiosity beats credentials.” Another: “Q: IS EVERYTHING ACCESSIBLE?” / “A: We’re working with the museum team to make all main areas wheelchair accessible and provide quiet zones. Reach out if you need specific accommodations.” The questions are separated by thick horizontal black lines. One answer includes a small inline black box with white monospaced text “EMAIL: HELLO@GLITCHMUSEUM.NIGHT” acting as a pausable contact tag.

Finally, the footer is a flat, heavy band at the very bottom with a black background and white text, about 160px tall. At the top-left of the footer, in medium-size uppercase monospaced font, is the text: “CITY SCIENCE MUSEUM PRESENTS / GLITCH MUSEUM NIGHT”. Below that, smaller text: “Address: 123 Particle Way, Cityname. Last entry at 00:30.” On the right side of the footer, aligned right, three simple brutalist social links appear stacked vertically: “INSTAGRAM”, “TWITTER”, “NEWSLETTER”, each in uppercase monospaced text inside a thin white rectangle outline with no fill, spaced about 8px apart, like crude buttons. At the very bottom center of the footer, a tiny line of gray monospaced text reads: “© 2025 GLITCH MUSEUM NIGHT — PLEASE DO NOT BE GENTLE WITH THE FUTURE.” There are no videos, no moving parts, and no charts or data visualizations anywhere, only static images and brutally honest text. The entire page feels like a rebellious, poster-like, printed zine turned into a website: bold, flat colors, sharp grids, deliberate misalignments, and a single-column backbone interrupted by occasional off-center blocks, all reinforcing the brutalist aesthetic and the chaotic energy of this one-night science, game, and 3D printing event.

instruction: Planning the generation details for the following website:
input: Design a minimal, single-column portfolio homepage for a freelance illustrator called “Linnea Shore Studio”, with a calm, airy feel and subtle asymmetries. The entire page sits on an off-white, slightly warm background (#FAF7F2) with generous vertical spacing, lots of negative space, and no visible grid lines. The design should use a restrained color palette: deep charcoal (#222222) for most text, a soft muted navy (#2E3A4A) for headings, and a single accent color—a desaturated coral (#E98A6F)—used sparingly for small details like underlines or icons. All text is aligned left in the primary column and set in two typefaces: headings use a modern serif with high contrast strokes and slightly rounded serifs (e.g. “Editorial New” style), while body copy and navigation use a simple geometric sans-serif with generous letter-spacing. The layout tends toward a central single-column (about 720px wide) centered in the viewport on desktop, but certain hero elements and images bleed a bit wider to create gentle asymmetries. At the very top, a thin, full-width horizontal strip forms the page header. It is the same off-white as the background but differentiated with a subtle 1px bottom border in a very light gray (#E3DFD8). Within this strip, left-aligned and constrained to the main column, is the logo text set in small caps serif: “LINNEA SHORE STUDIO” in deep charcoal, letter-spaced slightly. On the far right of this header, but still aligned roughly with the main content column, there is a very minimal navigation with three text links in the sans-serif: “Portfolio”, “About”, “Contact”. These links are set in small, all-caps letters with ample spacing, and when hovered they gain a thin coral underline that sits close to the text. Beneath the header, the hero section occupies most of the initial viewport and subtly breaks the single-column rule. The left two-thirds of the hero area is the main text column, while the right third is reserved for a tall, narrow hero image that slightly overlaps the main column width. On the left, at the top of the hero, there is a quiet, all-caps sans-serif label in small font reading “ILLUSTRATION • EDITORIAL • BRAND”. Below it, with generous line spacing, is the main headline set in large serif letters: “Quiet stories, illustrated with intention.” The line breaks are deliberate: “Quiet stories,” on the first line and “illustrated with intention.” on the second, with the period included. The headline is in muted navy, with the comma and period accented in coral. Underneath, a short paragraph of introduction in smaller sans-serif reads: “I’m Linnea, a freelance illustrator helping magazines, brands, and independent creatives tell slow, thoughtful stories through line, color, and texture.” This text is charcoal, with subtle increase in line-height for easy reading. Below the paragraph, two inline CTA buttons sit side-by-side but spaced widely, keeping the minimal aesthetic. The primary button is a simple rounded-rectangle outline with a 1px charcoal border and no fill, containing the text “View selected work” in small caps sans-serif. On hover (described visually), it fills with the desaturated coral and the text inverts to off-white. To the right, separated by generous whitespace, a secondary text-only link reads “Download portfolio PDF →” in underlined coral sans-serif, slightly smaller than the button text. On the right side of the hero, there is a single tall static illustration image that visually anchors the page. The image is vertically oriented, slightly taller than it is wide, with its right edge aligned with the right margin of the main column and its left edge overlapping into the text’s visual space without obstructing any content. The illustration itself is in a soft, hand-drawn style combining flat shapes and subtle pencil-like line textures. It shows a solitary figure sitting on a rocky outcrop overlooking the ocean at dawn, viewed from a slight aerial three-quarter perspective. The figure is simplified and not highly detailed—a person in a loose sweater and wide-leg pants, holding an open sketchbook on their lap, with a relaxed posture. The color palette of the illustration harmonizes with the page: the sea is rendered in layers of calm blue-green shapes with gentle gradients, the sky transitions from a pale peach near the horizon to a soft light gray at the top, and there are thin, slightly wobbly line details suggesting distant waves. In the foreground, a few stylized plants and grasses in muted green and deep navy frame the lower corners. The lighting is soft and diffuse, with no harsh shadows—just a gentle sense of early morning glow implied by slightly lighter tones around the horizon. The illustration is bordered by no visible frame; it appears as a clean rectangle with subtle, very faint shadow on its lower edge to give a paper-like presence against the background. After the hero, there is a generous vertical gap, then the “Selected work” section begins as the core portfolio. A simple, left-aligned section label in all caps sans-serif reads “SELECTED WORK”, in small charcoal text with a thin horizontal line in coral extending a short distance to its right, like an understated underline. Below this, the section title in serif reads “Recent collaborations” in muted navy. The portfolio entries appear as a vertical stack of three featured projects, each occupying its own block with subtle variation to avoid repetition. Each project block is full-width within the main column and composed of an image and text; the blocks alternate which element appears first. The first project has the image on top and text below. The image is a static editorial-style illustration for a magazine article and is horizontal, with a clean white border around it. It depicts a cozy interior scene of a person reading on a sofa surrounded by houseplants. The style is flat yet textured, with visible brush-like strokes and slightly rough edges. The perspective is slightly above eye level, showing the sofa placed diagonally; the reader is reclined with their legs tucked up, holding a book. The background wall is a pale warm gray with a minimalist framed print in coral and navy. Several potted plants with large leaves in varying green tones occupy the edges of the composition, their organic shapes contrasting with the linear edges of the furniture. Soft, diffuse light seems to come from the left, creating very gentle shadows under the furniture. Below the image, the project title appears in bold serif: “Slow Mornings for ‘Hearth Magazine’”. Underneath, a smaller sans-serif line in charcoal reads “Editorial illustration • 2024”. A short, two-line description in body sans-serif follows: “A full-page illustration accompanying a feature on everyday rituals, balancing quiet negative space with detailed objects.” Under that, a subtle “View project details →” link in coral sits flush left. The second project block inverts the layout: text first, then image. At the top, the title in bold serif: “Tidal Lines Branding Suite”. Below, the subtitle in sans-serif: “Logo and packaging illustrations for a small-batch surf wax brand.” Then a brief description in smaller sans-serif: “A monochrome, line-based system inspired by tidal charts and coastal rocks, designed for letterpress, packaging, and social assets.” Below this paragraph, the image is a horizontal mockup-style illustration showing a flat-lay of branded items: a surf wax box, a round tin, and a simple tag card. Each item is drawn in a semi-flat style, with the brand’s line-based logo—a looping, wave-like mark—printed in deep navy on off-white packaging. Thin contour lines suggest embossed details. The background of the image is a warm sand color, and a soft shadow from the packaging gives a sense of depth. The third project block returns to image-first but uses a slightly narrower image aligned slightly to the left, leaving more white space on the right for the text, creating a subtle asymmetry. The illustration shows a night-time wilderness scene: a simplified cabin in a forest clearing with a starry sky. The style is consistent with the rest—flat shapes with a hand-drawn quality. The sky is a deep navy with scattered tiny off-white and pale coral stars, some forming faint constellations. The cabin is a simple geometric form in desaturated browns with warm light spilling from the windows. Tall, stylized fir trees in dark green silhouettes frame the sides. A small human figure is seen walking toward the cabin, holding a lantern that casts a small pool of warm light on the ground. Shadows are soft and elongated, hinting at depth. On the right, the text reads “Night Walk Series Prints” as the title in serif, with the sans-serif subtitle “Personal work • Limited edition giclée prints” and a short description: “A series exploring quiet movement through nocturnal landscapes, focusing on contrast between deep blues and warm light.” Each project block is separated by ample whitespace and a faint 1px divider line in pale gray that does not span the full width, instead aligning with the left margin and stopping around halfway across the column to maintain a light touch. After the selected work, the “About” section appears, again left-aligned. A small section label: “ABOUT” in caps sans-serif. The main heading in serif reads “Rooted in the coast, drawn to stillness.” To the right of this heading, breaking the strict single column, there is a small circular portrait illustration of Linnea, overlapping the right side of the column slightly. The portrait is stylized and consistent: a simplified bust of a person with shoulder-length wavy hair, a calm expression, and soft coral blush on the cheeks. The background of the portrait circle is a muted pale blue. Under the heading, the body copy in sans-serif spans several lines: “I grew up near cold water and rocky shorelines, and that environment still shapes how I draw. My work is guided by slowness, small details, and the way light shifts over time. I collaborate with editors, art directors, and small teams to create imagery that feels quiet—but stays with you.” Below this text, a secondary paragraph reads: “When I’m not drawing, I’m usually out walking by the sea, collecting textures and colors for the next project.” At the bottom of the about section, a minimal two-column list of “Selected clients” appears, but still within the main width: the label “Selected clients” in small caps sans-serif, then four client names in a simple list: “Hearth Magazine”, “Tidal Lines”, “Northwind Press”, “Lumen Studio”. Each client name is separated by a faint dot (·) and the list wraps naturally at the column width. Below the About section, the “Process” section offers a glimpse into how Linnea works, while maintaining minimal visuals. A small label reads “PROCESS”, and the heading in serif: “How we can work together.” Below, three vertically stacked steps are presented with subtle separation. Each step is numbered with a tiny coral circle containing a white number (“1”, “2”, “3”) placed to the left of the step title. Step one title: “Listen & gather”, with a short sans-serif description: “We begin with your story, timeline, and any visual references. I ask questions, you share context.” Step two title: “Sketch & refine”, with description: “I create concept sketches, we refine together, and agree on a direction before final artwork.” Step three title: “Deliver & support”, with description: “You receive high-resolution files tailored for print and digital use, plus support for any adjustments.” Beside the steps, aligned slightly to the right and offset upward to create a gentle stagger, is a small monochrome illustration: a simple hand-drawn stack of three sketchbooks with a pencil resting on top, all in navy line work with minimal shading, on a transparent background so it appears to float on the page. Near the bottom of the page, a “Contact” section invites inquiries. The label “CONTACT” appears in small caps sans-serif. The heading reads “Let’s talk about your project” in serif. Below, a short paragraph explains: “For editorial, branding, and collaborative projects, please share a few details and I’ll respond within 2–3 business days.” The contact form itself is very clean and vertically stacked, with no boxes filled; just thin underlines. Each field label is small sans-serif in charcoal, and the input lines are simple 1px gray underlines. The fields, in order, are: “Name”, “Email”, “Project details”, and a simple dropdown labeled “Timeline” with placeholder text “Select an option”. Beneath “Project details” there is a slightly taller text area with four or five lines suggested by a longer underline. Under the form fields, a minimal checkbox appears with the label “Add me to the studio newsletter (optional)” in tiny sans-serif. The submit button is a simple solid coral rectangle with slightly rounded corners and small caps white text that reads “Send message”. Below the button, a line of reassurance in small, faded gray text: “You’ll receive a copy of your message for your records.” At the very bottom of the page, a narrow footer runs the width of the main column, aligned center but still within the column, reinforcing the minimal feeling. It contains two lines: the first line in sans-serif, tiny: “© 2025 Linnea Shore Studio. All rights reserved.” The second line features two simple text links separated by a small dot: “Instagram” · “Behance”, both in small sans-serif with a coral hover underline effect implied. Throughout the page, there are no videos, no animations, and no data visualizations of any kind—only static text and images. The overall feeling is one of calm, breathing space, and quiet confidence, with asymmetry introduced gently through image placement and occasional overlapping elements, while the core reading experience remains a clear, linear, single-column flow.

instruction: Planning the generation details for the following website:
input: Design a single-page event website titled "NEON NEXUS: FUTUREWORK SYMPOSIUM 2084" with a distinctly futuristic, high-tech aesthetic and a hierarchical, overlapping layout that feels like layered holographic panels floating in space. The page should look like a sleek operating system interface for a conference, blending clean minimalism with luminous sci-fi details. The overall background is a deep charcoal-black (#050609) with a faint vertical gradient to a slightly lighter graphite tone at the bottom, overlaid with a barely visible hexagonal micro-pattern in very dark, translucent gray. Behind everything, add soft, blurred holographic glows in cyan, magenta, and electric violet that subtly radiate from behind key sections, as if light is leaking from beneath glass panels. At the very top, centered horizontally and slightly inset from the top edge, place a semi-transparent, slightly frosted glass navigation bar that appears to float above the background. This bar is a rounded rectangle stretching almost the full width of the page, with 20px vertical padding and a subtle white-to-transparent vertical gradient, plus a thin neon cyan outer glow. On the left side of this nav bar, place a compact logo: a stylized, minimal line-art icon of a three-tiered, isometric cube network (three cubes connected via thin lines) in bright cyan and neon pink, accompanied by the text "NEON NEXUS" in uppercase. The logo text uses a narrow, futuristic sans-serif font, like Eurostile or a condensed geometric typeface, in pure white, with slightly increased letter spacing, and a smaller subtitle underneath in lighter gray reading "FutureWork Symposium 2084". On the right side of the nav bar, aligned horizontally, include the navigation links "Overview", "Speakers", "Schedule", "Venue", "Tickets", and a final pill-shaped button labeled "Join Waitlist". The nav links are in small-caps, medium weight, white text that turns to neon cyan on hover, with a thin cyan underline appearing underneath. The "Join Waitlist" button is a pill capsule with a gradient from deep violet to neon magenta, white bold text, and a faint outer glow; it slightly overlaps the lower boundary of the nav bar, emphasizing the layered design. Directly beneath this nav bar, occupying the top 60–65% of the initial viewport, create a hero section with a complex overlapping composition. The left two-thirds of the hero is a text panel: a semi-translucent glass card with subtle rounded corners, offset slightly down and right from the nav bar so they visually relate, casting a soft, diffuse shadow. At the top of this panel, place a micro-label in tiny uppercase text: "HYBRID EVENT • JULY 28–30, 2084" in thin neon cyan text with a small circular cyan dot separating elements. Below this, in large, bold, uppercase letters, set the main title: "REIMAGINING WORK IN A QUANTUM WORLD" in white, using a wide, futuristic sans font with generous line spacing. The title should be left-aligned, with some words breaking onto multiple lines for dramatic effect. Beneath the title, a subheading paragraph in a lighter gray reads: "Join 3,000 founders, creatives, technologists, and futurists for three days of immersive keynotes, spatial co-working labs, and live prototyping sessions on the future of work, collaboration, and human–AI synergy." This is in a clean, modern sans-serif font, regular weight, flush left, with about 60% opacity to feel softer. Below the subheading, add two horizontally aligned primary call-to-action buttons that slightly overlap each other. The left button reads "Secure Your Seat" and is a bright cyan-to-teal gradient rectangle with subtle rounded corners, white bold text, and a slight internal glow; on hover it brightens and emits a neon cyan border. The right button reads "Download Event Guide" and is a hollow glass button with a thin magenta outline and white text, with a faint diagonal line pattern inside the glass. Below the buttons, aligned left, place a slim, horizontal metadata bar with minimal icons: a tiny line icon of a calendar followed by "July 28–30, 2084", a pin icon followed by "NeoSphere Hub, Neo-Tokyo + LiveStream", and a user-group icon followed by "Limited to 3,000 in-person seats". These icons are thin, outline-style, in neon cyan, and the text is in off-white. On the right third of the hero section, overlapping the right edge of the text panel and pushing slightly upward, place a large, vertical rectangular hero image that feels like an isometric concept art render in a semi-photorealistic but stylized sci-fi aesthetic. The image shows an expansive, multi-level futuristic co-working atrium at night, with transparent glass floors, floating desks, and motion-blurred silhouettes of people collaborating. The space is lit by luminous floor strips in cyan and magenta, with holographic screens hovering over desks displaying abstract data. In the foreground, a balcony edge in dark metal frames the scene, with a few figures leaning and talking. Midground, there are clusters of work pods—hexagonal tables with glowing rims and semi-transparent chairs. Background, a towering glass wall reveals a cyberpunk-style cityscape, filled with vertical neon signs and airborne vehicles leaving streaks of light. The perspective is slightly angled, as if the viewer is looking down from a higher platform. The lighting should be cool, with high contrast between dark structural elements and bright neon details, and soft reflections on glass surfaces. This hero image should have slightly rounded corners and a soft cyan glow along its bottom edge, as if it is a holographic projection emerging from the page. Behind the hero image, offset up and to the right, place a thin, sentinel-like vertical card in semi-transparent violet glass with minimal details: faint circuit-like lines and a tiny glowing label near the top that reads "ACCESS: FUTUREWORK LABS" in tiny cyan text, purely decorative, overlapping the hero image to heighten depth. Below the hero section, shift slightly down and let elements overlap vertically so that the next section tucks under the hero text panel’s lower edge. This next segment is a "Why Neon Nexus?" overview section. It employs three staggered, overlapping cards that ascend diagonally from left to right. The entire section has a wide, faint horizontal divider line that glows softly cyan for a few pixels, then fades, as if a scanning beam is running under the cards. At the top left, a small section title in uppercase reads "WHY ATTEND" in neon cyan, and below it the larger heading "Designed for the new era of work" in white. Beneath this heading, a short paragraph in muted gray explains: "From quantum collaboration zones to silent deep-focus pods, Neon Nexus is designed to rewire how you connect, create, and build with a global community." Below this explanation, the three cards are: Card 1 on the left, slightly larger and higher, titled "Immersive Keynotes" with a subtext: "Experience live holographic keynotes with real-time AI translation and interactive Q&A overlays." Card 2 in the middle, slightly lower and overlapping the right edge of Card 1, titled "Spatial Co-Working" with subtext: "Work from modular pods, ambient VR lounges, and AR-enhanced collaboration bays that react to your workflow." Card 3 on the right, slightly lower and partially covered by Card 2’s left corner, titled "Future Skills Labs" with subtext: "Hands-on labs for AI-first workflows, bioadaptive productivity, and regenerative leadership." Each card is a semi-transparent, frosted glass rectangle with neon cyan corner accents—tiny 3–5px L-shaped corner markers—and a faint gridline pattern in the background. Titles are white, in a medium-weight sans serif; subtexts are lighter gray. Each card has a small icon at the top left in neon cyan outline style: Card 1 shows a stylized hologram pyramid projecting a figure; Card 2 shows a multi-level workspace with tiny blocks; Card 3 shows an abstract neuron circuit. All three cards cast soft shadows on the background and slightly overlap one another, creating depth and hierarchy. Continuing downward, add a "Featured Speakers" section dominated by a lateral overlapping layout. The section uses a slightly darker background overlay to visually separate it, and faint vertical lines in translucent cyan sweep behind, like a HUD interface. A heading aligned left reads "Featured Speakers" in white, with a smaller cyan label above it reading "VOICES FROM 2084". Beneath the heading, add a one-line description: "Global leaders reimagining how we work, build, and live alongside intelligent systems." Below, display four speaker cards in a horizontal scroll-style row (for desktop, all four visible; but design suggests you could scroll). These portrait cards should slightly overlap each other at their edges, with the central two cards emphasized (larger, slightly pulled forward with stronger shadows). Each card features an upper half with a stylized, high-contrast portrait of the speaker and a lower half with text details. The portraits are semi-realistic illustrations with a futuristic color grade: cyan, violet, and magenta lighting washes over faces, with soft rim light from digital interfaces. Speaker 1: "Dr. Amara Li"—a mid-30s Asian woman with short, asymmetrical bob haircut, wearing a minimalist high-collar black jacket with subtle circuitry patterns glowing faint cyan. Her background is a blurred city of neon skyscrapers with floating data glyphs. Speaker 2: "Jonah Reyes"—a Latine non-binary person with shaved sides and a textured top haircut, wearing translucent AR glasses and a dark, sleek bomber jacket with reflective strips. Behind them, glowing geometric shapes rotate in the background. Speaker 3: "Prof. Naima El-Tayeb"—a North African woman in her 40s with a calm expression, wearing a modern headscarf with metallic threads and a structured, futuristic blazer; behind her is a digital aurora of lines of code and neural networks. Speaker 4: "Ravi Kapoor"—a South Asian man in his 30s with a close-cropped beard and subtle cybernetic implant near one temple, wearing a minimalist suit with a luminous lapel pin. Each speaker card’s lower half is a glass panel with their name in bold white, their title in light gray (e.g., "Systems Philosopher, Quantum Commons", "Chief Architect, Synapse WorkOS", etc.), and a line in neon cyan reading "Keynote: [Title]" such as "Keynote: Designing Human-Centric Quantum Workflows". Tiny neon cyan chips along the card’s edges show decorative codes like "S-01" or "S-02". Below the speaker row, aligned right, is a small, hollow "View full lineup" button with cyan text and a simple right-pointing chevron icon. Next, introduce a "Schedule Snapshot" section anchored to the left, with a large overlapping timetable card on the right. At the left, the heading "Schedule Snapshot" in white sits above the cyan label "3 DAYS • 6 STAGES • 40+ SESSIONS". Below, a short line in muted gray: "Layered programming designed so you never have to choose between depth and discovery." On the right side, a tall, slightly tilted vertical schedule panel appears like a translucent holographic tablet floating over the background. The panel has a thin neon border that subtly shifts from cyan at the top to magenta at the bottom. Inside, divide the panel into three horizontal bands for "Day 1: Ignite", "Day 2: Build", and "Day 3: Integrate". Each band has a bold white subheading and a simple, clean listing of two or three example sessions with times in cyan and session titles in white, e.g., "09:30 • Opening Keynote: 'Weaving Quantum Workflows into Daily Life'", "14:00 • Lab: Designing Regenerative Co-Working Rituals". Thin horizontal dividers between sessions glow faintly. Slightly overlapping the bottom left of this schedule panel, add a smaller tag-shaped glass card reading "Live + On-Demand" in cyan with a small play icon (but clarify it is informational only, not a video player). Further down, transition into a "Spaces & Experience" section that visually mimics a futuristic real estate showcase of the co-working and venue features. The section heading, centered, reads "Spaces & Experience" in white. Just above it, a cyan micro-label states "THE NEOSPHERE HUB". Below the heading, place a wide, asymmetrical grid of three image tiles: one large on the left and two stacked on the right, all slightly misaligned to create natural asymmetry and overlapping. Each tile has rounded corners and a subtle cyan glow at edges, plus a delicate circuit pattern overlay. The largest left tile shows a photorealistic render of a spacious, multi-level co-working zone inside the event venue: sleek white and dark metal surfaces, floor-to-ceiling glass, modular desks that can shift shapes, and visitors using transparent displays layered with holographic interface elements. The lighting is cool and diffuse, with strips of neon cyan and magenta in the ceiling. People are casually dressed but with subtle futuristic fashion – smart fabrics, reflective accents, luminous wearables. The top-right tile shows a "Quantum Focus Pod": a small, capsule-like space with a reclining ergonomic seat, dim ambient lighting, and dynamic LED panels that display soothing abstract patterns. The pod's walls are soft gray, and a person inside has their eyes closed, surrounded by floating AR glyphs in pale cyan, representing focused work. The bottom-right tile depicts the "Sky Terrace": an outdoor rooftop lounge at night, overlooking a neon-drenched city skyline. Guests mingle around low tables that emit soft light; hovering orbs cast gentle illumination. The terrace has minimal greenery in sculpted planters with bio-luminescent plants. Below the grid, aligned left, a short paragraph in muted gray states: "Every zone inside NeoSphere is tuned for different modes of work—from code sprints in analog-free maker bays to silent reflection chambers synced to your biometrics." Under this, three small icon-with-text labels arranged in a row show "Biometric-Responsive Lighting", "Neurodiverse-friendly Zones", and "Zero-Latency Connectivity", each with a simple neon cyan icon (a pulse line, a branching mind icon, and a network node cluster, respectively). Continue downward to a section focused on the event’s "Tracks & Labs", using overlapping horizontal ribbons. Create three wide, horizontal bar-like panels stacked vertically, each shifted slightly left or right so their edges are not aligned. These panels are translucent with embedded diagonally oriented, faint geometric patterns. Label them "Track 01: Human x AI Collaboration", "Track 02: Spatial Co-Working & Hybrid Teams", and "Track 03: Regenerative Leadership & Wellbeing". Each panel includes a brief one-sentence description in muted gray and 2–3 small "Tag chips" in rounded pills with cyan outlines and tiny uppercase text such as "LIVE PROTOTYPING", "WORKSHOP", "FIRESIDE", "MICRO-LAB". Some of these tag chips overlap the edge of the panel and hover into the background, further emphasizing the layered aesthetic. Toward the lower-middle of the page, add a dedicated "Ticket Tiers" section with a more grounded, structured layout that still uses overlapping details. Use a centered heading "Ticket Tiers" in white, with the cyan label "LIMITED ACCESS" above. Below, present three pricing cards arranged in a loose row: "Virtual Pass", "Hybrid Pass", and "Founder Circle". Stagger their vertical positions so the middle "Hybrid Pass" card appears slightly elevated and overlapping the others’ edges, visually highlighting it as a recommended option. Each card is a glass panel with a subtle internal gradient, thin neon border, and a glowing accent in one corner (cyan for Virtual, violet for Hybrid, magenta for Founder Circle). Inside each card, show the tier name in bold white, a price in larger text (e.g., "₿0.15" or "Ξ0.08" to hint at futuristic currency), and a short bullet-like list of included benefits written as short phrases separated by tiny cyan dots rather than bullet icons, such as "Full livestream access • On-demand archives • AI-generated session notes". At the bottom of each card is a compact "Reserve" button in cyan with white text. Under the cards, center-align a narrow, full-width note in soft gray: "Need team access? Contact us for group and enterprise packages." with the words "Contact us" appearing as a cyan text link. As the user continues scrolling, present a "Location & Access" section split into two overlapping panels. On the left, a stylized map card shows an abstract, isometric map of "Neo-Tokyo" at night. This is not a standard map but a design-heavy illustration: dark grid streets with bright cyan lines, glowing nodes marking transit hubs, and a larger magenta pulsing circle over "NeoSphere Hub". Street names are replaced with minimal labels like "Transit Loop A", "SkyRail Node 7", etc., in tiny cyan text. The map card has a subtle glow and a thin border. Overlapping its lower right corner is a smaller, narrow, vertical pill card that reads "5 min from SkyRail Node 7" with a tiny train icon. On the right of this section, a content panel details the venue: heading "NeoSphere Hub" in white, address "Level 87, Neo-Tokyo Vertical District" in lighter gray, and a short paragraph describing the venue’s sustainable design and integrated virtual experience, in muted gray. Below that, a small list of travel options appears as three compact, pill-shaped items: "SkyRail", "Drone Taxi", and "Quantum Telepresence", each with a corresponding minimal icon. At the very bottom of this panel, include a tiny, understated link-style text "View access guide" in cyan. Near the footer, introduce a "Stay Connected" call-to-action band that partially overlaps both the previous section and the footer. This band is a wide, semi-transparent strip spanning the page width with a pop of color: a subtle diagonal gradient from dark cyan to dark violet. Inside, centered but with an asymmetrical composition, place the heading "Be the first to access future work labs." in white, and a short line below in soft gray: "Get curated dispatches on future-of-work experiments, new labs, and last-minute ticket releases." Beneath this, align horizontally a futuristic email input field and a "Join Waitlist" button. The input field looks like a glass capsule with a faint internal glow; placeholder text reads "Enter your work email" in light gray. The button is a bold, magenta-to-violet gradient pill with white uppercase text and a subtle outer glow, slightly overlapping the right edge of the input field. Below this band, the footer rests on a slightly deeper black background with a barely-there starfield texture, suggesting infinite space. Split the footer into three columns, but keep the layout light and airy, with generous spacing. In the left column, restate the logo in smaller form, with "NEON NEXUS" and "FutureWork Symposium 2084" underneath, and a brief tagline in muted gray: "Co-created by the NeoSphere Collective." The middle column shows two subheadings in cyan: "Quick Links" and "Experience". Under "Quick Links", list text links in white: "FAQs", "Code of Conduct", "Partners", "Press". Under "Experience", list "Hybrid Guide", "Accessibility", "Volunteer". The right column has "Follow the signal" in cyan, and a row of minimal icon buttons for future-facing social platforms (represented as simple glyphs, like a square, a triangle, a hexagon, etc.) in neon outlines that invert to solid when hovered. At the very bottom of the page, center-align a thin, faint line in cyan, above tiny light gray text reading "© 2084 Neon Nexus. All rights reserved. Built for the next chapter of work." All text throughout the page should use a cohesive typographic system: headings in a futuristic, geometric sans-serif with wide letterspacing and mostly uppercase; body text in a clean, legible sans-serif with regular weight and ample line height. Buttons and key labels are uppercase. The entire page should emphasize a hierarchical layout with multiple layers of overlapping translucent panels, cards, and tags, creating depth and a sense of holographic interface. No videos, animations, or charts are present—only static imagery, icons, and text, all arranged in a calm but visually rich, futuristic event homepage for a hybrid symposium about the future of work and co-working.

instruction: Planning the generation details for the following website:
input: Design a minimal, single-column–oriented homepage dashboard for an online coding tutorial platform called “Loopcraft Academy,” with a clean, airy, almost meditative aesthetic, no videos, no animation, and no charts or data visualizations. The entire page sits centered within a very light warm-gray background (#F5F5F2) that fills the browser, with the main content column framed by generous horizontal margins on desktop, so that the content column feels like a tall card resting in the middle of the viewport. The main column itself is constrained to a max-width of about 960px and has a pure white background (#FFFFFF), with subtly rounded corners (8px radius) and a barely perceptible soft shadow to lift it from the page. The overall spacing is relaxed: plenty of white space between sections, consistent vertical rhythm, and a strict typographic hierarchy. The primary font is a modern, geometric sans-serif (similar to “Inter” or “SF Pro”), with headings in a slightly heavier weight and body copy in regular weight. All text is dark charcoal (#1F1F1F) with secondary text in a muted gray (#777777). The accent color is a minimalist muted cobalt blue (#3A6DFF), used sparingly for key interactive elements and subtle highlights. At the top of the page, a slim horizontal header bar sits flush with the top of the white main card. On the left of this header, the logo text reads “Loopcraft” in a clean, lowercase wordmark in bold weight, with “loop” in charcoal and “craft” in muted blue; to the left of the text there is a very simple icon: a small outlined square composed of four thin rounded lines forming a continuous loop, drawn in the same muted blue. On the right side of the header, horizontally aligned and right-justified within the main column, are three compact navigation links: “Dashboard”, “Courses”, and “Community” in small uppercase letters, spaced apart evenly with subtle letterspacing, using the charcoal color; the currently active page, “Dashboard”, has a minimal indicator: a thin 2px blue underline directly beneath the text. To the far right of the header, slightly separated by some whitespace, is a circular user avatar placeholder: a small, 32px circle with a soft neutral beige background and an abstract monoline outline of a person’s bust in charcoal, centered within. Below the header, the hero section spans the full width of the content card, maintaining the single-column feeling but with some asymmetry in the internal composition. The hero background remains white, with no colored block behind it, emphasizing minimalism. At the top-left of this hero section is a greeting in small, uppercase gray text: “GOOD MORNING,” followed directly below by the user’s name in a large, bold heading saying “Welcome back, Alex.” Beneath this main heading is a single concise line of text in muted gray: “Continue your learning loop with focused, distraction-free coding sessions.” To the right of this text block but still within the same hero section, slightly overlapping into the right side while keeping the overall impression of a single column, is a tall, narrow minimalist illustration framed inside a very light gray outlined rectangle. This static image shows a flat-design, monochrome scene: a simple desk with a single slim laptop open at a 110-degree angle, viewed from a slightly elevated isometric angle. The laptop screen displays stylized lines of code as short horizontal bars in three shades of gray. Behind the laptop, in the background, are three abstract vertical rectangles of varying height representing books or monitors, each in a slightly different neutral gray tone. The composition is very clean, nearly monochrome, with a single hint of the accent blue used for one highlighted code line on the screen. The lines are crisp, shadows are soft and subtle, and the style is ultra-flat, with no gradients, just slight differences in tone for separation. Beneath the greeting text in the hero area, aligned left and reinforcing the dashboard function, is a compact “primary action strip” containing two buttons. The main button on the left is a filled button with text “Resume Course” centered in white, on a muted cobalt blue background, with a pill-shaped outline and subtle padding, no icon, and a very slight drop shadow to differentiate it from the background. To the right of it, spaced by a small gap, is a secondary button with text “Browse Paths” in charcoal, inside a pill-shaped outline with a thin 1px gray border, transparent white background. Both buttons are horizontally aligned but do not stretch fully across the width; instead they remain compact and centered relative to the text content, preserving white space around them. Below this hero area, a thin, light gray horizontal divider line spans the width of the main column, subtly separating the introductory narrative from the main dashboard content. The next section, titled “Today’s Focus,” appears as a self-contained card within the column, with a faint light gray border and slightly rounded corners inset from the main card. The section heading “Today’s Focus” is left-aligned in medium-weight, slightly larger text than body copy. Directly underneath is a concise line: “One concept, one small win.” in muted gray. Inside this card, the layout is vertically stacked, but with slight asymmetry through alignment differences. At the top of this card’s content, on the left, is a label chip reading “Ongoing Module” in tiny uppercase letters, with rounded ends and a soft, nearly white-blue background (#ECF1FF) and text in the accent blue. Below that chip is a larger lesson title: “Async JavaScript: Promises & Fetch” in bold, slightly larger than normal text. Beneath the lesson title is a two-line description: “You’re mid-way through understanding how the browser waits, listens, and responds. Stay in the flow with your next 18-minute block.” aligned left, in muted gray. To the right side of this card’s content, but still within the single column and not too wide, is a vertical stack of a progress label and a very minimal progress bar. The label reads “Progress: 42%” in small, charcoal text. Directly under it is a flat horizontal line representing progress: a thin 6px-high rounded bar stretching about half the width of the card. The background of the bar is a faint, neutral gray (#E3E3E3), and the filled portion on the left is the accent blue, occupying roughly 42% of its length. There are no numeric tick marks, only the visual impression of progress. Under the progress bar, a very small text line in gray reads “Next block: 3 mini-challenges, 2 code reviews.” At the bottom of this “Today’s Focus” card, aligned to the left and spaced apart from the description, is a subtle text-only link reading “View full module →” in the accent blue, no underline, but it changes to a darker blue text (described for hover in code) and is slightly bolded. Beneath the “Today’s Focus” card, a generous margin leads into the main “Your Paths” section. The heading “Your Paths” is larger, bold, left-aligned, and stands alone without any icon. A soft gray sublabel below reads “Structured sequences you’re actively following.” Here, instead of a grid, maintain the single-column ethos: three vertically stacked path cards, each spanning the width of the content column but with an internal asymmetry. Each path card has a white background, thin gray border, rounded corners, and is separated vertically by consistent spacing. Each card’s left side is reserved for textual path information, while the right edge features a narrow vertical column with a minimal tag. The first card is titled “Frontend Foundations” in medium-large bold text. Underneath, a single line of muted gray text says: “HTML, CSS, and the mental models to ship accessible interfaces.” Below that, three inline pill-shaped tags are arranged horizontally: “In Progress” with a blue outline and blue text, “12 hrs left” in gray outline and gray text, and “Beginner” in a very light gray outline and charcoal text. On the far right edge of the card, vertically centered, is a slim vertical tag: a small capsule reading “Path 1” in tiny uppercase letters, with a very faint gray background. The second card is titled “Modern JavaScript & Beyond”, with the description “From ES6+ essentials to patterns that scale without chaos.” Its inline tags are “In Progress”, “6 hrs left”, and “Intermediate” with the same styling, but the “Intermediate” tag has a slightly darker gray text, hinting at difference without breaking the minimal palette. The right-edge capsule reads “Path 2”. The third card is titled “Backend Basics with Node”, with description “Learn to build simple, composable APIs and connect them to real data.” Its tags are “Not Started”, “8 hrs total”, and “Beginner”; here the “Not Started” tag is a subtle outline-only capsule with muted gray text, no blue. The right-edge capsule reads “Path 3.” There are no decorative icons or illustrations within these path cards, just clean typography and white space. Under “Your Paths”, another slim light gray divider line visually separates into a more utility-like segment called “Quick Entry.” The heading “Quick Entry” is medium-sized and left-aligned, with a subtext line: “Jump into an environment that matches your energy.” There are two horizontally stacked tiles that slightly break the strict single-column symmetry but still fit within the column, each taking about half the width but with clear space between them. On the left, the tile “Practice Mode” appears as a white card with a soft gray border and small rounded corners. Its title “Practice Mode” is bold and medium-sized. Below it is a line in gray: “Start a blank coding scratchpad.” Beneath this text is a single, thin outlined button reading “Open Editor” in charcoal. On this tile’s far-left edge, aligned to the top-left of the text, there is a small, flat icon: a hollow square with a smaller filled square offset slightly inside, representing a window, drawn in gray lines only. On the right, the second tile “Review Mode” mirrors the layout but its text reads: “Revisit your last 5 code submissions.” and its button says “View History”. Its small icon is a series of three short, horizontal stacked rectangles, each slightly offset, like a minimalistic list, also in muted gray. Both tiles keep the ultra-minimal aesthetic, no color fill aside from white and gray. Beneath this, returning to the full-width single-column format, is a section titled “Notes for Later”. The heading sits left-aligned, and just beneath it on the right side of the heading row is a simple text link “View all notes” in blue. The section’s content is a single large, border-outlined text area, representing a static summary rather than an interactive input, with rounded corners and a faint “lined paper” effect: extremely subtle horizontal hairline gray rules running across the white background, evenly spaced, like faint notebook lines. Inside this box are three bullet points rendered as small filled circles in charcoal, followed by text. Each line uses the same font as the rest of the page, slightly smaller than body text, in gray. The bullets read: “Clarify async/await error handling patterns.”, “Re-watch section on event loop visualization.”, and “Try rewriting tomorrow’s project without any global variables.” The bullets are left-aligned with a fixed, structured indentation; there is generous padding within this note box so the text does not crowd the edges. Further down, after another breath of white space, is a “Signals” section—still minimal, leaning into the dashboard concept without graphs. The heading “Signals” is bold and left-aligned. Under this, three small horizontally aligned statistic cards appear in a row, each occupying about one-third of the content width on large screens, but stacked vertically in a single column on smaller screens (though the prompt just describes the finished layout visually for desktop: the cards are arranged side-by-side but clearly part of the same main column). Each stat card has no background fill other than white and uses only subtle separators. The first stat card on the left has a small label text “Streak” in uppercase gray at the top, and beneath it, in much larger bold numbers, “7” with a smaller gray line below stating “days in a row.” The second stat card has the label “This Week” and the main number “4.5h” with the line “focused coding time.” The third card shows “Sessions” and “23” with the line “completed mini-lessons.” No icons, no bars, just typography and spacing. Just below the “Signals” cards, aligned flush left, is a monochrome illustration: a static, flat-design image showing a minimalist calendar page with a circled date and a small dot next to several days, all in grayscale with one single accent-blue dot marking today. The calendar illustration appears as if lightly sketched with ultra-clean vector lines, no heavy borders, placed to subtly underscore routines without stealing focus. Finally, at the bottom of the main column, a narrow, unobtrusive footer rests with muted gray text, left-aligned, reading “Loopcraft Academy · Learn in quiet loops, at your own pace.” On the right side of the same footer bar, aligned right but within the same width, are two tiny, understated links: “Privacy” and “Terms” in charcoal, separated by a dot. The entire page emphasizes calm, clarity, and hierarchy: generous padding around all major blocks, consistent use of the accent blue only for actionable items (primary button, link texts, minimal chips, and a single highlight in an illustration), and a near absence of decorative noise, ensuring the dashboard feels like a serene control room for one’s coding practice, with all content static, no motion, and no data charts.

instruction: Planning the generation details for the following website:
input:
Design a stark, brutalist single-column portfolio homepage for a fictional digital artist and front-end developer, branded as “RAW LAYOUTS.” The overall page sits on an off-white background (#f3f3f3) with large, unapologetic black typography and harsh, rectangular blocks of color. There are no rounded corners anywhere—every box is a sharp rectangle. The main content follows a predominantly single-column flow centered on the page, but with deliberate, occasional horizontal shifts that create asymmetry. The page should feel like an unapologetic art-directed PDF printed on screen: monochrome dominant with 1–2 accent colors (a toxic acid green #b5ff00 and an electric blue #0066ff) used sparingly in blocks and underlines. Use a single sans-serif font that feels slightly grotesque and compressed—think of something like “Helvetica Now Display” or “IBM Plex Sans”—with heavy weights for headings (900) and normal for body. All text is left-aligned except where explicitly noted. The viewport background color is solid #f3f3f3; no gradients, no shadows, no soft effects.

At the very top, spanning full width, place a thin black horizontal strip (height ~40px) pinned to the top, with the site name in uppercase on the left and a brutally simple navigation on the right. The left side reads in small uppercase letters, tight letter-spacing: “RAW LAYOUTS / PORTFOLIO OF ARIA KLINE”. This text is pure black on the off-white strip; the strip itself has a 1px solid black border at its bottom edge. On the right side of this strip, aligned to the right with some breathing room, place three navigation links in uppercase, separated by vertical bars: “WORK”, “INFO”, “CONTACT”. These links appear as plain black text that does not change on hover (no animations described), but each link is underlined with a solid 1px line that looks like it was drawn manually: slightly offset from the baseline, which can be represented by positioning the underline 2px below the text. Below this top strip, leave a large vertical gap of around 120px of empty off-white space, making the page feel almost uncomfortable in its spacing.

Centered underneath, but aligned left in a fixed-width column (e.g., 760px wide) that remains centered in the browser, place the main hero block. This block consists of two sharply divided horizontal regions: on top, a brutalist textual statement; underneath it, a large, flat, poster-like image. The hero text area is a full-width rectangle with a solid black border (3px) and white background (#ffffff). Inside, aligned to the left with heavy padding (~40px top and bottom, 40px left, 40px right), the main heading reads in large uppercase text, almost absurdly big: “I DESIGN WEBSITES THAT REFUSE TO BE QUIET.” The font is black, bold, tightly tracked, and takes up two or three lines. Below the heading, a smaller subheading in regular weight, also black and left-aligned, reads: “Selected digital layouts, interfaces, and visual experiments by Aria Kline.” Beneath that, separated by a blank line, is a short line presented as a label, in uppercase and acid green: “BASED IN ROTTERDAM / AVAILABLE REMOTELY”. This label sits in a small rectangular acid green highlight bar behind the text (the text itself is black). The rectangle should precisely match the text width, with no rounded corners. The overall feel is stark and editorial.

Directly below this hero text block, with only a 10px gap that feels almost accidental, place a large static hero image that stretches the width of the main column. This is a generated image, not a video: a flat, graphic, poster-like illustration in a brutalist, almost screen-printed style. The image shows an abstract “digital workspace” composition from a straight-on, orthographic perspective. The background of the image is a flat split-color: the left two-thirds is a pale grey (#e0e0e0), and the right third is pure black. On the left pale grey side, several overlapping brutalist “windows” and rectangular panels represent website layouts: black rectangles, white rectangles, and acid green and electric blue accent bars, stacked asymmetrically. Some rectangles have thick black borders, some are solid filled blocks, and a few contain simple, minimalist icon-like symbols (a thin outline of a cursor arrow, a hollow circle, and a plus sign), all in black. On the right black side of the image, a simplified, almost silhouette-style figure sits at a desk viewed from the side, facing left. The figure is stylized with minimal detail: block shapes for the torso and head, colored in off-white with a thin black outline. The desk is a flat electric blue rectangle, and on it sits a simple monitor represented by a white rectangle with a black border showing a grid of black lines mimicking a wireframe. Lighting is flat: no gradients, minimal shading; edges are crisp and geometric, giving the whole illustration a poster-printed vibe. This hero image has a 2px solid black border, continuing the harsh boxed-in feeling.

Under the hero section, add a single line of text in small uppercase letters directly below the hero image, aligned left: “SCROLL DOWN FOR WORK THAT DOESN’T BLEND IN.” This line is in black with a thin electric blue underline spanning the full width of the text.

The portfolio “WORK” section follows, still in the same centered single-column. Start with a bold, oversized section label flush left reading: “SELECTED PROJECTS (2019–2024)”. This label is in uppercase black text against a solid acid green background rectangle that spans the full column width and has a height of about 60px. Inside this rectangle, the text is vertically centered with some padding. Immediately below, arrange a vertical stack of project entries. Each project is represented as a horizontal block that uses asymmetry but remains constrained to the central single column. The layout for each project: a left narrow column containing the project title, year, and role; and a right wider block containing either a static thumbnail image or a simple colored box with descriptive text over it. The left column is about 25% width of the main column; the right column takes the remaining 75%. The entire project block has a top and bottom border (1px solid black) and about 30px vertical padding.

For the first project, in the left column at the top, place the project title in uppercase bold: “00A / CODE CHALLENGE SUBSCRIPTION”. Directly underneath in smaller uppercase text, lighter weight: “2024 / INTERFACE + BRAND SYSTEM”. The right side shows a static thumbnail image representing a brutalist landing page for a coding challenge tutorial subscription. This image is a flat, straight-on screenshot-style mockup framed in a thin black border. The background of the mockup is off-white, with a giant black block at the top containing white text: “LEARN BY BREAKING THINGS.” Underneath this block are simple stacked rectangles mimicking content blocks: modules of acid green, electric blue, and white, each with black lines and pseudo-text. The lower part shows a simplified pricing table: three tall rectangles side by side, differing in fill color (one white, one acid green, one blue), with minimal black horizontal lines suggesting rows. No legible small text, but overall layout should clearly communicate a subscription pricing layout. The mockup’s composition is frontal, with no perspective distortion.

Second project: same structural layout. Left column title in uppercase bold: “00B / ECO-LIVING TUTORIAL HUB”. Below: “2023 / UX DESIGN + SYSTEM”. The right thumbnail is a flat mockup image of a homepage layout in a more muted palette: dark forest green background (#113322), off-white blocks, and sparse acid green accents. The mockup shows a huge off-white text banner in the upper third reading in bold uppercase: “LESS WASTE. MORE INTENT.” Beneath it, three stacked horizontal bands of different widths mimic article cards: each band has a small square icon on the left (simple line drawings of a leaf, a jar, and a plug), and a long rectangle representing a headline. At the bottom of the mockup, three circles arranged horizontally hint at pagination. All lines are black and crisp.

Third project: left column title: “00C / DIGITAL BOOK CLUB”. Below: “2022 / ART DIRECTION”. Right side: instead of an image, this project uses a solid white rectangle with a thick, 4px electric blue border and centered black text. The text, all uppercase and centered, reads: “THIS PROJECT IS INTENTIONALLY LEFT UNDER-DESIGNED.” Underneath, in smaller text, also centered: “(WORK IN PROGRESS / EMAIL FOR PREVIEW)”. This is a conceptual nod to brutalist incompleteness.

Insert a noticeable slab of empty white space (around 80px tall) between the “SELECTED PROJECTS” stack and the next section, reinforcing the sparse feeling. Then, introduce an “INFO” section, again within the same central column but shifting the content block slightly to the left so that its left edge aligns 40px left of the main column edge, creating a deliberate misalignment. At the top of this section, in a box with no fill, only a 2px black border, place the heading: “INFO / MANIFESTO”. Inside this box, left-aligned with generous padding, display a block of text in plain paragraph style. The first line is in uppercase: “I BUILD DELIBERATELY AWKWARD, HIGH-CONTRAST INTERFACES.” Below it, a short paragraph in sentence case, about 4–5 lines: “This portfolio is a rejection of frictionless sameness. I design structures where every decision is visible, every hierarchy is unapologetic, and every click feels intentional. If you’re looking for pastel gradients and rounded buttons, you’re probably in the wrong place.” Beneath this text, separated by a blank line, add a simple two-column list of capabilities. The word “FOCUS:” appears in uppercase on the left, followed by three stacked lines: “Interface layout systems”, “Design for code-heavy teams”, “Typographic identity for the web”. On the right, aligned horizontally at the top, place “TOOLS:” in uppercase, followed by “Figma / HTML / CSS / Basic JS / Pen + paper”. Maintain strict left alignment inside the box, but keep the box itself misaligned relative to the main column.

Directly under this box, but realigned with the main column, add a very minimal text-only “SELECTED CLIENTS + CONTEXT” line in small uppercase characters, then list names in a single line separated by slashes: “INDEPENDENT DEVELOPERS / SMALL STUDIOS / FORMAT-BREAKING BRANDS”. This line is black text with no decoration.

Continue downward to a small “CONTACT” section. This section starts with a thin horizontal black rule spanning the full central column. Above the rule, sitting slightly overlapping it, is a small white label box outlined in black with uppercase text: “CONTACT”. Below the rule, leave some padding, then present the main call-to-contact in large uppercase text: “EMAIL IS BEST.” Underneath in a slightly smaller but still bold uppercase, present a fake address: “HELLO@RAWLAYOUTS.STUDIO”. This email sits on a narrow acid green highlight bar that extends slightly past the text width on both sides. Underneath, add a simple note in sentence case: “No contact forms, no bots. Just send a message with what you’re building and why it should look like this.” This line is aligned left and occupies about half the column width, leaving the right half blank, emphasizing negative space.

At the very bottom of the page, center a small, horizontally aligned footer line in tiny uppercase black text, forming a subtle visual baseline at the bottom of the viewport: “© 2024 ARIA KLINE / BUILT WITH HTML + CSS / NO FRAMEWORKS”. This line sits inside a simple white footer strip with a 1px top border, spanning full width but with the text centered within the central column. There are no animations, no hover transitions described, and no charts, graphs, or data visualizations anywhere. The entire page relies on static, high-contrast compositions, blunt typography, strict rectangular geometry, and a nearly single-column rhythm disrupted occasionally by deliberate misalignment and brutal blocks of saturated accent color.

instruction: Planning the generation details for the following website:
input:
Design a tall, scrollable event landing page for a nonprofit music festival, styled like a high-end editorial magazine spread. The aesthetic is minimal, monochrome with selective muted color accents, heavy use of white space, and large serif typography, with a hierarchical and overlapping layout that breaks the grid in controlled, asymmetrical ways. The entire background of the page is a warm, off‑white (#f8f4ef) with a subtle paper grain texture, like fine art magazine stock. At the very top, a slim, centered masthead bar spans the width, with generous padding and no visible border. On the left of this bar, in small caps serif text, is the logo text: "HORIZON SOUND COLLECTIVE" in a charcoal grey (#222222), letterspacing slightly expanded, using an elegant modern serif font reminiscent of Didot. On the right side of this bar is a minimalist navigation in a small, uppercase sans‑serif: "ABOUT", "LINEUP", "PROGRAM", "TICKETS", and "SUPPORT", each spaced widely with no separators, aligned to the right edge; the active section "PROGRAM" is underlined with a thin, hand‑drawn style line in a muted terracotta tone (#c76f4c). Beneath the masthead, the hero area occupies almost the entire first viewport and is composed as a collage-like editorial spread: on the left two‑thirds, a large vertical photograph dominates, slightly overlapping the masthead and extending downward, with subtle drop shadow suggesting it is a print dropped on the page. The photo is a high‑contrast black and white, documentary‑style image of a small ensemble of street musicians performing in an urban courtyard during golden hour. The perspective is slightly low and angled, looking up toward the musicians, some seated and some standing, with soft sunlight glowing from behind them, casting long shadows on cobblestones. The foreground shows the blurred outline of someone clapping, while in the midground the musicians play cello, violin, and clarinet, wearing casual yet stylish clothes; the background has softly out‑of‑focus brick buildings and vines climbing a wall. Grain and film‑like texture are visible, enhancing the analog feel. On the right one‑third of the hero area, overlapping the photo’s right edge, is a tall, narrow text block on an almost white card (#fdfaf6) with a thin charcoal border and a slight shadow, tilted just a degree off straight to feel casually placed. At the top of this card, in small uppercase sans serif, is the label: "AN URBAN BENEFIT FESTIVAL". Below that, the main title reads across two lines in a large, bold serif: "HORIZON SOUND / NIGHTS", where "HORIZON SOUND" is in charcoal and "NIGHTS" is in a muted deep teal (#1d5052). Under the title, a subheading in smaller serif, italic, states: "A nonprofit evening series for community, music, and mutual aid." Beneath, in a slightly larger sans serif, the date and location are displayed: "September 27–29, 2025 · Riverside Warehouse District" centered on the card. At the bottom of this card, two horizontal buttons sit side‑by‑side, partially overlapping the lower edge of the hero photo: the primary button on the left reads "Get Tickets" in uppercase sans serif, white text on deep teal with softly rounded corners and subtle inner shadow; the secondary button on the right says "View Full Program" in charcoal text with a thin charcoal border and transparent background. Hover states (though static in the design) are implied: the primary button slightly deepens in color; the secondary fills with a pale terracotta wash.

Just below the hero, overlapping its lower edge and aligned slightly left of center, there is a narrow horizontal strip that looks like a cut‑out from a printed event program: a very light grey strip with a tiny uppercase label on the left, "In support of neighborhood arts education", and on the right a small, thin outlined pill tag with text "501(c)(3) nonprofit" in micro‑sized sans serif. Soft shadows and overlapping of elements give a stacked-paper feel. As we scroll down, the next section uses an asymmetrical, editorial two‑column layout with floating elements. On the left, a wide column with a headline in black serif reads: "A festival that sounds like the city it serves." Below it, in a narrower width than the headline to create white margin on the right, is a block of body copy in a legible serif, medium size, dark grey: three paragraphs explaining the mission: "Horizon Sound Nights is a three‑evening music event curated by community organizers, independent musicians, and local venues. All proceeds support free music education workshops for youth across the city." followed by "Each night brings together classical, jazz, experimental, and folk performances in a single, continuous listening experience—no VIP sections, no barriers." and "Think of it as an open‑door magazine issue, printed in sound: layered, imperfect, and deeply human." The paragraph lines are slightly narrower than typical, creating a comfortable reading column. On the right side, slightly higher on the page so edges do not align perfectly, is a tall, narrow image overlapping into the white space above: a full‑bleed, desaturated color photograph of a hand pinning a printed poster to a weathered bulletin board. The poster shows abstract geometric shapes in teal, terracotta, and mustard yellow. The photograph is shot from a close, angled side view; the focus is on the fingers and the texture of the paper, with the background blurred. The photo has a subtle warm tint.

Below this section is the "Featured Nights" section with a staggered, editorial layout resembling layers of magazine pages. The section title "Three nights, three distinct stories." appears, left‑aligned, using uppercase small serif for "FEATURED NIGHTS" as a pre‑label in muted terracotta, then the main line in large serif. Underneath, three overlapping cards represent "Night One", "Night Two", and "Night Three". They are arranged diagonally from left to right: the first card slightly lower and more left, the second slightly higher and centered, the third overlapping slightly on the right and a little lower again, creating a zigzag vertical rhythm. Each card is a vertical rectangle with a soft shadow and off‑white background. "Night One — Open Air Overtures" shows, at its top, a muted color photograph in a matte editorial style: a rooftop at dusk, with string lights crisscrossing overhead, silhouetted musicians setting up stands and chairs, and a pastel gradient sky transitioning from dusky lavender to deep blue. The perspective is slightly wide‑angle, taken from the corner of the rooftop, lines receding to the horizon. Below the photo, the card has a subheading: "FRIDAY · CLASSICAL & CONTEMPORARY" in small uppercase sans serif, followed by a short descriptive paragraph about this night. At the bottom, a small text line says "Starts 6:30 PM · Outdoor warehouse rooftop". The "Night Two — Midnight Crossings" card uses a photograph of a dimly lit, intimate interior warehouse space with exposed brick, a small stage, and a jazz trio mid‑performance. Warm, amber light highlights the saxophonist, with audience silhouettes in the foreground. The text underneath reads "SATURDAY · JAZZ, SPOKEN WORD, EXPERIMENTAL" and a brief description, with timing like "Doors 7:00 PM · Performances from 8:00 PM–1:00 AM". The "Night Three — River Echoes" card features a soft, moody image of a riverside promenade at twilight, lit by lanterns, with a small string quartet playing under a bridge. Gentle reflections shimmer on the water; the viewpoint is slightly elevated from the riverbank looking diagonally across. The text underneath reads "SUNDAY · FOLK, CHOIRS, COMMUNITY ENSEMBLES", with a note like "Afternoon to sunset · Family‑friendly, open lawn seating".

Transitioning downward, a thin horizontal rule in a broken line style anchors the next section, titled "Program Highlights". This area is laid out like a newspaper contents page. On the left, there is a vertical strip with the words "INSIDE THIS ISSUE" stacked vertically in tiny uppercase sans serif, rotated 90 degrees counterclockwise, hugging the left margin, textured in a faint terracotta. To its right, occupying two-thirds of the width, is a multi‑column list: alternating rows of performers and short blurbs, using a two‑column grid that’s slightly misaligned to feel handcrafted. Each performer line has the artist name in bold serif, a small en dash, then a short descriptor in lighter sans serif: "The Riverside Quartet — site‑specific classical ensemble", "Echo Cartography — ambient soundscapes and live mapping", "Marisol & The Overpasses — indie folk with brass", "Spoken Rivers — youth spoken word collective", etc. To the far right of this section is a narrow column with a stacked list titled "ALSO FEATURING" set in small caps and underlined by a thin line. Beneath it is a list of shorter names, separated by thin dotted lines. The entire section is mostly text, with careful spacing and rhythmic use of italic and bold, resembling a magazine table of contents.

Further down, there is a section with a more visual, human focus, titled "Faces & places of the festival." The title is set slightly overlapping the previous section’s bottom margin, as if printed over the seam of two pages, and to its left is a tiny caption in sans serif: "PHOTO ESSAY". This section uses a three‑image collage arranged asymmetrically. On the left, a large square photo shows a candid black and white shot of volunteers setting up folding chairs in an empty venue, the camera angle slightly high, emphasizing patterns of rows and the gestures of people lifting chairs. In the center, slightly offset upward so its top edge overlaps the header text, is a vertical color photo, slightly desaturated, of a musician’s hands tuning a violin under warm stage lighting, with soft bokeh lights in the background. On the right, a smaller horizontal photo is lifted above a white card containing text; the photo shows a nighttime cityscape with the festival warehouse outlined in string lights, shot from across the street, with subtle reflections on wet pavement hinting at recent rain. Underneath these images, on a white card that looks like a clipped article with a dog‑eared corner, is a block of text: "Every ticket funds transportation, instruments, and mentorship hours for young musicians across the city. Last year, over 300 students attended free workshops funded by this festival." Beneath this, three small caption lines, in tiny italics, label the photographs: "Volunteers before doors open.", "A quiet moment backstage.", "The warehouse from across 7th Street."

Next comes a clean, prominent section focused on the nonprofit mission and donation options, framed like a magazine sidebar. On the left side, taking about 60% width, a soft‑bordered box in a slightly darker cream (#f2e6dd) with subtle texture contains the title "Why we gather." in bold serif, followed by three short paragraphs in serif text that tell the story of the organization: how it started, who it benefits, and how funds are used. Key phrases like "free lessons", "instrument lending library", and "neighborhood rehearsal spaces" are set in bold to stand out. To the right, a narrow vertical panel looks like a torn‑out donation form, complete with a faint perforation pattern along its left edge. This panel has a heading "Support the work." in uppercase sans serif, and below it three tier boxes stacked vertically: each box has a light border and shows an amount and a description, for example: "$30 — funds one student’s workshop materials", "$75 — provides one month of rehearsal space", "$150 — sponsors a neighborhood ensemble for a season". Beneath these is a minimalist input group designed like printed form fields: a thin‑lined rectangle labeled "Custom amount" with a small dollar sign prefix, and below that, a full‑width deep teal button reading "Donate Now". Some microtext under the button, in very small grey sans serif, says: "Horizon Sound Collective is a registered 501(c)(3). All contributions are tax‑deductible as allowed by law."

Further down the page is a section for practical details, styled like a "Know before you go" column in an arts magazine. The header "Before you arrive" appears left‑aligned, with a hairline underline that extends into the margin. The content is divided into three columns: "Venue & Access", "What to Bring", and "COVID & Safety". Each column has its title in small uppercase sans serif and body text in serif. The "Venue & Access" column lists the address "Riverside Warehouse District, 214 Riverfront Ave." and notes that the venue is wheelchair accessible, with accessible restrooms and reserved seating, in short bullet‑like lines separated by em dashes instead of bullets. "What to Bring" suggests blankets, layers for evening chill, and encourages earplugs. "COVID & Safety" mentions that outdoor and indoor areas are well‑ventilated and encourages mask use indoors. The columns are separated by faint vertical lines that fade at the top and bottom, so they don’t feel like strict grid divisions.

Below that, a horizontally centered strip with a subtle grey background and top/bottom padding carries a call‑to‑action for tickets. The text reads "Sliding‑scale tickets from $15. No one turned away." in a mid‑size serif, centered. Beneath it, there is a single full‑width button, slightly narrower than the content column, reading "Reserve your seat" in uppercase sans serif, with reversed white text on terracotta and gently rounded corners. The button appears slightly lifted with a soft drop shadow, and its edges visually break into the white sections above and below, emphasizing hierarchy.

Near the bottom of the page, there is an email signup panel and social links, arranged in a slightly asymmetrical way. On the left, a small black‑and‑white portrait photograph in circular crop shows a community organizer speaking into a microphone at a past event, shot from the side. Next to it, a text block with a headline in serif reads "Stay in tune between festivals." Below is smaller body text: "Get field notes, community stories, and early program announcements in your inbox." Under the copy is a horizontal email field: a long, thin rectangle with the placeholder text "Enter your email" in light grey serif; to its right, overlapping slightly, is a slim deep teal button that says "Subscribe". On the right side of this section, vertically stacked social links are represented as text-only, all in tiny uppercase sans serif, each on its own line: "INSTAGRAM", "BANDCAMP", "NEWSLETTER ARCHIVE". They align flush right, with faint underlines. To their left, very subtle, are small minimalist icons outlined in charcoal (a camera outline, a musical note, an open envelope), drawn in a fine stroke.

The footer is deep charcoal (#111111), spanning full width, and feels like the back page of an arts journal. Typography in the footer is light grey. On the left, the nonprofit’s logo text "HORIZON SOUND COLLECTIVE" reappears in small caps, serif, with a little less tracking than in the header. Beneath it, in small sans serif, are contact details: "General inquiries: hello@horizonsound.org", "Press: press@horizonsound.org". On the right side of the footer, arranged in a clean right‑aligned stack, are three short links: "Nonprofit details", "Press kit", "Volunteer", each with a tiny arrow symbol (">") preceding the text. Along the very bottom edge, spanning the full width, a hairline in muted terracotta separates content from a last line in the smallest sans serif: "© 2025 Horizon Sound Collective · A community‑run nonprofit for listening, learning, and local sound." The entire page maintains a cohesive editorial look: a harmonious interplay of serif and sans serif fonts, a restrained palette of off‑white, charcoal, deep teal, and muted terracotta, and a hierarchical, overlapping layout with intentional asymmetry, as if each section were a carefully composed spread in a printed arts magazine, translated into a still, non‑animated webpage.
