instruction: Planning the generation details for the following website:
input:
Design a vertically scrolling event landing page titled “GRID SUMMIT 2025” that applies rigorous Swiss-style typography, asymmetrical composition, and a restrained two-column structure throughout, with no video, no animation, and no charts. The aesthetic is stark and modern: primary colors (red, black, white, and occasional light gray) with bold sans-serif type, clean grids, generous negative space, and strong horizontal and vertical alignment. The entire page sits on a pure white background. The main typeface looks like Helvetica or Univers: grotesque, neutral, with tight leading and consistent optical spacing. The overall page has a fixed-width central content column (for both main columns) centered in the browser, with wide white margins on the left and right.

At the very top is a narrow horizontal navigation bar aligned to the grid, with a white background and a thin black bottom border line spanning the full width. Inside a centered container, the left side (roughly 60% width) is mostly empty white space except for a small black text label in the upper left reading “GRID LAB”. It’s set in all caps, small size, and letterspaced slightly, aligned strictly to the left margin. On the right side of this bar (roughly 40% width), a horizontal list of simple navigation links appears, right-aligned: “ABOUT”, “SCHEDULE”, “SPEAKERS”, “TICKETS”, “CONTACT”. Each word is in uppercase, black, small-size sans-serif, spaced evenly with generous margin between them. There are no hover or active state descriptions, just static clean text. No logo graphic, only typographic branding.

Directly below, occupying the hero area that nearly fills the first viewport height, the layout becomes a distinctive two-column composition with an asymmetric structure. The left column is narrow, approximately 35% of the width; the right column is wider, 65%. The left column is mostly text and color blocks; the right column is dominated by a single large photograph related to the event. In the left column, at the top, a bold uppercase label in small red text aligned to the left margin reads “INTERNATIONAL TYPOGRAPHY & DESIGN EVENT”. Underneath, there is a large typographic title stacked vertically over three lines: “GRID” on the first line, “SUMMIT” on the second, “2025” on the third. Each word is set in huge black uppercase sans-serif, extremely bold, aligned flush left, with tight line spacing so the words almost touch vertically. The word “GRID” sits partially over a vertical red rectangle that begins just behind the “G” and runs down the left margin, about 150px tall and 20px wide, giving a striking vertical accent. Below the title, a smaller but still prominent block of text, set in medium weight, upper and lowercase, left-aligned, reads: “A three-day event exploring Swiss-style typography, grid systems, and modern editorial design.” This paragraph uses generous leading and is no more than three lines. Below that, a compact detail list appears: on one line in slightly smaller black uppercase text: “APRIL 18–20, 2025 · ZÜRICH, SWITZERLAND”. Below this, another line reads: “LODEK HAUS MOUNTAIN LODGE & DESIGN RETREAT”. These details align vertically with the left edge of the paragraph. At the bottom of the left column is a strong call-to-action bar: a rectangular button filled with solid red, left-aligned, with the label “GET TICKETS” set in white uppercase, bold, centered inside. To the right of this button, separated by a bit of white space, is a text-only link “DOWNLOAD PROGRAM PDF” in black uppercase but lighter weight. Both elements sit on the same baseline.

In the right column of the hero area is a large, static photograph that reaches almost to the top and bottom of the hero section and is aligned to the right edge of the main container. The image is rectangular, taller than it is wide, with its left edge aligning to the margin between the two columns. The photograph is in high-contrast, semi-desaturated style to feel aligned with Swiss aesthetics. The composition shows a mountain lodge in a stark alpine environment, from a slightly elevated, three-quarter perspective. The lodge building has a clean, modernist facade with simple geometric forms, flat roofs, and large glass windows, all rendered in cool tones of gray and white. Behind the lodge, jagged snow-covered peaks rise, fading into a pale, almost white sky. Foreground elements are minimal: a flat, snow-covered plane with faint footprints leading toward the lodge. The lighting is bright but diffuse, with soft shadows. The image has a subtle grain texture to evoke printed posters. A thin black border encloses the photo. In the lower right corner of the photograph, integrated as part of the image, a small black caption in uppercase reads “LODEK HAUS · ALT. 1800M”, aligned to the image’s bottom-right edge.

Below the hero, a clear band of white space about 60px high separates sections. The next main section is the “ABOUT THE EVENT” area, also structured as two columns but with reversed density: more text on the right and a smaller image and typographic elements on the left. A thin horizontal black rule extends from the left margin to about one-third of the container width at the top of this section, acting as a subtle separator. On the left column, at the top, a small uppercase label in black reads “ABOUT”. Under that, in bigger, bold black uppercase text, left-aligned, is “WHY GRID SUMMIT?”. Beneath this heading is a vertical stack of three short phrases, each on its own line, all uppercase but lighter in weight: “FOCUS ON SWISS TYPOGRAPHY”, “IMMERSIVE MOUNTAIN LODGE WORKSHOPS”, “PRINT & DIGITAL GRID SYSTEMS”. Each phrase is separated by thin horizontal hairline rules spanning the width of this left column, echoing a modular grid. Below these phrases, taking up the lower half of the left column, is a small black-and-white photograph, again with a thin black border. This photo shows a close-up of a designer’s hands arranging printed posters and grid layouts on a table. The camera angle is top-down; the posters feature big black type, red rectangles, and grid lines, all slightly out of focus around the edges but crisp in the center, with strong overhead lighting casting soft shadows.

On the right column of the “ABOUT” section, the main body copy appears. A bold, medium-sized sentence in black, sentence case, left-aligned, reads: “GRID SUMMIT 2025 is a curated gathering of designers, typographers, and educators committed to the precision of the grid.” Below it, a paragraph in smaller regular-weight body type in black details the event: “Over three days in a secluded mountain lodge, you will explore the principles of Swiss-style typography through lectures, hands-on workshops, and studio critiques. From modular grids for editorial layouts to responsive systems for digital interfaces, GRID SUMMIT is designed as a focused retreat for practitioners who value structure, clarity, and space.” Another paragraph follows, slightly separated by white space: “Each session is deliberately capped to maintain an intimate, studio-like atmosphere. In between sessions, you’ll share meals, snow walks, and informal critiques with peers, creating a temporary community built around the love of rational design.” The right column text aligns with the grid so its left edge matches the left edge of the hero title above, and its right edge lines up with the photograph from the hero section.

Further down, after a generous vertical space, is the “PROGRAM HIGHLIGHTS” section, also a two-column layout but with a stronger visual hierarchy. The top of the section features a large, left-aligned uppercase heading “PROGRAM” in black, with a smaller red uppercase subtitle below it reading “KEY SESSIONS & TRACKS”, slightly indented. Across the full width below this heading, a subtle light gray horizontal band spans the background behind only the upper part of this section, creating a soft block that holds the content cards. Inside this band, the content is arranged in an irregular grid that still respects the underlying two-column structure. On the left column, stacked, there are two session cards, each consisting of a block of text with a colored bar above. The first card’s bar is a horizontal red rectangle about 6px tall spanning the card’s width, with a label above it: “DAY 1 — THEORY” in small uppercase black text. Below the red bar, in bold uppercase is the session title “THE GRID AS LANGUAGE”. Under this, smaller text reads: “Lecture · Led by Prof. Lena Roth · 10:00–12:00”. A short paragraph in body size explains: “A deep dive into the history of Swiss-style typography, the emergence of grid systems in post-war Europe, and how they shape visual communication today.” The second card below it is similar but uses a black bar instead of red, and its label reads “DAY 1 — PRACTICE”. The title is “MODULAR TYPOGRAPHIC SYSTEMS”, with the description: “Hands-on workshop · Learn to construct modular grid systems for posters, editorial spreads, and responsive web layouts using real project briefs.”

On the right column of the “PROGRAM” section, two additional cards fill the space, arranged so one taller card aligns with the top of the left column’s cards, and one slightly narrower card aligns underneath but offset to create asymmetric white space. The top right card has a red bar with the label “DAY 2 — ENVIRONMENT”. The title reads “MOUNTAIN LODGE CRIT STUDIO”. The description: “Critique session · Present your grid-based compositions in the lodge’s communal hall, surrounded by alpine views and structured feedback.” The lower right card uses a black bar with the label “DAY 3 — OUTPUT”. Title: “PRINT, DIGITAL, AND BEYOND”. Description: “Panel discussion · Explore how Swiss-style principles translate into contemporary digital products, interactive systems, and variable type.” Each card’s text is left-aligned and there are clear, consistent margins around every card; the cards themselves have no borders, just the colored bars and the light gray band behind them.

Continuing downward, the next section focuses on “SPEAKERS”, returning to a more classic two-column typographic layout with strong grid cues. The section background is white again. At the top left of this section is the label “SPEAKERS” in black uppercase. Below it, in larger bold uppercase, is “GUEST FACULTY”. The left column holds the speaker names and roles as a vertical list, while the right column shows a single composite image. On the left column, there are three speaker entries, each separated by generous white space and thin horizontal rules. Each entry uses a modular typographic hierarchy: The name in large black uppercase, e.g., “LENA ROTH”. Below, a smaller uppercase line states the role and affiliation: “Professor of Typography, Zürich School of Design”. Below that, a short two-line description in sentence case: “Known for her rigorous explorations of grid-based editorial systems and her sharp critique of visual noise.” The second entry: name “MARCOS GIMENEZ”; role line: “Independent Art Director, Basel”; description: “Bringing real-world agency experience, Marcos bridges strict typographic systems with bold client work.” The third entry: name “AYUMI TAKEDA”; role line: “Interactive Systems Designer, Tokyo”; description: “Ayumi explores how static grids can inform motionless interfaces and adaptive layouts without sacrificing clarity.” All text is left-aligned to the same vertical axis.

In the right column of the “SPEAKERS” section is a single rectangular photograph, again monochrome or desaturated, with a thin black border, showing an interior of a minimalist conference room at the mountain lodge. The photograph is taken from a perspective near the back of the room, looking toward a long table with designers seated, their backs partially visible, while a large wall at the front displays a pinned collection of black-and-white posters and grid experiments. The room has large windows on the right side with views of snow-covered peaks and pine trees. The light coming from the windows is bright but cool, casting linear shadows with clear edges on the wood floor, reinforcing the grid motif. The overall tone is precise and calm.

Below this, a distinct “SCHEDULE SNAPSHOT” typographic block appears, spanning the full width but still organized into two vertical segments that hint at two columns. The heading, aligned left, reads “SCHEDULE SNAPSHOT” in uppercase black. Immediately beneath it, three days of the event are listed in a pseudo-table layout, but still purely typographic, no box outlines. The left segment holds the day labels, the right segment holds brief descriptions. On the left side, vertically stacked, each day appears as a bold uppercase label with red text: “DAY 1”, “DAY 2”, “DAY 3”, each followed by a thin vertical black line extending downwards to guide the eye. On the right side, aligned horizontally with each day, are descriptive lines in smaller black text: next to “DAY 1” it reads “Foundations of Swiss-style typography and grid theory”, next to “DAY 2” it reads “Hands-on workshops, alpine critiques, and evening discussions”, next to “DAY 3” it reads “Final presentations, closing reflections, and print exchanges”. The spacing is precise; the left and right segments align so that these pairs line up neatly, evoking a grid without drawing the grid itself.

Further down is a visually distinct “TICKETS & LODGING” section, which uses a large block of red in the right column to create a bold asymmetry. The section background is white as usual. The left column is text heavy, the right column contains a red pricing table. On the left, the heading “TICKETS” appears in uppercase black at the top, with a smaller uppercase label beneath it in gray reading “LIMITED TO 60 PARTICIPANTS”. Below, a short paragraph explains: “To preserve the intensity and intimacy of GRID SUMMIT, attendance is limited. All passes include full access to sessions, shared lodging at the mountain lodge, and communal meals.” Then, a small subheading “WHAT’S INCLUDED” in uppercase red introduces a compact, vertical text list of inclusions. Each inclusion is on its own line, preceded not by an icon but by a simple square bullet, represented as a small black filled square followed by the text in sentence case, for example: “Curated lectures and workshops over three days”, “Two nights of shared mountain lodge lodging”, “Breakfast, lunch, and dinner at the lodge”, “Printed program and grid workbook”. The bullets align perfectly in a vertical line.

On the right column, a large vertical red rectangle fills most of the column height, creating a strong color block. Inside this red block, ticket information is presented in white text, aligned to the left with generous padding. At the top of the block, the word “PASS” appears in small uppercase white letters. Below it, in very large bold white numerals and uppercase text is the price: “CHF 980”. Under the price, in smaller uppercase white text, is “FULL EVENT ACCESS · LODGING · MEALS”. A thin white horizontal line separates this area from smaller details below. Under the line, there are two rows of text, each row centered within the left padding: the first row: “EARLY REGISTRATION ENDS FEB 15” in uppercase with slightly tighter tracking, the second row: “SECURE YOUR PLACE WITH A 30% DEPOSIT”. At the base of the red block is a white-outlined button (transparent red fill, white stroke) labeled “REQUEST INVOICE” in uppercase white, centered within the button outline.

Next, the “LOCATION” section uses a very minimal approach, again working with two columns but with more white space and a subtle graphic. The background remains white. On the left column is the heading “LOCATION” in uppercase black. Beneath it, in bold uppercase, is “LODEK HAUS MOUNTAIN LODGE”, followed by address lines in smaller black text: “Grindelwald, Canton of Bern, Switzerland”. Below, a short paragraph: “Perched above the valley, LODEK HAUS is a modernist-inspired mountain lodge with expansive views and quiet communal spaces—an ideal context for disciplined typographic work.” On the right column is a simple static map graphic in flat design. The map is a stylized top-view illustration of the region, using only shades of gray for land contours, a light beige for the valley floor, and black lines for roads. A small black square marks the lodge location, with a thin black line pointing to a text label in uppercase: “LODEK HAUS”. The map has no intricate details, just the minimal elements needed to suggest location. The map is encased in a thin black rectangular border, and beneath it, aligned left, a small caption in gray text reads “Map is approximate. Exact directions provided upon registration.”

As the user continues downward, an “ACCESS & PREPARATION” section appears with a vertical text hierarchy but still grounded in a two-column grid. The heading at the top left reads “ACCESS & PREPARATION” in uppercase black. The content is arranged in two side-by-side blocks beneath this: the left block titled “GETTING THERE”, the right block titled “WHAT TO BRING”. Each title is uppercase red. Under “GETTING THERE” are three short sentences in small black text: “Nearest major airport: Zürich (ZRH).”, “Rail connection to Grindelwald, followed by lodge shuttle.”, “Detailed itinerary suggestions provided after ticket confirmation.” Under “WHAT TO BRING”, a similarly structured list reads: “Laptop or sketchbook for working sessions.”, “Warm clothing suitable for alpine conditions.”, “Examples of your past typographic work for critiques.”

Finally, near the bottom, the page presents a “STAY INFORMED” registration area which functions as a simple newsletter or interest form. This section runs across the full width but still respects the central container and soft two-column logic: text on the left, form controls on the right. The heading, aligned left, reads “STAY INFORMED” in uppercase black, with a smaller line under it reading “Join the GRID SUMMIT mailing list for updates on program details and future events.” in sentence case. The right side contains a clean email subscription form. There is a single horizontal row with two elements: an input field and a button. The input field is a white rectangle with a thin black border and no visible label, but inside is a light gray placeholder text reading “Email address”. Next to it is a black rectangular button with white uppercase text “SUBSCRIBE”. Both elements are of equal height and aligned on a single baseline. They float in ample white space with no decorative elements.

At the very bottom of the page sits a understated footer separated from the main content by a thin horizontal black line spanning the full width. The footer background remains white. Inside the footer, the content is arranged in a single row but visually suggestive of two columns. On the left, small uppercase black text reads: “GRID SUMMIT 2025 · Organized by GRID LAB”. Beneath it, in tiny gray text: “All information subject to change. © 2025 GRID LAB.” On the right, aligned to the right edge, are three minimalist text links in uppercase gray: “PRESS”, “PRIVACY POLICY”, “EMAIL”. Each is separated by a small vertical black bar (|). Throughout the entire page, there are no icons, no illustrations beyond the described photographs and map, no drop shadows, and no rounded corners: everything is strictly rectilinear, flat, and sharply defined. The design relies on alignment, white space, and typographic hierarchy, embracing the principles of Swiss-style typography and a calm, grid-conscious two-column layout, with subtle asymmetries arising from different block heights, red accents, and the placement of large image and color fields.

instruction: Planning the generation details for the following website:
input:
Design a single-page portfolio website in a strict Swiss-style typography aesthetic that feels like a hybrid between an independent music festival homepage and an online art gallery, with a strong hierarchical, overlapping layout, clean grids, and occasional asymmetries. The entire page sits on a light warm-gray background (#F5F5F3), with generous margins on all sides and a visible underlying grid implied by alignment and spacing. The primary typeface throughout is a neo-grotesque sans serif similar to Helvetica or Univers, used in various weights (Light, Regular, Bold), with absolutely no decorative fonts. All text is aligned either left or right; there is no centered text except in small captions as a deliberate contrast. Color usage is spare: mostly black (#111111) and very dark charcoal (#222222) type, with accent blocks and lines in a vivid red (#E42323) and deep electric blue (#0041FF). The overall impression is of a clean poster-like composition, with overlapping rectangular blocks of color and images, precise rules and lines, and clear typographic hierarchy.

At the very top, a slim horizontal navigation bar spans the full width, about 80px high, with a transparent background so the page background shows through. It is aligned to the grid, leaving a 60px margin on left and right. On the left, in bold uppercase small caps, is the portfolio logo text: “STUDIO KAE”. “STUDIO” is in regular weight, “KAE” is in bold, both in black, set on a single line, left-aligned. On the right of the nav bar, in a tight row with equal spacing, are four navigation items in uppercase, all in a smaller size and regular weight: “PROJECTS”, “ABOUT”, “EXHIBITIONS”, “CONTACT”. Each item is purely text, no icons, with subtle letterspacing. The active section (“PROJECTS”) is highlighted by a thin red underline (1px) that extends slightly beyond the text at both ends, emphasizing Swiss precision. On hover, conceptually, the other nav items would gain a blue underline; this is visually indicated by a faint blue line just beneath them, visible even in the static design as a narrow, almost ghost-like element.

Below the nav, the hero section occupies the top 60% of the first viewport height and is structured as an asymmetrical two-column grid that overlaps slightly. On the left, occupying roughly 60% width, is a large typographic block. The top-left corner features a small red label in uppercase: “PORTFOLIO”. Directly below, in large, bold, uppercase type that spans multiple lines, is the headline: “SOUND. SPACE. STRUCTURE.” The words are stacked vertically, each on its own line, left-aligned, with tight leading. “SOUND.” appears in black, “SPACE.” in the same size but in the electric blue, and “STRUCTURE.” in black again, creating visual rhythm. The period after each word is emphasized by being slightly oversized. Under this headline, aligned to the left edge, is a short descriptive paragraph in much smaller regular text: “Visual designer and art director exploring the intersection of independent music culture, exhibition design, and graphic systems.” The paragraph is set in a narrow column, with generous line spacing and no indentation, purely left-aligned. Beneath this text is a simple, outlined button made of a thin black rectangular border with inner padding; inside, in uppercase, reads “VIEW SELECTED WORK”. The text of the button is black and the border is black. The button aligns with the left edge of the text. Slightly overlapping the bottom-right corner of this text block is a thin vertical red bar, approximately 4px wide and 80px tall, functioning as a modernist accent.

On the right side of the hero, taking up roughly 40% of the width and extending slightly below the baseline of the left content, is a tall rectangular hero image framed with a thin black border and a subtle drop shadow. The hero image is a photorealistic but slightly desaturated overhead shot of a minimalist indoor stage setup in a white gallery space. The composition shows a rectangular raised platform with two matte black speakers on slender stands at the far left and right, a simple modular synthesizer in the center on a white plinth, and a scattering of low stools arranged semicircularly. The room has white walls and a polished concrete floor with soft daylight entering from unseen windows, creating long diagonal shadows across the floor that echo the page’s grid. On the back wall, we see a grid of monochrome poster designs for an imaginary music festival; each poster uses bold Swiss typography and blocks of red and blue on white. The camera angle is slightly elevated and centered, emphasizing the symmetry of the stage yet allowing some asymmetry in the posters. Overlaid in the top-left corner of this image, as part of the design, is a small semi-transparent white label reading “SELECTED INSTALLATION — 2024” in tiny uppercase letters. The right image slightly overlaps the top margin of the section below, reinforcing the page’s overlapping hierarchy.

Directly under the hero, spanning almost full width but constrained to the grid margins, there is a horizontal info strip that looks like a Swiss poster footer. It has a white background with a very thin top border line in light gray. Inside, the strip is divided into three equal vertical columns of text. In the left column, a small label “ROLE” is in uppercase red, with right below it the text “Art Direction, Visual Systems” in black. The middle column shows “BASED IN” in red, with “Zurich / Berlin” below in black. The right column shows “AVAILABLE FOR” in red, followed by “Festivals, Exhibitions, Visual Identities” in black. All of this text is quite small, creating a dense, information-rich baseline reminiscent of ticket or exhibition metadata.

The “PROJECTS” section follows, occupying the next large viewport. The section title is anchored to the far left, offset slightly from the hero by extra vertical whitespace. In the top-left corner of the section, in small uppercase blue text, is the label “01 — PROJECTS”. Directly under this, in large bold uppercase black text, aligned to the left, is “CURATED WORKS”. A thin black horizontal rule extends from the right of this title across about 40% of the section width, stopping abruptly in white space. The projects themselves are arranged in an overlapping grid of three rows, each row having two main columns but with alternating alignments to introduce asymmetry.

In the first row of projects, on the left is a large square project image. The image is a flat-design, high-resolution digital rendering of a festival poster pinned to a concrete wall. The poster design features a rigid 4-column grid, with alternating blocks of red and blue and white negative space. Black uppercase type lists an imaginary festival: “KLANGFELD 2024 — INDEPENDENT MUSIC & MEDIA FESTIVAL” across the top, with smaller text in strict columns listing artist names and dates. The background in the image is a rough, mid-gray concrete wall with soft, even lighting and minimal shadows. On the right of this image, vertically aligned with its center, is a narrow column of project text. At the top of the column, in uppercase red, reads “KLANGFELD FESTIVAL IDENTITY”. Underneath in bold uppercase black: “Visual identity for a three-day independent music festival exploring spatial sound and urban architecture.” Below this is a short paragraph in smaller regular text describing the project: “Poster system, wayfinding, and stage visuals designed using a strict modular grid and a limited color palette for coherent yet flexible event communication.” At the far right of this column, aligned with the bottom of the paragraph, is a minimal label in uppercase gray: “2023 / Branding, Print, Motion”. A very thin vertical black line separates the image and text columns, extending slightly beyond the top and bottom of the text block, enhancing the sense of structure.

In the second row, the arrangement flips: on the left is the project text, and on the right is the image. The text column begins with a blue uppercase label: “CITY RESONANCE EXHIBITION”. Below it, in bold uppercase black, “Spatial exhibition design mapping soundscapes to typographic installations.” Then a smaller text paragraph: “Immersive gallery layout combining field recordings, projected grids, and modular signage systems to visualize the acoustic signatures of different neighborhoods.” A small gray metadata line at the bottom reads “2022 / Exhibition, Installation”. To the right, the image is a photorealistic interior of a white gallery, shot from eye level, slightly off-center. The gallery features several tall, freestanding rectangular panels arranged in a staggered grid, each panel printed with black-and-white typographic layouts of city street names, overlapped with thin red and blue lines resembling metro maps. Speakers mounted discreetly near each panel suggest that they emit localized ambient sound. The floor is polished concrete, walls are white, and overhead track lighting casts sharp pools of light and crisp shadows that emphasize the geometry.

The third row is a horizontal band featuring two smaller projects side by side with overlapping colored blocks. On the left, framed in a blue rectangular background that extends beyond its content, is a simple project card. At the top, in uppercase white text: “MICRO-LABEL VISUAL SYSTEM”. Beneath, in smaller white text: “Identity and physical packaging for a cassette-only sonic arts label.” Next to this card, overlapping its right edge, is a small black-and-white image: a top-down shot of several cassette tapes neatly arranged in a grid on a white table. Each cassette has a minimalist label with black type, red and blue stripes, and a consistent typographic system. On the right side of the third row is another project card, this one sitting on a red background. In uppercase white at the top: “LIVE POSTER SERIES”. Beneath in smaller white type: “Weekly poster series for improvised music nights, exploring the boundaries of legibility.” Next to this card, slightly tucked under its bottom edge, is a small monochrome image showing a wall covered in a grid of posters, each with different typographic compositions and abstract red/blue shapes, photographed straight-on with flat lighting for a graphic effect.

Below the projects, a clean but visually distinct “ABOUT” section appears. Its top aligns with a horizontal red bar that acts as a section divider across a portion of the width. On the far left, in uppercase blue, is the label “02 — ABOUT”. To the right of this label, but lower, is a two-column layout. The left column contains a black-and-white portrait image: a high-contrast, grainy photograph of the designer shot with a 3/4 profile, looking slightly off-camera, against a plain light-gray background. The portrait is cropped in a tall vertical rectangle, aligned with the grid, with a thin black border. The lighting is soft, with gentle shadows on the face. On top of the bottom portion of this portrait, slightly overlapping the lower edge, is a small white rectangular overlay with black text in uppercase: “KAE MATSUMOTO — VISUAL DESIGNER”. The right column holds the about text. At the top, in bold uppercase, left-aligned: “DESIGNING SYSTEMS FOR LISTENING SPACES.” Below, a longer paragraph in regular text: “Kae Matsumoto is a visual designer and art director focusing on systems-based design for independent music festivals, galleries, and experimental labels. With a background in typography and spatial design, Kae works at the intersection of print, signage, and digital environments to build cohesive visual identities that respond to sound, movement, and architecture.” Below this, separated by extra line spacing, is a second paragraph: “Influenced by Swiss modernism, brutalist architecture, and DIY music culture, Kae’s work emphasizes clear structures, restrained color palettes, and flexible frameworks that can adapt across mediums while maintaining a distinct point of view.” Left of this about text, a very thin vertical light gray line runs from the top of the heading down to the bottom of the section, subtly aligning the portrait and copy.

Below the about text, within the same section, there is a smaller, secondary info band that looks like an index, aligned to the right column only. It has three labeled lines in small uppercase gray text: “SELECTED CLIENTS:”, “DISCIPLINES:”, and “TOOLS:”. Next to each, in black regular text, runs content in a single line: “Independent festivals, artist-run spaces, micro-labels”; “Art direction, typography, print, signage, digital layouts”; “InDesign, Illustrator, Figma, VS Code, Risograph”. These lines are evenly spaced, forming a neat, grid-aligned block.

The “EXHIBITIONS” section comes next and leans into a more poster-like Swiss composition. It starts with a large block of red stretching across about 60% of the width on the left side, offset from the top of the section by white space. Within this red block, at the top in small uppercase white text, is “03 — EXHIBITIONS & FEATURES”. Below, in large uppercase white type, right-aligned inside the red area, appears: “SELECTED SHOWS”. Alongside this red block, aligned to its right edge but starting lower, is a vertical list of exhibitions. Each exhibition entry is laid out as a three-column horizontal strip of text (but appears as a vertical stack because of spacing). The first column is the year in bold black uppercase: “2024”, “2023”, “2022”, etc. The second column is the exhibition name in uppercase black: “CITY RESONANCE, SOLO EXHIBITION”, “KLANGFELD FESTIVAL, VISUAL IDENTITY SHOWCASE”, “SOUNDPRINTS, GROUP EXHIBITION”. The third column, aligned right, is location and venue in smaller uppercase gray, e.g. “ZURICH — KUNSTHALLE”, “BERLIN — RADIAL GALLERY”, “TOKYO — ROOM 404”. Each entry is separated visually by a thin horizontal gray rule that spans the list’s width. To the far right of this whole section, there is a narrow, tall transparent column that contains only a vertical text rotated 90 degrees, running up the page: “EXHIBITIONS / PRESS” in black uppercase, emphasizing the vertical hierarchy.

As the page moves toward its lower half, the “CONTACT” section is designed as a minimal, almost brutalist block that still respects the Swiss aesthetic. The section begins with a small blue label in uppercase: “04 — CONTACT”. This sits above a bold, large text block that reads, in uppercase and left-aligned: “LET’S BUILD YOUR NEXT VISUAL SYSTEM.” Immediately below this, in slightly smaller uppercase but still prominent: “Currently available for collaborations from Q3 2025.” Then a more modest line: “Email” in uppercase red, followed by “studio@kaematsumoto.com” in black, regular weight, same line. Under that, a similar treatment for “Instagram” and “Are.na”, in which the labels are in uppercase gray and the handles/usernames appear in black text: “Instagram / @studio.kae”, “Are.na / kae-matsumoto”. All of this contact information sits on the left side of the section, aligning with the grid margins.

On the right side of the contact section, there is a simple newsletter subscription form that feels like a stripped-down Swiss news flyer. The form is contained in a subtle off-white box (#FAFAF8) with a thin black border. At the top of this box, in uppercase black text: “STUDIO BRIEFING”. Directly below, a short line of regular text: “Occasional updates on new projects, exhibitions, and process notes.” Beneath that, there is a single horizontal text input field, occupying most of the box width. The field has a white background, a thin gray border, and placeholder text in small gray uppercase: “ENTER YOUR EMAIL”. To the right of the input, aligned baseline, is a small button with a solid black background and white uppercase text: “SUBSCRIBE”. The button is strictly rectangular, with sharp corners, no gradients, and aligns to the grid so that the input and button form a precise, straight line across. To emphasize the Swiss grid, a thin horizontal red line runs just below the form box, starting slightly inside its left border and extending past its right border, bleeding into the white background.

The footer is restrained and compact, sitting at the bottom within the same overall margins as the rest of the page. It features a thin top border line in light gray. The left side of the footer contains a small line of uppercase gray text: “© 2025 STUDIO KAE”. On the right, mirroring the nav, is a short vertical stack of small uppercase links aligned right: “INFO”, “CREDITS”, “RSS”. They are spaced tightly and right-aligned to the margin. A very faint, dotted baseline grid pattern (tiny, evenly spaced light gray dots) is visible behind the footer area only, hinting at the underlying grid of the entire site without overwhelming the design.

Throughout the page, all clickable elements are indicated with subtle visual cues using only color and underlines; there are no icons or hover animations, and no video or motion elements appear anywhere. Images are static and crisp. No charts, graphs, or data visualizations are present on the page; the layout relies purely on typographic hierarchy, rectangular blocks of color, and carefully composed imagery to guide the eye. The entire design adheres to a clear, modular grid but allows strategic overlapping of images and color fields, plus occasional asymmetries in alignment, creating a Swiss-style, poster-like portfolio that feels at once disciplined and expressive, perfectly suited for a designer working where independent music culture meets exhibition design.

instruction: Planning the generation details for the following website:
input:
Design a minimal, single-column portfolio-style homepage for a fictional luxury travel experience curator called “Aeris Lane Studio”. The aesthetic is ultra-clean, with a predominately off‑white background, generous whitespace, and subtle, asymmetric composition that still reads mostly as a centered vertical flow. The base background color is a warm, soft ivory (#F7F3EE). All text is in a refined sans-serif font like “Neue Haas Grotesk” or “Inter” with tight letterspacing, and a single accent serif (“Canela” or “Georgia”) used sparingly for headings. The primary text color is a deep charcoal (#232323); accent text is a muted warm gray (#7C7570). Accent lines and borders are very thin (1px) and a soft taupe (#D3C6B6). There are no animations, videos, or charts; all images are static, with a muted, art-directed luxury feel, relying on negative space and carefully balanced asymmetry.

At the very top, a narrow, fixed-height header sits flush with the top edge, spanning full width but constrained content-wise to a centered column of max-width ~900px. The header background is the same ivory as the page, so it feels integrated rather than separate. Inside, left-aligned, is the small logo text “AERIS LANE” in all caps, letterspaced, using the serif typeface in a small size, about 14px, in charcoal. To the far right of the same line, aligned horizontally with the logo, are three minimal navigation links in uppercase sans-serif: “Work”, “About”, “Contact”. These are evenly spaced, each with subtle hover underlines implied by a 1px taupe line beneath when active or hovered; in the static design, show “Work” underlined with a thin taupe rule. The nav text is slightly lighter gray than the logo (#55504B) to keep hierarchy clear. The header has generous vertical padding (about 24px top, 20px bottom) and no box shadow, maintaining the flat minimalism.

Beneath the header, the hero section dominates the top fold with a strong central headline and an asymmetric image composition that still feels largely single-column. The main content column is centered, with about 15% viewport margin on either side. The hero’s left side (about 60% width of the content area) contains text, and the right side (about 40%) hosts a tall, narrow hero image, though both are still within the single-column center band, not touching edges. The main headline is a serif, large and elegant, in charcoal: “Quiet Journeys for Loud Minds.” The text is left-aligned, set around 40–48px, with a line height that lets it breathe. Just below, there is a short subheading in smaller sans-serif, lighter gray: “Portfolio of curated retreats, bespoke itineraries, and spaces designed for stillness.” This subheading is about 16px, with generous spacing beneath. Below the subheading, aligned left, a simple horizontal line (50px long, taupe) separates intro text from the primary call-to-action. Under the line, there are two buttons rendered as minimalist pill-like outlines: The primary button reads “View Selected Journeys” in uppercase sans-serif, charcoal text, with a 1px charcoal border and subtle 6px radius; it has ample horizontal padding. To the right, with a small 12px gap, the secondary button reads “Request Collaboration” in lighter gray, with a thinner taupe outline. These buttons are horizontally aligned but kept narrow, so they don’t dominate the width. The hero image on the right is a static, vertical, rounded-corner photograph-like graphic: a photorealistic, muted-toned image showing a lone figure walking along a curved boardwalk over still, pale turquoise water at dawn. The viewpoint is slightly elevated, looking diagonally across the boardwalk as it arcs toward a small, understated modern villa on stilts in the middle distance. The sky is hazy pastel with soft peach and light blue gradient, and the water reflects the colors with subtle ripples. The figure is small, semi-silhouetted in neutral-toned clothing, emphasizing vastness and calm. The image is framed with generous white margins around it, a thin taupe border, and a faint, soft drop shadow to give a sense of layering against the flat background.

Scrolling down, a slim horizontal divider line runs across the content column at about 60–70% width, centered, in taupe. Below it, a “Profile” section appears, maintaining the single-column layout but with a subtle offset: the heading text is aligned slightly to the left edge of the column, while body text is constrained to a narrower column beneath. The section heading reads “About the Curator” in small uppercase sans-serif, letterspaced, in muted gray. Below that, a slightly larger serif name line: “Aeris Lane, Experience Designer” in charcoal. The body paragraph below is left-aligned in sans-serif, around 15px, with a comfortable line height: “I design restorative travel experiences for entrepreneurs, artists, and leaders who crave depth and quiet. Every journey is a composition of light, texture, and time—crafted to leave room for reflection rather than noise.” This section includes a small, circular portrait image aligned to the left margin of the text block, with the text wrapping gently to its right. The portrait is a desaturated, photorealistic head-and-shoulders shot of a person with ambiguous, gentle features (gender-neutral), short dark hair, and soft lighting from the left. The background of the portrait is a blurred, warm neutral wall with hints of texture. The portrait is subtle and not overly sharp, slightly soft focus to match the luxe aesthetic. Under the paragraph, there is a single inline text link in charcoal labeled “Read full biography →” in small all caps, with a very thin underline.

Next, a “Selected Journeys” portfolio section covers most of the remaining page. It keeps a single main vertical column but introduces slight asymmetry in how each project block is laid out. The section begins with a small uppercase label centered: “SELECTED JOURNEYS”. Below it, a modest serif subheading also centered: “A portfolio of quiet luxury in motion.” Beneath, three project case studies are stacked vertically, each occupying its own “card” area with a generous vertical margin, separated by faint horizontal hairline dividers (taupe lines) that only span about 70% width, centered.

The first project block is titled “01 — Desert Mirage Residency”. The number and title are in a single line: the number in small, light gray sans-serif; the title in slightly larger serif, both left-aligned. Under the title, body copy in sans-serif describes the project in 3–4 lines: “A week-long residency for founders held among shifting dunes and wide horizons. Silent mornings, analog afternoons, and long-table dinners under a sky thick with stars.” Underneath this copy, aligned left, there is a small tag-like line of text in all caps, tiny font: “Location: Al Ula, Saudi Arabia · Focus: Creative Reset”. To the right of this project text, aligned asymmetrically so that it overlaps slightly into the right margin of the content column, is a wide rectangular image with gently rounded corners. The image is a photorealistic composition of a minimalist, open-air lounge area nestled in a desert landscape. The viewpoint is low and slightly off-center, capturing a long, low stone platform with simple cushions in muted sand and clay tones, a woven rug, and a low wooden table with a single ceramic vessel. Beyond the lounge area, towering sandstone cliffs rise in the background, softly lit by golden hour light. Shadows are long and soft; the sky is a gradient from soft gold near the horizon to dusky blue higher up. The overall color palette is warm neutrals, with textures of stone, sand, and fabric clearly visible but not overly busy.

The second project block, “02 — Alpine Clarity Retreat”, has reversed asymmetry: the image appears on the left, with text on the right, yet still aligned within the same central column. The title line reads “02 — Alpine Clarity Retreat” in the same style as the first. The corresponding image is a vertical, slightly taller-than-wide frame of a tranquil mountain spa environment. The vantage point is from inside a minimalist wooden cabin, looking out through a large floor-to-ceiling window. Inside, a single chaise lounge in light grey wool faces outward, with a folded blanket and a small side table holding a glass of water. Outside, the landscape is of snow-dusted alpine peaks and a pine forest shrouded in light fog, with muted blue and grey tones. The window glass catches a faint reflection of the interior, with soft natural light illuminating the scene, no harsh shadows. To the right of this image, the body copy reads: “A three-day winter retreat designed for deep work and deep rest. Morning focus sessions, afternoon thermal baths, and evenings spent by the fire with curated reading.” A tag line below notes: “Location: Engadin Valley, Switzerland · Focus: Deep Work & Recovery”. The layout intentionally leaves more whitespace below the image on the left, with the text block vertically centered relative to it, creating an asymmetric but balanced feeling.

The third project block, “03 — Island Stillness Studio”, returns to text-left, image-right alignment but slightly different proportions: the text block is narrower and more centered, with the image slightly bleeding closer to the right edge of the content column. The image is a horizontal rectangle showing a serene, modern overwater pavilion at dusk in a tropical setting. The camera angle is slightly above water level, looking directly at a pavilion made of light wood and glass, hovering over calm, deep teal water. The pavilion interior glows softly with warm light, revealing minimal furniture: a low platform bed with white linens, a single reading chair, and a large floor cushion. Outside, the water reflects both the pavilion’s warm light and the dusky sky, which transitions from lavender to indigo. A few palm fronds intrude from the top left corner, gently framing the view. There is no visible person; the space itself is the subject. To the left of the image, the description reads: “A floating studio for writers and thinkers, designed as a temporary island exile. Slow mornings, handwritten pages, and an endless horizon framed by floor-to-ceiling glass.” The tag line below: “Location: Nusa Penida, Indonesia · Focus: Solitude & Writing”. Under this third card, a minimalist text-only link centered reads “View complete archive of journeys →” in small uppercase sans-serif, charcoal with a fine underline.

Below the portfolio, a subtle “Services & Approach” section explains the curator’s practice in very minimal typography, without images. The heading is left-aligned: “How I Work” in uppercase sans-serif, followed by a short serif subheading: “Every journey begins with a conversation, not a destination.” Beneath, three short paragraphs are stacked vertically with generous spacing, each preceded by a tiny label in lighter gray like “01 — Listening”, “02 — Designing”, “03 — Stewarding”. Each paragraph is 2–3 lines, in sans-serif. Use phrases like: “We begin with your rhythms, your constraints, and your thresholds for silence.” The layout is strictly single-column here, ensuring calm and legibility.

Near the bottom, an “Inquiry” section acts as a simple contact area. It sits on a slightly darker, but still very light background band, e.g., #F0E7DC, spanning full width but still containing the content within the central 900px column. The section title, centered, reads: “Let’s design your next quiet journey.” Below, a single line of explanatory copy: “Share a few details and I’ll respond with a time for a brief conversation.” Under this, a clean, three-field contact form appears, arranged in a vertical stack. Each field label is in small uppercase sans-serif, charcoal, with ample spacing: “Name”, “Email”, “What are you seeking?” The input fields themselves are full-width, with 1px taupe outlines, 6px corner radius, and lots of internal padding. The “What are you seeking?” field is a multi-line textarea, a bit taller than the others. There is no placeholder text, only the labels. Beneath the fields, a single submit button sits left-aligned (not centered) with the text “Send Inquiry” in uppercase sans-serif. The button is solid charcoal, with white text, and a slightly stronger presence than previous outlined buttons—this is the primary action of the page. To the right of the button, in smaller gray text, a note reads: “Response within 2 business days.”

Finally, the footer is extremely minimal, with a very thin taupe line separating it from the inquiry band. The footer background returns to the main ivory. Within the central column, left-aligned, there is a tiny line of text: “© 2025 Aeris Lane Studio.” In the same line but right-aligned, three understated links appear in small all caps, spaced out: “Email”, “Instagram”, “Print Journal”. All links use the muted gray color, with the faintest of underlines. The entire page should feel like a quiet, printed editorial spread translated to the web—single-column at heart, with gentle asymmetries in image placement, meticulous typography, no animation, no video, and no data visualizations, solely static imagery and text that communicate a luxury, contemplative travel portfolio.

instruction: Planning the generation details for the following website:
input:
Design a minimal, airy landing page for a startup incubator tutorial subscription service called “Seedline Studio”, with a multi-column grid layout that feels structured yet slightly asymmetrical. The overall aesthetic is ultra-clean and modern, with a white background (#FFFFFF) and very light gray accents (#F5F5F5) to subtly define sections. All text uses a single sans-serif font family, described as “a clean, geometric sans-serif similar to Inter or SF Pro”, with clear hierarchy: large headlines in bold, section titles in medium weight, body copy in regular weight. The color palette is restrained: primary accent is a muted forest green (#1F5C4D), secondary accent is a soft sand beige (#E8DCC8), and a tertiary accent is a pale sky blue (#DCEBF7). All images use a restrained, flat-illustration style with thin linework and large areas of negative space; icons are simple, monochrome line icons. The page is centered within a max-width container of about 1200px, with generous padding (80px top and bottom, 40px left and right on large screens), and uses a 12-column grid that becomes 2-column then 1-column on smaller viewports (though responsiveness does not need to be implemented, just visually implied by alignment and scale).

At the top, a clean, fixed-height header spans the full width of the page, about 80px tall, with a white background and a very thin, barely visible bottom border in #EAEAEA. The header content is centered within the main 1200px container. On the left, occupying about 3 columns, is the logo: a simple wordmark “Seedline Studio” in forest green (#1F5C4D), set in medium-weight uppercase letters, with slightly spaced-out tracking. To the left of the text is a small, flat icon: a minimalist sprouting seed drawn with a single continuous line; the seed is an outlined oval and a thin stem with two tiny leaves, all in forest green. On the right side of the header, arranged in a horizontal row, are navigation links: “Curriculum”, “Founders”, “Pricing”, “FAQ”, and a slightly more prominent button-like link “Sign In”. Each normal link is in dark gray (#333333), regular weight, small caps, spaced evenly with 24px gaps. The “Sign In” link is outlined with a thin forest green border and has 8px vertical / 18px horizontal padding, rounded corners (18px radius), and forest green text, but the background remains white. On the far right, a subtle ghost button “Join the waitlist” is rendered with forest green text and a very faint sand beige background, also with pill-shaped corners.

Below the header, the hero section occupies most of the initial viewport height, with a two-column layout a little off-center to create asymmetry. The left column is slightly wider (about 7 of 12 columns); the right column is narrower (about 5 columns) and aligned a bit lower to feel staggered. The left side has a large headline at the top that reads: “Learn how to incubate your startup, one focused lesson at a time.” The headline is in bold, dark gray (#111111), left-aligned, with a large size (about 48px) and leading that leaves ample whitespace. Below it, a short subheading in a softer gray (#555555) reads: “Seedline Studio is a subscription-based library of incubator-style tutorials, playbooks, and founder office hours—designed to turn early ideas into launched products.” This text uses smaller font size (16–18px) with about 1.6 line height. Beneath the subheading, spaced by about 24px, there is a horizontal arrangement of two call-to-action buttons. The primary button on the left says: “Start a 14-day trial” and is filled with forest green (#1F5C4D), white text in semi-bold, and a pill-shaped silhouette with gentle shadows (soft blur, very light opacity, dropping downwards). The secondary button to its right says: “View curriculum” and uses a white background, a thin forest green outline, forest green text, and the same pill shape with no shadow. Under the buttons, in smaller light-gray text (#888888), is a trust line: “No pitch decks. No demo days. Just structured learning for real founders.” This is left-aligned and kept narrow (around 60% of the left column width) to preserve whitespace.

On the right side of the hero section, aligned to the center vertically but beginning just slightly lower than the top of the headline for a relaxed asymmetry, is a card-like illustration depicting the concept of a structured course path. The illustration sits on a soft sand beige rounded rectangle card that casts a faint shadow, giving it depth against the white background. The card is about 80% of the right column width, centered, with 24px padding. Within the card, there is a flat, isometric-style illustration: a top-down view of a modular board composed of overlapping, semi-transparent rectangles and circles in pale greens and blues. Each module resembles a card with a title bar; they are arranged like a branching flow, connected by thin, muted gray lines and small circular nodes indicating progression. Some cards include tiny icon glyphs: a tiny line chart, a chat bubble, and a small clock, all in line art. In the upper-left corner of the illustration card is the label “Your Week 1 Path” in small forest green text. Below the main hero card, aligned to the bottom-right of the hero section, a tiny understated line of gray text reads: “Trusted by solo founders, product teams, and small studios.”

Directly beneath the hero, separated by about 60px of whitespace, is a slim, full-width band with the text “Teams learning with Seedline Studio” centered in light gray (#777777), small size. Below this text, within the same band, is a row of 5 monochrome, very simplified logotype placeholders representing fictional partner companies, each rendered in light gray linework to match the minimal aesthetic: “northloop”, “quiet harbor”, “Featherline”, “Atlas & Co”, and “Crescent Labs”. Each logotype is simply stylized text in a thin sans-serif, with slight variations, and they are spaced evenly in a horizontal line.

The next major section is “What you’ll learn”, aligned in a three-column grid. A subtle horizontal divider, a 1px line in #EDEDED, stretches across the content area before this section. The section title “What you’ll learn” appears in bold, dark gray, left-aligned at the top-left. Under the title is a brief description in smaller gray text: “A structured, incubator-inspired curriculum, broken into focused tracks you can layer over your existing work.” Below this, three equally sized vertical cards are arranged side-by-side with 20–24px gaps. Each card uses a soft off-white background (#FAFAFA), thin border in #F0F0F0, and slightly rounded corners. Each has 24px internal padding and a small monochrome icon at the top, centered horizontally: the first card shows a flat, line-art rocket launching from a simple platform (thin lines, no gradients), the second shows an abstract flowchart icon with three connected rectangles and arrows, and the third shows a minimalist bar chart icon with three ascending bars. Beneath each icon is a card title in medium-weight dark gray, centered. The first card’s title is “Validate your idea”. Under the first title is a short list of three bullet points, left-aligned, small text in gray (#555555): “Design tight experiments”, “Map your early adopters”, “Interview for insight, not approval.” The second card is titled “Design your first product”, with bullets: “Scope a thin, lovable version”, “Capture constraints without stalling”, “Ship a simple prototype in 4 weeks.” The third card is titled “Build a repeatable practice”, with bullets: “Create a weekly founder ritual”, “Measure what actually matters”, “Avoid premature scaling.” Each bullet has a tiny circular bullet marker in pale green, aligned to the left margin.

Below this curriculum section, the layout introduces a more editorial, asymmetrical two-column section titled “How Seedline Studio works”. The overall section uses a light gray background band (#F5F5F5) that spans the full width of the viewport, with the content center-aligned within the 1200px grid. The title is in dark gray, bold, on the left column, with the right column beginning slightly lower (offset by about 40px) to maintain that gentle asymmetry. Under the title, in the left column, a paragraph of body text in gray explains: “Instead of a one-time accelerator sprint, Seedline Studio gives you an always-on library of structured tutorials, weekly founder prompts, and examples from real teams. Dip in whenever you’re ready to make the next decision.” Below this paragraph, a vertical stack of three numbered micro-sections appears as step labels, each line with a muted green number and bold step name. The text lines read: “1. Choose a track that matches where you are.”, “2. Follow short, focused tutorials and worksheets.”, “3. Join live office hours when you need feedback.” The numbers “1.”, “2.”, “3.” are in forest green, slightly larger, while the rest of the text is in gray. In the right column, slightly offset and aligned to the center, there is a vertical split card divided into two stacked rectangles separated by a thin line. The top part of the card has the heading “Self-paced, not self-doubted.” and below it, in smaller font, the copy: “Each track is built from the patterns we’ve seen across early-stage teams, distilled into clear, uncluttered lessons you can actually finish.” The bottom part of the card carries the heading “For individuals and small teams.” with body text: “Share a single subscription with up to 5 collaborators, keep notes in one place, and return to the same frameworks as your product grows.” This card has a white background with a soft shadow, and in the top right corner of the upper half is a small, circular line-art icon of two overlapping chat bubbles.

Following this, a testimonials section uses an off-center grid. The section heading, left-aligned, says: “Founders who learned in the margins.” in dark gray. Underneath, occupying roughly two-thirds of the width and aligned to the left, is a large quote box with a thin forest green border. Inside, set in a slightly larger, italicized type, the text reads: “Seedline felt more like a quiet, reliable studio than another high-pressure program. Each lesson was small enough to finish before the next fire drill.” Below the quote, the name is in bold: “— Maya Ortiz, co-founder at Quiet Harbor”, with the company name in lighter gray. To the right of this main testimonial, occupying the remaining third of the grid, are two much smaller, stacked quote cards, each with a light gray border and no fill, slightly overlapping vertically. The top small card reads: “We replaced our endless ‘strategy’ meetings with one hour of Seedline a week. The difference was immediate.” followed by “— Amir, product lead at Atlas & Co”. The bottom small card reads: “The prompts made it easy to discuss scary topics like pricing and positioning without getting stuck.” followed by “— Lina, solo founder”. There are no photos of people; the section stays text-focused and minimal.

Next, a pricing section returns to a centered, multi-column grid, but with another subtle asymmetry: one column is emphasized as the primary plan. At the top center, the title “Choose a simple subscription” is in dark gray. Below it, a single-line description in gray text states: “One plan for individuals, one for small teams. Cancel anytime.” Beneath this, two vertical pricing cards sit side-by-side with a 40px gap. The left card is narrower and de-emphasized slightly; it is titled “Solo” in small uppercase gray text. Beneath the title, the price is shown in large, bold forest green: “$19 / month”. Below the price, in smaller text: “For individual founders learning at their own pace.” Under that, a short list of benefits in bullet form: “Access to all tutorials”, “Weekly founder prompts”, “Archive of recorded office hours.” A slim, outlined button at the bottom says “Start trial” in forest green text. The right card, slightly wider and with a thicker border in forest green, is labeled “Studio (up to 5 seats)” with the price “$49 / month” in bold forest green. Below: “For small product teams and studios.” Benefits listed: “Everything in Solo”, “Shared workspace & notes”, “Priority office hour slots”, “Early access to new tracks.” At the bottom of this card, a filled forest green button reads “Start team trial” in white. Both cards have rounded corners and generous internal spacing, maintaining a sense of calm and clarity.

After pricing, a subtle FAQ section spans full width but keeps the content narrow. The section title “Questions, before you commit?” sits left-aligned in dark gray. Below are four question-answer blocks, arranged in two columns on larger screens. Each block is represented as a minimal accordion, but shown in “open” state for this static design: the question is a line in medium-weight gray, with a tiny plus/minus icon to its left (rendered as a simple line icon in #CCCCCC). The answers are smaller, lighter gray text beneath. Example questions and answers: “How is this different from an accelerator?” followed by “There’s no equity, no mandatory schedule, and no demo day. Seedline works alongside your existing commitments.” Another: “Can I switch plans later?” with answer: “Yes. You can upgrade or downgrade between Solo and Studio at any time.” Another: “Do you cover fundraising?” with answer mentioning: “We include perspective and frameworks, but we focus on building durable products first, fundraising second.” And one more: “Can my team share one login?” with answer: “Studio plans are designed for shared access, with separate profiles under one subscription.” The questions are vertically spaced with ample breathing room.

The final call-to-action band near the bottom uses a contrasting background: a very pale forest green tint (#F1F7F5) spanning the full width. Centered within, the text reads: “Build your own incubator, without leaving your work.” in bold, slightly larger font, dark gray. Below that, a single line in softer gray: “Start a focused, two-week trial. No calls, no cohorts—just clarity.” Underneath, centered, the main button in forest green says: “Start a 14-day trial”. Beneath the button, in very small, light gray text, is: “No credit card required.” The section has generous top and bottom padding so it feels like a concluding panel.

The footer is minimal and low-contrast, with a white background and a thin top border in #EAEAEA. Inside the 1200px container, the left side shows the logo icon (the sprouting seed) in a tiny form followed by the word “Seedline Studio” in small, muted gray text. On the right side, a horizontally arranged set of tiny links in gray: “Email us”, “Privacy”, “Terms”, “Status”, each separated by a simple dot “·”. Underneath, right-aligned, there is one last line in very small, lighter gray text: “© 2025 Seedline Studio. All rights reserved.” Throughout the page, whitespace is generous; elements have consistent vertical spacing (36–48px) and no unnecessary lines or decorations. There are no videos, no animated elements, and no charts or data visualizations anywhere; the entire design leans on clean typography, structured grids, and a few restrained flat illustrations to communicate the quiet, methodical feel of a tutorial subscription designed for founders.

instruction: Planning the generation details for the following website:
input: Design a cozy, hand-crafted single-column dashboard homepage for an eco-living tutorial platform called “GreenNest Studio,” with an organic, kid-friendly aesthetic that feels like a blend of a scrapbook and a nature journal, intentionally avoiding any videos, animations, or charts. The entire page sits on a warm, off-white paper-textured background with very subtle visible fibers and faint, irregular speckles, like high-quality recycled paper. Around the entire browser window, envision a generous outer margin that reveals this paper texture, with the main content column centered but slightly drifting a bit to the right (creating a gentle asymmetry). The primary color palette combines muted forest greens (#3A5A40), moss greens (#6B8F71), clay orange (#C97A40), and mustard yellow (#D9A441), with occasional accents of sky blue (#7DB7D5). All typography feels hand-made: headings use a bold, slightly uneven handwritten display font that looks like thick marker strokes, while body text uses a clean but rounded sans-serif with soft edges, for example similar to “Nunito,” in a dark charcoal (#303030). Text alignment is mostly left-aligned, but section titles sometimes float centered over torn-paper style banners to keep the organic feel. At the top, there is a full-width but not full-bleed header bar that appears as a torn strip of slightly darker recycled paper, stretching horizontally across the page but with wavy, irregular top and bottom edges, as if ripped by hand. On this strip, aligned to the left, the logo “GreenNest Studio” appears in dark forest green, written in the marker-style font. To the left of the text, there is a small illustrated logo icon: a hand-drawn bird’s nest made of intertwined brown twigs, cradling three small leaves instead of eggs in different shades of green, drawn in a flat, doodle-like style with slightly sketchy outlines. On the right side of the header strip, spaced evenly but not rigidly, are navigation links written in all caps in a smaller version of the marker font: “DASHBOARD,” “LESSONS,” “PROJECTS,” “COMMUNITY,” and “PROFILE.” These links are separated by small hand-drawn dot icons in clay orange that look like tiny stamped circles, slightly irregular in shape. There is no hover or animation described, but visually the active page (“DASHBOARD”) appears underlined with a rough, dry-brush green stroke. Directly beneath the header, still within the main column, appears a hero section that feels like a pinned note on a bulletin board. The hero background is a slightly larger rectangle of pale pastel green paper with corners clipped at different angles, giving the appearance of a hand-cut card, overlapping the page’s base background. This card has a faint shadow to imply it’s taped onto the page. At its top left and top right corners, there are small illustrated strips of washi tape: one is a strip of light beige tape with a repeating tiny leaf pattern, and the other is a muted mustard tape with diagonal stripes. Inside this hero card, on the left two-thirds, the main heading reads: “Welcome back, Willow!” in large, charcoal, hand-drawn display font, with each word slightly staggered vertically, creating a playful, uneven baseline. Below it, a subheading in smaller, rounded sans-serif says: “Let’s grow today’s eco-habits, one tiny step at a time.” in medium-dark green, sentence case. Under the text, there is a pill-shaped button that looks like a cut-out sticker: the button background is clay orange with slightly irregular edges; centered on it in white, all caps rounded sans-serif is the text “CONTINUE TODAY’S JOURNEY.” The button has no shine, but a thin hand-drawn outline in a darker orange gives it dimension. On the right third of this hero card, there is a static illustrated image in a flat, hand-painted style: a cozy kids’ reading nook made of a blanket tent set up indoors. The tent is created from a light-cream sheet draped over two wooden chairs (only the tops of the chair backs visible), with a string of small leaf-shaped cutouts hanging across the tent opening. Under the tent, two children are seated cross-legged on a round, woven jute rug. One child has dark curly hair and wears a moss green hoodie and clay orange pants, holding an illustrated book with a cover that shows a smiling earth with little arms. The other child has straight hair in a ponytail, wearing a mustard yellow sweater with a tiny leaf pattern and blue jeans, holding a pencil and a small notebook. Surrounding them are a few potted plants: a tall leafy plant in a terracotta pot on the left, and a smaller succulent on a wooden crate on the right. The background of this image is a softly painted indoor scene with warm off-white walls, a window with diffuse daylight, and a simple wooden shelf with a few books and another plant. The illustration is static, with soft shadows and warm lighting that gives a calm, homely feel. Beneath the hero section, the single-column layout continues, but with subtle staggered alignments and decorative borders. Next comes a “Today’s Seedlings” section, representing the user’s current eco-living tasks and lessons. A hand-drawn, horizontal, dark green line with tiny leaves sprouting at intervals acts as a divider between hero and this section. Centered over this line is a small label that looks like a cream paper tag with slightly rounded corners, “Today’s Seedlings” written on it in lowercase marker font, dark green, with a tiny doodled seed icon to the left (a simple brown teardrop shape with a tiny green sprout coming out). The main content of this section sits in a vertically stacked set of three rounded cards, each card representing a specific task, but to maintain organic asymmetry, each card is slightly offset horizontally: the first card is indented slightly to the right, the second slightly to the left, the third centered. Each card uses a subtle watercolor wash background: one in light moss green, one in pale blue, one in soft clay beige, with visible watercolor edge textures. At the top of each card, aligned to the left, is the task title in charcoal sans-serif bold: for example, “Plant-Based Lunch Challenge,” “Plastic-Free Bathroom Check,” and “Mini Compost Adventure.” Beneath each title, a short description in smaller text reads, for instance, “Swap one ingredient in your lunch for a plant-powered option today.” Each card also contains a simple, static hand-drawn icon on the right side: for the lunch challenge card, a sketch of a lunchbox with a leafy salad inside; for the plastic-free bathroom card, a bar of soap tied with a small string and a wooden toothbrush; for the compost card, a small brown compost bin with a cute smiling face and little veggie scraps drawn peeking out. At the bottom of each card, a horizontal progress indicator is displayed, but instead of a bar, it’s a row of three small illustrated seeds that transition into sprouts: seeds are filled with brown, half-sprouted seeds have a tiny green shoot, and full sprouts have two leaves. For example, a task that is two-thirds complete shows two sprouts and one seed. Next to these, aligned right, is a small pill label in soft green with the text “START,” “RESUME,” or “REVIEW” in all caps white. Continuing down, the next major section is “Your Garden Path,” which shows the user’s learning paths and modules. This section visually appears as a long vertical scroll of overlapping cards resembling pages of a nature journal taped onto the main background. The heading “Your Garden Path” is written in the marker font, centered, on a ribbon-shaped banner of muted mustard paper that curves gently, like a ribbon, with hand-drawn shadow lines underneath. Just below the heading, a brief intro line in smaller charcoal text says: “Pick up where you left off or explore a new eco-adventure.” Then, the content is a series of tall, rectangular cards stacked in a single column, but each card is slightly rotated a degree or two clockwise or counterclockwise for an organic, photo-collage feel, with faint drop shadows. The first card is titled “Water Wise Wonders,” with the title in a dark green brush-like font. At the top of this card there is a static image spanning the card width: a flat, illustrative scene of a child filling a metal watering can from a rain barrel in a backyard garden. The child stands in three-quarter view, wearing blue overalls and yellow boots, the rain barrel is deep teal with a wooden texture, and the garden around them is lush with tall sunflowers, leafy greens, and a low wooden fence. The sky is a soft gradient from pale yellow at the horizon to light blue at the top. Below the image is a short text block in sans-serif: “Learn how to collect, reuse, and respect water through simple daily rituals.” At the bottom of the card is a subtle “VIEW MODULE” text in green all caps on the right, with a small arrow hand-drawn to the right of the text. A small hand-drawn doodle of water droplets is scribbled near the bottom left corner, overlapping the edge of the card onto the page background. The second card similarly represents another path, “Tiny Trash Transformers,” with an image of kids sorting recycling in a bright kitchen, their faces partially turned away so the focus is on the action, and bins labeled with simple icons: a bottle, a paper sheet, and a leafy compost icon. The third card is “Nature Art Lab,” featuring a top-down illustration of a table filled with leaves, twigs, and colorful crayons arranged in a radial pattern, with small scissors and glue. Each card’s look and content are similar in hierarchy but distinct in color accent and imagery, all static illustrations, no motion. Below “Your Garden Path,” the column continues with a section called “Family Projects.” This section is slightly narrower than the previous ones, with more white space on left and right to create a feeling of intimacy. The label “Family Projects” appears left-aligned in marker font in dark green, with a tiny doodled house with a leaf roof as an icon to the left. Underneath, there are three project “tiles” presented vertically, each with a slightly different shape and width for a casual collage look. The first tile is a horizontal, wider card with a light beige background and a torn bottom edge. It contains the title “Weekend Balcony Garden” in clay orange, and beneath that three short bullet-like lines (not actual bullets, but tiny green leaf icons preceding the text) describing the project, for example: “Turn a tiny balcony into a blooming herb corner,” “Use upcycled containers for pots,” and “Invite kids to design plant labels.” On the right side of this tile is a static illustration of a small balcony railing with three mismatched pots—one tin can, one glass jar, one ceramic pot—each with a different herb, drawn in a flat style with subtle shading. The second tile is narrower and taller, centered, titled “Homemade Eco-Storybook.” It shows a vertical stack of handmade booklets tied with twine, and the description encourages kids to craft a story about a tree in their neighborhood. The third tile, slightly offset to the left, is titled “Zero-Waste Snack Picnic,” with small doodles of fruit, jars, and cloth napkins decorating its border. Each tile has soft pencil-like outlines and looks as if it was cut out and pasted onto the page. Continuing downward, there is a “Community Corner” section that feels like a corkboard pinned to the wall. A large rectangle spanning almost the full column width is filled with a cork texture illustration: warm tan color dotted with darker brown, with subtle irregular pores. Over this corkboard, several pinned notes are arranged in a loose grid but not perfectly aligned. Each note is a small card of varying pastel colors—light green, pale yellow, soft blue—and each one has a tiny pushpin illustration at its top: red, green, blue, each with a tiny shadow. The section heading “Community Corner” appears at the top left corner of the corkboard in white chalk-like text on a small dark green rectangle that mimics a mini chalkboard, with slightly fuzzy edges and faint chalk smudges. On the pinned notes, there is short, readable text representing community updates and discussions. For example, one note might be pale yellow with the title “Neighborhood Seed Swap” in a small marker font, with a snippet below: “Mira shared how her family started a weekly seed swap under the old oak tree.” Another light blue note might be titled “Rainy Day Craft Wins” and read: “Show & tell: upcycled lanterns made from jars and paper stars.” On one soft green note, a parent-friendly post titled “Screen-Free Sunday Ideas” lists three keywords separated by commas: “Story hikes, backyard concerts, nature bingo.” In the bottom-right corner of the corkboard, slightly overlapping the edge, there is a small circular sticker-like button in mustard yellow with the text “OPEN COMMUNITY” in uppercase green sans-serif. This button has a rough white outline as if it was cut from a sticker sheet. Beneath the corkboard, the page continues with a “For Grown-Ups” stripe, aimed at parents or teachers using the platform. This section is a horizontally centered strip of light clay beige spanning the width of the content column, with edges that look like they’ve been brushed with watercolor so that they fade into the paper background.
