On the left side of this strip, the text “For Grown-Ups” appears in charcoal in a slightly more formal sans-serif, with a smaller line below: “Guides, tips, and progress notes just for you.” On the right side, there are two small, side-by-side cards (still within the overall single-column alignment, but forming a small 2-column arrangement only inside this stripe). The left mini-card is titled “Classroom Packs” and has a simple icon of a stack of papers tied with a sprig of leaves. The right mini-card, “Progress Journal,” has a static icon of an open notebook with small star doodles on the page. Both mini-cards are rectangular with rounded corners and share a subtle shadow. Further down, the dashboard ends with a calm footer that reinforces the hand-crafted theme. The footer background is a deeper moss green, taking up a moderate vertical height but not overwhelming. On this dark green, in white rounded sans-serif, aligned left, is the logo “GreenNest Studio” again, smaller. Just above the logo, a delicate line drawing in a slightly lighter green runs horizontally: it depicts a continuous whimsical line that forms small houses, hills, trees, and nests, as if doodled by a kid. On the right side of the footer, in two neat lines, are small links with simple labels: “About,” “Help Center,” “Privacy,” and “Terms.” These are spaced with small vertical line separators, each link in a lighter, muted mint. Below these, right-aligned, there is a final line of text in small white letters: “Made with care for curious families.” The overall layout feels predominantly single-column, with everything flowing vertically and centered, but with occasional slight horizontal offsets, angled cards, and small internal 2-column groupings to create an organic, hand-designed scrapbook feel. All dividers are hand-drawn lines or leaf-vine motifs rather than rigid rules. No element is animated; every image is a static illustration, every button is simply a colored shape with hand-drawn edges, and there are no videos, clips, or dynamic charts anywhere on the page, preserving the calm, crafted dashboard atmosphere.

instruction: Planning the generation details for the following website:
input:
Design a static homepage for a contemporary Swiss-style tattoo studio called “NEUE LINE STUDIO,” with a strong International Typographic Style influence, rigorous grid, generous white space, and bold, asymmetric layout. The entire page uses a 12-column grid with consistent gutters and a visible underlying rhythm; show this rhythm subtly through alignment and spacing rather than visible grid lines. The dominant colors are pure white (#FFFFFF) for the background, deep black (#000000) for main text and lines, and a single accent color—a vivid vermilion red (#E53935)—used sparingly for highlights, small labels, and thin rules. The typefaces follow Swiss design tradition: all text is set in a clean grotesk sans-serif similar to Helvetica Neue. Main headings use a bold weight, all caps, with tight tracking; subheadings use medium weight; body text uses regular weight. All text is strictly left-aligned except for a few deliberate right-aligned micro-elements to create tension across the grid. The page height extends vertically with multiple scrollable screenfuls, but everything appears meticulously balanced and minimal, with lots of breathing room.

At the very top, a thin horizontal strip (about 60px high) stretches full-width. On the left, in the first three grid columns, the studio name appears in black uppercase text: “NEUE LINE STUDIO”. It uses a bold sans-serif, slightly condensed, sized large but not overwhelming. On the right, aligned to the last three grid columns, is a compact navigation set in small uppercase letters: “WORK”, “ARTISTS”, “STUDIO”, “BOOKING”, “CONTACT”. Each item is separated by a small vertical black rule. The nav text is black, with a thin vermilion underline that appears on the currently active section only; for the landing page, the underline sits under “WORK”. Directly below the nav bar spans a single, hairline-thin black rule across the entire width, visually anchoring the header.

The hero section occupies the full width under the header and about 70% of the initial viewport height. It adopts a pronounced asymmetrical grid layout. On the left two-thirds (about 8 columns), there is a large, high-contrast black-and-white photograph of a tattoo artist’s hand in mid-linework on a forearm. The style of the image is stark, documentary, almost Bauhaus photography: sharp focus on the machine and the line, shallow depth of field making the background fade into soft blur. The hand is gloved in black nitrile, the tattoo machine is metal with visible texture; the skin surface shows a bold geometric tattoo design in progress (clean straight lines and circles, reminiscent of Swiss design grids). The composition is shot from a slightly elevated angle, with light coming from the upper left, creating crisp highlights on the machine and subtle shadows on the skin. The background is neutral, slightly darker gray, with no distracting details. Overlaid in the bottom left corner of the photo is a small vermilion square label (around 40x40px) with white uppercase text “2025” in a tiny, tightly-tracked font, adding a Swiss poster-like accent. The image edge aligns exactly with grid gutters, with a narrow white margin between it and surrounding text.

On the right third of the hero area (about 4 columns), stacked vertically and aligned to the top, a block of text introduces the studio. At the top, in vermilion uppercase microtext, it says “ZÜRICH · CUSTOM TATTOO PRACTICE” set in very small size, spaced out. Below, a powerful headline in large, bold, black, all-caps letters, set flush-left: “PRECISION LINES.” Under that, on the next line, same style: “PERMANENT STORIES.” The two-line headline is broken so that “PRECISION LINES.” sits slightly to the left and “PERMANENT STORIES.” slightly indented, creating a staggered effect that still adheres to the grid. Beneath the headline, there’s a short, three-line paragraph of body text in regular weight: “NEUE LINE STUDIO is a Swiss-inspired tattoo atelier focused on clean geometry, considered composition, and calm, hygienic process. We design every piece like a poster: intentional, balanced, and built to last.” This paragraph uses a comfortable leading and aligns with the headline’s left edge. Below this, two horizontally aligned buttons span most of the column width but are visually minimalist. The primary button is a rectangular outlined button with a very thin black border, white background, and black uppercase label: “BOOK A SESSION”. The secondary button to its right, separated by a small fixed gap, has a black background with white uppercase text: “VIEW PORTFOLIO”. Both buttons have sharp corners, no gradients, and subtle drop shadows are avoided; instead, they rely on solid color blocks for hierarchy.

Running vertically along the far right edge of the hero section, aligned with the outer grid, there is a narrow, vertical rotating label effect but rendered static: black uppercase text rotated 90 degrees clockwise near the edge reading “SWISS TYPOGRAPHY · FINE LINE TATTOO”. It sits in a tiny font size, acting as a graphic element more than content.

Below the hero, a clear section break is defined by a thick (3px) horizontal black rule that stops at the 10th column, leaving open white space on the far right. Under this begins the “Featured Work” section. A small vermilion label in uppercase, at the left margin, reads “FEATURED WORK”. Beneath it, a mid-size subheading in black bold uppercase reads “SELECTED PIECES”. The section uses a three-column grid of images with caption blocks beneath each, running across the page with large white margins on either side. Each featured piece is a static image with a distinct style but unified in black-and-white photographic treatment, high contrast, and minimalist composition.

The first image, aligned with columns 1–4, shows a forearm tattoo in close-up, shot from a side angle. The tattoo is a minimal grid of thin black lines intersecting at right angles with small red circles at a few intersections; the red here matches the vermilion accent. The arm rests on a neutral gray surface; the background is softly blurred studio equipment. The lighting is soft and even, creating delicate shadows on the skin texture but preserving the crispness of tattoo lines. The second image, in columns 5–8, depicts a shoulder blade tattoo: a large, abstract arrangement of concentric circles and radiating lines, reminiscent of a Swiss poster diagram, shot from a slightly elevated rear angle. The subject’s back is turned, the tattoo forms the focal point, with the rest of the scene fading into a clean, light gray backdrop. The third image, in columns 9–12, shows a wrist and hand tattoo of a minimal typographic word set in all caps, aligned along the tendon; the typography matches the site’s font and reads “LINES”. The photo is taken from above, with the hand resting on a white surface, sharp shadows projecting diagonally, giving a graphic, poster-like feel.

Beneath each image is a caption area. The title line uses uppercase bold black text, e.g., “GRID ARM · 2024”, “ORBIT BACK · 2023”, “TYPE WRIST · 2024”, each with a thin vermilion rule to the left of the text acting as an accent bar. Below each title is a smaller, regular-weight line of copy describing the piece, such as: “Macro lines tailored to the wearer’s arm span, plotted on a custom grid.” The captions are left-aligned and occupy the same column width as each image, with exact vertical rhythm maintained between each column.

Below this gallery, the layout switches to a more asymmetrical artist spotlight section titled “ARTISTS”. On the left half (columns 1–6), there is a vertical block of text; on the right half (columns 7–12), portraits of artists are arranged in a modular grid. The section label “ARTISTS” appears in tiny vermilion uppercase above a bold black heading: “THE HANDS BEHIND THE LINE.” Below, a concise paragraph explains: “Our artists combine Swiss graphic sensibility with years of tattooing experience. Every composition is sketched, proofed, and refined on-grid before it meets your skin.” Beneath that, a list of three artists is set in a strict typographic system; each artist is presented as a three-line block separated by thin hairline rules. For example: line one (artist name, uppercase bold): “MIRA LÖWEN · LINEWORK”; line two (small uppercase): “SPECIALTY: FINE GEOMETRY, NEGATIVE SPACE”; line three (body text): “Known for precise micro-line patterns and quiet, meditative sessions.” The three artist blocks are stacked with generous spacing and aligned to the grid.

On the right side, three portrait images are arranged in a vertical column with offset placements that still adhere to the grid. Each portrait is a black-and-white, high-contrast photograph, waist-up, taken against a plain, light-gray wall. The subjects are slightly off-center, facing the camera with a neutral expression. The lighting is even but directional, casting faint shadows. The first portrait (Mira) occupies columns 7–12 at the top; below it, slight white space; then the second portrait (another artist, “JONAS REY · DOTWORK”) occupies columns 7–10 only, leaving white space on the right; the third portrait (third artist, “LIA KAMM · ABSTRACT”) occupies columns 9–12, creating a gentle diagonal visual progression while still respecting the invisible grid. Each portrait has a tiny black label in the bottom-left corner with the artist’s name in small uppercase text, reversed out on a translucent white rectangle so that it stays legible over any background.

Next comes the “Studio” section, focused on the environment and ethos. It spans full width but uses a strong horizontal division: on the left (columns 1–7) a large block of text and a simple floor plan graphic; on the right (columns 8–12) a single wide photograph. The section label “STUDIO” appears vertically in small black uppercase letters along the outer left margin, rotated 90 degrees counterclockwise, functioning like a spine. The main heading here reads “A CALM, ORDERED SPACE.” in bold black, all caps, with the period. Beneath is a slightly longer paragraph in regular body text: “We work in a quiet, bright studio near the Limmat, stripped to essentials: clean surfaces, carefully arranged tools, and a focus on clarity. Every session begins with a consultation on form, proportion, and meaning.” Under the paragraph, aligned to the grid, a simple line-based schematic of the studio is presented, as a static image. The schematic is a top-down flat-design map: white background, thin black lines outlining rectangular rooms—a reception, two tattoo bays, a sterilization room, and a small lounge. Each area is labeled in small uppercase black text (“RECEPTION,” “BAY A,” “BAY B,” “STERILIZATION,” “LOUNGE”). A single vermilion square indicates the entrance, labeled “ENTRY”. The style is highly minimal, reminiscent of architectural line drawings, with no shading and no textures, strictly lines and labels.

On the right side of the “Studio” section, a large horizontal photograph shows the interior of the studio in a photorealistic, black-and-white image. It’s shot from a slightly low angle to emphasize the high ceiling and negative space. The composition: white walls, a long white counter with neatly aligned tattoo machines and ink bottles arranged in perfectly straight rows. The chairs and furniture are industrial but minimal—black metal frames, white or light gray upholstery. Large windows let in natural light, casting rectangular patches of light on the polished concrete floor. Everything is spotless and uncluttered, evoking a laboratory aesthetic. A single plant in a white pot adds a soft organic shape without breaking the monochrome strictness. The image is framed so that its edges align precisely with the text and map on the left, maintaining the grid rhythm.

Below, a high-contrast horizontal rule in vermilion separates the content from the booking call-to-action section. This “Booking” section spans full width but is visually compressed into a flat, poster-like band. The background remains white, but a large, left-aligned heading in massive, bold, uppercase letters reads “BOOK YOUR LINE.” The text is so large that the line “BOOK YOUR” occupies one line and “LINE.” sits below, indented slightly. The typography is the centerpiece here. Under this, a narrow subheading in small uppercase black text says: “LIMITED SESSIONS · BY APPOINTMENT ONLY”. On the right side, justified to the last four columns, there is a minimal booking form rendered in a strict, grid-aligned layout. Each field label is uppercase and small, with a thin black rule underneath acting as the input line. The fields stack vertically: “NAME”, “EMAIL”, “PREFERRED DATE”, “DESCRIPTION”. Each input line is a straight, uninterrupted black line across the column width, no rounded corners or boxes. Below the last line, the submit button is a solid black rectangle with white uppercase text: “REQUEST SESSION”. To the left of the form, in small regular text, is a discrete privacy note: “We review every request within 48 hours and respond with available slots and preparation guidelines.” This note is left-aligned and aligned to the grid’s text baseline.

A small “Information Strip” section follows, using a subtle gray (#F5F5F5) background that spans the full width but only 200px in height. In this strip, three pieces of key info are shown in equal-width columns: “STUDIO HOURS”, “LOCATION”, and “CONTACT”. Each has a small uppercase heading in black, with content below in regular weight. For “STUDIO HOURS”, list: “Tue–Fri: 11:00–19:00”, “Sat: 10:00–17:00”, “Sun–Mon: Closed” on separate lines. For “LOCATION”, show: “Neustadtgasse 12”, “8001 Zürich”, “Switzerland”. For “CONTACT”, show: “hello@neueline.studio” and “+41 44 000 00 00”. Thin vertical black rules separate each column. The gray background provides a subtle band that visually supports the footer.

At the very bottom, the footer is minimal and strongly typographic. A thin black rule runs along the top of the footer. Inside, on the left side, there is a small logotype in uppercase: “NEUE LINE STUDIO” in bold, followed by a registered trademark symbol. Under it is a line in tiny uppercase text: “© 2025 NEUE LINE STUDIO · ZÜRICH”. On the right side, aligned to the final columns, are three small uppercase links: “INSTAGRAM”, “POLICY”, “CREDITS”, with ample letterspacing and no icons. There is also a small, right-aligned line of microtext at the bottom, barely larger than a caption, reading: “Site set on a strict 12-column grid · Inspired by Swiss typographic tradition.” Everything in the footer is black on white, no accent color, reinforcing the restrained conclusion to the page.

No videos, animations, transitions, or charts are present anywhere; all images are static, all interactions are imagined as simple hover or click states but not visually described as motion. The overall feeling is of a printed Swiss poster system translated to a responsive digital layout—precise, orderly, quietly dramatic, with every element locked into a grid and every use of color and type carefully rationed.

instruction: Planning the generation details for the following website:
input:
Design a whimsical, research-focused homepage for an imaginary institute called “Cloud Kite Research Lab,” with an overall two-column layout that feels playful but still organized, like an art exhibition meets a science library. The page sits on a soft gradient background that shifts from a pale sky blue at the top to a very light mint green at the bottom, with faint, semi-transparent doodles of kites, question marks, and tiny stars scattered like a subtle pattern. At the very top, a fixed-width central container (around 1200px wide) is centered on the page with generous white margins on both sides; inside this container, all content is arranged mostly in a two-column grid, with occasional full-width sections to break the rhythm.

At the top, spanning the full width of the container, is a wide, airy header bar with a white background, a slight drop shadow, and rounded bottom corners (only on the left and right bottom edges, radius about 16px). On the far left is the lab’s logo: a flat, minimalist icon of a cloud with a smiling face and a tiny kite string trailing down with a geometric kite, drawn in a friendly line-art style with a medium-thick navy stroke; the cloud is white, the kite is a warm coral with small yellow accents, and the smile is a simple curved line. To the right of the logo, the lab name appears as text: “Cloud Kite Research Lab” in a quirky but readable display font reminiscent of hand-drawn lettering, in dark navy, with each word on its own line for a stacked look; “Cloud Kite” is larger than “Research Lab.” Centered in the header, horizontally, is a simple main navigation with each item in small caps: “HOME”, “PROJECTS”, “PEOPLE”, “LIBRARY”, “JOIN US”. The font is a clean sans-serif, medium weight, in a muted dark gray; when hovered (described visually), the menu items gain a rounded pill background in pastel yellow. On the far right of the header, there’s a pill-shaped button reading “Subscribe to Curious Notes” in white text on a coral background, slightly larger than the nav text, with a soft shadow and a tiny line-art icon of an open envelope with a kite flying out of it to the left of the text.

Below the header, the hero section takes up most of the first viewport and respects the two-column feel. On the left, taking roughly 55% of the width, is the main hero text area. At the top of this left block is a small tag-like label that says “A Playful Home for Serious Questions” in all caps, very small, in a rounded rectangle with a pale yellow background and dark navy text. Below this is the primary headline: “We chase questions like kites in the wind.” It’s large, bold, and slightly irregular in letterform, in a dark navy color with subtle letterspacing, left-aligned. Underneath it, a short paragraph of copy in a friendly sans-serif font reads: “Cloud Kite Research Lab blends rigorous inquiry with a childlike sense of wonder. Explore our open notebooks, sketchboard experiments, and collaborative research projects in progress.” The paragraph lines are fairly wide with generous line spacing, making it feel airy. Below the paragraph are two side-by-side buttons spaced with a small gap: the primary button on the left is a wide rounded rectangle in vibrant coral with white text that says “Browse Live Experiments”; the secondary button to the right has a white background, a thin coral border, and coral text reading “Meet the Researchers.” Small icons appear in each button: a simple beaker with a tiny star above it for the first, and a pair of round spectacles for the second, both in thin line art style. Beneath the buttons, there is a small horizontal row of three circular avatar placeholders representing community members: each is a circle with a simple flat illustration of a person’s face in different skin tones, cartoonish style, with subtle shadows and pastel backgrounds, and next to them, a tiny caption in gray says “Trusted by 120+ curious minds”.

On the right side of the hero section, taking about 45% width, is a large, vertically oriented illustration card with a slightly off-white background and a thick border that alternates in color around the edges like a hand-drawn frame (segments of coral, navy, mustard yellow, and teal). Inside this illustration, displayed in a flat, slightly isometric perspective, is a whimsical rooftop scene: three researchers standing on top of a stylized building, each holding a kite string. The kites morph into floating research objects: one kite is shaped like a bar graph (but not a chart on the page, just an abstract shape), another like a text document with scribbles, and another like a paper airplane. The researchers are diverse in appearance, wearing colorful sweaters with simple geometric patterns. The background above them shows a sky transitioning from pale blue near the horizon to a deeper blue at the top, with simple, fluffy white clouds and faint, hand-drawn equations and doodles invisibly drifting in the air. The linework is clean and cartoonish, with bold outlines and flat shading; soft shadows beneath the characters and subtle highlights on the kites give the image depth. Within the illustration, small sticky-note-like shapes are pinned on the side of the building with scribbled symbols, hinting at experiments. The top right of this illustration card has a tiny floating label that overlaps its border slightly, reading “Sketchbook View” in a tiny typewriter-like font.

Beneath the hero, the layout resolves into a more formal two-column section titled “This Week in the Lab,” but it still has playful asymmetry. Across the full width of the container, centered and slightly above the columns, is a section heading: “This Week in the Lab” in a friendly serif font, medium-large, in dark navy. Underneath it is a very thin horizontal line, hand-drawn in look, that wiggles slightly instead of being perfectly straight. The left column (about 60% width) contains a stacked series of three feature cards representing current research projects. Each card has a white background with rounded corners and a faint pastel shadow (one in mint, one in lavender, one in pale yellow) to differentiate them. At the top of each card is a small tag: for example, the first card shows a tiny rounded label reading “Cognitive Curiosity” on a dusky purple background, the second “Playful Methods” on mint, and the third “Open Data Culture” on coral. Beneath each tag is a project title in bold, such as “Mapping the Shape of Questions,” “The Science of Productive Tangents,” and “Designing Playful Surveys.” Each card has a short, two-line description in smaller gray text. To the left of each description, inside the card, there is a small square thumbnail illustration: simple flat vector scenes like a stack of colorful question marks forming a mountain, a path that zigzags through doodled ideas, or a magnifying glass over a collage of sticky notes. Each thumbnail has a soft, monochromatic color scheme with a limited palette and simple geometric shapes, and is framed by a thin border in a complementary color.

The right column in this section (40% width) is a vertical “Notice Board” styled like a cork board. Its background is a textured, warm beige with subtle noise to emulate cork. At the top right of this board, a handwritten-style title reads “Notice Board” in dark brown. Pinned or taped onto this background are three overlapping note cards. One is a pastel pink sticky note at a slight angle with tape along the top edge, with handwritten-style text: “Lab Open Hour: Fridays, 3–4pm (Bring your wildest hypothesis)”. Another is a torn-edge white note clipped by a tiny illustrated binder clip at the top, reading: “Call for Collaborators: ‘Playful Surveys in Serious Spaces’ – Click to learn more.” The third note is a pale blue index card with faint lines and small doodled icons of a pencil and cloud, text reading: “New in our Library: ‘The Gentle Guide to Messy Data’”. Each card has a subtle drop shadow and overlapping edges to feel tactile.

Further down, a full-width band breaks the columns, with a background of soft off-white and gentle curved top and bottom edges that look like rolling waves. This section is titled “Our Research Tracks” and acts like an art exhibition wall of “tracks” of work. The title is centered in dark navy, and below it is a subtitle in smaller gray text: “Wander through our tracks like rooms in a curious museum.” Within this band, three "tracks" are arranged in a three-card horizontal layout, each card taking equal width but with slight overlap in vertical positioning so that the middle card sits a bit higher than the others, creating a stagger. Each track card is a tall rectangle with a subtle pastel gradient background (one in pink-to-orange, one in teal-to-blue, one in yellow-to-lime), and a white border. At the top of each track card is a small oval icon area containing a flat illustration: for example, “Track 01 – Questions” has an icon of a big, stylized question mark made of smaller icons, “Track 02 – Methods” has intersecting geometric shapes, and “Track 03 – Sharing” has an open book that emits tiny dots and lines like confetti. Beneath each icon, bold text reads “Track 01: Question Crafting”, “Track 02: Playful Methodologies”, and “Track 03: Open Sharing & Teaching”. Each card has a list of three bullet-like lines (but styled visually as small dots and indented text, not actual HTML bullets, described as: “Micro-studies on curiosity”, “Interview prompts as comics”, “Framing questions as journeys”, etc.). The text is left-aligned, small, and dark gray. At the bottom of each card is a link-style phrase in uppercase, bright coral, e.g., “ENTER THIS TRACK →”.

Below this band, the layout returns to a two-column approach where the left column is narrower and the right column wider, creating a visual asymmetry. On the left, the narrower column hosts a section titled “Meet the People behind the Kites,” with a small cloud icon beside the title. Under the title is a vertical stack of three circular researcher portraits, each portrait an illustrated character in flat, friendly style: different genders, skin tones, hair textures, and accessories (e.g., a person with curly hair and round glasses, another wearing a beanie, another with big headphones). Each circle has a colored ring matching one of the site’s accent colors. Next to each portrait, aligned horizontally, is the person’s name in bold, like “Dr. Mina Sol”, “Prof. Elias Chen”, “Samir Patel (Lab Cartographer)”, with their role in smaller gray text beneath: “Cognitive Play Specialist”, “Ethnographic Methods Lead”, “Community Research Steward”. The right, wider column is a “Lab Journals” preview. It contains two large horizontal cards stacked vertically, each representing a journal entry. Each journal card has a faint, off-white background with a subtle paper texture and a thin dotted border giving it a notebook feel. At the top left of each card, a small label reads “Open Notebook” or “Field Notes” in uppercase. Then a handwritten-style title such as “Why We Let Our Research Wander” or “What Kids Teach Us about Interview Design” in darker, slightly slanted text. Each card includes a two-line excerpt in a regular sans-serif font below, and on the right side of each card is a small, monochrome doodle like a pair of walking feet, a cluster of talk bubbles, or a playful compass. Each card has a bottom-right link reading “Read this journal →” in coral.

Closer to the bottom, there is a horizontal full-width “Join the Lab” call-to-action band that plays with shapes. The background is a gentle diagonal split: the left half is pale mint, the right half pale lavender, with a soft, wavy dividing line. On the left side of this band (aligned with the main container) is a headline: “Join us in the experiment of learning out loud.” in a friendly serif font, medium size, in dark navy, left-aligned. Under it are two short lines of text: “Subscribe to ‘Curious Notes’ for sketches, early drafts, and unfinished thoughts. No spam, just wonder.” Beneath this, a simple email input field appears as a long rounded rectangle with a white background, light gray border, and placeholder text reading “your.email@example.com”. To the right of the input is a matching rounded coral button with white text, “Get Curious Notes.” On the right side of this band is a tall, slim illustration of a giant notebook leaning slightly, with its pages open. Emerging from the notebook pages are tiny illustrated figures climbing up ladders made of dotted lines, holding research tools: a pencil, a magnifying glass, a sticky note stack. The style stays flat and cartoonish, with thin outlines and minimal shading.

Finally, at the bottom, the footer section is clearly defined with a deep navy blue background and white text, spanning the full width of the container but with content kept within the central width. The footer is divided into two main columns. On the left, there is the lab logo again in a smaller size, stacked above a brief mission line: “Cloud Kite Research Lab is a playful research collective exploring curiosity, questions, and collaborative methods in public.” This text is in a small, clean sans-serif, with a softer white tone. Beneath that are three tiny icons, spaced horizontally: a minimalist cloud, a tiny folded paper plane, and a star, each in line art white. On the right, the footer is subdivided into three very narrow columns of links, each with a tiny uppercase heading: “EXPLORE”, “CONNECT”, and “LEGAL”. Under “EXPLORE” appear links: “Projects”, “Library”, “Journals”. Under “CONNECT”: “Newsletter”, “Contact”, “Community Guidelines”. Under “LEGAL”: “Imprint”, “Privacy”, “Cookies (with chocolate chips)”. All links are in white, with an underline shown only on hover (described visually as a hand-drawn squiggle line appearing beneath), and the last link has a playful note: the word “chocolate” is in a slightly warmer off-white color for a subtle emphasis. At the very bottom, centered below everything, is a thin, faint line and a single line of small text: “© 2025 Cloud Kite Research Lab. Built with curiosity and plenty of sticky notes.” The entire page maintains a cohesive, playful research aesthetic, with a consistent two-column structure that occasionally bends into full-width sections and asymmetries, no motion or animation, and no charts or data visualizations anywhere—only static illustrations, text, and tactile, paper-like interface elements.

instruction: Planning the generation details for the following website:
input: Design a single-page landing site for a contemporary language school called “Helvetica Language Studio,” using a strict Swiss-style typography aesthetic, asymmetrically arranged within a mostly single-column flow. The visual tone is minimal, grid-based, and highly structured, with abundant white space, sharp alignment, and vivid, flat blocks of color. The background of the entire page is a clean, matte off-white (#F7F7F7) with no texture. The primary colors are a pure red (#E4002B), a deep black (#000000), and a neutral light gray (#E0E0E0), occasionally contrasted with a muted sky blue (#83B9FF) as an accent. All type is set in Helvetica or a similar neo-grotesk sans-serif; weights vary from thin to bold to create the hierarchy instead of decorative elements. The page uses a visible, modular baseline grid: vertical rhythm is clear, with consistent spacing between sections (roughly one and a half line-heights), and elements snap to an invisible 12-column grid, though the user mostly perceives a single main column down the center-left, with occasional blocks protruding to the right edge to create tension and asymmetry. At the very top, a clean, fixed header bar spans full width with a white background and a single thin black line under it. On the far left, aligned to the grid, the logo is purely typographic: “Helvetica Language Studio” in uppercase, tight tracking, black text; “Helvetica” is bold and larger, “Language Studio” is medium and smaller, stacked vertically with a subtle red vertical bar (a thin rectangle, about the height of the two lines of text) placed to the left of the word “Helvetica” like a Swiss poster accent. On the right side of the header, the navigation is a horizontal list of simple text links in uppercase: “PROGRAMS”, “METHOD”, “SCHEDULE”, “ABOUT”, “CONTACT”. The links are spaced generously with lots of air between them, set in a small, medium-weight type, and when hovered (described visually), the text turns red with a thin underline appearing at a perfect 1px thickness. To the far right, separated by a vertical gray hairline, there is a compact pill-shaped button outlined in black that reads “BOOK A TRIAL” in uppercase, small black text, with no fill by default and subtle rounded corners; on hover, it inverts to a solid black pill with white text. Beneath the header, the hero section is dominated by bold typography on the left and a structured collage-style image on the right, arranged asymmetrically but within grid discipline. The hero background is off-white, with a wide margin left and right. On the left, starting roughly one-third from the top of the viewport, there’s a large block of text in black: the headline is set in all caps, very bold, and aligned flush left, reading: “LEARN LANGUAGES LIKE A DESIGNER.” Each word occupies its own line, with generous leading so the block forms a tall, rectangular typographic structure. Below it, in smaller regular-weight sentence case, is a subheading paragraph that reads: “Helvetica Language Studio brings Swiss precision to language learning. Structured, visually-clear courses in English, German, French, and Japanese — designed for visual thinkers, creatives, and modern professionals.” The subheading text is a narrow width, keeping the column tight, with a ragged-right edge. Beneath the subheading are two horizontally aligned call-to-action buttons, still within the main column width but positioned slightly lower to allow breathing room. The left button is a solid red rectangle with sharp corners, white uppercase text reading “START FREE TRIAL”, and no icon. The right button is an outlined rectangle with a black border, transparent fill, and black text reading “VIEW PROGRAMS”; the negative space between them is roomy, and both have subtle drop shadows (very faint, neutral gray, offset slightly down) to lift them off the page. To the right of the hero text, forming a visual counterweight, is a large image block that extends nearer to the right edge of the screen. This is a static, flat-design illustration resembling a Swiss poster. The composition: a white rectangular canvas with a thin black border placed on a light gray background, slightly offset to create a layered feel. On this canvas, abstract figures of three learners are represented as simplified geometric shapes: rectangles for torsos, circles for heads, and minimal lines for limbs, all in flat colors (black, red, light gray, and sky blue). One figure sits at a rectangular desk with a simple black line drawing of a laptop, another stands pointing at a vertical column of text blocks, and another is seated with a book. Behind them, vertical and horizontal bars of color (red and blue) mimic a grid and suggest speech patterns or timelines. There are also small typographic elements in the illustration: uppercase words in black like “EN”, “DE”, “FR”, “JP” placed inside small rectangular modules, arranged vertically like a language menu. The style is minimal, flat, and sharp-edged, with subtle shadows under the canvas illustration to anchor it. There is no gradient; the lighting is implied only by the consistent direction of shadows cast by the illustration frame. Below the hero, separated by a thin horizontal black rule spanning the main column, the next section highlights the core approach in a mostly single-column flow. The section heading is small and uppercase in red: “OUR METHOD”. Under it, in larger but still moderate-weight black text, is the title: “Structure, clarity, and rhythm — in language and design.” This is left-aligned. Then, a three-step explanation is laid out vertically for a single-column feel, but each step is slightly indented to form a staggered, asymmetric ladder. Each step consists of a bold black number (“01”, “02”, “03”) aligned to an imaginary vertical line, with the text to the right of each number. The step titles are in bold: “Visual Grammar”, “Modular Practice”, “Real Conversations”, each followed by a short sentence in light-weight text: “We break languages into clean visual modules so you see the patterns behind every sentence.”; “Short, repeatable drills built like design grids — focused, finite, and easy to track.”; “Live sessions with native speakers, structured around real projects, not random small talk.” To the right of this textual column, aligned with the middle of the method section, is a tall, narrow static image of a stylized “language grid”: a vertical poster-like graphic showing a grid layout of horizontal bars and text blocks. It uses a white background with black horizontal lines that look like staff lines, and blocks filled with red and blue rectangles, some containing short black text snippets like “SUBJECT”, “VERB”, “OBJECT”, “TIME” in all caps. The composition is front-facing and orthogonal, reminiscent of a diagram pinned straight onto a wall with no perspective distortion. The next section showcases the available programs and appears as a stack of modular cards arranged vertically to preserve the single-column feel but offset slightly to create subtle asymmetry. The heading is red uppercase: “PROGRAMS”, followed by a black subheading: “Pick a language. Keep the grid.” Four “program cards” are stacked in a column; each card is a white rectangle with a thin black border and ample padding, separated by consistent vertical white space. On each card, the language and level are aligned left. For example, the first card reads “ENGLISH FOR CREATIVES” in bold uppercase black, with a smaller gray line underneath reading “Intermediate · Hybrid online sessions”. A short descriptive sentence in light black text follows: “Sharpen your professional English with briefs, pitches, and critiques as learning materials.” At the far right of the card is a compact vertical column of two elements, aligned top: a small uppercase label in red text like “8-WEEK TRACK”, and directly below it a small outlined mini-button reading “VIEW DETAILS”. The other cards are titled “GERMAN FOR DESIGNERS”, “FRENCH FOR TRAVEL”, and “JAPANESE FOUNDATIONS”, each with similarly concise descriptor and level lines, all in the same typographic style. Behind the cards, subtly visible but not interfering with legibility, are faint vertical gray guidelines spaced evenly, suggesting the column grid; these lines are hair-thin and stop before the edges of the page, giving a sense of structure without becoming decorative. Below the programs is a feature section that resembles a Swiss magazine spread with a typographic testimonial on the left and a cropped black-and-white still photo on the right. The section label in red reads “VOICES”, followed by a small heading in black: “Designers who changed how they speak.” Under it, a large italicized quote in black reads: ““It felt less like a language class and more like a design workshop where the material just happened to be French.”” Beneath the quote, in smaller caps, is the name “MARTA, UX DESIGNER, BERLIN”. To the right of this text, breaking slightly out of the central column, is a static photograph framed in a thin black line: a high-contrast, black-and-white photo of a modern classroom seen from a side angle. The viewpoint is from mid-height, looking diagonally across a long table with minimalistic chairs. On the far side of the table, out of focus, sits a student with a notebook open; the focus is sharper on a wall in the background where large sheets of paper with clean black type and simple line diagrams are pinned in a grid pattern. The lighting is bright but diffused, creating soft shadows; the aesthetic is clean and modern, matching the Swiss theme. The photo is monochrome to maintain the typographic focus; no faces are clearly recognizable, just the impression of people studying. The next section presents a clean schedule glimpse, still without interactive elements, just static typographic blocks. The section label is a small red uppercase “SCHEDULE OVERVIEW”. Under it, the main text is a black title: “A weekly rhythm that fits in your grid.” Below this, a simple, single-column list of sample weekly structure is displayed as three horizontally stretching blocks that sit one under another. Each block has a left-aligned day label in bold uppercase (e.g., “MONDAY”, “WEDNESDAY”, “SATURDAY”), with a thin vertical black rule separating it from the session description on the right. The description is in regular-weight black: “Live Studio Session · 18:00–19:30 (CET)” for Monday, “Project Review & Feedback · Self-paced” for Wednesday, and “Conversation Lab · 11:00–12:00 (CET)” for Saturday. The three blocks are connected visually by aligning all the vertical rules and day labels with the same grid line. Below the schedule, there’s a minimalist block of copy in smaller text explaining: “We sync our sessions with your existing work rhythm. No commute, no clutter — just clear, focused sessions.” The subsequent section explains the school’s philosophy in more depth through clearly structured text and simple iconography. The section title is in red uppercase: “WHY HELVETICA LANGUAGE STUDIO”. Underneath it, three key principles are presented in a vertical stack (not side-by-side), to preserve the vertical flow. Each principle has a small, square, flat icon in a single color placed to the left, aligned with the baseline of the heading. The icons are extremely minimal and geometric: for “Clarity Over Clutter”, the icon is a simple white square with a thin black outline and three evenly spaced horizontal black lines inside, representing clean lines of text; for “Consistent Structure”, the icon is a grid of four small squares in black outline; for “Design-Led Teaching”, the icon is a stylized speech bubble formed from a black rectangle with a cut-out triangle. The principle titles are bold uppercase black, with short explanatory text beneath in smaller regular weight, always left aligned. The overall impression is of systematic, well-ordered blocks of information with nothing extraneous. Near the end of the page, a clear, bold call-to-action section occupies nearly full width but adheres to the grid. The background of this section is a solid red block that spans from left edge to right edge, providing a sharp contrast with the rest of the page. Centered within the red background but slightly aligned toward the left to maintain Swiss asymmetry is a large, white, uppercase headline: “READY TO REDESIGN HOW YOU LEARN?”. Below it, in white regular text, is a short sentence: “Book a free 20-minute trial session and see how our grid-based method feels in practice.” Underneath is a wide white rectangular button with black uppercase text reading “BOOK YOUR TRIAL”. The button has no border and uses the negative red space around it to stand out; the margin above and below this call-to-action is generous, so the typography breathes. Finally, the footer area returns to an off-white background and is divided into three simple vertical blocks aligned left, each separated by generous space, maintaining the sense of a single-column but subtly differentiated via alignment and hierarchy. At the top of the footer, a small red rule runs horizontally across the main column. The first block contains the logo, again as “Helvetica Language Studio” in black, much smaller than the header logo. Below it is an address line in gray: “Based in Zurich. Teaching worldwide online.” The second block lists three links in small uppercase black text: “TERMS”, “PRIVACY”, “ACCESSIBILITY”. These are stacked vertically, with a thin vertical gray line to their left, echoing the grid motif. The third block contains minimalist contact information and social references in small, regular black text: “Contact: hello@helveticalanguage.studio” and a simple text line “Instagram · Behance · LinkedIn” with no icons, separated by midpoints. The footer ends with a tiny, centered line of text at the very bottom in gray: “© 2025 Helvetica Language Studio.” Throughout the entire page, there is no animation and no video; all images and illustrations are static, flat, and sharply defined, with crisp edges and no gradients. The Swiss-style typography, strict use of grid, restrained color palette, and generous negative space define the full character of the landing page.

instruction: Planning the generation details for the following website:
input: Design a minimal, single-column–oriented homepage for a research-focused site called "Quiet Circuits" that studies the neuroscience of focus and calm through contemplative practices. The aesthetic should be extremely clean and understated, with generous whitespace, subtle asymmetries, and a quiet, almost gallery-like feel. The background of the entire page is a soft off‑white (#FAFAF7), with no visible texture and ample padding around the edges so that the content appears as a narrow vertical column centered on large screens, but slightly offset to the left to introduce a gentle asymmetry. The primary accent color is a muted slate blue (#4A5A73), and a secondary accent is a pale moss green (#C9D5C2). Use a single sans‑serif font family throughout, such as a clean, neutral typeface similar to "Inter" or "Helvetica Neue". All text is aligned left, except for a few small centered captions to reinforce the minimalist research aesthetic. There are no videos, no animations, and no charts or data visualizations; the page is completely static and still. The hero area at the top is quiet and spacious. A narrow horizontal navigation bar sits at the very top, but it does not feel like a separate block; instead it appears as simple text links sitting on the same background color as the rest of the page. On the far left, the site name is set in small uppercase: "QUIET CIRCUITS" in slate blue, letterspaced slightly, with a light weight. To the right, spaced out with plenty of margin, are plain text links in a muted medium gray (#666666), each separated by at least 32px of space: "Research", "Methods", "Publications", "Team", "Contact". When hovered (conceptually; visual style is static here), they would be underlined, but in this design they appear just as regular text with no decoration. On the far right of the nav bar, aligned with the content column rather than the very edge of the screen, is a slim outlined button with the label "Request Access". It consists of a rounded rectangle outline in slate blue, very thin (1px), with transparent fill and the text "Request Access" in small uppercase slate blue. The navigation bar has subtle vertical padding but no shadow and no border, so it feels integrated with the page. Below the nav, there is a large hero section that spans the width of the centered content column. The hero is divided into two vertical slices, but still feels single-column: the left 60% is text, the right 40% is a tall, narrow hero image. The text block starts with a small superscript-like label in all caps, light gray: "FOCUS & NEURAL QUIETUDE". Below it, the main hero headline is large (approximately 48px on desktop), in slate blue, with a light but confident weight, using sentence case: "Researching calm in a distracted world." The line breaks should be crafted so that "Researching calm" is on the first line and "in a distracted world." is on the second. The headline is followed by a single paragraph of introduction in a medium gray, smaller body size: "Quiet Circuits is an independent research project exploring how contemplative practices, mindful movement, and structured rest influence attention, memory, and emotional regulation. We design slow, carefully controlled studies—and make our methods radically transparent." The paragraph has a generous line height and a maximum width of around 60 characters for readability. Below the paragraph, aligned left, there are two minimal textual CTAs rather than large buttons: first, a thin outlined pill-shaped button in slate blue with white text reading "View current study protocol", and immediately to the right, spaced by 16px, a ghost text link in slate blue that simply says "Download overview PDF". Both CTAs are small, understated, and do not visually dominate the rest of the hero. The hero image on the right is a tall, narrow photograph in subdued, desaturated tones, in a style that feels like a research-based lifestyle photograph. The image shows a top-down, slightly angled view of a clean, light wood desk surface with a single open notebook, a slim EEG headband resting neatly on the page, and a ceramic cup of green tea. The notebook pages are blank except for a faint grid pattern; the EEG headband is modern and minimalist, with a pale matte gray body and small, dark sensors. Soft natural light comes from the top left, casting very gentle shadows to the right and enhancing the calm mood, with no harsh contrasts. The background of the photograph is blurred so that the objects feel anchored but not overly detailed; hues are muted beige, soft gray, and subtle green from the tea. The photo has slightly rounded corners and a very thin off-white border, as if it were printed and placed on the page. Under the hero, a subtle horizontal hairline divider in a very light gray (#E6E6E0) runs across the width of the content column, with generous top and bottom margins to let it breathe. The next section is titled "Research questions" and is strictly single-column, aligned with the text from the hero. The section heading "Research questions" is in slate blue, slightly smaller than the main hero headline but clearly larger than body text, with light weight and no underline. Below the heading, three concise research questions are listed as individual blocks rather than in a bullet list, each block separated by vertical spacing. Each question starts with a tiny label in small caps, pale moss green, for example "RQ1", "RQ2", "RQ3". Beneath each label is the question in bold or semi-bold gray: "How does daily breath-focused practice influence sustained attention over six weeks?" then "What neural signatures accompany the onset of subjective calm during seated meditation?" and finally "Can gentle movement sequences improve error monitoring and recovery after cognitive load?". Under each question is a single, plain, 1–2 sentence elaboration in regular weight body text, with no icons or diagrams. Everything is aligned to the same left edge, with a clear vertical rhythm. Following this, there is a quiet section focused on methods, with a subtle asymmetry introduced through an image placed off to one side. The section heading "Methods, in detail" appears, again in slate blue, followed by a short, two-sentence paragraph in gray: "We prioritize clarity over spectacle. Every protocol we run is documented, versioned, and made accessible to other researchers and thoughtful practitioners." Below this paragraph, content is split into two vertical blocks that still feel vertically stacked rather than like a strict two-column layout; the left side holds a narrow text list of method pillars, and to the right, slightly indented and lower, is a small monochrome illustration that does not compete with the text. The left block contains three subheadings in small uppercase slate blue: "Design", "Measurement", and "Environment". Under "Design" is a line of text: "Randomized, controlled, with transparent inclusion and exclusion criteria." Under "Measurement": "EEG, heart rate variability, and subjective reports, synced and time-stamped." Under "Environment": "Soft light, quiet acoustics, and minimal visual clutter to reduce noise in the data." The right-side illustration is a simple line drawing in a flat, minimal style: a top-down schematic of a person sitting cross-legged on a floor cushion in a simple room. The person is abstract and geometric rather than realistic, composed of clean lines and simple shapes in soft slate outlines, with no facial features. The floor is indicated by a thin rectangle; on one side of the person is a minimal rectangular object representing a laptop displaying abstract waveforms, and on the other side, a small plant in a plain cylindrical pot. The background of the illustration is very light gray, and the lines are consistent in stroke weight. Everything is calm and unembellished. After a generous vertical gap, the next section presents recent publications, titled "Selected publications & preprints". This section stays firmly single-column. The heading is in slate blue, followed by a faint line of helper text in light gray: "We publish slowly and carefully; this list is updated quarterly." Below are three publication entries, each set as a tidy block. Each entry has a small, mono-spaced line at the top giving the year and status in uppercase: e.g. "2024 · PREPRINT", in medium gray. On the next line is the title in darker gray, sentence case and slightly larger: "Slow Respiration, Stable Networks: EEG Signatures of Breath-Focused Attention". Under that, a short line in lighter gray credits authors as "A. Ito, M. Chen, L. Rasmussen". The final line of each block is a tiny, understated text link in slate blue like "Read abstract" or "Open preprint", separated by small, subtle bullet-like middots. The publication blocks are separated by subtle thin horizontal dividers that do not touch the edge of the screen, only the content column. Immediately beneath this publications section, to the right of the column (sticking out slightly into the whitespace), there is a very small, narrow vertical note in rotated text, like a spine label, in light gray: "DOCUMENTS" creating a subtle, almost hidden typographic asymmetry. Next comes a "Study participation" section that explains how people can join or observe current work. The heading "Study participation" appears in slate blue. A short paragraph in gray explains: "We work with small, carefully screened groups of participants. If you are curious about joining or shadowing an upcoming study, please review our criteria below." Below this, instead of a bullet list, present three minimalistic boxed lines that look like form fields but are not interactive. Each box is a full-width rounded rectangle with a very faint border and padded inner text. The boxes say: "I am comfortable sitting quietly for at least 25 minutes." then "I have no history of epilepsy or photosensitive conditions." then "I am willing to refrain from caffeine for 4 hours before sessions." Each statement is in mid-gray; on the far right of each box, there is a small empty square outline that visually suggests a checkbox, but remains unfilled, keeping the page static and non-interactive. Beneath the boxes is a single line of small text in light gray, centered within the column: "These are not consent forms. They are starting points for conversation." Just below this, a very minimal almost-button appears: a plain text phrase "Email the research team" in slate blue, with a thin underline only beneath the words themselves, not across the entire width. The next section introduces the team in a restrained, almost directory-like manner. The heading "Research team" is in slate blue. Beneath, there are three researcher profiles, each taking the full width but designed with a slight offset of the portrait images to the left, overlapping the margin to create gentle asymmetry. Each profile consists of a small, square grayscale portrait photograph on the left, about 80x80 pixels, with rounded corners and a soft shadow that is barely perceptible. The portraits are shot in soft, diffused light against neutral backgrounds, showing individuals in relaxed, natural poses without exaggerated expressions—slight smiles, direct eye contact. They wear simple clothing in neutral colors. To the right of each portrait, the name is in darker gray, medium weight, e.g. "Lina Rasmussen, PhD". Below the name is a single line in small uppercase light gray indicating role: "PRINCIPAL INVESTIGATOR", "NEUROPHYSIOLOGIST", "METHODS & ETHICS". Under that is a one-sentence description in regular gray, such as "Studies how slow, regular breath shapes large-scale neural dynamics during rest." No social media icons are displayed; instead, under each profile, a very small, light gray text link reads "View CV (PDF)". Toward the bottom of the page, there is a very simple newsletter-style area framed as a research log subscription. The section heading reads "Receive field notes". A concise paragraph in gray says: "Every month or so, we send a short, text-only dispatch with ongoing findings, protocol changes, and recommended readings. No tracking pixels. No images. Just notes." Below this, there is a single horizontal email input field and a submit button, both minimal. The input field is a simple line—no background fill—consisting of a single horizontal rule in slate blue with placeholder text "you@example.org" in light gray sitting above the line as if it were a label. To the right, at the same baseline, is a small filled rectangular button in slate blue with white text "Subscribe". There are no icons. Finally, the page footer is extremely restrained and aligned with the same content column. A very thin horizontal line in a light gray sits above the footer content. The footer contains two lines: the first line in small gray text reads "Quiet Circuits is an independent, grant-supported research project based in Copenhagen."; the second line, even smaller and in lighter gray, reads "Contact: hello@quietcircuits.org · PGP key on request · Last updated: August 2025". There are no social icons, only small text. The entire page has consistent, airy spacing between sections, no bright colors or flashy elements, and a static, contemplative feel appropriate for a research site devoted to calm and attention. There are no moving elements, no animations, no video blocks, and no charts or data visualizations anywhere on the page.

instruction: Planning the generation details for the following website:
input:
Design a stark, brutalist-style homepage for a research-driven “Virtual Art Gallery” site titled “HARDLINES ARCHIVE.” The entire page feels like a mashup of a late-90s academic resource and an underground art zine, with a mostly single-column layout that occasionally fractures into asymmetrical side blocks, but always snaps back to a central vertical flow. The background of the entire page is flat, harsh #f5f5f5 light gray, with no gradients, and all main text is set in a monospace font like “Courier New” or another typewriter-style font, giving the entire page a raw, utilitarian feel. The default text color is solid black (#000000), with occasional use of a high-contrast red (#ff0033) and deep blue (#0000cc) for emphasis. All elements sit in a central column of content that is roughly 800px wide on desktop, centered on the page, with large margins of empty gray space on either side.

At the very top, spanning the full width of the page (edge to edge), place a thick horizontal banner approximately 120px high with a pure white background (#ffffff) and a harsh 1px black border at the top and bottom. Inside this banner, aligned to the left, the gallery’s name is written in huge uppercase monospace letters: “HARDLINES ARCHIVE” in black, very bold, with a slightly uneven letter spacing that feels almost like it was typed on a typewriter. Below it, slightly smaller and italicized in the same monospace font, appears the subtitle in red: “Research on Digital Brutalism in Contemporary Art Spaces.” To the right side of this banner, occupying the right third of the space, create a blocky, gritty black-and-white illustration. This image is a flat, high-contrast vector-style drawing of a virtual gallery room: a rectangular room seen from a slightly elevated perspective, with harsh, straight lines forming walls and a floor grid. On the walls, there are generic rectangular frames outlined in black with no images inside, giving an unfinished and clinical feel. The floor is a simple grid of black lines on white, receding toward a vanishing point. The entire illustration is boxed in a thick black border, as if it were one of the artworks. Beneath the headline text in the banner, on the left side, there is a small, all-caps tagline in dark blue: “ISSN 404-404 – ONLINE EXHIBITION STUDIES.”

Below the banner, there is a thin horizontal black rule that stretches across the width of the central column, marking the start of the main content. Underneath this rule, aligned with the left edge of the content column, place a narrow, tall vertical navigation strip on the left, about 200px wide, while the rest of the column to the right is the primary content stream. The navigation strip is not separated by a background color; instead, it is defined by left-aligned, stacked text links and an occasional thin vertical black line that runs down its right side for visual separation. The navigation section is titled in uppercase, small font at the top: “INDEX” in black. Below that, in a single column of links with generous spacing between each, are the main sections: “HOME,” “CURRENT ISSUE,” “ARTIST DOSSIERS,” “METHODS,” “ARCHIVE,” and “ABOUT / CONTACT.” Each of these is in uppercase, black text, with visible underlines as if they are default browser links; hovering is implied by design but not shown. When a link is active, it would appear in red; on this homepage, “HOME” is in red (#ff0033). Beneath the navigation links, still in this left strip, there is a block of all-caps text in dark blue called “BULLETIN,” followed by three brief, left-aligned, one-line notices: “Call for papers: ‘Interface as Gallery Wall’ – Deadline 01.03.2026,” “New: Field notes from No-UI Exhibitions,” and “Transcript: Panel on Brutalist Web Museums.” Each notice is in small monospace type, black, with a tiny square bullet (■) in red before each line.

To the right of this navigation strip, occupying the remaining width up to the central column limit, the main content begins with a brutalist “hero” section consisting of text and one flat, graphic image. At the top, there is a large, blocky headline in uppercase: “CURRENT STUDY: VIRTUAL WHITE CUBES, HARD EDGES.” The headline is set in black, centered horizontally in this main content region, and appears in a large monospace font. Beneath it, there is a subtitle in smaller text, left-aligned: “A research-based virtual exhibition on how brutalist aesthetics are reshaping digital art galleries.” Below the subtitle, there is a rectangular hero image centered within the column, framed by a 3px thick black border. The image is a stark, flat-design illustration in grayscale with a brutalist mood: it depicts an abstract 3D maze of gallery rooms rendered as interlocking rectangular prisms and corridors, all white and light gray, outlined in black. The viewpoint is from slightly above and to one side, giving a sense of depth and disorientation. Each wall has simple rectangular cutouts representing artworks, but they are just empty white spaces with thick black borders. The edges of the structures cast sharp, simplified shadows in darker gray, emphasizing geometry. There are no human figures, no textures, only pure shapes and lines. At the bottom-left corner of the image, inside the illustration, there is a small, black label in monospace reading “Fig. 1 – Modular rooms / No furniture.”

Directly beneath the hero image, still within the main content column, a short paragraph introduces the site in somewhat academic tone. The text is left-aligned, black, and uses a slightly smaller monospace size than the headline: “HARDLINES ARCHIVE is a research-oriented virtual gallery documenting how artists, curators, and designers inhabit the raw space of the browser. No gradients, no illusions of comfort—just work, text, and exposed structure.” The paragraph is followed by a second one, separated by a blank line: “This platform collects visual experiments, textual case studies, and methodological notes on the aesthetics of digital brutalism. Each room is an interface; each hyperlink is a threshold.”

Moving downward, the site transitions into a vertically stacked series of “Exhibition Notes” cards, each taking up the full width of the main content column but separated by generous whitespace and thin horizontal black rules. The section title is left-aligned, uppercase: “EXHIBITION NOTES / LATEST ENTRIES.” The words “EXHIBITION NOTES” are in black, while “/ LATEST ENTRIES” is in red. Below this title, each card has an asymmetrical composition: the title and metadata on the left, and a very small, square thumbnail image on the right that is visually aligned with the top of the card’s text. Each card is backed by a flat white background rectangle with a 1px black border, making them appear like index cards pinned to the gray page.

For the first card, the title is “Room Without Hover States” in uppercase, black, slightly larger than body text. Underneath it, in small text, appears “Case Study – by DR. ELLEN MORROW – 2025.11.09.” A horizontal black line separates this metadata from a two-line excerpt: “What happens when the visitor’s cursor no longer provides comfort? We analyze three galleries that remove all hover affordances, forcing the user into a blunt click-or-leave tension.” The excerpt is left-aligned and intentionally uses line breaks to maintain a brutalist rhythm. On the far right of this card, aligned with the title line, a small 80x80px square thumbnail image is placed. This thumbnail shows a blocky, isometric representation of a gallery floor: a top-down view of a rectangular room divided into three smaller rooms, drawn in black outline with no shading, and a single red square on one wall indicating an artwork. The rest of the square and background are white. The black lines are thick and crude, reminiscent of technical diagrams, with a heavy, ink-like quality.

The second card follows the same structural pattern but with a different thumbnail. The title is “Bandwidth as Wall Texture” in uppercase. Metadata line: “Essay – by JOONAS K. / NETWORK TOPOLOGIES LAB – 2025.10.28.” The excerpt reads: “In a gallery that loads in layers, buffering becomes a visible material. We examine how artists turn compression artifacts and slow render times into deliberate spatial qualities.” The thumbnail for this card is a flat design image: a rectangular screen shape at the center, with three stacked “loading bars” beneath it, drawn as chunky black rectangles in various lengths. Random jagged shapes (like glitch fragments) in red and blue float near the screen, suggesting data corruption. The background of this image is pale gray, and there are no gradients; all shapes are crisp, with straight edges.

The third card is titled “Against Seamless Navigation,” with metadata: “Field Notes – COLLECTIVE LOGBOOK – 2025.09.03.” The excerpt states: “Visitors keep asking, ‘Where is the back button?’ In this field note, we catalog user disorientation as a design tool, not an accident.” The thumbnail shows a maze-like arrow in black: a thick arrow that turns at right angles, creating a square, then another, then a dead end. It is drawn in flat, solid black on white, with no shading or decoration. The arrow never points to an obvious exit, emphasizing confusion.

After the Exhibition Notes section, the page shifts back to a more singular, column-centric element: a “Research Rooms” index that visually resembles a stripped-down floor plan. The section heading, large and uppercase, centered, reads: “RESEARCH ROOMS.” Below the heading, centered and slightly smaller, is a subtitle: “Choose a room. Enter an experiment.” Under this, the page shows a simple ASCII-map-like layout rendered as styled text: a series of horizontal rectangular blocks stacked vertically, each representing a “room,” but they are staggered left or right to create an asymmetric effect while still remaining within the vertical flow. Each room block has a thick black border and a white interior, with the room name in bold uppercase inside. For the first room, the block is wide and aligned with the left margin of the main content column. It reads: “ROOM 01 – SCREEN AS BRICK.” In the bottom-right corner of this block, there is a small note in smaller text: “3 projects / 1 interview / 12 images.” The second block is indented about 40px to the right relative to the first; it reads: “ROOM 02 – MONOCHROME CURATION.” The small note reads: “Grayscale-only galleries, no color allowed.” The third block is aligned back to the left margin, labeled: “ROOM 03 – ACCESS DENIED?” with the note: “Studies on refusal, error pages, and deliberate inaccessibility.” The visual impression is of a crude floor plan or stacked concrete units, but done purely with rectangles and text, with no photorealism.

Halfway down this “Research Rooms” section, to break the column subtly, a vertical text label appears on the far right of the main column, rotated 90 degrees clockwise in intention (though visually it can just be placed as small vertical text): “FLOOR 1 // VIRTUAL WING.” It is in dark blue, small font size, acting as a side annotation, while the main room blocks continue below it in the center.

Below the Research Rooms, the page includes a dedicated “Methods & Tools” section that feels like a rough lab notebook. The heading is left-aligned, uppercase: “METHODS & TOOLS.” Under it, there is a two-column layout only within this section: on the left, a list of textual entries; on the right, a plain, diagram-like static image. On the left, each method is listed as a short title followed by a terse description, with clear spacing and no bullets, just line breaks. For example: “Protocol #01: Screen Capture as Fieldwork – Systematic documentation of gallery walkthroughs using fixed-resolution screen recording.” Next line: “Protocol #07: ‘Broken Grid’ – A guideline for intentionally misaligning elements to produce a sense of structural honesty.” Another: “Tool: Archive.log – A shared log file for annotating interface anomalies across multiple users.” The tone is clinical but minimalist. On the right side of this section, aligned to the top of the first entry, sits a static black-on-white diagram image. It shows three stacked rectangles, each representing a “screen,” connected by harsh vertical lines. Each rectangle has a small label inside it: the top says “ENTRY,” the middle “FRICTION,” the bottom “EXIT.” The lines connecting them are thick black arrows pointing down. There are no curves; everything is drawn with straight lines and right angles, giving it a schematic, austere technical drawing feel.

Further down, the “Artist Dossiers” section presents a more directory-like list of researchers and artists connected to the virtual gallery. The heading is centered, large, uppercase: “ARTIST DOSSIERS.” Beneath it, three entries are stacked vertically, each separated by a thin black line. Each entry consists of a small portrait placeholder on the left and text on the right. The portraits are deliberately anti-glamour: each is a black-and-white, hyper-minimalist illustration of a head-and-shoulders silhouette, drawn in flat profile or near-profile style. For the first, imagine a simple flat design of a head and shoulders seen from the front, filled with solid black, against a white square background with a thick black border; the facial features are abstracted into basic geometric shapes—two short horizontal lines for eyes, a small vertical line for a nose. Next to this image, the text reads in uppercase: “DR. ELLEN MORROW” and below it, smaller: “Researcher in Interface Ecologies / Author of ‘Room Without Hover States’.” Below that, a short, abrupt biography: “Documents visitor micro-gestures and non-clicks in gallery contexts. Believes that comfort is a distraction.” The second dossier uses a similar silhouette image but in side profile, with a sharp, angular nose and shaved head outline, giving an almost bust-like appearance. The text reads: “JOONAS K.” and below: “Network Topologies Lab.” Bio: “Treats buffering as a sculptural medium. Charts the latency of aesthetic experience.” The third dossier silhouette has jagged hair and a slight tilt of the head. Text: “COLLECTIVE LOGBOOK” and underneath: “Multi-author field record of digital galleries.” Bio: “Compiles scattered notes, screenshots, and contradictory accounts into a single evolving ledger.”

Before reaching the footer, there is an “About the Archive” section, which occupies the full width of the main column and returns to pure text. The heading is left-aligned, uppercase: “ABOUT THIS ARCHIVE.” Underneath, in a block of text, the site explains itself in an intentionally manifesto-like paragraph: “HARDLINES ARCHIVE is not a neutral database. It is an ongoing experiment in how research, exhibition design, and interface structure collide. The site is deliberately uncomfortable: oversized type, exposed borders, and unforgiving margins. We track how these conditions reshape the way art is seen online.” A second paragraph reads: “All rooms in this virtual gallery adhere to a core principle: no simulated warmth. Walls remain flat. Shadows are mechanical, not atmospheric. We invite artists and researchers to inhabit this severity, to see what new forms of care or critique can emerge from such hardness.” At the end of this section, aligned to the right, there is a small, uppercase label in red: “VERSION 0.9 – DESIGN UNDER CONSTANT REVISION.”

Finally, the footer section spans the full width of the central column and is boxed in a heavy black border, making it feel like a separate module resting at the bottom of the page. The background inside the footer is white, in contrast to the gray page. On the left side of the footer, there is a small block of text: “CONTACT” in uppercase, followed by: “Email: info@hardlines-archive.org” and “Signal: +00 000 000 000.” Under that: “Postal (for objects, not letters): HARDLINES ARCHIVE, C/O Concrete Studies Dept., 4th Floor, Unnamed Building, Sector 3.” On the right side of the footer, there is a small, unadorned, text-only list in uppercase: “LEGAL / TERMS,” “COOKIES (NONE),” “OPEN CALLS,” each separated by vertical bars ( | ). Directly under this right-side list, in tiny uppercase monospace, aligned to the right, is a final line of text: “Built in the browser. No frameworks. No soft corners.” There are no logos, no social media icons, no color gradients anywhere in the footer. The entire page maintains its brutalist, static, and research-oriented character, relying solely on flat colors, harsh borders, monospace typography, and stark images composed of geometric shapes and high contrast, without any video, animation, or data visualizations.

instruction: Planning the generation details for the following website:
input:
Design a single-page brutalist portfolio website titled “NOISE & NEON” for an experimental digital designer and coder, with an aesthetic that feels raw, intentionally unfinished, and driven by overlapping layers, harsh colors, and bold typography. The entire page sits on a solid, slightly off-white background (“#F5F3EE”) that feels like aged printer paper, with obvious grid lines that appear as faint grey vertical and horizontal lines spanning the viewport, giving it a blueprint / layout sketch vibe. These grid lines are visible behind all content and sometimes are crossed by thick blocks of color that disrupt them. The default font across the site is a sharp, grotesque sans-serif, reminiscent of Neue Haas Grotesk or Helvetica, combined with occasional monospace “code” text. Text is mostly black (“#111111”) with accents in electric cyan (“#00E5FF”), acidic yellow (“#FFF600”), safety orange (“#FF5A00”), and dirty magenta (“#FF2F92”). There are no rounded corners anywhere; every shape is a hard rectangle, edge, or line. There is no subtle drop shadow; instead, use hard offset shadows in pure black or bright colors, like something cut-and-pasted.

At the very top, there’s a fixed header bar stretching full width, about 80px tall, with a flat neon yellow background and a thin black border on the bottom. Aligned to the far left is the site title in all caps: “NOISE & NEON” in a bold condensed type, large and slightly overlapping the header’s bottom edge by about 5px so the text seems to bleed into the content area. Underneath the main title text, in much smaller uppercase text, tight tracking, appears: “PORTFOLIO OF LINA KOVAC – DIGITAL DESIGN / CODE / CHAOS”. On the center of the header, overlapping both title and navigation slightly, is a small, intentionally misaligned black rectangle with white monospace text “v.2025” that looks like a label slapped on last-minute. To the right side of the header, aligned right but not perfectly, are navigation links in uppercase: “WORK”, “TEXT”, “ABOUT”, “CONTACT”. Each link is black text on the yellow background except the currently hovered or active section, which appears inverted (white text on a small black rectangle that sits behind it, offset and misaligned). Links have no hover animations, but the design suggests interactivity by their rough underlines: a hand-drawn-looking straight cyan line under the active link (though still made of straight pixels). The header remains fixed as the user scrolls, so content passes beneath it.

Immediately below and visually overlapping the header by about 40px is a large, brutalist hero section. The hero is a horizontally full-bleed collage-like area about 80% of the viewport height, with a central composition of overlapping blocks. On the far left, a tall vertical strip about 20% of the width is filled with a flat cyan background and very large black vertically stacked text reading “DIGITAL BRUTALISM” in uppercase, rotated 90 degrees counterclockwise so the user must tilt their head. Over the center-right part of this hero is a large image placeholder: an intentionally harsh, photorealistic but overexposed image of a city street café at night, shot from a low angle.
