Inside the image: a small independent coffee shop with large windows glowing orange from the warm interior light, a few silhouetted figures sitting inside, neon signs reflected in wet pavement, and the camera is slightly tilted (Dutch angle). The café sign reads “NOISE COFFEE” in blocky, white sans-serif letters; reflections of the sign appear distorted on the wet ground. The background features a dark urban scene, with vertical light streaks and blurry cars. The image edge is not framed neatly: it’s placed slightly off-center, overlapping the cyan strip on the left and covering the faint grid lines beneath. Behind this main photo, slightly offset upward and to the right, is another solid rectangle in dirty magenta, making the photo appear like a collage cut-out, with the magenta rectangle visible as a hard drop shadow on two sides. On the right side of the hero, there is a column of text on a pale background that looks almost like another sheet of paper laid over the main page. This column is about 30% of the width, with a thin black border and the heading in uppercase, bold: “I DESIGN SYSTEMS THAT LOOK BROKEN BUT WORK PERFECTLY.” Underneath, smaller body text in black reads in left-aligned paragraphs: “I’m Lina, a digital designer and creative coder exploring brutalist interfaces, glitchy coffee shop aesthetics, and unapologetically loud web systems. This portfolio is a playground of overlaps, misalignments, and intentional friction. Scroll if you’re not afraid of pixels out of place.” Below this text are three blocky button-like elements stacked vertically but spaced irregularly, each a full-width rectangle with a 1px black border and different background colors: first button is electric cyan with black uppercase text “VIEW CLIENT WORK”, second is white with black uppercase text “LAB EXPERIMENTS”, and third is transparent with just a black border and black uppercase text “READ PROCESS NOTES”. The third looks slightly misaligned, shifted 4px to the right, overlapping the right border of the text column and partially intruding into white space.

Under the hero, there is a section labelled “SELECTED WORK” where the layout becomes more hierarchical and overlapping. The section title is a huge uppercase word “WORK” in black that sits partly behind the project grid, as if someone printed the word and then dropped thumbnails on top of it. At the top left of this section, just below the hero, “SELECTED WORK” appears in smaller uppercase monospaced text on a black rectangle with white text, giving it the appearance of a label. The projects are presented as a two-column masonry-like layout with each project represented by a card that overlaps others slightly, creating a controlled chaos. Each card is a solid rectangle with a visible 1px black border, no rounded corners. The top-left project card, titled “NOISE COFFEE INTERFACES”, is wider than the others and overlaps the underlying grid lines. Its background color is a pale grey, with a horizontal image at the top: a flat-design illustration of a coffee shop interface on a laptop screen. The illustration shows a minimal UI: large blocky buttons, a brutalist menu listing “ESPRESSO / LONG BLACK / OAT LATTE / FILTER” in uppercase, a big black and white photo of a coffee cup with halftone dots, and harsh red error boxes that say “OUT OF STOCK” over some drinks. The UI illustration uses mostly black, white, and red, with occasional neon green highlights. Under the image, aligned left, the project title “NOISE COFFEE INTERFACES” appears in bold uppercase black. Below that, in smaller text: “E-commerce and ordering experience for an independent coffee shop that wanted to feel like a glitchy zine.” Further below, still in small text, is a metadata row: “Role: UX / UI / Frontend” and “Year: 2024”. At the bottom-right corner of the card is a crude rectangular black tag with white text “CASE STUDY →” that overlaps the border and extends 3px beyond the card, intentionally breaking the neatness.

To the right of this first card, there’s a taller narrow project card titled “CAFFEINE SIGNALS”. Its background is a flat dirty magenta with a centered, vertical monochrome image: a stylized, high-contrast illustration of a coffee cup morphing into a Wi-Fi signal icon. The cup is composed of thick black lines, and the Wi-Fi waves above it resemble radiating concentric arcs in neon cyan. The illustration style is bold, poster-like, with subtle halftone texture in the background. Below the image, the project title “CAFFEINE SIGNALS” appears in uppercase white text. Under the title, smaller white text reads: “Speculative data visualization for coffee consumption vs. online presence. Built with p5.js and WebGL glitches.” There is a small, underlined text-only link in cyan that reads “VIEW STATIC MOCKUPS”.

Below these two, the grid continues asymmetrically. On the left column, a smaller square card titled “404 CAFE LANDING PAGE” appears, overlapping the bottom of the “NOISE COFFEE INTERFACES” card by about 10px. This card has a safety orange background with a central black screen mockup: a simple landing page where the text “404 – COFFEE NOT FOUND” appears in giant letters over an empty coffee cup outline. The mockup is intentionally tilted a few degrees, and a white rectangle behind it forms an offset, like a printed piece taped at an angle. To the right, slightly higher so it looks like it’s peeking over, there’s another card with a white background titled “NEON POUR ANIMATED POSTERS” (even though the site uses no animation, this project card refers to animated posters as static thumbnails). The image is a still frame of a poster depicting a mid-air splash of coffee rendered as solid geometric shapes—sharp, angular shards in black and deep brown floating on a vibrant cyan background. Thin magenta lines outline some of the shapes, and small text around the edges reads “EXPERIMENTS IN MOTION & LIQUID GEOMETRY” in a tight uppercase font. Below the image, the description reads: “Series of motion studies for digital signage in coffee bars, focusing on brutalist typography and liquid abstraction.” There is a small caption in monospace at the bottom left: “Tools: After Effects, JS, Printer errors.”

Further down this section, give a sense of more projects by listing two more overlapping cards toward the bottom: one called “FILTER / NOISE / CODE” with a minimalist black background and large white monospace terminal-like text showing a fragment of code (e.g., “for (grain in pixels) { if (grain > threshold) { noise(); } }”), and one called “STANDARD ISSUE MENU” with a beige background and simple list-style layout showing a menu UI with overly thick black underlines and prices aligned in a rigid grid. Each card has its own unique color scheme but maintains the brutalist, high-contrast approach. The overlapping should create subtle layers: some cards slightly cover the corners of others, and some edges align perfectly with the invisible grid, while others intentionally break out.

Following the “WORK” section, insert a strong visual break with a full-width horizontal rule, a thick black line about 4px tall. Below this, a wide, slightly asymmetrical “TEXT & PROCESS” section presents written reflections like a raw, messy notebook. On the left side, taking about 60% of the width, is a large off-white rectangle with a thin black border, slightly tilted 1–2 degrees, making it look like a scanned sheet. At the top-left inside this rectangle, the heading appears in uppercase: “PROCESS NOTES / ESSAYS / ERRORS”. Beneath, several short text entries appear like a list but not neatly aligned; each is separated with thin, hand-drawn-looking horizontal lines in cyan. Example entries: one titled “Why Brutalist UI Makes Coffee Taste Better” in bold black text, followed by a short paragraph in smaller text explaining: “Brutalist interfaces demand attention the way an over-extracted espresso does: too loud, too bitter, yet somehow exactly right. I design interfaces that are more coffee stain than chrome polish.” Another entry titled “On Embracing Misalignment” with text: “Grids are frameworks, not prisons. I love letting elements slip—overlapping titles, cards that crash into margins, buttons that feel a pixel too big. The goal is tension, not chaos.” Each entry has a tiny label above it in uppercase monospace: “ESSAY #01”, “ESSAY #02”, etc. In the bottom-right corner of this sheet-like rectangle, overlapping its border, is a small cyan square with black text “READ ALL TEXT →”, again slightly off, breaking the frame.

On the right side of the “TEXT & PROCESS” section, occupying the remaining 40% width, is a vertically stacked column of “QUICK GLITCH NOTES” styled like sticky labels. There are three small rectangles with different background colors (magenta, electric cyan, and neon yellow), each with black uppercase titles like “LAYOUT EXPERIMENT: COFFEE CART”, “TYPE TESTS FOR MENU SCREENS”, and “ON DARK MODE & NIGHT CAFES”. Under each title, a single sentence note appears in smaller all-caps text. These sticky note rectangles slightly overlap each other and cast hard, offset shadows in black or a contrasting color, reinforcing the collage feeling.

Next, an “ABOUT” section shifts the layout again. The background behind this section turns very light grey, differentiating it from the rest of the page, but the grid lines remain faintly visible. On the left, there’s a half-width block that functions as a raw portrait card. At the top of this block is a heading in all caps: “ABOUT LINA KOVAC” in bold black. Beneath it, on the upper left corner of the block, is a square, black-and-white, high-contrast portrait image of Lina. The portrait is shot frontally with hard lighting and strong shadows; her face is partially obscured by a large coffee mug she’s holding close to her mouth. The mug is plain white with a single, crude black sticker reading “404” in marker-like text. The background of the portrait is a messy studio with visible cables, sticky notes on the wall, and a computer monitor displaying an overexposed interface. The image has a photocopy-style texture, with visible noise and halftone dots, and its edges are slightly rough, as if torn or xeroxed. To the right of the portrait and below it is strongly left-aligned text describing her: “I am a digital designer and creative coder working at the intersection of brutalist web aesthetics, independent coffee culture, and experimental interfaces. I build sites, prototypes, and visual systems that are more zine than showroom. Clients include small coffee roasters, underground galleries, and tech teams who aren’t afraid of visual noise.” Below this is a list of skills in two columns. The subheading “SKILLS / TOOLS” is uppercase, then a list: “Interface Design”, “Creative Coding”, “Design Systems”, “Art Direction”, “Web Prototyping”, “Brand Experiments”. Next to it: “Figma”, “HTML / CSS / JS”, “p5.js”, “Three.js”, “TouchDesigner”, “Printer Jam Hacks”. The list is simply plain text, no bullets, but separated by line breaks. At the bottom of the “ABOUT” block, there is a stark horizontal bar with three labels spaced out: “Based in: BERLIN”, “Operating Hours: 10:00–02:00 (CET)”, “Preferred Fuel: Double Espresso”, all uppercase.

On the right side of the “ABOUT” section is a blocky testimonial / statement area that looks like a ripped-out piece of a print magazine. The background is white with a thick black border on only two sides (top and left), leaving the right and bottom edges open. Inside, large italic serif text (a contrast to the sans serif elsewhere) says: ““WORKING WITH LINA IS LIKE PLUGGING A COFFEE MACHINE DIRECTLY INTO THE INTERNET.”” Underneath, in smaller uppercase monospaced font: “– Imaginary Client, Noise Coffee Roastery”. The mix of fonts adds tension and a slightly anarchic feel.

Finally, a “CONTACT / FOOTER” area occupies the bottom of the page. The background here is solid black, covering full width, with a taller-than-average height to feel like a poster. The main content is centered but not perfectly; everything is slightly off-grid. In the top-left of this footer block, the heading appears in uppercase white text: “CONTACT / COMMISSIONS / COLLABS”. Below it, larger, loud text reads in all caps: “EMAIL ME IF YOUR PROJECT CAN HANDLE SOME NOISE.” Under that, on a new line, the email appears in bright cyan, oversized monospace: “HELLO@NOISEANDNEON.STUDIO”. The email sits inside a hyper-rectangular, horizontally long cyan rectangle, slightly offset, with the text centered, giving it a button-like appearance but without rounded corners or hover states. To the right of this, in a narrow column, are links labelled “INSTAGRAM”, “ARE.NA”, “GITHUB”, “DRIBBBLE” stacked vertically. Each is white uppercase text on black, with a thin underlined effect made of broken segments instead of a clean line. Below these, there is a crude contact mini-form that looks almost like a paper form scanned in. It’s a white rectangle overlapping the black footer, with a slight rotation. The form fields are not styled with typical UI elements but as handwritten-like labels in uppercase: “NAME”, “EMAIL”, “PROJECT / BRIEF (100 CHAR MAX)”. Under each label is a thick black line suggesting a place to type. To the right of the lines, there are tiny black brackets “[ ]” mimicking checkboxes but with text next to them: “OK WITH WEIRD”, “NEEDS ACCESSIBILITY”, “LAUNCH DEADLINE: YESTERDAY”. At the bottom of this “form”, instead of a normal submit button, there is a crude black rectangle with white uppercase text “SEND NOISE”. This form looks intentionally impractical, as is typical for some brutalist sites.

At the very bottom of the page, flush with the black footer but separated by a thin white line, is a narrow strip that serves as a final note. On the left, small white text reads: “© 2025 NOISE & NEON / LINA KOVAC”. On the right, tiny uppercase text in grey reads: “Site deliberately misaligned. Best viewed with caffeine.” The overall page should feel like a controlled collision of elements: overlapping layers, offset blocks, harsh palettes, and an independent coffee shop-meets-digital-lab vibe, with no animations or videos and no charts or data visualizations, just static brutalist design with purposeful tension and hierarchy.

instruction: Planning the generation details for the following website:
input:
Design a single-column, editorial-style homepage for an independent “mountain lodge meets research retreat” subscription project titled “Alpenglow Studies”. The aesthetic blends the quiet, literary feel of a long-form magazine article with subtle alpine and academic cues. The entire page is centered within a generous off-white canvas (#FAF8F3), with wide left and right margins on desktop so that the main content column (about 780px wide) feels like a printed journal page floating in space. The background beyond this column is a very faint, almost imperceptible vertical gradient from slightly warmer cream at the top to cooler, pale gray at the bottom. The primary typeface throughout is a serif reminiscent of old book typography, such as “Georgia” or “Cormorant Garamond” for headings and pull quotes, paired with a clean humanist sans-serif like “Source Sans Pro” for body text, captions, and UI elements. The overall color palette is muted: dark charcoal (#1F2528) for most text, a soft pine green (#395C4D) for accents and headings, and a subdued rust (#B76A4D) for small details such as underlines, rule lines, and hover states.

At the very top, a narrow, fixed-position header bar runs full-width across the viewport, but its content is visually anchored to the same single column width as the rest of the page. The header bar background is slightly translucent off-white, allowing a hint of the page behind to show through as the user scrolls. Inside the bar, left-aligned within the main column, the site title appears in small caps serif: “ALPENGLOW STUDIES” in pine green, letterspaced. To the right, but still within the same column, are three minimalist navigation links set in small sans-serif uppercase with spaced-out letters: “Journal”, “Researchers”, and “Subscription”. They sit in a horizontal row, right-aligned, with plenty of breathing room and no visible separators; when hovered, each gets a thin rust-colored underline that appears with a slight gap from the baseline. At the far right of this header row, slightly separated, is a small, rounded-border button with the text “Sign In” in charcoal, outlined in a hairline rust stroke, white background, and a subtle drop shadow giving it a tactile, printed card feel.

Below this fixed header, the hero section occupies the full visible height of a laptop screen but remains visually constrained to the single editorial column. At the very top of the hero, centered, there is a small line of serif text in all caps reading “RESEARCH LODGE IN THE HIGH ALPS” in a soft gray, spaced out like a journal section label. Beneath it, aligned left, sits the large, multi-line title “Slow research in a fast world.” in deep charcoal serif, with a slightly oversized first letter “S” that descends below the baseline like a drop cap, emphasizing the editorial design. The title is followed by a short subheading paragraph in sans-serif: “Alpenglow Studies is a mountain lodge and editorial project where researchers retreat to think deeply, then publish slowly. Subscribe to read their field notes, seasonal reports, and long-form essays from the high alpine.” The body text is about 18px, with 1.6 line height, tightly edited, and left-aligned with a ragged right edge that recalls print. Underneath, a small horizontal rust-colored rule about 80px long creates a visual anchor on the left. Below the rule, a compact subscription call-to-action strip spans the width of the column: on the left, a single-line label in small caps serif “Become a founding reader”; beneath it, a simple email input field and button inline. The email field is a pale cream box with a very thin charcoal border, placeholder text reading “Enter your email” in light gray. To its right is a rectangular, slightly taller button filled with pine green, white sans-serif text “Start your 14-day reading trial” in sentence case. The whole CTA strip sits slightly raised from the background with a soft shadow, as if a clipped card laid on a page.

To the right of the hero text, but still within the overall column area, there is a tall, narrow editorial-style image aligned flush right, giving the layout a subtle asymmetry. This image occupies about two-thirds of the hero’s height and stays within the main column, leaving white space at the top and bottom. The image is a still, painterly yet photorealistic scene of a wooden mountain lodge at blue hour. The lodge is seen from a slightly elevated three-quarter perspective, perched on a snowy slope that descends toward the viewer, with a backdrop of layered, misty alpine peaks that fade into a deep indigo sky. Warm amber light spills from the lodge’s windows, casting soft, elongated highlights on the snow and revealing a subtle path of footprints leading from the foreground to the door. Sparse conifers, dark green, dot the slope, and wisps of fog curl around them. The style is realistic but with slightly softened edges, like a fine art photograph processed to feel like a painting. The lighting emphasizes the contrast between the cool blues and the warm orange glow inside the building. A thin white border surrounds the image, and its caption appears directly below in small italic serif type: “The observatory lodge at dusk, photographed by resident historian Lena Vorberg.”

When the user scrolls, the next section reads like an introduction in a literary journal. A generous amount of white space separates sections, with subtle vertical rhythm. A centered section label appears first: “About the lodge” in small caps serif, pale gray. Under it, aligned left, the heading “A research library above the treeline.” is set in pine green serif. The following text is a multi-paragraph block that spans the full column: “Each season, a small cohort of researchers, writers, and artists gathers at our high-altitude lodge to work on one project only. No email. No conferences. Just dense reading, slow walks in the snow, and handwritten notes in the quiet hours before dawn. When they descend, their work becomes part of this ongoing editorial experiment.” The copy continues in a second paragraph, describing how subscriptions fund the residency and how readers receive quarterly printed digests. The paragraphs are interspersed with subtle drop caps at the start of key sentences, and some phrases are emphasized using small caps instead of bold, reinforcing the print aesthetic.

Midway down this section, a three-item horizontal “manifesto strip” disrupts the strictly single-column look while still conceptually remaining one column. Three short columns sit side by side within the main width, each separated by generous white space instead of visible lines. Each mini-column has a small, uppercase sans-serif label in rust at the top (“SLOW”, “PLACE”, “RIGOR”), a small pine green serif subheading beneath (e.g., “We publish at mountain speed.”, “Research shaped by altitude.”, “Footnotes you can trust.”), and a short body of two lines of sans-serif text. These boxes have only bottom borders, a thin, hand-drawn-looking line in light gray, as if underlined on paper.

Below, the “Featured field notes” section introduces editorial cards for recent pieces. A section label “Field Notes” sits centered in tiny serif caps; beneath it, the heading “From the notebooks” appears left-aligned in charcoal serif. Three feature cards are stacked vertically, each occupying the full column width like feature stories in a magazine. Each card is separated by ample whitespace and a faint horizontal rule. The first card begins with a small, muted rust tag in sans-serif: “WINTER ISSUE 2025”. Below it, the title in larger serif: “Tracing snowmelt as an archive of forgotten rivers” in charcoal. The byline appears beneath in small italic serif: “By hydrologist Mara Kohn, Resident Winter Fellow”. Under the byline, a short excerpt: “In the high cirques above the lodge, streams disappear beneath the snowpack for months. Down below, we forget their routes. Up here, I follow their muffled sound, sketching a cartography of water that only exists in winter.” To the right of the excerpt, nudged slightly upward so its top edge aligns halfway down the text block, a small square black-and-white image is floated, about 160x160px, showing a top-down view of a scientist’s journal on a wooden desk: an open notebook filled with fine, handwritten notes and small diagrams of river paths, a fountain pen resting across the spine, a steaming enamel mug of coffee off to one side. The photo feels tactile, with visible paper grain, gentle morning light creating soft, diagonal shadows.

The second and third field note cards follow similar structures, but with different imagery: one shows a grainy, monochromatic photograph from inside the lodge’s small library—a high bookshelf, a metal ladder, a large window with snow pressed against the glass, diffused light illuminating dust motes in the air. The other presents an abstract, cyanotype-style close-up of pressed alpine plants pinned to a board. Each card ends with a subtle “Read the full note →” link in sans-serif, pine green, with the arrow rendered as a simple rightward chevron. No buttons, emphasizing the quiet reading-first design.

Next, the “Researchers in residence” section feels like an editorial profile page. At the top, a left-aligned small sans-serif label in rust reads “Current Cohort”. Below it, a serif heading: “This season’s residents.” The layout within this section introduces gentle asymmetry: instead of a grid, each researcher is presented in a staggered sequence of image then text, alternating sides, but all contained within the single central column. For the first researcher, a slightly larger portrait image is aligned flush left with the column edge, occupying roughly one third of the column width, and the bio text is to the right, occupying the rest. The portrait is a moody, grayscale photograph of a mid-30s woman in a wool sweater standing on a balcony, mountains blurred in the background; the style is documentary, with natural lighting and soft focus on the background. To the right, the researcher’s name appears in serif bold: “Dr. Mara Kohn”, followed by a small sans-serif line in gray, “Hydrologist, Winter 2025 Fellow”. A short biography paragraph follows, describing her work on alpine water systems, written in the same clean sans-serif used elsewhere. The second researcher entry flips this arrangement: bio text on the left, portrait on the right—a man with a notebook seated beside a stone fireplace inside the lodge, warm lamplight casting a chiaroscuro effect. The third researcher appears again with image left, text right: a non-binary artist-philosopher sitting at a heavy wooden table covered in reference books, a large window behind them showing subtle morning light on peaks. Each portrait is framed with a thin white border and subtle drop shadow, resembling prints laid on the page.

Following the profiles, a quiet “Subscription” section explains the membership model with a more structured editorial layout that hints at pricing without resorting to typical SaaS-style cards. A small serif label, centered: “Subscription”. Under it, the main heading in charcoal serif: “Support the lodge. Receive the work.” A brief paragraph explains that readers can subscribe either as “Digital Readers” or “Patron Members”, emphasizing that both options fund residency stipends and maintenance of the off-grid library. These two options are displayed as two tall, narrow vertical blocks stacked one above the other (no side-by-side pricing tables), each spanning the width but distinguished by background tints. The “Digital Reader” block has a background of faint parchment texture, with the title “Digital Reader” in pine green serif, a single line in sans-serif: “Full access to all new field notes, seasonal reports, and the digital archive.”, and a price line in slightly larger sans-serif: “€8 / month, billed monthly.” In the bottom right corner of this block, a minimal sans-serif text link “Become a Digital Reader →” is placed. The “Patron Member” block below is similar, but with a slightly darker, warm cream background and a subtle vertical pine green rule along the left edge, like the spine of a book. It is titled “Patron Member” in rust serif, with a description: “Everything in Digital Reader, plus a quarterly printed digest, behind-the-scenes research dispatches, and your name in our annual lodge ledger.” The price line reads “€22 / month, billed monthly.” Below is a simple button-like element: a rectangular outline box with text “Become a Patron Member” in small caps, centered, pine green stroke, white background, hovering slightly above the background with a subtle drop shadow. A brief footnote in tiny light-gray sans-serif below both blocks explains: “We keep subscriptions simple: no tiers beyond these two, no discounts, no urgency. You can pause or cancel anytime.”

After this, a “Research log” section showcases the editorial side of the site’s output in a more text-dense manner. The section heading “From the lodge logbook” appears in serif, with a thin rust underline. Below, a vertical list of date-stamped entries resembles a typed index. Each entry is a single line in monospaced sans-serif (evoking typewritten logs), with the date and a short description, for example: “2025-01-18 · Snow depth at the north ridge surpassed last year’s maximum by 12 cm.”, “2025-01-12 · Library catalog updated: 37 new marginalia entries from the Autumn cohort.” The entries are left-aligned and evenly spaced, with faint dotted leader lines connecting the date block to the description, like a traditional table of contents. This section has a quiet, archival feel, and there are no glowing colors or flashy elements—just understated typography.

Near the bottom, a “How we work” section reads like a long-form editorial explanation of the lodge’s philosophy. A centered serif heading reads “On slow research and altitude.” Below it, a single, long column of text in multiple paragraphs, including at least one pull quote set off to the right within the column: a large, italic serif block the width of about half the column, reading: ““The lodge is less a place to escape the world than a height from which to see it more clearly.”” This pull quote is framed by a thin pine green rectangle and placed such that body text wraps around it on the left. Along the far left margin of this section, faintly, an upright rust-colored word appears vertically as if printed on the edge of a magazine page: “ESSAY”. This adds a subtle asymmetrical flourish that still honors the single-column concept.

Finally, the footer grounds the page with simple, bookish details. A horizontal pine green rule stretches the width of the main column, like the bottom border of a page. Below it, aligned left, small sans-serif text in gray reads: “Alpenglow Studies · A mountain lodge and research experiment in the Eastern Alps.” On the next line, in the same style, “Contact: hello@alpenglowstudies.org”. On the right side of the footer, still within the column, three understated text links in small uppercase sans-serif show: “Print Edition”, “Residency Application”, “Press Kit”. Under these links, in the far bottom right corner, a tiny serif line reads: “ISSN (forthcoming)”, as a wink to its journal-like identity. The entire page uses only static images as described, no animations or video, no charts or data visualizations—just composed typography, restrained photography, and careful spacing to evoke the experience of leafing through a carefully edited, alpine-themed research magazine that happens to live on the web.

instruction: Planning the generation details for the following website:
input:
Design a single-page portfolio website for a fictional Swiss-style graphic designer named "Lina Schaub", inspired by the visual language of music festival posters but executed with a strict Swiss/International Typographic Style. The overall aesthetic is minimal, grid-based, asymmetrically balanced, with an emphasis on typography, negative space, and a restrained but bold color palette. The page uses a three-column grid on desktop, with occasional elements spanning two or three columns to create rhythm. The background is pure white (#FFFFFF), margins are generous on all sides, and there is a subtle vertical rhythm with consistent spacing between sections. The primary colors are black (#000000) for text, a vibrant red (#E02020) as an accent, and light grey (#F3F3F3) panels to break sections. Typography is the hero: all text uses a single grotesk sans-serif typeface family, such as "Helvetica Neue" or a very similar Swiss-style grotesk, with different weights and sizes to create hierarchy. All text is left-aligned by default, with some right-aligned and centered elements used sparingly for contrast. The page design is strictly static: no videos, no animation, no motion.

At the very top is a clean fixed-height header bar spanning full width, sitting on the white background with a thin 1px light grey bottom border. On the left, aligned with the main grid, is the designer’s name in small uppercase: “LINA SCHAUB” in bold, letterspaced sans-serif, approximately 14px, black. To the right, aligned to the same baseline, are minimalist navigation links in regular-weight caps: “WORK”, “ABOUT”, “NOTES”, “CONTACT”. The nav items are spaced evenly with generous tracking and whitespace between them; they sit flush right within the grid. On hover (conceptually), they simply shift color from black to the red accent; there are no underlines or animations in the design description, just visual states.

Below the header, occupying the full width but structured within a three-column grid, is the hero section. This hero is split: the left two-thirds of the page (columns 1 and 2) contain text, while the right third (column 3) contains a vertically-oriented hero poster image that embodies Swiss music festival aesthetics. The hero text area on the left has a top margin that creates a clear breathing space from the header. At the top left in small uppercase red text is the label: “PORTFOLIO / VISUAL SYSTEMS”. Below it, in very large, bold, black sans-serif, spanning two lines, is the main title: “GRAPHIC DESIGN FOR LOUD MUSIC & QUIET SPACES”. The words “LOUD” and “QUIET” are on separate lines, with deliberate line breaks to create a visually pleasing block of text. The font size is large (e.g., 48–64px on desktop), making this the dominant visual element. Under the title, a medium-weight paragraph of introductory text, about 2–3 lines, in 16px regular, left-aligned, reads: “I design identities, posters, and visual systems for festivals, labels, and cultural institutions — relying on strict grids, sharp type, and unexpected color.” Below this paragraph are two text-based call-to-action links in uppercase: “VIEW SELECTED WORK” and “DOWNLOAD PDF PORTFOLIO”. These are set in small uppercase, 14px, black text. Each CTA is underlined with a hairline rule that aligns to the grid; when conceptual hover is described, the underline and text shift to red.

On the right third of the hero, the hero image is a static, poster-like image contained in a clean rectangular frame with no rounded corners, respecting the grid edges. It is a single tall image, with a slight drop shadow (very subtle, soft grey) to give it a printed-poster-on-wall feel. The image itself is a flat, graphic, Swiss-style festival poster mockup viewed straight-on, with no perspective distortion. It has a solid red background with black and white geometric elements: a large black rectangle intersecting with a white vertical stripe, some horizontal hairline rules, and bold sans-serif type arranged asymmetrically. The visible text on the poster includes: “ECHO FESTIVAL 2025” in bold uppercase near the top left, “ZÜRICH / BASEL” in smaller uppercase beneath, and a vertical line of text along the right edge reading “JUNE 12–14”. The typography is crisp, flat, and grid-aligned, with no textures or gradients. The poster is framed by white space, with the image not quite touching the section’s edges, reinforcing a sense of order and precision.

Directly under the hero, spanning full width but visually divided by the underlying grid, is a thin horizontal rule in light grey that extends from the left margin to the right margin, acting as a section separator. Below it, a section titled “SELECTED PROJECTS” begins. This projects section is laid out in a modular grid, with each project represented as a horizontal band that occupies the full width, but the internal composition uses two or three columns depending on the project. The section title “SELECTED PROJECTS” is in uppercase, medium-weight, 14px, red text aligned to the left and sits above a black hairline rule aligned with the first grid column. The projects are numbered using a Swiss editorial style: “01”, “02”, “03”, etc., in large light grey numerals in the background, partially overlapping the content area but very low opacity so the text remains readable. For each project row, the left two columns contain text, and the right column contains a static image thumbnail.

Project 01 has the label at the top left in uppercase black text: “PROJECT 01 / FESTIVAL IDENTITY”. Below it is the project title in larger bold black text, approximately 24px: “NEON FIELDS MUSIC FESTIVAL”. Underneath, a short 3-line description in regular-weight, 16px black text describes the project: “Comprehensive identity system for a three-day experimental music festival. Print, digital, and spatial applications, designed on a strict modular grid.” Below the description, small caps metadata in grey lists: “ROLE: ART DIRECTION, DESIGN” and “YEAR: 2024”, laid out side-by-side in the same line, separated by a thin vertical rule. On the right, within column 3, is a static rectangular image of the festival identity. The image shows a flat-lay arrangement of printed materials on a neutral light grey surface: an A2 poster, two flyers, and a festival wristband. The poster features an abstract geometric pattern of rectangles and lines in black, white, and red, with bold sans-serif typography reading “NEON FIELDS” in large letters. Everything is shot from a top-down perspective, evenly lit, with soft shadows beneath the paper to emphasize physicality. The color palette in the image matches the site’s: white, black, red, and light grey.

Project 02 follows directly below with consistent spacing. The structure is the same, but here the layout is slightly varied: the image occupies the left column and a half, and the text shifts to the right, spanning the remaining one and a half columns, introducing a subtle asymmetry while maintaining the shared grid. The project label reads: “PROJECT 02 / RECORD LABEL SYSTEM”. The bold project title reads: “MONOCHROME WAVES LABEL”. The description paragraph says: “Visual identity and packaging series for an independent electronic music label, using strict typographic grids and monochrome palettes.” Metadata line in grey: “ROLE: VISUAL IDENTITY, PACKAGING” and “YEAR: 2023”. The image shows a photorealistic, top-down arrangement of three vinyl record sleeves partially overlapping, each in different shades of grey with black and white typographic layouts. One sleeve has a large typographic block reading “MONOCHROME WAVES 03” with a vertical bar of small type listing track names. The background is again a neutral light grey, with soft, diffuse lighting and crisp edges.

Project 03 and 04, if present, follow in similar fashion, each row maintaining consistency but introducing minor shifts in alignment: one project might have the text aligned flush right within its column, another might have an image that spans all three columns but with text overlay in the bottom left corner in white on a dark rectangular type box. Always the style is Swiss: no decorative flourishes, just clean grids, sharp edges, and strong typographic hierarchy. One of these projects can be “PROJECT 03 / POSTER SERIES” with the title “POLYPHONIC NIGHTS” and a description: “Poster series exploring typographic rhythm for a weekly experimental music night.” The preview image shows three posters pinned side-by-side to an off-white wall, viewed straight-on, each with different bold compositions of black and red type on white background.

Beneath the projects section, there is a horizontal strip that functions as a simple “Services & Approach” segment, still in the context of a portfolio. This strip is divided into three equal columns, each with a title in small uppercase bold, and body text in regular weight. The left block is titled “SERVICES” with a list in sentence form, not bullets, reading: “Identity systems, poster design, editorial layout, and exhibition graphics for cultural clients.” The middle block is titled “APPROACH” with descriptive copy: “Every project begins with a grid. From there, color, type, and rhythm are tuned to the specific energy of the client, whether that’s a late-night festival or a quiet gallery.” The right block is titled “SELECTED CLIENTS” and lists names as a single paragraph: “Echo Festival, Kino Nord, Mono Press, Quiet Rooms, City of Zürich.” The text in each column is aligned to the baseline grid, with consistent leading and spacing. This entire strip sits on a very subtle light grey background (#F9F9F9) that spans full width, distinguishing it from the pure white main background. A thin red vertical rule between the columns acts as a subtle column separator, aligned exactly to the grid lines.

After this, there is an “About” section anchored by the heading “ABOUT LINA” in uppercase, black, 14px, aligned to the left with a black underline rule. The section occupies two columns of text on the left and a single column portrait image on the right. The portrait is a black-and-white photograph in a strict, minimalistic style: Lena is seated at a plain white desk, facing slightly away from the camera, with a grid-lined sketchbook and a stack of posters visible. The environment is bright and overexposed white, with slight soft shadows. She wears a plain black turtleneck, which contrasts with the white surroundings. The image is framed in a simple black keyline, again no rounded corners. The text in the About section is concise, in regular 16px black type: “Lina Schaub is a Swiss graphic designer based between Zürich and Basel. She works with festivals, labels, galleries, and institutions to create typographic systems that are both rigorous and expressive. Her work is informed by the Swiss International Style, contemporary music culture, and the physicality of print.” Below this, a secondary paragraph in slightly lighter grey mentions: “Available for new collaborations from September 2025.” To the side or beneath, a very minimal “CV / BIO” link appears in uppercase with a hairline underline.

Continuing down, there is a narrow “Notes” or “Journal” preview strip, giving the page depth without overcrowding. The heading “NOTES” appears in uppercase red text, with a black rule below. Three short entries are arranged in a three-column layout, each one containing a small date line in numerical European format (e.g., “12.03.2025”), a short title in bold black such as “On Grids and Noise”, and one sentence of preview text in regular grey: “Reflections on mixing strict typographic grids with chaotic music lineups.” The dates are in small uppercase, the titles around 18px, and the summaries in 14px. Each note entry has a fine grey border on the top only, giving a modular card feeling but keeping the aesthetic restrained. There are no thumbnails here, only text, emphasizing typographic hierarchy and whitespace.

Near the bottom of the page, there is a “Contact” section that is almost reminiscent of a ticket or form, bridging the music festival inspiration and Swiss clarity. The section background switches to a solid very pale grey (#F3F3F3) spanning full width, clearly differentiating it from the rest. The section title reads “CONTACT & AVAILABILITY” in uppercase black text, centered horizontally within the grid but the content beneath it is left-aligned. Beneath the title, aligned to the left, a short paragraph reads: “For commissions, collaborations, or portfolio requests, please get in touch.” Below that, the email address appears as a bold, large, black text line: “hello@linaschaub.ch” with a subtle red underline extending the width of the text. Under the email, a carefully structured, static contact info grid lists: “STUDIO” with the address “Limmatstrasse 23, 8005 Zürich” and “SOCIAL” with the lines “Instagram / Behance” written in uppercase, thin black. These are arranged in two columns in the lower part of this section. The emphasis remains on clean lines and organized information; there is no form, just direct contact details.

Finally, the footer is very minimal and aligned tightly with the grid. It sits on a white background again, with a thin top rule in light grey. On the left, in small grey text, it reads: “© 2025 Lina Schaub. All rights reserved.” On the right, aligned with the nav in the header, are two small links in uppercase grey text: “INFO” and “COOKIES”. The entire page keeps consistent margins and alignment, with all major elements snapping to a multi-column grid that is visually implied by the alignment of text blocks, images, and rules. There are no decorative icons, no gradients, and no animations. Every line, block, and piece of text feels intentional, echoing the legacy of Swiss graphic design while referencing the contemporary culture of music festivals and cultural work.

instruction: Planning the generation details for the following website:
input: Design a minimal, two-column-leaning dashboard homepage for a fictional luxury spa booking platform called “Opal Retreat,” with a calm, high-end, almost gallery-like aesthetic. The entire page sits on a soft, off-white background (#F7F6F3) with generous whitespace and a sense of quiet. All primary text uses a clean sans-serif font like “SF Pro Text” or “Inter,” with weights from Light to SemiBold, and headings use a refined, slightly condensed serif like “Cormorant Garamond” for an understated luxury feel. The main content is centered within a max-width container of approximately 1200px, with 64px padding on left and right and ample vertical spacing between sections. At the top is a fixed-height header bar that visually anchors the page but is visually light: a white background (#FFFFFF), 1px bottom border in a pale warm gray (#E2DED5), and a slight drop shadow with very low opacity to separate it from the background. The header height is about 72px. On the far left of the header, aligned with the main content, is the brand mark: the word “Opal” in a delicate serif font, with “Retreat” in small caps sans-serif beneath it, both in a soft charcoal (#2B2A28). To the left of the wordmark is a thin, circular line icon representing an abstract opal stone: a 24px diameter ring with a subtle gap, drawn in a muted gold (#C1A26A). Centered in the header (between left logo group and right controls) are the top-level navigation links, spaced out horizontally with 32px gaps: “Dashboard,” “Appointments,” “Spas,” “Members.” The currently active item “Dashboard” is shown in charcoal with a 2px rounded underline in muted gold; the other items are a medium gray (#78746C) with no underline, switching to charcoal on hover. On the right side of the header is a compact user area: a circular 32px avatar placeholder with a very light gray silhouette of a person on a white background and a thin border, to the right of which is the text “Welcome, Aurelia” in small sans-serif, and a tiny downward-facing chevron icon in gray, indicating a dropdown. Directly beneath the header, the hero/dashboard overview section occupies the top of the page, structured as a two-column layout with asymmetry: the left column about 55% width, the right about 45%, separated by 48px gap. The left column begins with a small, all-caps label in muted gold reading “OVERVIEW” above the main headline. Below it, the main headline reads “Your serene schedule, at a glance.” in a large serif font, around 32px, dark charcoal, left-aligned with a comfortable line height. Below the headline is a softer secondary line: “Review today’s bookings, favorite spas, and upcoming escapes in one quiet space.” in a smaller, light-weight sans-serif, medium gray, around 16px. Under this copy, still in the left column, is a slim horizontal strip containing three minimalist stat cards arranged in a row with equal spacing, each card having no visible border but a subtle shadow and slightly different width to create a natural visual rhythm. The leftmost stat card is titled “Today’s Appointments” (sans-serif, 12px all caps, letter-spaced, gray) with the main metric “3” in large, 28px numerals, charcoal, and a small subtext below in a lighter gray: “2 confirmed · 1 pending.” The middle card reads “This Week” with the main value “12 sessions” and subtext “8 massage · 4 facial.” The rightmost card shows “Favorites” with value “5 spas” and subtext “Curated just for you.” Each card’s background is a very subtle warm white panel (#FBFAF7) with 12px corner radius and 16px padding, and each has a faint vertical accent line on the left edge in muted gold, about 2px wide. The right column of this top section is a tall, minimal “Next Appointment” summary card that visually feels like a vertical tile. Its container has a white background, 16px radius, 24px internal padding, and a thin border (#E7E2D8). At its top, left-aligned, is the label “Next Appointment” in small caps, gray. Below, the appointment details: “10:30 AM · Today” in semi-bold sans-serif, slightly larger than body text, charcoal; then “Oceanstone Spa, Suite 14” on the next line. Under that, slightly indented, is a subtle horizontal line dividing details and notes. Beneath the line sits a short note text: “Deep tissue massage · 90 minutes” in medium gray, and a small pill-shaped status tag on the right with text “Confirmed” in tiny caps, white on a muted sage background (#AABFA6). At the far bottom of this card, aligned right, is a small text-only button “View details →” in muted gold, no border. Behind this right column card, in the upper right corner, a subtle, decorative, non-interactive image is placed, partially overlapping but not obstructing the card: a vertically oriented, softly lit, photorealistic photograph of a spa scene, about 260px wide and 360px tall, with rounded corners. The photo shows a close-up of a light wooden tray on a marble countertop. On the tray sits a pale ceramic bowl filled with smooth white stones, a rolled beige towel, and a small amber glass dropper bottle with a gold cap. The background is softly blurred, with hints of green plants. The color palette is muted neutrals and greens, lighting is diffused and warm, highlights subtle, shadows soft, giving a calm, luxurious feel. The photo has a tiny drop shadow and is slightly shifted upward and to the right from the appointment card, creating a layered effect. Below this hero section, the page transitions into the main dashboard body, which continues the two-column tendency with asymmetrical segments. The main body is split visually by a faint vertical guide (not a visible line, just alignment) dividing a wider left column and a narrower right column. The left column begins with a “Today’s Schedule” section, heading in serif: “Today’s schedule” (24px, charcoal) with a small gray line of text beneath: “A gentle overview of your upcoming guests.” Under the heading is a vertical stack of three simple schedule cards, each spanning most of the column width, with 16px vertical spacing between them. Each card uses a white background panel with 12px radius, thin border in #ECE6DC, and 16px padding. Inside each card, content is arranged horizontally: on the left a vertical time strip, in the center booking details, on the right minimal action. The first card’s left strip shows “10:30” in semi-bold, large digits, charcoal, with “AM” in small caps directly beneath in lighter gray; the second card shows “1:15 PM,” and the third “4:00 PM.” The middle content for each card displays the guest name in semi-bold: “Guest: Lila Moreno,” “Guest: Marcus Hale,” “Guest: Hana Ito,” respectively, with smaller subtext below like “Oceanstone Spa · Deep tissue massage · 90 min” and “Azure Springs · Aromatherapy facial · 60 min,” in a soft gray. On the right side of each card is a minimal pill-shaped tag indicating status: “Arriving soon,” “Confirmed,” “Awaiting confirmation,” each with different subtle background colors: a pale amber, sage, and cool gray respectively, with matching deeper text colors. No icons are used here; the aim is quiet clarity. Under the schedule section, still in the left column, is another content block labeled “Favorite spas” in the same serif style, with a brief line: “Quickly access your most-loved retreats.” Below, instead of a grid, three spa summary tiles are arranged horizontally, but the center tile is slightly taller than the others to introduce gentle asymmetry. Each tile is a white rectangular panel with 16px radius and 24px padding, and no border but a slight shadow; inside each, at the top, is a small, rectangular image thumbnail representing the spa, with rounded corners. The first thumbnail is a minimal flat illustration: a top-down view of a circular hot tub filled with light teal water, white steam lines rising, set on a pale beige background with a few abstract leaf shapes. The second thumbnail (the taller tile) is a more detailed flat-style illustration of a candlelit room: a massage table from a three-quarter perspective, covered in a white sheet, with a folded towel and a small bowl of stones on it; on the right, a floor lamp with a round shade casts a warm yellow glow, and the walls are a muted taupe. The third thumbnail shows a simple line-art bamboo stalk and leaves in soft green on off-white, very minimal. Below each thumbnail is the spa name in semi-bold sans-serif: “Oceanstone Spa,” “Azure Springs,” and “Juniper House,” with a one-line description beneath, like “Seaside-inspired rituals” or “Urban sanctuary with onsen suites,” in small gray text. At the bottom of each tile, aligned left, is a small, understated text link “View details” in muted gold, with no icon. The right column of the main body is narrower and organized into stacked panels. At the top is a compact search and filter block for quick booking. Its container has a white background, 16px radius, 24px padding, and a thin border. The section title reads “Quick booking” in serif, 18px, charcoal. Below, two minimalist input fields are stacked vertically with 12px spacing: the first field has a placeholder label “Search spa or treatment” and a faint magnifying glass icon on the left, drawn with a 1px line in gray; the second field is labeled “Preferred date” with a tiny calendar outline icon aligned to the right inside the field. Fields are simple rounded rectangles with no fill and a 1px border in light gray, padding inside around 10px. Beneath these fields, aligned right, is a small solid button reading “Find availability” in white text on muted gold, with 4px radius, 12px vertical padding, and 20px horizontal padding, and no icon. Below this quick booking card in the right column is a “Notes” panel for internal reminders. The title “Notes for today” appears in serif, with a subtle description beneath: “A private space for your thoughts and follow-ups.” Inside this panel is a large, light text area styled as a white box with pale border (#E7E2D8), 12px radius, and placeholder text in a very light gray: “Write a gentle reminder or a thoughtful follow-up message…” in a smaller, italic sans-serif. There is no visible scroll content; it looks like a single-page note area. At the bottom right of this panel, a tiny, ghost-style button “Save note” appears, just text in muted gold with a very faint underline appearing on hover. Anchoring the lower part of the right column, there is one more small summary card, slightly offset to break the grid: a panel titled “Highlights” with two or three bullet-like lines but rendered without traditional bullets—each line begins with a tiny, 4px muted-gold circle, then text in gray. Example lines: “Two new spas added this week,” “3 members booked repeat sessions,” “One unreviewed experience.” These lines are all in a small body font and left-aligned, with 8px vertical spacing, giving a compact but airy feel. The panel background is the same warm white as other cards, with no border but a subtle shadow, and it is slightly aligned inward compared to the panels above, creating a gentle stagger. The overall page avoids any heavy separators; section divisions are indicated by whitespace and subtle typographic hierarchy. No icons are used except simple line icons (magnifying glass, calendar, chevron) and small geometric dots; there are no animations, no hover movement described beyond optional underline color changes, and no charts, graphs, or data visualization elements anywhere. At the very bottom, a minimalist footer spans the full width but aligns to the main container: a single row of tiny text in medium gray, left-aligned: “© 2025 Opal Retreat. Designed for quiet clarity.” To the right side of this footer row, in the same line, are two text links separated by a subtle mid-dot: “Privacy” · “Terms,” both in muted gold. The entire design expresses a calm, minimal dashboard for managing spa bookings, with a restrained two-column layout that occasionally shifts height and alignment to maintain visual interest while preserving a sense of order and tranquility.

instruction: Planning the generation details for the following website:
input: Design a static landing page for an online “Swiss Grid Design Workshop” with a classic Swiss typography aesthetic: strict grid, bold asymmetry, large typographic elements, and minimal but impactful color. The overall layout uses a two-column structure with disciplined margins and gutters, but with intentional variations in column heights and blocks to create dynamic tension. The page is full-width, centered within the browser window, sitting on a pure off-white background (#F7F7F7). The core palette is black (#000000), white (#FFFFFF), and a single accent red (#E53935). All text uses a neo-grotesque sans-serif font family reminiscent of Helvetica, with tight leading and generous letter-spacing for large display text, and slightly tighter spacing for body copy. The entire page feels like a poster translated into web form—no gradients, no shadows, no rounded corners, no decorative elements beyond grid lines and color blocks, and absolutely no animation or video. At the very top, a fixed-height header spans full width with a white background. It is divided into two main columns: on the left, the logo and site name; on the right, the navigation and a small accent label. The header content is contained within a central grid of 12 equal columns, offset by large left and right margins. In the left column block, aligned to the left, is a simple typographic logo: a small red square (flat color, 16x16px) to the immediate left of the uppercase text “GRID SCHOOL” set in bold, black, all-caps, tracking slightly expanded, about 18px. The square and text are baseline-aligned. To the right, occupying the last 6 columns, the navigation is right-aligned, with three links set in uppercase, 13px, neutral weight, black: “WORKSHOPS”, “ABOUT”, “LOGIN”, evenly spaced horizontally with generous breathing room. At the far right, slightly below the nav links, is a tiny pill-like text (but with square corners) reading “ONLINE” in red uppercase, 10px, with a very thin 1px black border, padding left and right 6px, top and bottom 2px; it sits as a quiet label rather than a button. Below the header, the hero section occupies almost a full viewport height and uses the two-column structure prominently. The left two-thirds of the content area has a stark, bold typographic block; the right one-third is a vertical composition of a static image placeholder and information. The left hero column sits on the off-white background with no image, only typography. At the top of the left hero column, a small uppercase label in red (#E53935) reads “LIVE ONLINE WORKSHOP” in 12px, left-aligned, with wide letter-spacing. Directly beneath, a huge headline occupies three lines, left-aligned, in black: “MASTER SWISS GRID / FOR DIGITAL LAYOUTS / IN 4 WEEKS”. Each line is set in ultra-bold, 52–60px, tight leading so the lines are close but not touching, and the slash characters are not visible; instead, the line breaks are natural. The entire block feels like a poster headline, aligned to a strict vertical axis. Below the headline, a thin horizontal black rule spans about one-third of the column width, starting at the same left edge as the text. Under the rule is a short subheading in smaller black text, 15px, regular weight, reading: “Learn the rigor of Swiss typography and grid systems, and apply them to modern web and product design. No decorative fluff. Just structure, type, and clarity.” The text is left-aligned with a comfortable line height. Below this subheading, there is a pair of horizontally aligned buttons. The primary call-to-action button is on the left: a solid red rectangle with sharp corners, about 160px wide, 40px tall, containing uppercase white text “JOIN THE NEXT COHORT”. The type is 12px bold, centered both vertically and horizontally. To its right, separated by 24px, is a secondary link-style button: just a thin black rectangular outline of the same size, with uppercase black text “DOWNLOAD SYLLABUS”, 12px bold, centered. On hover (described as a visual style, but static in the mock), both buttons would simply invert colors (primary: red border/white fill, black text; secondary: black fill/white text), but for the design here they are shown in their default state. The right hero column is subdivided vertically. The top segment holds a tall static hero image placeholder with a 3:4 aspect ratio, aligned to the right edge of the grid but with a slight inset margin from the top of the hero. The image is strictly geometric: a flat, minimalist illustration that encapsulates Swiss design principles. It shows a flat white poster pinned to a light gray (#EEEEEE) wall. The poster is vertically oriented and composed from a frontal, orthogonal viewpoint. On the poster, there is a black grid of thin lines: vertical and horizontal lines forming a 4×6 grid. In the upper left of the poster, in bold black, all-caps, is the word “BASELINE” aligned to a grid intersection. Across the lower portion of the poster, several red rectangles (flat, no gradient, no shadow) are placed strictly on the grid lines, creating an abstract yet structural composition. There are no human figures, no perspective distortions, only flat geometry. The lighting is soft and even, no visible shadows, consistent with a minimal studio setting. Below this image in the same right column, there is a structured text block resembling a narrow info card. It has a white background and a black 1px border that aligns with the grid. Inside, the content is left-aligned but constrained to the narrow width. At the top of this card is a line of small uppercase black text, 10px, reading “NEXT SESSION”. Below it, a larger, 18px bold black line reads “Starts April 08, 2026”. Beneath that, in a three-row list, each row is separated by a faint gray horizontal rule. Row 1: “Duration” in 11px uppercase gray (#888888) with “4 weeks · 2 sessions/week” in 13px black aligned to the right of the row. Row 2: “Format” with “Live + Recorded” in the same style. Row 3: “Level” with “Intermediate” aligned right. At the bottom of the card, aligned left, is a small red text “Spots limited to 24 designers” in 11px, regular weight. Below the hero section, a horizontal strip of white space acts as a buffer, followed by the main content region which still obeys the two-column idea but plays with asymmetry. The next section is titled “WHAT YOU WILL MASTER” and spans both columns, but the content is arranged into a left column of text and a right column of structural highlights. At the top left of this section, anchored to the grid, a red, all-caps section label in 11px reads “CURRICULUM OVERVIEW”, followed by a thin black rule below it. Directly under the rule, the section title in black: “What you will master” in mixed case, 24px, medium weight, left-aligned. Beneath this, the left column of this section contains three stacked typographic feature blocks with generous vertical spacing. Each block begins with a small black uppercase title (14px bold) and is followed by a few lines of light-weight body text. The first block title is “GRID SYSTEMS FOR SCREENS”. The description beneath, in 14px regular, reads: “Build adaptable column systems for responsive layouts, from simple 2-column structures to complex modular grids. Learn how to keep rhythm across devices.” The second block title: “TYPE HIERARCHY & SCALE”, with descriptive text: “Use typographic scales, line lengths, and spacing to create clarity and tension. Understand when to align, when to offset, and how to use white space as a design element.” The third block title: “VISUAL REDUCTION & CONTRAST”, with text: “Learn to remove everything unnecessary. Focus on contrast of size, weight, and color to guide attention without decoration.” The right column of this section contrasts with the left: it is mostly white with thin black lines indicating invisible grid structure. At the top, aligned with the section title, there is a minimalist typographic block that looks like a technical spec: three short lines, each starting with a tiny black square bullet. The lines read, in uppercase 11px mono-spaced style: “BASE GRID: 8PX”, “COLUMNS: 12”, “GUTTER: 24PX”. Beneath this, taking most of the remaining height, is a static abstract diagram representing a grid layout: a white rectangle with a black outline. Inside, fine gray vertical lines suggest columns, and two bold rectangular areas in red occupy different columns—one wide horizontal bar across the top third, another narrower vertical bar on the right lower third. This conveys asymmetrical balance. The diagram has no text inside; it's purely structural. Further down, a “WHO THIS IS FOR” section returns to a strict two-column composition but with unequal heights. A thin black horizontal line resets the section, spanning from the left margin across about 60% of the width. Above it, aligned to the left, a small black uppercase label in 11px reads “WHO IT’S FOR”. Below the line, the left column lists three short profiles, each in bold black, 14px uppercase headings followed by 13px regular descriptive lines. The headings are “PRODUCT DESIGNERS”, “BRAND DESIGNERS”, and “DEVELOPERS WITH AN EYE FOR TYPE”. Each has one sentence of description, for example under “PRODUCT DESIGNERS”: “Clarify your UI layouts and typography systems to ship cleaner interfaces faster.” On the right side, aligned to the top of this section, is a tall red vertical bar that spans from just under the label down through the section. On top of this red bar, white uppercase text, rotated vertically 90 degrees (described as rotated but rendered simply as vertical stacking in text): “STRUCTURE OVER STYLE”. The bar feels like a strong visual anchor tying the left and right sections. There is intentionally more white space on the right aside from this bar, emphasizing asymmetry. Below this, the page features a section that looks like a simple “module board” of what’s inside the workshop, structured strictly in grids without images. The section title, spanning both columns, reads “Inside the 4-week workshop” in mixed case, 24px black, left-aligned and sitting slightly into the left column. Beneath it, the content is arranged into a two-column grid of four rectangular modules (two per column), each with a black border, equal height, and generous padding. Each module is a static white card with a small red uppercase label at the top left (“WEEK 01”, “WEEK 02”, etc.), a bold black title (16px, short phrase), and two or three lines of smaller body text describing the focus. For instance, the first module’s label: “WEEK 01” in red 11px, title: “Foundations of Swiss Typography”, and description: “History, core principles, and how to translate analog poster thinking into responsive UI layouts.” These cards align rigorously, with perfectly equal spacing between them, emphasizing the underlying grid. Continuing down, the testimonial section deviates slightly from the rest by using full-width text but still respects the grid. This section is on a pure white background strip, running edge-to-edge horizontally. At the top left, black uppercase text 11px reads “PAST PARTICIPANTS”. Below, there is a single large pull quote in black, 20px, italicized (still in sans serif), spanning both columns but confined to the center width: ““This workshop completely changed how I structure interfaces. It feels like designing posters again—just on screens.”” Beneath the quote, aligned left under it, the attribution: “— Lena M., Product Designer at Formline” in 13px gray. On the right side of this section, aligned vertically with the quote but within the right column, is a small minimalist list of logos represented purely by text: three uppercase names in gray, evenly spaced vertically: “FORMA”, “NORTHSIDE STUDIO”, “PIXEL & GRID”. Each is all uppercase, slightly tracked out, and sits as though it were a text-only logo. Next, a concise section titled “Your instructor” occupies another two-column layout. In the left column, there is a black-and-white static portrait placeholder: a square frame with a thin black border, showing a stylized, high-contrast, flat-vector portrait of a person. The portrait is head-and-shoulders, facing slightly off-center to the right, with simplified shapes: black hair, gray midtones, white background, crisp edges, no gradients. The face is gender-neutral, with no intricate details, just clear graphic shapes. To the right of the portrait, still in the left column but offset, is the instructor’s name and bio. The name is in bold black 18px: “TALIA VOGEL”. Below that, a smaller uppercase label in red reads “TYPE & INTERFACE DESIGNER”. Underneath, in 14px regular, two short paragraphs: “Talia is a Swiss-trained designer working at the intersection of interfaces and editorial systems. She has led typography workshops in Zürich, Berlin, and online.” followed by “Her practice centers on clarity, structure, and the discipline of reduction—translating classic typographic thinking into modern product teams.” The right column for this section contains a minimalist list of credentials formatted like a vertical table: three rows with small uppercase label in gray (10px) on top and larger black text (13px) below: “EXPERIENCE” / “10+ years in digital product and editorial design”, “SELECT CLIENTS” / “Studio M, Frameworks, Urban Atlas”, and “FOCUS” / “Design systems, typographic grids, and information clarity.” Each block is separated by thin, full-width, light gray horizontal lines. Finally, the bottom of the page features a strong closing call-to-action and a strictly typographic footer. The CTA section returns to a more dramatic use of red. A wide horizontal band spans the full width of the main content area, with a red background (#E53935). Within it, the layout is two-column again but with the left column mostly text and the right column a simple checklist. In the left column, large white uppercase text, 26px, reads: “Ready to design with discipline?” Beneath it, smaller white 14px text: “Apply Swiss grid principles to every interface you touch. Join the next cohort and redesign your process.” Below this, there is a single primary CTA button in white with a black border, sharp corners, containing black uppercase text: “APPLY NOW”. To its right, as text-only, a white underlined link: “View syllabus (PDF)” in 12px uppercase. In the right column of this red band, aligned top, is a minimalist three-item checklist of what’s included. Each item begins with a small hollow square (white outline) as a pseudo-checkbox, followed by white text “Live weekly critique sessions”, “Recorded replays for all sessions”, and “Practical grid exercises with feedback”. At the very bottom, beneath the red CTA band, the footer returns to the off-white background and is relatively quiet and compact. A thin black line separates the footer from the content above. The footer is again split into two columns. On the left, small black text in 11px reads: “© 2026 Grid School. Built on Swiss principles.” On the right, aligned to the right edge, small uppercase links in gray (#777777) read “TERMS”, “PRIVACY”, and “CONTACT”, separated by thin vertical black lines. The entire page uses strictly rectangular elements, straight lines, and flat colors, with a strong typographic hierarchy and a clear two-column grid that occasionally flexes for asymmetry. There are no animated elements, no motion, and no charts or data visualization—only static, structural, text-forward content and minimal graphic abstractions that reflect Swiss graphic design sensibilities.

instruction: Planning the generation details for the following website:
input: Design a clean, rigorously structured homepage for an online coding tutorial platform titled “GRIDCODE.STUDIO”, rendered in a stark Swiss-style typography aesthetic. The page sits on a pure white background with a strict multi-column grid, using consistent vertical and horizontal gutters. The primary palette is black (#000000), white (#FFFFFF), medium neutral gray (#CCCCCC), and a single accent color: a vivid red (#E62020). Typography is entirely sans-serif, using one main family in different weights: think of a Grotesk typeface. Headings use a bold, all-caps style with tight letter-spacing; body text is regular weight with generous line-height. All text is left-aligned, with rare, purposeful right alignment to create tension and asymmetry. The whole page is framed by a 40px white margin on large screens, with a thin, light gray 1px rule bordering the content area, giving the impression of a poster-like layout. At the very top is a fixed-height header bar (about 80px high) running full width, background white with a thin 1px black bottom border. On the left of this header, aligned to the grid, the logo text “GRIDCODE.STUDIO” appears in uppercase, bold, condensed type, black color, slightly larger than body text. To the right, spaced evenly and aligned to a subtle 12-column grid, are the navigation links: “COURSES”, “TRACKS”, “BLOG”, “PRICING”, “ABOUT”. Each nav item is in uppercase, medium weight, small size, black text, with generous white space between them. On the far right, there are two compact buttons: first a text link “Log in” in regular black, then a pill-shaped outlined button with text “Start Free Trial” in black, with a 1px black border and 6px border-radius; on hover (to be described visually), the button fills with red and the text becomes white. No icons appear in the header; the look is purely typographic. Below the header, the main body is divided into a Swiss-inspired asymmetric grid where large typographic blocks, monochrome imagery, and structured sections create tension and hierarchy. The top “hero” section occupies the first visible screen height and is horizontally divided into two main columns with different widths: the left column takes about 55% of the width, the right 45%. In the left hero column, at the top, a bold, large, all-caps headline in black reads: “WRITE CODE. DESIGN SYSTEMS. THINK IN GRIDS.” Each phrase is set on its own line, with tight line spacing, and the words “CODE”, “SYSTEMS”, and “GRIDS” are highlighted by placing a thin red underline under each of those words only—a precise, horizontal line that aligns with the grid. Below the headline, separated by 20px of whitespace, a smaller, regular weight paragraph introduces the platform: “Gridcode.Studio is a structured online coding tutorial platform for designers, developers, and visual thinkers. Learn modern JavaScript, CSS, and frontend architecture through concise lessons inspired by Swiss typography and systematic design.” This text is black, left-aligned, with generous line-height. Under this paragraph sits a small, two-line label in uppercase, red text: “FOCUSED. RIGOROUS. PRACTICAL.” The label uses a smaller font size and extra letter-spacing, aligned with the left edge of the paragraph. Below this, horizontally aligned, are two primary call-to-action buttons. The first button, on the left, is a solid black rectangle with sharp 90-degree corners, containing white uppercase text: “BROWSE COURSES”. The second button, slightly to the right with a 16px gap, is an outlined rectangle with a 1px black border and white fill, containing black uppercase text: “VIEW LEARNING TRACKS”. Both buttons have equal height, with ample internal padding and crisp edges. Underneath the buttons is a very small line of text in gray: “No credit card required · Cancel anytime”, using a condensed, tiny, uppercase style. The right hero column features a large, vertically oriented, monochrome image boxed in a neat, thin black border with clear margin from the text. The image is a flat, high-contrast illustration that mimics a Swiss poster: on a white background, black rectangles and thin horizontal and vertical lines suggest a stylized browser window and code editor. The top part of the illustration shows a simplified browser header: three small circles (black outlines, white fill) in the upper left to suggest window controls, and a long horizontal rectangle beneath them representing an address bar. Below this, the main part of the illustration is divided into two vertical panels by a thick black line: on the left, a column of evenly spaced short horizontal black bars represent lines of code; on the right, a grid of thin gray lines forms a layout mockup, with some cells filled with black rectangles to suggest components. A few small red accent blocks (simple red rectangles) are placed sparingly within the grid to add color and visual rhythm, echoing the red accent color used in the text. The style is very flat, with no gradients and minimal shading, like a screen-printed poster. Below this main illustration, still within the right column, sits a compact typographic block: a small red uppercase label, “STRUCTURED LEARNING PATHS”, followed by a short 2-line black description in regular weight: “Courses are organized into visual tracks. Move from fundamentals to advanced topics without losing orientation.” The hero section ends with a thin, horizontal black rule at the bottom, stretching full width, perfectly aligned with the grid. Immediately beneath the hero, the page transitions into a three-column section titled “Featured Tracks”. The title “FEATURED TRACKS” is set flush left, all caps, bold, black, small size, and appears above the left column only, with a horizontal red line beneath it spanning about one-third of the page width. Each of the three columns beneath contains a vertically stacked card. The cards share a consistent structure and adhere to strict alignment but vary slightly in height for subtle asymmetry. Each card has a white background with a very thin gray border, squared corners, and generous interior padding. At the top of each card sits a small horizontal typographic tag in uppercase, red text indicating the difficulty level: e.g., “BEGINNER”, “INTERMEDIATE”, or “ADVANCED”. Below each tag is the track title in bold, black, uppercase: for instance, “JAVASCRIPT FOUNDATIONS”, “CSS LAYOUT SYSTEMS”, and “DESIGNER’S GUIDE TO FRONTEND”. The titles are slightly larger than body text. Under each title is a short black description, two to three lines long, written in sentence case: “Build core JavaScript skills with concise, example-driven lessons focused on clarity and structure.” For the CSS card: “Master Flexbox and Grid through a series of layout studies inspired by Swiss poster design.” For the design/frontend card: “Translate visual systems into code with a focus on typography, rhythm, and responsive grids.” At the bottom of each card, aligned to the grid, are two small pieces of meta-text arranged horizontally: black text like “12 lessons” and “3h 20m total”, separated by a centered dot. On the far right edge of each card, aligned vertically, a tiny black right-pointing triangle icon (pure geometric shape formed by a black triangle) implies forward movement; it is not decorative but functional, positioned in the midline of the card. The next section introduces “Course Library” in a more dense, modular grid, emphasizing the multi-column layout and Swiss-style rigor. This section spans the full width beneath “Featured Tracks” and is split into two main vertical regions: a narrow left-side vertical strip (about 20% width) and a wide multi-column right region (about 80% width). In the left strip, the heading “COURSE LIBRARY” appears in large uppercase black text, rotated 90 degrees counter-clockwise, running vertically up the side, centered in the strip.
