The overall aesthetic is harsh, minimal, and intentionally ugly, with strong vertical and horizontal alignments occasionally broken by misaligned borders and overlapping elements, emphasizing the brutalist portfolio nature of the independent artist tutorial homepage.

instruction: Planning the generation details for the following website:
input:
Design a single long-scroll homepage for a research-focused website called “Pastel Praxis Lab,” using a soft pastel aesthetic and a multi-column grid layout with gentle asymmetries. The overall feeling should be calm, intellectual, and inviting, combining the structure of a digital design agency homepage with the warmth of a board game café and the precision of a research studio. The page background is a very light, almost white, warm cream (#FFFDF8) with faint, barely-there watercolor-like blotches in soft mint, blush pink, and lavender in the corners, giving subtle texture without distracting from the content. The primary accent colors are a muted dusty lavender (#C0A7D8), a soft sky blue (#A7C8E8), pale sage (#C7DEC9), and a blush coral (#F8C7C7). Typography uses one elegant serif for headings, “Playfair Display” or similar, in a dark slate gray (#2A2A33), and a clean geometric sans-serif like “Inter” for body text in a soft charcoal gray (#404048). All text should be crisp and left-aligned where paragraphs appear, but headings and small labels can be centered in some sections for visual variety. The layout across the whole page uses a flexible 12-column grid with generous white space, and sections often use 2 or 3 columns with uneven widths (like 7/5 or 8/4) to achieve creative asymmetry. All corners of cards, buttons, and images are slightly rounded (8–12px radius), and subtle soft shadows (very diffuse, 15–25px blur, low opacity) give depth without harshness.

At the very top, a very slim, full-width pastel gradient bar (about 40px tall) fades from left to right in a soft gradient: pale lavender → mint → blush, with a tiny centered line of text in a light sans-serif, all caps, in medium gray: “NEW: Read our 2025 ‘Soft Systems in Everyday Design’ report →”. This text serves as a clickable banner, underlined with a dotted pastel line when hovered. Below this, the main header area consists of a transparent navigation bar overlaying a softly colored hero background. The nav bar spans the full width, with a height of around 80px, and has a slightly translucent white backdrop (blurred glass effect) so the hero background colors gently show through. On the left, aligned with the main grid, is the logo: a small, flat illustration of three overlapping pastel circles (lavender, mint, peach) forming a Venn diagram with a tiny central white star, next to the wordmark “Pastel Praxis Lab” in a dark slate serif font. On the right, navigation links sit in a horizontal row: “Home,” “Research Themes,” “Methods,” “Field Notes,” “Our Team,” “Contact.” Each link is set in the sans-serif, medium weight, small caps, with letterspacing, in dark gray. Hovering over a link would change its text color to soft lavender and add a very thin pastel underline. At the far right, separated by a thin vertical line in pale gray, is a pill-shaped button with rounded ends, filled with soft sky blue, labeled “Join a Study” in white uppercase text; when hovered, it slightly deepens in color and its shadow becomes marginally stronger.

The hero section immediately follows, occupying the full viewport height. The background features a gentle diagonal split: the left two-thirds is an ultra-soft gradient from cream to palest lavender, and the right third is a gentle offset block of very light mint with a layered collage-style illustration. On the left side, the main headline is large and set in the serif font, line-breaking across three lines: “Designing with / curiosity, / color, and care.” The line breaks are intentional. The words “curiosity,” “color,” and “care” each have a subtle pastel highlight behind them—soft rounded rectangles in lavender, coral, and mint respectively, with low opacity so the text remains easy to read. Below the headline, a short paragraph in sans-serif reads: “Pastel Praxis Lab is a research collective exploring how gentle interfaces, playful tools, and thoughtful systems can make digital life kinder and more meaningful.” Beneath this paragraph, there are two horizontal buttons aligned side-by-side with small space between. The primary button is a rounded rectangle filled with dusty lavender, labeled “Browse Our Studies” in white, medium-weight sans-serif text; the secondary button is an outlined style with a thin lavender stroke and cream fill, labeled “Download Overview (PDF)” in lavender text. To the left of these buttons, in smaller text, is a line with a soft gray icon of a small magnifying glass and the phrase “Open methods, open findings” in lowercase, with the icon and text slightly aligned to the baseline.

On the right side of the hero, there is a large, stylized illustration representing collaborative research. It is a flat, semi-isometric scene in pastel colors. The composition shows a soft, rounded rectangular workspace table in pale sage, viewed from an angled top-down perspective. Around the table are three faceless, simplified characters with rounded shapes and minimal features: one in a pastel blue sweater, one in a lavender hoodie, and one in a peach cardigan. They are seated on puff-like stools in complementary colors. On the table, there are scattered elements: stacks of pastel sticky notes, a small open laptop with a blank, light blue screen, a tablet showing a grid of colored blocks, a pair of white headphones, and a cylindrical pencil cup with pastel pens. Background elements include faintly drawn grids, floating sticky-note shapes, and light dotted lines connecting them, suggesting networks of ideas. The lighting is soft and even, with gentle shadows beneath the table and characters. In the top-right corner of the hero illustration, a small, circular badge overlaps the border slightly; it has a white center, thin coral border, and tiny sans-serif text: “Since 2018” in gray. Below the hero illustration, aligned to its bottom-right, there is a small vertically oriented label that reads “Scroll to explore ↓” in a narrow, uppercase sans-serif, with a tiny down arrow icon in muted pastel gray.

Beneath the hero, a horizontal strip acts as a “client logos”–style trust band, though here it shows types of communities the lab works with. It has a slightly darker cream background and a thin top and bottom border in ultra-light gray. At the center, a small, uppercase label says “We collaborate with” in light gray. Below, five monochrome pastel icons (line art style) spaced evenly across the width: a stylized university building, a small NGO house with a heart icon, a minimalist city government building, a tiny co-op café cup, and a laptop with a heart. Under each icon is a short line of text like “Universities,” “Nonprofits,” “Public Sector,” “Neighborhood Labs,” “Product Teams,” all in a soft gray, single line.

The next section introduces “Research Themes” in a three-column grid of cards with slight asymmetry in height and content. At the top-left of this section, aligned to the grid, a small label in coral says “Research Themes” in uppercase, and below it a larger serif heading reads: “What we’re exploring right now.” The background here is still cream but with a faint, almost ghost-like pattern of overlapping circles in very light pastel, reminiscent of the logo. Below the heading, three pastel cards sit side by side, each occupying about one-third of the width with consistent gaps. Each card has a soft, different pastel background: card 1 in pale lavender, card 2 in pale mint, card 3 in pale blush. The first card is slightly taller than the others, creating a gentle mismatched height variation. On each card, at the top, there is a small icon: Card 1 has a simple line-art icon of overlapping chat bubbles; Card 2 shows a rounded app window with sliders; Card 3 depicts a simple sequence of three rounded shapes in a path representing “journey.” Card titles use serif font, slightly larger, such as: “Playful Patterns in Everyday Tools,” “Gentle Interfaces for Complex Tasks,” “Community Rituals & Shared Space.” Beneath each title is a short, two- to three-line paragraph: “We investigate how playful micro-interactions can reduce friction and build trust in day-to-day digital tools.”, “We prototype soft, forgiving interfaces for workflows that are often rigid and intimidating.”, “We observe how communities gather, share, and co-create meaning both online and offline.” At the bottom of each card, a tiny tag row appears: pill-shaped tags in white with pastel text, such as “qualitative,” “co-design,” “prototyping,” “fieldwork,” each with tiny text. Hovering over a card would slightly lift it and deepen its shadow.

Continuing down, the “Methods” section uses an asymmetrical two-column layout that extends horizontally edge-to-edge. The left side is roughly 60% width, the right 40%. The background is split: the left panel is very pale sky blue, and the right panel is a nearly white sage. On the left, a heading in serif reads: “How we work.” Below it, three horizontal “method” rows are stacked with generous vertical spacing, each row containing a small pastel circular icon, a sub-heading, and a couple of lines of body text. The icons are line art: a notebook with a pencil (for “Field Interviews”), overlapping squares (for “Interface Prototyping”), and a tiny group of dots orbiting a central dot (for “Participatory Mapping”). The sub-headings are in a slightly bold sans-serif, like “Field Interviews,” followed by explanatory text such as: “We sit with people in their context—homes, studios, cafés—to understand how tools fit into their lives.” On the right panel, a vertical card floats, with a subtle drop shadow, containing a short, featured small block titled “Open Methods Library.” This card has a white background, a slim lavender border, and a small icon of an open book with colored tabs. The text inside says: “Download templates, prompts, and session plans used in our recent studies.” Below it, a small pastel button with only an outline and text “View Methods Library” sits centered.

Next comes a section that nods to the board game café inspiration while staying research-focused: “Field Kits & Playtables.” This section returns to the cream background but with a faint, soft diagonal pattern of very light, semi-transparent rectangles resembling cards or tiles. The heading in serif, centered this time, reads: “Field Kits & Playtables.” Underneath is a short centered line: “Tangible tools for messy questions.” Below, a staggered 3-column arrangement shows three product-like cards but they’re conceptual kits. Each card has a thin outline in a pastel color and a white interior. At the top of each, there is a photorealistic yet pastel-toned image: Card 1 shows a tabletop scene from a slightly elevated angle, in natural soft daylight, with scattered pastel-colored index cards, a pair of pastel highlighters, and a ceramic mug with tea in a muted blue cup; Card 2 shows a small fabric pouch in dusty pink spilling out wooden tokens in pastel colors, each token embossed with tiny icons like hearts, stars, and speech bubbles, on a smooth light birch table; Card 3 shows a spiral-bound workbook with a pastel cover, half open, with a mechanical pencil laying across, and faint printed frameworks visible on the page—boxes, arrows, circles. The photorealistic images are gently desaturated for softness. Beneath each image, there is a title like “Conversation Constellations Deck,” “Consent & Comfort Token Set,” “Reflection Workbook for Teams,” and a one-sentence description each, in sans-serif. Under the description, a small link text in pastel (“See how we use this kit →”) is aligned left.

Further down, there is a section titled “Current Studies” that uses a more structured multi-column grid. The section background is a very pale lavender wash, with upper corners slightly darker. At the top-left, the label “Current Studies” appears in coral uppercase, and below it a serif heading says: “Join a study or follow along.” Beneath, a 2-column layout displays two larger cards on the left column stacked vertically and one tall, detailed card on the right. Each card features a small pastel tag centered at the top that indicates status like “Recruiting,” “Full,” “In Analysis,” using different pastel backgrounds. For example, the first card on the left might show “Recruiting” on a mint background. Card 1 title: “Gentle Onboarding for Creative Software.” A short description: “Exploring how welcoming flows can reduce anxiety for new users of complex tools.” Below it, two lines show details in a small, icon-accompanied style: a calendar icon with “Sessions: March–May 2025,” and a location pin icon with “Hybrid: remote + on-site in Berlin.” A subtle “Learn more →” link appears at bottom-right of the card. Card 2 might be titled “Rituals of Check-In in Distributed Teams” with similar structure. The right tall card is a featured study with more space; at the top a pastel-blue tag “Featured Study,” the title “Soft Edges in Productivity Apps,” then a short paragraph, and a very simple monochrome illustration: a phone and tablet overlapping, their screens showing abstract pill-shaped UI elements. At the bottom of this tall card is a full-width mint button labeled “Request Participation Info.”

The “Field Notes” section that follows is styled like a modern blog or notebook. The background reverts to cream, and a horizontal pastel divider line (a soft gradient line from lavender to mint) separates it from the previous section. The heading, left-aligned, reads: “Field Notes & Essays.” To the right of this heading, there’s a small, pill-shaped toggle element with two labels: “Latest” and “Popular.” The currently active one (“Latest”) is filled with soft lavender; the other is just outlined. Below, a masonry-like two-column layout lists 4–6 cards of varying height. Each card is a white rectangle with a faint shadow, with the top featuring a rectangular image and the bottom showing title, metadata, and a snippet. One card’s image: a stylized flat design cityscape at dawn, rendered in pastel hues, with soft blocky buildings and a few rounded windows glowing softly. Another card’s image: a top-down view of a desk with scattered sticky notes forming a rough circle; another: a closeup of a hand holding a pastel marker writing on a large sheet of paper. Titles might include: “What We Learned from Bringing Story Dice into User Interviews,” “On the Quiet Power of Check-In Rounds,” “Three Ways to Make Research Debriefs Less Exhausting.” Under each title, a thin sans-serif line shows author name (e.g., “By Mina Cho”) and date. The snippet text is 2–3 lines of lighter gray body text. At the bottom of the section, centered, a small, subtle ghost-button reads “View all field notes.”

After this, a “Team” section introduces people in a playful, grid format. The background is tinted with an extremely light sage gradient from top to bottom. At the top-center, a small serif heading says “Our Team,” and underneath it, in sans-serif, “Researchers, designers, and facilitators with a shared love of pastels and good questions.” Then a 3-column grid of circular headshot cards appears. Each person’s card is vertically stacked: a circular portrait photo at the top, their name in bold sans-serif below, their role in smaller text, and a single line “Research interest:” followed by a phrase in italic. The portraits are stylized photographs treated with a pastel overlay and slight grain, showing diverse ages, genders, and ethnic backgrounds, all in casual attire. The backgrounds of portraits are solid pastel colors that contrast with the subject’s clothing. Below the three primary team members, there is a thinner row with 3 smaller circular avatars for collaborators, each with just a name below. A faint decorative motif of line-art shapes—abstract squiggles, stars, and dots in very light gray—appears behind the grid, anchored to the bottom-left and top-right corners.

Following the team section is a horizontal, narrow band called “Lab Values.” It uses a centered layout with three phrases separated by vertical lines in light gray: “Care over speed,” “Shared learning,” “Playful rigor.” Each phrase is in small caps serif, and hovering over each would reveal a short tooltip (though not displayed as text on the page, visually implied by a faint underline and a tiny “i” icon).

Closer to the bottom, the “Stay in touch” section acts as a contact and newsletter sign-up area. The background here is a delicate pastel gradient from very pale lavender at the top to cream at the bottom, with soft, almost invisible overlapping circles in the corners. On the left, a heading reads, “Stay in touch,” and beneath it a short paragraph: “We share study invitations, gentle tools, and reflections a few times a season. No noise, just notes.” Below this paragraph on the left side is a simple form: two fields side-by-side in wider screens (stacking on smaller widths): “Name” and “Email,” each with a white background, subtle border, and placeholder text in light gray. Below the fields is a small checkbox with label: “I’m open to being contacted about participating in studies.” The submit button is a medium-sized rounded rectangle in pastel coral, with white text: “Subscribe.” A microcopy note below the button says in very small text: “We’ll never share your information. Opt out anytime.” On the right side of this section is a minimal illustration: a flat design envelope in white with a mint flap, slightly opened, with pastel paper shapes (rectangles and circles) gently floating upward from it, all in the same color palette. A thin, soft shadow roots the envelope to the cream “ground.”

Finally, the footer spans the full width with a slightly darker cream background and a subtle top border line in pale gray. It is divided into three columns within the main grid. The first column shows a smaller version of the logo and the wordmark “Pastel Praxis Lab” stacked above a one-sentence descriptor in small text: “A small lab for big, gentle questions.” Below that, three small icons (monochrome pastel line art) link to social presences: a circle with “in” for LinkedIn, a ghost-like icon for a blog, and a small envelope for email. The icons are evenly spaced horizontally. The middle column has a list of text links stacked vertically: “Research Themes,” “Methods,” “Field Notes,” “Our Team,” “Contact,” each in sans-serif, small size, in mid-gray. The right column contains a short “Credits & Licensing” area: a small heading “Credits & Licensing,” followed by text: “Unless otherwise noted, our methods are shared under a Creative Commons Attribution-ShareAlike license.” Below that is a tiny text link “View licensing details →” in lavender. At the very bottom, centered, a slim line of small sans-serif text reads: “© 2025 Pastel Praxis Lab. Crafted with curiosity in Berlin and beyond.” The entire page maintains consistent vertical rhythm with generous spacing between sections and ensures the multi-column grid is evident but softened by occasional asymmetric card heights, varied column widths, and subtle overlay elements.

instruction: Planning the generation details for the following website:
input:
Design a stark, brutalist research homepage for an online “meta-book club” called “DISSECTING TEXTS” that teaches people how to run rigorous, research-driven book clubs. The page uses a strict, mostly two-column layout with intentional asymmetries, heavy typographic hierarchy, and very minimal color: pure white (#FFFFFF) background, deep black (#000000) text, and a single accent color—acid yellow (#FFF500) used for highlights and some borders. The entire page feels raw and constructed, with visible grid lines, harsh borders, and monospaced or grotesk fonts. Use a primary font that looks like a condensed grotesk (for headings) and a monospaced font (for body and code-like callouts). All text should appear exactly as quoted. At the very top, flush to the browser edges, there is a full-width horizontal strip about 60px tall with a white background and a 1px solid black bottom border. On the left of this strip, aligned with a left margin of 40px, the site title appears in harsh uppercase: “DISSECTING TEXTS / RESEARCH CLUB” in a narrow, bold grotesk font, about 18px, letter-spacing slightly expanded. Directly underneath this title, in much smaller monospaced text (10px, all caps) slightly indented, is a tagline: “BRUTALIST METHODOLOGIES FOR READING TOGETHER”. On the right side of this top bar, aligned with a right margin of 40px and bottom-aligned to the bar, is a horizontally arranged list of navigation links in monospaced uppercase text, spaced tightly with visible vertical separators: “ABOUT | METHODS | LIBRARY | LAB NOTES | CONTACT”. Each link appears as plain black text with no hover effect described, but each is separated by a thin 1px vertical line that reaches from the cap height of the text down to the baseline. Beneath this header bar, the rest of the page is visually divided into two main vertical columns spanning the viewport: a left column occupying roughly 40% width and a right column at 60%, with a fixed gap of 40px between them. A subtle 1px vertical black rule marks the boundary between the columns, but not as a continuous line; instead it is broken into 20px segments with 10px gaps, running down the page like a dotted pillar.

At the top of the content, in the left column, there is a blocky hero title occupying about 300px of vertical space. Against the white background, the words “HOW TO BUILD A RESEARCH-LEVEL BOOK CLUB” are stacked in four uneven lines, all uppercase, in an aggressive, bold grotesk font around 36–40px. Each word block is aligned to a rigid left edge, but the line breaks are slightly odd to create asymmetry: “HOW TO BUILD” on the first line, “A RESEARCH-LEVEL” on the second, “BOOK” on the third, and “CLUB” on the fourth. The third line “BOOK” is highlighted by being set inside an acid yellow rectangular block that stretches the full width of the text and extends 5px beyond the text on all sides, with a thin 1px black border, so “BOOK” appears as black text on yellow, while the other lines are plain black on white. Below this hero title, still in the left column, there is a very concise introductory paragraph in monospaced font, 12px, left-aligned, with a line height of about 1.6, reading: “This is a research site about the mechanics of reading, discussing, and documenting books in public. No cozy chairs. No cursive inspirational quotes. Only protocols, experiments, and detailed notes.” Beneath this paragraph, there is a black horizontal line spanning the width of the left column, 1px thick, with a label embedded in the line: at the far left, slightly interrupting the line, is a small acid yellow rectangle containing the word “VERSION 0.3” in tiny black monospaced text, 9px, all caps, as if stamped on.

Directly opposite this left hero, in the right column, there is a large static hero image that feels like a research board for a book club, rendered in a flat, high-contrast, graphic style—not photorealistic, more like a stylized architectural diagram. The image occupies the top of the right column and about 400–450px of height. The composition shows an overhead view of a brutalist workspace: a rectangular white table with thick black outlines sits slightly off-center, rotated a few degrees to break the grid. On the table are scattered rectangular “book objects” drawn as flat white blocks with black edges and no titles, a thick black marker pen, pages with grid lines and underlines, and sticky notes represented as acid yellow squares with black scribbles. The background of the image is a light gray concrete-like texture, with subtle speckling and a hint of darker gray shapes suggesting brutalist walls or slabs. Around the table, there are simple, boxy chairs drawn as black outlines, all empty, with harsh shadows cast to the right, emphasizing harsh overhead light. The perspective is a top-down axonometric view: no soft edges, just straight lines and hard angles. The lighting is stark with clear shadows and areas of empty white space. The entire image is framed with a simple 2px black border. No text appears inside the image; it is purely graphical.

Below the hero section, the page scrolls into the main content region, which continues the two-column structure but now with more intentional asymmetry. In the left column, under a generous 60px of white space from the hero area, there is a section titled “FIELD MANUAL” in uppercase bold grotesk, 18px, aligned left. Underneath this heading, there is a list of three main “protocols” arranged vertically, each inside a rectangular module that looks like a form field. Each module consists of a thin black rectangle full-width in the left column, with the top edge thicker (3px) than the other edges. Inside each rectangle, the protocol name sits at the top-left in monospaced uppercase text, 12px, followed by a short description line. The three protocol titles are exactly: “PROTOCOL 01: SELECTING TEXTS”, “PROTOCOL 02: STRUCTURING SESSIONS”, and “PROTOCOL 03: DOCUMENTING DISCUSSION”. Under each title, in smaller monospaced text (11px), is a single sentence. For the first: “How to pick books that generate friction, not consensus.” For the second: “Time-boxed segments, reading assignments, and silence quotas.” For the third: “From marginalia to public notes: exporting your findings.” Each module is separated by 16px of vertical space and has a very subtle acid yellow strip, 4px tall, attached to the top-right corner of the rectangle like a tab.

In the right column opposite “FIELD MANUAL,” there is a dense research excerpt block labeled “ABSTRACT”. At the top of this block is the heading “ABSTRACT” in uppercase bold grotesk, 18px, left-aligned with the right column’s inner margin. Below it, a large block of text is rendered in monospaced font, about 12px, justified so the text forms a clean rectangle. The text reads: “This project examines the book club as a collaborative research structure rather than a leisure activity. We collect, test, and refine small interventions—timers, scripts, constraints—that change how people read together. The goal is not better opinions about books, but better systems for thinking in public.” Around this text is a bold 2px black border, with the top border broken in one place where a small label appears: a small white rectangle overlapping the top border contains the text “WORKING PAPER” in black uppercase monospaced text, 9px, with a 1px black border. The entire “ABSTRACT” block sits slightly higher than the “FIELD MANUAL” in the left column, creating an asymmetrical z-line as the eye moves across the page.

Further down, the layout continues in two columns but with a large, obviously brutalist “index” section that looks like a research ledger. In the left column, after another 60px of vertical whitespace, there is a heading: “CURRENT EXPERIMENTS” in bold uppercase grotesk, 18px. Under this, instead of a normal list, there is a narrow grid resembling a table, but heavily stylized. Four rows represent different experimental book club projects, and three columns labelled “ID”, “TITLE”, and “STATUS”. The labels appear at the top of the grid in monospaced uppercase text, 10px, all caps, with an extra heavy top border (3px). Each cell is separated by thin black vertical and horizontal lines; the grid is slightly misaligned to give it a hand-constructed, imperfect feel (e.g., some vertical lines extend 3–4px beyond the outer border). The four rows contain these entries in monospaced text, 11px: Row 1: ID “E-01”, TITLE “SILENT FIRST HOUR”, STATUS “ACTIVE”. Row 2: ID “E-02”, TITLE “NO PLOT TALK”, STATUS “ARCHIVED”. Row 3: ID “E-03”, TITLE “ROTATING FACILITATOR”, STATUS “ACTIVE”. Row 4: ID “E-04”, TITLE “ANNOTATION ONLY”, STATUS “IN DESIGN”. The STATUS for “ACTIVE” appears on an acid yellow background strip within the cell (yellow behind the word, black text), while the other statuses are plain black on white. There are no hover or interactive effects described; the grid appears static.

On the right side, opposite “CURRENT EXPERIMENTS,” the right column features a vertically stacked series of three brutalist “callout cards” intended as tutorials. They are labeled “TUTORIAL: BUILDING A SESSION”, “TUTORIAL: DESIGNING A READING PACKET”, and “TUTORIAL: WRITING A SESSION REPORT”. Each card is full width in the right column, separated by 20px of whitespace. Each card has a white background with a 2px black border and a subtle texture: faint gray diagonal lines at 45 degrees, very thin and widely spaced, reminiscent of blueprint paper. Inside each card, the title is at the top in uppercase grotesk, 16px, aligned left. Beneath the title, on the left half of the card, is a short description in monospaced 11px text. For instance, for the first card: “A step-by-step scaffold for running a 90-minute session with constraints and roles.” The second card: “How to compile excerpts, questions, and research prompts into a portable packet.” The third card: “A minimal template for capturing what actually happened in the room.” On the right half of each card, occupying roughly 40% of the card’s width, there is a simple black-and-white icon drawn in a brutalist, flat, line-based style. For the first card, show a flat top-down diagram of a rectangular table with four small rectangles around it symbolizing chairs, each labeled with tiny monospaced text: “HOST”, “TIME”, “SCRIBE”, “WILD CARD”. For the second card, show a stack of three overlapping sheets of paper, each with different simple motifs: lines of text, a large black square representing an image, and a column of dots. For the third card, show a large rectangular sheet with horizontal lines and a single bold black rectangle at the top like a header, with a small checkbox in the lower right corner. Each icon is black on white with hard edges and no shading.

After this tutorial area, the page introduces a “LAB NOTES” segment that feels like a blog but remains brutally minimal. This section spans both columns but is treated as a two-column grid of entries, each entry presented as a narrow, bordered block. At the top, centered across the full page width (ignoring the column division), there is a section label: “LAB NOTES / LOG OF FAILED AND SUCCESSFUL INTERVENTIONS” in uppercase monospaced text, 10px, with a thin acid yellow underline that runs roughly half the width of the page and is intentionally off-center to the left. Below this, the content is split into two columns again: in the left main column, two stacked note entries; in the right main column, three stacked note entries, creating an asymmetrical imbalance (more content on the right). Each lab note is a rectangular block with a 1px black border, about 160–200px tall, with generous padding. At the top of each block is a small line of meta data in monospaced 9px uppercase: “NOTE 07 / FAILURE”, “NOTE 12 / SUCCESS”, “NOTE 15 / WORK-IN-PROGRESS”, etc. The word after the slash (“FAILURE”, “SUCCESS”, “WORK-IN-PROGRESS”) appears on an acid yellow capsule background (rounded rectangle) with black text. Below this meta line, each entry has a short title in bold grotesk, 14px, such as “Too Many Questions”, “The Timer That Changed Everything”, “When Nobody Wants to Facilitate”, and a few lines of monospaced body text (11px) that look like notes or observations. The text should be generic-sounding but thoughtful; for example: “We tried a session with 25 pre-written questions. Participants read less and optimized for answering. Future iterations will cap prompts at three.” The layout ensures the note blocks are aligned to each column’s inner margins, with 20px vertical spacing between blocks, and the bottom edges of the notes in left and right columns do not line up perfectly, creating a jagged visual rhythm.

Near the bottom of the page, returning to the more rigid two-column structure, there is a “METHODS” section in the left column and a “SIGN UP FOR STUDY INVITES” block in the right column. In the left column, the “METHODS” heading is in uppercase bold grotesk, 18px. Under it, three short paragraphs describe the approach, each preceded by a tiny square bullet, 6x6px, filled with acid yellow. The paragraphs in monospaced 11px text read: “We treat every session as an experiment with variables, controls, and clear outcomes.”, “We publish detailed session templates and debriefs, not summaries of books.”, and “We prefer constraints over vibes, scripts over spontaneous facilitation.” The bullets line up perfectly on a vertical axis, reinforcing the harsh grid. In the right column, a large box with a bolder 3px black border and a subtle drop shadow (simple offset shadow, 3px down and right in a flat gray) contains a brutalist-style sign-up form. The heading inside the box, aligned left, reads “SIGN UP FOR STUDY INVITES” in uppercase bold grotesk, 18px. Below it is a short monospaced description, 11px: “Occasional emails with new protocols, open experiments, and debriefs. No marketing.” Then comes a vertical stack of form fields, each spanning the full width of the box: a label in uppercase monospaced 10px (“EMAIL”, “NAME”, “WHAT DO YOU WANT TO STUDY?”), followed by a white input area with a 1px solid black border and square corners. The “WHAT DO YOU WANT TO STUDY?” field is a taller textarea-style box, about 80px high. At the bottom-right of the form, aligned to the right edge, is a brutalist button: a rectangular block filled with black, with the label “ENROLL” in uppercase monospaced 11px acid yellow text. The button has no rounded corners, and the text is slightly off-center vertically, giving an intentionally unfinished feel. Above the button, in tiny 8px monospaced text, is a kind of disclaimer: “Submissions are stored in an open, shared log. Do not share private information.”

Finally, at the very bottom of the page, a stark footer spans the full width, about 80px tall, separated from the main content by a thick 3px black horizontal rule that runs edge to edge. The footer background remains white. On the left side, aligned with the 40px main margin, there is small monospaced text, 9px, reading: “DISSECTING TEXTS is an ongoing research project by an informal collective of readers, designers, and facilitators.” On the right side, aligned with the right margin, there are two lines of tiny system-like text, also monospaced 9px. The first line: “CURRENT BUILD: 0.3 / LAST UPDATE: 2025-02-03”. The second line: “CONTACT: research@dissectingtexts.club”. A very faint grid of vertical lines, 1px light gray and spaced about 120px apart, runs behind this footer only, echoing the idea of a modular layout grid without overwhelming the design. Throughout the entire page, there are no rounded corners except for the capsule background behind the “FAILURE/SUCCESS” labels in Lab Notes; edges are mostly sharp and rectangular. There are no videos, animations, or moving elements described anywhere, and no charts, graphs, or explicit data visualizations: the page relies solely on text, static illustrations, icons, boxes, and brutalist typographic structure to communicate its research-oriented, systematically experimental approach to book club design.

instruction: Planning the generation details for the following website:
input:
Design a neo-skeuomorphic landing page for an eco-tourism tutorial subscription homepage called “Evergreen Guide”. The entire page should feel like a lovingly crafted digital desk of a nature guide, where interface elements subtly imitate tactile objects, but with a modern, clean restraint. The color palette is composed of deep forest greens (#0B3425), warm earthy browns (#4A3220), soft parchment beiges (#F5F0E7), and accents of moss green (#6B8F5B) and burnished brass (#C79A4A). The background of the page is a matte, slightly textured dark green, reminiscent of a painted wall, with faint, almost imperceptible noise and very soft vignette towards the edges. At the center is a wide, card-like container with rounded 24px corners that seems slightly raised from the background via soft, blurred shadows; it resembles a leather-bound planner laid on a table. This main container uses a parchment-beige background with subtle vertical grain texture, and a dark brown 2px border with a barely lighter inner highlight to suggest a beveled edge. The layout tends toward a two-column design: left side primarily textual and interactive, right side more imagery and feature cards, but with slight asymmetry in widths: the left column is about 55% width, the right 45%.

At the top inside the main container is a header bar resembling a stitched leather strip running horizontally, slightly darker than the card background, with a gentle inner shadow that makes it feel inset. In the top-left corner is the logo: a circular emblem that looks like a small brass compass inset into the strip. The compass icon is rendered in detailed flat-3D style: a warm brass ring with subtle highlights and a faint engraved texture, inside which is a deep green face with a minimalist white leaf-shaped compass needle pointing northeast and tiny, engraved cardinal letters “N”, “E”, “S”, “W” in a delicate serif font. Next to this icon is the brand name text “Evergreen Guide” in an elegant but legible serif typeface resembling Garamond, set in dark forest green, slightly embossed with a soft highlight along the top edges of the letters and a subtle shadow along the bottom, giving a neo-skeuomorphic raised-metal effect. The font size here is around 26–28px. To the right of the logo, along the header strip, is a horizontal navigation that looks like a series of engraved labels. The nav items read “Home”, “How It Works”, “Curriculum”, “Stories”, and “Login”. Each word is in a small caps serif font, dark brown on the slightly lighter leather, with very subtle debossing: an inner shadow and faint highlight around the text making it appear pressed into the surface. The currently active item “Home” has a tiny brass dot indicator beneath it, appearing like a rivet. In the top-right corner of the header strip is a pill-shaped “Join Now” button that looks like a small, polished wooden inlay: rich medium-brown gradient with visible grain, beveled edges, and a gleaming highlight across the top-left. The label “Join Now” is centered in uppercase sans-serif white text, slightly engraved into the wood, with a thin brass outline around the pill.

Below the header, the main hero section occupies the upper two-thirds of the viewport inside the card. The hero is split into two columns. The left column contains the primary heading, subheading, description, and a pair of call-to-action buttons. At the top left is the main headline, aligned left, reading “Learn Eco-Tourism Like a Local Guide.” in large, 42–48px serif type, deep forest green, with a subtle inner gradient from slightly lighter at the top to darker at the bottom of each letter, hinting at three-dimensional ink pressed into paper. Behind the headline text, the parchment background is just slightly lighter, like a faint glow, to draw attention. Underneath the headline is a subheading in slightly smaller, 18–20px sans-serif text in a warm dark gray, reading “Weekly, hands-on tutorials for ethical adventurers who want to explore, protect, and give back to the places they visit.” This text appears printed on the parchment with a slightly darker grain visible within the letters, and extremely subtle drop shadow making it feel just above the paper surface. Below the subheading, a paragraph of body copy in 16px serif font, line-height generous, in dark brown, reads: “Each lesson feels like leafing through a guide’s field notebook — practical routes, real community stories, and step-by-step tips for low-impact travel. No fluff. Just grounded wisdom from guides, rangers, and local hosts.” The paragraph is left-aligned with a 24px gap below the subheading, framed by faint dotted guide lines at the left margin, reminiscent of a ruled notebook but very subtle and low-contrast.

Below this body text sit two main call-to-action buttons arranged horizontally with a small 16px gap, aligned left. The primary button on the left reads “Start Free Trail Lesson” and appears as a slightly raised, rectangular button with rounded 14px corners. It imitates a pressed, smooth green leather tab: a gradient of moss green with a hint of texture, beveled edges, and a soft shadow underneath. The text on it is uppercase sans-serif white, centered, with an embossed effect created by a tiny inner highlight and drop shadow. On the right is the secondary button, reading “Browse Lesson Samples”, styled like a beige cardstock tag outlined with a dashed brass-colored border and a tiny circular “hole” graphic in the top-left corner, as if it could be attached to something. The text is dark forest green, uppercase sans-serif but slightly thinner, appearing printed on the tag. Below the buttons, there’s a compact, horizontal strip that mimics an old film caption plate: a dark green rectangle with slightly rounded corners and soft gloss. Inside, aligned left, is a small circular avatar image placeholder followed by the testimonial text. The avatar is a small, circular, neo-skeuomorphic frame that looks like a brass medallion with a very faint engraved rope pattern on its rim; inside, a photorealistic portrait-style placeholder of a smiling, outdoor-oriented woman in her 30s with a backpack, standing in front of blurred greenery, lit by soft natural light. To the right of the avatar, in a small 13–14px sans-serif white font, italics, the text reads: ““These tutorials completely changed how I move through wild places.” — Mira K., trail guide”. The avatar slightly protrudes above the strip, casting a small shadow onto the parchment.

The right column of the hero section is occupied by a large, skeuomorphic “field notebook” mockup that doubles as a hero image. It appears like an open, slightly angled notebook resting on the parchment background, with its own gentle drop shadow that makes it pop. The notebook cover is visible as a slightly thicker dark brown leather spine on the left side with faint stitching along the edge. The open pages are a warm, slightly yellowed cream, with faint horizontal ruled lines. On the right-hand page is a photorealistic, full-bleed image framed by the paper, giving the impression of a printed, glossy photograph taped into the notebook. The image depicts a lush, misty forest valley at dawn: moss-covered rocks in the foreground, a narrow winding dirt path leading into dense forest, tall conifer trees on both sides, and distant hazy mountains catching the first light. The lighting is soft and diffuse, with gentle mist and a slightly desaturated, cinematic tone. The composition is from a slightly elevated viewpoint, looking down the path, inviting the viewer forward. A small, semi-transparent label in the lower-left corner of the photo reads “Sample Trail: Cloudveil Ridge” in a tiny white sans-serif font on a dark green translucent strip, with rounded corners to match the aesthetic. On the left-hand page of the notebook, there is hand-drawn-style content resembling a guide’s scribbles but rendered as clean design: at the top, a small pseudo-ink illustration in muted green of a compass and pine trees; below it, three stacked bullet points in a handwritten script typeface reading: “Pack light. Pack local.”, “Ask permission. Listen more.”, and “Leave every trail better than you found it.” Each line sits on the ruled paper, slightly angled, with a hint of simulated ink bleed around strokes. At the bottom of the left page is a small rectangular stamp-like element with serrated edges, labeled “Member Tutorial 01: Forest Etiquette” in condensed uppercase text.

Directly below the hero content, the main card continues with a subtle horizontal divider that looks like a gently embossed groove running across the full width of the container; it’s a slightly darker beige line flanked by faint highlights. Beneath this divider, the content area transitions into a two-column layout with more clearly defined sections but still within the same overarching card. On the left, aligned under the left hero column, is a section titled “How Evergreen Guide Works” in 24px serif, dark green, with a small, brass leaf icon to its left: the leaf is slightly raised, with a brushed metal sheen. This section consists of three vertically stacked, numbered “step cards” that resemble small, index cards partially overlapping each other with subtle shadows, each with rounded 18px corners and a slightly textured paper surface. Each card has a brass-colored circular badge in the top-left corner with a raised number “1”, “2”, or “3” in white, as if the number is embossed enamel. The first card is titled “1. Subscribe to the Trailbook” with subtext below: “Choose a monthly or yearly plan. You’ll unlock a growing library of place-specific eco-travel tutorials, updated every week.” The second is “2. Follow real guide-led routes” with subtext: “Each lesson includes maps, local context, and ethical guidelines you can print, save, or use on your device offline.” The third is “3. Give back as you explore” with subtext: “A portion of each subscription supports community-led conservation projects in the regions we cover.” Titles are in dark green serif; body in dark brown sans-serif. Each card surface has a very faint drop shadow making it appear slightly lifted off the parchment.

On the right side beneath the hero notebook, aligned under the right hero column, is a section that looks like a small stack of archival photo sleeves, labeled “Inside Your Weekly Tutorials” in a 20px serif, dark green, centered above them. This section is composed of three overlapping rectangular tiles laid out in a loose, slightly staggered grid, each representing a type of content. Each tile has the aesthetic of a printed photograph with a white border and very subtle warping of the edges to hint at physicality. The first tile, top-left, shows a photorealistic overhead shot of a guide’s table: a folded paper trail map with dotted lines, a compass, a reusable water bottle, and a small notebook, all lit by warm natural light from the top-left, casting gentle shadows. At the bottom of this tile, overlaying the photo, is a translucent dark green label reading “Step-by-step route breakdowns” in white sans-serif text. The second tile, slightly overlapping the first and lower-right, shows a close-up photo of a local guide talking with a small group of travelers in a rural village setting, with earthen houses and a terraced field in the background; warm tones and shallow depth of field keep the focus on the guide’s expressive hands as they gesture. Its label reads “Local voices and cultural context”. The third tile, shifted slightly behind the others, shows a small arrangement of eco-gear: biodegradable soap, reusable food containers, and a cloth bag, arranged on a wooden surface with dappled sunlight, labeled “Practical eco-gear checklists”. Each tile casts a delicate shadow on the parchment, and the overlapping creates a layered, tactile feel.

Further down the page, another embossed horizontal divider suggests a new section. This section spans the full width of the inner card but is visually organized into a two-column layout again. The left column features a lightly inset “Curriculum Overview” panel designed like the inside of a drawer organizer. The panel background is a slightly darker beige with a thin brass outline and softly rounded corners, inset into the main card with an inner shadow to indicate depth. Within this panel are four “lesson drawer” rows, each spanning the width of the panel. Each row resembles a shallow, wooden drawer front with horizontal wood grain and a tiny brass label holder on the left side; inside the label holder, each row has a small text like “Module 1”, “Module 2”, etc. Next to the label holder, on the drawer surface, is bold text describing the module: “Module 1 — Foundations of Eco-Tourism”, “Module 2 — Traveling with Local Communities”, “Module 3 — Low-Impact Trail Craft”, and “Module 4 — Supporting Conservation Projects”. Under each title is a single-line description in smaller serif text, dark brown, e.g., “Understand the core principles of leaving places better than you found them.” All drawers are “closed” visually, but the top one has a slight 3D extrusion, as if it’s pulled out by a few pixels, casting a deeper shadow, implying that it’s currently in focus. The right column of this section holds an “Upcoming Trail Highlights” panel styled as a pinboard. The panel background mimics cork, with a fine, repeating texture and small darker flecks. On this cork-like surface are three pinned note cards. Each note card is a small rectangular card with rounded corners, each in a slightly different muted pastel tone (muted sage, pale sand, light clay), with a drop shadow and a tiny brass pushpin graphic near the top center casting a sharp, tiny shadow. The note texts read, for example: “June — Cloudveil Ridge: high-altitude ethics & fragile soil”, “July — Mangrove Paths: traveling with tidal ecosystems”, and “August — Desert Tracks: water wisdom & night navigation”. The text is in a casual script font, dark brown, as if handwritten. One corner of one card is slightly curled, with a gradient and shadow to emphasize materiality.

Lower on the page, a subtle gradient shadow marks transition into the subscription section. Here, the background of the main card subtly shifts to a slightly warmer parchment tint, as if a different sheet of paper has been layered on top. This “Pricing & Membership” section again uses a two-column layout but with unequal width: the left column narrower, the right wider. On the left is a single tall “Standard Trail Pass” block styled like a vertical ticket stub. It has a perforated edge effect along its right side: a series of small, semi-circular cutouts along the border. The ticket is a light greenish-beige with a faint diagonal stripe pattern very low in contrast. At the top of the ticket, in uppercase condensed serif text, centered, is “STANDARD TRAIL PASS” in dark green. Below that, bold bigger text reads “$12/month” and a smaller line “Billed monthly, cancel anytime.” Bullet-like lines in small serif font describe key features: “Weekly eco-tourism tutorials”, “Access to route & gear library”, “Offline-friendly lesson PDFs”, “Supports community-led projects”. To the right of this ticket, in the wider column, is a slightly larger, more prominent “Guide Circle Pass” card, resembling a hardcover journal cover. The card has deep moss green fake-leather background with a subtle grain and a lighter bevel around the edges. Centered at the top is “GUIDE CIRCLE PASS” embossed in gold-like serif letters. Below, “$96/year” is displayed in large white serif font, with a smaller note “2 months free, billed yearly.” Beneath, a list of benefits in white sans-serif: “Everything in Standard, plus:”, “Quarterly live Q&A sessions with local guides”, “Early access to new trail regions”, “Members-only community trail log.” At the bottom of this card, a wide, pill-shaped button sits centered, appearing as a burnished brass plate with engraved text “Choose Guide Circle” in dark green. The brass has a slightly reflective highlight and blurred reflections hinting at the surroundings, but still subtle. Below the two pricing options, centered and spanning both columns, is a faintly framed line of smaller text in serif font: “We offer sponsored passes for guides and students. Email us at hello@evergreenguide.org”.

Near the bottom of the main card, there is a “Stories from the Trail” section with a subtle header in 22px serif, dark green, left-aligned. This section showcases three testimonial/story cards arranged horizontally in a three-column layout but responsive enough that they could stack. Each card looks like a small polaroid photograph taped onto a slightly darker beige backing. The top of each card is slightly overlapped by a translucent tape strip graphic — off-white with a tiny bit of crinkled texture and minor shading. Within each polaroid frame is a photorealistic travel scene: the first shows a wide-angle view from behind of a traveler standing at the edge of a cliff overlooking a vast, green valley with a river winding below, early morning light casting long shadows and fog in the lowlands; the second depicts a small group of travelers sitting in a circle with a local elder under a large tree, with sun filtering through leaves, earthy ground, and simple stools; the third shows a close-up of a boot stepping carefully over a mossy log on a forest trail, with dew droplets on leaves around. Under each photo, in a caption area on the polaroid, in handwritten-style font, are short titles: “Finding new ways to tread lightly”, “Meeting the people who call these places home”, and “Learning to see every trail as a living story”. Beneath each polaroid, on the beige backing, there is a small line of text in a tidy serif font: “Read Ella’s story”, “Read Darius’s story”, “Read Lina’s story”, all in dark green and underlined with a thin solid line as if penciled. Each card has a soft shadow, giving them the impression of being loosely scattered but arranged neatly.

At the very bottom inside the main card is the footer area, which is narrower in height but designed to look like the inner back cover of a journal. The background becomes a slightly darker, smoother leather-like brown with visible but subtle grain, and the top edge has an inward curve with a highlight, resembling a pocket. On the left side of the footer, in small serif text, lush off-white, are the footer navigation links: “About”, “FAQ”, “Contact”, “Press Kit”, “Privacy Policy”, “Terms”. They are aligned left, each separated by a small brass dot bullet, and the text appears slightly engraved, with a darker inner shadow. On the right side of the footer, aligned right, is a small block of text reading “© 2025 Evergreen Guide. Crafted with respect for every trail.” in the same off-white serif. Above this line, three small circular icons mimic embossed stamps: a leaf, a mountain, and a droplet of water, each rendered as simple line icons recessed into the leather with a faint, lighter ring around them. The entire main card casts a gentle shadow on the deep green page background, and around it, slightly visible in the background margins, are faint, unfocused suggestions of other “objects” on the desk — perhaps the blurred edge of a map on the left and a faint silhouette of a plant leaf on the right, rendered as extremely soft shapes in a slightly darker green to add depth but not distraction. Throughout the page, all inputs, buttons, and interactive elements (like potential forms for email capture, if included under the hero — a small, inline field labeled “Enter your email to preview a free lesson” with a rounded rectangle input that looks like an inset slot in the parchment and a tiny embossed “→” icon button) maintain this neo-skeuomorphic balance: subtle dimensionality and tactile references, but clean, uncluttered typography and a restrained palette giving the overall site a calm, modern, and trustworthy feel, with no videos, no animation, and no charts or data visualizations present anywhere.

instruction: Planning the generation details for the following website:
input: Design a single-page personal portfolio homepage with an organic, hand-crafted aesthetic that feels like a creative mountain biking guide and maker’s journal combined. The overall layout tends toward a loose two-column structure with natural asymmetries, slightly uneven alignments, and tactile textures, as if the page were assembled from paper, ink, and sketchbook scraps laid on a wooden table. The color palette is earthy and muted: warm off-white paper (#F9F3E7) as the main background, deep forest green (#1F3D2B) for headlines and key accents, burnt orange (#C86A3A) for highlights and buttons, and soft slate gray (#5B646A) for body text. The font choices reinforce the handcrafted feel: headings use a slightly irregular, hand-drawn serif (resembling something like "Cormorant Garamond" with rough edges), while body text uses a clean but friendly humanist sans-serif reminiscent of "Nunito" in a medium gray. All text appears crisp but not overly digital—line spacing is generous, and margins breathe with plenty of whitespace, as though content is taped to a textured sheet of cream cardstock. At the very top is a narrow, full-width header bar with a subtle paper fiber texture and a faint vertical grain. On the left side of this header, there is a hand-drawn logo that reads: "Trailcraft Studio" in dark forest green, the word "Trailcraft" in a loose script with slightly varied stroke width and "Studio" in small caps serif just below it. To the right, horizontally aligned but slightly mis-offset for organic charm, are navigation links in small uppercase sans-serif, letterspaced: "ABOUT", "TRAIL PROJECTS", "SKETCHES", "JOURNAL", "CONTACT". Each link is separated by a thin, hand-drawn vertical line that looks like it was drawn with a fine pen, a little wobbly instead of perfectly straight. When a navigation item is hovered, imagine a brushstroke underline of burnt orange appearing beneath it, slightly uneven and textured. Below the header, the hero section uses a two-column layout with a gentle asymmetry. On the left, taking about 55% width, is a large hand-illustrated hero image within an irregular, torn-paper style frame whose edges are rough and slightly shadowed, giving a layered collage feel. The hero image is a detailed, semi-realistic watercolor illustration of a mountain biker paused at the top of a forested ridge at golden hour. The viewpoint is slightly elevated behind and to the side of the rider’s shoulder, showing the bike angled toward a winding singletrack trail that snakes down through pine trees into a valley. The rider wears a matte dark helmet, muted teal jersey, and charcoal shorts, with a small frame bag on the bike. The trail winds into a hazy background of layered blue-green mountains. The sky is warm with soft oranges and muted pinks near the horizon, fading into cooler blues above. Foreground details include scattered rocks, roots, and tufts of alpine grass. The illustration has visible brushstrokes, a gauzy texture, and subtle speckles, as though painted with watercolor on cold-press paper, with soft edges and organic, flowing lines. On top of the lower right edge of this image, attached as if paper-clipped, is a tiny Polaroid-style mini-photo illustration: a simplified ink-and-watercolor sketch of a bike tire carving through dirt, dust kicking up, with the handwritten caption "Chasing lines" in black ink under the mini-photo. To the right of the hero image, aligned vertically, is the main introductory text. At the top right, in forest green, a small label in uppercase sits like a stamped tag: "PORTFOLIO OF LENA MARIS". Below that, the main headline in large hand-drawn serif reads: "Organic Trails & Crafted Code". The words "Organic Trails" are in forest green, and "& Crafted Code" in burnt orange. The headline is centered in its column but not perfectly square with the page, slightly angled by a degree or two as if a paper piece were taped on. Underneath is a short introductory paragraph in slate gray sans-serif, left-aligned: "I’m Lena — a trail builder, mountain bike guide, and front-end developer crafting digital experiences with the same care I give to hand-shaped singletrack. This is where my rides, sketches, and code experiments meet." Beneath this paragraph are two horizontal buttons, but rendered as hand-drawn rectangles with subtle brush textures and slightly rounded corners, stacked side by side with a small gap. The left button is filled with burnt orange and labeled in uppercase white sans-serif: "VIEW TRAIL PROJECTS". The right button has a transparent background with a forest green hand-drawn border and text reading: "SEE MY CODE" in forest green, and a tiny ink-drawn arrow pointing right at the end of the text. Under these buttons, a hand-drawn arrow curling downward guides the eye to the next section, with a small handwritten note in thin italic script: "Scroll to explore the terrain". The next major area is the "About" section, which keeps a two-column feel but staggers elements to feel organic. The section background is still off-white but with a faint overlay of a subtle off-white topographical map pattern, especially toward the edges, fading toward the center. On the left, a vertical column contains a hand-drawn portrait illustration in a circular frame. The portrait is a semi-realistic, soft colored-pencil drawing of Lena: a woman with medium-length wavy dark hair tied into a low messy bun, wearing a flannel shirt and a light windbreaker with a zipped pocket, smiling slightly as she looks to the right. Behind her, loosely sketched mountains and trees are drawn in soft greens and grays. The circular frame has a slightly rough pencil line. Underneath this portrait, arranged like pinned notecards, are three small, overlapping rectangular "badge" snippets. Each looks like a tiny card of recycled paper taped to the page with a sliver of translucent washi tape. The first card reads "Trail builder since 2012". The second reads "Front-end dev & UX tinkerer". The third reads "Guide for small-group MTB retreats". On the right of this column, occupying about 60% of the width, is a text block titled "About the Rider & Maker" in a modest hand-drawn serif, forest green. The title is left-aligned and underlined with a faint, uneven horizontal ink line. The body copy below is in slate gray sans-serif, left-aligned and broken into short paragraphs of 3–4 lines for readability. It explains Lena’s background: "I grew up at the base of the Rockies, sketching trails in my notebooks and building jumps in the backyard. Over time, the same curiosity that led me down new lines on the mountain led me into the world of web design. Now I split my time between shaping dirt, leading small-group rides, and building hand-crafted digital experiences for outdoor brands, local makers, and non-profits." Another paragraph: "My work blends analog and digital: field notes scribbled in rain-soaked notebooks, GPS tracks and elevation profiles, and hand-coded interfaces that feel as tactile as the terrain they represent." The section ends with a handwritten-style, slightly angled quote near the bottom right of the text block: "Good trails — and good interfaces — flow so naturally you forget someone built them." Below the About section is the primary portfolio showcase titled "Trail Projects". This section uses a two-column grid that occasionally breaks into staggered tiles, like an uneven gallery wall. The background remains off-white, but a wide horizontal brushstroke of very pale sage green runs behind the section title. Centered at the top, the title reads "Trail Projects" in large serif and a subheading below in smaller sans-serif: "Selected rides, routes, and digital maps I’ve shaped by hand." The project cards are arranged in two vertical columns with alternating heights, giving a masonry layout feel. Each project card looks like a slightly curled piece of paper with a soft drop shadow, as though it sits above the page. In each card’s upper portion is a rectangular illustration about bikes and trails; below is descriptive text. For example, the first card in the left column is titled "High Ridge Flowline". Its illustration is a semi-realistic gouache-style painting viewed from a wide, slightly elevated angle: a sinewy, tan dirt ribbon weaving down from a high ridge through conifers, with the trail highlighted by a thin white dotted line overlay. A tiny icon of a rider is mid-berm in the midground, leaning into a banked turn, dust trailing. The sky is muted blue with a few wispy clouds; shadows from trees stretch across the trail, suggesting late afternoon. The artistic style has bold, visible brush strokes and slightly exaggerated curves to emphasize flow. Beneath this image is project text: a small label "TRAIL DESIGN & MAPPING" in uppercase burnt orange, then a bold project title "High Ridge Flowline" in forest green, and a 2–3 sentence description in gray. Example: "A 12 km blue flow trail carved into the north side of Cedar Peak, designed for continuous rhythm and minimal braking. I planned the route on paper first, then translated it into a custom interactive map and trail signage system." At the bottom of this card, there is a small row of hand-drawn icons: a tiny elevation profile squiggle, a minimal bike icon, and a small compass. To the right of "High Ridge Flowline" is another card titled "Forest Loop Micro-Site". Its illustration features a flat, semi-abstract screen mockup drawn in an ink-and-watercolor style, showing a laptop with a stylized map of a loop trail and big friendly labels like "START", "LOOKOUT", and "RIVER CROSSING". The laptop sits on a wooden table surface with a coffee cup and small plant in the periphery, all sketched with loose lines and muted colors. The description reads: "A single-page storytelling site for a local community loop, combining hand-drawn maps, rider testimonials, and real-time conditions updates." Other project cards include one called "Night Ride Signals" with an illustration of a night forest scene with a winding trail lit by a string of glowing lanterns hanging between trees, rendered in deep blues and warm yellows; and another named "Alpine Switchback Series" with a narrow trail zig-zagging across a steep, rocky alpine face, tiny riders visible as colored specks. Each card has a small, underlined text link at the bottom right corner in forest green that says "View details →". Between every two cards vertically, there is ample whitespace so the layout doesn’t feel rigid. The next section, offset slightly by a narrow full-width hand-drawn horizontal line, is "Sketches & Field Notes". The background here subtly shifts: still off-white, but with more visible paper texture and faint smudges like graphite touches. This section is more obviously two-column: on the left, a column of sketched imagery; on the right, written notes, almost like a notebook transcription. On the left side, at the top, there is a collage of three overlapping sketch images, each in a slightly different style and orientation like pinned scraps. One is a black-and-white ink drawing from a top-down perspective of a trail winding around contour lines, with tiny hand-lettered labels such as "rooty bit", "loose rocks", and "viewpoint". Another is a rough pencil sketch of a handlebar and gloved hands seen from the rider’s perspective, with a trail and trees stretching ahead; shading is rough, with visible crosshatching. The third sketch is a colored pencil drawing of a trail signpost at an intersection in a dense forest, with arrows to "Ridge Line" and "River Run", the tree trunks rising vertically with patches of moss and dappled sunlight. Each sketch has a faint shadow as if raised, and short handwritten captions under each in gray ink, such as "Testing new line options", "Morning climb notes", and "Signage concept". Below these a bit further down, still on the left, is a narrow horizontal image of a simple, hand-drawn grid notebook page with a few scribbled symbols, coffee ring stains, and a small doodle of a bike tire. On the right column of this section, there’s a title "Sketches & Field Notes" in forest green serif, underlined with a quick brushstroke. Below it, a series of short note-like paragraphs, each introduced by a small hand-drawn icon. For example, one entry titled "Translating Terrain into Interface" is preceded by a tiny icon of a switchback trail. The text reads in a conversational tone, as if from a field notebook: "I start with rough pencil sketches, mapping the flow of a ride and how it might translate into user journeys. Switchbacks become navigation choices. Climbs represent the effort required of a user. Descents are those moments of pure, frictionless delight." Another entry, "Guardrails & Berms", is preceded by a small safety helmet icon and talks about designing safe edges in UX similar to protective berms on trails. There might be 3–4 such entries, each separated by a light dotted line that looks hand-drawn. Below this, a small rotated note is "taped" at the lower right, reading: "Most ideas start in the dirt and on paper long before they hit a screen." Next is the "Journal" section showcasing recent posts, arranged again in two columns but more like cards stacked vertically with some offset alignment to keep the organic feel. The background returns to the simple textured off-white. The section title "Journal" is centered in a larger script-like font, with a subtitle below in gray: "Stories from the trail and the pixels in between." Each journal entry is a card with a small illustrated thumbnail on the left and text on the right. For instance, one entry titled "Designing a Trailhead for New Riders" has a thumbnail illustration of a wooden trailhead kiosk with a map and posters, drawn in colored pencil with rough wood grain detail, surrounded by small pine trees, slightly angled, shadows falling to the right. The card’s text uses a hierarchy: date in small uppercase gray ("APR 03, 2025"), title in forest green, and a two-line excerpt in gray. Another entry "What Mountain Biking Taught Me About Onboarding" shows a stylized bike wheel turning into a loading spinner icon, half organic, half UI. The entries have a thin, uneven border line in light gray, as if drawn by hand, with corners that don’t perfectly meet. "Read story →" appears in tiny forest green text at the bottom of each card. Throughout the page, small organic embellishments appear: faint leaf silhouettes at the corners of some sections, tiny ink splatters near headings, and occasional micro-doodles such as a small rock pile or a miniature topographic line cluster. Finally, the lower part of the page features a clearly divided "Work With Me" and "Contact" area, still within the two-column motif. On the left side, under a subheading "Services & Collaborations" in forest green serif, there’s a simple handwritten-style checklist with small square boxes drawn in pen next to each item: "Trail design & route consulting", "Small-team guided rides & skill sessions", "Outdoor brand UX / UI collaborations", "Story-driven single-page experiences". Each box has a slightly different shape to emphasize the human touch. On the right column, a contact form appears styled like a short paper form clipped onto the page. The form background looks like a slightly darker sheet of recycled paper with soft dog-eared corners and a subtle drop shadow. At the top in script, it says: "Let’s shape something together". Below, there are labeled fields with small, all-caps labels in gray sans-serif: "Name", "Email", "What are you dreaming up?". The input fields are lightly outlined with hand-drawn rectangles, the lines wobbling slightly but consistent enough to feel intentional. The message area is a taller rectangle resembling a blank note card. At the bottom of the form, a burnt orange button says "SEND A NOTE" in white uppercase, with a slight ink bleed effect at the edges. Beneath the form, in tiny gray typewriter-style text, is a line: "Or reach me directly at: hello@trailcraft.studio". The footer anchors the page with a narrow, deep forest green band that spans the width of the page. On the left, in small white sans-serif, it reads: "© 2025 Trailcraft Studio · Hand-built on two wheels and a text editor." On the right, three tiny monochrome icons appear, drawn as simple outlines: a bike icon, a small mountain range, and a tiny code bracket symbol "{}". Each icon is evenly spaced but slightly different in line thickness, embracing the imperfect aesthetic. The entire page should feel cohesive and calm, combining the spirit of a mountain biking club homepage with a personal, craft-focused portfolio, without any video, animation, or charts—only static, richly detailed, hand-crafted visuals and typography that make the digital page feel like an analog trail journal come to life.

instruction: Planning the generation details for the following website:
input:
Design a static, non-animated, data-free dashboard webpage in a distinctly brutalist aesthetic, themed as an operations control panel for a fictional food delivery service called “NIGHTSHIFT NOODLES”. The page has a full-width, fixed background in flat, slightly off-white (#f7f3ea) with no gradients, but the interface itself sits within a bold, rigid multi-column grid made of thick black borders, hard edges, and harsh contrast. The entire page feels like a utilitarian admin panel hacked together in the late 90s but with purposeful brutalist flair: clashing colors, visible outline boxes, overlarge type, and unapologetically dense information blocks. The font across the entire page is a monospace typeface that looks similar to “IBM Plex Mono” or “Courier New”. All text is rendered in uppercase only, with tight letter spacing and relatively compact line height. The primary text color is pure black, with occasional sections in bright neon colors to denote status. No animations, transitions, video, or charts appear anywhere—everything is static and stark.

At the very top is a thick horizontal header bar spanning the full width of the viewport, about 120px tall, with a solid, jarring lemon yellow (#ffe82b) background and a very strong 4px black border at the bottom edge. On the left half of this header, aligned flush left with 24px margin from the left edge and vertically centered, is a chunky wordmark: “NIGHTSHIFT NOODLES // DISPATCH DASHBOARD” in huge monospace black text, all caps, with “NIGHTSHIFT NOODLES” on the first line and “DISPATCH DASHBOARD” on the second. The font size is very large (around 36–40px), making it slightly spill into two lines. On the right half of the header, there are three rectangular pill-like buttons aligned horizontally and right-justified, separated by 8px gaps, each with heavy black outlines (3px) and harsh background colors: the first is a wide button saying “SHIFT: OVERNIGHT (20:00–06:00)” on a flat cyan (#2af0ff) background; the second says “VEHICLES ONLINE: 18” on a vivid pastel pink (#ffa8d8) background; the third says “ALERTS: 03 CRITICAL” on a glaring lime green (#b5ff3b) background. These buttons have no hover effects; they are just static, solid parcels of color. The header ends with a full-width black line that visually anchors it to the content below.

Directly underneath, the body is divided into a three-column brutalist grid with slight asymmetry: a narrow left column occupying about 22% width, a wide central column around 46%, and a medium right column around 32%. Each column is composed of stacked rectangular modules with varying heights, thick black borders, and occasionally clashing background stripes. All modules are separated by 12px of raw background space, making a rigid but slightly chaotic layout. The vertical scroll reveals more modules, but everything adheres to that three-column structure.

In the left column at the top sits an aggressive navigation panel labeled “PRIMARY CONTROLS” in bold black text on a stark white background. The section header is inside a rectangular bar at the top of this module, with a black fill and its text in white: “PRIMARY CONTROLS”. Below this header bar, the navigation options are stacked vertically as full-width, tall, clickable blocks with hard hover-looking styling but actually static. Each nav item has a different background color, all with 2px black borders: “DASHBOARD HOME” on sour neon yellow (#ffe82b), “ORDERS QUEUE” on pale cyan (#d0fbff), “RIDERS & ZONES” on washed-out orange (#ffc46c), “KITCHEN STATUS” on pale mint (#d9ffd5), “CUSTOMER FEEDBACK” on nearly white gray (#f2f2f2), and “SYSTEM SETTINGS” on lavender (#e8d4ff). Each nav item’s label is left-aligned, in upper-left corner padding of 12px, and the font is slightly larger than body text, around 16–18px. On the far left of each nav item, inside the block, is a simple flat monochrome icon created as a solid shape: for example, a square with a smaller square cutout for “DASHBOARD HOME”, a stack of three rectangles for “ORDERS QUEUE”, a stick-figure-like icon for “RIDERS & ZONES”, etc., all in black and extremely simple, almost childlike, with no shading.

Below this nav panel, still in the left column, is a rectangular module with a harsh red background (#ff002b) and white text, bordered in black, labeled “CRITICAL ALERTS LOG”. At the top is a black strip across the full width containing the label “CRITICAL ALERTS LOG”. The body of this module is a simple vertical list of static alert items, each separated by thin 1px black horizontal lines. Each alert is a single line of uppercase text, for example: “00:17 — MISSING RIDER CHECK-IN: ZONE C / UNIT #12”; “00:23 — CUSTOMER COMPLAINT: COLD NOODLES / ORDER #99821”; “00:41 — KITCHEN B: BROTH LOW WARNING”. Each alert line begins with a small black square bullet and then the text, all in monospace, aligned left. The module height is enough to show 6–8 alerts before whitespace.

The central column begins with a bold, brutalist hero-like module titled “LIVE ORDER SNAPSHOT”. This module takes up roughly the full width of the central column and about 280–320px height, with a slightly gray off-white background (#f3f3f3), a solid black border, and a thick black header bar on top. Inside the header bar, the title “LIVE ORDER SNAPSHOT” appears in white, left aligned. On the right side of this header bar, there is a rectangular label with a glaring neon magenta background (#ff2fae) and black text: “LAST REFRESH: 00:45:03”. The main body of this snapshot module is subdivided into three vertical columns separated by thin black lines, but they are part of the same module. On the left sub-column, at the top, is a large numeric block with “TOTAL ORDERS IN QUEUE” in smaller text above and a massive, bold number “128” underneath. The number is black, occupying almost two-thirds of the module’s height, and set against the same off-white as the module background. Below this, separated by a 2px black line, is another smaller block with the label “AVG PREP TIME (MIN)” and the number “14.6” in a slightly smaller but still prominent size. The middle sub-column lists a static table-like list of order statuses with three rows. Each row is a strip with a background color: “COOKING” on faded orange (#ffb06e) with the count “37”; “OUT FOR DELIVERY” on mint (#cbffd6) with the count “54”; and “PENDING PICKUP” on pale blue (#cdefff) with the count “37”. The left side of each strip has the label, and the right side has the number, all separated by a thin vertical black line. The right sub-column shows a block with the label “TOP OVERLOADED ZONES” at the top, then a numbered list from 1 to 3 with their corresponding zone names and a simple horizontal block indicator made from solid rectangles, not charts per se but decorative bars: “1. ZONE C — DOWNTOWN CORE”, “2. ZONE F — NIGHT MARKET STRIP”, “3. ZONE A — RIVERSIDE LOOPS”. Each zone entry has a short, wide rectangle next to it: Zone C’s rectangle is the longest, bright orange, Zone F’s is medium, pale yellow, Zone A’s is shortest, gray, all purely decorative without scale marks.
