Directly below this central hero module, still in the central column, there is a “KITCHEN LINE ITEMS” module, a box with a stark white background and a thick 3px black border. The title bar at the top is a strip with a solid black background and white text “KITCHEN LINE ITEMS”. The content is a text-dense list, resembling a brutalist table without gridlines: each line shows a kitchen station followed by colon and their current item focus, all in uppercase, left aligned, monospaced. Examples: “GRILL STATION: CHARRED PORK BELLY SKEWERS / ORDERS: 09”; “NOODLE STATION A: RAMEN BASE / ORDERS: 21”; “NOODLE STATION B: UDON / ORDERS: 14”; “FRY STATION: GYOZA & TEMPURA / ORDERS: 18”. Each line is separated by noticeable vertical spacing but no rules, producing a raw, text-block feel, echoing a terminal readout. On the far right of each line sits a small solid rectangle badge in different colors indicating status, like “SMOOTH” in green, “STRESSED” in yellow, “JAMMED” in red, all uppercase in tiny type.

Further down the central column, another module titled “RIDERS CURRENTLY ACTIVE” features a flat, rectangular grayscale photo-like image on the left side and a dense text list on the right within the same module. The module background is an uncolored white with a thick black border. The header bar is black with white text “RIDERS CURRENTLY ACTIVE”. Below, the left half of the module contains a static image: a high-contrast, desaturated photograph-style illustration (not animated) of several delivery riders on bicycles and scooters at night, under harsh streetlights. The scene shows a wet asphalt street with reflections indicating recent rain, tall anonymous buildings in the background with a few lit windows, and the riders in simplified, semi-photorealistic style wearing square insulated backpacks with the “NIGHTSHIFT NOODLES” logo printed in white on black. The perspective is slightly low, eye-level from the street, looking down a lane; the riders’ forms are outlined with thick black strokes, adding a graphic novel quality. The image is grayscale except for a single neon accent stripe on each backpack in bright electric blue, which stands out brutally against the monochrome. The right half of the module lists riders in a column of text lines, each line like “UNIT #03 — RIDER: T. KAWASAKI — ZONE C — ETA 06 MIN”, separated by thin black dotted lines. The font is the same monospace, with the unit number in slightly bolder weight.

The right column begins with a “ZONE MAP (ABSTRACT)” module. This module is a tall rectangle bordered in black, with a flat light gray background (#ebebeb). The header bar is a horizontal strip at the top with black background, white text “ZONE MAP (ABSTRACT)”. The interior contains a stylized, blocky, top-down map representation drawn as a series of rectangular regions with no geographic realism, only harsh geometric zones. Each zone block is outlined in thick black lines and filled with a different flat color: “ZONE A” in pastel sky blue, “ZONE B” in light purple, “ZONE C” in hot red-orange, “ZONE D” in pale green, “ZONE E” in light yellow, “ZONE F” in a punchy magenta. The zone labels are uppercase textual overlays in black, placed directly at the center of each polygon. No roads or details, just a brutalist grid of shapes. In the lower right corner of this map module, there is a small legend block: a white box with a black border, listing the zones and a short descriptor in tiny text like “ZONE C — RESTAURANT DENSE / HIGH CONGESTION”. The map is static, no interactive behavior.

Beneath the map in the right column lives a module titled “CUSTOMER MOOD SNAPSHOT”. It keeps the brutalist style but uses a loud background color: a saturated pastel pink (#ffbcd1) with a solid 3px black border. The header bar is black with white text. The content is text-only, arranged in three vertically stacked boxes inside this module. Each inner box is an outlined, white rectangle containing an uppercase label and a short phrase. The first box has the title “LAST 50 REVIEWS (TEXT SENTIMENT)” with a large central word underneath: “MOSTLY HUNGRY / IMPATIENT” in bold black, centered. The second box says “PEAK ORDER TIME SURGES” with “21:00–23:00” in bold, centered. The third box is labeled “TOP COMPLAINT PATTERN” with “DELIVERY WINDOW ESTIMATE INACCURATE” displayed prominently. There is no graph; only text and geometric boxes arranged symmetrically.

At the bottom of the right column, there is a rough system info module called “SYSTEM STATUS”. It features a dirty, light beige background (#f7f3ea) that matches the page but with a more intense 4px black border and a subtle, all-caps label at the top left in small text: “SYSTEM STATUS”. Inside, the content mimics a terminal console log with a monospace text block, left-aligned, black text on beige, no boxes. The lines are like: “OK — DATABASE PING: 34MS”, “OK — KITCHEN TERMINALS: 7/7 ONLINE”, “WARN — ZONE C GPS DRIFT DETECTED”, “OK — SMS GATEWAY CONNECTED”, etc. Each line begins with a textual status like “OK”, “WARN”, “FAIL” in uppercase, followed by an em dash and description. Where “WARN” appears, the word itself is lettered in red (#ff002b) to stand out.

At the very bottom of the central column, spanning slightly into the left column in a subtle asymmetry, there is a wide, shallow module called “MANUAL OVERRIDES”, about 150px tall. It cuts across the gap between the left and central column visually, overlapping both columns’ width to break the strict grid. It is rendered as a block with a solid charcoal background (#222222), a brutal 4px white border, and inverted text: white uppercase monospace. Inside, minimalist rectangular pseudo-buttons are displayed in a row: “PAUSE NEW ORDERS”, “LIMIT DELIVERY RADIUS”, “FORCE KITCHEN COOL-DOWN”, “SEND SYSTEM-WIDE ALERT”. Each pseudo-button is simply a rectangle outlined in white with no background fill, the text centered; again, no hover or click states, purely static. On the far right inside this module, the text “ADMIN ACCESS REQUIRED” appears in tiny uppercase.

The footer at the bottom of the page is a slim, full-width bar, about 60px high, with a plain white background and a 2px black top border. On the left side it contains a single line of small uppercase text: “© 2025 NIGHTSHIFT NOODLES. INTERNAL USE ONLY.” On the right side, right-aligned, there are three text-only links in uppercase with simple underlines: “LEGAL”, “PRIVACY”, “LOG OUT”. No icons, no animation. Throughout the entire design, there are no gradient effects, no dropshadows except for the implicit heavy outlines, no soft rounded corners except selectively on a few larger buttons, and absolutely no moving, animated, or data-visualization elements. The whole page exudes a raw, uncompromising brutalist dashboard spirit: structural, text-heavy, rigidly boxed, with vivid flat colors and thick black lines framing every piece of information in a functional yet stylistically aggressive way.

instruction: Planning the generation details for the following website:
input:
Design a single-page editorial-style dashboard homepage for a fictional online coding tutorial subscription service called “Loopline Studio”, with a refined magazine aesthetic and a multi-column grid that allows for subtle asymmetry. The color palette is muted and sophisticated: off-white (#F7F4EF) as the main background, deep charcoal (#1F1F23) for headings, warm taupe (#B49A7D) accents for lines and buttons, and a soft desaturated teal (#6F8C8E) for secondary highlights. The overall feel is like a high-end print technology magazine translated into a digital dashboard. The page is centered within a very wide viewport, with generous margins on the left and right, and the content is contained in a max-width layout that resembles a newspaper spread. Use a two- and three-column grid interchangeably to create rhythm, with clearly visible vertical grid lines implied by alignment and subtle fine rules. The typography is key: headings use a high-contrast editorial serif like “Playfair Display” in bold, with tight letter-spacing, and body text uses a clean, readable sans-serif like “Inter” or “Source Sans Pro”. The entire page background is solid off-white, without gradient, but there are thin 1px horizontal rules in light taupe separating some sections, giving the feeling of printed editorial layout.

At the top, a slim, centered masthead bar spans the width. The background of this masthead is the same off-white as the page but it is visually separated by a 1px bottom border in very light grey. On the left side of this masthead is the brand: the text “Loopline Studio” in small caps serif, dark charcoal, slightly larger than the navigation, with the word “Loopline” in bold and “Studio” in regular weight. To its left is a small square logo: a minimalist line icon in taupe of a looping cable forming an abstract “L” shape, drawn with a single continuous stroke, flat design with no gradient. The navigation menu sits in the center, horizontally aligned, using uppercase sans-serif letters with small tracking: “Overview”, “Courses”, “Projects”, “Community”, “Account”. Each item is separated by equal spacing, and the currently active item “Overview” is underlined with a thin taupe line that does not extend beyond the word width. On the right side, there is a compact user profile area. A small circular avatar (photorealistic, neutral lighting) shows a person in their late 20s with short curly hair and glasses, facing forward, on a soft grey background. To the right of the avatar, stack two lines: top line “Welcome back,” in tiny uppercase sans-serif, and below it the user’s name “Jordan Hayes” in bold. All masthead elements are vertically centered in a bar about 70px high.

Below the masthead is the hero section, which is editorial and dashboard-like rather than splashy. It spans the full width of the content area and is divided into two unequal columns, left side wider. The left column contains the main hero headline and key stats; the right column holds a feature card. On the left, at the top, a slim label in uppercase sans-serif reads “TODAY’S FOCUS” in taupe. Beneath it, a large magazine-style headline in serif reads: “Design your next sprint like a seasoned developer.” The headline is left-aligned, about three lines long, with a line-height that allows plenty of breathing space. Below the headline is a short subheading in sans-serif: “Curated lessons, practice prompts, and code reviews — all laid out like your favorite digital magazine.” It is medium gray, with a softer tone than the heading. Further down, aligned with the left margin, are two side-by-side pill-shaped buttons. The primary button has a background in deep charcoal, with text “Start today’s session” in uppercase white sans-serif, and rounded pill corners; when hovered (visually indicated), imagine the background lightens slightly and a subtle inner shadow appears. The secondary button is an outline style: off-white background, a taupe border, and text “Browse all tracks” in charcoal. Below the buttons, aligned to the left, is a small horizontal strip of micro-metrics using an editorial-style layout: three miniature mini-blocks in a row, each with a tiny label (uppercase, muted gray) and a larger stat (bold charcoal). The columns read: “Streak” with “12 days”, “Weekly Goal” with “8 hrs”, and “Next Review” with “Wed, 8:30 PM”. Thin vertical taupe separators between them mimic print column dividers.

The right hero column contains a prominent “Today’s Layout” card that looks like a magazine cover inset. The card has a faint drop shadow and a slightly warmer off-white background, with generous padding. At the top of the card, a small sans-serif label in teal reads “Today’s Path”. Beneath, a mid-sized serif title: “Async JavaScript, Demystified” with the first letter capitalized for each word. Below the title, depict a static illustration, not a photo: a flat-design graphic of a code editor window. The editor window is in perspective, slightly angled from the top-left, with a dark slate background and a few stylized lines of code in bright, muted colors (teal, soft yellow, coral). The window sits on an abstract tabletop made of overlapping rectangles in taupe and pale teal, suggesting layered pages. There are no animated elements; the illustration is static, with soft shadows. Under the illustration, a minimalist checklist: three bullet rows with subtle round bullet icons in taupe and brief text to the right: “30-min concept walkthrough”, “Interactive practice prompts”, “Peer-reviewed project brief”. Each line uses sans-serif text. At the card’s bottom right corner, a subtle label shows a small circular tag icon followed by “Level · Intermediate” in fine sans-serif font.

Below the hero, introduce a section divided by a thin horizontal taupe rule spanning the content width, giving the feel of a section boundary in a magazine. This section is titled “Your Editorial Dashboard” in smaller serif text, centered at the top of the section. Beneath that, use an asymmetrical three-column grid. The left column takes about 50% width and shows a “Reading List” tile stack; the middle and right columns are narrower, containing “Practice Queue” and “Community Highlights” respectively. Each tile or module resembles a magazine article excerpt card: off-white background, hairline borders, and a tiny drop shadow to distinguish it from the page.

In the left “Reading List” column, at the top left is the section label in uppercase sans-serif: “Reading List”. Next to the label, right-aligned within this column, is a small ghost button “View all” in small caps, with a simple underlined text rather than a framed button. Underneath are three stacked article cards with equal vertical spacing. Each card has a thin taupe left border running full height, about 3px thick, like the edge of a printed column. The first card shows at the top a category tag in a pill: “JAVASCRIPT” in tiny uppercase white text on a taupe background. Below it is a headline in serif: “Callbacks vs Promises: A Practical Story”. A short two-line excerpt in gray sans-serif follows: “Follow the evolution of async patterns through real-world snippets and annotated refactors.” At the bottom of the card, a micro-row displays a tiny calendar icon (flat, monochrome taupe) followed by “Today · 18 min read” in small text, and to its right a small bookmark outline icon indicating it can be saved. The second card has a teal pill tag “FRONTEND”, title “Styling Systems that Scale”, and an excerpt: “From utility-first CSS to design tokens, explore layout strategies that won’t fight you later.” The meta line reads “Yesterday · 24 min read”. The third card has pill tag “CAREER”, title “Writing Code Reviews with Editorial Clarity”, excerpt: “Apply editorial thinking to your comments — framing feedback like a thoughtful editor’s note.” Meta line: “Mon · 9 min read”.

The middle column “Practice Queue” begins with its title in uppercase sans-serif and a tiny icon of a checkmark box in taupe. Beneath are modular practice task rows styled like compact index entries. Each row has a left-side label and a right-side status. For example, first row: on the left, bold text “Array method drills”; beneath it in smaller text “7 practice prompts · JavaScript basics”. On the right, aligned top-right, a rounded label with a soft teal background and white text “In progress”. The second row: “Layout sketching with CSS Grid” with subtext “3 scenarios · Visualizing columns & rows”; on the right, a subtle taupe outlined badge “Queued”. The third row: “Error handling narratives” with subtext “Turn crash logs into stories”, and right-aligned badge with charcoal background and white text “Due today”. Each row is separated by ultra-thin light grey horizontal lines, reminiscent of ledger lines in a printed schedule.

The right column “Community Highlights” features user-content cards. The section title reads “Community Highlights” and below it a smaller tagline: “Notes from the Loopline readership”. The first highlight card shows a quoted community post. At the top, a small circular avatar (different person than the header, photorealistic, lit softly, background neutral) followed by “@dev_aurora” in bold small sans-serif and “· 3h ago” in muted text. Below is a short quote in italic serif: “Loved how today’s async lab felt like editing a puzzle — each refactor page made the story clearer.” Beneath the quote, two small tags in pill form: “#feedback” in taupe outline and “#async” in teal outline. A thin rule separates this from the next highlight, which is a “Top Thread” card: title in small caps “Top Thread” with a tiny chat-bubble icon, then a bold serif headline: “How do you ‘edit’ your side projects?” and below, a subline “42 replies · Latest at 2:19 PM”. The card ends with a small inline list of three circular overlapping avatars indicating participants.

Continuing downward, introduce a full-width section that feels like a feature spread in a magazine. This section showcases “Tracks & Series” and uses a two-row, two-column grid with asymmetry. The section background remains off-white but is visually separated by increased top and bottom padding and another subtle horizontal rule at its top. The section heading is centered at the top and reads: “Curated series, like recurring columns” in serif, with a smaller sans-serif subtitle below: “Follow long-form learning arcs designed as digital editorial series.” Below, the content: On the first row, the left column features a large series card and the right column has two smaller stacked cards. The large card on the left has a pale teal background with a soft vignette in the corners, and white serif text. At the top, a tiny tag: “Signature Track”. The main title: “From Pseudocode to Production”, big and bold, left-aligned. Under it, a three-line description: “Eight episodes that map the journey of an idea from scribbles in your notebook to deployable code, with annotated checkpoints and editorial cues.” At the bottom, a slim horizontal information strip with three small segments separated by vertical lines: “8 episodes”, “Average 40 min”, “New issue every Monday”. The two smaller stacked cards on the right have more neutral backgrounds and are separated by a narrow gap. The top one is titled “Refactoring Clinic” with a subheading “Case-by-case annotation of real-world code.” It includes a small monochrome illustration of an open notebook with code-like lines on one page and editing marks (circles, arrows) on the other, drawn in a flat line-art style. The bottom card is “Frontend Layout Almanac” with subheading “Short studies in responsive composition, from mobile notes to widescreen spreads.” It uses a simple schematic illustration: three overlapping screens (phone, tablet, desktop) each with skeletal grid lines, in taupe and grey.

On the second row of this section, invert the asymmetry: the right column holds a large card and the left column holds two smaller stacked cards. The smaller card on the top left is “Console Diaries” with subtext “Debugging as daily journaling practice”, and the lower one is “Review Room Roundtables” with subtext “Curated discussions from mentor-led code reviews.” Each has a tiny rectangular badge in the top-right corner indicating difficulty, such as “Beginner” or “Advanced”, with teal or taupe backgrounds. The large right card is dedicated to a “Featured Guest Series”. It has a deep charcoal background, contrasting the rest of the page, with the title in large white serif: “Guest Editor Series: Systems Thinking in JS”. The description in slightly smaller grayish-white sans-serif: “Each month, a guest engineer edits a week of lessons, bringing their own narrative, metaphors, and marginalia to the material.” At the bottom left, a minimalist flat-design portrait illustration: a side-profile of a person with short hair, in simple shapes, with a teal silhouette against the charcoal background, with no facial details, just abstract contour; this sits inside a small circular frame. To the right of the portrait, the text “Current Guest Editor · Mina K.” in small caps.

Further down, have a thin horizontal line that subtly separates the next area, which is a three-column footer-like dashboard zone titled “Account & Progress” aligned left in bold serif. The three columns have equal width and are visually aligned with the columns above. The left column, “Session Summary”, is a compact panel with a subtle grey background. It lists three lines of text formatted like a summary box in a print report: the top line in small caps “Today’s Session”, below that a bold big text “62 minutes focused”, and below that a smaller line “You’ve completed 3 of 4 planned segments.” There is a bullet-style list of those segments: “Intro: Async storytelling”, “Practice: Callback to Promise rewrite”, “Lab: Error narratives”, “Review: Queueing for tomorrow”. Each bulleted item is preceded by a tiny circular indicator; filled circles for completed items, an empty circle outline for the remaining one. The middle column, “Subscription & Access”, displays the user’s plan details. It has a title in uppercase sans-serif, and beneath it, a subtle box with a thin border. Inside, the plan name “Loopline Pro · Annual” in bold, followed by “Renewal date: May 14, 2026”. Underneath, there are three text buttons stacked vertically: “Manage billing”, “Pause subscription”, “Apply a gift code”, each aligned left, charcoal text, with a small right-pointing arrow glyph to the right. The right column, “Notifications”, contains a vertical list of recent alerts, each styled like a small editorial footnote. The first item reads “New margin notes added to ‘Callbacks vs Promises’”, with subtext “From Editor · 1h ago”. The second: “Your comment was highlighted in ‘Layout sketching with CSS Grid’ thread”, with subtext “Community · 4h ago”. Each notification is separated by a thin horizontal rule, and a small bell icon (outline only) sits next to the “Notifications” title.

Finally, at the bottom of the page, there is a minimalist footer aligned with the main content width, separated from the content above by extra spacing and a faint taupe rule. The footer uses a two-column layout. On the left, the brand is repeated in small: the logo icon and “Loopline Studio” in serif, followed by a short, quiet tagline in tiny sans-serif: “Learning, edited for developers.” Below that, three small inline links: “About”, “Editorial policy”, “Support”. On the right side of the footer, right-aligned, there’s a tiny language selector “Language: EN” with a subtle downward arrow, and below it a small line of text “© 2026 Loopline Studio · All rights reserved.” The entire page maintains a calm, print-inspired aesthetic: no animations, no motion, no charts or data visualizations, only statically laid-out typographic and card-based elements arranged in a multi-column grid that feels like a modern digital magazine dashboard for coding education.

instruction: Planning the generation details for the following website:
input:
Design a tall, scrolling event landing page for a “Swiss Mind & Motion Weekend” yoga retreat, executed in a strict Swiss-style typography aesthetic with a flexible multi-column grid and intentional asymmetries. The overall background is pure white (#FFFFFF), with black (#000000) and a single accent color—vibrant vermilion red (#E3342F)—used sparingly for emphasis, rules, and highlight blocks. Typefaces are limited to a single sans-serif family used in strict hierarchy: “Helvetica Neue” or a very similar neo-grotesque, with bold uppercase for titles, medium weight for subheads, and regular weight for body. All typography respects a baseline grid, and the entire page uses a 12-column layout with generous gutters. The visual language is stark and clean: no drop shadows, no gradients, no decorative flourishes, no rounded corners. Only rectangles, straight lines, and solid-color blocks. Images are minimal, composited as rigid rectangles aligned to the grid, and treated almost as graphic fields. There are no animations or videos; everything is static and feels like a printed Swiss design poster translated into a responsive webpage.

At the very top, a slim horizontal strip spanning the full width, 56px tall on desktop, contains the primary navigation aligned to the grid. At the far left, anchored to the first and second columns, the event “logo” is purely typographic: a two-line lockup reading “SWISS / MIND & MOTION” in tight uppercase black text. “SWISS” is on the first line in “Helvetica Neue Bold, 12px, letter-spacing: 0.12em” and “MIND & MOTION” is directly below in “Helvetica Neue Bold, 14px, letter-spacing: 0.08em”; both are left-aligned, flush to an invisible vertical grid line. To the right, starting around column 6, the navigation menu is in a straight horizontal row: “ABOUT”, “SCHEDULE”, “RETREAT GUIDES”, “LOCATION”, “REGISTER”. Each item is set in “Helvetica Neue Medium, 11px, uppercase, letter-spacing: 0.16em” with generous spacing between words, aligned on the baseline. Hover and active states (though visually static here) are represented by thin, 1px black underlines that sit directly beneath the text. At the far right, aligned to columns 11–12, there is a slim outlined button reading “REGISTER NOW”. It is a simple 1px black rectangular outline, no fill, with text in “Helvetica Neue Bold, 11px, uppercase” centered both vertically and horizontally; the button’s height aligns with the nav strip height so it looks like a precise modular box.

Below this, the hero section occupies almost the full viewport height. It is divided into asymmetric columns. On the left two-thirds (columns 1–8), a large, bold typographic block features the event title in a strict grid-based composition. Top-left, there is a small label in vermilion: a filled rectangular strip spanning the width of columns 1–3, 24px high, containing the text “YOGA RETREAT” in “Helvetica Neue Bold, 10px, uppercase, white text, letter-spacing: 0.22em, centered”. Directly beneath, starting at a generous margin, the title reads in three lines: “SWISS” on the first, “YOGA” on the second, “WEEKEND” on the third. Each line is “Helvetica Neue Bold, 62px, all caps, black” with tight leading so the lines visually lock into a strong rectangular block. The words are left-aligned and slightly indented from the left edge of the grid. Under the title, a concise subheading sits in the same column width, reading: “A minimal, alpine-inspired retreat for clarity, breath, and grounding.” in sentence case, “Helvetica Neue Regular, 16px, line-height 1.6, black”. Below that subheading, there is an even smaller body paragraph, three lines, describing the ethos in a neutral tone: “Spend three days in the Swiss Alps exploring breathwork, slow flow yoga, and mindful stillness. Structured like a Swiss poster, lived like a deep exhale.” set in “Helvetica Neue Regular, 13px, black”, aligned left.

At the bottom of the hero text column, aligning with the baseline grid, two horizontal calls-to-action sit side by side with crisp structure. On the left, a solid black rectangle button labeled “RESERVE YOUR SPOT” in white, “Helvetica Neue Bold, 11px, uppercase, letter-spacing 0.14em” centered. To the right, a ghost button with 1px black border and no fill reading “VIEW SCHEDULE” in black text, same type treatment. Between them, there is a 16px gap. Both buttons are exactly 44px in height and extend across columns 1–4 (left) and 5–8 (right).

On the right third of the hero (columns 9–12), there is a vertical, grid-aligned image block extending from just under the nav until about two-thirds of the viewport height. The image is a high-contrast, black-and-white photograph treated in a clean, documentary style: an overhead shot of a group of five people on minimalist yoga mats arranged in a perfect grid in a bright studio with concrete floor. The camera is directly above (orthographic-style perspective), capturing geometric alignments of the mats as strict rectangles. Each person is in a simple seated pose, faceless silhouettes softened by diffused top lighting. Shadows are soft, minimal, and fall in consistent directions, emphasizing the grid structure rather than individual features. There is no clutter—just the mats, floor, and the framed bodies. Around the image, there is a thin white margin aligned to the columns. Below the photo, in small uppercase text, there is a caption: “PHOTO: MINDFUL GRID PRACTICE, STUDIO ZÜRICH” in “Helvetica Neue Regular, 9px, black, letter-spacing 0.18em”, left-aligned to the image edge.

Directly under the hero, a horizontal strip acts as an info bar spanning full width, 80px in height, with a very light grey background (#F7F7F7). Inside, three columns of concise event details are evenly spaced (each occupying four grid columns). Each block is arranged vertically with label and info. The first block has label “DATES” in small uppercase, “Helvetica Neue Medium, 9px, letter-spacing 0.24em, black” and below it in a larger type, “SEPT 20–22, 2025” in “Helvetica Neue Bold, 14px, black”. The second block label is “LOCATION” with main text “FLIMS, SWITZERLAND” in the same format. The third is “CAPACITY” with “ONLY 32 PARTICIPANTS” in bold. Subtle 1px vertical dividers in light grey (#E0E0E0) separate the three blocks, aligned perfectly to the baseline.

The next section, “ABOUT THE RETREAT”, is structured as a three-column layout with intentional asymmetry. On the left (columns 1–4), near the top, the section title “ABOUT THE RETREAT” is in uppercase, “Helvetica Neue Bold, 18px, black”. Below it, a horizontal black rule, 2px thick and exactly as wide as the title text, underscores it. Beneath, a paragraph of body text runs about 5–6 lines: “Swiss Mind & Motion Weekend is a curated three-day experience focusing on precision, presence, and simplicity. Each session is structured to balance quiet introspection with intentional movement, echoing the calm order of Swiss design.” set in “Helvetica Neue Regular, 13px, line-height 1.7, black”. A second short paragraph in smaller type (12px) adds: “Expect guided yoga practices, silent morning walks, and structured rest periods—no digital distractions, no clutter, just clear space and clean breath.” aligned left. In the center column (columns 5–8), there’s a stacked list of program highlights, each separated by a thin 1px black line that spans the column width. Each highlight occupies its own rectangular vertical block: a small heading in uppercase (e.g., “MORNING SILENCE PRACTICE”, “ALPINE BREATHWORK LAB”, “EVENING SLOW FLOW”), “Helvetica Neue Medium, 11px” followed by one concise line in regular 12px explaining it. The text is left-aligned, with plenty of white space between lines. On the right (columns 9–12), there is a tall vertical photograph cropped as a rigid column: a black-and-white image of a minimalist alpine interior, shot straight-on. The composition shows a large window with thin black frames opening to a distant view of vague mountain silhouettes, with a single yoga mat lying parallel to the window. The room is bare: smooth concrete floor, white walls, no decor. Soft natural light streams in, creating a sharp rectangle of light on the floor that aligns visually to the grid lines. A tiny caption beneath reads: “VENUE: ALPINE STUDIO, FLIMS” in 9px uppercase.

Further down, a large section titled “PROGRAM STRUCTURE” uses the full width but respects the 12-column grid. The title sits near the left margin over columns 1–4 in uppercase, “Helvetica Neue Bold, 18px, black”. To its right, spanning columns 5–12, a thin horizontal red rule in vermilion (#E3342F) runs across, slicing the white space and aligning with the title’s baseline. Below this, the section is split horizontally into two bands. The top band is a three-column layout summarizing each day: Day 1, Day 2, Day 3. Each occupies four grid columns. Each day column has a small label “DAY 1 / ARRIVAL + GROUNDING”, “DAY 2 / DEEP PRACTICE”, “DAY 3 / INTEGRATION + DEPARTURE” in uppercase, 11px, medium weight. Under each, a timestamp list is set in monospaced-style layout using Helvetica with equal spacing, like: “08:00–09:00  SILENT WALK”, “09:30–11:00  FOUNDATIONS FLOW”, etc. Each line is left-aligned with the times and activity separated by two spaces, giving a clean, information-design feel. Text is “Helvetica Neue Regular, 11px, black”. A subtle 1px horizontal line separates each time block, aligning horizontally across all three columns for visual rhythm.

The lower band of this section, separated by a generous margin of white space, holds a “WHO IT’S FOR” and “WHAT YOU’LL NEED” dual-column layout. On the left (columns 1–6), the subheading “WHO IT’S FOR” in uppercase, 13px, bold, with a short paragraph: “For practitioners of all levels who appreciate structure, silence, and understated design. No previous yoga experience required—only a willingness to slow down.” On the right (columns 7–12), subheading “WHAT YOU’LL NEED” with a bulletless list of short lines, each beginning with a small black square (2x2px) aligned to a vertical grid line, followed by text: “Comfortable clothes for movement”, “Warm layers for cool alpine mornings”, “A notebook and pen (no laptops during sessions)”, “An open, curious mindset.” All text in black, 12px.

Next, an intentionally stark section presents the retreat guides in a modular card layout with strong vertical alignments. It begins with the title “RETREAT GUIDES” in uppercase, bold, 18px, left-aligned at column 1. Below, the grid is divided into three equal vertical cards occupying columns 1–4, 5–8, and 9–12, each card separated by 24px of white space. Each card is a simple black border rectangle (1px stroke) with no fill, extending down into a consistent height. At the top of each card, there is a rectangular greyscale portrait image area. The portraits are minimalist black-and-white photographs, with clean, even lighting and uncluttered backgrounds. For example, the first portrait shows a woman in profile, short hair, neutral expression, looking slightly downward, against a plain white backdrop; the composition is centered, shoulders cropped mid-chest, with soft but directionally consistent lighting from the left, casting a thin shadow on the right cheek. The second shows a man facing the camera, arms crossed loosely, wearing a simple black t-shirt, with a plain light grey background; the third depicts a non-binary person seated on the floor, legs crossed, back straight, minimal background. All images maintain a neutral, calm mood with no dramatic contrast, emphasizing clarity and structure. Under each photo, the guide’s name is printed in uppercase, bold, 13px, e.g., “ELENA MÜLLER”, “JONAS KELLER”, “ARI LEE”. Beneath the name, their role is in smaller uppercase, 10px: “LEAD YOGA FACILITATOR”, “BREATHWORK & SILENCE”, “EMBODIED DESIGN & MOVEMENT”. A short bio paragraph in regular 11px tells their background in a clean, neutral tone, confined to three or four lines, left-aligned. Below each bio, a set of small uppercase tags, each within a thin outlined rectangle with no fill, like “VINYASA”, “MINDFULNESS”, “GRAPHIC DESIGN”, appear in one line, separated by 4px gaps, using “Helvetica Neue Medium, 9px.”

After the guides, a strong asymmetrical section titled “SPACE & SURROUNDINGS” breaks the page’s rhythm. The left side (columns 1–7) is occupied by a large black-and-white photograph of the Swiss alpine landscape. The photo is a wide, high-resolution shot with a very linear, almost architectural composition: a low, minimalist building with a flat roof and large glass panes in the foreground, and behind it, layered silhouettes of mountains fading into atmospheric haze. The horizon line is positioned roughly one-third up from the bottom, aligning with a grid line. The sky is overcast but light, reinforcing a calm, monotone palette. The right side (columns 8–12) contains a vertical sequence of text blocks. At the top, the heading “A CLEAR FRAME FOR YOUR PRACTICE” in uppercase, 14px, bold. Below that, a descriptive paragraph, 12px, about the venue: “The retreat takes place in a purpose-built studio near Flims, surrounded by quiet forest and mountains. Floor-to-ceiling windows frame the alpine landscape like a poster, while pared-back interiors keep your attention on the present moment.” Under this, there is a minimalist mini-map representation: not an interactive map, but a static graphic block 200px tall, white background, with a simple black outline of Switzerland in flat, abstract style (like a contour shape). A small black dot indicates approximate location, captioned “Flims” in 10px. Below the map, a two-line text in uppercase 10px reads: “EASILY ACCESSIBLE BY TRAIN FROM ZÜRICH. SHUTTLE AVAILABLE FROM FLIMS STATION.”

Next comes the “PRICING & OPTIONS” section, centered in the page but left-aligned text. The main heading is in uppercase, bold, 18px, and sits slightly right of center to create asymmetry, starting at column 3. Below it, a horizontal black rule spans across columns 3–10. Two pricing options are presented below in a side-by-side configuration: “STANDARD” on the left (columns 2–6) and “SUPPORTER” on the right (columns 7–11). Each option is contained in a simple black outline rectangle with ample padding inside. The option name is at the top in uppercase 11px; below that, the price is rendered as “CHF 680” and “CHF 820” respectively, in “Helvetica Neue Bold, 26px”, black. A short description below each in regular 12px explains what’s included: “Shared room, all meals, all sessions, shuttle from Flims station.” and “Private room, all meals, all sessions, additional 1:1 alignment session.” At the bottom of each pricing box, a thin horizontal vermilion strip (4px high) runs the full width of the card, acting as a subtle highlight. Under the boxes, aligned to the left margin of the section, a single line of text in 11px notes: “Sliding-scale options available upon request. We reserve a limited number of spots for reduced-fee participants.” in regular weight.

The next section focuses on practical FAQs, styled like an understated brochure. The heading “FAQ” is large, uppercase, 18px, left-aligned at column 1. Underneath, the content is arranged in two columns (columns 1–6 and 7–12) of accordion-like question blocks, but visually represented as static separated items with no interactivity. Each question is a short, uppercase line in 11px medium weight, followed by its answer in 11px regular. Between each Q&A, a long 1px grey line (#D9D9D9) stretches across the column width. Example questions: “DO I NEED PRIOR YOGA EXPERIENCE?”, “WHAT IS THE CANCELLATION POLICY?”, “ARE MEALS INCLUDED?”, each followed by concise, calm answers set in sentence case, left-aligned and respecting the baseline grid.

Near the bottom of the page, a strong call-to-action section with a vermilion backdrop spans the full width, breaking the mostly white layout. The background is solid vermilion (#E3342F). On top, all text and elements are in white. The section height is approximately 300–360px. On the left (columns 1–7), the heading reads “READY TO STEP INTO STILLNESS?” in uppercase, “Helvetica Neue Bold, 24px, white”, aligned left and anchored near the top. Below it, a short paragraph in 13px white: “Secure your spot for Swiss Mind & Motion Weekend. We will review your registration and confirm within 48 hours.” At the base of the left side, a large white button with no border reads “REGISTER NOW” in vermilion text, uppercase, 12px bold, centered; the button is 48px tall and spans columns 1–4. Beneath the button, in small uppercase 9px, white text reads: “LIMITED TO 32 PARTICIPANTS.” On the right side of this red section (columns 8–12), a minimal contact form appears, aligned to the grid. It contains three text fields stacked vertically for “Name”, “Email”, and “Briefly, what draws you to this retreat?”, each with white 1px underlines only (no full rectangle borders) and white placeholder labels in uppercase 9px above each line. The fields themselves are described as white-on-red text entry lines, with sample placeholder text in a soft white tint, e.g., “ENTER YOUR NAME”. Below the fields, a tiny white checkbox with the label “I agree to the retreat guidelines.” in 9px uppercase sits left-aligned. The form submit button is a simple white outline rectangle labeled “SUBMIT INQUIRY” in white uppercase 11px, aligned under the fields within columns 8–11.

Finally, the footer rests on a very light grey background (#F4F4F4) and spans the full width with a height of about 220px. Inside, content is arranged across three columns. On the left (columns 1–4), the event name appears again, smaller: “SWISS MIND & MOTION WEEKEND” in uppercase, 11px, bold, black, followed by a two-line address in 11px regular: “Hosted by Studio Linie Klar / Flims, Switzerland”. Below, a tiny line of text: “Designed with a Swiss grid. Practiced with a quiet mind.” On the center column (columns 5–8), there is a simple contact block: label “CONTACT” in uppercase 10px, and below it an email address “hello@mindandmotion.ch”, and a phone number in international format, both in 11px regular. On the right (columns 9–12), there is a minimal text-only social and policy section with small uppercase links: “INSTAGRAM”, “TERMS”, “PRIVACY”. Each word is spaced horizontally with vertical dividers (“|”) in between. At the very bottom edge of the page, centered, a single thin black horizontal line runs from column 1 to column 12, and under it, in 9px uppercase grey text, centered, is the copyright line: “© 2025 SWISS MIND & MOTION WEEKEND. ALL RIGHTS RESERVED.” The entire page feels like a series of precise posters stacked in sequence, heavily guided by a multi-column grid, the consistent use of Helvetica, sharp rectangular blocks, and deliberate negative space, with no animations, videos, or data visualizations—only pure Swiss-style typographic clarity in service of a yoga retreat experience.

instruction: Planning the generation details for the following website:
input:
Design a high-contrast, graphic, two-column research-oriented homepage for a fictional site called “Spectral Craft: Research in Luxury Jewelry Tutorials.” The overall aesthetic is stark and editorial, with a monochrome base (deep matte black and crisp white) punctuated by a single accent color: a saturated metallic gold (#D9B55A) used sparingly for emphasis. The entire page sits on a pure black background, with generous white margins and asymmetric, rectangular blocks that hint at a two-column grid without strictly obeying it, producing a slightly off-kilter, avant-garde feel. The typography is bold and modern: all headings are set in a geometric sans-serif that resembles Futura or Avenir in all caps, ultra-bold, and tightly tracked; body text is a highly legible serif resembling Times or Georgia, with a slightly increased line height for a research publication feel. All text is white on black, except for small accent labels and subtle rules in gold. There is no animation, no video, and no charts; every visual is static and carefully composed.

At the very top, a solid black horizontal strip spans full width, with a thin gold line at the bottom acting as a subtle separator. Centered vertically in this strip is a minimalist logo on the left: a flat, geometric icon—a white outlined diamond shape made of intersecting straight lines, quasi-technical drawing style—followed by the site title in uppercase white text: “SPECTRAL CRAFT.” The logo and title sit flush left with a 60px margin from the edge. To the right, arranged horizontally but with deliberate spacing, are navigation links: “Research Focus,” “Tutorial Library,” “Methodology,” “About the Lab,” and “Access Portal.” Each nav item is white uppercase text in a smaller geometric sans font, spaced evenly with about 30px between them. On the far right is a pill-shaped outline button with a thin gold border and transparent black interior, containing the text “Join the Study” in white uppercase. When described, the button should have a subtle drop shadow (soft, diffuse, dark gray) to indicate interactivity, but no animation.

Beneath the nav, the hero section dominates the initial viewport. The hero is a split two-column layout with asymmetric proportions: the left column taking about 40% width, the right about 60%. The left column is a vertical block of rich black with white text, aligned to the center vertically. At the top of this column, a small gold uppercase label reads “RESEARCH PLATFORM.” Below this, in large white uppercase letters, is the main headline: “DECONSTRUCTING LUXURY: A RESEARCH-DRIVEN APPROACH TO JEWELRY TUTORIALS.” The headline spans about four lines and is left-aligned. Under the headline is a short paragraph of body text in white serif, describing: “Spectral Craft is a research lab exploring how creators learn, teach, and perfect luxury jewelry skills online. We dissect tutorials, map learning pathways, and publish structured guidance for advanced makers.” This text is no more than four sentences. Below the paragraph are two horizontally arranged buttons. The left button is a solid gold rectangle with white uppercase text: “Explore Research Library.” The right button is a black rectangle outlined with a fine white stroke, white uppercase text: “Download Study Abstract (PDF).” There is about 16px of spacing between the buttons. At the very bottom of the left column, in much smaller uppercase mono-spaced text, is a line: “CURRENT PHASE: OBSERVATIONAL STUDY · COHORT III” in slightly dimmer white.

The right column of the hero is a large, static, high-contrast photographic collage image, framed by a thin white border with a 20px margin between border and browser edge. This image should be described as a black-and-white, semi-photorealistic collage of jewelry-making scenes presented in a tiled composition. In the foreground, near the bottom right, a sharply lit close-up of a jeweler’s hands holding a fine ring with tweezers; the ring has a large faceted diamond rendered as a nearly white shape with sharp, geometric reflections, but the entire image remains monochrome. The hands are in soft grayscale, with deep shadows emphasizing texture. In the middle ground, slightly blurred, a top-down view of a workbench scattered with tools: calipers, skeletal ring molds, a small digital scale. They appear in silvery tones with strong specular highlights. In the background, abstract geometric overlays: thin white lines and gold outlines connecting points like a network diagram, with small gold labels such as “STEP 01,” “STEP 06,” and “MICRO-TECHNIQUE NODE” placed near certain tool clusters. The lighting is dramatic, with a single strong light source from the top left casting deep shadows toward the bottom right. The entire collage feels like a research poster and a contact sheet combined, crisp and graphic, but static.

Scrolling down, the next section occupies a full-width band but is internally divided into two main columns to the user’s eye. The section background remains black with subtle, almost imperceptible dark gray diagonal stripes at 5% opacity to add texture. On the left, a column taking 55% width is titled “Our Research Focus” in large uppercase white sans-serif. Beneath it, three subheadings stacked vertically, each in smaller bold uppercase white text, followed by a brief paragraph in white serif: “Cognitive Load in Visual Tutorials,” “Sequencing Fine-Motor Skills,” and “Designing for Expert Curiosity.” Each paragraph is a few sentences explaining how the lab studies how jewelers process complex visual information, how step sequences affect learning, and how to keep advanced practitioners engaged. Each subheading is preceded by a small, thin vertical gold line, like a minimal indicator, positioned just to the left of the text. On the right side, the column is narrower and begins about halfway down relative to the left, creating an intentional vertical misalignment. The right column features a tall, narrow static image that looks like a black-and-white blueprint of a ring. The blueprint is drawn in ultra-thin white lines on a matte black background, showing multiple views: top view, side view, and a zoomed detail of a prong setting. Around the ring are small gold annotations in tiny uppercase text such as “ANGLE OF REFRACTION,” “USER GAZE PATH,” and “HAND POSITION VECTOR.” This image is enclosed in a simple white rectangular outline with rounded corners. Under the blueprint, a short block of text in white uppercase sans-serif titled “Why Tutorials?” summarizes in one small paragraph how tutorials are treated as data for the lab.

The next section below is titled “Feature Studies” and has a more modular, almost card-like two-column grid. The background is still black, but this section is visually separated by a thicker white horizontal rule at the top and bottom. Within it, three study highlight cards are arranged as two columns and two rows: one large card on the left spanning full height, and two smaller stacked cards on the right. The large left card has a subtle dark charcoal background (#111111) with a thin white border. At its top left corner is a small gold uppercase label: “CASE STUDY 01.” Below is a mid-sized heading in white: “The 32-Step Diamond Setting Tutorial.” Below the heading is a short, tight paragraph in white serif describing the study. The lower half of this card is occupied by a static, high-contrast grayscale still image: a top-down view of a paused tutorial screen, but simplified and stylized, showing a jeweler’s bench, an overlaid grid, and white rectangular “step markers” hovering over different parts of the scene with numbers “01,” “08,” “16,” and “32” in black text on white rectangles. The background of the still is almost black; only the hands, tools, and step markers are bright, creating a stark contrast. The two smaller right cards mirror this styling but with different images and titles: the top right card labeled “CASE STUDY 02” with the title “Looping Motifs in Engagement Ring Tutorials,” and the bottom right card labeled “CASE STUDY 03” titled “Micro-Gestures: The Invisible Lessons.” Their images might show a close crop of a hand rotating a ring, and a silhouetted hand gesture sequence, respectively. Each card includes a small, white uppercase text link at the bottom right that reads “Read Abstract →” using a right-pointing arrow glyph rendered in Helvetica-like sans, white.

Below this, the layout shifts slightly: a wide, horizontally oriented “Methodology Overview” section that visually sits as a band spanning the width, with content internally constrained to the main central column. The title “Methodology Overview” is centered at the very top in uppercase white sans-serif. Under the title, however, content is arranged in a staggered two-column format. On the left, a rectangular block with a white border and a subtle inner shadow contains three numbered pillars of the methodology: “01 Data Collection,” “02 Structural Analysis,” “03 Applied Prototyping,” each title in gold uppercase with short white serif descriptions beneath. The numbers “01,” “02,” “03” are large, hollow, outline-only numerals in white that sit behind the text, partially overlapping, giving a layered, graphic effect. On the right, offset slightly down, is a static conceptual illustration: a flat-design, black-and-white schematic diagram of a “tutorial dissection board.” It shows a long horizontal white rectangle representing a timeline, with vertical white stripes dividing it into equal segments. Small gold rectangles float above certain segments, labeled “Gesture,” “Camera Move,” “Tool Change,” “Concept Shift” in tiny uppercase white text. This illustration has strictly no animation; it is like a frozen high-contrast schematic. Below the illustration is a single line of white monospace text: “We dissect visual sequences frame by frame, mapping structure and intent.”

Next, a “Tutorial Library Preview” section uses the two-column layout in a more literal way. The background is flat black, but within the central content width, the left column is dedicated to filters and taxonomy, and the right column shows a grid of research-tagged tutorial exemplars. On the left, at the top, a small uppercase gold label: “LIBRARY INDEX.” Below, a white uppercase heading: “Structured Tutorial Types.” Underneath are three category blocks stacked vertically: “High-Fidelity Workshop,” “Studio Vignette,” and “Macro Detail Study.” Each category name appears in bold uppercase white text, followed by a one-sentence description. Beside each category, aligned to the right edge of the column, is a tiny, empty square outlined in white, like a filter checkbox (but static here). The right column shows three large horizontal “tutorial preview strips” stacked vertically, each spanning nearly the full right column width. Each strip is a static, wide, high-contrast grayscale image reminiscent of a filmstrip: a horizontal sequence of four rectangular frames separated by thin white lines. The top strip, titled in white uppercase text above it “Platinum Solitaire Workshop,” shows four frames: a close-up of hands marking metal, a saw cutting a band, a torch flame frozen as a bright white shape, and a ring being polished. The second strip, “Black Studio Close-Ups,” shows stylized close-ups of rings and bracelets on a deep black surface, with dramatic spotlighting and harsh shadows. The third strip, “Experimental Gem Cuts,” shows abstract, refracted light patterns over faceted stones, almost scientific in appearance. Under each strip is a small line of text in white serif: “Annotated for structural pattern analysis.” No strip moves; they are static, crisp slices of content.

Further down, an “About the Lab” section reintroduces asymmetry. The background remains black, but the content is contained in a slightly narrower column to create generous side margins. The title is left-aligned and reads “About the Lab” in uppercase white sans-serif. The layout is split again: on the left, a rectangular portrait-style image; on the right, a text block. The image is a high-contrast, black-and-white photograph of a minimal, modern studio space taken from a slightly elevated angle. The studio features a long, black table with a single ring under a spotlight, a large monitor showing a frozen frame of a jewelry tutorial (only abstract shapes visible), and a simple white board on the wall covered in thin, hand-drawn white lines and sticky-note-like rectangles representing mapped tutorial arcs. The lighting is stark, with one strong overhead light creating deep shadows. There are no people visible; the focus is the workspace and its analytical tools. This image is framed with a thin white border. On the right side, the text is in white serif, three short paragraphs describing the fictional research team’s background in digital education, jewelry design, and cognitive psychology. Below the paragraphs is a horizontal list of three “lead investigators” presented as text only, no portraits: each line showing a name in bold uppercase white followed by their discipline in smaller uppercase gold, such as “ELENA RIOS — DIGITAL PEDAGOGY,” “MARCUS KLEIN — LUXURY DESIGN,” and “DR. NEHA AGRAWAL — COGNITIVE SCIENCE.”

Toward the bottom of the page, add a stark “Access & Participation” section designed as a high-contrast call to action but still research-focused. This section has a faint dark-gray rectangular panel centered on the black background, creating a framed effect. At the top left of the panel, a small gold uppercase label reads “ACCESS & PARTICIPATION.” Below, in larger uppercase white text spanning two lines: “REQUEST ACCESS TO THE RESEARCH LIBRARY.” A concise paragraph in white serif underneath describes criteria for access, limitations, and how the lab collaborates with expert makers and educators. Below the paragraph is a two-column layout: on the left, a very minimal static “sign-up form” depiction; on the right, a brief FAQ. The form column shows three horizontal white-underlined text fields with placeholder text in small gray uppercase: “Full Name,” “Professional Focus,” and “Institution or Studio.” Beneath, there is a small white checkbox outline accompanied by the label “I agree to participate in non-commercial research uses.” Below the fields is a wide, rectangular gold button with white uppercase text: “Submit Access Request.” The FAQ column lists three questions in small uppercase white sans-serif, each followed by a short white serif answer: “Who is this research for?” “Can I reference your findings?” and “Do you run live sessions?” Answers emphasize that this is a research site, not a public tutorial platform, and that participants receive structured write-ups instead of live classes.

Finally, the footer is a compressed but sharply divided band at the bottom. The background is pure black with a thick gold horizontal line at the top separating it from the rest of the page. Inside, content is arranged in two columns. On the left, a small version of the diamond line icon appears in white, with the text “SPECTRAL CRAFT RESEARCH LAB” in uppercase white to its right. Below, in much smaller white serif, is one sentence: “Spectral Craft is a fictional research initiative exploring the design of luxury jewelry tutorials.” On the right, aligned right, are three vertically stacked white uppercase links: “Contact,” “Ethics & Consent,” and “Imprint.” At the very bottom center, in tiny uppercase gray text, is the line: “No animations, no video, no tracking — static by design.” The entire page should feel like a hybrid between a luxury brand, a research lab, and a stark graphic magazine layout: strictly high-contrast, with clean lines, hard edges, rectangular shapes, static imagery, and a consistent two-column logic that occasionally breaks for visual tension, but never uses motion, charts, or data visualizations.

instruction: Planning the generation details for the following website:
input:
Design a playful, two-column dashboard homepage for a “digital illustration subscription studio” called “ColorBloom Studio,” aimed at indie creators and small brands. The page sits on a full-bleed, soft cream background (#FFF8F0) with scattered pastel, semi-transparent doodles—tiny stars, circles, paint splatters, and squiggles—in mint (#C9F5E8), peach (#FFD6C2), and lavender (#E5D7FF) faintly floating behind everything like watermarks, giving the page a whimsical, hand-crafted feel. The main layout favors a two-column structure with asymmetries: the left column is slightly wider, housing navigation and primary content; the right column holds summary widgets and user info, designed as playful floating “cards.” Use a rounded, friendly sans-serif for all headings—such as “Baloo” or a similar bubbly font—and a clean, modern sans-serif like “Inter” for body text. Headings are in deep charcoal (#333333) for contrast, body text in a soft gray (#666666). The default accent color is a bright, playful coral (#FF6F61), used sparingly for buttons, icons, and highlights. At the top, a sticky header bar runs across the entire width, about 80px tall, with a very subtle linear gradient from pale peach (#FFEAE0) on the left to pale mint (#E6FFF7) on the right. On the far left of the header, aligned with soft padding, sits the ColorBloom Studio logo: a circular icon approximately 40px in diameter, containing a flat-design illustration of three overlapping blobs of color (coral, teal, and yellow) forming an abstract flower, with a small white sparkle icon near the top-right edge; to the right of the icon is the logotype text “ColorBloom Studio” in bold, dark charcoal, with “ColorBloom” slightly larger than “Studio”. Centered in the header is a simple search bar: a rounded rectangle with a white background, subtle shadow, and a faint grey outline; inside on the left is a small magnifying glass icon in coral, followed by placeholder text “Search illustrations, artists, or packs…” in light gray. On the right side of the header is a tiny horizontal cluster: a small bell icon filled in coral with a white dot indicating “3” notifications (the number rendered as a tiny white circle overlapping the top-right), a circular avatar placeholder for the user—a simple flat illustration of a smiling face in pastel tones, or if generated, a stylized cartoon head with purple hair and a mint background—and to the right, the username text “Hey, Luna!” in medium-weight charcoal. Beneath the header, the page body begins with a main content area split into two columns: the left column occupies roughly 65% of the width, the right column 35%, with a generous central gutter. Everything is neatly padded and aligned to an invisible grid with ample white space between modules.

At the top of the left column is a “Welcome panel” card that feels like the hero of the dashboard. This card has rounded corners, a subtle drop shadow, and a background gradient that transitions from soft lavender at the top-left (#F2E9FF) to pale pink at the bottom-right (#FFE6F0). On the left half of this card is text; on the right half is a whimsical illustrative image. The text block at the top-left reads, in big friendly type, “Good morning, Luna!” on one line, and below it, slightly smaller but still large and bold, “Ready to splash some color today?” All text is left-aligned. Beneath this, a one-line subheading in smaller, regular-weight gray text: “Here’s a snapshot of your illustration world in one cozy place.” Under the text block are two rounded buttons side-by-side. The primary button is a vibrant coral pill with white, bold text that says “Start a New Project” and a tiny white plus icon inside a circle on its left. The secondary button is an outlined style with a white background, rounded edges, a coral border, and coral text reading “Browse Inspiration”. The illustrative image on the right side of this welcome card shows a flat, cartoonish scene of a digital workspace: a tablet on a desk viewed at a slight three-quarter angle, with a stylus floating slightly above it. On the tablet’s screen is a colourful abstract illustration—big organic shapes, like blobs of teal, sunshine yellow, coral, and purple layered together, with a couple of doodled stars and squiggly lines. Around the tablet float small, flat icons: a tiny color palette with blobs of paint, a smiling star, and a miniature folder with a heart on it. The background behind the desk is simple, with a soft gradient and a couple of faint, oversized circles in pastel colors. Lighting is soft and even; no hard shadows, but subtle shading to give the elements slight depth in a playful, vector-art style.

Directly underneath this hero card in the left column, there is a narrow horizontal section titled “Today’s Shortcuts,” presented as text and icon chips. The title “Today’s Shortcuts” is in medium-weight bold, charcoal text, left aligned. Below it is a row of round-cornered pill buttons that look like tags, each with a small flat icon to the left: “Upload sketch” (icon of a paper with an upward arrow in mint), “Request custom pack” (icon of a small magic wand with sparkles), “Invite collaborator” (icon of two overlapping smiling faces), and “View brand boards” (icon of a small grid of four squares). Each pill has a white background with a thin pastel border and changes to a slightly filled pastel when hovered, implied by the design.

Below the shortcut row, still in the left column, sits a two-card full-width stack. The upper card is “Your Active Projects” and the lower card is “Latest from the Studio”. The “Your Active Projects” card is a white, rounded rectangle with a soft shadow and a coral accent line along the top edge. At the top-left of this card is the heading “Your Active Projects” in bold charcoal, with a small, pastel green dot to its left for emphasis. On the top-right inside the card is a thin coral text link “View all” with a tiny right-arrow icon. Inside this card is a vertical list of three project rows, each row separated by faint horizontal divider lines. Each project row is composed of a small thumbnail image on the left, a text block in the middle, and a pill-shaped status badge on the right. The thumbnails are small rounded rectangles. Project 1 thumbnail: a stylized flat illustration of a coffee cup character with a smiling face, steam forming a heart-shape above; background is a soft beige with scattered coffee beans as tiny dark brown ovals. Project 2 thumbnail: a bright, neon-color cityscape in flat style, with buildings as rectangles and triangles, windows lit up in contrasting colors, and a gradient sky from purple to teal. Project 3 thumbnail: a pastel, dreamy landscape with rolling hills, a sun peeking over the horizon and big fluffy clouds outlined in a slightly darker pastel. To the right of each thumbnail, the project title in bold text: “Cozy Café Mascots”, “Neon Night City Pack”, and “Dreamy Dawn Wallpapers”. Below each title in smaller text is a one-line descriptor like “Illustration pack for Morning Mug Co.”, “Hero illustrations for landing page redesign”, and “Wallpaper set for subscription release #12”. On the far right, each row has a rounded status badge: one in mint with text “In progress”, one in soft amber with text “Review needed”, and one in soft lavender with text “Draft”. The badges have white text and tiny matching-colored dots at the left. 

The “Latest from the Studio” card directly beneath it is slightly shorter but similar style (white card, rounded corners, subtle shadow), with a small lavender accent line along the top edge instead of coral. The heading reads “Latest from the Studio” with the word “Studio” in coral to subtly highlight it. Inside are three horizontally-arranged content snippets, each acting like a small pill-shaped chip with an icon and a short text: the first shows a tiny icon of a brush stroke in coral and the text “New: ‘Soft Gradients’ illustration set just dropped!”, the second has a tiny megaphone icon and text “Community challenge: Draw your desk buddy”, and the third has a tiny calendar icon and text “Upcoming: Live Q&A with our lead illustrator”. These are on a single row that can overflow horizontally if space is tight but is shown as fitting neatly on larger screens.

Further down in the left column is a larger, more visual card titled “Explore Illustration Packs.” This card spans the full width of the left column and feels like a mini-gallery. The card has a very pale mint background (#F3FFF9) with rounded corners and a faint dashed border pattern in pastel colors along its edges, adding to the playful aesthetic. At the top-left of the card, in larger text, is “Explore Illustration Packs” with a small heart icon outline beside it filled with coral. Underneath that is a line of smaller gray text: “Curated bundles, ready to drop into your next project.” Below this title area, the content inside is arranged in a three-column grid of pack tiles with equal spacing between them (if screen width is limited, it could wrap into two rows of two). Each tile is a small card with an image at the top and text below. Tile 1: “Whimsical Workspace” shows a flat illustration of a cluttered yet charming desk with a laptop, potted plants, sticky notes, a steaming mug with a smiley face, and floating icons above; colors are pastel greens and oranges against a neutral background. Tile 2: “Galaxy Doodles” shows a deep navy background sprinkled with stars, planets in bright pastel rings, and smiling comets in a cartoonish style. Tile 3: “Friendly Foodies” shows cute anthropomorphic foods—smiling strawberries, a waving slice of pizza, a dancing donut—on a pale yellow background. Each tile’s text area beneath the image includes the pack name in bold and a short comfortingly chatty description like “For cozy product shots & work-from-home vibes” or “Perfect for sci-fi blogs & astro-themed feeds”, plus a small coral link “View pack”. There is a tiny label above the grid, aligned to the right, showing a pill with three text labels “Most loved”, “Newest”, “For web” like a segmented filter; “Most loved” has a filled coral background with white text, while the others are outlined.

Now move to the right column. At the top, horizontally aligned with the big welcome card, is a compact user summary card titled “Your Color Snapshot.” The card is vertically oriented and narrower, with rounded edges and a background gradient flowing from pale teal at the top (#E3FFF7) to soft yellow at the bottom (#FFF9D9). The heading near the top reads “Your Color Snapshot” in a medium-weight, slightly smaller font than the main hero heading. Under the heading are three stacked metric rows, each with a pastel, circle-shaped icon on the left and text on the right. The first row has a coral droplet icon with a tiny sparkle and text “Palette streak: 7 days in a row” in small text. The second has a lavender folder icon and text “Active packs: 12”. The third has a mint star icon and text “Favorites saved: 34”. There is no chart here, just text and icons. At the bottom of the card, a smaller, rounded button in white with coral border reads “View account details,” aligned centered.

Directly beneath this snapshot card is a “Quick Actions” card in white with rounded corners and a shadow. The title at the top-left reads “Quick Actions” in bold charcoal. Inside, three action tiles are arranged in a vertical stack with dotted dividers between them. Each tile has a small flat icon on the left and a brief task description with a friendly microcopy. First tile: an upload icon (an arrow pointing upward out of a flat folder) with text “Upload new asset” in bold and smaller text under it saying “Add your sketches or finished illustrations.” Second tile: a link chain icon with text “Connect to your brand kit” in bold and microcopy “Sync colors, logos, and fonts in one click.” Third tile: a sparkle icon with text “Get a surprise palette” and microcopy “We’ll generate a playful color mix just for you.” Each tile has a subtle hover state implied via a slightly deeper background tone.

Below “Quick Actions” is a “Messages & Requests” card with a soft lavender tint background (#F8F3FF) and rounded corners, slightly taller than the previous card. At the top, a heading reads “Messages & Requests” with a tiny speech bubble icon to its left. Inside, there are two “message snippet” rows. Each row contains a small circular avatar on the left (flat colorful placeholder faces), the sender’s name in bold (e.g., “Jun, Studio Artist” and “Mira, Client”), and a one-line preview with text like “Mockups look great—want to try a bolder palette?” and “Could we add a couple of whimsical plant doodles?” Each row has a pill-shaped tag on the right side showing the status: “Unread” in coral and “Replied” in mint.

The last card on the right column is “Upcoming Reminders,” a small but tall card with a white background, rounded corners, and a dotted vertical line running along the left inside edge in pastel colors that connect a set of bullet-like markers to each reminder. The heading at the top reads “Upcoming Reminders” and a smaller, light gray line under it says “Stay on top of your creative flow.” Inside, three reminders are stacked vertically. For each, a small circular marker connects to the dotted line, followed by bold text like “Client review: Cozy Café Mascots,” “Upload assets: Galaxy Doodles pack,” and “Renew subscription,” and under each in a smaller line, the due time such as “Today · 4:00 PM”, “Tomorrow · 10:00 AM”, “Next week · Monday”. At the bottom-right of this card is a tiny, subtle link in gray: “Manage reminders”.

Throughout the dashboard there are subtle decorative elements that maintain the playful aesthetic without clutter: faint, semi-transparent doodles behind some cards (e.g., a large, almost watermark-like paint splash behind “Explore Illustration Packs”), tiny star icons sprinkled near some headings, and occasional hand-drawn underline squiggles in pastel behind emphasized words like “splash” in “Ready to splash some color today?” in the hero, as if highlighted by hand. All buttons use consistent rounded pill shapes, and all icons are flat, minimal, and outlined with soft curves rather than sharp angles. The footer at the very bottom of the page is simple and unobtrusive: a narrow strip with a pale gray background and faint top border. Centered inside it is small, light gray text: “Made with color and care by ColorBloom Studio” with a tiny heart icon in coral replacing the word “love” implied by the heart. There are no videos, no animations, and no charts or data visualizations anywhere on the page—only static illustrations, icons, and text in a lively yet calm, dashboard-like composition.

instruction: Planning the generation details for the following website:
input:
Design a striking, high-contrast event landing page for a one-night-only design and photography showcase titled “MIDNIGHT INTERIORS: A Night of Light, Shadow & Space.” The entire page uses a high-contrast graphic aesthetic, relying on stark blacks, bright whites, and bold accent colors, arranged in a multi-column grid layout with intentional asymmetries and overlapping blocks. The background of the page is a deep matte black (#050507), with subtle, almost imperceptible diagonal noise texture reminiscent of fine paper grain. All typography is rendered in a combination of a bold, condensed sans-serif for headings (similar to “Bebas Neue”) and a clean geometric sans-serif (similar to “Futura” or “Avenir”) for body text and captions; text is either pure white (#FFFFFF) or a near-white (#F5F5F5) for softer contrast, with accents in an electric blue (#3B82F6) and a vivid neon orange (#FF7A1A). The page is full-width, with content contained in a centered grid of 12 columns and roomy horizontal padding; vertical spacing is generous, with large gutters between distinct sections, but individual elements are tightly composed to feel intense and graphic.

At the very top, a thin, fixed-position header bar spans the full width, with a black background slightly darker than the page, separated from the rest of the content by a 1-pixel white line along its bottom edge. On the left side of the header, the event logo appears as minimalist text: “MIDNIGHT INTERIORS” in uppercase, ultra-bold condensed sans-serif, white, slightly letterspaced. The words are stacked on two lines, with “MIDNIGHT” above “INTERIORS,” aligned left, occupying about one quarter of the header width. To the right, a minimalist navigation, aligned right and arranged in a tight horizontal row with clear spacing, includes the links: “Event”, “Exhibitors”, “Schedule”, “Venue”, and a high-contrast button labeled “Get Tickets”. The navigation links are set in small uppercase white text; when hovered, they invert to black text on a white rounded rectangle pill shape. The “Get Tickets” button is a pill-shaped capsule with a white fill, black text “GET TICKETS” in bold uppercase, and a thin neon orange border; on hover it reverses to neon orange fill with black text. The header casts a very subtle, soft drop shadow downward.

Below the header, the hero section fills the initial viewport height and is divided asymmetrically into two main columns: a large left column occupying about 7/12 of the width, and a narrower right column taking 5/12, with some elements overlapping the grid. The left column features a dramatic, high-contrast hero image of a dimly lit modern interior space photographed at night. The scene is photorealistic: a sleek living room with a low, dark charcoal modular sofa and a slender metal coffee table with a reflective black glass top. A single floor lamp with a cylindrical white shade casts a spotlight that pools on the textured concrete floor, creating long, sharp shadows. The background wall is matte black with vertical grooves, and a large framed abstract photograph of geometric light streaks in white and electric blue hangs slightly off-center. Light from the lamp creates bright highlights on the edges of the furniture while most of the space recedes into deep shadow, emphasizing contrast and texture. The vantage point is eye-level, slightly angled so we see the corner of the room leading into darkness. Overlaid on the lower left of this image, without obscuring important details, is a semi-transparent black rectangle containing the event title text in large white uppercase: “MIDNIGHT INTERIORS” on one line, and beneath it in smaller but still prominent size: “A Night of Light, Shadow & Space”. Below this, in a smaller line of thin uppercase text in neon orange: “EXPERIENTIAL POP-UP EXHIBITION · ONE NIGHT ONLY”.

The right column of the hero is a solid black panel broken into a vertical grid of staggered content blocks. At the top, aligned to the top-right, a small white label reads “MAR 22 · 2026 · BERLIN” in tight uppercase, with a small thin white horizontal line extending from its left edge like an underline. Below that, a bold numeric block shows the date: a large “22” in enormous white digits, with “Saturday” in smaller uppercase on the left, and “March 2026” on the right in light-weight text, all aligned on a central vertical axis. Directly under the date, a white rectangular button with thin electric blue border reads “VIEW SCHEDULE” in uppercase black text; on hover, the fill turns electric blue and the text becomes white. Toward the mid-bottom of this right column, a slightly offset, vertically oriented text block introduces the event in 3–4 concise lines: “Immerse yourself in an after-dark showcase of contemporary furniture, interior styling, and conceptual photography. Explore curated rooms, live styling sessions, and guided photo walks through bold, high-contrast spaces.” The text is left-aligned, white, and set in medium weight sans-serif with slightly expanded line height to create breathable rhythm. At the very bottom of the hero, spanning across both columns but constrained to the grid and aligned slightly overlapping the hero image, sits a wide, pill-shaped CTA bar with a white background and very subtle black shadow, containing the text “Limited capacity · Reserve your spot now” on the left, in small uppercase black letters, and a dark pill button on the right that says “RESERVE TICKETS” in white uppercase, filled with solid black and outlined with neon orange, giving a layered high-contrast effect.

Below the hero, the page transitions into a multi-column information section titled “ABOUT THE EXPERIENCE”. The section background remains black but now features a subtle diagonal split with a slightly lighter charcoal gray on the left half, creating a faint visual divide. The section title “ABOUT THE EXPERIENCE” is centered at the top, in tight uppercase white, flanked by two short thin white lines extending outward on both sides. Beneath the title, the content divides into three columns that occupy the full width. The left column features a tall, narrow image of a modern dining area: a long, raw oak table with dramatically lit sculptural chairs in matte black, overhead pendant lights that are simple cylindrical tubes emitting strong downward white beams, casting crisp circular pools of light and stark shadows. The image is high-contrast, photorealistic, and shot from a slightly low angle for drama. The middle column contains text blocks with white headings and near-white descriptions.
