The first heading reads “Curated Interior Vignettes” followed by body text: “Walk through five immersive interior environments, each designed around a different contrast story—black and white, warm and cool, matte and reflective.” A thin neon orange line separates it from the next, “Live Styling Performances” with description: “Watch interior stylists reconfigure spaces in real time, using light, shadow, and texture to transform mood before your eyes.” Another thin line then “Photography Walkthroughs” with copy: “Learn to frame interiors like a pro with guided sessions focused on negative space, leading lines, and dynamic contrast.” The right column displays another tall image, a photorealistic close-up of a dark, textured armchair near a window at night; a slim slice of cool bluish moonlight cuts across the seat, creating a diagonal highlight contrasting the deep shadows around it. This image has a narrow white border and appears slightly “raised” with a soft shadow, overlapping the boundary between columns.

Next, a feature section for tickets and passes appears, structured in a three-column grid of pricing cards that stand like graphic posters against the black background. The section title, left-aligned in the top-left corner, reads “TICKETS & ACCESS” in uppercase white. A short one-line subtitle below it in near-white: “Choose the experience that matches how deep you want to go.” Underneath, three rectangular cards sit side-by-side with equal width and equal height but slightly differing accent colors. Each card has a solid black base but is framed by a thick border—the leftmost in electric blue, the middle in white, the rightmost in neon orange. The first card, labeled “STANDARD PASS” at the top in a small uppercase blue label, shows a large white price “€49” centered, with “General Admission” in a smaller uppercase beneath. Text bullets (styled as short lines, not actual bullets) read: “Access to all interior installations”, “Entry to photography gallery”, “One complimentary signature drink”. The card’s CTA button at the bottom is a white rectangular button with black uppercase text “CHOOSE STANDARD” and a thin blue border. The middle card, slightly taller and visually emphasized, labeled “EXHIBITORS & CREATORS” in a small uppercase white label, shows “€89” in bold white, followed by “Pro Access” underneath. Its perks: “Early arrival for low-crowd viewing”, “Access to behind-the-scenes setup”, “Reserved seating for live styling showcases”. Its button is a white pill with black text “CHOOSE PRO”, surrounded by a thin neon orange outline. The right card, labeled in orange “VIP NIGHT SESSION”, shows “€149” in white and perks: “All-day and late-night access”, “Private walkthrough with a curator”, “Exclusive print & digital lookbook”, “Priority entry and lounge access”. The button is reversed: solid neon orange fill with black text “CHOOSE VIP” and a subtle white outer border. On hover, each card will invert its border and background combinations but stays consistent in a high-contrast graphic style.

Scroll further to an “Exhibitors & Creators” showcase section featuring a dynamic, grid-based layout that feels like a cross between a photography portfolio and a furniture catalog. The heading “EXHIBITORS & CREATORS” is set in the upper left corner in white uppercase, with a smaller right-aligned label on the same horizontal line reading “MEET THE MINDS BEHIND THE SPACES” in thin uppercase near-white. Below this, the layout consists of an asymmetrical grid of six tiles, arranged in three columns and two staggered rows so that some tiles span two columns and others fit as squares, creating a broken grid structure. Each tile contains either a photo of interiors or a portrait of a featured creative, treated with a consistent photographic style: high-contrast, selectively lit, with deep shadows and bright highlights. For example, one tile spanning two columns shows a wide shot of a monochromatic black and white reading nook: linear bookshelves, a single lounge chair under a narrow beam of light, and a strong diagonal shadow across the floor. Another tile shows a close-up portrait of a photographer in side profile, half their face in shadow, lit by a single rectangular softbox, with a blurred background of hanging frames. Beside each image, or overlaying its lower-left corner in a small black rectangle with a thin white border, the creator’s name appears in uppercase white, such as “STUDIO NOIRFRAME · INTERIOR DESIGN” or “ELENA KRAUS · ARCHITECTURAL PHOTOGRAPHY”, with a one-line descriptor in smaller text beneath, like “Specializing in cinematic black-and-white living spaces.” Hover states on these tiles slightly lift the image and intensify the contrast, with a faint blue or orange outline appearing around the photograph.

Following this, a section introduces what the event offers to attendees interested in learning and subscribing to future content. While this page is for the event itself, it hints at an ongoing “Midnight Interiors Collective” subscription. This section is structured as a two-column split, with the left side featuring text and the right side a set of carefully placed hero images describing a future membership concept. The left column begins with a bold title: “BEYOND ONE NIGHT: MIDNIGHT INTERIORS COLLECTIVE”. Below it, in body text: “Join our invite-only membership to access monthly virtual walkthroughs of newly styled interiors, high-contrast photography tutorials, and members-only events in unconventional spaces.” Three labeled feature lines follow, each beginning with a short uppercase label in neon orange followed by description text in white: “INTERIOR LAB” – “Exclusive behind-the-scenes breakdowns of each room, from layout to lighting and finish selection.” “PHOTO STUDIO” – “Guided sessions teaching you how to shoot interiors with dramatic contrast using everyday gear.” “COLOR & SHADOW” – “Monthly case studies on how designers control mood with pure black, stark white, and minimal color.” Below these, a minimalist outlined button reads “REQUEST INVITE” in uppercase white, with a hairline white border; on hover the background fills in white and the text flips to black. On the right column, three overlapping rectangular images float in a staggered stack on the black background, each with a thin white border and soft shadow. The top image shows a meticulously styled bedroom with a dark wall, crisp white bedding, and a single pendant casting a perfect circle of light on the headboard, shot from slightly above the bed; the middle image shows a grid of printed photographs laid out on a black table, all depicting stark interior scenes and geometric compositions; the bottom image shows a moodboard pinned to a wall, consisting of fabric swatches, paint chips in black, gray, and white, and small printed interior vignettes in high contrast, photographed from a slightly diagonal top-down perspective.

Next, a “Schedule Snapshot” section presents the structure and timings of the night in a graphic tabular format while staying within the high-contrast, static aesthetic. The header “SCHEDULE SNAPSHOT” appears on the left, and on the right a small monochrome icon of a clock—a flat-design white outline of a round clock with black interior and white hands—is shown. Below, a three-column timetable block sits on a dark charcoal panel that is subtly lighter than the page background to distinguish it, with a thin white border and square corners. Each column denotes a phase of the evening: “ARRIVAL & FIRST LIGHT”, “LIVE SESSIONS”, and “AFTER HOURS GALLERY.” Each column header is in uppercase white, with a horizontal line in electric blue underneath. Beneath each header, time slots are listed with the time in neon orange on the left, and the session name in white on the right, separated by thin dotted white lines. For example, under “ARRIVAL & FIRST LIGHT”: “18:00” – “Doors open & low-light welcome tour”; “18:30” – “Guided walkthrough: The Contrast Rooms”. Under “LIVE SESSIONS”: “19:30” – “Styling in Motion: Transforming a Living Room”, “20:30” – “Capturing Negative Space: Photo walkthrough”. Under “AFTER HOURS GALLERY”: “21:30” – “Open shooting & lounge access”, “23:00” – “Closing reflections & final pass.” The entire block stands centered on the page, but the title sits slightly left-aligned, producing a subtle asymmetry.

The venue section follows, combining minimalist map styling with a bold architectural photograph. The heading “VENUE” is centered at the top of this section, with the subtitle “Industrial Loft · Kreuzberg, Berlin” in smaller text underneath. The layout splits into two uneven columns, the left narrower than the right. The left column contains practical information: in white text, “Location” followed by “RAW STUDIO LOFT, Schlesische Straße 27, 10997 Berlin”, then “Nearest U-Bahn” with “Schlesisches Tor (U1)” and “Hours” listing “Saturday, March 22 — 18:00 to 23:30”. Each label appears in uppercase neon orange, with the details in near-white, left-aligned with generous line spacing. Beneath this, a simple, stylized static map is shown in a small rectangle: a high-contrast, flat-design black map with white streets, a bold white line for the river, and a single electric blue circular marker indicating the venue location with a small label “RAW STUDIO LOFT” in white. The right column features a wide, full-height photograph of the venue interior: a former industrial warehouse converted into a loft space. The image is photorealistic and high-contrast, shot from a slightly elevated, wide-angle view showing exposed brick walls painted in dark gray, polished concrete floors with reflections of overhead lights, steel beams, and large, gridded windows. The nighttime scene emphasizes interior lighting: linear fixtures on the ceiling created long, rhythmic highlights, while the far wall is partially in deep shadow. The image extends almost edge-to-edge within its column, framed with a thin white line.

Beneath this, a testimonial strip reinforces the event’s ethos. On a solid black background, a thin horizontal line stretches across the page in electric blue, interrupted in the center by the text “WHAT ATTENDEES SAY” in uppercase white within a small black capsule. Below the line, three short testimonials are displayed in a three-column layout, each framed by minimalist typographic marks. Each testimonial begins with a large, stylized white quotation mark and the review text in near-white, such as: “I’ve never seen interiors curated so precisely for the camera. Every corner felt like a still from a film.” Underneath, the attendee’s name is set in uppercase white, for example “JANA L.”, with a small descriptor in neon orange, such as “Interior Photographer.” The columns are evenly spaced, and each testimonial block has a thin white border and a soft inner shadow, giving a print-like card appearance floating over the black.

Near the bottom, a “Stay Connected” and “Newsletter” band stretches horizontally, spanning the full width but constrained to the grid. This band has a white background to create a stark reversal from the black above and below. On the left side, in black uppercase text, a title reads “STAY IN THE DARK (BUT NEVER OUT OF THE LOOP)” with a smaller line beneath in gray: “Monthly dispatches on spaces, shadows, and upcoming MIDNIGHT INTERIORS events.” On the right side of this band, an email subscription form is arranged in a horizontal row: a rectangular input field with a light gray outline and placeholder text “Enter your email address” in subtle gray, followed by a bold black button with white uppercase text “JOIN THE LIST”. To the right of the button, three small minimalist social icons sit: a white outline camera icon for Instagram, a white outline square for Behance, and a white outline play-button-triangle-on-square for an image-centric platform. The icons are simple geometric line drawings with no fill, turning black on hover when the background behind them is white.

The footer returns to the deep black background, with content arranged in a three-column grid. The left column repeats the “MIDNIGHT INTERIORS” logo in small size, stacked text, and beneath in tiny uppercase near-white text: “Concept & curation by Nightframe Studio.” The middle column lists a few text links in vertical alignment—“FAQ”, “Accessibility”, “Contact”, “Press Kit”—in white, with subtle underlines appearing on hover. The right column shows event details in compact text: “MIDNIGHT INTERIORS · March 22, 2026 · Berlin”, followed by a tiny line in gray: “All imagery and layouts used for promotional purposes only.” At the very bottom, centered and in tiny gray text, the copyright line reads “© 2026 Midnight Interiors. All rights reserved.” A thin white horizontal rule separates this final line from the rest of the footer.

Throughout the page, key interactive elements like buttons and navigation links maintain clear high-contrast states without animation or motion—they simply switch between black, white, and accent colors on hover. There are no videos, no animated effects, no parallax; the experience is entirely static, driven by the interplay of bold typography, photorealistic high-contrast interior and photography imagery, and a carefully structured multi-column, grid-based layout with subtle asymmetries and overlapping compositional elements.

instruction: Planning the generation details for the following website:
input: Design an editorial-style research tutorial homepage titled “TIDELINES: A Research Journal for Independent Creators” that blends the feel of a minimalist magazine cover with a slightly chaotic, overlapping layout. The overall page sits on a very light, warm gray background (#F7F5F2) that frames a centered, fixed-width main column (max width around 1200px) but allows some elements (like pull quotes and tags) to break out into the margins for a sense of creative asymmetry. The visual tone is subdued and sophisticated, with a limited color palette of soft sand beige (#E8DFC8), deep ink blue (#1F2A3C), muted terracotta (#C8745E), and occasional accents of pale seafoam green (#D3EEE5). Typography feels editorial: primary serif font resembling “Libre Baskerville” for headings and pull quotes, with a clean grotesque sans-serif similar to “Inter” for body text, navigation, and labels. The entire site is static — no animations, no hover effects described beyond color changes, no video or moving images, and no data visualizations. At the very top, a full-width but shallow header bar (about 80px tall) sits on a pure white background. On the far left, aligned with the main content column, is the logo lockup: the word “TIDELINES” in uppercase serif, letter-spacing wide, in deep ink blue; below it in smaller uppercase sans-serif, all caps and spaced out, in gray: “RESEARCH TUTORIALS FOR INDEPENDENT CREATORS”. On the right side of this header, laid out horizontally in neat sans-serif, are navigation links: “Surf School Methods”, “Digital Product Systems”, “Fashion & Aesthetics”, “Freelance Web Design”, “Artist Practice”, and “Library”, each in small caps, ink blue, with generous letter spacing. The final item on the right is a pill-shaped button with a thin ink blue border and white fill reading “Subscribe to Research” in small sans-serif, medium weight. Beneath the header, the hero section occupies roughly the first screen height and uses a split, overlapping layout evocative of a magazine feature spread. The left two-thirds of the hero area is dominated by a large editorial-style feature block on a soft sand beige card that appears to slightly overlap a background image to its right. The card has generous inner padding and a subtle, soft drop shadow (very blurred, low opacity) to create a sense of layering. At the top of this card, in small uppercase sans-serif, ink blue, is the label: “FIELD NOTE 001 • SURF SCHOOL AS LAB”. Below that, the main headline in large serif reads: “How to Think Like a Surf Instructor (Even When You Design Software)”. The headline is left-aligned, with line breaks that create a poetic rhythm, and the text is deep ink blue. Underneath, a short subheading in smaller serif italics, muted terracotta, reads: “A research tutorial on reading waves, pacing instruction, and building resilient learning loops.” Below the subheading, a two-column layout appears: on the left, three short bullet-like lines (but without bullet icons, just line breaks) in sans-serif, dark gray, slightly smaller; each line starts with a bold keyword in ink blue: “Wave Literacy •”, “Embodied Onboarding •”, “Retention Rituals •” followed by brief explanations like “Translate ocean cues into user flow signals.”, “Structure teaching arcs like sets, not sprints.”, “Use post-session debriefs instead of release notes.” On the right of this hero card’s lower half is a vertical column with meta information: a tiny circular author portrait image at the top (described below), then text reading “Researched & written by” in small uppercase sans-serif gray, followed by “Mara Keene” in serif, ink blue. Beneath, a tiny horizontal rule (1px, ink blue, about 30px wide) separates this from a line that reads “Duration: 45 min tutorial • Difficulty: Intermediate” in sans-serif. The small circular portrait image is a stylized illustration, not a photo: a flat-design, minimal face of a woman in side profile looking toward the right, with wavy dark hair, set against a circular background of muted terracotta; the illustration is simple, with clean vector lines and no shading, evoking the style of editorial avatars in design magazines. Overlapping the right side of the hero card and extending slightly behind it is a large rectangular image (in a 4:3 ratio) that takes up the right third of the hero section. This image is photorealistic but art-directed in a muted palette to fit the site: a wide shot of a small group surf lesson at dawn. The camera angle is slightly elevated, looking diagonally down the beach at a line of beginners and a single instructor. In the foreground right, the surf instructor stands in three-quarter view facing the group, gesturing toward the ocean with one arm extended; we see just enough detail to infer a focused expression but no recognizable identity. The instructor wears a muted navy rash guard and sand-colored boardshorts. In the midground left, four learners stand near their surfboards on the wet sand in various poses of attentive listening. Their boards create a repeated diagonal pattern; colors are slightly desaturated: pale teal, soft yellow, white with a faded stripe. The ocean is in the background, with low, gently breaking waves and a soft horizon line under a pastel sky blending pale lavender, peach, and soft blue. Lighting is early morning soft: long, gentle shadows, no harsh contrast, with a slight haze that makes the scene feel contemplative and calm. The overall aesthetic is editorial photography with a matte finish, not glossy, and no visible logo marks. Overlaying the bottom-left corner of this surf image is a small off-white translucent box containing in tiny uppercase sans-serif text: “Visual Study: Group Instruction Dynamics, 6:12am, North Shore”. Positioned slightly below and overlapping the bottom of both the hero card and the surf image is a slim horizontal strip acting like a section divider, using a muted terracotta background. On this strip, aligned left is the text “CURRENT RESEARCH THREAD: TEACHING AS DESIGN PRACTICE” in small uppercase white sans-serif. On the far right of this strip is a very simple pagination-like indicator: three small squares outlined in white, with the first one filled in solid white and the others only outlined, suggesting multiple hero features without any actual interaction. Below the hero, the page transitions into a multi-column editorial layout that highlights different tutorial tracks aligned with specific creative domains (surf school, digital products, fashion, freelance web design, independent artist practice). The next large section is titled, in centered uppercase serif, ink blue: “RESEARCH TUTORIALS BY PRACTICE”. Beneath this title, a thin ink blue line runs horizontally across about half the page width, centered. Below that, the layout breaks into a staggered two-column grid that feels slightly asymmetrical. On the left, a tall, narrow column with more text; on the right, a wider column featuring overlapping article cards. On the left column, top, a small serif subheading reads: “Choose a domain, then borrow its logic.” followed by a short paragraph in sans-serif explaining: “Each tutorial in TIDELINES is built as a small research study. We start with a real-world practice—surf instruction, luxury fashion styling, studio art routines—and extract mental models you can apply to the way you teach, design, and build.” Beneath this paragraph, vertically spaced tags are listed like a column of capsule filters: “STRUCTURE”, “PACE”, “AESTHETICS”, “RISK”, “RITUAL”. Each tag is in uppercase sans-serif, slightly condensed, inside a rounded rectangle stroke in ink blue with a transparent fill, aligned left with even vertical spacing. These tags visually align with the top edges of cards in the right column, creating a sense of invisible grid. On the right column, three overlapping tutorial cards are arranged diagonally downwards from top left to bottom right, each representing a different domain. The top card is for digital products: a rectangular card with an off-white background and a thin ink blue border, slightly rotated a few degrees counter-clockwise. At the top, in small uppercase sans-serif, terracotta text reads “DIGITAL PRODUCT TUTORIAL”. Below, the title in serif ink blue: “Designing Onboarding Like a Tide Chart”. Under that, a brief synopsis in sans-serif gray: “A structured study in creating onboarding flows that ebb and flow with user attention, instead of forcing linear progress.” To the right of the text, taking up roughly one-third of the card width, is a small illustration: a flat, minimalist diagram of a tide chart drawn as a gentle sine wave line in ink blue against a pale seafoam background, with a few labeled points represented by tiny circle markers in terracotta. The illustration is simple, no text labels rendered, just implied. The card includes at the bottom in small sans-serif, “Read the research tutorial →” in ink blue. Slightly overlapping underneath and offset to the right is a second card, larger, representing luxury fashion: its background is a muted warm gray with a subtle paper grain texture. Its label at top left reads “LUXURY FASHION TUTORIAL” in very small uppercase sans-serif, spaced out, in ink blue. The title beneath, serif, large, reads: “Building Tutorial Sequences Like a Capsule Collection”. Under this, two short, left-aligned lines in sans-serif describe: “How runway storytelling, seasonal arcs, and fit sessions can inform the way you layer educational content.” On the left side of this card is a stylized flat illustration: a front-view of a dress form mannequin in pale beige, outlined cleanly in ink blue, with three semi-transparent overlapping garment shapes (a jacket, a dress, a coat) layered over it in slightly offset muted colors (terracotta, seafoam, navy), evoking layering and collection. The third card, partially tucked behind the others so only about two-thirds shows, represents freelance web design: the label reads “FREELANCE WEB DESIGN TUTORIAL”, title in serif: “Proposal Walkthroughs as Live Studio Critique”, and a short sentence about using critique frameworks from art school to shape client proposals. Its illustration is a flat, slightly isometric top-down view of a desk with a laptop, a stack of printed mockups slightly askew, and a single coffee cup, all in minimal outlines and muted colors with subtle shadow to give depth. You can see a thin overlapping of these cards, with a faint stacking shadow effect giving the impression of paper clippings laid on a table. Following this section, a horizontal full-width band breaks the vertical flow, acting like a pull-quote and mid-page editorial interlude. The background of this band is deep ink blue, and it stretches edge-to-edge of the viewport, with generous padding top and bottom. Centered within, in large serif white text, is a quotation: “Every practice hides a tutorial in its muscle memory. Research is how we learn to see it.” Beneath, right-aligned, is the attribution in small sans-serif white: “— From the editor’s letter, Volume 01”. This band visually resets the reader before the next section. Below the pull-quote band, the background reverts to the warm gray of the page. The next section is a three-column “In-depth Studies” layout that feels like a research journal table of contents. The section heading, aligned left, reads: “Latest in-depth research tutorials” in serif, ink blue. Below, a faint horizontal rule runs almost fully across the main column. In the grid below, three vertical columns list different hero-level studies with slightly varied heights to maintain an asymmetrical editorial feel. Each study has a vertical layout with a small image on top, then text. In the first column is an independent artist tutorial. Its image is a photorealistic but stylized shot of an artist’s studio from a slightly elevated corner angle, late afternoon light streaming in. On the left, an easel holds a half-finished abstract painting with layered brushstrokes of teal, terracotta, and white. On the right, a sturdy work table is scattered with sketchbooks, pigment tubes, and a ceramic mug; a chair is slightly pulled back as if the artist just stepped away. The background is a textured white wall with two pinned-up sketches. The light creates soft, elongated shadows, and the overall tone is warm and contemplative. Beneath the image, the label in small uppercase sans-serif reads “INDEPENDENT ARTIST PRACTICE”, followed by the title in serif: “Designing Tutorials as Studio Rituals”. A short summary in sans-serif explains: “Replace rigid lesson plans with recurring studio blocks, critique windows, and reflection days drawn from independent artists’ routines.” At the bottom, a line of small meta text in gray: “Includes: sample weekly ‘studio syllabus’ and reflection prompts.” The second column presents a surf-specific follow-up study. The image is a close crop of a waxed surfboard deck laid in the sand, photographed from above. The wax pattern is visible as irregular, chalky ridges; a single piece of driftwood lies diagonally across a corner of the board; sand grains catch the light. The board’s color is a muted seafoam green, slightly scuffed, and the background is blurred sand with shallow depth of field. Under the image, the label: “SURF SCHOOL FIELD STUDY”, title: “Note-taking While Wet: Capturing Learning Moments Between Sets”, and a summary about how surf instructors document small behavioral shifts mid-session and how this can inspire notes-taking habits in digital teams. The third column features a subtle luxury fashion research piece. Its image is a stylized overhead shot of a curated outfit layout on a flat surface: a folded cream silk blouse, a pair of tailored navy trousers, a thin leather belt, and a single heeled shoe, arranged in a visually pleasing diagonal composition. The background surface is a soft beige cloth; light comes from the top-left, creating gentle shadows and giving the objects a tactile, luxe feel. Below, the label: “LUXURY FASHION STYLING STUDY”, title: “Sequencing Lessons Like Wardrobe Changes”, summary about pacing tutorials through shifts in complexity and mood, drawing parallels to changing outfits across a day. This three-column section ends with a simple line at the bottom, right-aligned text in small sans-serif: “View the complete research library →”. Further down, a narrow but tall vertical section to the left breaks the grid with a “Methods” sidebar that overlaps slightly with an adjacent main content region. This “Methods” block uses a pale paper texture background and is slightly offset from the main grid, with its top edge starting higher than the section title next to it, to create a playful imbalance. At the top of this block, in uppercase serif, ink blue: “METHOD NOTES”. Below, in smaller sans-serif, a list of three brief numbered principles, each with a bold title and single-sentence explanation, each separated by a thin ink blue horizontal line: “01 • Longitudinal Observation”, “02 • Translational Metaphor”, “03 • Embodied Prototyping”. To the right of this “Methods” column, taking up roughly two-thirds of the width, is a more dense text-focused section titled “How these tutorials are built” in serif. Beneath, a multi-paragraph explanation set in narrower columns with justified text, giving the impression of an academic article. There are no bullet points here, just paragraph breaks. Some phrases are in italics for emphasis. Occasional small inline “figure” references in light gray (like “Fig. 1: The wave set model”) appear, but there are no actual charts or visualizations, just text. A thin vertical rule in the margin between the Methods block and the main text underlines the journal-like feel. As the page nears the bottom, a “Subscription & access” band appears with a slightly darker warm gray background, spanning full width of the main column. On the left of this band, a box titled in serif: “Receive a new research tutorial each month” contains a brief explanation in sans-serif about the subscription: “Members receive one deeply researched tutorial per month, complete with field notes, prompts, and cross-domain models.” Below, an email input field with a fine ink blue outline and placeholder text “you@example.com” in gray, and to its right a rectangular button filled with ink blue and white sans-serif text: “Start your research subscription”. Small text beneath in very light gray: “No ads. No algorithmic feeds. Just one long, careful read.” On the right side of this band, a smaller callout card with a muted terracotta background and white serif text highlights a free sample: “Download a free excerpt from ‘How to Think Like a Surf Instructor’”. Below that, in smaller sans-serif: “A 12-page PDF with diagrams, session transcripts, and workshop questions.” The bottom of the page features a quiet, minimalist footer in a slightly darker shade of the overall background. The footer is divided into two horizontal rows. The top row has three columns: left column with “TIDELINES” in uppercase serif and tiny text about the project’s origins; middle column with “Sections” and a short list of text links (“Surf”, “Digital Products”, “Fashion”, “Freelance”, “Artist Practice”, “Library”) in sans-serif; right column titled “Elsewhere” with simple text links “Newsletter” and “Contact”. The bottom footer row is a thin strip containing, on the left, in tiny sans-serif, gray text: “© 2025 TIDELINES Research. All rights reserved.” and on the right, “Made for independent instructors, designers, and artists.” The overall composition should feel layered and hierarchical, with cards overlapping images, vertical columns slightly misaligned for visual interest, and occasional elements like tags and pull quotes stepping into the page margins. No element should animate or move; everything is static and print-like, echoing the aesthetic of a well-designed research journal translated into a web layout, with rich imagery but no videos, no motion, and no charts or data visualizations of any kind.

instruction: Planning the generation details for the following website:
input:
Design a single-page dashboard-style homepage for an “artisan chocolate tutorial subscription” platform called “CocoaCraft Studio” in a refined neo-skeuomorphic aesthetic, with a hierarchical, layered layout that uses overlapping card-like panels casting soft, realistic shadows. The page sits on a deep, textured background: a rich, matte cocoa-brown backdrop (#2E221C) with a subtle leather grain and faint, almost imperceptible debossed patterns of cocoa pods and cacao leaves in the midtones, giving the sense of an old chocolatier’s notebook cover. The overall page content is centered within a wide, fixed-width dashboard frame with rounded corners and a very subtle outer drop shadow, creating the feel of a physical control panel floating above the background. This main dashboard frame has a slightly lighter, warm espresso tone (#3A2B23) with a satin sheen and faint vertical brushing, as if made from brushed metal tinted with chocolate; the edges are beveled with a thin inner highlight to emphasize depth.

At the very top of the dashboard, a prominent neo-skeuomorphic “header bar” runs horizontally, spanning the full width but with generous left and right margins inside the main frame. This header bar looks like a gently raised strip of polished wood, a warm pecan color with visible grain and a smooth, waxy finish. It has subtly rounded corners and a soft drop shadow beneath it; its top edge has a crisp highlight strip that catches an imaginary light source from the top-left corner. On the far left of this bar, inset in an oval plate that looks like brushed brass, is the logo: a stylized embossed cocoa bean icon, semi-realistic and slightly beveled, in dark chocolate brown, with the brand name “CocoaCraft Studio” to the right in small caps. The logo text is set in a refined serif typeface, like “Cormorant Garamond,” colored in a pale cream (#F7E5C9), with slight letter spacing and a subtle inner shadow, so it appears stamped into the brass plate. To the right of the logo, but still within the wooden bar, are three navigation buttons: “Dashboard,” “Tutorial Library,” and “My Recipes.” Each is a pill-shaped button that looks like a tactile leather tab: rounded, slightly raised, with stitching detail along the edges. The inactive tabs are a dark mocha with stitched edges in a lighter brown; the active tab “Dashboard” is a slightly lighter caramel leather with a strong highlight at the top curve and a deeper shadow below. The labels are in a clean sans-serif font, like “Inter,” in small uppercase letters, colored cream, centered both vertically and horizontally. On the far right side of the header bar is a compact profile widget resembling a small polished metal badge: a circular user avatar plate with a faint bevel and a tiny engraved rim, containing a flat-illustration avatar of a person wearing a chef’s hat, colored in muted browns and creams. To the right of the avatar, engraved into the wooden bar itself, is the user name “Welcome, Elise” in small serif text, cream-colored with a soft inner shadow as if carved into the surface.

Below this header bar, the page layout opens into a hierarchically layered dashboard with multiple overlapping panels. The top section is dominated by a large, asymmetric hero panel that overlaps partially over secondary panels beneath it. This hero panel is slightly offset to the left, resembling a framed chocolate tasting platter. It has a thick, rounded frame that imitates a mix of ceramic and glossy plastic: warm off-white (#F5EDE1) with a gentle chamfered inner edge and a fine drop shadow that makes it float above the background. Inside this hero panel, the left two-thirds are occupied by a photorealistic hero image: a top-down view of an artisan chocolate workspace. In the image, a dark stone slab table forms the background, with scattered cocoa nibs, a brass scoop, a partially open cocoa pod, and several pieces of hand-crafted chocolate bars with textured toppings (sea salt, crushed nuts, dried berries). There’s a pair of hands visible from above, one holding a chocolate-dipped whisk and the other steadying a copper mixing bowl filled with luscious melted chocolate swirling in a spiral. The lighting is warm and directional from the upper left, casting soft shadows and gentle highlights on the chocolate’s glossy surface. The composition is tightly cropped and slightly off-center, with the focus on the swirling chocolate in the bowl and a shallow depth of field, giving the scene an intimate, hand-crafted atmosphere.

On the right third of this hero panel is a content area with a slightly textured parchment background, like fine paper laid into the frame. At the top of this area, in large, elegant serif text in a dark chocolate color, centered left-aligned, is the heading: “Master the Art of Handcrafted Chocolate.” Below it, in smaller but still readable serif text in a lighter mocha tone, is a subheading: “Your personalized dashboard for step-by-step video tutorials, tasting notes, and recipe experiments—all in one richly curated studio.” (Though it says “video tutorials,” no actual video players or animations are depicted; this is purely textual.) Under the subheading is a horizontal row of two tactile buttons. The primary button on the left is a thick, pill-shaped capsule with a satin caramel gradient, heavily rounded and physically raised, with a sharp top highlight and subtle bottom shadow; it reads “Continue Your Journey” in uppercase sans-serif, cream-colored, centered. The secondary button is to its right, slightly smaller and visually “pressed” inward, appearing like an inset button: it has a flat, darker brown surface with a very thin, lighter beveled edge and the label “Browse All Tutorials” in a lighter cream, all uppercase. Both buttons are spaced with generous negative space above and below, enhancing the physicality and layering.

Beneath this hero panel, but partially tucked behind its lower edge to reinforce the overlapping hierarchy, are three primary dashboard modules arranged in a loose, asymmetrical grid: a “Today’s Plan” card on the left, a “Skill Path Progress” vertical card overlapping the middle, and a “Favorite Flavors” card slightly offset to the right. The “Today’s Plan” card is a horizontally oriented, slightly curved rectangle that resembles a soft leather planner page. Its surface is mid-tone brown with a smooth, almost rubberized texture, and its edges are rounded with a faint embossed border. At the top-left of this card is the title “Today’s Plan” in small caps serif, cream-colored, slightly embossed outward. Directly below the title are three stacked “task” rows that look like physical sliders. Each row has a left segment with a small, circular “chocolate truffle” toggle that appears three-dimensional, like a shiny truffle icon embedded in the slider track, and a right segment with text. The first row has the label “Tempering Basics – 25 min,” the second “Ganache Variations – 40 min,” and the third “Flavor Pairing Notes – 15 min,” all in a neat sans-serif font, left-aligned in a warm cream tone. Each row’s track is a softly recessed groove, a darker brown, with the truffle toggle slightly raised above it and positioned at different points along the track to suggest varying completion, but no dynamic animation. On the upper right corner of the “Today’s Plan” card, there is a small round badge that looks like a wax seal in deep burgundy with an embossed cocoa leaf icon, and the text “FOCUS MODE” etched into it in tiny uppercase letters.

The “Skill Path Progress” card in the middle is taller and slightly overlaps both the “Today’s Plan” card and the “Favorite Flavors” card, creating a sense of depth. This card resembles a vertical metal control panel with a subtle brushed texture, a dark mocha metal color with gently rounded corners and a prominent bevel. At the top-center of this card, engraved in narrow uppercase letters, is the title “Skill Path.” Under the title is a vertical arrangement of three large, tactile progress “dials,” each occupying its own horizontal strip. Each dial is a circular gauge with a metallic ring and an inset, leather-like center. The first dial is labeled “Fundamentals” (at the bottom of the dial), the second “Textures & Fillings,” and the third “Artisan Finishes.” Each dial’s ring has small engraved ticks around the circumference, and a thick, rounded indicator segment that appears filled with a lighter caramel tone up to different angular extents, making them look like gauges. At the center of each dial, engraved numbers like “65%,” “40%,” and “80%” are displayed in a condensed serif font, with a soft inner shadow that makes them look etched. Beneath the three dials, at the bottom of the card, is a rectangular, brass-like plate slightly inset with the phrase “View Learning Path Details” in very small uppercase sans-serif letters, cream-colored, giving the feel of a label plate on machinery.

On the right, the “Favorite Flavors” card is shaped like a glass-topped box with rounded edges, as if displaying a set of chocolate bonbons. The outer frame is dark, polished wood with a thick, rounded edge and a glossy sheen; the top surface looks like glass with subtle reflections and a faint diagonal highlight. Inside this “glass” area, four large, three-dimensional “flavor tiles” are arranged in a 2x2 grid, each appearing as a square chocolate bonbon with distinct textures and toppings. Each tile is labeled beneath it with serif text: “Sea Salt Caramel,” “Hazelnut Gianduja,” “Raspberry Truffle,” and “Single Origin 70%.” The bonbons have realistic lighting and shadows, and each has a unique textured topping: coarse salt crystals, chopped hazelnuts, red berry dust, and a smooth glossy surface with a fine cocoa powder dusting, respectively. The surface beneath the bonbons is a soft suede-like lining in a pale beige tone. At the very bottom of the card, on the wood frame, appears a small engraved text centered: “Tap to curate your tasting profile.” (This remains textual guidance; no animation or actual interactivity is visually depicted.)

Below this middle row of cards, a secondary layer of modules is visible, again overlapping slightly. On the left, beneath “Today’s Plan,” there is a horizontally wide “Recently Watched Tutorials” card that feels like a retro tape deck. The card body is a darker brushed metal rectangle with rounded corners and strong inner beveling. The top-left corner bears the title “Recently Watched Tutorials” in small uppercase sans-serif, cream-colored, as if printed onto a small laminated label stuck to the metal. Below this, there are three horizontally aligned “tutorial strips,” each resembling a cassette-like cartridge embedded into the panel. Each strip contains a small, square thumbnail image on the left, followed by text on the right. The first thumbnail shows a photorealistic close-up of hands pouring tempered chocolate into a mold, with neat, shiny ripples; the second thumbnail shows an overhead shot of rows of filled chocolate shells lined up on parchment; the third shows a macro shot of a chocolate bar with a sharp diagonal break revealing a glossy interior. Each thumbnail is framed by a tiny beveled metal frame. To the right of each thumbnail, the tutorial titles are written in bold sans-serif text: “Perfect Snap: Tempering 101,” “Silky Centers: Ganache Techniques,” and “Finishing Touch: Shiny Shells,” followed by smaller subtext in a slightly muted cream color indicating length: “25 min,” “40 min,” and “30 min.” On the far right side of this card, aligned vertically, are three small, round, recessed buttons with a tiny engraved triangle icon, resembling physical play buttons, each aligned to its corresponding strip—static, no indication of actual motion.

To the right side, under the “Favorite Flavors” card, there is a “Tasting Notes Journal” card that resembles an open, clipped notepad. Its base is a slightly raised beige surface with soft curves, with a faint paper fiber texture; at its top edge is a metallic clip rendered as a brushed brass clamp casting a crisp shadow, making the card look like a sheet held onto the dashboard. On the paper, the heading “Tasting Notes Journal” is written in a handwritten-style script font, dark brown, as if in ink. Below it are three sample entries, each represented as lightly ruled lines with small embossed checkboxes on the left, unfilled. Next to the first checkbox: “Single Origin Ecuador – hints of citrus, balanced acidity.” Next to the second: “Madagascar 72% – bright red fruit, lingering finish.” Next to the third: “Milk Chocolate Caramel – smooth, buttery, light crunch of sea salt.” The lines and text are slightly indented, with a subtle shadow that gives the illusion of ink pressed into textured paper. At the lower-right corner of this card, there is a small, circular “Add Note” button that looks like a physical rubber stamp pad, in dark burgundy, with the words “Add Note” curved along the top inside the circle in tiny cream uppercase letters.

On the far right edge of the dashboard, running vertically from near the top of the hero section down past the middle cards, is a slim, overlapping utility column that looks like a stacked row of metallic tabs partially sliding out from behind the main content. This column’s base is a darker, almost gunmetal chocolate tone with a subtle vertical brushed texture and rounded outer edge. Three small, rectangular, slightly protruding “tab buttons” are arranged vertically with slight gaps. The first tab near the top is labeled “Inbox” with a small envelope icon embossed above the text; the second tab is labeled “Community” with a tiny embossed group-of-people icon; the third tab is labeled “Settings” with a small gear icon. Each icon is monochrome, simply formed by raised metal outlines. The tabs themselves have a strong sense of depth with clear highlights and shadows, and they cast slim shadows onto the underlying column, enhancing the layered feel.

At the very bottom of the page, there is a footer that feels like the base of the physical device. It is a wide, slightly thicker horizontal bar, darker than the main background frame, with a rubberized texture and rounded corners that align with the main frame. It has subtle screw-head details at the far left and far right—small, engraved circles with tiny cross slots, implying a crafted, mechanical device. Centered in this footer is the text “CocoaCraft Studio · Crafted with care for curious chocolatiers” in small sans-serif, a muted beige, lightly embossed into the material. On the left side of the footer are two small, pill-shaped tags that read “Privacy” and “Terms,” respectively, each like tiny rubber labels slightly raised from the surface. The entire design employs a combination of serif fonts for primary headings and warm body text, and clean sans-serif fonts for labels and functional text, always rendered with subtle inner shadows, highlights, or embossing to maintain the neo-skeuomorphic illusion. Whitespace is generous around each card, but the layers overlap enough that one card’s corner gently covers another’s top edge, constantly reinforcing the theme of depth and tangible hierarchy, with the lighting consistently coming from the upper-left to create coherent shadows and highlights. No videos, animations, or charts are included on this page; every component is static and visually suggestive of physical controls and artisan tools in a chocolate craft studio turned into an elegant dashboard.

instruction: Planning the generation details for the following website:
input: Design a homepage for an eco-conscious, hand-crafted research site titled "WildLines Field Journal" that feels like a cross between a field notebook, an eco-living blog, and a mountain-biking tour tutorial archive, with an organic, tactile aesthetic and a multi-column grid layout that intentionally breaks symmetry with overlapping elements and varied column widths. The entire page sits on a slightly warm, off-white paper-textured background (#FBF7F0) with faint, irregular speckles and the subtle suggestion of hand-torn edges along the left and right sides, as if the page is laid on a wooden desk. There are no videos, animations, or data visualizations anywhere; everything is static but richly detailed and layered. At the very top, a narrow, centered notification strip runs full-width, only about 40px tall, with a pale sage green background (#E2F0DF) and slightly darker sage text in a small serif font that reads: "New: Spring 2026 research notes on regenerative trails now available." On the right side of this strip is a tiny hand-drawn bell icon in dark forest green line style, with faint pencil-like sketch lines, aligned vertically with the text. Below this, the main header spans the full width but is constrained to a max content width of about 1200px, centered, with generous padding. On the far left of the header is the logo: the text "WildLines", written in a loose, hand-lettered script that mimics ink brushed on paper, in dark forest green (#1E3A2F), slightly slanted, with a subtle watercolor bleed effect at the edges of each letter; beneath it in small uppercase serif letters is the tagline "FIELD JOURNAL" in a dusty charcoal gray. To the left of the logo, slightly overlapping its bottom-left corner, is a small circular emblem: a sketched outline of a mountain with a tiny trail line winding up its slope, all in thin, imperfect forest green strokes on an off-white circle with a torn-paper textured edge. The navigation menu sits to the right, forming a horizontal row of links in a clean, lightly condensed serif font, letter-spaced slightly: "Research Notes", "Trail Tutorials", "Eco-Living Guides", "Gear Lab", and "About". The active link "Research Notes" is underlined with a hand-drawn olive-green line that wavers slightly rather than being perfectly straight. Hover states are represented visually by slightly darker text and a faint, soft pencil underline. Far right is a pill-shaped button with a gentle watercolor gradient from pale moss green to slightly deeper olive, containing the text "Join the Journal" in small-caps serif, off-white; the button has a subtle drop shadow and roughened mask edges suggesting hand-cut paper. Beneath the header, the hero section uses a 2-column layout with a natural asymmetry: the left column is about 60% width and contains the main hero image, while the right column is 40% and contains the headline, copy, and newsletter subscription module. The hero image is a large, stitched-in photograph bordered by a faux masking-tape effect: at each corner, a small beige rectangular strip overlies the image, like it’s taped into a notebook. The image is a high-resolution, slightly muted, photorealistic scene seen from a first-person perspective of a mountain bike trail on a ridgeline at golden hour: the viewer sees the handlebars of a well-worn mountain bike at the bottom foreground, textured with a bit of dust and a faded sticker of a leaf; the trail winds ahead over rocky, packed dirt and sparse grass, curving gently to the right; on the left, a valley falls away into layers of misty hills; on the right, low alpine shrubs and wildflowers add touches of muted reds and yellows. Light is warm and directional from the left, casting long, soft-edged shadows and subtle lens bloom on the far ridges. The sky has a gradient from soft amber near the horizon to pale blue, with thin, wispy clouds. Overlaid subtly on the bottom-left corner of the image, like an annotation, is a small label in a typewriter-style mono font reading: "Ridge Line Study / Alt. 2,100m / Soil: Mixed loam & gravel". The right column sits slightly higher than the left (their top edges are misaligned by about 30px), reinforcing the hand-assembled feel. At the top of the right column is a small label in all caps, very small serif text, in an earthy rust color: "FIELD RESEARCH ARCHIVE". Below it is the main headline, in a large, warm charcoal serif with subtle ink-bleed texture, left-aligned: "Slow research for fast trails." Underneath is a subheading set in a smaller, soft dark green serif, slightly italicized, reading: "Field-tested notes, eco-living experiments, and longform trail tutorials for riders who care about the land they ride." Below the text is a hand-drawn horizontal divider line in faint graphite, slightly uneven and breaking in one spot as if the pencil lifted from the page. Under that line, a subscription module appears like a pinned-on card: a rounded-rectangle panel with a slightly darker paper tone and shadow, titled in small uppercase text: "Get new field notes in your inbox". Below this, a single-line email input field with a soft graphite-gray border and a tiny pencil icon at the left within the border; placeholder text reads "you@fieldnotebook.com" in a light gray serif. To the right of the input is a hand-painted-looking green button with the text "Subscribe" in off-white, semi-bold small-caps. Under the form, in very small, light gray text, is: "No spam. Just hand-written style summaries." with a tiny doodled envelope outline at the end. The main content below the hero uses a multi-column grid, organized into vertically stacked sections, each using asymmetric column layouts and overlapping cards that look like clipped papers and pinned photos. The first major section is a "Latest Research & Tutorials" area with a three-column masonry-style grid that spills across the full content width. A small header label sits slightly left of center: "Latest from the field" in uppercase, tiny serif text. To the right of that, aligned on the same baseline, a very small filter line appears: "Filter by:" followed by pill toggles "All", "Trail Design", "Regenerative Habits", "Gear Experiments". The selected pill ("All") has a soft olive background and dark green text; the others are just outlined. The grid beneath consists of six content cards, all in slightly different widths and heights, staggered vertically so they do not align perfectly. Each card looks like a piece of paper or index card, with slight rotation (just a few degrees) and long, subtle shadows, some overlapping the edge of adjacent cards. The first card on the left is a medium tall card with a small, rectangular image at the top: an overhead shot of a researcher’s wooden desk, with a hand-drawn trail map, a field compass, a sprig of pine needles, and a notebook open to a page with sketched elevation lines; the style is crisp, photorealistic, but colors are subtly desaturated. Below, in a dark charcoal serif title: "Designing switchbacks that heal, not scar." Beneath the title, a two-line excerpt in muted gray: "We rode the same line for 60 days and tracked soil compression, runoff paths, and plant rebound." At the bottom of the card, in tiny muted text, left-aligned: "Trail Design • 14 min read". At the right-bottom corner is a tiny circular badge in rust red with a white acorn icon, indicating a featured research note. The middle column’s top card is wider and shorter, without an image; its title, in bold serif, reads "A 7-day micro-guide to regenerative living for riders". Under it, three short lines of text resembling a bulleted list but indicated by tiny hand-drawn dots, not actual bullet glyphs. The third column’s top card features a narrow vertical photo of a rider pushing a bike up a steep, root-laced forest trail under dappled light, with mossy trunks and deep greens; the card’s title: "Reading root systems as trail storytellers". Each card uses subtle different paper colors: warm ivory, pale gray, light sand, giving collage variety. Beneath this section, a horizontal strip spans the width, visually looking like a torn-out notebook page taped on both ends. The torn top edge is jagged, with small hand-drawn dots mimicking spiral-bound holes along the top. This strip is a call-to-action to join the research membership. On the left side of the strip is the text "Become a supporting reader." in medium serif; beneath it, in smaller text, "Access full field reports, annotated trail maps, and longform eco-lab writeups." On the right side, two buttons: one outlined in forest green with "View membership options" and the other filled with a soft, pale gold with "Support from $5/month". Below this strip, slightly overlapping it and pinned by a drawn “paperclip” at the top-left corner, is a narrow vertical column on the left that acts as a sidebar: it’s titled "Field Tools & Index" in small uppercase text. This sidebar stretches down the page and contains stacked mini-cards that line up loosely with the main content columns to the right. One mini-card has a list titled "Trail conditions notebooks" with three small tags below: "Alpine", "Temperate forest", "Desert". Another mini-card features a tiny sketched icon of a reusable water bottle and reads "Gear that lasts a decade: our annotated list". The sidebar background is a slightly darker paper tone with faint grid lines, like graph paper, in very light gray. To the right of this narrow sidebar, the main area continues with a two-column section that alternates emphasis column by column. The section’s heading, centered above both columns, reads: "Deep Dives & Eco-Living Experiments" in a slightly larger serif with a faint brush stroke behind the text in translucent moss green. The two columns beneath are of unequal width: the left column is about 55% width, the right 45%. In the left column, a large card features an image at the top: a cozy interior of a small cabin-like workspace filled with plants, wooden shelves lined with glass jars of soil and seed samples, a mountain bike leaning by the door, and a big window showing a blurred forest outside; the lighting is soft and warm, with visible dust motes. Beneath the image: the article title "Designing a home that rides with your trail habits." and a small label "Eco-Living Guide" in a hand-drawn rounded rectangle. In the right column, two smaller stacked cards: the top one shows a close-in photograph of a gloved hand testing soil texture on a trail, with small notes overlayed in a faint typewriter font ("moist layer", "compact zone"), while the lower card is text-only with a lightly sketched illustration of a plant sprout emerging from tire tread marks, line-drawn in dark green with no fill. Interleaved among these content blocks, small hand-drawn marginalia appear: for example, at the outer right margin, near the top of the "Deep Dives" section, a faint pencil doodle of stream ripples and the handwritten note "Ask: where does the water go after a storm?" appears rotated slightly. Another doodle near the bottom left margin is a tiny drawing of a pinecone with the label "Found at 1,800m". These are decorative and static, like annotations scribbled in a notebook. Further down, there’s a section titled "Trail Tutorials Library" with a slightly different visual treatment: a grid of three vertical “spines” that emulate the look of stitched notebooks laid side-by-side. Each spine is a tall, narrow card with a vertical title, as if printed on a book spine, such as "Beginner Trail Ethics", "Intermediate Line Reading", "Advanced Trail Repair". Each spine has a different color: faded green, muted brown, soft gray-blue, with a canvas-like texture and simple geometric stamp icons at the top (a small triangle, a circle, a square) in thin-line style. Clicking a spine mentally would lead to a collection; to the right of the grid is a small explanatory text block: "Each tutorial is a longform, step-by-step trail and habit guide, built from field data and practical ride notes." In this block’s bottom-right corner is a small, rectangular button reading "Browse all tutorials" with a subtle ink smudge around it. Slightly below this, aligned more to the left to maintain asymmetry, another content strip appears with a two-column layout: on the left, a hand-drawn style map snippet depicting a stylized mountain ridge and trail loops in warm browns and greens, intentionally simplified yet textured like watercolor with thin ink lines and gentle gradient washes; small labels in all caps note "RIDGELINE LOOP", "FOREST SPUR", "RIVER CROSSING". On the right, text reads: "Local projects & collaborations" with a subtext: "We share trail audits, community workshops, and shared research projects with riding collectives and land stewards." Below this is a list of three small, card-like entries with partner names and locations ("Fernline Collective — Cascadian Coast", "Desert Line Stewards — High Mesa", "City Spill Group — Urban wild edges"), separated by faint dashed pencil lines. Near the bottom of the page, there is a gentle divider: a wide, faintly darker strip of paper with a low-opacity botanical illustration repeating horizontally, featuring line drawings of ferns, small wildflowers, and stones; the illustration is thin and monochrome in pale gray-green, like a page watermark. On this strip sits a small, centered testimonial-like quote in italic serif: "‘WildLines has become our go-to reference whenever we rethink a trail section.’ – Cedar Loop Collective". The footer occupies the full width and feels like the back page of a notebook. It has a slightly darker, warm recycled-paper background with visible fibers. The content is divided into three vertical columns but they are not perfectly aligned; one column sits a bit higher than the others. The left column shows the logo "WildLines" again, smaller, with the text "Field research for riders & places." beneath, and a small set of hand-drawn social icons that look like ink sketches: a tiny mountain outline labeled "Mastodon", a simple leaf for "Newsletter", and a wave for "RSS". Each icon is monochrome and outlined only, with a handwritten label beneath each. The middle column is titled "Explore" and lists footer links vertically in small serif: "Research Index", "Trail Tutorials", "Eco-Living Lab", "Gear & Tools", "Community Projects". There is a faint, vertical pencil line to the left of this list, like a margin rule on a notebook page. The right column is titled "Support & Contact" and has two segments: one small line "Become a supporting reader" with a text link styled like a handwritten underline, and below, contact information in small serif: "Email: hello@wildlinesjournal.org" and "Signal (text only): +1-555-0134". At the very bottom, centered in tiny, light gray serif text, is the copyright line: "© 2026 WildLines Field Journal. Crafted slowly on unceded lands." with a tiny, subtle hand-drawn dot at each side of the line, like decorative bullet points. The typography throughout combines a primary serif for body and headings (with subtle print-like imperfections) and a simple, humanist sans-serif only for small labels and interface elements like pill filters and button microtext. Color palette stays in muted, organic tones: forest greens, moss, rust, charcoal, warm paper, and the occasional soft gold, and all lines, borders, and underlines are slightly imperfect or textured, reinforcing the hand-crafted, research-notebook aesthetic. No motion, no animation, and no charts or data visualizations: only static, richly layered, organic visual elements that evoke a tactile, analog research journal translated into a modern, multi-column web layout.

instruction: Planning the generation details for the following website:
input: Design a desktop-first homepage for a fictional research-forward language exchange tutorial subscription platform called “LinguaLab”, using a neo-skeuomorphic aesthetic throughout. The page should feel like an elegant, physical research notebook combined with high-end audio equipment and lab instruments, rendered with subtle depth, soft shadows, and textured surfaces, while still being clean and legible. The overall structure is a two-column layout with creative asymmetries: a wider left column for content and a narrower right column that feels like a vertical “instrument panel” or control sidebar. The background of the entire page is a muted, off-white textured surface that resembles slightly fibered paper with a faint warm-gray tone (#F4F1EC), with very subtle noise/grain for a tactile look. At the very top, centered horizontally, is a narrow, rounded rectangular “status bar” panel floating slightly above the background with a soft drop shadow: its background is a light, creamy beige with a glossy sheen and a faint highlight line at the top edge, as if it were a lacquered strip. Inside this status bar, left-aligned, is a small circular logo: a stylized, line-art globe with interconnected dots forming constellations inside, in a dark slate color. To the right of the logo is the site name in a serif font, “LinguaLab”, in medium-large size, in a deep ink-blue (#1C2541). To the far right of this status bar, aligned horizontally, are four navigation links in a minimalist sans-serif font: “Research”, “Method”, “Tutorials”, “Pricing”, in a slightly smaller font, dark gray text with soft, embossed-looking letterforms that cast tiny shadows downward, giving them a subtly raised feel; when hovered, they would appear slightly inset instead, but here it is just depicted as normal, raised text. Below the status bar, the layout splits into two columns with a comfortable gutter of white space between. The left column, occupying about two-thirds of the width, begins with a large hero panel that looks like a slightly opened leather-bound research folio resting on the page. The panel has rounded corners, a soft inner shadow, and a fine stitched border on the left edge in a lighter beige color, evoking a physical notebook spine. The folio background is a slightly darker parchment color with a subtle crosshatch texture. At the top-left inside this hero panel is the main heading text: “Evidence-based language exchange, designed like a lab.” in a large, elegant serif typeface with high contrast (e.g., Didone style), deep navy color, and slight letter spacing. Beneath it, in slightly smaller sans-serif body text, is the subheading: “LinguaLab is a research-driven platform for serious learners and tutors who want to test, iterate, and optimize every conversation.” The text is left-aligned and appears subtly engraved into the folio surface: each letter has a faint inner shadow and a soft highlight on the upper left edge, giving a gently carved effect, but still highly readable. Below this text is a horizontal pill-shaped panel with a very slight metallic sheen, like brushed aluminum embedded into the folio. Inside it, aligned left, is an icon of a tiny magnifying glass over a speech bubble in fine line-art style, and next to it, bold text that reads: “Start your 7-day research trial”. To the right of that, separated by a thin etched vertical divider line, is a smaller line of text: “No credit card. Just data.” in lighter gray. This call-to-action pill looks like a physical button: softly beveled edges, small specular highlights, and a subtle shadow on the lower-right side. To the right side of the hero panel, occupying its right half, is a large, neo-skeuomorphic “instrument cluster” illustration representing the platform’s observable data and tutorial guidance. This is a static, highly detailed image. Imagine a slightly angled top-down perspective of a brushed aluminum console inset into the folio surface, with three primary round “gauges” arranged in a triangular pattern, each with tiny 3D ticks and labels: one labeled “Fluency Sessions” with a needle pointing to “75%”, another labeled “Correction Density” with its needle at a moderate level, and a third labeled “Confidence Index” trending high. Below and surrounding the gauges are small rectangular toggle switches and sliders with rounded knobs, some with miniature labels like “Native Partner”, “Self-Recording”, and “Peer Review”. All labels are in tiny, precise sans-serif, engraved into the console. Lighting is soft and coming from the upper left, casting gentle shadows on knobs and edges; the entire cluster has fine, subtle reflections and a faint vignette, giving it a premium instrument feel. Colors are mostly desaturated metal tones (silver, soft graphite) with tiny accents of teal and amber for indicator lights, which appear as non-animated, softly glowing circles. Below the hero panel in the left column is a section titled “What we’re researching” with the heading set in a mid-sized serif font, dark navy, aligned left. This heading appears on a slightly raised tab that looks like a paper divider tab attached to the top of a larger card. The card below spans the left column width and is divided into three horizontal rows, each resembling a clipped-on report sheet with a faint shadow as if layered. Each row has a subtle cream-colored background and a thin inner border with rounded corners, giving the impression of a physical card nested into the page. The first row has a small, embossed icon on the far left: a cross-section of a brain combined with a speech bubble, rendered in two-tone line-art (dark navy outlines with a pale teal fill for accents). Next to it is the text block: Title in bold small-caps serif, “Experiment 01 — Turn-taking and retention”, followed by two short lines of body copy in a muted dark gray: “We track how micro-pauses, overlaps, and prompts influence retention in real-time exchange. Every tutorial is tuned to maximize spaced recall.” The second row repeats the pattern with an icon representing a waveform and a clock (symbolizing cadence), and text: “Experiment 02 — Cadence over correctness” as the title, followed by two lines of explanation. The third row has an icon of two overlapping silhouettes with dotted arrows between them, titled “Experiment 03 — Feedback without friction”. Each icon appears slightly embossed, with soft shadows, as if printed onto a thin enamel badge that is attached to each row with tiny rendered metal rivets. To the right of each experiment description, there is a small, circular “Info” badge looking like a physical push button with the lowercase letter “i” engraved, giving a tactile point of interaction even though it is static. Continuing down the left column, there is a section that mimics a physical lab notebook spread with sticky notes. This section has a slightly darker, textured background like rougher paper, bounded by a very subtle embossed border. At the top-left of this section is the heading “Tutorial tracks, like playlists for your practice.” in a medium-large serif font, deep navy. Below the heading, arranged in a staggered two-by-two grid that breaks the strict column feel, are four large “sticky note” cards, each slightly rotated a few degrees to give an organic, analog feel, with soft drop shadows creating a layered, tactile visual. Each sticky note has a pastel tone (muted teal, muted ochre, muted rose, muted lavender), with the top edge darker as if the note has a shadow from its own slight curl. Each note contains a mini title in handwritten-style script font, such as “Analytical Pair”, “Daily Debug”, “Story Stitch”, and “Accent Lab”. Below each title is a three-line, small sans-serif description in dark gray, aligned left, describing that type of tutorial track in concise phrases. On the bottom-right corner of each sticky note is a tiny circular stamp icon, embossed, that looks like a rubber-ink stamp with text like “BETA” or “REVIEWED” in a ring around a small symbol (e.g., a star, checkmark, or atom icon), giving each track a research status. At the very bottom of the left column is a wide, shallow drawer-like panel that appears as if it could slide out of the paper. This panel has a soft metallic outline and a subtle top lip, with a tiny engraved label in the center reading “View all tracks and methodologies” in small, uppercase sans-serif. On the far right of this drawer lip is a small recessed groove, suggesting where a finger could pull, further emphasizing the skeuomorphic illusion. The right column, occupying about one-third of the page width, feels like a vertical control sidebar, spine, or research device stacked with panels. It starts below the global status bar and extends down along the entire height. The top right section is a compact, vertically-oriented card with a slight glass-like translucent effect, like a smoked-glass screen inset into a brushed aluminum frame with rounded corners. Inside this screen is a static monochrome profile image: a simplified, semi-realistic monochrome portrait illustration of a person wearing over-ear headphones, seen in three-quarter view, speaking into a small studio microphone. The style is high-contrast grayscale with soft shading, reminiscent of an ink wash portrait scanned onto the device. Beneath the portrait is text in a clean sans-serif: “Matched Tutor Prototype” in bold and below it “Adaptive native partner for English ↔ Japanese exchange.” in lighter weight. This entire card casts a subtle, soft rectangle shadow on the background, suggesting a device resting on the page. Below this “device” section is a slightly wider panel that feels like a physical clipboard with a paper sheet clamped at the top by a metallic clip. The clip has a polished chrome look with highlights and a darker underside. The paper under the clip is a light cream tone with a faint grid pattern (very subtle), and on it is a list titled “Session Blueprint” in a small serif heading. The contents are a vertical list of three “phases,” each with a small circular checkbox rendered as a raised ring and the phase title to the right, like: “Phase 1: Warm-up diagnostics”, “Phase 2: Guided exchange tutorial”, “Phase 3: Post-session reflection notes”. Each line is separated by faint horizontal rules that look like lightly embossed edges. Small annotations in tiny cursive script appear beside the phases, as if scribbled: e.g., next to Phase 2, a tiny note reading “↑ adjust tempo here” with an arrow drawn. These annotations are faint gray, like pencil, contrasting with the printed, darker text. The bottom area of the right column is a heavier, darker-toned module that feels more like a research console. It is a large panel with a deep charcoal gradient background, subtle radial highlight in the center top, and rounded corners with a beveled, metallic edge. Inside it is the title, “Your Lab Profile Snapshot” in a small, uppercase sans-serif font with wide tracking, in a desaturated teal color. Below the title, there is a vertical arrangement of three rounded rectangular “indicator strips”, each recessed into the panel like small wells. Each strip has an inner gradient from deep charcoal at the edges to a slightly lighter tone in the center, with a glossy highlight running horizontally across them, as if made of glass. Inside each strip is a single, fixed textual metric, e.g., “Streak: 12 consecutive days”, “Active languages: English, Japanese, Spanish”, “Average session length: 26 minutes”. To the left of each metric is a small circular indicator that looks like a glass LED lens, in amber, teal, or soft green, with a faint specular highlight but no glowing animation—just the hint of depth. The text inside the strips is white, medium-weight sans-serif, left-aligned, and sharp. At the very bottom of this console panel is a smaller, secondary row of almost flush buttons that look like tactile keys, each with slightly convex tops and engraved labels like “Edit Profile” and “Export Notes”. These are aligned right, with small, consistent spacing. Throughout the page, typography pairs a refined serif (for headings and some labels) with a neutral, humanist sans-serif (for body and UI text). All text is crisp, carefully spaced, and never crowded, with generous margins and padding that emphasize a research-focused, uncluttered atmosphere. All skeuomorphic elements share a common visual language: soft inner shadows, subtle outer shadows, careful highlights, realistic yet understated textures (paper fibers, brushed metal, leather spine, glass screens), and rounded corners that feel ergonomic. Dividers between sections are rarely simple lines; instead, they are slight differences in elevation (raised or inset panels), faint embossed rules, or shadow transitions suggesting overlapping physical layers. The overall color palette is composed of off-whites, warm grays, soft beige, charcoal, deep navy, and accents of muted teal and amber. No element is animated, no videos or moving graphics appear; all imagery is static and meticulously rendered to feel like a still-life composition of tactile research instruments and language-learning artifacts arranged on an elegant desk. The bottom of the page ends with a narrow, lower “toolbar” strip that spans the full width, visually grounded with a darker beige background and a subtle top inner shadow as if it is an inset groove on the table. On the left, small engraved text reads “© LinguaLab Research Collective”. On the right, three tiny, square icon buttons appear: a stylized stack of papers (for “Documentation”), a minimal lock symbol (for “Privacy”), and a small waveform-like icon (for “Changelog”), each button rendered like miniature metal keys slightly raised from the surface, completing the neo-skeuomorphic research aesthetic.

instruction: Planning the generation details for the following website:
input:
Design a stark, brutalist-style personal portfolio homepage that looks like it was assembled from raw wireframes and bold print materials, with a predominantly two-column layout that occasionally breaks into asymmetric blocks. The overall background is pure, flat white (#FFFFFF) with no gradients or textures anywhere; all elements sit crisply on top with harsh, solid edges. The only colors used are solid black (#000000), a single harsh neon accent color such as electric lime (#C6FF00), and occasionally mid-gray (#CCCCCC) for subtle dividers. All text is in one monospace font—describe it as “IBM Plex Mono” or any similar monospace type—rendered in solid black, with minimal weights (regular and bold only), no italics, and almost all text left-aligned. The hero section at the top spans the full width, but visually the page is split into two vertical columns: a dominant left column occupying about 65% of the width, and a narrower right column occupying about 35%, with a tall, uncompromising vertical rule between them—a 2px solid black line that runs from just beneath the header bar almost to the footer. At the very top, a fixed-position header bar runs across the full width, 64px tall, with a white background and a black 1px border at its top and bottom, giving it a boxed, printed feel. On the far left of this header is a simple text logo in bold, uppercase, black monospace reading: “PORTFOLIO // 2025”. Below it, in much smaller uppercase black text on the next line, it reads: “BRUTALIST ART & CODE”. On the far right of the header, a horizontally arranged, no-frills navigation uses uppercase words, separated by vertical bars, all in black: “HOME | WORK | TUTORIALS | ABOUT | CONTACT”. When hovering, nav items invert to black background and white text, but in the static design, show them as regular black text on white.

Beneath the header, the hero area is divided into the two main columns. The left hero column is a tall, monochrome statement block with heavy typography. At its top, aligned left with generous 48px padding from the left edge, there’s a large, bold, uppercase heading in black, sized around 48–56px, reading: “INDEPENDENT ARTIST. BRUTALIST TUTORIALS. ZERO POLISH.” Each phrase is on its own line, tightly tracked, so the type looks dense and loud. Below this, separated by a 16px gap, a smaller subheading in regular weight, uppercase, around 16px, reads: “A PORTFOLIO OF UNFINISHED EDGES, RAW LAYOUTS, AND WORK-IN-PROGRESS TUTORIALS.” Under this, a thin, black, horizontal line spans about half the width of the left column. Beneath the line, left-aligned, a block of body text (also monospace, about 14px) sits in a restrictive width, reading: “I build fractured interfaces, sharp layouts, and digital experiments. This portfolio is a catalogue of pieces and tutorials that refuse rounded corners and pastel gradients.” The paragraph is broken into two or three short lines, with a line-height slightly tight, evoking the compressed density of a printed zine.
