Below the paragraph, two brutally simple CTA buttons sit side by side with no rounded corners. The left button is a solid black rectangle with white uppercase text reading: “VIEW WORK”. The right button is a white rectangle with a 2px solid black border and black uppercase text reading: “ACCESS TUTORIALS”. When hovered, in the static representation, show them as unchanged, but emphasize their sharp rectangular shapes and the slightly off-center alignment that feels deliberate and severe: they are left-aligned with the text block, but one is slightly taller than the other, creating a subtle asymmetry.

In the right hero column, there is a single large, static image acting as a brutalist “poster”. This image fills most of the right column width and is stacked vertically with minimal margin from the top (about 32px from the header bottom). The poster image is a flat, high-contrast graphic, 3:4 aspect ratio, designed in a black-and-white brutalist style with a splash of the neon accent. The composition: a white background panel with thick black borders. In the center, there’s a stark, stylized isometric desk drawn in black lines—no shading—showing a simple monitor, a keyboard, and a drawing tablet. The perspective is slightly skewed, deliberately off, with straight, unforgiving lines. On the monitor screen, in gigantic black monospace letters on a neon lime rectangle, it says: “TUTORIAL: BREAK THE GRID”. Around the desk, floating black rectangles and thin lines reference interface windows and layered frames, some of which include tiny, illegible pseudo-text lines drawn as alternating horizontal dashes. The neon accent appears only as blocks behind some of these floating rectangles and the main title on the monitor, producing a harsh, jittery contrast against the black lines. The entire poster casts a sharp, rectangular drop shadow directly downward in a solid mid-gray, as if it’s a printed sheet pinned onto the page.

Below the hero, the page continues with a slightly offset two-column layout, where the left column remains wider, but individual sections sometimes span both columns or cut across the vertical rule. Immediately under the hero on the left side, there is a “Featured Work” section. Its title is in uppercase black text, 18px, aligned with the hero heading and reading: “FEATURED WORK (SELECTED)”. Underneath, instead of a neat grid, there is a staggered list of project entries, each inside a simple rectangular box with a 1px black border and no background. The boxes run vertically down the left column with uneven heights, creating a stacked collage effect. Each project box has a loud, uppercase project title at the top (about 20px), a thin black line under the title, and then a short description in smaller 14px text. For example, one project card reads: Title: “01 // INTERFACE AS COLLAGE”. Description: “A layout study that collides overlapping panels, amputated buttons, and misaligned text.” At the far right of this card, aligned with its top edge but still within the card, there is a small, square, black placeholder block (like an icon) with white uppercase text centered inside: “VIEW”. Another project might read: “02 // NOISE GRID PORTAL” with a description: “Experimental landing page using nothing but noise textures, solid borders, and harsh overlaps.” Some project cards extend slightly into the right column, crossing the vertical rule by a few pixels, but still remaining legible, reinforcing the brutalist disregard for perfect alignment.

Directly opposite the “Featured Work” section in the right column, there is a tall, stark panel titled “SUBSCRIPTION TUTORIALS” in uppercase, black, about 16px, right below the hero poster. This panel looks like a long, narrow receipt: a tall white rectangle with a thick 3px black border and thin horizontal rules separating lines of text. At the top of the panel, inside, a bold label reads: “UNSTYLED TUTORIAL FEED”. Below this, three or four tutorial “tiers” are presented, not as pricing cards but as rough text blocks with minimal typographic hierarchy. For example, one subsection reads: “TIER_01: ‘BASIC DISRUPTION’ — 3 PART EMAIL SERIES ON BREAKING LAYOUT SYMMETRY.” Another might read: “TIER_02: ‘HARSH GRIDS’ — FULL PROCESS BREAKDOWN OF MY BRUTALIST UI WORKFLOWS.” The text is stacked line by line with very little padding, and each tier is separated by a thin black horizontal rule that does not extend fully to the edges, leaving jagged white space. At the bottom of this receipt panel, a black, full-width bar with white uppercase text reads bluntly: “SUBSCRIBE FOR UPDATES”. Below this bar, in tiny, uppercase monospace text, an intentionally clinical notice reads: “NO FREE TRIALS. NO DISCOUNTS. ONLY TUTORIALS.”

Further down, the layout flips: an “About the Artist” section is placed mainly in the right column, creating an asymmetry. This “About” section is contained in a wide but low white box centered in the right column, with its left edge slightly overlapping the vertical rule by about 16px, visually encroaching on the left. The box has a black border and a loud title in uppercase: “ABOUT // UNFRIENDLY INTERFACES”. Inside, two columns of text are arranged within the box itself. The left inner column contains a short, stark bio: “I’m an independent digital artist and interface designer focused on brutalist aesthetics, glitchy grids, and unapologetically sharp layouts.” The right inner column lists a series of blunt statements, each on its own line in uppercase, such as: “NO SOFT SHADOWS.”, “NO ROUNDED CORNERS.”, “NO UNNECESSARY ANIMATIONS.” Each statement is separated by a faint horizontal rule. Above this “About” box, there is a small, black-and-white portrait placeholder: a square image displayed like a photocopied ID photo. The portrait shows a faceless, abstract figure: a simple outline of a head and shoulders in black line art, with no detailed features, against a flat white background. Across the figure’s eyes, a thin neon lime rectangle with black uppercase text reads: “ARTIST”. The portrait has a thick black border and is aligned so it slightly overlaps the top border of the “About” box, disturbing the clean separation.

Meanwhile, below the “Featured Work” list in the left column, a section titled “PROCESS NOTES” appears. The title is uppercase, slightly smaller than “Featured Work”, and followed by a 2px thick black line. Underneath are three rectangular “note” blocks, each styled like torn pages from a notebook but rendered with straight edges only (no torn effects). Each note is a white box with a black border, a small uppercase label in the top left corner (e.g., “NOTE_01”, “NOTE_02”), and a paragraph of tight, almost justified text explaining the artist’s approach to brutalist design. One note might read: “Layouts are treated as physical posters stacked inside a browser window, not as smooth digital canvases. Overlapping elements are deliberate ruptures, not accidental misalignments.” These notes align left but are slightly indented compared to the “Featured Work” cards above, creating stepped horizontal edges.

Near the bottom of the page, spanning almost full width but still respecting the two-column logic, is a section devoted to direct engagement: “CONTACT & COMMISSIONS”. The title is huge, uppercase, and aggressive, centered between the two columns and partly overlapping the vertical rule. The text reads: “CONTACT & COMMISSIONS (SERIOUS INQUIRIES ONLY)”. Below, the section is split again into two halves. On the left, a brutally simple contact form is presented as stacked, labeled fields. Each field label is uppercase, slightly bold, aligned left: “NAME”, “EMAIL”, “PROJECT OUTLINE”. The input areas themselves are nothing but empty white rectangles with 1px black borders, spanning the width of the left column. The “PROJECT OUTLINE” field is taller, resembling a text area with additional vertical height but the same strict rectangular border. At the bottom of the form, a full-width submit button is rendered as a black rectangle with centered white uppercase text reading: “SEND UNPOLISHED REQUEST”. No icons, no extra decoration.

On the right side of the “Contact & Commissions” section, instead of social media icons, there is a list of “UNSOCIAL LINKS”—simple textual URLs, each on its own line in uppercase, with harsh labels and ASCII-like styling. For example: “EMAIL: HELLO[AT]BRUTALIST-PORTFOLIO[DOT]COM”, “GITHUB: /RAW-LAYOUTS”, “DRIBBBLE: /NO-ROUNDED-CORNERS”. Each link line has a small square checkbox to its left, like a form option, but they are simply solid black squares that do not indicate state—purely decorative in this static design, underscoring the anti-UX aesthetic. Above this list, in smaller type, a blunt notice reads: “I DO NOT RESPOND TO ‘CAN YOU MAKE IT MORE FRIENDLY?’ REQUESTS.”

The footer at the very bottom is compressed, spanning full width with a white background and a 1px black top border. Inside the footer, aligned left, tiny uppercase text reads: “© 2025 BRUTALIST PORTFOLIO. ALL EDGES INTENTIONAL.” On the right, also in tiny uppercase, there is a deadpan line: “BUILT WITHOUT FRAMEWORKS. NO COOKIES. NO TRACKING.” The vertical rule between the main columns terminates just above the footer, leaving a small gap of white space, emphasizing vertical tension.

Throughout the entire page, there are no rounded corners, no drop shadows except the single harsh shadow behind the right-column hero poster, and no gradients, textures, or animations. All images are flat, high-contrast, mostly black-and-white with occasional neon lime blocks, and all text is in solid, rigid monospace uppercase, with some small body text in sentence case but still monospace. The layout leans heavily on white space and stark black lines, with intentional misalignments and overlaps that produce a chaotic yet controlled brutalist portfolio aesthetic that feels like a hybrid between an independent artist’s tutorial subscription homepage and a raw exhibition catalog, all firmly static with no motion or data visualizations of any kind.

instruction: Planning the generation details for the following website:
input:
Design a single-page “neo-skeuomorphic” dashboard homepage for a high-end co-working space membership platform titled “NestWorks Studio Hub,” blending tactile, semi-realistic interface elements with a contemporary, slightly asymmetrical and overlapping layout. The entire viewport feels like a polished, physical control console sitting on a softly lit wooden desk, without any moving or animated parts and without charts or data visualizations. The background of the page is a subtle, photorealistic, desaturated oak wood texture in a cool neutral tone, with a very soft vignette around the edges. Over this background, place multiple overlapping, floating panels and cards that cast soft, realistic shadows and have slightly raised edges, beveled corners, and faint highlights, as if pieces of matte lacquered plastic and brushed metal have been arranged carefully. The color palette is a combination of warm off-whites, soft taupes, deep charcoal, and accents in muted teal and brass.

At the very top, centered horizontally and slightly inset from the edges, place a wide, rounded-rectangle “status bar” panel with a soft off-white base and a subtle gloss strip along its top edge, giving the impression of a gently curved surface catching light from above. This panel is about 80% of the viewport width, with generous padding and a small drop shadow that fades into the wood texture below. Inside, on the left side, the brand name “NestWorks” appears in a modern, semi-serif wordmark: “Nest” in a dark charcoal color and “Works” in muted teal, using a clean display font with lightly flared serifs. Below the brand name, a smaller tagline reads: “Where focused minds share crafted spaces.” in a delicate, light-weight sans-serif font, medium gray, left-aligned. On the right side of this top panel, place two pill-shaped buttons, slightly raised as if they are physical rocker switches. The left button is labeled “Log In” in uppercase, with a charcoal label on a matte off-white pill with a fine brass border and a subtle inner shadow, suggesting it’s pressable. The right button is “Join Now” in uppercase, white text on a muted teal pill that looks slightly more elevated, with a stronger drop shadow and a faint metallic rim, making it feel like the primary action. Between these buttons, a tiny circular indicator light—a small, raised disc with a pale green glow effect—has text to its right: “Status: Spaces Available” in tiny, uppercase gray letters, giving the bar a dashboard-like presence.

Below this, slightly offset to the left and overlapping the bottom of the top panel by a few pixels, is the main hero console: a large, horizontally-oriented panel with rounded corners and a very subtle vertical gradient from warm ivory at the top to a soft, pale stone color at the bottom. It is divided into two main sections: on the left, a somewhat larger area (about 60% of the width) with imagery, and on the right, a slightly narrower area with interactive cards. The entire hero console appears to rest on small, photorealistic brass “posts” at its corners—tiny cylindrical pegs casting soft shadows—reinforcing the neo-skeuomorphic feel. On the left side, embed a static hero image window resembling a framed glass panel inset into the console with a thin brushed-chrome frame and bevels. The image shows a photorealistic wide-angle interior of a sunlit co-working lounge: mid-century modern chairs in soft tan leather and neutral fabric, a large communal wooden table with sleek black metal legs, indoor plants with large green leaves in ceramic pots, a bar-height counter with stools in the background, and floor-to-ceiling windows revealing a blurred cityscape. Warm natural light floods the scene from the right side of the image, casting soft shadows and highlighting the textures of wood and fabric. The perspective is slightly elevated, looking down into the space. At the bottom-left corner of this image window, overlay a small, semi-translucent label that looks like a physical brass nameplate with engraved text: “Flagship Loft · Downtown” engraved in thin, dark letters, with subtle engraved shadows.

To the right of this hero image, within the same main console, is a stacked arrangement of content modules. At the top, aligned with the top of the hero image, a title reads: “Today’s Workspace Dashboard” in a medium-weight sans-serif font, charcoal color, left-aligned, followed by a smaller subtitle below it in lighter gray: “Plan your day, reserve your spot, and explore your studio community.” Below this, three slightly overlapping cards are arranged in a stepping ladder formation: the first card is closest and appears most elevated; the second is slightly behind and down-right; the third is further back and down-right again. Each card has a rounded rectangle shape with a matte off-white surface and delicate inner shadows around the edges. The top-most card is labeled “Quick Reservation” at the top left, with a small embossed icon of a calendar: a rounded square with two small mechanical “hooks” at the top, and tiny, carved-in date boxes. Under the title, there are three tactile dropdown-like capsules laid horizontally: the first reads “Location: All Studios,” the second “Seat Type: Hot Desk,” and the third “Time: 9:00 AM – 5:00 PM.” Each capsule looks like a physical toggle: an inset rectangle with a subtle inner shadow and slight highlight at the top edge, and a tiny downward chevron icon that appears embossed rather than drawn. Below this, a large rectangular button spans the width of the card, labeled “Check Availability” in white, uppercase text on a teal background with rounded corners, looking like a friction-fit rubber button. It has a sharp highlight along its top edge and a stronger drop shadow beneath, suggesting a pressable control.

The second, slightly overlapped card is titled “Membership Overview” and includes three horizontal “info strips” that resemble glossy plastic sliders. Each strip has a label on the left and a tactile meter on the right: the first strip says “Days Remaining: 24” with a meter comprising a series of small, raised capsules—like a row of physical pill-shaped LEDs—half of them filled with muted teal, the rest a soft gray. The second strip says “Guest Passes: 3” with three filled teal capsules and two empty ones. The third says “Locker Access: Active” with a single rectangular LED-like block glowing softly in teal, and a tiny engraved padlock icon next to it. The third, rear-most card is titled “Community Notes” and appears slightly faded, with a scribble-like icon reminiscent of a sticky note. Inside, three short lines of text look like typed notes, such as “· ‘Community Brunch at 10:30 AM in Lounge A.’,” “· ‘Podcast Booth maintenance 2:00 PM – 3:00 PM.’,” and “· ‘Soft opening: Rooftop Garden Deck.’” Using a light monospaced typeface in medium gray suggests a digital print-out on a beige card.

Beneath the main hero console, the layout continues in a slightly asymmetrical fashion. On the left side, directly under the hero image and extending downwards, place a tall, retro-inspired vertical “Studio Selector” module that resembles an elongated, slightly curved panel with a brushed aluminum texture and framed edges that look like they’re screwed into the wood background with small, photorealistic screw heads at the corners. At the top of this module, in all caps and spaced-out letters, it says “STUDIO SELECTOR” in engraved-looking text with a subtle depth effect. Below, four stacked selection tiles represent different NestWorks locations. Each tile is a pill-shaped button with a small embossed icon and text. The first tile is “Downtown Loft” with an icon of a small outline of a city skyline. The second is “Garden Atrium” with a leafy branch icon. The third is “Harbor View” with a sailboat icon. The fourth is “Night Owl Annex” with a crescent moon icon. Each tile has a gentle gradient from lighter at the top to darker at the bottom, with a faint highlight line, making them resemble physical rocker switches. The currently selected tile, “Downtown Loft,” glows softly around its edges in teal, and its icon appears slightly raised. To the right of each tile, a small oval indicator acts like a tiny analog readout, with engraved text such as “OPEN,” “LIMITED,” or “QUIET” in uppercase, using tiny letters that look physically engraved and filled with dark ink.

To the right of the “Studio Selector,” overlapping its midsection and extending toward the center-bottom of the page, is a “Workspace Panels” section containing three wide, horizontally aligned cards describing different space types. These cards appear like removable index cards clipped to a soft surface. Each card has a subtle paper texture and a small, metallic, U-shaped clip at the top left that casts a tiny shadow. The leftmost card is labeled “Focus Pods” in a slightly bolder font with a tiny headphone icon embossed next to the title. Below, a short description in regular-weight sans-serif text reads: “Sound-dampened personal pods with adjustable lighting and ergonomic seating.” In the bottom-right corner of this card, a small, rounded, brass-colored button says “View Pods” in uppercase, dark text. The middle card is titled “Open Desks” with a minimal desk icon, and text that reads: “Flexible seating in communal areas, perfect for collaboration and casual work.” Its button says “Explore Desks.” The rightmost card is titled “Private Studios” with a tiny door icon, and its description reads: “Enclosed offices for teams needing privacy with access to all shared amenities.” Its button says “See Studios.” The three cards are slightly rotated at different angles, with the middle card being the most straight-on, creating a natural, asymmetrical, yet balanced arrangement.

Further to the right of the “Workspace Panels” and slightly overlapping their right edges, place a vertical “Today’s Schedule” panel that resembles an old-school flip clock, but flattened into a clean neo-skeuomorphic style. The panel is a tall, dark charcoal rounded rectangle with a slightly textured surface, as if made from soft-touch plastic. At the top in small, uppercase, light-gray text is the title “TODAY’S SCHEDULE.” Beneath it, three timeline entries appear as horizontal flip segments. Each segment is a pill-shaped inset with beveled edges and a split down the middle, implying two flaps. The first segment reads “09:00 AM · Quiet Hours Begin · Lounge A & Focus Pods.” The second reads “11:30 AM · Member Mixer · Coffee Bar.” The third says “03:00 PM · Workshop: Personal Branding · Studio 4B.” The time portion on the left uses a slightly bolder, digital-like font in a bright but soft teal, while the rest of the text is in a lighter gray, all aligned to the left. Tiny, subtle shadows around each flip segment and a faint highlight line at the top make them resemble real mechanical flaps, though they are static.

At the very bottom of the page, spanning the width but respecting some margin from the edges, is a footer panel that looks like a slim, mounted metal plate embedded into the wooden surface. This footer has a brushed metal texture in a warm gray tone, with a barely visible grain. On the left side, embossed text reads: “NestWorks Studio Hub © 2025” in small caps, with the letters appearing slightly engraved. In the center, three minimal navigation links—“About,” “Memberships,” and “Contact”—are rendered as engraved-label style text with a subtle drop shadow. On the right side, there are three circular icon “dials” that look like tactile controls: one with a stylized, embossed envelope symbol for email, one with a minimal “in” symbol for LinkedIn, and one with an abstract globe icon for the website. These circular icons have brushed metal rims and flat, off-white centers, and each appears slightly indented as if pressed into the footer plate.

Throughout the page, ensure all text uses clean, legible sans-serif typography (aside from the brand and occasional engraved labels), with clear hierarchy: headings around 24–32px, subheadings 16–18px, and body text 14–16px. All text is static and there are no animated or video elements. There are no charts, graphs, or data visualizations; the dashboard feel comes instead from these tactile controls, switches, and panels. Spacing between panels is generous, with about 24–40px of whitespace separating each major module, allowing the overlapping and asymmetry to feel intentional and airy rather than cluttered. Shadows are soft, radiating out in a slightly warm hue, and highlights are subtle but present on all edges that should feel touchable. The overall impression is that the user is staring at a beautifully crafted, physical control board for their co-working life—calm, slightly retro, but distinctly modern and digital in content.

instruction: Planning the generation details for the following website:
input:
Design a whimsical, playful, single-column leaning landing page for a service called "SnowFlick Studio Pass", a fictional monthly subscription that sends members behind-the-scenes film production content from ski lodges and snowy mountain film sets. The aesthetic should blend cozy ski lodge warmth with cinematic flair and a childlike sense of fun, using a soft, pastel winter color palette. The overall layout is mostly single-column, with content stacked vertically, but it occasionally breaks into gentle asymmetries: images offset to one side, curved cards overlapping the main column, and decorative shapes that peek out from behind sections. The page background is a soft vertical gradient that transitions from a pale sky blue (#E8F4FF) at the top to a creamy off-white (#FFF9F2) at the bottom, with faint, barely-there, hand-drawn snowflake doodles scattered in the background at low opacity. A subtle vignette effect around the edges of the viewport gives the feeling of looking through a frosty window. At the very top, a narrow, centered navbar sits within the main column, floating on a rounded rectangle with a translucent frosted-glass effect and a soft drop shadow. The navbar background is semi-transparent white with a slight blur; it has pill-shaped rounded corners and is horizontally centered with generous top and bottom padding. On the left inside the navbar is the logo: a playful hand-lettered wordmark reading "SnowFlick" in rounded, slightly irregular script, colored in a warm raspberry red (#FF4E7A), with a tiny cartoon clapperboard resting on the "S". The clapperboard is illustrated in a flat, modern style, with a teal body, white diagonal stripes on the top, and a tiny snowflake etched into the clap bar. Beneath "SnowFlick" in smaller uppercase sans-serif text is "STUDIO PASS" in muted charcoal gray. To the right of the logo are four navigation links in a simple, rounded sans-serif font: "How It Works", "What You Get", "Lodge Stories", and "Pricing". They are spaced out with equal gaps, text in medium gray, that turns raspberry red on hover; in the prompt we show the default state in gray. At the far right of the navbar is a pill-shaped button that reads "Join the Crew". This button has a gradient fill from raspberry red to warm coral, white bold text, subtle inner shadow, and a soft drop shadow that makes it look like a puffy sticker.

Below this, occupying the hero section, is a large, playful composition that still fits within the central column but spills outward a bit. The hero background behind the content is a broad, rounded rectangle with a soft blue-to-lavender gradient and a very subtle noise texture, creating a tactile, cozy feel. The hero has large top and bottom padding, making it feel open and airy. At the center, in large, friendly, rounded display type, is the main headline: "Snowy sets. Cozy cuts. Every month." The words "Snowy sets." are in a dark navy (#1C2740), "Cozy cuts." is in raspberry red, and "Every month." is in a teal-blue (#2AA6C5). The font is bold, with slightly exaggerated rounded terminals, giving it a playful, modern mood. Below the headline is a short subheading in a smaller sans-serif: "Subscribe to behind-the-scenes film magic from real mountain lodges and snow-dusted sets—shot, snuggled, and shipped straight to your screen." The text is centered, medium gray, with a line height generous enough to feel airy. Under the subheading are two primary hero call-to-action elements centered in the column: first, a large pill-shaped button that reads "Start My Free Snow Week" in white, bold, slightly larger text on a gradient background that blends teal into deep blue with a subtle glow, and second, a ghost button to its right that reads "Peek Inside" in medium gray text with a thin raspberry red border and transparent fill, both aligned on the same horizontal line. Below the buttons, a single line of tiny text reads "No ice-cold contracts. Cancel anytime." in a lighter gray, giving it a playful reassurance.

To the right of the hero text (still within the hero block but breaking the strict single-column feel), there is a large, curved-corner hero illustration framed inside an irregular organic blob shape that overlaps the gradient background. The illustration is a whimsical, semi-flat but slightly dimensional scene of a ski lodge film set. The viewer perspective is slightly above eye-level, looking diagonally down into a cozy lodge interior that opens onto snowy mountains through big window panels. The lodge is made of warm honey-brown wood with chunky beams and cozy textures. In the foreground, a quirky cartoonish camera crew is filming. A character with a bright teal beanie, oversized round glasses, and a puffed raspberry red parka stands behind a vintage-style movie camera on a tripod, which points toward a small, improvised set. The camera is drawn with rounded shapes, a glowing teal recording light, and a tiny hanging charm shaped like a snowflake. To the left, another character in a mustard yellow sweater and patterned wool socks holds a boom microphone wrapped in a fuzzy white windshield that looks like a tiny snowdrift. On the right, an actor stands on a rug shaped like a melted snowflake, wearing a comically large puffer coat in pastel blue, tossing fake snow in the air; the fake snow is depicted as small, round dots and star-shaped flakes, sparkling with soft highlights. Behind them, large lodge windows frame a snow-covered landscape: pine trees dusted with snow, gentle rolling hills, and distant mountain peaks painted in gradients of blue and lavender. Soft, golden light spills from lanterns hanging from the beams; shadows are soft and diffuse, and everything feels lightly glowing. Little details include a clapboard leaning against a crate, mugs of hot cocoa with swirling steam on a side table, and reels of film stacked like snowmen in the corner. The style has smooth vector shapes with subtle gradients, soft shadows, and slight texture on wooden surfaces and clothing for warmth. Around the hero illustration, popping out of the blob shape, are small doodles: hand-drawn white stars, scribbly snowflakes, and tiny film sprocket holes scattered like confetti.

Scrolling down, the next section is a full-width band within the main column titled "How SnowFlick Works", centered. This section sits on a slightly darker off-white panel (#FFF3E6) with rounded top corners that visually separate it from the hero. The heading is in playful, uppercase sans-serif, navy colored, with a smaller raspberry red underline stroke that looks hand-drawn. Below, three step cards are stacked vertically to preserve the single-column feel, but each card has a gentle asymmetry: the icon circle is alternately aligned left, center, and right. Each card is a rounded rectangle with a white background, a subtle drop shadow, and thin teal outline. The first card shows at the top-left a circular icon: a stylized envelope wearing a tiny beanie and scarf, drawn in flat pastel colors, with small white snowflakes orbiting it. To the right of the icon, the text block includes a step label "Step 1" in teal small caps, then a title in bold navy reading "Pick Your SnowPass", and a short description in smaller gray text: "Choose a monthly or seasonal SnowFlick Studio Pass, tailored to how often you like your cozy chaos." The second card, slightly overlapping the bottom of the first, has its icon centered at the top: a film clapperboard that, when closed, looks like a tiny cabin roof dusted with snow. Underneath is "Step 2" in teal, then "We Film In Real Lodges" in navy, with the description: "Our crew sets up in actual mountain cabins and ski lodges, capturing the real behind-the-scenes of indie film shoots amid the snow." The third card shifts its icon to the top-right: a rounded rectangle illustration of a smartphone showing a thumbnail grid of snowy film scenes, with a progress bar under one. Below are the texts "Step 3", "You Stream The Snowy Magic", and "Every month, unlock fresh, cozy, chaos-filled footage, bloopers, and commentary from the mountain set."

Next, a section titled "What You Get Inside" appears, again in the central column but visually framed by two asymmetrical decorative shapes: on the left, a teal blob with tiny white dots suggesting snow, and on the right, a raspberry red blob partially cropped by the viewport. The section title is centered in navy, with a subheading directly below in gray reading: "Cozy bundles of film nerd joy, straight from the slopes." Below, a single-column stack of card-like feature blocks explains different subscription perks. Each feature card has a playful icon on the left and text on the right, both aligned in a flexible row that snaps full-width on mobile. The first feature card marks "Behind-the-scenes footage" with an icon depicting a small film reel wearing ski goggles, the reel holes forming big round eyes. The description reads: "From first slate to final wrap, see how we turn snowed-in cabins into cinematic gold." The second card, "Warm-up commentary tracks", has an icon of a speech bubble emerging from a steaming mug of cocoa; the description: "Director and crew commentary that feels more like a fireside chat than a film school lecture." The third, "Exclusive snow day bloopers", is illustrated with a cartoon crew member slipping on ice, camera flying, but still smiling; the text: "Every flub, snowball fight, and ‘who put the mic in the hot tub?’ moment, captured for your delight." The fourth, "Monthly set design breakdowns", shows a tiny cutaway diagram of a lodge interior, with labeled arrows and doodled notes; the description: "How we hide cables in knit blankets, fake snow in cereal, and turn log walls into light bounces."

Following this is a visually striking testimonial section called "Lodge Love Notes". The background of this section is a soft blue band with a faint pattern of overlapping, translucent hearts and snowflakes. Centered at the top in white text is the title "Lodge Love Notes", followed by a smaller white line: "What our subscribers are toasting with cocoa about." Below, three testimonial cards are arranged in a single vertical column, each card with rounded corners, white background, and a subtly uneven outline that makes them look hand-cut. The first card features a small circular avatar on the left: an illustrated person wearing a knit beanie with a pom-pom, smiling, drawn in a friendly, cartoonish style. Next to it, the name "Mila, aspiring cinematographer" appears in navy, with the quote underneath in gray italics: "SnowFlick makes me feel like I’m on set, minus the frostbite. I literally pause to take notes." The second card shows "Jay, cozy-movie addict" and the quote: "The commentary tracks feel like a slumber party in a ski lodge—except everyone knows what a LUT is." The third card shows "Rin, mountain-town local" and the quote: "I live near these lodges, and somehow SnowFlick still makes them look more magical than I see every day." Each card has a tiny icon in one corner: a heart-shaped snowball, a doodled star, or a tiny clapperboard, varying by card.

Below this is a section called "Peek At A SnowFlick Session", designed as a large, static preview image in the center of the page, mimicking a streaming interface without any animations. The heading is centered, navy, with a smaller subheading in gray: "A still from 'Cabin Cue Chaos' — one of our member-favorite shoots." Underneath is a wide image of a tablet device shown in three-quarter perspective, slightly rotated, with rounded edges and a thin white bezel, hovering on a soft drop shadow. On the tablet screen, a mock video player UI is depicted, but frozen: a large play button icon sits in the middle, but no motion is shown. The still image on the tablet screen is a different angle on a lodge set: a director wrapped in a plaid blanket, holding a megaphone, stands on a stack of snow-packed apple crates while pointing toward a camera. The camera and crew are facing a window where snow gently piles on the sill. Fairy lights drape around a doorway, glowing warmly. The color palette in this still uses warm oranges and cool blues, giving a cinematic color-graded vibe, with a soft depth of field blur around the edges. Below the static preview, in small gray captions, it reads: "This is a static preview. In the real SnowFlick Studio Pass, you’ll explore the full session."

Next comes a "Choose Your SnowPass" pricing section. The background is a soft gradient from warm cream to light blush, with faint, white, doodled price tags and snowflakes drifting diagonally behind the content for visual interest. The section title is centered in navy, followed by the line "No avalanche of options. Just two cozy passes." There are two pricing cards stacked vertically for a single-column feel, but they differ in size and decoration, adding asymmetry. The first card is labeled "Monthly SnowPass" with a small teal tag-shaped badge at the top that reads "Most Popular" in white uppercase text. The card background is white with a subtle teal border and small snowflake icons stamped into each corner. The price is shown in large, bold text: "$14 / month". Underneath is a list of perks, each preceded by a tiny icon: a star, a film reel, a cocoa mug, all drawn in outline style. The visible textual bullet items (presented as separate lines of text) read: "New behind-the-scenes bundle every month", "Access to full past-season archive", and "Cancel anytime, no frosty fees." At the bottom of the card is a large button reading "Start Monthly Pass" in white on a teal gradient. The second card below has a slightly larger height and a faint gold border, titled "Seasonal SnowPass". Its price reads "$39 / 3 months", with a small, playful note in gray under it: "Perfect for winter hibernation." Its perks read: "Three months of monthly bundles", "Exclusive seasonal lodge special", and "Printable 'Director of Cozy' certificate." Its button reads "Go Seasonal & Save" with a coral-to-raspberry gradient.

After pricing, introduce a section called "Meet The Tiny Crew", lightheartedly introducing the team. The title is centered, navy, and the subtitle in gray reads: "The humans behind the snow globes and storyboards." Below, a vertically stacked series of character cards show illustrated team members in playful, cartoonish portraits. Each portrait is framed in a rounded rectangle with a pastel background and subtle circular halo behind the character’s head. For example, the first card shows "Lena — Frostbite Director" in bold navy, with a smaller line "Loves: slow zooms, hot cocoa, and socks with tiny clapperboards." Her portrait: she wears a fluffy white earmuff headset and a raspberry scarf, holding a small director’s viewfinder, with snowflakes stuck to her eyelashes. The second card shows "Tomas — Chief Snow Wrangler", with the description: "Keeps fake snow and real snow politely separated." His portrait shows him with a teal beanie and a shovel slung over his shoulder, but instead of dirt, his shovel holds coils of film. The third card, "Aya — Cozy Lighting Wizard", with the description: "Can bounce light off anything, including marshmallows." Her portrait has her wrapped in fairy lights, holding a handheld LED panel that softly glows. Each portrait uses a soft shading style with simple shapes, warm highlights, and gentle shadows.

Near the bottom of the page, add a playful FAQ section titled "Curious? Cold Feet?" in navy, centered. Below, the subtitle in gray: "We warmed up the answers for you." The FAQs are displayed as a vertical accordion-like stack, each question in a rounded rectangular bar with a white background and a soft drop shadow. The questions are in navy with a small plus icon at the right. In the design, show three expanded questions with their answers visible beneath them, inside the same rounded card. For example, the first question: "Is this for film pros or cozy movie fans?" with the answer text: "Both! If you love seeing how scenes are made—or just love the mood of a snowed-in set—you’ll feel right at home." The second: "Do I need fancy gear to enjoy this?" with the answer: "Nope. A phone, tablet, or laptop works just fine. The only recommended accessory is a blanket." The third: "What if I melt and want to cancel?" with the answer: "You can cancel anytime from your account dashboard. Your access stays active until your current pass ends."

Finally, the footer section stretches across the same central column but is visually anchored with a slightly darker navy background (#121930) that fades into transparency at the very top edge. Within this footer, the content is centered but slightly asymmetrical: on the left, the "SnowFlick" logo appears smaller, in white, with the clapperboard icon inverted to white outlines. Under it, in tiny, warm gray text, is the tagline: "SnowFlick Studio Pass is a fictional service that gives you very real cozy feelings." On the right side of the column is a vertical list of footer links in white text: "About", "Support", "Gift a Pass", "Snow Ethics". Beneath them, a horizontal row of small, rounded social icons (static, not animated) shows simplified white line icons for a camera, a film reel, and a mountain, each inside a small circular button with a teal outline. At the very bottom center, a tiny line of text in pale gray reads: "© SnowFlick Studio Pass. All snowballs reserved." Scattered faintly in the footer background are tiny star shapes and snowflakes, barely visible, completing the playful, cozy, film-meets-ski-lodge aesthetic, with no videos, no animations, and no charts or data visualizations anywhere on the page.

instruction: Planning the generation details for the following website:
input:
Design a cozy, organic, hand-crafted event page for a community workshop series called “Harvest & Harmony: Urban Garden Weekends,” with a layout that feels like layered paper, overlapping cards, and slightly crooked, human-made arrangements rather than rigid grids. The entire page sits on a warm, off-white, slightly textured background that resembles recycled paper with very subtle fibers and speckles, and the color palette uses muted greens (sage, moss, olive), warm terracotta, soft mustard, and charcoal pencil gray. All edges feel soft and irregular—no harsh perfect rectangles; instead, imagine cards with subtly imperfect borders, faint shadows as if they were lightly taped or pinned to a corkboard. The primary font for headings is a hand-lettered, brush-style typeface that looks like natural ink strokes (“Harvest Script”), with slightly varying stroke widths; body text uses a clean, rounded sans-serif (“Garden Sans”), in a dark charcoal color for comfortable reading. The page has generous white space and an asymmetrical, hierarchical layout, with some sections overlapping slightly, as if stacked pieces of paper and photos.

At the very top, across the full width, place a narrow, hand-drawn-looking navigation strip, like a watercolor wash of very pale sage green that doesn’t fully reach the edges, leaving irregular margins. On top of this wash, aligned slightly left of center, is the simple logo: a small, circular hand-drawn icon of two crossing gardening tools (a trowel and a pruning shear) inside a loose, imperfect circle, all in a dark moss green, followed by the text “Harvest & Harmony” in the brush script, and beneath it in tiny uppercase “URBAN GARDEN WEEKENDS” in Garden Sans. To the right, but not perfectly aligned (slightly higher than the logo baseline), are simple text navigation links in dark gray, in uppercase Garden Sans with extra letterspacing: “About”, “Workshops”, “Schedule”, “Location”, “Tickets”, and “FAQ”. Each link has a tiny, hand-drawn underline that appears like a subtle pencil squiggle, uneven, but static (no animation). On the far right is a pill-shaped button that looks like a slightly wrinkled piece of craft paper taped onto the nav: the button background is a warm terracotta with a soft shadow; its label reads “Get Tickets” in white uppercase Garden Sans, centered, with a slightly thicker hand-drawn border around the button in deeper terracotta.

Below this strip, occupying the top half of the screen, is the hero section, using an overlapping collage style. On the left two-thirds, there is a large, softly rounded rectangular block of pale cream (slightly lighter than the page background) with a torn-paper bottom edge; it sits on top of the background, dropping a gentle shadow. At its top left corner, slightly overlapping the nav, there’s a small piece of “washi tape” drawn as a translucent strip of pale mustard with tiny white polka dots, holding the panel in place. Inside this block, the main headline is large and hand-lettered in the brush script, dark moss green, reading: “Grow Together in the City.” Below it, a subheading in medium Garden Sans, dark charcoal, left-aligned, reads: “Join us for a weekend of hands-on workshops, soil-under-your-fingernails joy, and community connection in our rooftop garden oasis.” Beneath the subheading, there is a vertical spacing gap, then a row of key event details laid out horizontally in small, slightly overlapping tags that look like seed packet labels. Each “tag” is a rounded rectangle with a muted textured fill and a hand-drawn border. The first tag is pale sage with “Next Session” in tiny uppercase and “May 18–19, 2025” in slightly larger sentence case. The second tag is warm cream with a small, hand-sketched calendar icon (a square with two loops at the top) and the text “Sat & Sun · 9 AM – 4 PM”. The third tag is soft terracotta with a tiny sprouting plant doodle and the text “Rooftop Garden · Old Mill District”. These tags overlap slightly, like cards casually laid on top of each other.

Just below these tags is a pair of side-by-side call-to-action buttons aligned left. The primary button appears like a thick seed packet label, in deep moss green with a textured look and off-white uppercase text: “Reserve Your Spot”. It has a thin, wobbly hand-drawn border in a darker green. To its right, slightly lower and overlapping the bottom right corner of the green button, is a secondary button drawn as a transparent outline, with just the hand-drawn border in terracotta and the text “View Workshop Themes” in terracotta. Below the buttons, at the bottom left of this hero panel, is a small line of microcopy in tiny Garden Sans reading: “Limited to 24 gardeners per weekend. All skill levels welcome.” This text is accompanied by a small hand-drawn heart-shaped leaf icon.

On the right side of the hero section, overlapping the cream headline panel, is a large, vertically oriented hero image that feels like a printed photograph taped to the page. The image has slightly rounded corners and a thin white border with a subtle shadow, and is rotated just a few degrees counterclockwise to feel casual. The image is a realistic photograph-style illustration of a diverse group of people (various ages, genders, and ethnic backgrounds) in casual clothes, standing and kneeling around a raised wooden planter bed on a sunny rooftop. They are laughing and planting herbs and vegetables. The background shows soft-focus city buildings and a warm, late-afternoon sky with golden light. Foreground details include rich, dark soil, green seedlings, a coil of hose, and terra cotta pots. The lighting is warm and natural, with gentle soft shadows. A small piece of washi tape in pale sage, with a diagonal striped pattern, appears drawn across the top edge of the photo, as if holding it in place. Slightly tucked behind this main photo, peeking out at the top right, is a smaller Polaroid-style photo overlapping its edge: this secondary image shows a close-up of a person’s hands cupping a small plant with visible roots, against a blurred garden background; it’s also rotated and pinned visually by a tiny hand-drawn pushpin icon sketched above it in dark gray.

Scrolling down, the next section is a wide, off-white panel with a subtle shadow, visually overlapping the bottom of the hero section. This panel’s top edge is irregular, like torn notebook paper. It introduces the event with the heading “What Is Harvest & Harmony?” in the brush script, centered at the top. Below, the content is arranged in a two-column layout, but slightly asymmetrical: the left column is narrower for text, and the right column is wider and stacked with smaller illustrated notes. The left column contains two short paragraphs in Garden Sans, dark charcoal, with comfortable line spacing. The text explains that “Harvest & Harmony is a monthly rooftop workshop series for city dwellers who want to learn how to grow herbs, veggies, and pollinator-friendly plants in tight spaces — without any prior gardening experience.” The second paragraph emphasizes community and mindfulness in gardening. Below the paragraphs, a small “leaf bullet list” presents three key values, each bullet being a tiny leafy branch icon in sage: “Hands-on learning in small groups”, “Sustainable, low-waste gardening practices”, and “Tea, snacks, and conversation under the open sky.”

In the right column of this section, three small overlapping cards float, each representing a highlight. They’re arranged in a loose triangular cluster. The top card is a pale sage green rectangle with a slightly darker edge and a doodled outline of a watering can in the top left corner. It has the title “Guided by Local Growers” in bold Garden Sans and a short line: “Our facilitators are neighborhood gardeners, herbalists, and compost enthusiasts.” Slightly below and to the right, overlapping the corner of the first card, is a warm cream card with a small sketch of a bee and flowers in one corner and the title “Rooted in Community” with a single sentence about collaborative planting and sharing harvest tips. The third card, slightly tilted and overlapping both, is a pale mustard card with a hand-drawn sun icon and the title “Mindful Moments” with text about short grounding exercises between activities. Each card casts a subtle drop shadow and looks like pieces of paper pinned onto the background.

Next, introduce a section that outlines the workshop themes, displayed as a loosely organized “Workshop Garden Bed” grid, where the layout is hierarchical yet imperfect. The section background is a faint, almost ghostly illustration of a top-down garden plan in light gray lines (beds, paths, herbs scribbled in), barely visible behind the content. At the top left, the heading “Weekend Workshop Themes” appears in large brush script, aligned left, with a tiny doodle of a sprouting seed next to it. Beneath it, a short explanatory line: “Each weekend focuses on a different theme—choose the one that makes your green thumbs tingle.” Below, instead of a strict grid, six theme cards of varying widths and staggered heights form an organic mosaic, each with its own subtle color.

The first card, in the top left, is the largest, with a pale sage background and a simple hand-drawn border. Its title reads “Balcony Herb Basics” in bold Garden Sans. Below, a brief description: “Learn how to grow basil, mint, thyme, and more in containers, plus how to harvest and dry your herbs.” On its right side is a small, flat-design illustration: a terracotta pot filled with lush green herbs, on a slanted surface, with a soft shadow, done in a minimal yet tactile style. To its right, slightly raised and narrower, is a soft cream card: “Compost & Soil Health” with a line about building healthy soil in small spaces, and a doodle of a compost bin with arrows around it. Beneath these, slightly spanning across, is a wider pale terracotta card: “Pollinator-Friendly Planting” with text about attracting bees and butterflies with native flowers, plus a small illustration of a bee hovering above a daisy. On the lower left, a shorter mustard card: “Seed Starting & Propagation” with text about starting from seeds and cuttings, and a small illustration of labeled seed trays. To its right, overlapping the bottom of the pollinator card, is a sage card: “Container Veggie Patch” describing tomatoes, peppers, and leafy greens in pots. Lastly, tucked into the bottom right corner and slightly rotated is a smaller cream card: “Herbal Tea & Tinctures” about using your harvest in calming teas, with a doodle of a steaming mug with little leaves. Some cards have tiny mock “labels” in their top corners, reading “Beginner-Friendly” or “Hands-On” in tiny uppercase.

Below the themes, transition into the “Upcoming Dates & Schedule” section, with a more linear but still hand-crafted layout. The heading “Upcoming Weekends” appears centered, with a light horizontal divider underneath that looks like a pencil stroke. Under this heading, instead of a table, place a stack of overlapping “event passes” — three or four horizontal cards, each representing an upcoming weekend, arranged vertically with slight stagger so their edges show. Each pass card has a torn ticket shape, with a muted colored edge band and a cream center area. For example, the first card has a moss-green edge and cream center, labeled in bold: “May 18–19, 2025 — Balcony Herb Basics.” Below, in smaller text, “Saturday & Sunday · 9:00 AM – 4:00 PM · Rooftop of The Old Mill.” Under that, three small icons with labels: a leaf icon with “Beginner”, a tiny clock icon with “2 x 3-hour sessions daily”, and a small group icon with “Up to 24 participants.” On its right side, there is a small, outlined button that looks like a stamped label: “View Details”. The second card below is terracotta-edged with the heading “June 15–16, 2025 — Pollinator-Friendly Planting” and similar descriptors. The third card is mustard-edged for “July 20–21, 2025 — Container Veggie Patch.” To the left of the stack, slightly overlapping the first card, is a small vertical “legend” strip explaining the icons: three mini rows with icon plus label, drawn like a marginal note. On the right side of this section, aligned with the top passes, is a narrow vertical note card pinned like a sticky note saying: “Psst… Workshops run rain or shine under covered pergolas. Dress for a bit of dirt!”

Further down, create a “What You’ll Learn, Step by Step” section that feels like an illustrated checklist. The heading is left-aligned: “Dig In: Your Weekend at a Glance” in brush script. Below, a timeline is drawn down the center of the section as a hand-drawn wavy vertical line in light gray, slightly crooked, with small leaf doodles along its path. On the left and right of this line, at alternating heights, are content bubbles representing segments of the weekend. Each bubble is a soft, rounded cream rectangle with a colored border, containing a mini-heading in bold Garden Sans and two lines of text. For example, the first bubble on the left: “Morning Welcome & Garden Tour” with text about meeting the group and exploring the rooftop beds. On the right at the same vertical level: “Soil, Containers & Planning” with text about choosing pots and soil mixes. Further down, left: “Hands-on Planting Session”, right: “Tea Break & Q&A”, left: “DIY Pest Control & Plant Care”, right: “Closing Circle & Take-Home Guide.” Some bubbles slightly overlap each other, creating visual hierarchy, and each has a tiny doodled icon (a sun, a mug, a notebook). This section reinforces the sense of an accessible, guided experience.

Following this, include a section with testimonials from past participants, designed like pinned postcards on a corkboard. The background of this band changes to a soft, cork-textured beige with faint speckles. The header, centered at the top, reads: “Stories from the Garden” in brush script. Below, three testimonial “postcards” are staggered and overlapping. Each card is a slightly different size and color, with hand-drawn borders and pretend “postage stamps” in one corner. For example, the first card, slightly larger and placed to the left, is a faded sage card with a rectangular white area containing a quote in italics. The quote text: “I never thought I could grow anything on my tiny balcony. Now I have basil, chives, and more — and new friends, too.” Beneath the quote, a small line reads “— Maya, first-time gardener” in a smaller sans-serif. The top right of the card has a small stamp illustration: a square with a tiny tomato drawing. The second testimonial card in the center is terracotta with a cream interior, with the quote: “The weekend felt like a mini retreat in the middle of the city. I left with soil under my nails and a calmer mind.” At the bottom: “— Jamal, yoga teacher.” The third, on the right, is pale mustard with the quote: “The facilitators made everything feel so approachable. I finally understand my plants’ needs!” signed “— Lina, plant parent in training.” Some cards overlap, casting shadows, and a couple of small doodles (a string of twine, a clipped paperclip) add to the handmade feel.

Next, introduce a “Meet Your Garden Guides” section in which facilitator profiles are presented as Polaroids arranged in an uneven row. The heading “Meet Your Garden Guides” is centered at the top. Beneath, four portrait Polaroid-style frames are arranged across the width, each slightly rotated differently. Each Polaroid has a photo area at the top and a white label space at the bottom. The photos are semi-realistic illustrations of different people standing in garden settings: one is an older woman with gray hair and overalls holding a watering can; another is a younger person with curly hair holding a tray of seedlings; another is a person with a straw hat clipping herbs; another is a person wearing glasses next to a worm compost bin. Their names are in handwritten script in the white label area: “Marta · Urban Farmer”, “Rae · Herbalist”, “Kenji · Compost Nerd”, “Simone · Balcony Specialist”. Below each Polaroid, a short one-line description in small Garden Sans describes what they’re known for (e.g., “Marta has been transforming city rooftops into edible gardens for over a decade.”). A couple of hand-drawn leaves and stars are scattered around, but the overall layout still respects an invisible baseline.

Under the guides, include a section making it clear what participants should bring and what is provided. Title this section, left-aligned: “What’s Included (and What to Bring)” in brush script. Below, two overlapping vertical lists are drawn as clipped notepads. The left notepad has a pale cream page with a torn top edge and a faint grid pattern, with “We Provide” written in small uppercase at the top. Below, a simple checklist with square boxes and checkmarks lists: “All soil, containers, and tools”, “Organic seeds and seedlings”, “Printed take-home guide”, “Herbal tea, coffee, and snacks”, and “Aprons and gloves to borrow”. The right notepad, partially overlapping and slightly lower, has a pale sage-green page, with “Please Bring” at the top and a list: “Comfortable clothing you don’t mind getting dirty”, “A reusable water bottle”, “A notebook (optional)”, “Sun protection (hat, sunscreen)”. Each notepad has a faint shadow and the impression of being clipped with a small metal binder clip drawn at the top.

Approaching the bottom of the page, place a prominent “Ready to Plant Something New?” call-to-action section. This section uses a soft sage background, framed by a hand-drawn border made of tiny leaves and tendrils. The headline, centered and large in brush script, reads: “Ready to Plant Something New?” Beneath, in smaller text: “Reserve your weekend in the garden before spots fill up.” Below that, centered, is a simple, warm-toned form that appears like a paper sign-up sheet taped onto the colored background. The form’s background is off-white with gentle shadows and has three fields aligned in a single column: “Your Name”, “Email Address”, and a dropdown-style field labeled “Choose Your Weekend”. Each field is represented visually as a softly outlined rectangle with a hand-drawn look. The dropdown shows placeholder text: “Select a date & theme…” in light gray. Beneath the fields, a large button in deep moss green with cream text reads “Confirm My Spot.” Below the button, a tiny note in small font reads: “We’ll confirm your registration and send you all the details within 24 hours.” The form is slightly narrower than the section, leaving breathing room on both sides.

Below this call-to-action, introduce a “Getting Here” section that provides location context in a hand-crafted way. The background returns to the off-white textured paper. On the left half, the heading “How to Find Our Rooftop Garden” in brush script is followed by two short paragraphs describing the venue—“The Old Mill Rooftop, in the heart of the warehouse district”—and transportation tips (nearest metro stop, bike parking). Beneath the text, a small list of accessibility notes mentions elevator access and seating. On the right, instead of a high-tech map, place an illustrated map card, like a sketched neighborhood map. The map is a top-down drawing in muted colors, with simple, slightly wonky lines indicating streets, blocks, and the river. A small icon of a brick building with a leafy rooftop marks “The Old Mill Rooftop,” with a hand-drawn arrow pointing and label text. A tiny compass in the corner, plus small icons indicating “Metro Station”, “Bike Rack”, and “Parking Lot”, are sprinkled around. The map card is taped in place with two pieces of faux washi tape at opposite corners.

Finally, at the very bottom, design a footer that feels like a simple, grounded end to the page. The footer has a darker, desaturated sage-green background and uses cream-colored text for contrast. At the top of the footer, centered, is a small horizontal line drawn like a twig. Above it sits a tiny, simplified version of the logo icon (the trowel and pruning shears) in cream. Below the twig line, three columns of text appear, but their alignment remains slightly organic, not perfectly rigid. The left column, titled “Stay in the Loop” in small uppercase, includes a one-field email subscribe strip (a hand-drawn rectangle representing the email input with placeholder “your@email.com” and a small button labeled “Join the Garden Notes”). The middle column, titled “Quick Links”, lists footer navigation items: “About”, “Workshops”, “Schedule”, “Tickets”, “FAQ”, “Contact.” Each is preceded by a tiny leaf bullet. The right column, titled “Follow Along”, shows small, hand-drawn icon circles (in cream outlines) representing social platforms: a sprouting leaf icon for the gardening blog, a camera icon for photo-sharing, and a chat bubble for a community group—each labeled beneath with text like “Photos & Stories” and “Community Forum.” At the very bottom center, in tiny Garden Sans, is the copyright line: “© 2025 Harvest & Harmony · Grown with care in the city.” There is a final thin, hand-drawn horizontal line just above this text, echoing the organic, hand-crafted style seen throughout the page.

No videos, animations, or data visualizations are present anywhere on the page; all elements feel static, tactile, and analog, like a lovingly assembled scrapbook for an urban gardening event.

instruction: Planning the generation details for the following website:
input:
Design a bold, high-contrast, multi-column portfolio homepage for an adventure travel photographer titled “EDGE LINES,” using a graphic, poster-like aesthetic with rigid grid structure and deliberate asymmetries. The page has a fixed-width central canvas (around 1200–1400px) centered on a pure black background (#000000) that extends to the edges of the viewport. The core canvas background is off-white (#F5F5F5), with crisp, 1px black grid lines forming a subtle multi-column structure and visible margins around all content; every section feels like part of an editorial layout in a graphic design magazine. The primary color palette is black, off-white, a stark electric red (#FF0033), and occasional steel gray (#CCCCCC). The typography is loud and modern: headers use a condensed, uppercase sans-serif (like “Impact” or “Oswald”) in pure black or red; body text and captions use a monospaced font (like “IBM Plex Mono”) in dark gray or black; accent labels and small UI elements sometimes invert with white text on black rectangles. The header is a horizontal strip spanning the width of the central canvas, with a slightly thicker top border (2px black). On the left, aligned vertically within the header, is the bold logotype, all uppercase: “EDGE LINES” in a condensed sans-serif, black letters on a white rectangle outlined in red. Directly beneath that, in small monospaced text, is the tagline “ADVENTURE PHOTOGRAPHY PORTFOLIO” in uppercase, left-aligned, tight letter-spacing. On the right side of the header, a horizontal navigation menu aligns to the baseline of the logo: menu items “WORK”, “JOURNALS”, “ABOUT”, “CONTACT” appear in uppercase, monospaced text, equally spaced, black on off-white; when hovered, they invert to white text on black rectangular pills. At the far right, a thin outlined button reads “BOOK A SHOOT” in uppercase red text with a black border, white fill, and sharp corners; on hover, it flips to solid red with white text.

Below the header, the hero section occupies the full width of the central canvas and about 60% of the initial viewport height, divided into an asymmetric grid: on the left two-thirds, a tall, dominant hero image; on the right third, text and project quick-links stacked vertically. The hero image is a high-contrast, photorealistic black-and-white photograph with harsh lighting and deep shadows: a lone mountaineer viewed from a low angle, standing on a narrow ridge with jagged alpine peaks fading into mist behind; the sky is stark and overexposed, nearly white, creating a sharp silhouette. The mountaineer wears dark gear with a hint of red accent on a strap or carabiner to tie into the page palette. The photo is framed within a white rectangle with a 3px black border that overlaps the top grid line slightly for a deliberate, modern layout break. At the bottom-right corner of the image is a small, horizontal black label with tiny white monospaced text: “CASE STUDY 07 — ALPINE EDGE”. Overlapping the lower-left portion of the hero image is a narrow vertical column, a white strip with a black border that contains a red vertical line and rotated uppercase monospaced text reading “PORTFOLIO FEATURED SERIES” running top to bottom.

On the right side of the hero, within the right third column, is the main title, large and compressed: “CHASE THE EDGE.” in all caps, black, left-aligned, with tight leading, spanning two lines. Below it is a red, 1px horizontal rule. Under that, a short introduction paragraph in smaller monospaced text, medium gray: “I’m Theo Lin, an adventure photographer capturing the geometry of wild places—ridges, horizons, and human silhouettes at the limits of endurance.” The text is left-aligned with generous line spacing. Beneath the paragraph are three stacked quick-link cards, each representing a featured project. Each card is a small, wide rectangle with a white background, a thin black outline, and uses a mini-grid: on the left, a tiny monochrome thumbnail (cropped 3:2) in black and white with red accent; on the right, text. The three card titles are in uppercase monospaced text: “ARCTIC FRACTURES”, “DESERT LINES”, and “NIGHT ASCENT”. Below each title, in smaller gray text, is a two- or three-word description like “Glacial crevasses & frozen patterns”, “Sand, shadow, and horizon geometry”, “Headlamps, stars, and climbing routes”. Each card has a small angled arrow icon (simple black triangular arrow) on the far right, pointing right, indicating a link. The cards are separated by thin black dividing lines aligned with the global grid.

Below the hero, a wide, horizontal strip acts as a section divider: a full-width black band about 80px tall within the central canvas. Centered inside this band is a line of white uppercase monospaced text: “SELECTED FIELDWORK PROJECTS_” with an underscore at the end, giving a terminal-like feel. Two thin red horizontal lines sit above and below this text, inset from the left and right edges to create a graphic rule. Immediately under this band, the main portfolio grid begins, laid out as a three-column grid with consistent gutters, running down most of the page. Each grid item is a project card composed of a large image above text, with all cards aligned to the grid vertically, but the images themselves and text overlays create asymmetry.

In the first row of the portfolio grid, the left column features a tall card that spans two rows: “ARCTIC FRACTURES”. Its image is a stark aerial photograph in high-contrast monochrome with subtle cyan tint: fractured sea ice seen from above, forming sharp polygonal shapes; thin dark cracks form graphic lines against the white ice. The composition leans diagonally from the top-left to the bottom-right, echoing the site’s focus on lines. The card has a 2px black border and a small red square in the top-right corner, like a graphic accent. Below the image, on a solid white background, the project title appears in uppercase condensed sans-serif, black; underneath, in small monospaced gray text: “Greenland ice shelf, 2023 — Aerial patterns and disappearing edges.” To the right of this tall card, the middle and right columns show two standard-height cards. The first is “DESERT LINES”: a panoramic shot of golden sand dunes in monochrome, but with strong contrast where the ridges are nearly black and the flanks are bright, almost white; the shadows create graphic, sweeping curves. The card has a caption: “Erg Chebbi, Morocco — Dunes as pure geometry.” The third card is “RIDGELINE SOLO”: a close-up from behind of a climber walking along a knife-edge ridge; the background mountains are blurred, creating a shallow depth of field; the climber’s jacket has a bold red stripe, the only color element. The caption reads “Dolomites, Italy — Human scale on impossible lines.”

As the grid continues downward, each row mixes card sizes: some spanning multiple columns, others forming smaller clusters. One row might have a wide, two-column card titled “CITY VERTICALS” showing an ultra-high-contrast shot of skyscrapers from below, converging toward a vanishing point, rendered in black and white with neon red reflections in a few windows; and a narrow third-column card titled “SEA CLIFFS” showing rugged, craggy cliffs from a drone’s perspective, waves crashing in stark white against jet-black rock. Another row might feature three equal smaller cards: “ICE CAVES”, “VOLCANIC FOG”, “URBAN NIGHT RUNS”, each with corresponding high-contrast imagery—ice cave interiors with glowing entrance light; a volcano slope with mist swirling in layered gradients; runners captured in long exposure, leaving light trails through a cityscape. Each project card has consistent typographic hierarchy: uppercase title, short descriptive line, and a tiny monospaced label like “SERIES_03” in a black pill with white text, placed near the bottom right corner. On hover (conceptually, visually hinted by design), cards show a thin red outline and the title shifts from black to red.

On the far left of the portfolio grid section, occupying the first column but spanning multiple grid rows, is a vertical sidebar panel that adds structured information and personal branding. It has a white background, boxed with a thick 3px black border, and is divided into stacked blocks. At the top, a small portrait photo in black and white: a side profile of Theo, wearing a beanie, camera strap visible, looking off toward a distant mountain; the background is blurred; lighting is directional, with a strong highlight behind him creating a rim light edge. Beneath the portrait, large uppercase text reads “THEO LIN” in black, followed by a thin horizontal black line. Below that, three lines of monospaced text: “Adventure Photographer”, “Based in Vancouver & on the road”, “Available worldwide”. Beneath this bio block, another block titled “FOCUS AREAS” lists “Expedition documentaries”, “Brand campaigns”, “Editorial series”, each on its own line with a small red square bullet. At the bottom of this sidebar is a vertical label along the left border, rotated 90 degrees: “PORTFOLIO INDEX_” in small uppercase monospaced white text on a black strip, adding a graphic design touch.

Further down the page, below the main portfolio grid, a horizontally split section introduces “JOURNALS” on the left and “GEAR & PROCESS” on the right, separated by a thick vertical line that aligns with the grid. The background returns to off-white with faint gray diagonal lines in the corners, giving a subtle texture. On the left, the “JOURNALS” heading appears in large uppercase condensed black text, with a horizontal red bar underlining it and extending slightly beyond the word. Below it, three journal entry cards stacked vertically, each comprised of a text block with a thin left border in red. The first journal title reads “Field Notes: Storm over the Fjord”, with a short excerpt in gray monospaced text: “We hiked six hours to outrun the weather and instead walked straight into the storm. The best frames came when visibility collapsed to almost nothing.” A small, simple date line appears above titles, like “MAY 14, 2024” in uppercase, light gray. The second journal entry is “Why I Travel with a Single Prime Lens” and the third is “Drawing Lines: Composition Tricks from the Road”; each has similarly short excerpts hinting at both narrative and technical focus. On the right side, the “GEAR & PROCESS” column is more diagrammatic: the heading is uppercase black, but beneath it are three minimalist rows, each with a bold label and a short statement in monospaced font. For example, “CAMERA” in black uppercase on a white box, followed by a description: “Minimal, durable, weather-sealed bodies that can survive ice, sand, and salt.” Then “LENSES” with: “Ultra-wide for scale, 35mm for stories, long glass for compressing chaos into lines.” Finally, “METHOD” with: “Travel light, wake before the first light, leave room for accidents.” Each row is separated by thin horizontal black rules.

Near the bottom of the page is the “ABOUT & CONTACT” band, spanning the full width of the central canvas with a split black-and-white background, diagonally divided from bottom-left (black) to top-right (off-white). On the left black side, white text introduces the photographer: “ABOUT” in uppercase monospaced, followed by a larger sentence in mixed case: “I grew up between grey city grids and blue mountain outlines. Now I chase those edges with a camera.” Below are three lines of slightly smaller monospaced text listing select clients: “SELECTED CLIENTS: Summit Co., Northline Outdoor, Urban Axis Magazine.” On the right off-white side, the “CONTACT” heading is black uppercase. Under it is a simple, high-contrast contact form with only essential fields: “Name”, “Email”, and “Project or destination”. Each field is represented as a thinly outlined rectangular input with black borders and white fill, labels in uppercase above the lines. A larger multiline textarea labeled “Tell me about your next expedition” sits below, also outlined in black. At the bottom of the form, aligned left, is a wide black button with white uppercase text: “SEND INQUIRY”. To the right of the form, a vertical stack of small black icons in simple line style represent social links: a minimalist camera icon for Instagram, a play button inside a rounded square for portfolio reels (though no video embedded, just a link), and a folded map icon for route-sharing platforms; next to each icon, tiny monospaced text labels “INSTAGRAM”, “PRINT SHOP”, “LOCATION LOG”.

Finally, the footer occupies a slender strip at the bottom, full width of the central canvas, with a white background and a black top border. On the left, small gray monospaced text reads “© 2024 EDGE LINES / THEO LIN”. On the right, aligned to the grid, three micro-links in uppercase monospaced text appear: “INFO”, “LICENSES”, “CREDITS”. Between these columns, a thin red horizontal line runs halfway across from the left, stopping short of the right column, a final graphic accent. The entire page maintains strong, high-contrast visual language: sharp black lines, bold red accents, white backgrounds and black backgrounds that flip the hierarchy, a consistent, precise multi-column grid that occasionally breaks with overlapping elements or spanning cards. Every photographic image is described as sharp, high-contrast, predominantly monochrome with carefully placed red accents—jackets, ropes, headlamp glows, reflections—reinforcing the brand’s identity and the theme of “edge lines” in both subject and composition. No videos, animations, charts, or data visualizations appear; the experience is static but visually dynamic, relying solely on still images, typography, and grid-based layout to convey a sense of motion and adventure.

instruction: Planning the generation details for the following website:
input: Design a minimalist, two-column dashboard-style homepage for a digital illustration tutorial subscription platform called “Inkline Studio”. The overall look is extremely clean and calm, with abundant white space, very light gray accents, and a restrained color palette of soft charcoal (#222222), pale warm gray (#F5F5F3), a muted accent teal (#38A3A5), and a desaturated lavender (#A79AD8). The page sits on a full-bleed background of pure white, with the main content centered and constrained to a max-width of approximately 1200px, leaving wide white margins on larger screens. All text uses a single sans-serif font such as “Inter” or “SF Pro Display” with a very light, airy feel and consistent use of medium and semibold weights. The entire layout leans towards a two-column structure: a narrower left sidebar column acting as a navigation and overview rail, and a wider right content column that holds the core dashboard content. Despite this, there are asymmetries in card widths and visual weight that keep the design from feeling rigid or grid-like. At the very top, spanning the full width of the content area, there is a minimalist header bar with a subtle shadow. This header bar has a white background, about 64px tall, with very thin bottom border line in very light gray. On the left side of this header, aligned to the main content margin, is the logo lockup: a small, flat-icon style symbol of a fountain pen nib combined with a tablet pen, drawn in simple geometric shapes and filled in muted teal, followed by the wordmark “Inkline Studio” in black, set in small caps and medium weight, with generous letter spacing. On the right side of the header bar, aligned to the right margin, there is a compact horizontal cluster: a small circular avatar placeholder (a solid light gray circle with a thin darker gray border, suggesting a user photo), next to which is the username text “Hello, Mariah” in charcoal, and to the far right a minimal icon-only bell shape for notifications, outlined in charcoal with no fill. The main body of the page starts immediately beneath the header bar. The layout is divided into two primary vertical sections: a left sidebar column of fixed width (about 260px) and a flexible right content column. The left sidebar begins about 24px below the header and runs from near the top of the viewport to the bottom, but it is designed as floating cards rather than a solid block. At the top of the left column, there is a prominent but minimal dashboard title section: the text “Dashboard” in large, bold font (around 22px), aligned left, with a tiny muted teal square (8x8px) to its left acting as a subtle accent. Below “Dashboard”, in smaller, lighter gray text, is the subtitle “Your daily drawing overview.”. Under this title area, separated by 24px of whitespace, is a vertical navigation card. This navigation card is a rounded rectangle with a very pale gray background (#FAFAF8), subtle inner shadow, and about 16px padding inside. It contains a vertical stack of navigation items, each consisting of a simple icon and text. Each nav item is in a single line with left alignment, about 40px high, with ample vertical padding. The nav items are, from top to bottom: “Overview”, “Courses”, “Sketch Sessions”, “Resources”, and “Account”. The “Overview” item is highlighted as the current selection: the text “Overview” is in charcoal and semibold, and the entire row is filled with a slightly darker pale background (#F0F0EC) with a thin teal accent bar (2px wide) on its left edge.
