The remaining items are plain text in soft medium gray, with icons that are simple outlined glyphs: a grid of 4 squares for “Overview”, a small open book for “Courses”, a pencil over a sheet for “Sketch Sessions”, a bookmark icon for “Resources”, and a gear for “Account”. The icons are all outlined in the same medium gray and are monochrome for a minimal look. Below the navigation card, separated by more whitespace, is a slender progress overview card. This card is also a rounded rectangle with a white background and faint border. Inside it says “Streak” in tiny uppercase letters, light gray, followed by “7-day sketch streak” in heavier charcoal text. Below, instead of a chart, there are seven small circles arranged horizontally, each representing a day; five circles are filled in muted teal, and the last two are outlined only, indicating incomplete days. Beneath these circles is an understated text in light gray reading “Keep sketching today to maintain your streak.”. At the very bottom of the left column, near the lower viewport edge but not touching it, there is a simple “Support & FAQs” link in small, light gray text which turns teal on hover (described as such). The right content column is the visual focus of the page. It starts at the same vertical level as the left “Dashboard” title but occupies the rest of the page width. At its top is a wide hero-style welcome card that does not span the entire width; instead, it sits left-aligned within the right column, with generous right margin, making the layout feel slightly asymmetric. This hero card has a white background, very soft gray border, and 24px rounded corners with subtle drop shadow. The left two-thirds of this card contain text, while the right one-third contains a flat illustrative image. On the left side of the hero card, there is the headline in larger type, “Good evening, Mariah.” in charcoal, with a period, set in semibold around 26px. Below it, a slightly smaller line reads “Ready to draw for 20 minutes?” in medium weight, hugging the headline closely. Under that, in muted gray and smaller text, is a two-line paragraph: “Pick up where you left off in your beginner shading course, or explore a new prompt to stretch your style.”. Below this paragraph there are two horizontally aligned buttons: the primary button on the left is a pill-shaped rectangle filled with muted teal, with the text “Resume Course” in white, center-aligned, bold, with soft, subtle shadow; the secondary button to its right is an outlined pill with no fill, just a thin gray border and the text “New Prompt” in charcoal. The spacing between the buttons is generous, and the whole button group is left-aligned with the text above it. On the right side of this hero card, there is a flat, minimal illustration image inside a slightly inset frame. The image depicts a clean digital drawing workspace with a tablet lying slightly angled on a white desk. The tablet screen shows a simple line-art portrait in progress: abstract, smooth curved lines forming a face with closed eyes and flowing hair. A stylus pen hovers just above the screen. The style of the illustration is flat and vector-like, with solid colors and no gradients: the desk is off-white, the tablet is black with a subtle dark gray border, the lines on the portrait are charcoal, and the stylus has a muted teal tip. The background behind the desk in the image is a soft gradient block of very pale lavender to white, giving a hint of color without visual noise. This image has soft drop shadow on the card background to make it feel tangible. Below the hero card, the right column is divided into two main horizontal bands: a “Today’s Focus” band and a “Library & Recommendations” band. The “Today’s Focus” band is the top one, with its own section label. At the top-left of this band is the label text “Today’s Focus” in uppercase small text, light gray, followed by “Continue where you paused.” in regular charcoal text. This label sits above a row of three horizontal resource cards arranged side-by-side within the right column, with small gaps between them. Each card is a rectangular tile with a white background, rounded corners, and a thin, very faint border. The leftmost card is slightly wider than the other two, adding an asymmetry. Inside each card, the layout is consistent: a small thumbnail image at the top, followed by the title, short description, and a subtle metadata line. In the first card, the thumbnail image is a soft, flat composition showing a hand holding a digital pen over a lightly sketched sphere and cube on a tablet screen, focusing on basic shading. The hand is simple and stylized with minimal detail, using two tones of warm beige. The background of the thumbnail is a very pale teal. Beneath this image is the title “Shading Basics: Light & Form” in medium-weight charcoal text, and under it a single line in soft gray: “Lesson 4 · 18 min remaining”. At the bottom of the card is an understated pill-shaped label in the lower left corner that says “In progress” in small, teal text with a very light teal border. The second card’s thumbnail shows a flat-style arrangement of a tablet with multiple small panels, like a comic page layout, each panel a different pastel color. The title below reads “Clean Linework for Characters”, with the line “Lesson 2 · 5 min remaining”. This card also has a tiny circle icon near the upper right corner indicating it is bookmarked, filled in teal. The third card’s thumbnail shows a stack of color swatches or paints arranged in a row, each block a muted pastel. The title reads “Soft Color Palettes that Pop” with the line “Micro lesson · 7 min” below it. This card has a thin, outlined button in the bottom right corner that says “Start” in small charcoal text. Beneath the “Today’s Focus” band is a subtle horizontal divider line that spans only the right column’s content width, not the full page. Below this divider begins the “Library & Recommendations” band. On the left side of this band, aligned with the left edge of the right column, is the section heading “Your Library” in bold charcoal text; to the right of this heading, in smaller, quieter gray text, is a clickable label “Browse all” with a tiny rightward arrow symbol. This band is structured into a two-column sub-layout inside the right column: on the left is a vertical list of text-only library entries, on the right is an asymmetric stack of two recommendation cards. On the left side, the “Your Library” list appears as a minimalist list of three items, each a horizontal row with slightly darker background on hover (described) but by default just white. Each row contains the course title in charcoal, main font weight, with a small tag beneath or beside it indicating its status. The first row’s title is “Fundamentals of Figure Drawing”. Beneath it, in smaller gray text, it says “Chapter 3 · Gesture & Flow · In progress”. To the far right in that row is a tiny vertical stack of three light gray dots (an options menu icon). The second row’s title is “Procreate for Complete Beginners” with the hint text “Completed · Rewatch segments” and a tiny, faint checkmark icon to the left of the status. The third row’s title is “Dynamic Lighting for Scenes” with the hint “Not started · Add to plan”. Each row has a very subtle bottom border in extremely light gray, visually separating them. On the right side of the “Library & Recommendations” band are two vertically stacked recommendation cards, each slightly offset in width and alignment to create an intentional, minimal asymmetry. The top recommendation card is larger and extends slightly beyond the right edge of the library list, almost touching the right margin of the main content. It has a soft lavender-tinted background (#F4F2FF), rounded corners, and moderate padding. At the top of this card, in small uppercase letters, is the text “Suggested next”, and below it, larger, is “Try a 5-minute warmup sketch”. Under this is a short line of copy reading “Short, simple prompts to loosen your lines before longer sessions.”. In the lower left of the card is a small, flat icon depicting a timer or stopwatch in muted teal with a very simple, circular outline. To the right, in the lower corner of the card, is a small minimal button with text “View warmups” in charcoal on a white background, with rounded corners and a faint border. The second recommendation card beneath it is narrower and shifted slightly to the left, creating a stepped look when seen with the one above it. This card has a pale teal-tinted background (#E9F7F6), and displays a simple message “New this week: Texture experiments.” with subtext “Discover quick drills to build confidence with brushes.”. A small outline icon of a textured paintbrush stroke appears as a diagonal swash near the top-right of the card, rendered in charcoal lines with a speckled dot pattern. Towards the bottom right is a small inline text link “Explore” in teal. Throughout the right column, there is consistent vertical spacing of around 32px between sections and cards, and the margins between text and edges inside cards are generous, making the page breathe visually. The overall aesthetic remains quiet; there are no drop shadows except very subtle ones under major cards like the hero card, and no gradients except inside the illustration content. The typography hierarchy is clear but restrained, with no overly large text. Buttons and controls are uniformly minimal with no heavy borders or loud hover effects described only as subtle color shifts (for instance, the primary teal button darkening slightly on hover, the outlined buttons filling with a very pale tint of teal). There are no charts, graphs, or data-driven visualizations—only text, icons, and static illustrative images. No videos, GIFs, or animations appear anywhere; all imagery is static and flat. The whole page feels like a serene, uncluttered command center for a creative user who wants to quickly see where they are in their digital illustration learning journey and decide calmly what to do next, guided only by text, cards, and simple iconography rather than dynamic or data-heavy visualizations.

instruction: Planning the generation details for the following website:
input: Design a playful, pastel-toned dashboard homepage for a fictional freelance illustrator called “Dot & Doodle Studio,” presented as if it’s both a personal portfolio hub and a whimsical control center. The overall layout follows a loose two-column structure: on desktop-sized screens, the left two-thirds acts as the main content column while the right third functions as a stacked sidebar of panels and widgets. The background of the entire page is a very light, warm cream (#FFF9F2), overlaid with faint, oversized doodles of stars, squiggles, and tiny paint splatters in semi-transparent mint, peach, and lavender, positioned diagonally from top-left to bottom-right so they never overwhelm the content. Everything has soft, rounded corners and generous whitespace. The primary accent color is a bright coral-pink (#FF6B81), paired with a mint-green secondary accent (#78E3C6) and a deep slate-navy (#223049) for primary text. The main font is a friendly geometric sans-serif similar to “Nunito” for headings and navigation, with a rounded, slightly condensed look; body text uses a clean, legible sans-serif similar to “Inter.” Headings are left-aligned, large, and bold; body text is medium gray (#55555A), with line spacing that feels airy. Every panel casts a subtle, soft-drop shadow to make it feel like stacked paper cards floating above the background. At the very top is a full-width sticky header bar about 80px tall with a pristine white background. On the far left of this header is the logo: a small, circular badge with a thick outline in coral-pink, inside which is a flat, cartoon-style mascot: a smiling, round-headed character with a paintbrush for hair, rendered in simple shapes and solid colors. To the right of the logo is the brand name in bold text: “Dot & Doodle Studio” in slate-navy, with “Dot & Doodle” heavier-weight and “Studio” in a lighter weight. Centered in the header is a minimalist navigation with four links spaced evenly, each in small caps: “Dashboard,” “Projects,” “Clients,” and “Inspiration.” When hovered, they display a rounded-pill highlight in a pale mint with coral text. On the right side of the header is a compact user profile cluster: a small circular avatar image of the illustrator in a flat, stylized portrait (side profile, orange bob haircut, large round glasses, smiling), next to two lines of text: “Hello, Dottie!” in bold and below it “Illustrator • Brand doodler” in smaller, muted text. Next to this is a pill-shaped button in coral-pink with white text reading “+ New Project,” slightly taller than the nav links and with a subtle inner highlight suggesting a tactile button. Beneath the header, the main content sits inside a max-width container that is centered on the page with generous margins on left and right. The top of the main content starts with a hero area that spans both columns but visually leans toward the left. On the left side of this hero area is a stacked set of texts and status chips. At the top, large text reads “Good morning, Dottie!” in bold, slate-navy, with a playful, tiny dotted underline in alternating coral and mint dots. Below, a subtitle in medium gray reads: “Here’s your creative cockpit for today’s doodles, deadlines & daydreams.” Under this, a row of three pill-shaped status chips is aligned horizontally with small gaps: the first chip is pale mint with a tiny paintbrush icon on the left and text “3 Projects in progress”; the second chip is pale lavender with a tiny clock icon and text “2 Deadlines this week”; the third chip is pale coral with a sparkly star icon and text “1 New client inquiry.” Each icon is in flat style, single-color line plus fill, consistent with the rest of the site. To the right of this hero text, occupying the top of the right-hand column, is a wide, card-like hero illustration, slightly offset so its left edge overlaps a bit into the left content area, creating asymmetry. This hero illustration sits inside a rounded rectangle card with a soft gradient background from pale peach at the top to pale mint at the bottom. Centered in the illustration is a playful, isometric scene of a miniature studio desk floating in space. The desk is a pale wooden surface with rounded edges, atop which are scattered art tools in a flat, cartoon style: an oversized sketchbook open with loose doodles of characters, a vibrant tablet with a stylus leaning on it, small ink bottles in pastel hues, a mug with a happy face, and some floating sticky notes. Behind the desk, faint line-drawn plants and a hanging lamp shape fade into the background gradient. Small sparkles and stars float around the edges of the illustration, suggesting creativity. Below the hero area, the main two-column layout becomes more distinct. The left two-thirds of the page is divided into vertically stacked content sections: a “Today’s Tasks” card, a “Current Projects” list, and a “Recent Sketches” preview grid. The right column stacks a “Client Inbox” card, a “Color Palette of the Day” card, and a “Moodboard Tiles” gallery, with each card maintaining the playful aesthetic. In the left column, the first main card is titled “Today’s Tasks” in bold slate-navy text at the top-left. The card has a white background and rounded corners, with a faint, dotted border in very light gray, like perforated paper. To the right of the title, at the top-right of the card, is a small, pill-shaped toggle with two labels: “Today” and “This week.” The “Today” side is filled in coral with white text, while “This week” is outlined only. Below the header of this card is a vertical list of four to-do items, each represented as a rounded rectangular row with a soft colored accent bar on the left. Each row includes a chunky, circular checkbox on the far left (empty or filled with a checkmark), a task title, a smaller line of detail text, and a tiny colored label on the right indicating the client. The first row’s accent bar is a thin coral strip; the checkbox is half-filled with a stylized check, implying in-progress. The task title reads “Refine mascot sketches for ‘Sunny Sips Cafe’” in medium-bold text, with a small gray subtext line: “Due today • 3 reference images attached.” On the right side of this row is a small rounded label in pale yellow reading “Sunny Sips.” The second row’s accent bar is mint; its checkbox is empty. The title reads “Storyboard frames for indie game ‘Starloop’ trailer,” with subtext: “Due in 2 days • 12/24 frames done.” Its client label is a pale lavender pill reading “Starloop Studio.” The third row has a lavender accent bar; its checkbox is fully checked and filled with coral. The title: “Upload final icons for ‘Pixel Paws’ app,” subtext: “Completed • Ready for client review,” and a pill label in pale blue reading “Pixel Paws.” The fourth row is a pale blue accent, with title: “Draft proposal layout for art zine collab,” subtext: “Due Friday • Need color palette ideas,” and a pill label reading “Zine Collective” in pale pink. Between each row is a faint horizontal divider line. Underneath “Today’s Tasks” and separated by a gentle 24px gap is the “Current Projects” card. This card title reads “Current Projects” with a small pastel folder icon to its left. Below the title is a horizontally scrollable row (for wide screens) of three medium-sized project cards, each with a slightly different pastel background and minimal illustration that symbolizes the project. Each project card is a vertical rounded rectangle with a colored header band, a thumbnail illustration, and text details. The first card has a pale yellow background. In its top-left is a small heading: “Sunny Sips Cafe” in a warm orange. Beneath, in slightly smaller text, it reads “Brand characters & window illustrations.” The thumbnail illustration occupies the central portion and shows three cheerful coffee cups with faces, each a different pastel color, arranged in a semi-circle. They sit on a stylized café counter with a tiny plant pot, with a simple flat-design backdrop of a café awning above them. At the bottom of the card is a coral progress pill that says “Phase 2: Sketch Refinement” with a tiny dot indicator next to “2/4 milestones.” The second card has a pale mint background with the title “Starloop” in teal. Subtext reads “Launch animations & Twitch emotes.” The thumbnail is a flat illustration of a small rocket shaped like a pencil, blasting off amidst comical star-shaped characters, with a purple space background speckled with dots. At the bottom is a lavender pill reading “Phase 1: Concepting • 1/3 milestones.” The third card has a faded lavender background with the title “Pixel Paws” in blue, and subtext “App icons & onboarding scenes.” The thumbnail illustration shows four icon tiles, each depicting a different cartoon cat doing something silly (jumping, sleeping, pawing at yarn) in a clean flat style, each tile in a different pastel. At the bottom is a mint pill that reads “Phase 3: Final Polish • 3/3 milestones.” Immediately below “Current Projects” is the “Recent Sketches” card. This card has the title “Recent Sketches” and a small text link aligned right that says “View full gallery →” in coral. The interior of the card is a three-column grid of small square thumbnails with slightly uneven spacing to make it feel handmade, like a corkboard collage. Each thumbnail is a different illustration sample: one shows a pencil-drawn character on textured, off-white paper, scanned and slightly tinted, with soft shadows suggesting the paper’s curvature; another is a digital sketch of a crowded city street of cartoon animals in simple shapes, all outlined in midnight blue; a third shows color swatches and facial expression studies. Each thumbnail has rounded corners and when hovered, a faint overlay appears with the text “Open sketch” centered in white. The right column begins under the hero illustration with the “Client Inbox” card near the top. This card’s header reads “Client Inbox” with a small, friendly envelope icon, its flap shaped like a smiling mouth. On the top-right of the card header is a small pill showing “3 new” in coral text on a pale pink background. Inside the card are three preview items stacked vertically. Each item row has on the left a small circular avatar with a flat, playful portrait of a person or logo (e.g., a café cup, a game controller icon), and on the right the client or project name, a snippet of the latest message, and a tiny colored dot indicating status. For example, the first row has an avatar of a coffee cup, the title “Sunny Sips Cafe,” and below that in lighter gray text, the message snippet: “We’re obsessed with the latest latte doodles—can we see a version with a cat barista?” On the far right of the row is a tiny mint dot with a tooltip label “On track.” The second row’s avatar is a small pixelated game controller icon for “Starloop Studio,” snippet: “Just confirming the star palette you suggested—we love the neon purples!”, and a coral dot labeled “Awaiting reply.” The third row has a stylized paw logo for “Pixel Paws,” snippet: “The onboarding illustration is purr-fect! One small tweak to the background color…,” and a pale gray dot labeled “Reviewed.” Each row has soft separation lines. Below the “Client Inbox” card is a narrower card titled “Color Palette of the Day.” This card has a white interior but its top border is a thick band in a gradient from coral to lavender. Beneath the title is a short descriptive sentence in small text: “Today’s vibe: ‘Soft Morning Café’ — gentle, creamy hues with playful pops.” Below this, five color swatches are arranged horizontally, each in a rounded square tile with the hex code underneath in tiny monospaced text. From left to right: a buttery cream (#FFF3C7), a blush pink (#FFB5C2), a muted coral (#FF7A6B), a deep teal (#1E6F6F), and a muted plum (#6B456B). The swatches appear slightly lifted with a delicate drop shadow, and faint doodles of leaves and little circles float behind them, extremely faded. Below this is a small link: “Save palette to library” in coral. Further down in the right column is a larger, more visually playful card titled “Moodboard Tiles,” which feels like a mini board of inspiration. The card background is off-white, but inside it has irregularly arranged polaroid-like tiles overlapping each other slightly, each representing an inspiration image. Each tile has a white border, a drop shadow, and a tiny piece of “washi tape” illustrated at one corner in pastel patterns (stripes, dots, checks). One tile shows a sunlit café interior with arched windows, pastel chairs, and a leafy plant, depicted in a painterly, soft, semi-realistic illustration style, with warm light streaks and gentle gradients. Another tile shows a close-up of a hand holding colored pencils and markers, stylized with crisp outlines and flat, bold colors. Another shows a cluttered artist’s desk from a top-down perspective, filled with sketchbooks, sticky notes, and a laptop. The tiles overlap just enough to create depth without hiding too much of each image. At the bottom-right of the card is a small coral button with rounded edges labeled “Shuffle inspiration.” Toward the page’s lower region, still within the main container, there is a full-width section that breaks the two-column layout slightly, overlapping both columns. This section is a wide, ribbon-like call-to-action card with a pale mint background and subtle, hand-drawn starbursts peeking from its edges. On the left side of this ribbon is a bold heading reading “Need a break? Try a doodle prompt!” and below it smaller text: “Step away from client work and play with a tiny challenge.” On the right side is a rounded rectangle mini-panel with an off-white background and a drop shadow that holds a random prompt example in scripted type: “Draw your favorite snack as a tiny superhero.” Beneath that prompt is a secondary button in coral reading “Generate another prompt.” The page footer is simple, quiet, and anchored to the bottom of the content, not sticky. It spans full width, with a slightly darker cream background (#F7EBDD). Left-aligned text in small, muted gray reads “© 2025 Dot & Doodle Studio. Made with pencils & pixels.” To the right are two small text links: “Portfolio site →” and “Contact & commissions,” each underlined with a dashed, playful underline that becomes solid on hover. Throughout the page, hovering over cards or buttons slightly lifts them and deepens their shadows, but there are no animations or moving elements—only static, state-based visuals. All icons and illustrations are consistent in a flat, friendly, vector style with soft curves, thick outlines in a slightly darker hue than their fill colors, and no complex gradients except where specified (such as the hero illustration background and the “Color Palette of the Day” top border). The overall feeling should be of a cheerful, approachable creative dashboard that balances a clear two-column structure with subtle asymmetries in overlapping cards and varied card sizes, making the interface feel playful yet organized, without any video, animation, or chart-based visualization.

instruction: Planning the generation details for the following website:
input:
Design a high-contrast, graphic landing page for an independent artist portfolio homepage titled “NOIR LINES — Portfolio of Jax Mercer,” with a bold, mostly two-column layout that plays with asymmetry and strong blocks of black and white. The entire page uses a stark black-and-white palette with occasional sharp accent colors (electric cyan and neon red) used sparingly for emphasis. The overall aesthetic is graphic and print-like, reminiscent of risograph posters and brutalist web design: oversized typography, thick lines, hard edges, and confident whitespace. The body font is a clean, geometric sans-serif (like “Space Grotesk”), while headings use an ultra-bold, slightly condensed grotesque (all caps with tight tracking). The background is predominantly off-white (#f5f5f5), with large black sections and vertical bands creating contrast as the user scrolls. The page is centered within a maximum width, with generous margins on left and right, and a simplified two-column grid that shifts between 60/40 and 40/60 proportions for interest.

At the very top, a thin horizontal strip spans the full width with a pure black background and a small line of text centered in small, uppercase white letters: “JAX MERCER — INDEPENDENT ILLUSTRATOR & GRAPHIC ARTIST — AVAILABLE WORLDWIDE”. Below this strip, the main header section sits above the fold, structured as two asymmetrical columns. The left column is about 60% of the width with a clean off-white background, while the right column is narrower and fully black, stretching to the top edge of the browser and down slightly past the hero section, creating a black vertical bar on the right side of the screen.

Within the left hero column, at the top left, the logo is displayed: a simple outlined square icon with a diagonal line dividing it from top-left to bottom-right, rendered in solid black on the off-white background. To the right of the logo, aligned horizontally across the top, is a minimalist navigation menu with each item in uppercase black text and spaced out with generous letter spacing: “WORK”, “ABOUT”, “PROCESS”, “CONTACT”. Hover states should be described visually: when a nav item is hovered, a thin neon red underline appears, perfectly straight and aligned, while the text remains black. Below the nav and logo, occupying most of the left side, is the primary hero text. The main heading reads in very large, all-caps black type, stacking across three lines with a left alignment: “GRAPHIC STORIES. / BOLD LINES. / NOISE & SILENCE.” The slash characters are part of the text, and each line is separated by tight vertical spacing, creating a poster-like feel. Beneath this heading, a short subheading in smaller, regular-weight sans-serif text reads: “I’m Jax Mercer, an independent illustrator and graphic artist turning ideas into sharp, high-contrast visuals for music, print, and brands that dare to be loud.” This subheading is left-aligned and wraps within the column, with a comfortable line height. Below the subheading, two horizontally aligned buttons sit side by side with slight space between them. The primary button is a rectangular solid black button with white uppercase text: “VIEW PORTFOLIO”. The secondary button is an outlined button: a white background, a 2px black border, and black uppercase text: “BOOK A PROJECT”. On hover, describe that the primary button inverts (with white background and black text) while the secondary button’s background shifts to electric cyan with black text, maintaining the black border.

The right hero column on the black vertical bar contains a large, graphic hero image framed within white margins top and bottom, centered in the column. The image is a static, stylized illustration representing Jax’s work: a flat, high-contrast, poster-like scene in black, white, and electric cyan. The composition shows a close-up of a person’s face in three-quarter view, rendered in simplified, angular shapes. The background is solid black; the face is mainly white with thick black lines defining the nose, lips, and eye, while a streak of electric cyan cuts diagonally across, passing over one eye like a mask. The person wears over-ear headphones drawn in pure white outlines with a cyan accent ring, and the headphone cable forms a sweeping curve that disappears off the bottom edge of the illustration. The lighting is stark: no gradients, only hard-edged shapes. The illustration is framed by a thin white border, giving the impression of a printed poster pinned against the black column.

As the user scrolls slightly, they encounter the next section, “Selected Work,” which continues the two-column feel but flips the dominance: the left column is now narrow and black, while the right column is wide and off-white. Between the hero and this section is a horizontal divider: a 2px black line stretching about 75% of the width, aligned slightly to the left so it doesn’t feel perfectly centered. On the left, against the black background, in small uppercase white text, a label sits at the top: “SECTION 01 — SELECTED WORK”. Beneath that, vertically aligned in the left column’s center, is a rotated text element reading “PORTFOLIO” in white, rotated 90 degrees clockwise so it runs bottom-to-top. This rotated word hugs the inner edge where the black column meets the off-white main content, like a vertical spine.

The right “Selected Work” column contains a grid of three featured projects arranged in a vertical stack with consistent spacing. Each project card is a horizontally split block: an image thumbnail on the left and text on the right, but with alternating alignment to create rhythm (project 1 image left/text right, project 2 image right/text left, project 3 image left/text right). The first project card has a light gray (#e9e9e9) background with a thin black border and slightly rounded corners (3px radius). The image is a static, graphic poster preview: a flat, high-contrast composition of a concert poster. It shows a stylized electric guitar in black silhouette at a slight angle, with cyan lightning bolts radiating from it against a white background, and large black block letters at the top reading “NIGHT STATIC TOUR” (this text is part of the image, not HTML). The project text to the right is left-aligned: a project title in bold uppercase black letters: “NIGHT STATIC — TOUR POSTER SERIES”, a short description in regular weight: “Poster and merch illustration for an experimental rock band, exploring tension between noise and negative space.” Below this, in small uppercase letters, a category label appears in neon red: “MUSIC • PRINT”. Underneath the description sits a small text link in black uppercase: “VIEW CASE STUDY →”, with the arrow included in the text; on hover, describe that a thin black outline forms around the entire card, giving it a subtle emphasized state. The second project card reverses: the text block appears on the left, with a clean white background, while the image sits on the right, slightly overlapping the edge of the card with a drop shadow (soft, black at low opacity) giving it a layered effect. This image shows an album cover artwork: a minimal, black-and-white square with a thick black circle in the center, inside of which a jagged electric cyan waveform cuts across. The album title “ECHO ROOMS” is set in small black monospaced text below the circle in the image. The text content to the left reads a bold title: “ECHO ROOMS — ALBUM ART”, followed by: “Vinyl and digital cover art series exploring isolation, distortion, and repetition.” The category tag is cyan: “MUSIC • DIGITAL”. The third project card returns to image-left/text-right; its image is a mockup of a zine cover laid flat against a white background, rendered in flat illustration style. The zine cover is black with a white rectangle in the middle, containing chaotic hand-drawn lines in neon red and cyan, and the title “LINES BETWEEN” in narrow white uppercase type at the top of the cover. The text on the right reads: “LINES BETWEEN — ART ZINE”, with a description: “Self-published riso-inspired zine of line-based illustrations and micro-stories.” The category label here is black text on a small cyan pill-shaped background: “PERSONAL • PRINT”.

Below the “Selected Work” section is a full-width band titled “Process,” designed with a black background and white text, split into two columns but more balanced (50/50). A white heading sits at the top-left of the black band in large uppercase text: “HOW I WORK”. To its right, at the same vertical level but right-aligned, is a smaller label in electric cyan uppercase: “SECTION 02 — PROCESS”. A subtle pattern appears in the black background: a faint, diagonal hatching texture created from thin, semi-transparent dark gray lines, giving the panel a tactile, printed feel without distracting from the text. In the left column, there is a numbered list of three steps presented as stacked blocks separated by thin white horizontal lines. Each step starts with a large, outlined number (e.g., “01”, “02”, “03”) in an outlined font, with only the stroke visible and the interior transparent, giving a hollow appearance. To the right of each number, in bold uppercase white text, a label appears: “DISCOVERY”, “DESIGN”, and “DELIVERY”. Beneath each label is a short explanatory sentence in smaller white mixed-case text, like “We look at your project, references, and constraints to define a clear visual direction.” for step one, “I sketch, iterate, and refine graphic directions until the concept clicks — then push it into final artwork.” for step two, and “Final assets arrive as clean, ready-to-use files that feel sharp in print and alive on screens.” for step three. The right column in this section features a static, abstract illustration representing the artist’s process: a flat, geometric composition made of overlapping black and white rectangles and a single cyan circle, all plotted on a faint off-white grid of thin lines. The cyan circle sits slightly off-center, cutting across the boundary between a black rectangle and a white rectangle, suggesting a node connecting different phases. There are no gradients, just crisp shapes with hard edges and clean intersections, all top-down view.

Further down, the “About” section reverts to an off-white background and a more conventional 40/60 two-column layout, but with an asymmetrical shape behind the text. The title “ABOUT JAX” appears in all-caps black text left-aligned at the top of the left column. Beneath it is a descriptive paragraph in mixed-case black text: “I’m an independent illustrator and graphic artist based between print and pixels. My work pulls from punk flyers, jazz sleeves, and the clean tension of Swiss typography. I collaborate with musicians, small brands, publishers, and anyone who loves strong visuals and stronger ideas.” Below the paragraph is a small block of “Credentials” in a dense but clean layout: a bold uppercase subheading “SELECTED CLIENTS” followed by a line with names in uppercase separated by centered dots: “ECHO ROOM RECORDS · STATIC PRESS · NEON HARBOR · CITYLINE STUDIO”. Under that, another uppercase subheading reads “FOCUS AREAS” followed by “Album art · Poster design · Visual identities · Editorial illustration” in smaller sentence case. The left column background remains off-white, but a large, irregular black shape sits behind the text, hugging the top-right corner of the left column and fading before the text reaches the bottom, like an abstract brush stroke or ink spill with sharply cut vector edges. The white text of the headings sits on top of this shape where they overlap, ensuring legibility.

The right column of the “About” section contains a static portrait illustration of Jax, drawn in the same high-contrast, graphic style as the hero image. The portrait is a bust view, facing slightly toward the center of the page, set against a pure white background. The figure’s face is defined by bold black areas and white negative space, with stylized, angular features and a calm, focused expression. The hair is depicted as a solid black shape with a subtle jagged silhouette, and the figure wears a simple, solid black t-shirt. A neon red outline traces the right edge of the portrait silhouette, creating a halo-like effect that separates the black from the white background. Over the upper-left portion of the portrait, a cyan geometric overlay, shaped like a semi-transparent rectangle, intersects the face slightly, reinforcing the layered, graphic aesthetic. The portrait is framed by a thin black border with equal padding on all sides, giving it the appearance of an art print.

Below “About,” a thin horizontal divider, composed of a dashed black line, extends across only the central 60% of the page width, centered, retaining white space on either side. This leads into a “Services” section presented in a two-column layout that behaves more like stacked blocks at narrower widths. The background returns to pure white for a sharper contrast. The heading at the top left reads “WHAT I DO” in large uppercase black text, with a small cyan rectangle behind the words “WHAT I” as a highlight bar, slightly misaligned vertically for a deliberate, screen-printed look. Underneath, a short line in small uppercase gray text reads: “ILLUSTRATION & GRAPHIC DESIGN SERVICES”. Two columns beneath this heading list individual service cards, each a simple outlined box with black borders, slightly rounded corners, and plenty of padding. Each card has a service title in bold uppercase black text, such as “ALBUM & SINGLE ART”, “POSTERS & FLYERS”, “BRAND & LOGO MARKS”, and “EDITORIAL ILLUSTRATION”. Below each title, a concise description in sentence case explains the offering, for example: “Singular covers, cohesive album systems, and visual worlds around your sound.” Each card includes a small tag at the bottom left: a tiny black circle bullet followed by cyan uppercase text: “STARTS AT FLAT RATE”. At the bottom right of each card, in small uppercase gray, the text “DETAILS ON REQUEST” appears. The cards are arranged in two rows and two columns, with even spacing and aligned edges.

Near the bottom of the page, a strong “Contact” call-to-action section spans the full width with a stark black background. At the top center of this section, in small uppercase text in cyan, it reads: “SECTION 03 — CONTACT”. Centered below is the main CTA headline in oversized white uppercase text: “LET’S BUILD SOMETHING LOUD.” Beneath this, a slightly smaller line in white reads: “For collaborations, commissions, or questions, drop a line and I’ll reply within 2–3 days.” The lower portion of this section is divided into a two-column form layout, aligned within a narrower central container so there’s ample black space on the sides. The left column features a minimalist contact form on the black background, with each field labeled in uppercase white text and black input boxes outlined in white. The labels read “NAME”, “EMAIL”, and “PROJECT DETAILS”. The text input boxes and textarea have no fill (transparent) with only 1px white borders and white placeholder text that says “Type here…” in sentence case. The “PROJECT DETAILS” field is a taller box for longer messages. Below the fields is a solid white rectangular button with black uppercase text: “SEND MESSAGE”. On hover, envision the button background becoming electric cyan with black text. The right column lists alternate contact methods and social links in vertical format. A small subheading in white uppercase text reads: “DIRECT EMAIL”, followed by a clickable email address in cyan, underlined: “hello@noirl ines.studio” (with no space in the actual URL: “hello@noirlines.studio”). Beneath that, another subheading reads “ELSEWHERE”, followed by a vertical list of social platforms in uppercase white text (“INSTAGRAM”, “BEHANCE”, “DRIBBBLE”), each with a tiny cyan square to the left as an icon. No icons are graphic logos, just the squares and text.

At the very bottom, the footer occupies a slim band on an off-white background. A thin solid black line defines its top edge. Within the footer, on the left, small uppercase gray text reads: “© 2025 JAX MERCER. ALL RIGHTS RESERVED.” On the right, aligned to the footer’s edge, are two subtle text links in small uppercase black font: “PRIVACY” and “CREDITS”, separated by a centered dot. As an additional graphic detail, just above the footer and slightly overlapping it, a small horizontal row of three tiny symbols appears, aligned to the center of the page: a hollow square, a hollow circle, and a hollow triangle, all outlined in black and equally spaced, referencing the geometric motifs seen throughout the site. The entire page should feel cohesive, sharp, and uncompromisingly high-contrast, with every section clearly demarcated by changes in background, bold typographic hierarchy, and strong graphic elements, while the underlying structure remains a mostly two-column layout with playful asymmetry and no motion, video, or data visualizations anywhere on the page.

instruction: Planning the generation details for the following website:
input:
Design a single, scrolling event page with a refined editorial aesthetic for a one-day creative conference titled “Ink & Impact: A Day of Storytelling for Social Change.” The overall layout tends toward a single column, with subtle asymmetries created by image alignment, pull quotes, and varied text widths. The page background is a soft, off-white paper tone (#F8F4EF) with a faint, almost imperceptible fibrous paper texture, giving it a print-magazine feel. The primary typography is a high-contrast serif for headings, such as “Playfair Display” or similar, with elegant hairlines and pronounced thick strokes, and a neutral grotesk sans-serif for body text, such as “Inter” or “Helvetica Neue.” All body text is a very dark charcoal (#1A1A1A), avoiding pure black. Margins are generous, with content centered in a narrow column approximately 760px wide on large screens, while full-width background color blocks occasionally stretch edge to edge to create editorial rhythm.

At the top, a clean, minimal navigation bar sits over a subtle horizontal hairline. The nav is centered at the top of the viewport, with ample white space above and below. The background is the same paper tone as the body, but with a slightly darker, 1px bottom border line in a warm gray (#D4CFC5). On the left side of the nav, in small caps serif, appears the event name: “Ink & Impact.” The text “Ink & Impact” is in a rich black ink color (#111111), letterspaced slightly, and aligned left within the main column. On the right, aligned to the same column, there is a horizontal list of simple text links in the sans-serif font, all in uppercase and small font size: “About”, “Speakers”, “Schedule”, “Workshops”, “Venue”, “Register”. The links are spaced with generous letter-spacing. Hover states are described as changing the text color from charcoal to a muted rust (#B5523B) and adding a thin underline reminiscent of underlined print headlines. Above the nav, in the top-left corner of the viewport but slightly outside the main column and broken into the margin, is a small, circular emblem: a thin black circle with a tiny ink droplet icon in the center, drawn with a single line in minimalist, line-art style.

Below the nav is the hero section, which occupies roughly the first screen height, feeling like the cover of a magazine. The hero uses a two-layer composition: the primary content is centered text, but behind it, aligned slightly right and bleeding off the screen, is a large, vertically oriented photographic image with a strong editorial feel. The main hero background remains the paper tone, but the right half of the hero is overlapped by this tall, narrow image, which is approximately 40% of the screen width and extends from just under the nav to slightly below the fold. The image is a soft, muted, realistic photograph of a long wooden table in a sunlit studio space, shot from a slightly elevated perspective. On the table lie scattered open notebooks, fountain pens, a laptop partially visible, and printed zines, with hands of diverse people reaching in—one hand holds a pen poised above a notebook, another holds a mug of coffee. The color palette is warm, with caramel-colored wood, white and cream paper, and small accents of deep blue ink and rust-colored ceramic cup. The depth of field is shallow, with the central notebooks and hands in sharp focus and the background slightly blurred. Light comes from a large unseen window to the left, casting soft, diffuse shadows and gentle highlights on the paper and pen nibs. The style is photorealistic, with fine detail visible in paper fibers, pen reflections, and skin tones.

Overlapping both the plain background and partially intruding on the image, in the center-left of the hero, is the main title text in large serif, left-aligned but centered within the main column. The title reads: “Ink & Impact: A Day of Storytelling for Social Change.” The words “Ink & Impact” are on one line in very large type, while “A Day of Storytelling for Social Change” sits beneath in smaller yet still bold serif. The type is black ink (#111111), with a slight tracking increase to give an airy, magazine headline feel. Beneath the title, there is a slender horizontal rule about 40px wide, in the rust accent color (#B5523B), left-aligned with the title. Below this rule, in sans-serif body type, is a short paragraph: “Join writers, designers, activists, and community organizers for a one-day gathering exploring how narrative, design, and digital media can drive real-world change.” This paragraph is single-column, about 60 characters wide, and the lines are well-spaced for readability. Under this copy is a line with the event details in small caps: “October 12, 2025 • Brooklyn, New York • Hybrid Event”. Each bullet is separated by a middle dot, the text in a warmer gray.

Below the event details, two horizontally arranged buttons are left-aligned but still within the main column. The primary button reads “Register Now” in uppercase sans-serif. It is a pill-shaped button with slightly rounded edges, a solid rust background (#B5523B), and white text. The button has a soft drop shadow that mimics the effect of a physical label resting on paper. The hover state lightens the rust slightly. Next to it, with 16px space between, is a secondary ghost button reading “View Full Program” in uppercase, charcoal text with a thin charcoal border and transparent background that turns to a very light tint of rust on hover. Slightly above and to the right of these buttons, overlapping the hero image, is a vertical, rotated word “EVENT” in small caps serif, in pale gray, acting as a subtle decorative element like a side label from an editorial spread.

As the user scrolls, the hero smoothly transitions into the “About” section, which feels like a long-form magazine article. The section is centered and keeps the single-column layout, with body text in generous line height. The section heading “About the Gathering” appears in small serif uppercase above the main headline, with a small dot on either side: “• ABOUT THE GATHERING •”. Below this, the main subsection title “Stories that move people to action.” is in italic serif, slightly smaller than the hero headline, and aligned left. Under it are two paragraphs of text describing the event’s purpose. The first paragraph reads: “Ink & Impact brings together storytellers, strategists, and changemakers who believe that words — when crafted with care — can shift perspectives, build empathy, and inspire collective action.” The second paragraph reads: “Throughout the day, you’ll move between keynotes, intimate workshops, and collaborative labs, leaving with tangible skills, new collaborators, and a renewed sense of what’s possible when narrative meets purpose.” On the left margin of this section, from about halfway down the paragraphs, there is a vertical pull quote that intrudes slightly into the negative space outside the main column, creating subtle asymmetry. The pull quote reads, in oversized serif italics and light gray (#A59C90): ““Stories don’t just reflect the world — they help shape it.”” Under the quote, in small caps, is “Keynote Speaker, Maya Ortiz”. The quote text is rotated slightly by a few degrees to feel like something pasted from a print layout.

Below the “About” text, the next section is “Featured Speakers”. The year indicator “2025 SPEAKERS” appears as tiny caps above a thin horizontal rule extending across the column width. The main section title “Voices at the Frontlines of Change” appears in bold serif, center-aligned this time to create variation. Under this, a short intro paragraph is center-aligned: “From investigative journalists to grassroots organizers, our speakers span disciplines but share a commitment to storytelling as a tool for justice.” Instead of a grid, the speakers are presented as vertical, editorial profiles to maintain the single-column feel, each separated by generous vertical whitespace and a faint horizontal hairline. Each speaker profile uses a two-column asymmetry within the main column: on the left, a square portrait photograph about 140px wide; on the right, their name and bio.

Each portrait is a photorealistic, softly lit head-and-shoulders image against a neutral background, with subtle differences for character. For example, the first speaker image shows a mid-30s Latina woman with curly dark hair pulled back loosely, wearing a simple black turtleneck and a small silver hoop earring. She looks slightly off-camera with a thoughtful expression. The background is a very soft gradient from warm gray to off-white, with natural, even lighting and gentle shadows on her cheek. The style is modern editorial photography, not overly retouched. Under her image, on the right, the text reads: “Maya Ortiz — Investigative Journalist & Narrative Strategist.” Her name is in bold serif and the descriptor in small caps sans-serif. The bio beneath, in regular sans-serif, reads: “Maya’s reporting on housing justice and labor rights has appeared in publications across the globe. At Ink & Impact, she’ll open the day with a keynote on how journalists can partner with communities to co-create more honest narratives.” Similar treatments follow for two more speakers: “Ravi Kumar — Creative Director, People’s Studio.” with a portrait of a South Asian man in his early 40s, beard, round glasses, and a denim shirt, seated with arms loosely crossed, warm directional lighting from the right; and “Leah Chen — Community Organizer & Digital Campaigner.” with a portrait of an East Asian nonbinary person in their 20s, short hair dyed teal at the ends, wearing a crisp white shirt, framed in front of a blurred urban mural background. Each bio paragraph is roughly 3–4 lines. To add a print-like touch, to the left of each speaker name is a small, minimalist icon in thin black lines (a fountain pen for Maya, a stack of posters for Ravi, a megaphone for Leah), placed very small and subtle.

After the speakers, the next section titled “The Day at a Glance” outlines the schedule. The section heading appears in serif, left-aligned, with “PROGRAM” in tiny uppercase above it. Under the heading “The Day at a Glance” is a short descriptive line in smaller italic serif: “Designed like a well-paced story: beginning with intention, building momentum, and closing with reflection.” Instead of a complex timetable, a vertical timeline runs down the center of the column, maintaining the one-column rhythm. A thin vertical line in a light rust tone (#D8A18C) extends from the top to bottom of this section, with small circular nodes marking each time block. To the left of the line, in small caps, are the times: “9:00 AM”, “10:30 AM”, “12:00 PM”, “1:30 PM”, “3:00 PM”, “5:00 PM”. To the right, in larger serif, are the session titles and in sans-serif, short descriptions. For example, at 9:00 AM: “Opening Keynote: Rewriting the Stories We Inherit” with body text: “Maya Ortiz explores what it means to report, write, and organize in ways that center the people most affected.” Another session at 1:30 PM: “Workshop: Mapping Your Movement’s Narrative” with body text: “In this participatory session, you’ll chart your campaign’s story arc and leave with a messaging framework you can share with your team.” The vertical line and nodes are delicate, giving a light, unobtrusive sense of structure; the text still dominates like a print program.

Next comes an editorial-style “Workshops & Labs” section, introduced by a full-width background block in a slightly darker, warm parchment color (#EFE3D5) that stretches edge-to-edge, creating a visual break. Within this block, the content remains within the central column. At the top left of the block, in small serif uppercase, it says “WORKSHOPS & LABS”. The main heading “Roll up your sleeves.” appears in bold serif, slightly larger than body text but smaller than major headlines. Below, an introductory paragraph spans the full column, describing the hands-on sessions: “Beyond lectures and panels, Ink & Impact is built around intimate sessions where you can ask questions, experiment with new tools, and receive thoughtful feedback from peers and mentors.” Below this paragraph, three workshop descriptions are stacked vertically, each with an understated thumbnail image aligned alternately left and right to create a gentle zigzag in the otherwise single-column layout.

Each workshop thumbnail is a rectangular photo with rounded corners, about 260px wide. The first image, aligned left, shows close-up hands of diverse skin tones arranging printed photos and text fragments on a table; the perspective is from above, and the color palette has warm browns, whites, and pops of orange highlighting sticky notes. The lighting is soft and diffuse, with crisp detail on fingertips and paper edges. To the right of this image, the workshop title is in bold serif: “Lab: Designing Campaign Narratives”. Below, in sans-serif: “Co-led by Ravi Kumar and Leah Chen, this lab walks you through turning abstract values into concrete storytelling campaigns — from slogans to social posts.” The second workshop reverses: text on the left, image on the right. Its image depicts a small group seated in a circle in a studio, chairs mismatched but cohesive, with large sticky notes on the wall behind them bearing handwritten ideas, shot at a slight angle giving depth. The third returns to image left, text right. Each workshop’s text includes a small tag line in small caps gray above the title, such as “INTIMATE SESSION”, “SKILL-BUILDING”, or “PEER REVIEW”.

Following this, there is a short “Who This Is For” section, returning to the plain background. The heading “Who this is for” is in italic serif, centered. Rather than bullet points, a short list is expressed in three compact paragraphs, each starting with a bold phrase: “For storytellers:” followed by “journalists, poets, copywriters, and creators who want their work to carry more weight off the page.”; “For organizers:” followed by “campaigners, facilitators, and community leaders looking for narrative tools to sharpen their strategy.”; “For designers & makers:” followed by “graphic designers, UX/UI folks, developers, and artists who translate stories into tangible experiences.” This section is laid out as a single column, but the first word of each sentence (“For”) is styled in small caps to add nuance.

Next is the “Venue & Experience” section, which combines imagery and careful text. The heading “Venue & Experience” is left-aligned. Below it, in smaller serif, is the line “Hosted at The Glasshouse, an industrial loft in Brooklyn transformed for a day into a living newsroom.” Beneath this, a large, wide photograph spans the width of the main column (not full-screen, but wider than the speaker portraits). The photograph is a realistic, wide-angle view of a spacious loft with large paneled windows letting in golden afternoon light. Exposed brick walls blend with white plaster. Long communal tables are arranged with simple wooden chairs; each table has scattered notebooks, small potted plants, and glass carafes of water. A printed banner on the far wall reads “INK & IMPACT” in large, black serif lettering on off-white canvas. People are seen mingling in small groups, mid-conversation, blurred slightly to emphasize atmosphere over individuals. The style is documentary-like with natural lighting, soft shadows, and warm color tones, giving a welcoming, tactile impression. Below the image, three short paragraphs describe the experience, each introduced by a bolded heading: “Hybrid, by design.” explains there is a live-stream and dedicated digital host; “Slow, intentional pacing.” describes ample breaks, writing corners, and reflection prompts; “Accessibility in focus.” notes ASL interpretation, sliding-scale tickets, and step-free access. At the very bottom of this section, a small inline map illustration appears — not an interactive map, but a stylized line-art drawing in black and light gray, showing a simple street grid with a highlighted square labeled “The Glasshouse” and a small subway icon with the text “10 min walk from Bedford Ave Station.” The map uses ultra-thin lines and subtle shading, like something drawn in the margins of a newspaper.

Approaching the bottom of the page, a lightly framed “What past attendees are saying” section uses a single, large testimonial. The section title is centered, in serif, with small caps “TESTIMONIAL” above. A faint, thin rectangular border encloses the testimonial like a printed box. Inside, the quote is in oversized, light italic serif, centered: ““I left Ink & Impact with a notebook full of ideas and a phone full of new collaborators. It felt less like a conference and more like a daylong editorial jam session.”” Below, in small, medium-gray sans-serif, on one line: “— Jordan Riley, Community Publisher”. A small, monochrome icon of an open notebook with a pen lies to the right of the attribution, sized very small.

The page concludes with a strong, minimalist call-to-action section, using a subtle inversion of the color scheme. This final band has a soft, warm gray background (#E2DDD2) that stretches full width. The content remains in the central column, but the elements are slightly larger. At the top, in small serif uppercase, it reads “JOIN US”. Below, the primary heading in large serif states: “Ready to write what’s next?” The line is left-aligned, with a slightly tighter line height to feel intentional. Beneath, a short supporting sentence in sans-serif: “Reserve your spot, pull out your favorite pen, and bring the stories you’ve been waiting to tell.” Under this sentence, the primary button appears again: “Register Now” in white text on rust background, centered this time. Underneath, a smaller text link in muted charcoal reads: “Download the full schedule (PDF)” with a thin underline and a tiny download arrow icon to its right, drawn as a simple line triangle with a vertical line. At the very bottom of the page, a minimalist footer appears on the same gray background but separated by a thin top border. The footer has, aligned left within the column, small sans-serif text: “Presented by Common Thread Collective, a nonprofit storytelling incubator.” Under that, a line with links “Contact”, “Accessibility”, “Code of Conduct”, “Press Kit” in tiny uppercase with vertical bar separators. On the right side of the footer, but still within the column, are three small, monochrome social icons—an outline of a pen nib (linking to a blog), a simple “in” for LinkedIn, and a minimal camera outline for Instagram—each drawn with 1px strokes, no fills, and arranged horizontally with soft spacing. There are no videos, animations, or any moving elements on this page; all images are static, all interactions are limited to hover color changes and link clicks, and the overall experience feels like browsing a carefully designed print feature, translated to a single, vertically flowing, editorial-style web layout.

instruction: Planning the generation details for the following website:
input: Design a high-contrast, graphic, two-column-leaning homepage for an eco-friendly café tutorial site called “Brew Green Lab”. The aesthetic should feel like a blend of an independent zine and a research portal: bold, poster-like compositions, stark contrasts, geometric shapes, and type-driven layouts. The color palette is minimal but punchy: pure white (#FFFFFF) backgrounds contrasted with deep charcoal black (#111111), bright plant green (#34C759), and a single accent highlight in a vivid electric teal (#00D0FF). Fonts are clean and modern, with a typewriter-meets-laboratory vibe: headings use a tall, condensed sans-serif like “Space Grotesk” in uppercase; body text uses a monospaced font like “IBM Plex Mono” for a research-report feel. Everything feels flat and graphic, with no gradients, but with plenty of high-contrast blocks, sharp edges, and occasional halftone-style dot patterns. The page is full-width, but the primary content is centered in a max-width container, leaving generous white margins on both sides. The overall layout tends towards two columns on desktop (left for main narrative, right for navigation, summaries, and callouts), but sections occasionally flip this or stretch into asymmetrical full-width bands to keep rhythm and visual interest. At the very top, spanning the full width, there is a narrow black strip as a utility bar, about 60px high. The background of this bar is solid charcoal (#111111). On the far left, aligned flush to the container margin, the text logo “Brew Green Lab” appears in uppercase, in bright white monospaced type, slightly letterspaced: “BREW GREEN LAB”. On the right side of the utility bar, there is a small pill-shaped tag with a thin white border and transparent background containing the text “OPEN-SOURCE CAFE RESEARCH” in tiny uppercase white sans-serif. The bar has no shadows, but below it, a razor-thin green horizontal rule runs across the full width, acting as a sharp divider. Below this, the hero section occupies the top third of the viewport: a strong two-column layout with the left column containing text and the right column containing a graphic image. The left hero column sits on a white background with text strictly left-aligned. At the top of this column, a small, rectangular label in electric teal with white uppercase text reads: “CASE STUDY SERIES”. Below this, with large, commanding typography, the headline reads in three lines: “Design an Eco-Friendly Cafe.”, "Test It Like a Lab.", “Share It with the World.” Each line is in black condensed sans-serif, all uppercase, with the first and third line slightly larger than the middle, and the period on each line is slightly enlarged so it feels like a graphic dot. Beneath the headline is a short, two-line monospaced intro paragraph in dark grey text: “Learn the research-backed framework for creating low-waste, community-driven cafes. From energy usage audits to circular menu design, every step is documented.” Below this copy, there are two horizontally aligned buttons. The primary button is a filled rectangle in bright green with white uppercase sans-serif text that reads: “START THE TUTORIAL”. It has crisp, 90-degree corners and a thin black outline, giving it a high-contrast graphic look. To its right, separated by about 24px, is a secondary button with a white background, a thin black border, and black uppercase text reading: “DOWNLOAD FIELD GUIDE (PDF)”. Both buttons have no drop shadow, only bold outline; when hovered (described visually, not animated), imagine the border thickens and the background inverts colors. The right side of the hero is a bold graphic illustration occupying roughly the right half of the section, contained within a black rectangular frame that almost touches the top utility bar. The illustrated image is flat and vector-style: a stylized top-down view of a cafe interior rendered in orthographic perspective using only black, white, green, and teal. At the center, a white circular cafe table seen from above is covered in precise, diagrammatic objects: a green ceramic mug with visible steam rising in three smooth, white, S-shaped lines; a black notebook with white grid lines and a small green sticky note on its upper right corner; a white tablet device with a black screen showing tiny grid-like icons hinting at data; and a thin, teal ruler running diagonally across the table. Around the table, on the white floor, are solid black shapes indicating chairs, and a large, flat graphic plant with sharp, angled leaves in green and teal occupies the bottom left of the frame. The background floor is mostly white with a few thin black lines suggesting tiles, and in the upper corners of the frame, circular icons: a minimal leaf icon in a white circle with a green outline, and a tiny cafetiere icon in black inside a white circle. The composition is flat, with no gradients, but strong shadows drawn as pure black rectangles extending at 45 degrees from objects, giving a high-contrast, printed-poster feel. Below the hero, a wide horizontal divider band about 80px tall stretches across the screen: the left half is white, the right half is black, forming a stark diagonal split from mid-left to bottom-right. On the white side, in small uppercase monospaced text, it reads “RESEARCH-DRIVEN MODULES”; on the black side, in white text, it reads “DOCUMENT EVERY DESIGN DECISION.” Immediately after this divider, the main tutorial overview section begins, designed as a two-column grid with asymmetrical widths: a wider left column (about 65%) for content and a narrower right column (35%) for callouts and navigation. The background stays pure white. The section heading, aligned to the left, is in uppercase black sans-serif: “1. Overview: From Idea to Impact”. A thin green underline runs beneath the heading. The left column then presents a structured yet editorial-feeling block of text. The first paragraph reads: “This tutorial treats your cafe like a living research project. Instead of rushing to open, you’ll prototype, document, and iterate—minimizing waste and maximizing local impact.” Below it, a three-item numbered list is displayed with oversized numbers in green, aligned in a vertical column. Each number “01”, “02”, “03” is in huge condensed sans-serif, partially overlapping faint grey horizontal rules. To the right of each number is a short description in monospaced text: “01 — Map your cafe’s systems: energy, water, ingredients, and waste.”; “02 — Design low-impact alternatives and run small-scale tests.”; “03 — Share your findings with your community and adapt together.” On the right column of this section, a narrow vertical panel with a charcoal background appears like a research sidebar. At the top of this dark panel, white uppercase sans-serif text reads “QUICK NAVIGATION”. Below, three stacked white-bordered boxes act as text-only buttons, each with a label in monospaced uppercase white text: “Module 1: Systems Mapping”, “Module 2: Low-Waste Operations”, “Module 3: Community Experiments”. Each box has a tiny green square in the top-left corner, reminiscent of an interface checkbox. At the bottom of this sidebar panel, a white monospaced label says “EST. TIME: 6–8 HOURS” with a thin teal line above it. Scattered, subtle dotted patterns in light grey appear behind the panel, like a halftone texture, restricted to the right side. The next section breaks the two-column pattern briefly with a full-width strip in black, about 300px tall, called “Field Notes”. On the left half of this black band is large white heading text in uppercase: “Field Notes from Real Cafes”. Under it is a short monospaced paragraph in greyish white: “We’ve interviewed cafe founders, baristas, and sustainability researchers. Each note below links their experience to a specific tutorial step.” On the right half of this band, arranged in a vertical stack, are three rectangular white cards with no drop shadow, evenly spaced with generous padding. Each card contains a compact quote. The first card’s header text in uppercase green reads: “Case: Rainwater Espresso Bar”; beneath that, in black monospaced text, the quote: “We cut water use by 34% with a simple pitcher rinse system—tested over one month.” The second card: header “Case: Night Owl Library Cafe” in teal, quote: “Tracking food waste by hand for two weeks changed our menu more than any trend report.” The third card: header “Case: Forest Line Kiosk” in green, quote: “A no-disposable policy only worked after we prototyped alternative take-home containers.” Each quote has a tiny, vertical black line at the left, like a quote indicator. Just below this full-width band, the layout returns to a two-column structure, but now inverted: the right column is larger. This section introduces “Module 1: Systems Mapping”. On the left, occupying about 35% of the width, is a tall, white block with a black border that reads “MODULE 1 / SYSTEMS MAP” in uppercase letters stacked vertically near the top, with each word separated by a thin horizontal grey line. Below is a simple list in monospaced black text: “Inputs: energy, water, ingredients.”; “Outputs: drinks, food, warmth, community.”; “Emissions: waste, noise, carbon, data.” The right column is the main focus here and features a strong graphic image, again in a clean flat style, showing a cafe layout diagram from a slightly isometric, but still stylized, viewpoint. The ground is a white rectangle with thin black lines marking zones: “BAR”, “SEATING”, “STORAGE”, “BACK-OF-HOUSE” written in uppercase labels. Arrows in bright green and teal travel across the diagram to show flows: a green arrow from “STORAGE” to “BAR” labeled “Beans”; a teal arrow from “BAR” to “SEATING” labeled “Drinks”; and a black arrow from “SEATING” to a small black bin icon labeled “Waste”. At the top right corner of the diagram, a tiny white label on black background says “DRAFT V1.0”. Around the diagram, some black geometric shapes—triangles and circles—float like annotation markers. Beneath this section, there is a short, centered instructional prompt in small uppercase text: “Print this map, mark your actual cafe, and photograph the annotated version.” The next major section, “Module 2: Low-Waste Operations”, restores the classic left-heavy two-column. The section’s background alternates to a very light grey (#F5F5F5), visually differentiating it from prior sections but still keeping a high-contrast aesthetic. The heading is black uppercase sans-serif with a green square to its left: “2. Low-Waste Operations”. The left column has three subsections, each with a small research-feeling label strip, a subheading, and a short paragraph. The first label strip is a narrow teal bar with white uppercase text: “EXPERIMENT A / CUP LIFECYCLE”. Below, a subheading in black text reads: “Track Every Cup”. The paragraph in monospaced text: “For one week, track every single cup served: dine-in, reusable, and single-use. Use a simple tally sheet next to the espresso machine.” The second block has a green label: “EXPERIMENT B / ENERGY”, subheading “Shift Your Peak Load”, and the paragraph: “Record your machine and light usage by hour for three days. Test turning off or dimming non-essential equipment during slow periods.” The third block uses a black label with white text: “EXPERIMENT C / MENU DESIGN”, subheading “Design for Leftovers”, and paragraph: “Redesign one menu item so its leftovers can become the base of another dish or drink. Document before-and-after waste levels qualitatively.” In the narrower right column of this section, three vertically stacked square illustrations are placed, each a minimal icon-like image with a white background and thick black borders, roughly 1:1 ratio. The top illustration depicts a stack of cups in pure graphic form: three overlapping, tilted cup silhouettes in black, green, and teal, with a bold black arrow circling around them, indicating reuse. The middle illustration shows a stylized, side view of an espresso machine drawn as a black rectangle with white knobs, a green power indicator circle, and a teal plug icon, with zigzag lines around it suggesting energy flow. The bottom illustration shows a flat top-down view of a plate divided into two halves, one half hatched in black, the other half in green, with small black arrows pointing from the green area to a smaller plate to imply re-use of leftovers. Each illustration is captioned beneath in monospaced uppercase text: “CUP FLOW”, “ENERGY PATTERNS”, and “MENU LOOPS”. Between each illustration, thin horizontal black lines span the full width of the column. Further down the page, a section for “Module 3: Community Experiments” introduces a slightly different asymmetry: on large screens, it uses a staggered layout where the heading and summary span both columns at the top, and then two columns below have misaligned cards. The heading is centered and reads “3. Community Experiments” in uppercase condensed sans-serif, slightly larger text. A small subtitle below, in dark grey monospaced type, reads: “Design experiments with your neighbors, not just for them.” Below this, two columns of experiment cards appear, but the left column starts slightly higher, creating a stepped effect. In the left column, the first card is titled “Experiment: Neighborhood Brew Panel”, with the title in black uppercase sans-serif, and body in monospaced text: “Invite regular guests to sample new low-waste menu items and document their feedback verbatim.” A thin teal border frames this card. Below it, another card titled “Experiment: Dish Swap Day” reads: “Host a day where guests bring their own mugs or containers and track how it changes your disposable usage.” On the right column, offset vertically, one card titled “Experiment: Repair Cafe Fridays” says: “Partner with local fixers to host monthly repair sessions. Offer free drip coffee for participants. Document energy and materials saved.” Each card has a small icon in its top-right corner: a white speech bubble on a green circle for the panel, a stylized cup on a teal circle for the swap day, and a wrench-and-leaf icon in white on a black circle for the repair cafe. All icons are flat, high-contrast, with bold outlines. Near the bottom of this section, a pale grey, dotted rectangle spans the full width with monospaced text: “Log each experiment like a research paper: Date, Hypothesis, Method, Observations, Next Steps.” Attached to the bottom right of this rectangle, slightly overlapping, is a small black circle with white uppercase text “TEMPLATE INSIDE PDF”. As we near the page’s end, a “Resources & References” section appears, returning to a straightforward two-column layout on white background. On the left, the heading “Resources & References” is in uppercase black text with a small teal triangle icon to its left, pointing right. Below is a categorized list of text resources, each with a label in uppercase green (e.g., “GUIDE”, “ARTICLE”, “TOOL”) followed by a black monospaced title, such as “GUIDE — Zero-Waste Cafe Blueprint (v1.2)”, “ARTICLE — ‘Measuring a Cup’s Carbon Footprint’”, “TOOL — Water Use Checklist (printable)”. On the right, there is a tall, white, bordered box with a light grey background labeled at the top in small uppercase text: “CITATION FORMAT”. Inside this box, in monospaced text, is an example citation, formatted with line breaks: “Brew Green Lab. ‘Design an Eco-Friendly Cafe.’ 2025. https://brewgreenlab.example/tutorial”. At the bottom of this box, a thin black horizontal line separates the citation from a small note: “All materials licensed under Creative Commons Attribution 4.0.” Finally, the footer sits at the very bottom on a black background. It is divided visually into two unequal columns. The left side contains the site name once more in white uppercase monospaced type: “BREW GREEN LAB”, with a tiny green dot after “LAB” as a final accent. Below this, three small links written in uppercase grey monospaced text are stacked: “ABOUT”, “CONTRIBUTE DATA”, “CONTACT”. The right side of the footer includes a simple, stark contact field: a white rectangle, no rounded corners, with placeholder text “your.email@domain.com” in light grey monospaced font, and a black button attached to its right edge containing white uppercase text “JOIN RESEARCH LIST”. Under this input, aligned to the right, is a small line of grey text: “No spam. Just field reports and updated protocols.” The entire page uses only static elements—no animation, no motion, no charts—and relies on stark contrasts, consistent monospaced typography, geometric illustration, and a disciplined two-column structure with intentional asymmetrical breaks to convey the sense of a rigorous, visually bold, eco-friendly cafe research tutorial.

instruction: Planning the generation details for the following website:
input: Design a playful, comic-book-inspired event landing page for an independent zine and illustration festival called “INK SPLASH WEEKEND.” The aesthetic feels like a hand-drawn, vibrant comic universe colliding with a real-world event page: bold outlines, halftone patterns, overlapping panels, speech bubbles, and asymmetrical card layouts arranged in a loose but clearly hierarchical structure. The page background is a soft off-white with a very subtle, pale-blue halftone dot pattern fading toward the edges, giving it a printed-paper feel. At the very top, a full-width sticky header bar spans the page, colored bright canary yellow with a slightly darker yellow diagonal stripe pattern barely visible. The header height is medium, with generous horizontal padding. On the left is the event logo: a playful, hand-lettered wordmark “INK SPLASH WEEKEND” in thick black ink, letters slightly wobbly like they were drawn with a brush pen, each word stacked, and a small stylized ink droplet splashing off the “I” in “INK.” Behind the text is a rough, irregular comic “burst” shape in cyan with a black outline, like an action sound effect balloon.
