To the right of the logo, spaced evenly, is a horizontal navigation menu with uppercase items in a comic sans–inspired but more polished font: “ABOUT,” “WORKSHOPS,” “ARTISTS,” “SCHEDULE,” “TICKETS,” “FAQ.” Each menu item is black text, with a hover state described as changing to magenta with a thick underline that looks hand-drawn and slightly wavy. On the far right, a slightly overlapping, pill-shaped button sits half inside the header and half dipping below it into the hero section, emphasizing a layered look. This button is filled with bright magenta, has a thick black outline, and the text “GET PASSES” in small caps white, bold, centered. The button has a subtle shadow that looks like a rough, gray brush stroke beneath it. Below the header is the main hero section, occupying most of the first viewport with a comic cover–style composition. The hero background is a dynamic, angled split: on the left two-thirds, a pale teal with scattered white starburst doodles and faint black ink splatter; on the right third, a vertical strip with a gradient from deep purple at the top to dark blue at the bottom, overlaid with a faint halftone pattern. At the center-left, overlapping both background colors, is a large, asymmetrical white panel shaped like a tilted comic panel with thick black outline and slightly rounded corners, casting a crisp shadow onto the background. Inside this panel, at the top, a small yellow banner with black outline and rounded corners reads in tiny uppercase text “SEPT 13–15 · PORTSIDE WAREHOUSE, BROOKLYN, NY” in black. Below the banner, in very large, bold, hand-drawn style letters is the main hero text: “Dive into a weekend of ink, imagination, and indie comics.” The words are broken into three lines, left-aligned, with “ink, imagination” displayed in a darker, thicker line weight, and the word “indie comics” underlined with a scribbly cyan line. Beneath the headline is a smaller subheadline paragraph in a neat, sans-serif font: “Join artists, zinesters, and doodlers from around the world for three days of workshops, live draws, and panels on self-publishing.” It’s dark gray, left-aligned, and occupies about half the width of the panel. Under the paragraph are two overlapping call-to-action buttons: the primary button on the left is a cyan rectangle with chunky black outline and slightly rounded corners, text “BUY WEEKEND PASS” in uppercase white. Slightly tucked under its lower-right corner is a white button with a black outline reading “VIEW SCHEDULE” in black uppercase; it is shifted down and right so they overlap like layered panels. To the right of this central panel, positioned more in the hero’s right side, is a large illustrated hero image in a flat, comic art style. The illustration shows a diverse group of three characters at a long table overflowing with art supplies. The viewpoint is slightly above, angled down, as if the viewer is standing across the table. The first character on the left is a person with medium brown skin, a shaved undercut with bright blue hair on top, wearing round glasses and a mustard yellow hoodie. They are laughing and drawing with a black brush pen on a sketchbook, ink splashes flying upward in exaggerated motion lines. The middle character, with light skin and curly pink hair tied in a high puff, is leaning forward excitedly, wearing a teal apron over a white T-shirt, holding a zine with a bold cover that says “ZAP!” in thick yellow letters on a red background with a starburst. The third character on the right has dark skin, shoulder-length locs, and is wearing a purple beanie and dark red sweater; they are inking with a dip pen, with small bottles of ink, stacks of mini comics, and a coffee cup around them. The table is cluttered with markers, washi tapes, stickers, erasers, and mini posters. In the background, slightly blurred, are tall shelves filled with colorful books, zines, and poster tubes. Above the characters, comic sound effects like “SCRIBBLE!”, “SWISH!”, and “POP!” appear in small, colorful action bubbles (yellow, pink, cyan) with black outlines. Some of these sound bubbles break the boundaries of the image and overlap the background area, emphasizing the overlapping comic-panel layout. Beneath the hero, the next section overlaps upward into the hero, creating a layered effect: a wide strip that looks like a torn-out page edge, with an irregular top border shaped like ripped notebook paper and a subtle shadow behind it. This section has a warm cream background and is labeled as the “ABOUT THE FESTIVAL” area. At the top left of this strip is a small, hand-drawn label in a white rounded rectangle with black border reading in uppercase “WHAT IS THIS?” A few pixels below is a larger heading in bold black ink-style letters: “A playground for print, panels, and DIY storytelling.” The paragraph below uses a legible, slightly rounded sans-serif font in dark gray, left-aligned, with line height generous and margins wide. The text reads: “INK SPLASH WEEKEND is a three-day independent comics and zine festival packed with hands-on workshops, creator talks, live risograph demos, and cozy corners for swapping stories. Whether you’re a seasoned illustrator or you just love doodling in the margins, this weekend is your permission slip to get messy with ink.” On the right side of this strip, partially overlapping the text and extending beyond the section’s boundaries, are three small Polaroid-style image cards fanned and clipped together with a drawn binder clip illustration. Each card is a simple illustration: the top card is a flat design of a bustling convention table scene from a top-down perspective: zines laid out in neat grids, hands exchanging cash, and a simple “$5” price tag. The second card shows a close-up of a risograph machine in bright teal and coral, with paper feeding through and a soft spray of ink texture suggested. The third card shows an overhead view of a sketchbook spread filled with doodles, speech bubbles, and a coffee ring stain, drawn in muted blues and pinks. Moving down the page, a new section appears with a bold, slightly tilted heading in a magenta brushstroke shape at the top left: “CHOOSE YOUR TRACK.” The background here is white with faint light-gray comic panel outlines forming an asymmetrical grid behind the content. The content is divided into three vertical, overlapping cards representing event tracks: “MAKE,” “LEARN,” and “MEET.” Each card is a tall rounded rectangle panel with a thick black outline and a different background color (cyan for MAKE, pastel yellow for LEARN, soft lilac for MEET). The MAKE card is slightly larger and in front, overlapping the edges of the other two. At the top of each card is a small icon inside a circular frame: MAKE features a crossed pen and brush icon in white on a dark teal circle; LEARN shows an open book with a speech bubble in white on a coral circle; MEET has two overlapping cartoon speech bubbles in white on a navy circle. Below each icon is a heading in all caps black text (e.g., “MAKE: HANDS-ON WORKSHOPS”), followed by a short description in dark gray sans-serif. For example, the MAKE description reads: “Roll up your sleeves for guided sessions on character design, inking techniques, mini-comic folding, and risograph basics.” LEARN’s text: “Dive into talks on self-publishing, building an audience, and turning your passion into a sustainable practice.” MEET’s text: “Connect with fellow creators, swap zines, and join low-pressure critique circles and sketch jams.” At the bottom of each panel is a simple list of 3–4 sample session titles as bullet-point style mini-panels, each in a tiny rounded rectangle with an off-white background, black outline, and hand-drawn bullet icons like stars, hearts, and little ink droplets. Below this track section is a full-width band with a bold, comic-strip-style look for the “FEATURED WORKSHOPS” showcase. The background is a deep navy blue with large, semi-transparent halftone dot patterns. Across the width, there’s an irregular, multi-panel comic strip frame: three wide horizontal panels sitting side by side, separated by thick white gutters and black outlines, slightly tilted so they aren’t perfectly parallel. Each panel is a card describing a featured workshop. Inside the first panel, a heading in white uppercase reads “Character Lab: Designing Expressive Heroes & Weirdos.” Below is a one-line description in smaller, white sans-serif: “Sketch and iterate your own cast of characters with illustrator Mira Ortiz.” To the left of the text, taking up about one-third of the panel, there is a flat illustration of a character sheet: multiple character faces and full-body poses in a grid, drawn in bold colors (orange, teal, purple), each with exaggerated expressions, with a pencil and a brush pen crossing over the bottom right corner of the sheet. The second panel has heading text “Risograph 101: From Sketch to Print,” with a description and a stylized illustration of overlapping paper prints in fluorescent pink, teal, and yellow, slightly misregistered to evoke risograph effects, with a small risograph machine icon in the corner. The third panel’s heading is “Zine in a Day: Fold, Staple, Share,” with text and a flat illustration of folded mini-zines fanning out from a central point, each cover featuring simple bold designs like lightning bolts, speech bubbles, and stars. Each panel also includes a small “LIMITED SPOTS” tag in a tiny yellow rectangle with black outline, placed at the top right corner of the panel. Scrolling further, a diagonal section divider slices across the screen, like a giant comic panel border, transitioning into the “ARTIST ALLEY” section. The background switches to a soft pink with scattered doodles – tiny drawings of pens, books, stars – in a faint darker pink. This section features an asymmetrical grid of artist profile cards that overlap and vary slightly in size. At the top left is a heading: “MEET THE CREATORS” written in a chunky, black, all-caps font, with “creators” underlined by a wavy multicolored line. Beneath it is a short intro paragraph: “Over 60 indie artists, zine collectives, and micro-presses bring their worlds to life at INK SPLASH WEEKEND.” The artist cards are rectangular with white backgrounds, black outlines, and slight drop shadows. They are arranged in two rows, but some cards are offset vertically so that edges overlap, creating a layered collage effect. Each card features a circular avatar illustration of the artist in a simple, friendly cartoon style, with distinctive hair and accessories, on the left. To the right of each avatar is the artist’s name in bold black text, a small handle (like “@inkandaster”) in gray, and a one-line description in smaller gray text (“Slice-of-life comics and cozy cityscapes.”). At the bottom of each card are two tiny pill buttons: one labeled “ARTIST PAGE” and the other “FAVORITE ZINE,” both outlined in black with white fill and small black text. In between some cards, as if tucked behind them, are tiny decorative elements: a sketched roll of washi tape, a doodled stack of zines, a little ink bottle icon. Below the grid is a centered, wide, white button with a black outline that says “VIEW FULL ARTIST LIST” in uppercase black, with a tiny right-pointing arrow drawn like a doodle. Next, the “SCHEDULE SNAPSHOT” section appears, with a background that looks like notebook paper: white with faint horizontal light-blue lines and a subtle pink vertical margin line on the left edge. At the top, slightly overlapping the previous section via a shadow, is a taped-on label: a small white rectangle “taped” to the page with illustrated masking tape strips at its corners in pale yellow. In thick, hand-drawn black text, it reads “SCHEDULE SNAPSHOT.” Below this, the schedule is presented as three vertical day columns labeled “FRI,” “SAT,” and “SUN,” each inside their own lightly colored rectangular panel (soft yellow, mint, and lavender) with irregular edges like torn paper and black outlines. The day labels are in large uppercase letters at the top of each panel, with small doodles nearby (a tiny sun for FRI, a star cluster for SAT, a coffee cup for SUN). Within each day panel, sessions are listed in chronological order as stacked mini-cards: rectangular strips in white with black outlines, each featuring the time on the left in bold gray text (e.g., “11:00 AM”), the session title in black, and a tiny tag icon on the right (like a tiny pen, book, or people icon indicating workshop, talk, or mingle). The cards slightly overlap vertically, with shadows implying depth, like sticky notes layered on each other. At the bottom right corner of the entire schedule section is a small, white button reading “OPEN FULL SCHEDULE” in black, underlined with a hand-drawn line. Continuing down, an eye-catching, overlapping call-to-action band invites sign-ups. This section uses a bright cyan background with a large, angled magenta banner that looks like it’s been slapped on top. On the left side of the banner, in large white uppercase text with a slight tilt, is “SECURE YOUR SPOT.” Underneath, in smaller white text: “Weekend passes, day tickets, and pay-what-you-can options available.” On the right side of the banner is a stacked ticket pricing overview contained in overlapping ticket-shaped cards. The primary ticket card, front and center, is a white rectangle with perforated edges drawn with dotted lines and a bold black border. It has the title “WEEKEND PASS” in thick black letters, a large price “$65” beneath in bold black, and a short gray description: “Access to all three days, plus exclusive sketchbook session.” Slightly behind it, offset to the left and right, are two more ticket cards partly hidden: one soft yellow labeled “DAY PASS” with “$30,” and one soft pink labeled “STUDENT / ACCESSIBILITY” with “Pay-what-you-can” in smaller text. Below these cards, centered, is a wide magenta button with white uppercase text “GET TICKETS,” with a black hand-drawn outline extending slightly beyond the button edges so it looks like a scribbled frame. Toward the bottom of the page is a friendly “FAQ & DETAILS” section with a background in pale lavender and faint white speech bubbles scattered across it like a pattern. The section heading is written within a big, stylized speech bubble outlined in black, with tail pointing toward the content, reading “Got questions?” in bold black letters. Inside this section, FAQs are laid out as vertical accordion cards: each question is a white rounded rectangle with a bold black outline, left-aligned black question text like “Do I need to be an artist to attend?” followed by a plus/minus icon in a small round badge at the right side; the plus/minus icon is a black drawn circle with a white interior and a hand-drawn plus sign or minus sign. When described as “expanded,” a card shows the answer text beneath the question in a lighter gray, taking up the full width inside the card. The answers are written in a friendly tone, such as: “Not at all! We welcome curious readers, doodlers, and anyone who loves stories on paper.” The cards are stacked with small gaps, and a subtle drop shadow emphasizes each. Finally, the footer spans the full width as a darker anchor: a deep navy background with a top edge that looks like torn paper, mirroring earlier sections. At the left, the event logo “INK SPLASH WEEKEND” reappears in smaller form, white text on a cyan burst shape with a black outline. Below it, in small white sans-serif text, is a short line: “Organized by Paper Rocket Collective.” In the center are three columns of links in uppercase gray text: “EVENT,” “CONNECT,” “SUPPORT.” Under “EVENT” are links like “Code of Conduct,” “Venue & Accessibility,” “Press Kit.” Under “CONNECT” are small social media icon placeholders drawn as simple outlined shapes: a square for Instagram with a tiny circle inside, a bird-like shaped icon, and a triangle for a video platform, each inside white circles with black outlines. Next to each icon is the platform name in white text. Under “SUPPORT” are links “Volunteer,” “Sponsor,” “Donate a Ticket.” On the right side of the footer is a small newsletter sign-up strip: a white rounded rectangle input field with thin black outline and faint placeholder text “Enter your email for doodle-filled updates,” followed by a small pie-slice-shaped button in magenta with white uppercase “SIGN UP” text. Around the footer content are a few tiny doodles, like ink splashes and stars, drawn with white lines. Throughout the entire page, fonts are consistent: headings use a bold, hand-drawn comic-style display font with thick strokes and slightly uneven baselines; body text uses a friendly, rounded sans-serif for readability. Colors are high-contrast but harmonious, centered on a palette of cyan, magenta, bright yellow, navy, lavender, and cream, always outlined in thick black or dark gray to evoke comic panels. All elements lean into overlapping layers—buttons partially overlapping sections, image cards fanning over text panels, angled banners slicing across backgrounds—while maintaining a clear visual hierarchy, so the eye moves from hero headline to track options, workshops, artists, schedule, and tickets in a smooth, playful flow. There are no videos, animations, or data visualizations anywhere on the page—everything is static, illustrated, and patterned like a comic book come to life as an event homepage.

instruction: Planning the generation details for the following website:
input: Design a high-contrast, graphic, multi-column portfolio homepage for a freelance design tutor called “GRID // STUDIO”. The site should have a bold, minimalist, poster-like aesthetic inspired by Swiss graphic design and risograph printing, with a strictly black-and-white base palette and one accent color: a saturated safety yellow (#FFD800). The overall layout is a multi-column grid with clear vertical and horizontal rhythm, but with occasional asymmetries: some columns are wider, some modules overlap slightly, and some sections break the grid with diagonal cuts or oversized typography. All text is in uppercase and uses two fonts: a condensed, bold sans-serif for headlines (similar to “Impact”) and a clean, monospaced font for body text and labels. The background is pure white, and the content is mostly black with yellow used for emphasis, hover states, and tags. Thin hairline rules (1px) in black and yellow clearly separate modules, and content sits on generous margins so the page feels like a modern print editorial spread. There are no videos, animations, or charts—everything is static, sharp, and graphic. At the top, a full-width header bar with 40px vertical padding stretches across the screen. The header background is white, with a 1px black line at the bottom that extends across the whole viewport. On the left third, aligned to the left margin, is the studio mark: “GRID // STUDIO” in bold condensed black type, about 24px, with “GRID” and “STUDIO” separated by two slashes. Directly beneath it in smaller monospaced font (12px) is the subtitle: “FREELANCE DESIGN TUTORIALS & PORTFOLIO”. On the right two-thirds of the header, a horizontal navigation is aligned to the right, with evenly spaced nav links in monospaced uppercase, 14px, letter-spaced: “WORK”, “TUTORIALS”, “ABOUT”, “SUBSCRIBE”, “CONTACT”. Each nav item has a tiny square 6x6px safety yellow block immediately to its left. Above the nav, aligned on the far right edge, is a small label in monospaced font, 10px, reading “PORTFOLIO / LEARN” with a thin 1px black underline only beneath the word “LEARN”. Just below the header, the hero section occupies almost the full first viewport height and uses a three-column layout. The hero background stays white, but a large black rectangular block occupies the left half of the hero area, starting flush with the left margin and extending about 60% of the height. Inside this black block, in huge, left-aligned condensed white text (about 64px), the main headline reads: “DESIGN STRONG, TEACH CLEAR.” Below the main line, in smaller monospaced white text (14px), there is a two-line subheadline: “PORTFOLIO OF SELECTED PROJECTS + STEP-BY-STEP TUTORIALS FOR BRAND, LAYOUT, AND POSTER DESIGN.” On the bottom left corner of the black block is a safety yellow rectangular button with black monospaced text, all caps: “START LEARNING”. The button has a 1px black border and sharp corners. Under the button, in very small monospaced white type (10px), is a note: “NO FLUFF. JUST PROCESS.” The right two columns of the hero area are reserved for three stacked image tiles, all framed by thin black borders and separated by 12px of white space, forming an asymmetrical collage. The top tile is a vertical poster mockup: a flat-design graphic showing a stylized coffee dripper, kettle, and mug rendered as geometric shapes (triangles, circles, rectangles) in black and safety yellow on a white background, with bold text “BREW GUIDE POSTER” at the top in black. The middle tile is a horizontal film review tutorial cover: a collage-like graphic with three overlapping rectangles representing film still frames, outlined in black and filled with white and soft gray, with a big safety yellow circle behind them. A horizontal black bar at the bottom edge has the word “FILM REVIEW LAYOUT” in white uppercase text. The bottom tile is a freelance design subscription cover: a grid of small black and white rectangles with one big safety yellow square in the center, and overlaid black text reading “CLIENT WORKFLOW TEMPLATE” aligned to the left. Each tile has a small label in the bottom right corner, monospaced 10px: “CASE STUDY 01”, “CASE STUDY 02”, “CASE STUDY 03”. Between the hero text block and the image column there is a narrow vertical safety yellow strip about 8px wide running from top to bottom of the hero section, creating a sharp graphic division. Beneath the hero, a “Featured Tutorials” section spans the width but is structured as a four-column grid. A large section heading sits in the leftmost column and slightly intrudes into the second column: “FEATURED TUTORIALS” in bold condensed black text (32px), with a thin horizontal safety yellow bar underneath stretching into the second column. Beneath this heading, the body copy in monospaced 12px reads: “LEARN HOW EACH PROJECT WAS BUILT—FROM FIRST SKETCH TO FINAL EXPORT.” To the right, three tutorial cards sit in columns two, three, and four. Each card is a tall rectangle with a white background, 1px black border, and a simple internal layout: at the top, a half-height graphic thumbnail in a flat, high-contrast style; below, title and meta info. The first card, titled “COFFEE EQUIPMENT POSTER”, features a thumbnail composed of an isometric workspace: a top-down, slightly angled view of a desk with a stylized espresso machine, scale, grinder, and pour-over setup. Everything is reduced to black and white geometric shapes with safety yellow highlights on key elements (such as the machine buttons and the kettle handle). The poster appears as a sheet of paper on the desk with a grid of icons and bold typography. Under the image, the title “COFFEE EQUIPMENT POSTER” is in condensed black text (18px), followed by a small yellow pill-shaped tag with black text: “LAYOUT GRID”. Below that, in monospaced 12px: “Learn the process behind a minimalist poster set: grid systems, hierarchy, and limited palettes.” The second card is “FILM REVIEW ZINE SPREAD”, with a thumbnail showing a two-page magazine spread laid flat, viewed from slightly above. The left page has a full-bleed black-and-white still of a film scene as a large rectangle, the right page has a bold safety yellow title bar across the top and a column of justified text below. Overlaid arrows and tiny annotations in safety yellow indicate “MARGIN”, “BLEED”, and “BASELINE GRID”. Under the image, the title “FILM REVIEW ZINE SPREAD” appears, then a yellow tag reading “EDITORIAL”, and description: “Step-by-step zine layout: balancing image-heavy pages with readable type.” The third card is “FREELANCE BRAND KIT”, with a thumbnail showing a spread of business cards, a letterhead, and a social media profile mockup, all using simple black lines and yellow blocks as accents, arranged in a fan on a white surface. Underneath, title and tag “FREELANCE” plus the description: “Build a client-ready brand kit: typography pairing, icon systems, and deliverable prep.” There is no hover behavior described; everything is static. A thin horizontal black rule separates this section from the next. The next section, “Portfolio Grid”, is more expansive and uses a dense multi-column layout reminiscent of a magazine index. The section title, aligned to the left margin, reads “SELECTED WORK” in condensed black 28px, with the word “SELECTED” filled in black and “WORK” outlined (stroke only). Under it, a small caption in monospaced 12px: “A CURATED MIX OF CLIENT PROJECTS AND PERSONAL STUDY PIECES.” Below that, the content area is divided into five columns with gutters. Various project blocks occupy one, two, or three columns horizontally, and heights vary, creating an asymmetric but orderly mosaic. The top row has a large horizontal project spanning three columns on the left and two smaller stacked vertical projects on the right. The large horizontal piece is a mock exhibition poster for an art show: a photorealistic black-and-white photo of a gallery interior with abstract paintings, slightly desaturated, with a bold overlay. Over the photo, a pure black rectangle sits in the top third with safety yellow condensed text: “ART EXHIBITION POSTER — ‘LINES & LIGHT’”. At the bottom, a thin yellow line and small text: “ROLE: CONCEPT, LAYOUT, TYPESETTING”. The two right-side vertical projects include an “ART SUPPLY BRANDING” tile and a “SUBSCRIPTION LANDING PAGE” tile. The “ART SUPPLY BRANDING” tile shows a flat-lay photograph-style illustration in grayscale: a tube of paint, a brush, a sketchbook, and a palette knife arranged diagonally on a white surface, with the paint tube label in safety yellow. The “SUBSCRIPTION LANDING PAGE” tile shows a minimalist web layout drawn in outline: header bar, hero block, sign-up form, and three feature icons, all black lines with one large yellow rectangle representing the primary call-to-action area. Each project tile has its title in condensed text at the bottom left edge, and just below, in small monospaced text, a line like “CATEGORY: BRANDING” or “CATEGORY: WEB DESIGN”. Additional project tiles fill the grid below: a narrow vertical “COFFEE BREW GUIDE INFOGRAPHIC” (a tall modular layout with icons of coffee gear and arrows, all black line art with yellow accents) spanning one column, a two-column “FILM FESTIVAL IDENT” static frame (a bold geometric logo and typographic lockup), a block showing “CLIENT: CAFE ORBIT” with a mockup of a to-go cup and menu in black and white, and a “PERSONAL: RISOGRAPH SERIES” featuring three overlapping printed sheets with off-register black and yellow shapes to suggest risograph misalignment. All tiles guard generous white space around them, and 1px rules maintain the grid sense. The next section, “How the Tutorials Work”, is horizontally split into two unequal columns. On the left two-thirds, a vertical stack of text blocks explains the methodology. The heading reads “HOW THE TUTORIALS WORK” in condensed 26px. Below are three numbered mini-blocks, each with a bold number “01”, “02”, “03” in large black monospaced type, about 24px, followed by a label line in bold condensed 16px and a short descriptive line below in monospaced 12px. “01 / BREAKDOWN” with description “Each tutorial dissects a real project into stages with screenshots and explanations.”; “02 / PRACTICE” with “Download templates to trace, then rebuild layouts from scratch.”; “03 / APPLY” with “Adapt the process to your own client briefs and personal projects.” On the right third, a single tall, narrow image sits. This image shows three stacked sheets of paper, each sheet representing one of the steps: the top sheet has “STEP 1: ANALYZE” in black with a simple wireframe of a layout; the middle sheet says “STEP 2: BUILD” with a grid of rectangles; the bottom sheet says “STEP 3: REFINE” with a tighter composition and a yellow highlight around the focal area. The sheets are illustrated with subtle drop shadows on a white background so they look like physical prints. Beneath this section is an “About the Designer” band with a contrasting layout: a full-width horizontal strip with a black background. The content is centered in a three-column grid. In the left column, a square, high-contrast black-and-white portrait image shows the designer in side profile, lit from one side to create a sharp chiaroscuro effect, wearing glasses and looking at a large monitor showing a grid-based layout. The portrait is stylized slightly like a graphic novel illustration, with crisp edges and minimal shading, but still recognizable as a person. A thin safety yellow frame outlines the portrait. In the middle column, white text provides a short bio: heading “ABOUT” in condensed 20px, then a paragraph in monospaced 12px: “I’m a freelance designer focused on layouts that look as intentional as they read. GRID // STUDIO is where I document my client work and turn each project into a clear, teachable process for other designers.” Below, in smaller type: “Specialties: Editorial Design, Brand Systems, Poster Art.” The right column has a narrow vertical block with a yellow background and black monospaced text: “AVAILABLE FOR 1:1 SESSIONS” at the top, then two bullet-like lines without icons: “Portfolio feedback” and “Layout critiques” separated by thin black dividers, and at the bottom, a simple underlined text link: “REQUEST A SESSION”. Below this dark band, a “Subscribe & Resource Library” section returns to a white background and is split into two main columns. On the left, a compact subscription form with strict geometry. The section label in small monospaced uppercase is “SUBSCRIBE”, then a large heading: “STAY SHARP.” in condensed black 28px, with a period in safety yellow. Below a short description line reads: “Get new tutorials, breakdowns, and downloadable templates in your inbox.” The form itself consists of two horizontal fields: the first is a long rectangular input with a 1px black border and placeholder text in monospaced gray: “YOUR EMAIL ADDRESS”. To its right, aligned in the same row, a shorter safety yellow rectangular button with black uppercase text: “JOIN THE LIST”. Below the form, a tiny note in monospaced 10px: “No spam. Just practical design process.” On the right, a “Resource Library” mini-grid: three small resource tiles in a three-column layout. Each tile is a white rectangle with a black border, with a small icon at the top and text below. Icon styles are consistent: ultra-simple black line icons with a single yellow accent: a grid icon (a 3x3 square grid with one yellow cell), a pen tool icon (Bezier curve with a yellow anchor point), and a document icon (stack of papers with a yellow corner fold). The text under each icon: “GRID TEMPLATE PACK”, “LOGO CRITIQUE CHECKLIST”, “CLIENT BRIEF QUESTIONNAIRE” in monospaced 11px. A thin black rule separates the subscribe section from the final content band. The last content section before the footer is a compact “Contact & Credits” strip laid out in three columns with subtle asymmetry. The left column has the heading “CONTACT” in condensed 18px, then email and social in monospaced 12px: “Email: hello@gridstudio.design”, “Instagram: @grid.studio”, “Behance: /gridstudio”. The middle column contains a small block of text in monospaced font: “WORKING REMOTELY FROM: BARCELONA / OPEN TO COLLABORATIONS WORLDWIDE.” The right column has a compact list of “TOOLS USED” in uppercase monospaced 10px: “TOOLS USED: INDESIGN, ILLUSTRATOR, FIGMA, RISOGRAPH PRESS.” The footer is a narrow band with a white background and a thin black top border. In the footer, centered, is a tiny line of text in monospaced 10px: “© GRID // STUDIO — BUILT ON A GRID, OF COURSE.” In the very far bottom-right corner, aligned to the edge, a tiny safety yellow square (10x10px) acts as a final graphic punctuation. Throughout the page, all images, text, and shapes are static with no motion or animation; the overall impression is a carefully composed, print-like, high-contrast portfolio that doubles as a clear entry point into a design tutorial subscription ecosystem.

instruction: Planning the generation details for the following website:
input: Design a softly lit, single-column portfolio homepage for a fictional digital photography tutor called “Lumen Atelier,” using a gentle pastel aesthetic throughout. The page has a calm, airy feeling, with lots of white space and rounded edges, and almost everything is vertically stacked in one main column centered on the screen, but with occasional subtle asymmetries in image placement and background shapes to keep it visually interesting. The overall background of the page is a very pale warm gray with a faint gradient from top to bottom, transitioning from a whisper of pastel blush pink at the top to an almost white pastel blue at the bottom; this gradient is extremely subtle, so at a glance the page almost looks off-white. The main content sits in a central, narrow column (about 900–1000px wide), with generous left and right margins that allow the background colors and floating shapes to breathe. The base typeface for body text is a clean, rounded sans serif, like “Nunito” or “Quicksand,” in a charcoal-gray color (#333333), and headings use a soft, slightly condensed serif with delicate thick-thin contrasts, in a darker muted slate blue (#32445c), making it feel artistic but still modern. At the very top is a simple, fixed-position navigation bar that spans the full width of the page but remains visually light. It has a transparent background that gradually fills with a semi-opaque pastel cream (#fff7ef) as the user scrolls; at the initial view, it lays directly over the hero area. The nav bar height is modest, with generous vertical padding. On the left, the logo is text-based: “Lumen Atelier” in the serif heading font, set in a medium size with the word “Lumen” in pastel mauve (#c493b8) and “Atelier” in slate blue. A small, flat-design icon of a camera aperture — a simple circular shape with six softly rounded blades in alternating pastel mint and pastel coral — sits to the left of the text logo. On the right side of the nav bar, aligned horizontally, are four navigation links in the rounded sans serif: “Portfolio,” “Lessons,” “About,” and “Contact,” in a muted gray-blue color with plenty of letter-spacing; when hovered, they shift to pastel mint and gain a soft underline with a rounded shape. At the far right is a small pill-shaped button reading “Join the Workshop,” in lowercase, with a pastel peach background (#ffd5b8), white text, and a subtle drop shadow; on hover it deepens slightly in color and the shadow grows a bit stronger. Below this nav bar, filling most of the first visible screen, is a hero section that strongly defines the aesthetic. The hero background is a soft, rounded rectangle that sits in the center column but extends just slightly wider than the column on both sides, giving a gentle asymmetry. This shape has a pastel gradient from lavender at the top left to mint at the bottom right, extremely soft and washed-out. Overlaid on this background, arranged in two loose columns but still within the single central column, is the main hero content. On the left side is the primary heading, large and centered in the vertical middle of the hero section, reading: “Cultivate your eye for light.” The text is in the serif heading font, with “Cultivate” and “eye” in slate blue and “your” and “light” in pastel mauve, creating a subtle color rhythm. Below the heading is a short subheading in smaller sans serif text: “Personalized digital photography lessons, crafted with a gentle, creative touch.” This is left-aligned, medium gray, and sits with generous line spacing. Under the subheading are two buttons aligned horizontally but slightly offset: the primary button on the left reads “Explore the Portfolio” with a pastel mint background (#c7f2e3) and dark slate-blue text; to the right, a ghost-style button with a thin pastel mauve border and transparent interior reads “Start Learning” in matching mauve text. Between the buttons a small amount of breathing space is left. On the right side of the hero area is a prominent, softly bordered image in an artful, slightly skewed frame. The image is a photorealistic scene of a young photographer in profile holding a mirrorless camera to their eye, standing near a large window with diffused late-afternoon light streaming in. The tones in the photograph are muted and pastel: the photographer wears a pale blue shirt; the background has creamy white walls and a soft-focus cityscape outside. The lighting is gentle, with no harsh shadows, giving a dreamy mood. The image itself is framed within a rounded rectangle card with a thin white border and a drop shadow feathered so that it looks like a physical print hovering over the gradient. Behind this main image, slightly offset and partially overlapped, are two smaller, desaturated “polaroid” style thumbnails: one in the upper left corner showing a close-up of pastel-colored flowers on a windowsill in soft focus, and one in the lower right showing a slightly abstract composition of blurred city lights at dusk, rendered in pastel blues and pinks. These background thumbnails are semitransparent, like decorative elements, and not meant to be clicked. Between the left and right hero content, some very faint, hand-drawn-style line doodles in a slightly darker gradient (soft arcs and zigzag lines) connect the heading to the image, evoking the idea of a creative journey. Below the hero section, a small downward-pointing chevron icon in pastel gray suggests scrolling; it is static, not animated. The next section, “Featured Work,” begins with a centered heading in serif font: “Featured Work,” and a short one-line description beneath in sans serif: “A few quiet moments, captured in gentle color.” The section sits on a white background card that contrasts with the page background and has softly rounded corners at the top. Within this section, the layout still follows the central column, but each featured project is presented as a stacked card with a slight asymmetry. Each card is a wide, rounded rectangle with a very subtle shadow, full-width within the central column. At the top of the first card is an image spanning about two thirds of the card’s width, aligned left; to the right of the image is a column of text. The first featured image is a pastel-toned city street at dawn, photorealistic, shot with a shallow depth of field from a low angle, where the foreground shows wet cobblestones reflecting soft pink and blue light, midground has silhouetted pedestrians walking away from the camera, and the background fades into softly blurred buildings and streetlights. The entire scene is bathed in a gentle peach and lavender light with long soft reflections, giving a serene feeling. Below the image and text block, a thin, pale gray divider line spans the card. The text block to the right includes a project title in bold sans serif, “Pastel Mornings,” followed by a two-line description: “A series exploring the hush of early city light, with an emphasis on soft color and quiet details.” Below is a small line in a lighter gray reading “Digital photography · Street · 2024.” At the bottom right of the card is a minimalist link styled as text with a tiny arrow: “View full series →” in pastel mint. The second and third featured work cards follow a similar structure but alternate image alignment: the second card has its photorealistic image aligned to the right and text to the left. This second image shows a close-up of a pair of hands holding a ceramic coffee cup by a window, with steam rising in soft curls, backlit by pale morning light; the color palette is pastel cream, soft terracotta, and muted sky blue, with the window frame visible but out of focus in the background. The third card returns to image-left layout and shows a beach at twilight, with a long exposure capturing silky water and blurred waves, the sky a gradient from pale peach near the horizon to soft lilac above; small rocks in the foreground have soft shadows, and everything feels tranquil. Each card maintains enough padding and white space that the section feels uncluttered. Between the “Featured Work” and the next main section, there is a narrow horizontal strip with a gentle, abstract pastel watercolor texture stretching the full page width, but still faint and translucent so it doesn’t dominate. Centered on this strip is a small piece of italicized text in the serif font: ““Photography is the story I fail to put into words.” – Destin Sparks” in a soft gray, serving as a visual and thematic break. The next main section is titled “Soft Light Lessons” and focuses on tutoring services. This section returns to a plain, almost white background within the central column, with a large heading left-aligned: “Soft Light Lessons,” and a short explanatory line: “One-on-one and small group sessions tailored to your pace.” Below, three vertically stacked cards describe different offerings. Each card is a rounded rectangle with a very light pastel background: the first in a nearly white lavender haze, the second in pale mint, the third in soft peach. Each has a small flat icon at the top left: for “Beginner Focus” a simple flat line-art camera icon with a big circular lens, in pastel mauve outline; for “Creative Composition” a flat overlapping set of frames in pastel coral and blue; for “Editing in Gentle Tones” a flat screen with three sliders in mint, mauve, and peach. Each card’s title is in bold sans serif: “Beginner Focus,” “Creative Composition,” “Editing in Gentle Tones.” Beneath each title is a concise description spanning two or three lines, in a slightly smaller gray font. At the bottom of each card is a soft, underlined text link in pastel accent color reading “View details.” Slightly overlapping the right edge of this section, but still within the central visual column, is a small decorative image: a flat, pastel illustration of a vintage film camera, drawn in a simplified isometric style, using pastel mint, mauve, and peach blocks of color, placed as if leaning into the section from the right, adding a playful asymmetry without breaking the single-column flow. Next comes an “About the Artist” section, slightly narrower than the previous sections, emphasizing intimacy. It begins with a centered serif heading: “About Lumen Atelier,” in slate blue. Below, the content splits into two vertically stacked blocks, still within the column but subtly separated. The top block is a circular portrait image aligned center: a soft, semi-realistic illustration of a person in their early 30s, with short, wavy hair, wearing a light pastel green shirt, softly smiling, with a camera strap diagonally across their chest. The background of the portrait is a blurred pastel pink and blue gradient, giving it a dreamy vignette effect. The portrait has a thin white border and a subtle shadow so it appears like a cut-out photo. Beneath the portrait, the second block is a centered paragraph of text introducing the artist: “I’m Aria, a photographer and educator who believes in making creative practice feel light, slow, and kind. Through Lumen Atelier, I help people notice the quiet magic of daily life—from the way light spills across a morning table to the pale glow of the city after rain.” This text is in the sans serif, with generous line spacing. Under this, a short, three-item list presented as softly colored “tags” sits horizontally centered: each tag is a small rounded pill with pale borders and pastel text, reading “gentle teaching,” “mindful practice,” and “soft color stories.” They are not interactive, just decorative labels. Below the About section is a “Testimonial” area set against a subtle pastel-block background: a large, softly irregular shape that looks like a wash of watercolor in pale mint, over which a white card floats in the center. Inside the card, a quotation in serif italics reads: ““Aria’s lessons helped me finally slow down and see light in a new way. It never felt intimidating—just quietly inspiring.”” Beneath this, right-aligned, is the name “— Mira, student” in sans serif with a pastel mauve accent. A tiny flat icon of a light beam or prism sits in the lower left of the card in outline style, reinforcing the theme. Near the bottom of the page, just before the footer, is a gentle call-to-action section titled “Ready to see softly?” with the heading left-aligned and in a slightly larger size, in slate blue serif. Below, a short line of text says: “Share your work, ask a question, or book a session. I’d love to see how you see.” Below this, centered, are two elements stacked vertically: a minimalist email capture form and a secondary link. The email form is a long rounded input field with a very pale border and placeholder text “Enter your email” in light gray; to the right of the input inside the same pill is a compact button with a pastel peach background reading “Stay in touch.” The secondary link below is text-only, reading “Or send a message instead” in pastel mint, underlined in a soft, rounded style. The footer section is a low-contrast, compact band at the bottom, spanning the full width but conforming to the central column for its content layout. The background is a soft pastel lavender-gray. Inside the footer, elements are vertically stacked and centered: first a tiny version of the aperture icon in monochrome pastel gray; then the text “Lumen Atelier” in small caps; beneath that, a single line of navigation links separated by small dots: “Portfolio · Lessons · About · Contact,” in muted gray; and at the very bottom, the smallest line of text reading “© 2025 Lumen Atelier. Crafted with soft light.” in an even lighter gray. All links in the footer change to pastel mint on hover. Throughout the page there are no animations or videos; everything is static and serene. There are no data charts or visualizations. Icons and illustrative elements share a consistent flat, minimal style with rounded corners and pastel color fills, while photographic content is all photorealistic but edited in a soft, muted, pastel color palette, with gentle lighting and minimal contrast, reinforcing the cohesive “soft pastel digital photography tutorial portfolio” aesthetic.

instruction: Planning the generation details for the following website:
input:
Design a soft pastel, multi-column dashboard-style homepage for an online art class tutorial subscription service called “Pastel Studio”. The overall vibe is calm, creative, and slightly whimsical, with gentle gradients, rounded corners, and a modular grid that feels organized but not rigid. The background of the entire page is a pale, almost white lavender (#F8F5FF) with subtle, barely visible watercolor blotches in the corners in pastel peach, mint, and baby blue, giving a hand-painted texture without drawing too much attention. The primary accent colors are soft coral (#FFB3AB), dusty lilac (#C9B0FF), sage green (#BDE3CC), and muted sky blue (#A9D6FF). The primary text color is a very dark gray with a slight purple tint (#2F2936). The main font is a friendly, rounded sans-serif similar to “Nunito” for headings and buttons, with a slightly more neutral sans-serif similar to “Inter” for body text. All module containers have 20px border-radius, faint pastel shadows (blurred, in a slightly darker version of their background color), and generous padding.

At the top, a fixed-height header bar spans full width with a white, slightly translucent background (90% opacity) and a subtle bottom border in pastel lilac. It has a left-aligned logo area, a centered navigation, and a right-aligned user area. On the far left is the logo: a small flat illustration icon of a paint palette in pastel tones (palette body in soft cream, paint blobs in coral, mint, and blue) inside a circle with a thin coral outline, followed by the brand text “Pastel Studio” in a rounded, soft-black font, medium size. The background behind the logo is plain white, making it stand out from the watercolor page background. In the center of the header, evenly spaced across, are three nav links in uppercase, small, letterspaced text: “Browse Classes”, “My Courses”, and “Community”, using the dusty lilac color that turns slightly darker on hover. The currently active link “My Courses” has a small rounded pill-shaped highlight behind it in very light lilac (#EEE6FF). On the right side of the header, there is a circular user avatar (32px) showing a flat illustration of a person with shoulder-length hair in muted blue, wearing round glasses, set on a pastel peach background. Next to it is the username in small text, “Hello, Alex”, in soft black, and to its right a pill-shaped button with the label “Upgrade Plan” in white text on a gradient from coral to peach, with a slight glow and subtle drop shadow.

Below the header, the content sits within a centered, wide container with generous left and right margins. The dashboard uses a three-column feel but with asymmetries: a main central column that is slightly wider, a narrower left column, and an equally narrow right column, all separated by 24px gaps. At the very top of the main content area, spanning all three columns, is a welcome strip that feels like a hero bar but compact in height. This strip has a soft gradient background from very light peach on the left to very light blue on the right, with a wavy top edge (imagined as a subtle irregular curve) and a flat bottom edge. On the left side of this bar, there is a text block: a small label in uppercase pale lilac reading “Today’s Studio Overview”, then a larger, warm headline that says “Welcome back, Alex 👋” (the waving hand is plain text). Below the headline is a single line of body text: “Pick up your brushes where you left off, or explore something new today.” in a medium gray. Under that, two pill-shaped buttons side by side: the primary button “Continue ‘Watercolor Landscapes’” in white text on a coral background with subtle gradient, and a secondary outlined button “Start a New Course” with coral text and a thin coral border on a white background. On the right side of this welcome bar is an illustration panel occupying about one-third of the width. The illustration is a flat, semi-isometric drawing of a tidy artist desk: a sketchbook open in the center with a soft pink page, showing a simple line drawing of a mountain landscape with pastel watercolor wash; a ceramic cup holding brushes and colored pencils in pastel hues sits to the right; a tablet device lies to the left with a faint outline of an art app interface; the background is a minimal room with a large window showing a hint of sunset sky in soft gradients. The edges of the illustration are rounded and the image sits inside a white frame with a slight drop shadow, overlapping slightly into the central column below to introduce intentional asymmetry.

Beneath this welcome strip, the page splits into the three-column grid. The left column starts with a “Today’s Focus” card. This card has a white background with a sage green accent strip along the top edge (8px high) and contains a heading “Today’s Focus” in soft black, left-aligned, medium-large font. Below the heading are three small rectangular tags arranged in a horizontal row with slight gaps. Each tag has a pill shape with soft pastel backgrounds: “Color Theory” on pale yellow, “Layering” on pale mint, and “Textures” on pale lilac, each with small soft-black text and a tiny circular colored dot to the left of the label. Underneath is a short paragraph in smaller gray text: “We’ve curated a few lessons to deepen your understanding of color mixing and texture this week.” At the bottom of this card there is a small text link in coral labeled “View suggested lessons →”, right-aligned. Directly below this card, still in the left column, is a “Quick Tools” module, a grid of simple icon buttons. The card title “Quick Tools” appears at the top, with a subtext line: “Jump into your most-used features.” Below, a 2x3 grid of square tiles with rounded edges, each with a pastel background and a centered monochrome icon. The tiles, from top-left to bottom-right: “Upload Artwork” (icon of a cloud with an up arrow, on a very light blue background); “Reference Library” (icon of a small open book, on pale coral background); “Brush Presets” (icon of a paintbrush tip, on pale mint); “Color Palettes” (icon of a grid of four small squares, on pale lilac); “Downloads” (icon of a downward arrow into a tray, on pale beige); “Support” (icon of a speech bubble, on pale pink). Each tile has its label centered under the icon, in small text. There is a small hover effect envisioned as a slightly darker border and a subtle elevation.

The central, slightly wider column holds the user’s courses. The top card here is titled “Continue Learning” in large, bold soft-black text. To the right of this heading is a small pill label “3 in progress” with a tiny circular badge in coral showing “3” in white. Below, the main body of this card is divided into three stacked course mini-tiles, each with an image thumbnail on the left and text on the right, giving a vertical list. Each course tile is a horizontally oriented rectangle with a white background and a faint colored border strip indicating progress color-coded: the first has a coral strip, the second lilac, the third mint. The first tile shows a thumbnail on the left: an overhead view illustration of a watercolor painting in progress, with blues and greens blending softly on thick paper; a hand holding a brush poised above the paper; a ceramic mixing palette with small pools of color nearby. On the right side of the tile is text: the course title “Watercolor Landscapes for Beginners” in bold; underneath, smaller gray text “Lesson 4 of 12 • 35 min remaining”; below that, a thin rounded progress bar in a very light coral track with a filled segment about 40% across in a richer coral. To the far right inside the tile is a small ghost button outlined in coral labeled “Resume”. The second tile has a thumbnail of a digital tablet on a desk with a stylus drawing a stylized portrait in pastel colors; the title “Digital Portraits in Procreate”; subtext “Lesson 2 of 8 • 18 min remaining”; a lilac progress bar about 20% filled; and an outlined button “Resume”. The third has a thumbnail showing a close-up of hands shaping clay on a pottery wheel in muted earth tones; the title “Clay Sculpting Essentials”; subtext “Lesson 7 of 15 • 42 min remaining”; a mint progress bar about 60% filled; and a “Resume” button in mint outline. Below this card in the central column, separated by generous whitespace, is another module with a slightly different design to break uniformity: a pastel blue background card titled “New for You” aligned left, with slightly larger heading text. Within this card, the top half shows a horizontal row of two feature course cards side-by-side. Each feature card is narrower, with a vertical orientation, a strong hero image, and overlay text at the bottom. The left feature course card image: a flat illustration of a desk filled with sketchbooks, color swatch cards, and a hand painting a small botanical illustration; the overlay at the bottom, on a semi-transparent white band, reads “Botanical Ink & Wash” and beneath, smaller “New • Beginner • 6 lessons”. The right feature image: a stylized abstract canvas with bold pastel strokes and shapes, leaning against a wall with paint tubes scattered nearby; overlay text reads “Pastel Abstracts: Shapes & Color” with subtext “Trending • Intermediate”. Under these two feature cards, still within the “New for You” module, is a small line of text in gray: “Handpicked based on your recent classes.” followed by a right-aligned coral text link “See all recommendations →”.

The right column contains personal and community elements. At the top is a “Today’s Schedule” card with a white background and a thin vertical accent line in pastel blue along the left edge. The heading “Today’s Schedule” appears in soft-black. Beneath it are three horizontal rows representing upcoming sessions or reminders, each row with a subtle colored icon and text. The first row has a tiny circular icon filled with coral, and to its right text “Live Q&A: Watercolor Basics” in slightly bold text, with a second line in smaller gray “Today • 5:00 PM”. The second row has a mint icon, with “Practice: 20-minute sketch” and “Today • 7:30 PM” below. The third has a lilac icon, with “Community critique session” and “Tomorrow • 3:00 PM”. At the bottom of this card is a small, light gray line “All times in your local timezone.” Next, beneath a small gap, is a card titled “Streak & Progress” with a background in a very pale mint. On the left inside this card is a large number in oversized, bold, soft-black text, “7” with the label underneath “days in a row” in smaller gray; on the right side is a stylized circular ring that’s 70% complete, in a gradient of coral and peach, encircling a small icon of a paintbrush. Under the ring is a small note: “Keep going! 3 more days for your next badge.” The card feels balanced and slightly asymmetric, with the circular element aligning more to the top.

Below these in the right column is a “Community Corner” module with a subtle pastel peach background and white inner content box inset to create a card-within-a-card feel. The title line reads “Community Corner” in a friendly, larger font. Inside, there are two small stacked posts from other learners, each with a miniature avatar, text, and a tag. The first mini-post: on the left, a small circular avatar showing a person with short hair, in a soft lilac background; to the right, the name “Mina K.” in medium weight and, under it, “shared new work in ‘Watercolor Landscapes’”; underneath this text, a small rounded tag with border reading “Feedback requested” in coral text. The second mini-post: avatar showing a person with curly hair on a mint background; text “Leo A.” and under it “started ‘Digital Portraits in Procreate’ yesterday”; and a tag “Say hi” in lilac text. At the bottom right of the “Community Corner” card is a small coral link: “Open community →”. The card edges are rounded and there is generous padding inside, with the avatars and text aligned in a tidy vertical stack.

Throughout the page, subtle dividing lines between sections use a very faint, almost invisible lavender tint, and modules are separated by consistent vertical margin spacing. The bottom of the page features a narrow footer bar aligned with the main content width rather than full bleed, with lighter gray text aligned left reading “© 2025 Pastel Studio. All rights reserved.” and, on the right side, three small text links spaced evenly: “Privacy Policy”, “Terms”, and “Help”, all in muted lilac that darkens on hover. The overall feeling is calm, approachable, and artful: soft pastel blocks of content floating on a light lavender canvas, organized into a three-column grid but with occasional overlapping elements and varied card shapes to keep the design lively yet harmonious.

instruction: Planning the generation details for the following website:
input: Design a bold, brutalist-style homepage for a luxury watch retailer called “IRON SECOND” that feels like an unapologetically graphic dashboard rather than a traditional store. The entire page sits on a stark, flat background of pure #f5f5f5 off-white, with thick black (#000000) borders around almost every block, creating a rigid multi-column grid that still allows for asymmetry and overlapping layers. The primary typeface throughout is a grotesque sans serif reminiscent of Helvetica or Neue Haas Grotesk, always in uppercase for major elements; headings are extra-bold, often set at very large sizes (48–72px), and body copy is set at 14–16px, all in pure black or a deep charcoal #111111. No gradients, no shadows, no rounded corners, and absolutely minimal decorative frills; the aesthetic is harsh, print-like, and poster-esque. At the very top, a brutalist header bar stretches full width with a solid black background. Inside this bar, aligned left with generous horizontal padding, the brand name appears huge: “IRON SECOND” in white, ultra-bold, all caps, at about 40px, sitting flush against the top with almost no vertical padding, giving the feeling of an oversized magazine masthead. To the right, there is a raw-feeling navigation composed of plain text links in white, all caps, tightly spaced but separated by thin vertical white lines: “COLLECTIONS | RARITIES | ABOUT | JOURNAL | SUPPORT”. All links sit baseline-aligned, no hover states indicated, and feel almost like menu entries in an old terminal. In the far right corner of the header, there is a small rectangular button outlined in white with no fill that reads “CART [0]” in small uppercase text, slightly misaligned vertically to emphasize the brutalist, imperfect grid aesthetic. Directly beneath the header, the page is divided into a two-column hero layout: a wide left column occupying roughly 65% of the width and a narrower right column taking about 35%. The left column is dominated by a massive hero block that feels like a billboard. It has a thick black border and a white background, with an oversized text headline positioned slightly off-center, not perfectly vertically aligned: “TIME IS A LUXURY. WASTE IT DELIBERATELY.” in huge, all-caps, bold black letters, with each line manually broken so that “TIME IS A LUXURY.” is on top and “WASTE IT DELIBERATELY.” sits below, slightly indented. Beneath the headline, aligned left, is a harsh, compact subheading in small uppercase text: “BRUTALIST TIMEPIECES FOR OBSESSIVES, COLLECTORS, AND INSOMNIACS.” Below that, still inside this left hero block, is a very simple rectangular “SHOP THE GRID” button: black fill, white uppercase text, centered horizontally, no hover; the button is tall and chunky, with hard 90-degree corners and a thin inset border line, and it is separated from the text above by an obvious gap of white space. On the right side of the hero section, the narrower column is vertically split into two stacked blocks with equal height, each with thick black borders and off-white backgrounds. The upper block is labeled at the top left with a tiny all-caps tag “FEATURED WATCH” in a brutalist, almost mechanical font at 10px. Below this label, occupying most of the block, is a static product image of a luxury watch shot in a stark, high-contrast, photorealistic style. The watch is centered in the frame on a flat, almost clinical light-gray background; it is a stainless-steel, minimalist watch with a brushed metal bezel, a matte black dial, and thin stick hour markers in a pale silver; there is no logo visible on the dial, reinforcing the brutalist minimalism. The watch is photographed straight-on at eye level, with sharp, crisp lighting that creates a harsh reflection on the crystal and solid, sharp-edged shadows beneath the lugs. At the bottom of this block, aligned left, is a small bold text line: “MODEL: VOID-01 / €8,900” with the price in the same size and weight as the model name, emphasizing bluntness over hierarchy. Beneath this, smaller text reads “Hand-wound. No complications. No compromise.” in simple sentence case, but still in the same grotesque typeface. The lower block, labeled “WAITLIST STATUS” at the top left in the same small uppercase style, is an intentionally raw grid of text rows separated by hairline black rules. Each row is a single line in monospaced-looking uppercase text: “VOID-01 — SOLD OUT”, “OBSIDIAN-24 — LIMITED”, “SPECTRAL GMT — OPEN”, stacked vertically with equal spacing. The words “SOLD OUT”, “LIMITED”, and “OPEN” are all aligned in a right-hand column, creating a utilitarian table-like feeling. There are no colors other than black and white in this block; the hierarchy is created purely by spacing and thickness of rules. Below this hero region, the layout transitions into a three-column grid that feels like a dashboard of modules. Each module is a rectangle with thick black borders and varying heights, like tiles in a control panel. In the left column, at the top, is a block titled “TODAY’S DROP” in all caps, aligned left, at about 24px. Beneath this title is a vertical strip image: a close-up, photorealistic photograph of a watch crown and bezel. The camera is very close, capturing the ridged metal texture of the crown in sharp detail, with strong directional lighting casting deep shadows between the ridges. The background is out-of-focus dark gray, so the chrome highlights on the crown stand out as bright white glints. Under the image, there’s a list of three text lines, each in uppercase, styled like an announcement board: “LAUNCH: 19:00 GMT”, “UNITS: 25”, “RULE: ONE PER WRIST”. The middle column at this level contains a “COLLECTION INDEX” module that plays like a raw table of contents. Inside, the top row is a header with “BRUTAL COLLECTIONS” printed at the left and, right-aligned, “VIEW ALL →” in a slightly smaller but still uppercase font. Below, the space is divided into three horizontal bands, each with a left-aligned collection name in large uppercase, and right-aligned details. The first band reads “MONOCHROME” on the left, with “12 MODELS” on the right; the background of this band is a very pale gray (#eaeaea), while the others remain white. The second band reads “NIGHTSHIFT” with “8 MODELS”, and the third “DISCONTINUED” with “ARCHIVE ONLY”. Thin black lines separate the bands. In the right column, at the same horizontal level, is a tall module titled “SHOP RULES” in large uppercase, a nod to old-school manifestos. Inside is a block of text broken into short, blunt sentences, each line separated by extra leading: “NO FLASH SALES.”, “NO DISCOUNTS.”, “NO INFLUENCER CODES.”, “NO ARTIFICIAL SCARCITY.”. The text is set large, almost like a poster pasted on the wall, and is centered both vertically and horizontally, with no imagery—just black on off-white. Further down, the grid continues with asymmetry: the left column now contains a wider module that spans both the left and middle column widths (a deliberate disruption of the neat three-column grid), labeled “MATERIALS & BUILD” at the top left. Inside, the content is arranged in two side-by-side subcolumns that look like a technical spec sheet. The left subcolumn is titled “CASE & DIAL” and lists terse bullet-like lines without actual bullet glyphs: “316L STEEL ONLY”, “NO PLATING”, “DOUBLE-DOMED SAPPHIRE”, “LUME: MINIMAL”. The right subcolumn is titled “MOVEMENT” with lines: “SWISS MECHANICAL”, “NO BATTERIES”, “SERVICEABLE FOR DECADES”, “ADJUSTED BY HAND”. All text is uppercase, with small caps used for the labels and slightly larger for each spec line. The rightmost column at this level is reserved for a “CUSTOMERS ARE NOT ALWAYS RIGHT” module, that feels like a provocative manifesto box. It contains a single block of paragraph text, left-aligned, small (about 13–14px), in sentence case rather than uppercase, explaining the brand philosophy: “We design for those who care more about torque than trends. If you need a logo to feel something, this is not your store.” The text sits surrounded by ample white space, emphasizing the brutality of the brand voice. Beneath these, another row of modular tiles spans the width in three roughly equal columns. The left tile, titled “SIGN-UP FORM”, is a contact-style block with a very raw, form-like layout: two input fields stacked vertically, both full-width with thick black borders and white fill, labeled in small uppercase text above each: “EMAIL ADDRESS” and “COUNTRY”. Inside each field, a placeholder in ghosted light gray uppercase text reads “TYPE HERE” aligned left. Beneath these fields is a checkbox with a square, black-outlined box and to its right the label in uppercase: “I ACCEPT SILENCE — NO WEEKLY SPAM.” A chunky black rectangular button centered below reads “JOIN THE WAITLIST” in white uppercase. The middle tile is an “OUTSIDE OPINIONS” section featuring two short text testimonials displayed like printed clippings. Each testimonial is a separate sub-box with a thin black border inside the tile, containing a line such as: ““Violently minimal.” — Horology Quarterly” and ““Feels like a watch made of concrete and intent.” — Obscure Objects Review”. The type is all uppercase, tightly tracked, with the publication names in slightly smaller size. The right tile is labeled “STORE HOURS / CONTACT” and displays a split layout: on the left side, a vertical list of hours: “STUDIO HOURS (BERLIN)”, then on their own lines “MON–FRI: 11:00–19:00”, “SAT: 12:00–18:00”, “SUN: CLOSED”, all in uppercase sans-serif. On the right side of this tile is a tiny, intentionally crude map placeholder: a solid white rectangle with a thick black border, with only a simple black crosshair icon in the center and “BERLIN” printed underneath it, as if it were a stripped-down coordinate map. At the very bottom of the page, a full-width footer bar in pure black echoes the header, but reversed in hierarchy. It is divided into three visual zones separated by thin vertical white lines. The left zone contains in small white uppercase text: “© IRON SECOND. BUILT TO OUTLAST YOU.” The middle zone lists three plain text links stacked vertically, aligned left: “TERMS”, “PRIVACY”, “RETURNS”, all in white uppercase with no icons or embellishments. The right zone has bold uppercase text that reads “DO NOT FOLLOW US” then, beneath it in smaller white text: “NO SOCIAL MEDIA. NO NEWSLETTER ARCHIVE.” The entire page feels like a cold, brutally functional watch dashboard—gridded, harsh, monochrome, using collaged blocks of text and photorealistic images of watches to create a raw but luxurious impression, with no video, no animations, and no charts or data visualizations anywhere, only static rectangular modules, dense typography, and stark product photography.

instruction: Planning the generation details for the following website:
input:
Design a single-page futuristic dashboard homepage for an independent sci‑fi bookstore tutorial subscription service, combining the feel of a control center and a sleek shop landing page. The aesthetic should be high-tech yet warm, using a dark, desaturated backdrop with vivid neon accents. The entire page is viewed on a large desktop screen with generous horizontal space. The background is a deep charcoal gradient, going from almost‑black (#05060A) at the top to a slightly lighter graphite (#151821) toward the bottom, with subtle, semi-transparent hexagonal and circuit-trace patterns drifting diagonally from the top-left to bottom-right. These patterns are soft and low contrast, as if etched into glass, giving the impression of a holographic interface without any movement. Tiny, static specks of light resembling distant stars are scattered sparingly across the background, concentrated more toward the upper corners, all in cool blues and teals.

At the very top is a narrow, semi-transparent glassmorphism-style navigation bar fixed to the viewport, stretching full width. It has a slightly frosted appearance, with a blurred background effect and a thin, luminous cyan border along its bottom edge. On the far left, there is a compact logo: a flat, minimalist icon of an open book whose pages form a subtle circuit pattern, colored in a neon teal (#27F3D9) with a faint inner glow. To the right of the icon, the brand name appears in all caps: “NEBULA STACK” in a condensed, geometric sans-serif font, light-weight, pure white, letter-spaced, about 24px. Navigation links are arranged horizontally in the center: “Dashboard”, “Library”, “Tutorials”, “Collections”, “Support”. Each link is in small caps, 14px, light gray (#B0B6C4) with generous letter spacing, and when hovered they would be indicated by a subtle, static visual change: the text becomes white and gains a thin cyan underscore running the full width of the word, but in the design there is no motion—only the hovered state is visible as a static example on the word “Dashboard”. On the far right of the navbar, two pill-shaped buttons overlap slightly: a smaller outline button “Sign In” and a prominent filled button “Start Free Trial”. “Sign In” has a transparent interior with a thin lilac outline (#C66BFF) and lilac text; “Start Free Trial” is filled with a vertical gradient from electric purple (#9B5CFF) at the top to vibrant magenta (#F54FA4) at the bottom, with white text at 14px. Both buttons have soft drop shadows that glow faintly to accentuate the futuristic style.

Below the navigation, the hero section occupies approximately the top 45% of the page height, with an intentionally asymmetrical layout. On the left two-thirds is the main headline and subscription explanation; on the right one-third is a large, floating card representing the user’s “Current Mission”. The left area has a subtle vertical division: the headline block sits upper left, while a smaller overlapping status chip sits slightly above and to the right of the headline block, partly intruding into the right-hand “Current Mission” card area, creating the hierarchical overlapping feel. The primary headline reads: “Curate Your Own Sci‑Fi Saga.” in large, bold, modern sans-serif font (around 46px), colored in off‑white (#F4F6FB) with a slight outer glow. Beneath it, a subheading in two lines of smaller, 18px text reads: “A futuristic dashboard for independent sci‑fi book lovers. Track your reading, unlock narrative-building tutorials, and chart your journey across the literary cosmos.” This text is left-aligned, light gray, and constrained to about 60% of the width of the left column. Under the subheading, there is a horizontal row of two primary call-to-action buttons: a large gradient button labeled “Explore Tutorials” and a secondary outlined button labeled “Browse Bookstore”. The “Explore Tutorials” button uses the same purple-to-magenta gradient as the navbar’s trial button, with a subtle, static inner glow, white text at 16px, and slightly rounded corners. The “Browse Bookstore” button has a thin cyan border, transparent interior, cyan text, and a small inline icon of a simple line-art book, also cyan, positioned to the left of the text. Below these buttons, a short reassurance line in a smaller 13px font reads: “No ads. Cancel anytime. Tutorials led by independent authors.” in muted gray.

Slightly overlapping the top-right corner of the headline block is a small, rectangular status chip that looks like a holographic tag. The chip is semi-transparent teal with a soft blur, rounded corners, and a thin white border. Inside, in condensed white text at 12px, it says: “CURRENT MOOD: NEON NOIR” on the first line and “Suggested reading path updated 3 hours ago.” in smaller gray text beneath. A tiny circular dot in bright neon green appears to the left of “CURRENT MOOD”, implying an active status.

To the right of the hero text, occupying roughly one-third of the width, is the “Current Mission” card. This card appears to float above the background with a pronounced drop shadow and a subtle cyan outer glow. It is taller than it is wide, with slightly rounded corners, and has a layered composition: a stylized hero image at the top and textual details below. The top half of the card contains a static, illustrated scene: a flat yet dimensionally shaded sci‑fi cityscape at night viewed from an elevated balcony, in a semi-realistic digital illustration style. The foreground shows the silhouette of a figure reading a book, their back turned to the viewer, sitting at a low table with scattered glowing book spines. The midground has sleek, angular towers and holographic billboards in neon blues, magentas, and violets; some billboards display abstract book cover shapes and illegible futuristic glyphs. The background recedes into a hazy skyline under a deep indigo sky with two crescent moons. Light sources from the billboards cast subtle reflections on metallic surfaces, with sharp edges and crisp lines. Below this image, within the card, text reads in small uppercase teal letters: “CURRENT MISSION” followed by the larger title “Decode the Neon Archives” in 20px white text. A short description below in muted gray says: “A four-part reading tutorial guiding you through cyberpunk classics and indie zines.” At the bottom of the card, two pill-shaped labels sit side by side: one says “Progress: 2 / 4 Chapters” with a thin magenta outline and magenta text; the other says “Difficulty: INTERMEDIATE” with a cyan outline and text. The card’s background is a very dark navy with subtle diagonal line textures.

Directly below the hero area, spanning almost the full width, is a stacked, overlapping band of three large horizontal cards arranged in a slightly staggered fashion, as if they were physical panels fanned out. The central card is in front, larger, and aligned a bit higher; the left and right cards sit slightly behind, peeking out. This band is labeled in a small label at the upper-left corner: “Today’s Priority Streams” in uppercase, white, 12px font. The central card, slightly overlapping the bottom edge of the hero section, is a “Reading Queue” card with a dark, semi-transparent background and a subtle cyan line running along its left edge. Its title, “Reading Queue”, appears in white sans-serif text at 18px, with a small icon of stacked rectangles (representing books) to the left. Below the title, three horizontal strips list currently queued books. Each strip contains: a tiny square thumbnail on the left showing a stylized book cover (each cover featuring abstract geometric shapes in neon colors and bold diagonal lines), the book title in white, a small label “Tutorial Linked” or “Standalone” in tiny uppercase colored text, and a right-aligned tag indicating estimated reading time like “3h 20m”, “5h 10m”, in muted cyan. The strips are separated by thin, faint horizontal divider lines. The left overlapping card is titled “Author Signals” with pale violet highlights, featuring short, two-line messages from authors in a sleek sans-serif typeface, each prefaced by a small circular avatar (line-art style portraits, minimal detail, flat colors) and a slim vertical accent line in lilac. The right overlapping card is “Upcoming Drops” with a magenta accent; it uses a grid of three narrow rows, each row showing a small book cover thumbnail with a futuristic, illustrated design (for example, a lonely spaceship above a ringed planet, or a figure standing in a glowing forest), the release date in tiny caps, and a tag like “Exclusive Tutorial Included”. The staggered arrangement creates natural asymmetry: the left card moves slightly lower than the right, and the central card slightly higher, all with soft shadows overlapping each other.

Beneath this overlapping band, the page divides into two main columns extending most of the remaining vertical space. The left column is wider and focuses on tutorials and learning paths; the right column is narrower and focuses on shop discovery and user profile. In the left column, the first section is titled “Tutorial Tracks” in a medium-weight, 24px white font. Under the title, a thin cyan underline extends partway across.
