This section contains a hierarchical grid of three large “track” cards arranged asymmetrically: two cards on the top row and one full-width card below, offset so that the bottom card slightly overlaps the lower edges of the top two.

The top-left track card, labeled “Galactic Beginner Path”, has a dark slate background with a faint starfield texture and a thin, glowing teal border. At its top, a small tag “LEVEL 01” appears in a bright green rectangle, with white, pixel-like text. The card’s hero image is a stylized illustration of a young reader floating in zero gravity in a small capsule filled with floating books, all rendered in semi-flat style with smooth gradients, glowing edges, and simple but expressive line work. The capsule window shows a swirling galaxy outside. Below the image, text describes the track: “Build your foundation with accessible sci‑fi classics and narrative design basics.” Three bullet-like labels, rendered as horizontal capsules without bullet icons, say: “Worldbuilding 101”, “Character Archetypes”, “Reading Rituals”. Each capsule is outlined in light cyan with transparent center. A “View Track” button sits in the bottom-right corner of this card, small, with a cyan border and white text.

The top-right track card, “Temporal Story Architect”, is slightly taller and narrower. It uses a gradient border from magenta to violet and a dark navy background with a grid overlay pattern reminiscent of a holographic schematic. Its hero image is a flat, isometric-style representation of a time circuit diagram shaped like a circular bookshelf, with books arranged along the circumference and glowing arcs connecting them. The text beneath reads: “Learn how to rearrange timelines, flashbacks, and alternate universes like a pro.” At the bottom of this card, three small rectangular tags appear, filled with muted colors: “Intermediate”, “Time Travel”, “Nonlinear Plots”. The tags are aligned left and spaced evenly. The card slightly overlaps the right edge of the “Galactic Beginner Path” card, emphasizing layering.

The bottom full-width card, “Deep Space Narrative Lab”, spans the entire width of the column and overlaps the lower edges of both upper cards by a few pixels. It has a dark purple-gray background and a more elaborate illustrated scene across its top half: a lab-like chamber filled with transparent, floating panels, each showing stylized book covers and lines of code-like text, all in neon outlines and soft glows. Two silhouetted figures stand at consoles, their bodies framed by arcs of light. The perspective is slightly off-center, adding dynamic tension. Text below reads: “Experimental tutorials for advanced readers: narrative hacking, fragmented storytelling, and interactive fiction design.” On the right side of this card is a vertical column of three micro-panels labeled “Module 01”, “Module 02”, and “Module 03”, each micro-panel being a dark rectangle with a small icon (a broken circle, a branching path, a tilted cube) and a short label such as “Fractured Timelines”, “Second-Person Experiments”, “Unreliable Narrators”. Each micro-panel is separated by thin, glowing lines.

Further down the left column is a “Reading Environment Controls” section, resembling a dashboard control panel. The title, in 18px all caps, reads: “Reading Environment Controls” with small subtitle text beneath: “Fine‑tune your experience across devices.” The controls are presented as a series of horizontal toggle-like segments and sliders arranged in a two-column grid. One row shows “Theme” on the left with a small static visual representation of two miniature screens: one dark with neon text, one light with warm gray text. The dark theme miniature is slightly larger and has a thin teal outline indicating selection. Another row shows “Focus Mode” with two pill buttons: “Standard” (outlined) and “Immersive” (filled with teal), demonstrating that “Immersive” is selected. A third row shows “Annotation Layer” with three small square buttons labelled “Off”, “Personal”, “Community”. “Personal” is highlighted in magenta, while the others are outline-only. Each row has a small, thin vertical divider line between labels and controls. All text here is tiny (12px–13px), clean, and white or light gray.

The right column begins near the overlapping “Today’s Priority Streams” band and runs downward. At the top of this column is a compact “Profile Snapshot” card that appears as a small, floating panel. It has a dark background with a thin cyan border and a subtle drop shadow. On its left is a circular profile avatar image: a stylized digital illustration of a person with short hair wearing a pair of thin, neon-outlined reading glasses, and a faint holographic interface reflected in their lenses; the style is clean, semi-flat, with soft shading. To the right of the avatar, the username appears in white text: “ASTRA_READER” with a smaller label beneath in muted gray: “Member since 2029”. Below this, three small data chips show key stats: “Books in Orbit: 27”, “Tutorials in Progress: 3”, and “Reading Streak: 12 days”. Each chip is a small rectangle with dark background, rounded corners, and a thin, colored border (cyan, magenta, and violet respectively).

Directly below the “Profile Snapshot” is a vertical “Collections” section. The title “Curated Collections” appears at 18px, white, with a short subtitle: “Navigate curated shelves by mood and theme.” Two large collection cards are stacked with slight overlap, offset horizontally to increase the sense of layering. The top card, “Outer Rim Mysteries”, is a wide rectangle with an illustrated background: a silhouette of a space station orbiting a distant planet, with a cluster of small, glowing windows and a ring of faint blue light around the station; the style is atmospheric and soft, leaning toward painterly but still simplified. Text overlays this in off-white: “Outer Rim Mysteries” in 20px, and below it a line saying: “Noir investigations at the edge of known space.” In the bottom-right corner of the card is a small, white chevron icon (>) indicating it links deeper. The second collection card, slightly beneath and shifted to the right, is titled “Solarpunk Sanctuaries”. Its background illustration shows a green, terraced city with trees growing out of buildings, and fiber-optic-like light strands connecting rooftops; colors are fresh greens, golds, and soft blues with flat yet softly shaded style. The text overlay reads: “Solarpunk Sanctuaries” and “Hopeful futures rooted in ecology and community.” A thin, glowing border in soft green frames this card.

Continuing down, the right column features a “Bookstore Highlights” strip. The title reads: “Bookstore Highlights” in crisp white text. Beneath it is a horizontal row of three small, compressed cards representing individual featured books. Each card is a small rectangle with a book cover illustration dominating the upper portion (for example, one cover might show a lone figure standing at the edge of a crystalline canyon under twin suns; another might depict a retro-style starship launch; another a city suspended above a black hole). The bottom portion of each card shows the title in white (e.g., “Edge of the Crystal Void”), the author in smaller gray, and a tiny label such as “New”, “Staff Pick”, or “Indie Exclusive” in a sharp-edged pill. The cards are separated by generous spacing and share a faint glow. This section is aligned flush left with the column and leaves some empty space on the right for breathable layout.

Toward the bottom right is a narrow “Support & Community” panel. This panel is a tall, slim card with a lilac border. At the top, a small heading reads: “Need a co-pilot?” in friendly, italicized text, followed by “Support & Community Hub” in small caps. Inside are three horizontally separated rows. Row one: a small icon of a headset with a minimal microphone, outlined in cyan, next to the label “Live Help (beta)” in white, with a tiny descriptor in gray: “Connect with a human within 2 hours.” Row two: a small icon of a group of three abstract silhouettes in violet, next to “Community Shelf Swaps” and descriptor “Trade recs with other readers.” Row three: a simple question mark icon in a circle, next to “FAQ & Onboarding Tutorials”. Each row has a thin dividing line between it and the next.

At the very bottom of the page, a full-width footer spans edge to edge, but visually it appears as a slightly darker band of the same palette, with a top border that glows faint teal. The footer is divided into three columns. The left column repeats the logo icon in miniature and the text “NEBULA STACK” beneath it, followed by a short, tiny, gray paragraph: “Nebula Stack is an independent sci‑fi bookstore and tutorial platform, powered by authors and readers—not algorithms alone.” The center column contains small white footer links, stacked vertically: “About”, “Press”, “Careers”, “Privacy Policy”, “Terms”. The right column has a tiny label “Stay in orbit” and a simple email input box with placeholder text “Enter your email for cosmic dispatches” in light gray, followed by a small rectangular button labeled “Subscribe” in white text on a cyan background. Below the three columns, centered, a very small line of text in muted gray reads: “© 2029 Nebula Stack. All rights reserved.” Every section is static, with no motion implied anywhere; all visual effects such as glows, gradients, and blurs are purely stylistic, not animated. Throughout the page, typography is consistent: a modern, geometric sans-serif for headings, a clean, slightly condensed sans-serif for body text, and monospaced digits for stats and labels, all aligned with precise spacing and careful use of whitespace so that despite the overlapping and hierarchical layering, the layout feels carefully orchestrated and distinctly futuristic.

instruction: Planning the generation details for the following website:
input:
Design a vertically scrollable event homepage for an online art class tutorial subscription called “NOISY LINES // 48-HOUR LIVE LAB”, rendered in a unapologetically brutalist aesthetic. The page should look like a deliberately chaotic yet precisely aligned print poster translated to web: harsh contrasts, visible grids, clashing colors, big typography, sharp rectangles, and almost no rounded corners. The background of the entire page is a solid, slightly off-white color (“#F5F2E8”) that feels like cheap paper. The base font is a monospace typeface that looks like a system terminal font, used for body text and smaller labels, while headlines use a grotesque sans-serif in uppercase with heavy weights. Many elements use bright, flat colors: neon yellow, electric blue, pure red, black, and grayscale. The layout throughout is a multi-column grid—roughly a 12-column structure—where sections often use asymmetric combinations of 2, 3, or 4 columns, with some elements spanning unusual widths to emphasize brutalist irregularity, but alignment lines are still sharply respected.

At the very top, a fixed-height header bar spans full width and is about 90px tall. It has a flat neon yellow background (“#FFF700”) with a thin 2px black bottom border. On the far left is the event wordmark: text in all caps reading “NOISY LINES // LIVE LAB” in black, using a tall condensed sans-serif, sized very large so it almost touches the top and bottom edges. Directly under this title, in much smaller monospace text stacked in two lines, appears “ONLINE ART CLASS TUTORIAL SUBSCRIPTION” and then “48-HOUR IMMERSIVE EVENT”, both aligned left. On the far right, laid out horizontally, are three brutally plain navigation links: “SCHEDULE”, “INSTRUCTORS”, “SIGN UP”. Each is in uppercase, black monospace, spaced far apart, and each is enclosed in a thin rectangular outline that looks like a rough wireframe box. When a nav item is hovered (describe the static state, but visually indicate this): its outline is filled pure black with neon yellow text, so one of them (for example, “SIGN UP”) is displayed in that “active” appearance to highlight it. Beneath the header, but not fixed, is a narrow black strip (20px tall) that acts like a ticker but is static: on the left in tiny white monospace text is “NEXT COHORT: APRIL 26–28, 2026”, and on the right, aligned, “STREAMING WORLDWIDE // LIMITED SLOTS”.

Below this, the hero section consumes the full width, with a height equal to approximately one viewport. It is structured as a multi-column grid with an intentional asymmetry. On the left two-thirds, there is a massive headline block, with a background panel in pure white over the off-white page, giving a slight “paper-on-paper” layered effect. The headline reads: “DRAW BAD, FEEL GOOD.” in extremely large, uppercase sans-serif, pure black, left-aligned, with a line-height that makes the words stack tightly. Under it in smaller text is a subheading: “A 48-hour brutalist art bootcamp for people who want to unlearn perfection.” in black monospace, all lowercase, left aligned. Below that is a blocky call-to-action area: two rectangular buttons side by side but slightly misaligned vertically. The first button says “GET A PASS — $89” in uppercase, bold monospace, black text on a flat electric blue rectangle (“#0066FF”), with a thick black border and sharp corners. The second, smaller rectangle sitting a little lower says “VIEW SCHEDULE” in uppercase black text on a white rectangle with a black border. To the right of these buttons, but overlapping slightly, is a small square label on a red background: “EARLY ACCESS ENDS MARCH 31” in tight white monospace. Below the CTA, aligned left, are three short bullet-like lines, but instead of bullets they are prefixed with “//”: “// LIVE CRITIQUES WITH REAL ARTISTS”, “// ON-DEMAND TUTORIAL ARCHIVE”, “// DOWNLOADABLE CHAOS WORKSHEETS” in black monospace.

On the right third of the hero, spanning the full height of the hero section, sits a large rectangular hero image bordered in solid black with no drop shadow. This image is a digital collage in flat, brutalist vector style: a top-down view of a cluttered artist’s desk rendered in bold blocks of color, almost like a printmaking poster. The background of the image is a pale cyan field. Scattered around are geometric representations of art tools: a thick black marker, a red pencil, a neon yellow ruler, a blue paintbrush, and sheets of paper. The papers are off-kilter rectangles in white and beige, with black squiggly lines, false UI frames, and abstract faces drawn in rough, jagged strokes. On one sheet is a simplistic line drawing of a face, one eye bigger than the other, nose crooked, mouth a zigzag. Shadows are depicted as sharp, solid-color shapes in dark gray, creating a harsh, graphic feel. Overlaid on top of this collage is a black rectangular label overlapping the lower-left corner of the image, containing white uppercase text: “NO POLISHED PORTFOLIOS. JUST PROCESS.” The whole image feels flat, with no gradients, mimicking screenprinted posters.

Immediately beneath the hero, there is a horizontally spanning strip that functions as a brutalist “event meta info” grid. The background is pure black. This strip is divided into four equal vertical columns. Each column has a label in small uppercase neon yellow monospace at the top, and a large white number or phrase below in the same monospace font but enlarged. The columns show: “DURATION” with “48 HRS”; “SESSIONS” with “16 LIVE TUTORIALS”; “TIME ZONES” with “5 MAJOR CITIES”; and “REPLAYS” with “30 DAYS ACCESS”. Thin vertical white divider lines separate each column. The text is center-aligned within each column, and the whole strip feels like a rigid data table.

The next section introduces the event concept and sits within a multi-column layout that uses the 12-column grid more explicitly. The section heading at the top left in small uppercase black monospace reads “ABOUT THE EVENT” inside a thin outlined box. Underneath, spanning approximately 7 of 12 columns on the left, there is a block of body text. The heading above this block says “WHY BRUTALIST ART FOR YOUR BRAIN.” in bold uppercase sans-serif, black, slightly smaller than the hero headline. Below is 3–4 paragraphs of lorem-like descriptive text in lowercase monospace, left aligned, black, explaining that the event is a rough, hands-on art lab where participants are encouraged to reject aesthetic perfection and focus on process, mistakes, and experimentation. The lines are spaced tightly, and there are seemingly arbitrary line breaks to mimic typewritten text. On the right side of this section, taking up the remaining 5 columns, is a vertically stacked set of three narrow, full-width rectangles that look like “post-it notes” pinned to the layout. Each rectangle is a different flat color: top is neon yellow, middle is bright red, bottom is electric blue. Each has a small label in black uppercase monospace at the top left, like “WHO IT’S FOR”, “WHAT YOU’LL DO”, “WHAT YOU’LL GET”, followed by short, punchy bullet-like lines preceded by double slashes “//” describing target participants (e.g., “// overthinkers with dusty sketchbooks”), activities (e.g., “// timed anti-perfection drills”), and benefits (e.g., “// messy digital portfolio”).

Following this is a section that lays out the “CONTENT TRACKS” in a multi-column brutalist grid. The section background remains the off-white paper tone, but there are bold black horizontal rules at the top and bottom of the section. The heading is centered at the top in uppercase sans-serif: “CHOOSE YOUR CHAOS TRACK.” Directly beneath, a three-column layout displays three tall, narrow cards, each representing a track. Each card is a flat rectangle with a different color background (left: solid white, center: neon green, right: pale pink) with thick black borders and a title at the top in large uppercase black text: “TRACK A // LINE”, “TRACK B // COLOR”, “TRACK C // COLLAGE”. Inside each card, the layout is vertically stacked: a small black-and-white icon at the top (for LINE, an abstract hand holding a thick pen drawing a jagged line; for COLOR, a blocky paint palette with blobs in neon colors; for COLLAGE, overlapping rectangles and circles). The icons are flat, minimal, strongly outlined in black. Below, each card has a list of 4 session titles in uppercase monospace with rough numbering like “01: HYPER-SPEED SKETCHING”, “02: WRONG-HANDED DRAWING”, etc. At the bottom of each card is a tiny label “SKILL LEVEL” followed by a crude black horizontal bar that is partially filled with white segments, representing difficulty, reminiscent of an old loading bar, with no gradients.

Below the tracks, the “LIVE SCHEDULE SNAPSHOT” section appears, which also includes a data-driven visualization. This section has a stark, pure white background and is enclosed within a thick, double-lined black border that spans nearly full width but with generous left and right margins. At the top left inside the bordered box is the title “LIVE SCHEDULE SNAPSHOT (UTC)” in uppercase monospace, with a small black square bullet preceding it. The section is split into two main columns: on the left, a simplified timetable; on the right, a minimal horizontal bar chart visualizing enrollment interest per session. On the left half, the timetable is a rigid grid table: seven rows corresponding to sessions and three columns: “SESSION”, “TIME (UTC)”, “FOCUS”. Borders are solid black, no shading. The session labels are uppercase like “SESSION 1”, “SESSION 2”, and times like “10:00”, “13:00”, etc., with focus labels like “LINE DRILLS”, “COLOR CHAOS”, “COLLAGE RIOT”. On the right half, the enrollment interest chart is rendered as five horizontal bars labeled with session names and numeric values. Bars are thick black outlines with a colored fill—the first is neon yellow, then electric blue, then red, then green, then pure black representing highest interest. Each bar stretches proportionally across a fixed horizontal scale, with a small numeric label at the right end, like “120”, “95”, and so on. Axes are minimal: a vertical list of session titles on the left in tiny uppercase monospace, and a faint horizontal line at the baseline. Above the chart, a tiny caption in grey monospace reads “projected seat claims per live session”.

Continue with the “INSTRUCTORS” section next. This section intentionally breaks the grid slightly: its background is a large, slightly darker beige rectangle that spans the center of the page, leaving off-white margins at left and right. At the very top left of this rectangle is a title “INSTRUCTORS WHO BREAK THEIR OWN RULES.” in uppercase sans-serif. Below, the grid divides into three columns, each containing a brutalist profile card. Each instructor card is a sharply rectangular box with a heavy black border and no internal padding except a few pixels. The top half of each card is a black-and-white photorealistic portrait image, cropped to show shoulders and head, but overlaid with graphic interventions: bright, flat colored blocks covering eyes, mouth, or backgrounds. For example, the first instructor: a young person with short shaved hair, front-facing, neutral expression, the background is plain light gray; a thick neon yellow bar covers their eyes horizontally, and a red square overlaps the bottom right of the image. The second: an older person with curly hair, three-quarters view, a blue triangle overlays the top left; the third: a person with long hair, high-contrast lighting, with a jagged white outline drawn around their profile. All portraits are grayscale underneath these color blocks, with sharp, high contrast lighting and clear shadows. Below each image, in all caps monospace is the instructor’s name, such as “AVA / LINE AGITATOR”, “ROXY / COLOR SABOTEUR”, “MIN / COLLAGE ENGINEER”. Below the names are 3-line mini bios in lowercase monospace, densely packed and slightly misaligned to look like typewritten notes. At the far right of this section, spanning the height of the three cards, is a vertical oversized black text rotated 90 degrees reading “NO HEROES. JUST GUIDES.” in white uppercase letters.

Next, a “WHAT YOU GET” section arranges benefits in a more freeform brutalist grid. The page returns to the off-white background. The top has a thin, long black rule across the entire width. Below, aligned to the left, is a smaller heading inside a black rectangle that reads “YOUR SUBSCRIPTION PASS INCLUDES:” in white uppercase monospace. Down the page, there is a three-by-two grid of modules, but with some modules merged: it feels like someone cut and rearranged a newspaper layout. Each module is a different background: simple solid colors or white, each with thick black borders. The content: text-only blocks like “UNLIMITED REPLAYS FOR 30 DAYS”, “LIFETIME ACCESS TO 40+ RECORDED BRUTALIST TUTORIALS”, “DOWNLOADABLE EXERCISE PDFs”, “PRIVATE DISCORD CHAOS ROOM” etc., all in uppercase sans-serif or monospace. Some modules have small line icons: an icon of a stack of papers, a cloud with a downward arrow, a chat bubble with an exclamation mark. The style is flat, stark, with icon shapes made from simple thick black lines. One block has a background in pure black with neon yellow text reading “NO REFUNDS. NO REGRETS.” in extremely bold uppercase, centered both ways.

Towards the bottom, create a raw, transactional “SIGN UP NOW” section that looks like an ugly but powerful form. This section has a pure red background covering full width, with a heavy black top border and heavy black bottom border. At the top left, big text in white uppercase sans-serif reads “GET IN OR GET OUT.” Under it in smaller monospace, also white, “LIMITED TO 300 SEATS // 201 CLAIMED”. The form is arranged in a grid of rectangles with no rounded corners, all with black borders and white backgrounds, slightly misaligned so they feel “off”. Fields include “EMAIL”, “PREFERRED TRACK (A/B/C)”, “EXPERIENCE LEVEL (BEGINNER/INTERMEDIATE/CHAOTIC PRO)”. Field labels are uppercase monospace in black above each white rectangle. The input areas are blank white blocks with no placeholder text. Below the fields, spanning most of the width, is a monstrous CTA button: a huge black rectangle with “CLAIM YOUR PASS — $89” in uppercase neon yellow, centered. Under this button, aligned right, is a tiny disclaimer in white lowercase monospace: “tax may apply. art insecurity not included.” To the left side of this section, there is a small vertical stack of checkbox-style text in white: “[ ] i accept the abrupt aesthetics terms”, “[ ] i understand this will get messy.”

Above the footer, insert a narrow “TESTIMONIAL FRAGMENTS” strip. This strip has a grayscale noise-texture background (like photocopy noise), still flat but visually rough, with no gradients. Across it, three brutalist quote cards appear as white rectangles with black borders, staggered horizontally and slightly overlapping. Each has a short, uppercase monospace quote like ““MY SKETCHBOOK LOOKS LIKE A CRIME SCENE NOW.”” and the name below in smaller text like “— JAY, LAST COHORT”. The typography is tight and the quotes intentionally weird.

Finally, the footer is a stark, compressed band at the bottom. Background is pure black. Inside, the content is arranged in three narrow columns aligned to the baseline. The left column has small, uppercase white monospace text: “NOISY LINES // LIVE LAB” on the first line, “© 2026 NOISY LINES STUDIO” on the second. The center column has minimal links in white uppercase monospace: “FAQ”, “CONTACT”, “INSTAGRAM”. The right column shows in tiny grey monospace “BUILT WITH TOO MANY LAYERS.” Above the footer, there is a very thin neon yellow line that spans the width like a final underline of the entire site. The entire page overall must feel extremely structured yet visually aggressive: no subtle gradients, no soft shadows, no animations. All borders are sharp, all colors flat and bold, lots of obvious grid lines and exposed layout logic, embodying a brutalist poster-turned-webpage for an online art class tutorial subscription event.

### Dataset

| Session                | ProjectedSeatClaims |
|------------------------|---------------------|
| Session 1: Line Drills | 120                 |
| Session 2: Color Chaos | 95                  |
| Session 3: Collage Riot| 80                  |
| Session 4: Night Sketch| 60                  |
| Session 5: Critique Lab| 140                 |

instruction: Planning the generation details for the following website:
input:
Design a futuristic, research-oriented homepage for a fictional project called “Quantum Flow Lab” that studies the intersection of mindfulness, movement, and neural optimization. The overall layout uses a two-column structure with tasteful asymmetries and generous white space, set against a sleek, sci-fi aesthetic. The background of the entire page is a very dark desaturated navy-blue (#050814) that subtly transitions via a vertical gradient to a slightly lighter deep space blue (#0b1230) toward the bottom, with faint, soft-focus hexagonal patterns scattered diagonally in the top-right and bottom-left corners. Tiny, dim white and cyan specks hint at a starfield, giving a cosmic research feel without overwhelming the content. The main content area is constrained to a centered max-width, with about 80px margin on left and right on desktop; the page uses a modern, slightly rounded sans-serif font like “Inter” or “SF Pro” for body text, and a geometric, futuristic display font like “Orbitron” for headings. All headings are in a bright, cool white (#f5f7ff), while accent colors are electric cyan (#3df5ff) and soft neon violet (#a953ff). Hover effects on clickable items are subtle glows or underlines in cyan.

At the very top, a fixed, semi-transparent navigation bar spans full width, with a slightly blurred, frosted-glass effect, as if made from translucent dark glass (#050814 at 80% opacity). The nav bar height is about 70px. On the far left, there is a compact logo: a small, glowing cyan circle containing a minimalist line-art icon of a meditating figure in lotus pose, drawn with ultra-thin neon cyan strokes, overlaid with a faint circuit pattern. To the right of the icon, in a thin futuristic font, the site name reads: “QUANTUM FLOW LAB”. “QUANTUM” is in bright white, “FLOW” in cyan, and “LAB” in violet. On the right half of the nav bar, there is a horizontally aligned menu with evenly spaced links in small uppercase letters: “Research”, “Protocols”, “Data Atlas”, “About”, “Contact”. The default link color is a muted light gray (#c8d0ff) that shifts to cyan and gains a thin cyan underline on hover. At the far right, a pill-shaped button with a fine cyan border and transparent fill reads “Join Study Beta”; on hover it fills with a smooth cyan gradient and the text shifts to dark navy.

Below the navigation, the hero section is full-width but visually contained. It follows a two-column layout with text-heavy content on the left (occupying roughly 55% of width) and a large hero image on the right (45%), slightly overlapping into the background. The hero’s left column starts with a slim, pill-shaped label aligned to the left, reading “RESEARCH PORTAL v1.3” in tiny uppercase letters, with a tiny cyan dot at the left and a vertical separator line before “LIVE”. “LIVE” is in neon violet. Below it, the primary headline is large, all caps, in the futuristic display font, left-aligned: “MAPPING THE MIND–BODY QUANTUM FIELD”. The words “MIND–BODY” are in pure white; “QUANTUM FIELD” is in cyan. The typography is bold and sharply tracked, with extra spacing between letters to emphasize the futuristic tone. Under the headline, a short descriptive paragraph in smaller, light-gray font reads: “Quantum Flow Lab is a collaborative research initiative exploring how mindful movement, breath sequences, and neurofeedback converge to reshape human focus, resilience, and calm.” Below that, a line of smaller text in a monospace font (like “IBM Plex Mono”) mimics a console readout: “Status: DATA CAPTURE ACTIVE · 217 practitioners enrolled”. The word “DATA CAPTURE ACTIVE” is in cyan, while the rest is in soft gray. Beneath this line, two horizontally placed call-to-action buttons appear: on the left, a solid cyan rectangular button with subtle rounded corners reads “Access Protocols”. On hover, it glows slightly and shifts to a cyan-to-violet gradient. To its right, a ghost-style button with a thin violet border reads “View Research Summary”, with a faint outline and text in violet that brightens on hover.

On the right side of the hero section, there is a large, high-detail, semi-isometric illustration (static image, not animated) contained within a rounded rectangle card that appears to float slightly with a soft drop shadow. The illustration depicts a serene, female-presenting figure in a cross-legged meditation pose, seen from a 3/4 angle, sitting on a minimal white circular platform that appears to hover above a dark void. The figure is stylized semi-realistically with soft gradients and subtle sci-fi details: her body is wrapped in a simple, form-fitting, minimal yoga outfit in muted charcoal gray with fine glowing cyan lines tracing along her spine, shoulders, and legs like neural pathways. Her eyes are gently closed; a soft halo of translucent cyan rings encircles her head, resembling EEG waveforms frozen in time. Behind her, large translucent layers of overlapping geometric shapes — hexagons, circles, and vertical lines — glow softly in cyan and violet, giving the sense of holographic data streams. The background of the illustration is a dark, blurred abstract environment with faint grids and nodes, as if she is in a virtual research chamber. To the right and above her shoulder, several small floating panels display micro-visualizations: a tiny line graph with a cyan line, another with purple dots connected by fine lines, and a circular “focus meter” ring partially filled; all are crisp, thin-line, UI-like elements, but they’re decorative, not actual interactive components. The lighting in the illustration is soft and cool, with faint reflections on the platform and subtle shadows beneath the figure.

Immediately below the hero section, maintaining the two-column rhythm but with slight asymmetry, a “Research Snapshot” strip spans the width with a dark, slightly lighter background band (#0b1025). This band is about 200px high and displays three compact statistical cards horizontally centered, each separated by thin vertical translucent dividers. Each card uses an icon and text stacked vertically. The first card shows a minimal, line-art icon of a brain with orbiting dots in cyan. Below the icon, in bold white text: “720+ Guided Sessions Logged”. Below that, in small gray text: “Across breath, flow, and stillness protocols.” The second card uses a stylized heartbeat or waveform icon in violet and cyan gradient. The stat reads: “18% Avg. Focus Gain”, with subtext: “Measured via EEG-based attention indices.” The third card has a hexagonal network icon in cyan. Its stat reads: “26 Global Research Partners”, with subtext: “Yoga scientists, neuroscientists, and systems designers.” All text is centered, and on hover, each card’s border glows faintly in cyan.

Below this, the page transitions into the main two-column research layout, labeled “Core Protocol Library” on the left, with detailed cards on the right. The overall grid has a subtle vertical dividing line: a faint, semi-transparent cyan line running from above the section title down to the footer. On the left, at the top of this main content region, a section heading reads, in small caps, “CORE PROTOCOLS” in violet. Below it, a slightly larger H2-level heading reads “Structured Sequences for Measurable Calm”. A brief description paragraph explains: “All Quantum Flow sequences are tracked in controlled studies, combining posture, breath, gaze, and timing into reproducible protocols.” Below this text, there is a stacked vertical list of three “protocol filters” that look like pill-shaped toggles, aligned to the left: “Stillness Calibration”, “Dynamic Flow”, and “Breath-Focused”. Each pill has a thin border; the active pill (“Dynamic Flow” by default) is filled with a subtle cyan gradient and the label text is in dark navy, while inactive pills are transparent with white text that switches to cyan on hover. Under the filters, a small line of monospaced text reads: “Filter by: intensity, duration, neural baseline ⇢”.

On the right side of this “Core Protocol Library” section, three protocol cards stack vertically with generous spacing. Each card is a rounded rectangle with a dark navy background, a thin inner glow border, and subtle drop shadow. Inside each card, the layout is itself a mini two-column: on the left a small, square thumbnail image, and on the right the protocol text. The first card is titled “QF-01: Equilibrium Spiral”. The thumbnail image shows a top-down view of a minimalist yoga mat rendered like a luminous circuit board: faint glowing cyan and violet lines forming a spiral overlay on a pale, neutral mat, set in a dark room with a soft spotlight. The protocol name appears in bold white, followed by a short description in gray: “A 12-minute sequence pairing spiral spinal movements with slow, elongated exhale counts.” Below the description is a micro data strip in monospace: “Duration: 12:00 · Intensity: Low · Participants: 182”. A tiny cyan dot precedes each value. The second card is “QF-07: Photon Flow”. Its thumbnail shows a stylized side profile of a person mid-warrior pose, silhouetted in cyan lines against a grid of glowing triangles, as if posed inside a holographic chamber. The description reads: “Dynamic standing postures sequenced to synchronize heart-rate variability and visual focus.” Micro data strip: “Duration: 22:30 · Intensity: Moderate · Participants: 94”. The third card, “QF-12: Nebula Stillpoint”, has a thumbnail of a person lying on their back (savasana) on a floating platform in a dark starfield, with faint colored nebula clouds swirling softly in the background; the figure is outlined with gentle cyan edge-lighting. Description: “A supine stillness protocol used as a reset between high cognitive load tasks.” Data strip: “Duration: 08:45 · Intensity: Minimal · Participants: 133”. Each card’s title is left-aligned and on hover, the card border glows brighter and the title becomes cyan.

Further down, a lateral asymmetry is introduced: on the left, the layout now shows a large data visualization card occupying about 60% of the width; on the right, stacked qualitative text elements occupy 40%. This section is introduced by a label above the chart on the left: “Data Atlas” in violet small caps, with a thin cyan underline stretching about 80px. The main heading below this label reads “Focus Index Impact Across Protocols” in bold white. The data visualization itself is a static, interactive-style line chart presented as a flat design card with a subtle inner shadow. The chart has a dark background (#050814) and grid lines in faint bluish gray. The axes are labeled in white text: the horizontal axis at the bottom is “Session Number”, ranging from 1 to 6, with each tick labeled “1”, “2”, “3”, “4”, “5”, “6”. The vertical axis on the left is “Average Focus Index (0–100)”, with ticks at 40, 50, 60, 70, 80, 90, all labeled. Three distinct colored lines represent three protocols: a cyan line with circular data points labeled “QF-01: Equilibrium Spiral”, a neon violet line with square data points labeled “QF-07: Photon Flow”, and a soft teal line with triangular data points labeled “QF-12: Nebula Stillpoint”. A legend at the top-right of the chart card shows a small colored line and marker next to each protocol name. The chart shows all three starting around 50–55 at session 1 and increasing over time, with “Photon Flow” rising most steeply, “Equilibrium Spiral” steadily, and “Nebula Stillpoint” more gently but consistently. There are small, faint vertical gridlines and subtle glow on the markers. Above the chart, in tiny monospaced text, a line reads: “Sample: n=60 · Focus Index derived from EEG + self-report compositing.”

On the right side of this chart section, a column of text cards provides qualitative notes. At top, a short block of text titled “What the data suggests” in bold white summarizes: “Dynamic standing sequences (QF-07) produce the fastest short-term gains in focus index, while gentle spiral movements (QF-01) show more consistent gains over repeated sessions.” The paragraph is light gray and left-aligned. Below this, a smaller card displays a quote from a practitioner, with a faint quotation mark icon in cyan at the top-left: “After three Photon Flow sessions, I could feel my attention ‘lock in’ faster, even during stressful sprints.” The attribution appears below in a slightly smaller size: “– Participant 43, software engineer”. The quote card’s background is a slightly lighter navy with a thin violet border. Beneath the quote, a microtable-like pair of lines in monospaced text highlights: “Median Focus Gain after 3 sessions: 14.7 points” and “Median Focus Gain after 6 sessions: 24.3 points”, with the numbers in cyan.

Continuing downward, the layout returns to a more balanced two-column distribution for an “About & Methodology” section. On the left, a heading in small caps reads “METHODOLOGY”, followed by a larger heading “Where Yoga Meets Neural Engineering”. A paragraph explains the approach in more technical language: “We design each Quantum Flow protocol as a layered system: physical posture, breath cadence, gaze focus, and auditory environment. These variables are tracked through wearables and closed-loop feedback systems to create a reproducible map from sequence design to neurocognitive outcomes.” Below, three bullet-like horizontal tags appear in a row, each a small pill with micro text: “EEG & HRV tracking”, “Controlled breathing ratios”, “Gaze fixation mapping”. These pills are outlined in cyan, with the text in white. On the right side of this section, there is a tall illustrative image of a futuristic lab studio. The image shows an elongated room with minimalistic dark floor and walls, everything bathed in cool cyan underlighting. Along one side, there are three platforms spaced evenly, each a white, slightly glowing rectangle that seems to hover above the floor. On the platform in the foreground, a person is captured mid-yoga pose (perhaps a variation of a lunge), with thin, glowing cyan bands gently encircling their wrists, ankles, and head, connected to invisible sensors. Above each platform, translucent rectangular panels float, displaying line graphs and waveforms in neon hues. The perspective is slightly angled, leading the eye down the row of platforms and into the distance. The style of the image is semi-photorealistic with clean, futuristic lighting, soft highlights on surfaces, subtle reflections on the polished floor, and no visible cables or clutter.

Below this, a narrower band runs across the full width titled “Collaborative Framework”. It’s slightly darker and features a subtle dotted grid pattern. On the left side of this band, small logos represented as placeholder shapes (simple geometric marks) for partner institutions appear in a horizontal row: four or five compact, rounded-square glyphs in white outline, each with a unique abstract icon inside (a neuron-like network, a minimal lotus, a hexagon lattice, a waveform) and labeled beneath in tiny gray text as “NeuroSync Lab”, “Urban Yoga Collective”, “Cognitive Systems Institute”, and “Nova Design Studio”. On the right side of this band, a line of text states: “Open to new collaborators in 2026: submit your lab profile and proposed protocol synergy.”

As the page approaches the bottom, the two-column layout shifts to highlight a “Join the Research” section. On the left, a block of text invites potential participants: heading “Enroll as a Practitioner” in bold white, followed by a paragraph: “We’re onboarding experienced yoga instructors, movement scientists, and focused amateurs into small, guided cohorts. Participation includes structured sequences, biometric tracking kits, and debrief sessions.” Below the text, a vertical form with minimal styling offers three inputs stacked with uniform spacing and thin cyan borders: a single-line text field labeled “Full Name”, an email field labeled “Email Address”, and a dropdown-style field labeled “Primary Practice Background” with placeholder text “Select one…”. Under the fields, a small checkbox with label reads: “I agree to anonymized data being used for aggregated research.” The checkbox itself is a small square with a cyan border; when checked, it shows a tiny cyan check mark. Beneath the checkbox is a cyan “Request Enrollment” button that spans the width of the form; on hover, it brightens and displays a faint outer glow. On the right side of this section, a compact card titled “Upcoming Cohort Windows” lists three date ranges in a stacked layout, each line with a small cyan dot bullet and white text: “Cohort 09 · Jan 08 – Feb 02”, “Cohort 10 · Mar 11 – Apr 05”, “Cohort 11 · May 06 – May 31”. Below this card, a microtext line in monospace reads: “Location: Remote + partner studios · Time zones: US/EU/APAC”.

The footer returns to a full-width band in the deepest navy, with a thin cyan line running horizontally across its top edge. Inside, the content is center-aligned. On the left half, the logo appears again, smaller, with “QUANTUM FLOW LAB” beneath in a single line. On the right half, two short vertical stacks are separated: one labeled “Navigation” with tiny links “Research”, “Protocols”, “Data Atlas”, “About”, “Contact”; the other labeled “Ethics & Privacy” with links “Data Policy”, “Consent Framework”, “IRB Overview”. All links are small, light gray, with a cyan underline on hover. At the very bottom, a slender line of text in micro font reads: “© 2026 Quantum Flow Lab. This site is part of an ongoing research initiative. Not medical advice.” The overall design remains still and clean throughout: no animated elements, only static imagery and static charts, with the subtle futuristic aesthetic emphasized by glows, gradients, and the interplay of cyan, violet, and deep navy tones.

### Dataset
| Session | QF-01_Equilibrium_Spiral | QF-07_Photon_Flow | QF-12_Nebula_Stillpoint |
|--------|---------------------------|-------------------|-------------------------|
| 1      | 52                        | 50                | 54                      |
| 2      | 56                        | 58                | 57                      |
| 3      | 61                        | 66                | 59                      |
| 4      | 65                        | 72                | 62                      |
| 5      | 69                        | 77                | 65                      |
| 6      | 74                        | 81                | 68                      |

instruction: Planning the generation details for the following website:
input:
Design a tall, vertically scrolling, single-column brutalist dashboard homepage for an “online language exchange subscription” service called “LinguaGrid,” with a visual tone that feels raw, utilitarian, and unapologetically digital, using high-contrast colors, harsh geometric blocks, and visible gridlines. The entire page sits on a flat, slightly off-white background (#F8F7F2) with a faint, visible grid of thin, light gray lines (like graph paper) spaced evenly, giving a mechanical, blueprint feel. The base font throughout is a monospaced typeface reminiscent of old terminal fonts, with a second, condensed grotesk used only for loud headings. All headings use uppercase letters with tight letter-spacing. The color palette focuses on pure black (#000000), electric blue (#0047FF), neon yellow (#F8FF00), and emergency red (#FF0033), with occasional flat gray (#D6D6D6) blocks. There are hard, 1px borders everywhere, sometimes doubled, with no rounded corners anywhere. All sections are primarily stacked vertically (single-column), but within some sections there are asymmetric side-by-side blocks that break alignment in a controlled, brutalist way.

At the very top, spanning the full width, there is a fixed-position header bar at the absolute top edge, 80px tall, with a solid neon yellow (#F8FF00) background and a strong 2px black bottom border. On the left, aligned to the page margin, sits the logo: the word “LINGUAGRID” in uppercase, bold, condensed grotesk, black letters, slightly too large and slightly touching the top border, intentionally cramped. To the right of the logo, still in the header, there are three square buttons aligned horizontally with slight gaps: “DASHBOARD,” “SESSIONS,” and “ACCOUNT,” each inside a flat white box with a 1px black stroke, uppercase monospaced text, and when hovered (describe as a design state, not animation) they are shown with a filled electric blue background in mock-ups. On the far right of the header is a blocky, unstyled text link “LOG OUT” in red uppercase monospaced font, with a thin black underline that looks hand-drawn (slightly offset from the text). The header casts no shadow; it is starkly flat.

Below the header, spanning almost the entire viewport width except for 40px left and right margins, is the hero/dashboard overview section. This section has a split background: the left two-thirds is pure white, and the right third is electric blue, divided by a vertical 3px black line. The left side is taller than the right, creating an abrupt, misaligned bottom edge that exposes some of the grid-paper background below on the right side. On the left side, near the top, is the main title: “LANGUAGE EXCHANGE CONTROL PANEL” in huge uppercase condensed grotesk, black, left-aligned, with uneven line breaks so it appears slightly ragged. Directly under it, in smaller monospaced font, is the subtitle: “Configure your weekly conversation practice like a command line.” Below this, separated by a thick black horizontal rule, is a blocky, table-like card with a light gray background and black border, labeled at the top in a small, uppercase label: “ACTIVE SUBSCRIPTION”. Inside this card, in three rough rows, there are left labels and right values arranged in a two-column grid: “Plan:” with value “PRO EXCHANGE / 5 SESSIONS / WEEK”, “Languages:” with value “SPANISH • JAPANESE • GERMAN”, and “Next session:” with value “Today 19:00 UTC with @maria_es”. Each label is uppercase black text, each value is in electric blue text. At the top-right corner of this card is a small black rectangle containing white text: “EDIT PLAN” like a crude button.

On the right third of the hero section (the electric blue panel), centrally placed is a tall, rectangular image placeholder with a white border, meant to be a flat, semi-illustrated representation of a language exchange video call interface. The image shows three stacked, rectangular “video frames” aligned vertically, each with a simplified, flat-design avatar: the top frame shows a person with brown skin, a flat, geometric face with minimal features, wearing orange headphones and speaking into a stylized microphone; the middle frame shows a person with pale skin and short blue hair, with a speech bubble containing the Japanese characters “こんにちは” in white on a dark speech bubble; the bottom frame shows a person with medium-toned skin and glasses, with a small German flag icon next to their face. The background of this image is a flat, dull gray, and there are rough, black outlines around each “video” rectangle. Overlaid at the top-left of the image is a small red, boxy label reading “LIVE MATCH MOCKUP.” Below the image, in small, black, uppercase monospaced text, is a caption: “This is a static schematic representation. Not an actual interface.”

Below the hero section, separated by a thick, full-width black horizontal bar, is a section titled “TODAY’S STATUS” with the title printed in black inside a narrow yellow bar that stretches from the left margin halfway across the page, leaving the rest blank, creating an asymmetrical top border. The section background is plain off-white. Within this section, there is a minimalist, brutalist-style stats row: three rectangular metric blocks stacked vertically (so still single-column, but each block is wide, with heavy borders and generous padding), each with a different accent color stripe on the left. The first block shows “Minutes spoken this week” in small uppercase monospaced text, and a huge number “145” in black, with a thin vertical neon yellow bar on the left edge. The second block shows “New partners matched” and the number “3”, with a left strip in electric blue. The third shows “Vocab cards reviewed” and the number “87”, with a left strip in red. Each block has visible, pixel-like 1px grid lines inside, giving a chunky, overdesigned tabular feel.

Directly beneath this stats section is a data visualization panel, still part of “TODAY’S STATUS.” This panel has a simple white background, a thick black border, and the title “SPEAKING MINUTES / LAST 7 DAYS” at the top in uppercase monospaced font. Inside, there is a hand-drawn-looking bar chart occupying most of the width. The X-axis is a straight, bold black line with 7 tick labels: “Mon”, “Tue”, “Wed”, “Thu”, “Fri”, “Sat”, “Sun” in small uppercase. The Y-axis is labeled “Minutes” with horizontal grid lines every 20 minutes. Each day has a vertical bar, slightly too wide and touching each other: Monday’s bar is neon yellow up to 30; Tuesday’s is electric blue up to 45; Wednesday’s is neon yellow up to 60; Thursday’s is electric blue up to 50; Friday’s is neon yellow up to 20; Saturday’s is red up to 70; Sunday’s is electric blue up to 40. Above Saturday’s bar, the value “70” is printed in a small red, boxed label, like an annotation reading: “Peak conversation load.” The chart axes and bar edges are intentionally a bit rough, like they were manually drawn with a pixel brush, to stay within the brutalist style. Under the chart, aligned to the right, is a tiny note: “SOURCE: SESSION LOG / AUTO-GENERATED.”

Below the analytics, there is a wide strip with a muted gray background and a thick top border, titled “YOUR EXCHANGE GRID” in uppercase black letters. Inside this section, the layout breaks the single-column feel slightly by having staggered, overlapping cards, but still arranged so they visually descend in a single vertical flow. First, on the left, there’s a large, white, rectangular card with black border titled “Next session in 02:13:45” in red uppercase, like a countdown label. Below, in black text, it lists: “Partner: @maria_es (Madrid)” on one line, “Language: Spanish (C1)” on the next, “Topic: Street food & local markets” on the next. To the right of this card, slightly lower (so they do not align perfectly), is a smaller card titled “Queued sessions” listing three bullet items with square bullets (small filled black squares): “Tomorrow 07:00 — @ken_ja — Japanese,” “Wed 19:30 — @lena_de — German,” “Fri 21:00 — @ana_br — Portuguese (trial).” The cards have no drop shadows; they just sit starkly on the gray strip. At the bottom-left of this section, there is a black rectangular button with white uppercase text “OPEN SESSION GRID,” shaped like a flat brick, placed in a way that feels almost haphazard.

Following this is a section that looks like a big, brutalist “command console” for practice settings. It has a pure white background with a thick black border, and at the top, spanning the width inside the border, there is a black strip with white text: “PRACTICE SCRIPT CONFIGURATION.” Below, the content is laid out as a series of labeled, raw input-like rows. Each row has a label in uppercase monospaced font aligned right in a narrow left column, then a long horizontal “input field” represented as an outlined rectangle with a faint gray fill and 1px border. The fields themselves show prefilled values: under “PRIMARY LANGUAGE:” the field reads “Spanish (conversation, B2-C1)”; under “SECONDARY LANGUAGE:” it reads “Japanese (casual, A2-B1)”; under “FOCUS THIS WEEK:” it reads “Fillers, hesitation phrases, and polite disagreement.” Another row labeled “ERR0R TOLERANCE LEVEL:” (note the intentional zero) shows “Medium — correct every 3rd mistake.” Below the configuration rows, separated by a dotted black line, is a block of monospaced “console output” text in a faint gray, aligned left, reading: “> Plan compiled successfully. > Next conversation script ready. > No critical warnings.” To the right of this console output is a chunky, black button labeled “EXPORT SCRIPT” in white uppercase.

Next comes a “MATCHED PARTNERS” section, which feels like an analog contact sheet. The title “MATCHED PARTNERS” is printed in large uppercase letters that partially overlap a thin, horizontal red line. The background is off-white with a faint, repeated pattern of tiny, gray “…” ellipses diagonally, giving noise. In this section, three partner profiles are arranged vertically, each as a long horizontal strip card with a black border, but each slightly indented or offset left-right so they don’t line up perfectly, emphasizing brutalist asymmetry while staying essentially single-column. Each card has a left side and right side. The left side shows a small, square portrait image in flat illustration style: for “@maria_es,” the illustration is a woman with brown skin, wavy dark hair, wearing bright yellow headphones; the background of the avatar square is electric blue. For “@ken_ja,” the avatar is a person with short black hair, wearing round glasses, with a red circle behind their head referencing the Japanese flag. For “@lena_de,” the avatar is a person with light skin, blonde short hair, with a background divided into black, red, and gold vertical bands abstractly referencing the German flag. Each avatar is drawn with minimal facial features, strong outlines, and flat colors. To the right of each avatar, the partner’s handle appears in bold monospaced uppercase (e.g., “@MARIA_ES”), then below in smaller text “Location: Madrid, ES” and “Native: Spanish / Practicing: English.” On the far right of each card, there are two tall, narrow buttons stacked vertically: “VIEW PROFILE” and “MESSAGE,” each with a white background and black border; the “MESSAGE” button’s text is red. Along the far right side of the entire section, running vertically, is the text “PEER-TO-PEER LANGUAGE EXCHANGE” in tiny uppercase, rotated 90 degrees, printed in light gray, like a marginal annotation.

Further down, there is a more sales-oriented section called “SUBSCRIPTION LEVELS,” but still presented as part of the dashboard. The title is printed inside a thick, black-outlined rectangle with a neon yellow fill, slightly too wide for the column so it bleeds into the page margins. Within this section, three plan boxes are stacked vertically like a set of punchy flyers taped to a wall. The first box is labeled “BASIC” with a background of plain white and a black border; it lists “2 sessions / week,” “Audio only,” “1 language,” “Community text chat access” as bullet points with square bullet icons. The second is labeled “PRO” with a background of pale neon yellow and a thicker black border; it has bullet points “5 sessions / week,” “Video + audio,” “Up to 3 languages,” “Priority partner matching,” and “Session transcripts.” The third is “STUDIO” with an electric blue background and white text, bullet points “Unlimited sessions,” “Video + audio + text correction,” “Up to 5 languages,” “Dedicated coach (weekly),” and “Advanced analytics.” Each plan has its price printed at the bottom in very large, crude monospaced numerals: “$19 / mo,” “$39 / mo,” and “$89 / mo” respectively. Below each price is a small rectangular button: “DOWNGRADE” for plans above the current, grayed out, and “CURRENT PLAN” for PRO (assuming PRO is the current plan) in a black box with white text.

Below the plans, there is a thin, high-contrast information strip resembling a ticker but not animated. It’s a full-width, black bar with white text that reads: “SYSTEM NOTES: You are on a PRO plan. Your next billing date is 2024-11-01. Your current streak: 12 days.” Each piece of information is separated by a tiny vertical line.

Near the bottom of the page, there is a heavy, textual section meant to feel like a manual, titled “HOW TO EXCHANGE WITHOUT SMALL TALK.” The title is in all caps, black, set large and left aligned with a thick underline that extends far past the text. The section consists of two long, text columns—but since the page is single-column dominant, these “columns” are really just two text blocks stacked; the visible difference is that the left block is narrower and aligned left, and the right block is narrower and indented, almost like sidebar commentary. The left block is labeled “Protocol v1.3” and includes a short paragraph: “Start each session with your counterpart’s goal in mind, not your own. Use a timer to alternate languages in strict intervals. Take notes in your target language only.” The right block is labeled “Field Notes” and includes lines of text like: “Silences are signal, not failure. Let them happen.” and “Ask for the word you need, not the word you think exists.” Between these two text blocks, there is a thin, vertical red line running the full height of the section like a divider, slightly off-center.

The footer is stark and structured like a technical appendix. It has a light gray background with a thick black border on the top and no border below. In the top-left of the footer is the text “LINGUAGRID” in uppercase black. Below it are three stacked links in small uppercase monospaced font: “TERMS,” “PRIVACY,” “SUPPORT.” On the right side of the footer, aligned to the top, is a small, black-outlined box with the heading “DEBUG INFO” in tiny uppercase. Inside this box are two lines: “Version: 0.9.8 (beta)” and “Latency: 124ms (simulated).” At the bottom center of the footer, in a very small, pale gray monospaced font, is the text: “This interface is a conceptual dashboard. Some elements are illustrative only.”

Overall, the page must feel coherent: lots of hard edges, visible outlines, occasional misalignments as a stylistic choice, bold color blocks that clash slightly, heavy use of monospaced text and uppercase headings, minimal imagery beyond flat, graphic representations of people and interface elements, no gradients, no shadows, and absolutely no animation or motion. Every visible text string is exactly as quoted above, and the chart should correspond precisely to the dataset below.

### Dataset
| Day | SpeakingMinutes |
|-----|-----------------|
| Mon | 30              |
| Tue | 45              |
| Wed | 60              |
| Thu | 50              |
| Fri | 20              |
| Sat | 70              |
| Sun | 40              |

instruction: Planning the generation details for the following website:
input:
Design a minimal, calm, data-rich dashboard webpage for an electric vehicle charging management platform called “VoltField”. The overall layout is a light, almost ethereal interface that uses a hierarchical, overlapping card system with subtle natural asymmetries. The background of the entire page is a soft off-white (#F7F7FA) with a faint vertical gradient from slightly cooler at the top to slightly warmer at the bottom, almost imperceptible, giving depth without distraction. The primary accent color is a muted electric teal (#26C6DA) used sparingly for emphasis, while a deep charcoal (#22252B) is used for primary text. All typography uses a clean sans-serif typeface similar to “Inter” or “SF Pro”, with three main font sizes: large headings at 28–32px, section titles at 18–20px, and body text at 13–14px. The entire page is shown within a centered, wide container with generous left and right margins, giving the feel of white space breathing around the interface.

At the very top of the page, a fixed-height header bar spans the full width, but the content is centered and constrained. The header background is pure white (#FFFFFF) with a subtle bottom border: a 1px light gray line (#E2E3EB). On the far left, the logo is a simple, compact horizontal lockup: an abstract icon and the wordmark. The icon is a flat, vector-style circular symbol: a soft teal circle with a stylized, minimalistic white lightning bolt cutout that is slightly rounded at the edges. To its right, the text “VoltField” is in bold, tight letter-spacing, dark charcoal, about 18px. To the right of the logo, there are three navigation links aligned horizontally, spaced evenly: “Overview”, “Stations”, and “Reports”, all in small uppercase letters (12px), gray (#7A808F), and when hovered they would become charcoal with a thin teal underline (the state can be suggested visually by having “Overview” already in that active style). On the right side of the header, there is a small search bar and a compact profile area. The search bar is a pill-shaped input with a thin gray border, containing a tiny magnifying glass icon on the left (simple line icon, charcoal), and faint placeholder text “Search stations or sessions…” in light gray. To the far right, a circular profile avatar with a very light gray border shows a static, simple placeholder image: a flat illustration of a neutral user silhouette in grayscale with a slight shadow, and next to it the text “Alex Carter” in semi-bold charcoal, and below in tiny uppercase letters “Admin”.

Below the header is the main content, arranged into a three-column asymmetric grid that subtly overlaps in z-index and vertical position, with some cards slightly sliding over others. At the very top left of the content area, there is a large, dominant hero stats card that sets the hierarchy. This card is a wide, slightly taller rectangle with rounded corners (16px radius), white background, and a very soft drop shadow (0,8px,24px, rgba(15, 23, 42, 0.06)). It is positioned so that its bottom edge slightly overlaps the cards below it. In the top left corner of this hero card, in small gray uppercase text, it reads “TODAY’S NETWORK STATUS”. Below that, aligned left, the main heading reads “Live Charging Overview” in 28px bold, charcoal. To the right of this text, on the same hero card but aligned to the right side, there is a compact horizontal strip showing three key metrics, each in its own mini-vertical stack with no dividers: “Active Sessions”, “Energy Delivered (kWh)”, and “Utilization”. Each metric label is in tiny uppercase gray, and beneath each, the metric value is large and bold teal. For example, “Active Sessions” shows “128”, “Energy Delivered (kWh)” shows “4,320”, and “Utilization” shows “68%”. Under each main value, a tiny caption in muted gray indicates the change vs yesterday, using subtle colored text: “+12 vs yesterday” in teal for positive, “-3 vs yesterday” in soft red (#E57373) if negative, with a small up or down arrow icon.

Below the hero text, occupying the bottom two-thirds of the hero card on the left side, is a simple static schematic-style map illustration, not a real map, but a conceptual stylized one. It shows a minimal city grid in ultra-light gray lines with no labels, on a white background. Five or six teal circular nodes indicate charging station clusters; each node has a faint concentric, semi-transparent teal halo to show activity. Smaller pale gray dots indicate inactive or offline stations. The map uses flat, 2D top-down design with slightly rounded corners, and each teal node has a tiny white lightning bolt icon in the center. Off to the right side of this hero card, partially overlapping the map slightly but clearly legible, there is a vertical list of three “Alerts” with a small, thin left-side teal line. Each alert is a single line of text, e.g., “Station VF-21 experiencing slow charging speeds” and “Scheduled maintenance at Hub West in 2 hours”, using 13px text, charcoal for the main words and soft gray for the station IDs. Each alert line has a tiny, flat icon at the far left: a hollow circle for minor, a small triangle for warning, all in muted colors. These alert pills do not have borders; they simply float within the card with bit of spacing between them.

Directly below the hero card, the main content is divided into two primary columns: a wider left column (about 65% width) and a narrower right column (about 35% width). In the left column, slightly tucked under the hero card so the top edge is behind it by about 20px, there is a large white card titled “Energy Output – Last 7 Days”. The title is left-aligned at the top in bold 18px charcoal, with a subtle date range selector aligned to the right side of the card header, which reads “Range: Last 7 Days” in small gray text and a tiny downward arrow icon, implied as a dropdown. The card’s content is a clean, minimalist line chart representing daily energy delivered in kWh. The chart area has a faint grid of horizontal lines only, light gray, with no vertical grid lines. The X-axis runs along the bottom and displays dates “Mon”, “Tue”, “Wed”, “Thu”, “Fri”, “Sat”, “Sun” in small charcoal text; the Y-axis is along the left side displaying numeric labels “0”, “500”, “1000”, “1500”, “2000”, “2500”, “3000” in a smaller gray font. A single teal line, 2px thick and smoothly curved between data points, represents the daily energy: Mon at 1800, Tue at 2100, Wed at 1950, Thu at 2300, Fri at 2600, Sat at 2400, Sun at 2200 kWh. Each point on the line is indicated by a small solid teal circle. The line has a subtle, semi-transparent teal fill beneath it with a gradient that fades to transparent as it approaches the X-axis, giving a soft, minimal area-chart feel. At the top right of the chart area, a tiny, lightly rounded pill label “Total: 15,150 kWh” appears in 11px font inside a nearly transparent teal-tinted background. The card has comfortable padding, with generous whitespace around the chart.

Below this chart card in the left column, slightly offset to the right to create a gentle asymmetry, is a shorter, wide card titled “Top Stations by Utilization”. This card’s header is similar: title on the left, a small “View all stations” text link on the right in teal. Inside, a simple table is presented with three columns: “Station”, “City”, and “Utilization”. The table header row is in uppercase tiny gray text, and the rows are separated by ultra-thin gray dividers. The station names are short IDs like “VF-01 Central Hub”, “VF-07 Riverside”, “VF-12 Airport”, “VF-21 Tech Park”, and “VF-34 Eastside”. The cities are simple names like “Seattle”, “Portland”, “San Jose”, etc., in muted charcoal. The Utilization column shows percentages like “82%”, “79%”, “75%”, “72%”, “69%”, with the percentage values right-aligned and colored depending on relative performance: top 3 in teal, others in charcoal. Each utilization value is accompanied by a very slim horizontal progress bar behind it: a faint, light gray track with a teal fill proportionate to the percentage, aligned to the right of each row, but not overwhelming—more like a visual hint.

In the narrower right column, two vertically stacked cards are slightly overlapping in the vertical axis to create depth. The upper card is slightly taller and titled “Live Sessions”. It has a white background and subtle shadow. At the top right of this card, a small mute control is replaced with a static toggle indicator: a simple pill with text “Auto-refresh” and a tiny circular knob filled with teal indicating it is on. Inside the card is a concise vertical list of about six live charging sessions. Each session row is a compact, horizontally arranged stack: on the left a small circular icon with a thin teal border and a tiny white bolt in the center; next to it the EV model name in bold (e.g., “Tesla Model 3”, “Nissan Leaf”, “VW ID.4”), then a lighter gray text showing the station ID like “VF-01 · Bay 3”, and on the far right a clear teal text label indicating the charging progress percentage such as “76%”, “54%”, “92%”. Under each percentage, in tiny gray text, it reads “Est. 00:24 remaining” or similar times. The rows have subtle differences in background shading on hover implied by a barely-visible, slightly darker gray strip on alternate rows, but overall keep the minimal look.

Directly beneath it, partially overlapping upward so that its top edge tucks under the bottom of the “Live Sessions” card, appears a smaller card titled “Network Health”. This card contains three large, centered donut charts arranged in a horizontal row inside the card: “Online Stations”, “Fast Chargers”, and “Occupied Bays”. Each donut chart is a simple, thin ring graphic with no 3D effect, using a teal segment and a very light gray segment. For example, “Online Stations” is labeled “92%” in the center text, “Fast Chargers” shows “64%”, and “Occupied Bays” shows “71%”. The labels beneath each chart are in tiny gray uppercase. The teal segment of each ring uses the same brand teal, and the remainder of the ring uses #E4E5EF. There are no legends; the numbers and labels provide the necessary information. At the top right corner of this card, an ultra-small text label reads “Status updated 2 min ago” in muted gray.

In the far left but visually slightly recessed background, behind the main columns, a very faint vertical strip from top to bottom contains a sidebar-like hint without being functional: thin, linear icons in light gray for “dashboard”, “map”, and “settings” stacked vertically, with the “dashboard” icon filled slightly darker to suggest the current view. They are partially obscured by overlapping main content cards, giving the feeling that the dashboard cards float above a subtle underlying scaffolding.

Just below the fold, but still visible in the main view, there is an understated lower band that spans across both columns near the bottom of the page. This band is not a card but a transparent overlay area with no shadow, containing two sets of information aligned left and right. On the left, in small gray text, it reads “Last sync: Today at 14:32 · Timezone: PST” and on the right, a minimalist button-like text label “Export report” in charcoal with a hairline rectangle around it, and a tiny download arrow icon.

The color palette remains minimal throughout: mostly white and soft grays with teal accents. No element uses bright or saturated secondary colors other than occasional soft red text for negative trends. All icons are line-based, flat, and monochrome (charcoal or gray) with teal only when highlighting an active or positive state. No video or animated component is present; everything is static yet arranged in a way that suggests a living system. Spacing between cards is generous, with at least 24px gaps, and cards are aligned in a grid that is subtly broken by a few overlapping edges, emphasizing hierarchy: the hero card overlaps the chart, the “Live Sessions” overlaps the “Network Health” card. The entire interface feels like a quiet, carefully structured overview where the most important information—the live utilization and energy output—is visually prominent but rendered with restraint and clarity.

### Dataset

```csv
Metric,Mon,Tue,Wed,Thu,Fri,Sat,Sun
Energy Delivered (kWh),1800,2100,1950,2300,2600,2400,2200
```

instruction: Planning the generation details for the following website:
input:
Design a minimal, single-column leaning portfolio homepage for a freelance digital educator who creates subscription-based tutorials for independent bookstores. The aesthetic is calm, literary, and modern, with a lot of white space, a restrained color palette, and subtle typographic hierarchy. The entire page sits on a soft off-white background (#FAF8F4), with a fixed, very thin top border line in a light warm gray (#E2DFD8) that spans the full width of the viewport, giving the page a framed, print-like feeling. The layout is predominantly single-column, aligned to the center with a max content width of around 920px, but with occasional subtle asymmetries like images slightly breaking the column or blocks shifted a bit left or right to add visual interest. The overall font pairing: headings use a serif typeface that feels like a book title, such as something similar to “Cormorant Garamond” or “Libre Baskerville,” and body text uses a clean, neutral sans-serif reminiscent of “Inter” or “Helvetica Neue.” Text is mostly left-aligned, with some small centered elements like section labels or decorative details. There are no animations, transitions, or videos; everything is static and quiet, like a beautifully typeset book page.

At the very top, a slim, centered navigation bar sits within the same max-width container. The nav is visually light, with generous padding above and below. On the far left, there is a small text logo in uppercase serif letters: “ALYSSA RAY / TUTORIALS FOR BOOKSTORES” in a muted charcoal gray (#2E2B28), letterspaced slightly. There is no icon, just text. On the far right, still within the same horizontal line, there are four small plain-text links in the sans-serif font, all uppercase, spaced with equal gaps: “ABOUT”, “PROJECTS”, “CLIENTS”, “CONTACT”. The links are in a medium gray (#6C6963). Hover is not described as an animation; instead, the design suggests their state visually: in the mock, “PROJECTS” appears as the current section, shown in charcoal with a very thin, 1px underline in the same charcoal, spanning just under the word. Beneath the navigation bar, a subtle horizontal rule in light gray (#E2DFD8) runs across the content width, giving a sense of separation.

Below the nav, the hero section occupies the first visible screen. The hero is vertically spaced with generous top and bottom padding, creating a breathing, book-cover-like composition. On the left two-thirds of the content column, there is the main headline in large serif type, perhaps 42–48px, low contrast but legible, in charcoal: “Thoughtful tutorials for independent bookstores.” The headline is broken into three lines, with “Thoughtful tutorials” on the first line, “for independent” on the second, and “bookstores.” on the third, with generous line height and a period at the end. Right below it, a one-sentence subheading in smaller sans-serif, in a softer gray (#7C7972), reads: “I design simple, repeatable training for teams who love print, stories, and slow conversations.” This subheading is about 16px, left-aligned, with space above and below. Beneath the subheading, still left-aligned, is a very minimal call-to-action row in the same sans-serif font. On the first line: a subtle outlined button that is more like a pill-shaped label than a typical web button. It has rounded corners, a thin 1px border in charcoal, and light off-white interior, containing the text “View tutorial library” in 14px uppercase sans-serif, charcoal. To the right of that, with a bit of space, is plain text acting as a secondary link: “Download portfolio PDF” in the same 14px sans-serif, underlined in a fine gray line. Both are small and understated, aligning with the minimal aesthetic.

On the right side of the hero, slightly offset, there is a tall, narrow, static hero image that feels like an artful photograph of a small independent bookstore interior. The image is approximately one-third of the width of the content area and aligned so that its top edge starts slightly lower than the headline, creating gentle asymmetry. The image is a soft, muted, photorealistic photograph: a wooden bookstore table in the foreground, shot from a slightly elevated, three-quarter perspective. On the table are neatly stacked trade paperbacks in earthy-toned covers (cream, muted terracotta, dusty blue), with a simple ceramic mug of black coffee slightly out of focus at the right edge of the frame. In the midground, a low bookshelf extends horizontally, filled with spines of books in neutral and soft colors, arranged neatly, no harsh colors. The background shows a blurred suggestion of more shelves and a warm, diffused daylight coming from the left edge of the photo, creating gentle highlights on the book spines and soft shadows under the table. The whole photo has a warm color balance and a slight matte finish. There are no people, no text on book covers visible enough to read—just shapes and colors that convey “bookstore” and calm. The image has slightly rounded corners and a very thin white border that makes it feel like a printed photograph placed on the page.

Below the hero, there is a narrow, centered, all-caps section label in tiny serif text: “PORTFOLIO” in charcoal, spaced widely and separated from the hero by ample whitespace. Under this label, an intro block of text in one column introduces the creator. The heading, in serif, about 28px, reads: “Designing training that feels like reading a good book.” Below it, a short paragraph in sans-serif, about 16px, in medium gray reads: “I’m Alyssa, an independent instructional designer and facilitator. For the past 7 years, I’ve helped bookstores and small presses create sustainable, low-noise tutorial systems: onboarding guides, hands-on sales workshops, catalog navigation training, and collaborative reading circle frameworks.” The paragraph is set with comfortable line spacing and restrained width. A second paragraph follows, slightly shorter: “This portfolio collects a selection of subscription-based tutorial series and bespoke projects. My work emphasizes clear language, printed companions, and a pace that respects attention.”

After this introductory text, the “Selected Projects” section begins. It is structured as a vertical stack of project cards, each taking the full content width but internally structured with subtle left-right asymmetry.
