Above the stack, a simple left-aligned serif title about 24px reads: “Selected tutorial series”. Beneath it, in small uppercase sans-serif, there is a label “3 ongoing subscriptions” in a light gray. The first project card has a light beige background (#F4F1EB), slightly darker than the page background, with rounded 8px corners and internal padding. On the left, there is a block of text with a small uppercase label “SUBSCRIPTION SERIES” in tiny sans-serif, then the project title in bold serif, 22px: “Front Table Conversations”. Below the title, a single-line description in sans-serif: “A monthly tutorial series for staff who curate front-of-store tables and seasonal displays.” Farther down, a structured list in sans-serif, 14px, uses simple line breaks rather than bullets, each line beginning with a label in medium gray followed by the data in charcoal: “Client: Moss & Alder Books, Portland”, “Format: 12-part video-free tutorial series with print companions”, “Scope: Staff curation, conversational sales, seasonal storytelling.” On the right, within the same card and vertically centered, is a small static image thumbnail that looks like a flat-lay design mockup of printed materials. This image shows three overlapping A5 booklets on a neutral linen-textured background, seen from above. The booklets’ covers are abstract and minimal: one in muted green with a simple white line drawing of a book stack, one in soft terracotta with a small white circle, one in pale cream with a thin horizontal line near the top; no visible text on covers. The lighting is soft and even, with very subtle shadow under each booklet to suggest depth.

The second project card is similar, but the internal layout flips: the image appears on the left, text on the right, creating gentle rhythm. Its background is plain white but framed with a thin, light gray border. The image is a grayscale, flat illustration in a very minimal, line-art style: an overhead view of an open workbook next to a pencil and a single sprig of dried plant, all outlined with thin charcoal strokes on an off-white background. The project text on the right has a label “MICRO-COURSE LIBRARY”, then the title: “Quiet Mornings at the Register”, and a description: “10 short, scenario-based tutorials that help new staff learn register flow without feeling rushed.” Below, the format line notes: “Format: Printable scripts, reflective prompts, and audio transcripts delivered as PDFs (no streaming).” The third project card returns to the previous background color and layout (text left, image right). The title is “Reading Circles for Younger Readers”, with a description about designing a suite of discussion prompts and pacing guides. The associated image is a softly colored, flat illustration of a circle of three simple, abstract chairs around a low round table with a stack of books in the center, top-down perspective, very minimal, with muted browns, soft blues, and creams, and no visible characters.

Below the project stack, there is an understated “Process” section that visually resembles a calm, typed lecture note. At the top left, a small uppercase sans-serif label reads: “PROCESS”. Below it, the heading in serif: “How I build subscription tutorials”. This section uses a single column of numbered steps, aligned left, each number in a small, circular outline in charcoal with a white fill, placed to the left of the step title. Step 1 title: “Listen to the room”, with a short description: “We start with a 60–90 minute conversation about your store’s rhythms, staff experience, and what’s already working.” Step 2: “Draft the spine”, describing how she turns that into a simple, multi-part outline. Step 3: “Write and test”, and Step 4: “Deliver and refine”, each with 2–3 lines of explanatory text in quiet gray. On the right side of this Process section, aligned with the same baseline, is a small, vertical monochrome photograph, narrow and tall, showing a person’s hands (no face visible) holding a stack of printed handouts clipped together on a wooden table with faint book spines blurred in the background. The image is in black-and-white, with soft contrast, emphasizing the tactile paper and the edge of the wooden table.

Further down the page, there is a “Client Snapshot” section that includes a single data visualization integrated in a minimal, print-like manner. The heading, left-aligned serif, reads: “Client snapshot”. Below it, in sans-serif, a single sentence: “Most of my work sits with small, independent spaces who return year after year.” Under this, left-aligned and spanning most of the content width, is a static bar chart illustration rendered in a flat, monochrome style, with no animation. The background of the chart is white with faint horizontal grid lines in a very light gray (#ECEAE5). There is a vertical y-axis on the left with tick labels “0”, “5”, “10”, “15”, and “20” in a tiny sans-serif font in medium gray. The horizontal x-axis at the bottom has three category labels: “Independent bookstores”, “Small presses”, and “Other cultural orgs”, each in small sans-serif under its respective bar. Three vertical bars stand centered above these labels. The bar for “Independent bookstores” is the tallest, reaching up near the “18” mark, filled with solid charcoal. The bar for “Small presses” is shorter, around “7”, in a medium gray (#8B8781). The bar for “Other cultural orgs” is slightly taller than the second, around “5”, in a light gray (#C4C0B8). Above each bar, in tiny sans-serif text, are exact values: “18 clients”, “7 clients”, “5 clients”. To the right of the chart, there is a soft gray caption in small type: “Based on 30 long-term client relationships from 2018–2024.” The chart is framed within a very light gray hairline border and has ample white space around it so it doesn’t feel crowded.

Below this section, the page transitions to a “Selected clients” list. There is a thin horizontal line, then a heading: “Selected clients & collaborators”. Underneath, a simple two-column list appears, but still within the central column width. Client names are in sans-serif, medium gray, and include lines like “Fern Street Books (Seattle)”, “Third Window Press”, “Waxwing Children’s Bookshop”, “North Shore Literary Cooperative”, and “Maple Row Books & Coffee”. The list is unadorned, with slightly increased line spacing for clarity.

Near the bottom of the page, the “About & Contact” section brings it back to a more personal tone. A centered small label in uppercase sans-serif reads “ABOUT”. Beneath it, the heading in serif, slightly smaller than the hero but larger than body text, reads: “Slow, text-first tutorials for teams who care about readers.” The body text paragraph introduces Alyssa personally, including a line such as: “Before freelancing, I spent 5 years managing an independent shop’s children’s section and training new booksellers.” Off to the right side of this block, slightly protruding from the main column, there is a small, square, black-and-white portrait photo. It’s a soft, candid head-and-shoulders photograph of Alyssa sitting at a wooden table in front of a wall of blurred bookshelves. She wears a simple dark sweater; her expression is calm and approachable, looking slightly downward toward an open notebook, which is partially visible in the lower part of the frame. The portrait uses natural, diffused light and has a shallow depth of field so the shelves behind are blurred into soft blocks of tone.

Finally, the contact block is understated but clear. It begins with a left-aligned serif subheading: “Current availability”. Below it, in sans-serif: “Booking new subscription builds from October 2025 onwards.” Under that, another serif subheading: “Get in touch”. Below, there is a very simple contact panel with two lines: “Email: hello@alyssaray.studio” in charcoal, and “Detailed project brief (optional)” in gray, followed by a static representation of a text area – just a light gray rectangular outline with softly rounded corners and faint placeholder text inside in light gray italics reading: “Share your bookstore, your team, and what you hope a tutorial might change.” To the right of the textarea is a thin, minimalist rectangular button with a charcoal border and the word “Send note” in small uppercase sans-serif, charcoal, centered. There is no visual suggestion of interaction beyond the existence of the button. The very bottom of the page has a quiet footer: a thin horizontal rule, then in tiny sans-serif, centered: “© 2025 Alyssa Ray. Built low-tech, designed for quiet attention.” All elements across the page maintain the same muted palette, restrained typography, static imagery, and abundant white space, conveying a portfolio that is both functional and aesthetically aligned with the contemplative atmosphere of independent bookstores, with a subtle emphasis on subscription-based tutorial work and an integrated, clearly readable static data visualization.

### Dataset
```csv
Client Type,Number of Clients
Independent bookstores,18
Small presses,7
Other cultural orgs,5
```

instruction: Planning the generation details for the following website:
input:
Design a single-page research website for a fictional “Swiss School of Visual Systems,” presented in a strict Swiss-style (International Typographic Style) aesthetic, serving as a research hub and tutorial homepage for a “Modular Surfboard Hydrodynamics Lab.” The overall layout uses a consistent two-column grid (left column approximately 35% width, right column 65%), but embraces deliberate asymmetries within sections. The background is pure white (#FFFFFF) with abundant negative space. All typography is sans serif, using a Helvetica-like font. Use a strict typographic hierarchy: small uppercase for labels, large bold titles, and light-weight body text. The main accent color is a rich ultramarine blue (#2246FF), accompanied by neutral grays (#111111 for main text, #777777 for secondary text, subtle grid lines in #E6E6E6). The default text alignment is left, never centered, but blocks are aligned to an invisible grid, producing a clean, editorial layout.

At the very top, include a thin horizontal strip (about 60px tall) spanning full width, with no background color, just elements carefully placed. On the far left, aligned to the page margin, place the site logo as pure text in all caps: “SWISS SCHOOL OF VISUAL SYSTEMS” in small, tight uppercase Helvetica, black, aligned to the top edge of the strip. Directly beneath it, in medium weight blue text, slightly indented, is a smaller line: “Research Lab: Modular Surfboard Hydrodynamics”. On the far right of this top strip, aligned to the baseline of the logo, place a minimal navigation list in uppercase, spaced with clear gaps and no borders: “RESEARCH”, “TUTORIALS”, “PUBLICATIONS”, “ABOUT”, “CONTACT”. Each nav item is black text; the currently active section, “RESEARCH”, is in blue and underlined with a thin, precise 1px blue line flush to the baseline.

Below this top strip, the hero section occupies most of the initial viewport. It adheres to the two-column structure. On the left column, positioned with generous top margin, present a bold, large title in black: “HYDRODYNAMIC SYSTEMS FOR MODULAR SURF PRACTICE”. The title is set in uppercase, with each word on its own line, ragged-right, forming a compact vertical block of text. Immediately below, a thin horizontal rule in light gray (#E6E6E6) spans the width of the left column. Under this rule, a short lead paragraph of body text in dark gray explains the lab: “A research-driven framework for teaching surfboard dynamics, paddling efficiency, and wave interaction through modular, reproducible experiments.” This text is 14–16px, light weight, generous line spacing, and aligned to the grid. Under the paragraph, include a small, uppercase label in blue: “CURRENT STUDY — 2025.01” and beneath it in black, slightly larger: “TUTORIAL: MEASURING DRAG IN CONTROLLED WAVE POOLS”. To reinforce the Swiss-style, there is no button in the hero; instead, a text link at the bottom of this column reads: “View methodology ↓” in blue, underlined, set in small caps, aligned to the same left margin.

In the right column of the hero section, place a large, static hero image illustrative of the research, using a minimalist, flat yet slightly technical style. The image shows a top-down axonometric view of a modular surfboard and simplified wave pool. The surfboard appears as a clean, elongated, rounded rectangular shape, white with a faint mid-gray outline, segmented into three modules separated by thin, dashed blue lines. Between segments, tiny black labels in a monospaced style read “NOSE MODULE”, “CORE MODULE”, “TAIL MODULE”. The background suggests a rectangular wave tank: a flat, pale blue plane with subtle parallel lines indicating wave direction, drawn as thin, evenly spaced, slightly curved blue lines flowing from left to right. In the top right of the image, a minimal coordinate grid can be seen: thin gray lines forming a grid pattern, with two axes labeled in small black text “FLOW (m/s)” on the horizontal axis and “DRAG (N)” on the vertical axis, though this grid is merely decorative and has no plotted data. Lighting is neutral and shadowless, as in a technical illustration. The composition is deliberately offset so that the surfboard is slightly lower than center, leaving white space at the top. The entire image is framed by a very thin gray line that aligns with the underlying grid of the page.

Beneath the hero, introduce a section titled “RESEARCH OVERVIEW” that spans the full width but still uses the two-column layout for its inner content. On the far left, at the top of this section, place a small, uppercase label in blue: “RESEARCH PROGRAM”. Directly below it, in bold black, slightly larger than body text, is the headline: “From ocean waves to typographic grids.” Beneath, a brief paragraph in dark gray explains the conceptual bridge between surf hydrodynamics and Swiss-style systems design: “Our lab models surf conditions using modular, parameter-driven experiments. Each tutorial decomposes complex wave behaviors into discrete, visual modules that can be taught, tested, and replicated.” In the right column of this section, create three vertically stacked “research axis” blocks, separated by thin gray lines. Each block has a small uppercase heading in black, followed by a one-sentence description in gray: “AXIS 01: BOARD GEOMETRY” with “Study the influence of length, rocker, and edge sharpness on drag and stability.”; “AXIS 02: PADDLING EFFICIENCY” with “Measure stroke cadence, arm angle, and board displacement in controlled conditions.”; “AXIS 03: WAVE INTERACTION” with “Analyze how modular boards respond to varying wave heights, periods, and angles.” Each title is left-aligned, with the axis number in blue, and the text aligned on a vertical grid.

Next, a “Key Metrics” data visualization section appears, continuing the two-column structure but visually distinguished by a subtle, pale gray background band (#FAFAFA) spanning the full width from edge to edge, with generous top and bottom padding. On the left column, place a concise heading in uppercase black: “KEY HYDRODYNAMIC METRICS” and below it, slightly smaller in gray: “Comparison of drag across board configurations (test series M-24).” Insert a short note beneath in very small gray text: “Data collected at 1.5 m/s flow in a controlled wave pool using 3-axis load cells.” On the right column, present a static, precise bar chart visualization. The bar chart is drawn on a white rectangle with a thin black border, sitting within the gray band, aligned to the right column’s grid. The chart title at the top in small uppercase black reads: “AVERAGE DRAG FOR MODULAR SURFBOARD CONFIGURATIONS”. The horizontal x-axis lists four configurations: “Short Flat”, “Mid Hybrid”, “Long Curved”, “Control Board”. These labels are set in small, black, sans serif text, slightly rotated or tightly spaced but still readable. The vertical y-axis represents average drag in Newtons, labeled “Drag (N)” in uppercase to the left of the axis. The axis has a range from 0 to 60, with tick marks at 0, 10, 20, 30, 40, 50, and 60, each labeled in small gray text. Each configuration is represented by a single vertical bar with no gradients: “Short Flat” in solid ultramarine blue (#2246FF), “Mid Hybrid” in a lighter blue (#6284FF), “Long Curved” in black (#111111), and “Control Board” in medium gray (#B0B0B0). The bar heights are proportional to specific values: “Short Flat” at 42 N, “Mid Hybrid” at 37 N, “Long Curved” at 29 N, and “Control Board” at 51 N. The bars are separated by equal white space and aligned to the underlying grid. Above each bar, place the numeric value in small black text, e.g., “42 N”, “37 N”, etc. Below the chart, aligned to the right, a miniature legend shows four small squares in the corresponding colors with labels “Optimized Speed (Short Flat)”, “Balanced Training (Mid Hybrid)”, “Stability Focus (Long Curved)”, and “Baseline (Control Board)” in tiny gray text.

Following this, an “Experimental Tutorials” section appears, structured as a vertical list of tutorials in the left column, with a corresponding visual index in the right column. The section heading in the left column reads in uppercase black: “EXPERIMENTAL TUTORIAL SERIES”. Underneath, in smaller gray text: “Guided, repeatable modules for surf schools and research studios.” Below, three tutorial entries are listed, separated by subtle horizontal gray rules, each numbered with a large, blue, monospaced number in the left margin of this column (for example “01”, “02”, “03”), aligned to a narrow vertical strip. Each entry has a bold title in black and a brief description: “01 — Baseline Paddling Trial” in bold black, with a subtitle in gray: “Establish a reference stroke cadence and drag profile for your current setup.”; “02 — Modular Nose Swap” with subtitle: “Test how different nose shapes affect early wave entry and paddling resistance.”; “03 — Tail Rocker Iterations” with subtitle: “Explore tail curvature’s impact on maneuverability without sacrificing stability.” To the right of each entry’s text, in the right column, place a simple, abstract static diagram corresponding to each tutorial, rendered in black, blue, and gray on white. For “Baseline Paddling Trial,” the image shows a side view outline of a surfer lying on a board, minimalistic and geometric: simple line segments for body and board, with three parallel blue lines underneath to indicate water flow. For “Modular Nose Swap,” the image shows three board nose profiles in a row from left to right (flat, pointed, rounded), each a simple outline with a small blue dot marking the attachment point. For “Tail Rocker Iterations,” depict three board tails stacked diagonally: one almost flat, one moderately curved, and one strongly upturned, all minimal outlines with a baseline horizontal gray reference line showing their different angles. Each diagram is contained in a small square frame with a thin border, aligned to the grid.

Below the tutorials, insert a minimalist “Methodology Notes” section that intentionally breaks the strict two-column symmetry. Let the left column contain only a narrow vertical stack of uppercase labels in gray, serving almost as a typographic key: “VARIABLES”, “INSTRUMENTS”, “ENVIRONMENT”. Each label is in small uppercase with generous spacing between letters. The right column is wider here and contains three corresponding paragraphs, each aligned with one of the left labels via horizontal rules. Under “VARIABLES” describe concisely in black text: “Board length, nose profile, tail rocker, fin placement, wave height, and flow speed.” Under “INSTRUMENTS”: “3-axis load cells, motion-tracking markers, synchronized metronome audio, and calibrated depth sensors.” Under “ENVIRONMENT”: “Indoor wave pool at 22°C, laminar flow section for baseline tests, choppy section for advanced maneuvers.” Separate these paragraphs with thin gray horizontal lines that extend from the left edge of the right column to its right margin, tying them back visually to the labels.

Further down, introduce a “Publications & Data” section, again restoring the two-column structure. On the left, in uppercase black: “PUBLICATIONS & DATASETS,” and beneath, in gray: “Selected outputs from the Modular Surfboard Hydrodynamics Lab.” Below, list three items in a simple typographic style, each item number in blue followed by a title and small annotation: “Paper 01 — ‘Parametric Modularity in Surfboard Drag Reduction’ (Preprint, 2024).”; “Paper 02 — ‘Visualizing Wave–Board Interaction Through Grid-Based Systems’ (Journal Submission, 2025).”; “Dataset 03 — ‘M-24 Drag Series: Baseline & Modular Configurations’ (CSV Download).” The right column holds a clean, static table-like visualization of a subset of the dataset, without gridlines on the page but align the values to an invisible grid. At the top right, a small label in uppercase blue reads: “EXCERPT — M-24 DRAG SERIES”. Underneath, rows of text-labeled data are presented monospaced, like a printed table: headings in black “Config | Length (cm) | Nose Type | Tail Rocker (°) | Avg Drag (N)” followed by a few sample rows in gray with occasional blue values: “Short Flat | 180 | Flat | 2 | 42”, “Mid Hybrid | 200 | Hybrid | 4 | 37”, “Long Curved | 220 | Curved | 6 | 29”, “Control Board | 210 | Standard | 3 | 51”. Keep the alignment precise and monospaced to echo technical documentation.

An “About the Lab” section comes next. Use generous top margin and white space. In the left column, the heading reads in uppercase black: “ABOUT THE LAB”. Beneath, in small gray text: “Swiss School of Visual Systems — Surf Hydrodynamics Unit.” The right column contains a concise block of body copy in black: “The Modular Surfboard Hydrodynamics Lab is an interdisciplinary research unit at the Swiss School of Visual Systems. Drawing from fluid dynamics, interaction design, and typographic systems thinking, the lab develops tutorials and tools for surf schools, coaches, and independent researchers. Our methods prioritize clarity, reproducibility, and visual structure.” Below this text, aligned to the same right column, a small text-only block in uppercase gray lists location and contact details: “BASEL, SWITZERLAND / RESEARCH@SSVS.CH”.

At the bottom of the page, include a stripped-down footer that again reveals the underlying grid. A thin horizontal rule in light gray spans the full width. Below, the footer is split into two columns, but with uneven vertical content. On the left, in small uppercase gray text: “© 2025 SWISS SCHOOL OF VISUAL SYSTEMS — MODULAR SURFBOARD HYDRODYNAMICS LAB”. Under that, a line in blue: “Download full dataset and methodology (PDF, CSV).” On the right side of the footer, right-aligned, add three small, all-caps text links in black, evenly spaced: “IMPRINT”, “PRIVACY”, “ARCHIVE”. There are no icons or social media logos to maintain the austere aesthetic. The entire page feels like a carefully set research catalog: no rounded corners, no gradients (beyond the flat colors described), no dropshadows, and no animations or motion of any kind—only static text, diagrams, and the single bar chart. Whitespace and the typographic grid are the primary “design elements,” emphasizing a clean Swiss-style research identity for a surfboard hydrodynamics tutorial homepage.

### Dataset

```csv
Configuration,Length_cm,Nose_Type,Tail_Rocker_deg,Avg_Drag_N
Short Flat,180,Flat,2,42
Mid Hybrid,200,Hybrid,4,37
Long Curved,220,Curved,6,29
Control Board,210,Standard,3,51
```

instruction: Planning the generation details for the following website:
input:
Design a vertically scrollable single-page landing site for a high-end finance consulting firm called “Helvetic Capital Studio”, using a strict Swiss-style typography aesthetic with strong grids, bold alignments, large negative space, and controlled asymmetry. The entire page sits on an off-white canvas (#F7F7F5) with a subtle paper-like texture barely visible, and a fixed, ultra-thin baseline grid implied by the consistent vertical spacing between elements. The overall feel is minimal, typographic, and editorial, with occasional overlapping panels and blocks of solid color that create a hierarchical layout. The main color palette is black (#111111), pure white (#FFFFFF), a saturated red (#E10600) used sparingly as an accent, and a cool mid-gray (#B3B3B3) for secondary text and rules. Typefaces are limited to two: a primary grotesk sans-serif reminiscent of Helvetica Neue used in multiple weights (Light, Regular, Bold, Black) and a monospaced font reminiscent of IBM Plex Mono for tiny labels, axis text, and small data notations. The entire page uses left-aligned text, except where explicitly stated, and almost no rounded corners—everything is sharp, geometric, and rectilinear.

At the very top, a rigid 12-column grid spans the full width of the viewport with generous margins on left and right (approximately 100px each on desktop). A fixed, horizontal, white navigation bar sits pinned to the top edge, extending across the whole width with a 1px bottom rule in very light gray (#E0E0E0). On the far left, in a bold, black sans-serif, all caps, is the logo text: “HELVETIC CAPITAL STUDIO”. The first line “HELVETIC” appears in slightly larger type above “CAPITAL STUDIO” which is aligned flush left; they are stacked, but tightly leading, occupying about 2 columns in the grid. On the far right of the nav, aligned along the same baseline, is a compact navigation menu composed of four links set in uppercase, regular weight: “SERVICES”, “INSIGHTS”, “ABOUT”, “CONTACT”. They are spaced evenly, with wide tracking and visible between-item spacing, and on hover they are imagined to invert from black text on white background to white text in a red (#E10600) rectangular highlight. Between the logo and menu, aligned to the center-right, is a small label in the monospaced font reading “Zurich / London” in mid-gray, acting as a subtle location marker. The nav bar casts a hairline drop shadow (extremely subtle) to separate it from the hero section beneath.

The hero section immediately below fills most of the initial viewport height with a strong typographic composition that uses overlapping blocks. On the left two-thirds of the hero, beginning slightly below the nav, is the main headline: “Precision in Capital, Clarity in Strategy” set in huge, bold sans-serif letters. The words are broken across three lines: “Precision in Capital,” on the first, “Clarity in” on the second, and “Strategy” on the third. The first two lines are in black; the last word “Strategy” is in red (#E10600), making it the focal point. The lines are left-aligned, with significant leading, and slightly overlapping a vertical red bar that runs from the top of the hero down about two-thirds of its height on the left side. This red bar is about one column wide, flush with the left page margin, and sits behind part of the headline, so that the text appears to cross its edge; the overlapping is achieved by the text being layered in front. To the right of the headline, occupying the right third of the hero, is a vertical stack of three blocks: at the top, a small label in uppercase mono font: “INDEPENDENT FINANCIAL CONSULTANCY”; below it, a short paragraph in regular weight, black, small size that says: “We build disciplined, data-driven strategies for family offices, founders, and funds navigating complex global markets. Our practice combines Swiss typographic rigor with quantitative research and clear, actionable advisory.” Beneath this paragraph is a very restrained call-to-action: a rectangular outlined button with black 1px border and no fill, containing the text “REQUEST A STRATEGY SESSION” in uppercase, small sans-serif, black. On hover, imagine the button’s background filling with black and the text in white, but there is no animation described—only the state. Beneath that main CTA, aligned left and separated by a bit of space, is a secondary textual link (no button styling) in gray that reads: “Download firm overview (PDF)” with a thin, horizontal gray line extending about 40px to its right, reinforcing the Swiss grid aesthetic.

On the far right edge of the hero, partially overlapping the main hero text area but constrained to the grid, is a tall, white card with a very light gray border. This card is labelled at the top in small uppercase mono: “SELECTED METRICS — 2024”. Inside the card is a minimalist bar chart visualization illustrating client portfolio performance versus benchmark, designed in a strict Swiss-inspired data style. The chart background is pure white, with a very thin gray frame and a discrete grid of horizontal light gray lines. The x-axis has three category labels in tiny sans-serif: “HCS CLIENTS”, “MSCI WORLD”, “EURO STOXX 50”. The y-axis is percentage return, running from 0% at the bottom to 18% at the top, with four thin tick labels: “0%”, “6%”, “12%”, “18%” in monospaced gray text. Each category has a single vertical bar: for “HCS CLIENTS”, a solid black bar reaching up to 14%; for “MSCI WORLD”, a lighter gray bar reaching 9%; for “EURO STOXX 50”, a gray bar reaching 7%. Above the black bar, in tiny red uppercase, is the label “+14.0%”; above the gray bars, in gray uppercase, “+9.0%” and “+7.0%” respectively. At the bottom right of the chart, aligned to its border, there is a tiny caption in monospaced font: “Net annualized returns, 2019–2024 (model composite)”. This card visually overlaps the right side of the hero background but has a clean shadowless cut-out appearance, emphasizing the structured, paper-like Swiss layout.

Below the hero, a wide horizontal strip stretches across the page with a light gray background (#EFEFEF). This strip uses a 3-column modular layout to introduce service pillars. On the left edge of this strip, occupying the first column and aligned top, is a vertical red label rotated 90 degrees (text still described in normal orientation: “SERVICE LINES”) in uppercase mono, adding a graphic element. The three service blocks are each in their own column, with no boxes—just text, rules, and space. Each block has a small red square (10x10px) at the top left as a bullet, followed by the service title in uppercase bold sans-serif: “STRATEGIC ALLOCATION”, “RISK & LIQUIDITY”, “FAMILY OFFICE ADVISORY”. Below each title is a short 2–3 line description in regular black: for Strategic Allocation: “Designing long-term, multi-asset portfolios aligned with your real-life obligations, not just market indices.” For Risk & Liquidity: “Scenario modeling, downside mapping, and liquidity planning calibrated to your actual cash flows.” For Family Office Advisory: “Operating models, governance structures, and reporting frameworks that scale with your capital.” Between each column is a thin vertical gray rule spanning the height of the strip, reinforcing the grid.

The next section is “Our approach,” combining overlapping typographic blocks and a secondary data visualization. The background returns to off-white. A bold, left-aligned section title near the top left reads “Method, not guesswork.” in large black sans-serif. The period at the end is red, providing a subtle accent. Immediately beneath this title, partially tucked under its bottom edge, is a horizontal white panel spanning about two-thirds of the width, containing a three-step process laid out horizontally. Each step has a numeric label in big, hollow outlined numbers (“01”, “02”, “03”) in light gray, with the actual text set on top in black. Under “01”, text reads: “Diagnose” on one line, and below: “We map your existing structures, exposures, and constraints with forensic detail.” Under “02”: “Design” and “We model allocations, liquidity rails, and risk budgets for clarity under stress.” Under “03”: “Deploy” and “We partner with your existing banks and managers to implement and monitor, not replace.” The three blocks are separated by thin vertical rules aligned to the grid. The overlapping effect is that the top edge of this panel slightly intrudes over the final line of the section title, but the type remains readable because of layering order.

To the right of this process panel, occupying the remaining third of the width and aligned to its top, is another clean data visualization—a minimalist line chart showing portfolio drawdown vs. benchmark during a hypothetical stress period. The chart is inside a white rectangle with very fine border. The title above the chart, in small uppercase, reads: “MAX DRAWDOWN — CLIENT STRATEGY VS. BENCHMARK (2020–2022)”. The chart itself has a white background and a single set of faint horizontal gridlines. The horizontal axis is time, with labels “Q1 ’20”, “Q2 ’20”, “Q3 ’20”, “Q4 ’20”, “Q1 ’21”, “Q2 ’21” in mono gray along the bottom. The vertical axis represents drawdown in %, from 0% at the top to -30% at the bottom, with tick labels “0%”, “-10%”, “-20%”, “-30%” along the left. There are two lines: a thick black line for “HCS CLIENT STRATEGY” and a thinner gray line for “MSCI WORLD”. In Q1 ’20, both lines start at 0%. By Q2 ’20, the gray line plunges to -28%, while the black line reaches only -15%. Over subsequent quarters, both lines trend back toward 0%, with the black line recovering faster and crossing -5% by Q4 ’20, while the gray line remains around -10%. In Q1 ’21 and Q2 ’21, both lines hover around -3% and -2% respectively. A small legend beneath the chart uses black and gray line segments followed by labels “HCS CLIENT STRATEGY” and “MSCI WORLD”. A fine red horizontal rule at -20% highlights that level, with a tiny red mono label at the right end: “Stress threshold”.

Continuing downward, the next major section showcases brief case-style vignettes with a strong typographic grid and an overlapping image panel. The section starts with a small red uppercase label: “SELECTED SCENARIOS”. Directly under it, occupying the left half of the page, is a stacked set of three narrow case headers, each separated by thick, black horizontal rules. The first header reads “From liquidity chaos to a 3-year runway.” in bold black. Under it, in small regular text, a one-sentence description: “Founder with a concentrated equity position restructured into a sequenced liquidity ladder with defined drawdown tolerances.” The second header: “Reframing risk for a multi-generational mandate.”; description: “Family office portfolio shifted from product-driven exposure to goal-based allocation across public and private markets.” The third: “Integrating ESG without performance drag.”; description: “Institutional mandate re-engineered to meet ESG guidelines while maintaining tracking error within pre-agreed bounds.” These case snippets are dense, text-only, arranged vertically with equal spacing.

On the right half of this section, overlapping the vertical middle of the case headers, is a large square static image, described textually: a high-contrast, black-and-white photograph in a stark, modernist style. The image shows an overhead view of a modern concrete staircase inside a minimalist building, shot from the top looking straight down. The composition emphasizes sharp diagonal lines of the stair edges and railings that intersect the frame, creating a repeating geometric pattern of rectangles and parallelograms. Shadows fall in clean, crisp planes, creating alternating bands of light and dark. No people are visible; the image is purely architectural. The photo is framed with 20px of white border against the off-white page background, almost like a print mounted on a gallery wall, and a tiny caption in mono beneath reads: “Structured decisions, layered over time.”

Following the case section, a narrower band presents a compact “Credentials & alignment” area with a more compressed layout. A single line of text, running horizontally and aligned to the left margin, reads in small uppercase gray: “REGULATED. INDEPENDENT. FEE-ONLY.” Between each word is a 4px red square as a tiny separator. Beneath this line, on the left side, a vertical list of small, mono-styled bullet items in black: “> FINMA-REGISTERED PARTNERS”, “> NO RETROCESSIONS OR PRODUCT COMMISSIONS”, “> DISCRETE, NON-PUBLIC CLIENT WORK”. On the right side of this band, right-aligned but within the grid, is a simple, unframed, all-caps label: “We sit on your side of the table.” set in bold black, serving as a thematic statement.

The “Insights” section comes next, styled like a modern financial journal index, with asymmetric layout. A red horizontal rule spans two-thirds of the width, starting at the left margin. Above the left end of that rule, a tiny uppercase mono label reads: “INSIGHTS”. Below the rule, three article previews are arranged in a two-column asymmetrical layout: the first article occupies the entire left column vertically and the other two stack in the right column. Each article preview consists purely of text (no image), with strong typographic hierarchy. For the first article (left column): the date in mono gray, like “04.2025”; then the title in bold black sentence case: “What a 200 bps regime shift really means for your allocation.”; then a 2–3 line synopsis in regular gray: “Why regime shifts demand structural responses instead of tactical patchwork—and how to measure the real cost of inertia.” At the end of the synopsis, a right-angled arrow symbol “→” in black, implying a link. On the right column, the first article (top): date “02.2025”, title “Liquidity is not the same as optionality.” and a brief synopsis: “How to design liquidity rails that preserve optionality without forcing you to sell at the wrong time.” The second article (below it): date “12.2024”, title “Your benchmark is not your boundary.” and synopsis: “Why sticking to a single market index can hide concentration risk and misalign your time horizon.” Each item is separated with extra whitespace, allowing the grid to breathe.

Near the bottom, the “About & contact” section merges firm description and a clear contact call, with overlapping columns. On the left side, within roughly 7 columns of the grid, the heading reads in bold: “A small studio for complex capital.” followed by a body paragraph in regular text: “Helvetic Capital Studio is an independent advisory practice led by partners with backgrounds in investment banking, quantitative research, and family office management. We work with a limited number of clients at any given time, so strategy and implementation remain aligned.” Below this paragraph is a smaller text block labeled “We typically work with:” in gray, followed by a list of three bullet points (simple black square bullets): “Founders with concentrated equity positions”, “Family offices between CHF 50m–500m AUM”, “Institutions seeking a second-opinion risk lens”. On the right side, overlapping slightly into the left area but still within the grid, is a white contact card with a thin black border. At the top of the card in uppercase mono is the title “Request a conversation”. Below are three minimal fields represented as underlined labels rather than form inputs: “Name”, “Email”, and “Context (optional)”, each followed by a long horizontal gray line where the user would imagine entering text. The labels are in small uppercase sans-serif, black; the lines are faint gray. At the bottom right of the card is a solid black rectangular button with white uppercase text: “SEND INQUIRY”. Directly beneath the card, aligned to the left boundary of the card, is a tiny gray mono disclaimer: “No mailing lists. No blasts. One considered reply.”

Finally, the footer anchors the page with a strong horizontal band of black at the very bottom, about 180px tall, spanning the full width. Inside this black band, the grid persists. On the left, in small white mono text, is the firm name repeated: “HELVETIC CAPITAL STUDIO”. Below it, in slightly lighter gray, are the office coordinates: “Zurich — Talacker 41, 8001 Zurich” and “London — 25 Copthall Ave, EC2R 7BP”. On the right side of the footer are two narrow columns. The first column lists, in tiny uppercase gray with small leading, links: “SERVICES”, “INSIGHTS”, “ABOUT”, “CONTACT”, each on its own line. The second column has regulatory and privacy text: “Regulatory disclosures”, “Privacy policy”. At the bottom right corner of the footer, in subtle gray mono text, is the line: “© 2025 Helvetic Capital Studio. All rights reserved.” A hairline red vertical rule extends upward about 40px from the top edge of the footer into the main page, located at one of the grid column boundaries, creating a visual hook that ties the bottom of the page back into the red system used throughout. The overall impression is of a precisely structured, typographically driven landing page for a finance consulting studio, with clear, measured hierarchies, overlapping panels that respect an underlying grid, a strict Swiss design lineage, and two clearly defined data visualizations anchored by explicit numeric values that reinforce the firm’s quantitative credentials, all without any motion or video.

### Dataset

```csv
Chart,Category,Value
Bar,HCS CLIENTS,14.0
Bar,MSCI WORLD,9.0
Bar,EURO STOXX 50,7.0
Line,Q1 2020,HCS CLIENT STRATEGY,0
Line,Q1 2020,MSCI WORLD,0
Line,Q2 2020,HCS CLIENT STRATEGY,-15
Line,Q2 2020,MSCI WORLD,-28
Line,Q3 2020,HCS CLIENT STRATEGY,-8
Line,Q3 2020,MSCI WORLD,-15
Line,Q4 2020,HCS CLIENT STRATEGY,-5
Line,Q4 2020,MSCI WORLD,-10
Line,Q1 2021,HCS CLIENT STRATEGY,-3
Line,Q1 2021,MSCI WORLD,-4
Line,Q2 2021,HCS CLIENT STRATEGY,-2
Line,Q2 2021,MSCI WORLD,-3
```

instruction: Planning the generation details for the following website:
input:
Design a single-page, Swiss-style, multi-column dashboard homepage for a “home renovation project intelligence platform” called “PlanGrid Studio,” with a focus on crisp typography, rigorous alignment, and a grid-driven layout that still allows subtle asymmetries. The visual concept is a designer’s control center for tracking multiple renovation projects at once. The overall page background is a soft off-white (“#F7F7F5”) to evoke drafting paper, with key content blocks sitting on pure white cards (“#FFFFFF”) and accent lines in a muted architectural blue (“#345C7D”) and muted concrete gray (“#A4A9AD”). The typography is strictly Swiss-style: use a Helvetica or Univers-like grotesk sans serif throughout; text is always left-aligned except for a few intentionally centered labels; headings are bold and often in all caps, with ample leading and generous margins. The layout is built on a visible 12-column grid that subtly reveals itself via thin, semi-transparent vertical guides: faint 1px vertical lines in very light gray (“#E3E3E0”) running from top to bottom, spaced evenly, giving a sense of order without overwhelming.

At the very top, a narrow horizontal top bar spans full width, 72px high, with a white background and a 1px bottom border in light gray (“#E0E0DD”). On the far left is the logo area: the wordmark “PlanGrid Studio” in uppercase, using a bold, slightly condensed sans serif. “PLAN” and “STUDIO” are in black, while “GRID” is in the architectural blue, all in a single line: “PLAN GRID STUDIO”. The logo is flush left with the first grid column and has generous padding above and below so it sits exactly vertically centered in the bar. To the right of the wordmark, aligned along the same baseline, is a tiny square icon: a 16x16px outline of a house made from thin, clean lines, in black stroke, with a small grid pattern of three light-gray squares inside the house shape, representing modular renovations. In the center of the top bar, but slightly to the right (asymmetrical placement), are three primary navigation links: “Dashboard”, “Projects”, “Reports”, in uppercase, small font size; each is spaced widely so the whitespace feels deliberate. “Dashboard” is black, indicating the active state, while “Projects” and “Reports” are medium gray (“#7D7F82”). On hover, they would be underlined with a 1px blue line (mentioned in the design). On the far right of the top bar, aligned to the right-most grid column, there is a compact profile area: a small circular avatar placeholder (28px diameter) filled with a neutral gradient (light gray to slightly darker gray), with an initial “M” in the center in white; to its left, in very small uppercase letters, is “MIA KELLER” in black, and below it, lighter gray text “Lead Designer”. Next to the avatar, a simple outlined icon of three horizontal lines (a minimalist menu) in black sits as a touchpoint for secondary controls.

Below the top bar, the main content is divided vertically into a left column (about 35% width), center column (about 40% width), and right column (about 25% width), all aligned strictly to the 12-column grid but with different heights to create an asymmetrical rhythm. There is a top margin of 40px between the top bar and the main content. The left column starts with a large typographic hero block: against the off-white background (no card, just the bare page), a bold heading reads “Renovations, rendered in real time.” in black. This line is set in large, uppercase-like letterspacing but sentence case text, with tight tracking and generous leading, spanning about 5 grid columns wide. Directly below, a smaller sub-heading in medium gray reads: “Track budgets, timelines, and on-site progress across every room, floor, and facade—with Swiss precision.” The sub-heading is narrower in width, with slightly smaller font size and more line spacing. Beneath the sub-heading, a horizontal row of two pill-shaped filter controls sits flush left: each is a simple outline in light gray, with labels in uppercase black text: the first says “ACTIVE PROJECTS (7)”, the second “THIS QUARTER”. The currently active filter (“ACTIVE PROJECTS (7)”) is filled with a very pale blue (“#EEF3F8”) and has its text in blue, while the inactive filter is white with gray text. A faint 1px vertical divider line aligns exactly with the right edge of this hero text block, continuing down behind subsequent sections, reinforcing the grid.

Directly under the hero text on the left, there is a tall, white card labeled “Project Priority Queue,” occupying about half the viewport height, with a subtle 4px corner radius and a 1px border in very light gray. The card’s header is a narrow strip at the top, with the title “Project Priority Queue” in uppercase, small bold black type on the left, and a tiny label on the right side that reads “Sorted by risk score” in light gray, smaller text. Inside the card, the content is structured like a minimalist table. There are six project rows, each 48px high, separated by hairline light-gray horizontal rules. Each row contains, from left to right, black text for the project name, medium-gray text for location, and a bold numeric risk score aligned right. For example, the first row shows “Loft Conversion — Green Street” in black; below it in smaller gray, “Zurich, 3rd district”, with a small colored tag next to the project name: a narrow vertical bar (4px wide, 24px high) on the far left colored in a strong muted orange (“#E67E22”) to indicate high risk. On the far right of this row, flush right, the number “87” appears in bold black, with a small label under it in gray text “Risk score”. The second row is “Terraced House Extension — Elm Row”, location “Basel, riverside”; color tag in yellow (“#F1C40F”), risk score “72”. The third is “Kitchen + Bath Refresh — Lakeview Apt.”, “Geneva, lakeside”; color tag in blue (“#3498DB”), risk score “55”. The fourth is “Roof Insulation Upgrade — Old Mill”, “Bern, industrial quarter”; tag in orange, risk score “80”. The fifth is “Facade Restoration — Lindenplatz”, “Zurich, old town”; tag in yellow, risk score “64”. The sixth is “Garden Studio Build — Forest Edge”, “Lucerne, hillside”; tag in blue, risk score “43”. Hover states are implied: row background becomes a very faint gray, but the design stays flat and crisp, with no heavy shadows.

The central column is the main data visualization area, creating a focal point. Near the top, aligned so the top edges line up with the hero block and the priority card, there is a wide white card titled “Budget vs. Timeline Overview”. The title sits in the top-left of the card in bold black uppercase text, with a small gray label under it “Current quarter: Q3 2025”. In the top-right of the card, there is a tiny dropdown label “View: All projects ▼” in light gray text. The card occupies roughly 60% of the viewport height, emphasizing its importance. Inside this card, on a flat white background, is a restrained, Swiss-style combination chart that introduces color with discipline. The x-axis runs horizontally and has six equally spaced ticks labeled in small, uppercase gray text: “Project 1”, “Project 2”, “Project 3”, “Project 4”, “Project 5”, “Project 6”. The y-axis on the left is labeled “Budget (kCHF)” in vertical, uppercase gray text, with tick marks at 0, 50, 100, 150, 200, 250, each labeled. A second y-axis on the right, in lighter gray, is labeled “Timeline (weeks)” with ticks at 0, 10, 20, 30, 40. The chart itself is minimal, with thin lines and clean geometry. For each project there is a vertical bar showing budget: square-edged, about 20px wide, in muted architectural blue (“#345C7D”) with 80% opacity. The heights correspond to these explicit budget values: Project 1: 120, Project 2: 200, Project 3: 90, Project 4: 160, Project 5: 75, Project 6: 130 (all in kCHF). Superimposed over the bars is a thin orange line (“#E67E22”) with circular markers indicating timeline in weeks: Project 1: 18, Project 2: 32, Project 3: 14, Project 4: 26, Project 5: 12, Project 6: 20. The line is crisp, with no glow or shadow. Each marker is a small, solid circle, and when visually hovered (described in design, not animated), a small rectangular tooltip appears above it, white with a 1px gray border, shadowless, containing two lines: for example over Project 2 marker, the tooltip text reads “Loft Conversion — Green Street” on the first line in black, and “Budget: 200 kCHF · Timeline: 32 weeks” in gray below. Below the main chart, within the same card, is a compact legend aligned left, listing two items: a small blue square with label “Budget” and a small orange circle with label “Timeline”. To the right of the legend, aligned right, there is a line of tiny uppercase gray text “Source: Internal estimates · Updated daily”.

Directly beneath the “Budget vs. Timeline Overview” card in the center column, there is a slender, white, full-width strip that looks like a caption bar, 64px high, separated by 24px of whitespace. It contains a single line of large, uppercase, tightly tracked text: “SEVEN ACTIVE RENOVATIONS · FOUR CITIES · ONE CONTROL ROOM.” in black. This line is centered horizontally but left aligned within its grid container, so it runs from about column 3 to column 10, floating in the middle, creating a textual “rule” linking all columns visually. A very faint, light-gray horizontal line runs just above and below this strip, reinforcing the Swiss grid aesthetic without becoming ornamental.

The right column is a vertical stack of concise, statistic-driven cards. At the top, aligned with the top of the priority card on the left, is a short, wide card titled “Today’s Snapshot.” The title is in uppercase bold black, aligned top-left inside the card. The card is about 180px high, with a 1px gray border, no shadow, and a slightly off-white background (#FAFAF7) to differentiate it. Inside, a three-column grid displays key metrics. Each metric is a vertical pair: a small uppercase label in gray over a large number in black. From left to right: first metric label “ON-SCHEDULE TASKS” with value “142”; second metric label “DELAYED TASKS” with value “19” in orange; third metric “TEAMS ON SITE” with value “11” in blue. Beneath these, in a single line at the bottom, sits a mini sparkline chart: a thin horizontal line in gray with small black dots indicating daily task completion over the last week, no x-axis labels, just a single tiny label at the far right in light gray: “Last 7 days”.

Below “Today’s Snapshot” in the right column, there is another card titled “Room-by-Room Progress.” This card is taller, roughly 280px high, and contains a clean, text-based progress breakdown with lightly colored horizontal bars. The header text “Room-by-Room Progress” is in black uppercase on the top-left of the card, with a small filter label in the top-right: “Filter: Green Street Loft ▼” in light gray. Inside, there are four rows, each representing a key area, stacked vertically with generous spacing: “Kitchen”, “Bathroom”, “Living Room”, “Roof”. For each, the room name appears in black, and beside it, aligned right, a numeric completion percentage in medium gray. Under each label, a horizontal bar spans across, aligned perfectly to the grid. The “Kitchen” bar is 78% filled with blue (“#345C7D”) and labeled “78% Complete”; “Bathroom” is 64% in blue; “Living Room” is 52% in blue; “Roof” is 38% in orange (“#E67E22”) to indicate lagging. The unfilled portion of each bar is a very light gray. The bars are flat, with no gradients, and consistent height. There are no icons, only pure typographic hierarchy and rectangles.

The final card in the right column, at the bottom, is a narrow note-style block titled “Site Notes.” It has no border but is separated by generous whitespace and a single black hairline rule above it. The title “Site Notes” is in small uppercase black, and below it are three one-line notes, each prefixed by a thin horizontal dash, in a monospaced version of the sans-serif to emulate an architect’s annotation. The notes read: “— Inspect insulation joints on Old Mill roof before Friday.”, “— Confirm marble slab delivery for Lindenplatz facade.”, “— Schedule walk-through for Lakeview Apt. bathroom tiling.” The text is left-aligned, black, small, but with generous line height.

On the far left edge of the page, spanning the full height from beneath the top bar to the bottom, there is a minimal vertical sidebar navigation. It is only about 72px wide, with a pure white background and a subtle right border in light gray, aligning with the first grid line. This sidebar contains four simple line icons arranged vertically, centered: a small square house icon (current, in black), a stack of three rectangles for “Projects” (gray), a bar chart icon for “Analytics” (gray), and a cogwheel for “Settings” (gray). Each icon is minimal, drawn with thin strokes, and aligned to an underlying 8px grid. To the very bottom of this sidebar, in tiny uppercase gray text, vertically oriented and rotated 90 degrees, is the word “GRIDLINE 12”.

The footer of the page is a thin, understated band at the bottom, about 56px high, full width, with the same off-white background as the main page. A single horizontal black rule runs along its top edge. On the left side, aligned with the main grid, is tiny uppercase text in gray: “© 2025 PLAN GRID STUDIO — HOME RENOVATION INTELLIGENCE”. On the right side, aligned to the last grid column, are two links: “Imprint” and “Privacy”, in small uppercase black, spaced widely with no decoration; on hover they would show a minimalist underline.

No videos or animations are present anywhere; the interface is defined solely by typography, grids, and static, flat elements. All shadows, if present, are extremely subtle or absent; the overall look is sharp, planar, and highly legible. The entire composition should feel like a cross between a modern Swiss poster and an architect’s project board, emphasizing alignment, numeric clarity, and disciplined color usage, with the data visualization card in the center column acting as the primary data-driven focal point.

### Dataset

```csv
Chart,Project,Budget_kCHF,Timeline_weeks
Budget vs. Timeline Overview,Project 1,120,18
Budget vs. Timeline Overview,Project 2,200,32
Budget vs. Timeline Overview,Project 3,90,14
Budget vs. Timeline Overview,Project 4,160,26
Budget vs. Timeline Overview,Project 5,75,12
Budget vs. Timeline Overview,Project 6,130,20
Room-by-Room Progress,Kitchen,NA,78
Room-by-Room Progress,Bathroom,NA,64
Room-by-Room Progress,Living Room,NA,52
Room-by-Room Progress,Roof,NA,38
Today’s Snapshot,On-schedule tasks,NA,142
Today’s Snapshot,Delayed tasks,NA,19
Today’s Snapshot,Teams on site,NA,11
Project Priority Queue,Loft Conversion — Green Street,NA,87
Project Priority Queue,Terraced House Extension — Elm Row,NA,72
Project Priority Queue,Kitchen + Bath Refresh — Lakeview Apt.,NA,55
Project Priority Queue,Roof Insulation Upgrade — Old Mill,NA,80
Project Priority Queue,Facade Restoration — Lindenplatz,NA,64
Project Priority Queue,Garden Studio Build — Forest Edge,NA,43
```

instruction: Planning the generation details for the following website:
input:
Design a tall, single-column brutalist-style homepage for an “independent music festival subscription” portfolio site called “LOUDPASS”. The page should look like a designer’s portfolio but themed around an ongoing subscription concept for indie festivals, using a deliberately raw, utilitarian aesthetic: hard edges, almost no rounded corners, harsh contrast, visible grid lines, and large typography. The background of the entire page is a flat, slightly warm off-white (#f7f3ec) with occasional solid color blocks (neon green, electric blue, harsh yellow, deep black) breaking the monotony. The default typeface is a grotesque sans-serif, such as one that resembles “Nimbus Sans” or “Arial Black” for headings (all caps, very bold, tight letter-spacing), and a monospaced font (like a modern “Courier” clone) for body text. All text is left-aligned in the main column, except for a few intentionally jarring center-aligned subheadings. The layout is mostly single-column centered on desktop, about 900px wide, with wide margins on both sides in off-white, but brutalist design elements occasionally protrude into the left or right margins as if breaking the grid.

At the very top, a fixed-height, full-width header bar spans the screen with a solid black background. Inside, aligned to the main content column but visually stretching edge-to-edge, the site title “LOUDPASS” is in oversized white uppercase text on the left (font size about 32px, no logo, just text). Next to it, slightly lower and overlapping the baseline, in tiny neon green text (monospaced, all caps) is the subtitle: “INDEPENDENT FESTIVAL SUBSCRIPTION // PORTFOLIO”. On the right side of the header, aligned with the main column but pushed toward the edge, are three navigation links in an all-caps grotesque font, spaced far apart: “WORK”, “ABOUT”, “DATA”, each in mid-gray (#cccccc) that turns into harsh yellow (#ffe600) on hover. The links are plain words, no underlines, but each has a thin 1px bottom border in the same gray that snaps to yellow on hover. The header casts no shadow; instead, there is a 1px solid white line at its bottom, giving a sharp separation.

Below the header, after a gap of 40px, the hero section appears as a bold, asymmetrical block almost like a printed flyer taped onto the page. The hero is a large, off-center rectangle of electric blue (#1f5fff) occupying the width of the main column but with a 20px margin on the left and only a 5px margin on the right so it feels slightly shifted. The hero has no rounded corners and a thick 4px black border. Inside this blue block, on the left two-thirds, there is huge white text in uppercase: “SUBSCRIBE TO CHAOS.” (font size about 72px, line breaks so “SUBSCRIBE TO” is on one line, “CHAOS.” on the next). Below, smaller but still large white text reads: “A portfolio of one designer’s imaginary festival subscription product.” On the right third of the hero block is a brutalist hero image, surrounded by a white inner frame with a 3px black stroke. The image should be a highly stylized, almost poster-like flat design collage: a slightly tilted, rectangular ticket stub dominating the center, rendered in a flat, photorealistic-meets-poster style. The ticket is bright neon green with black text “LOUDPASS” and tiny repeated text “ADMIT ONE / REPEAT // REPEAT” along the edges, with distressed texture and visible noise to emulate silkscreen printing. Around the ticket, set against a black background, float abstract shapes resembling stacked speakers and tall scaffolding structures: tall, rectangular silhouettes with tiny white squares for speaker grills, some with diagonal stripes in yellow and magenta. A jagged, zig-zag lightning bolt in white cuts behind the ticket from top-left to bottom-right, adding motion. The composition is slightly perspective-tilted, like looking at a flat poster photographed from a small angle, causing subtle trapezoidal distortion. Lighting is minimal, more graphic than realistic; hard-edged shapes, no gradients except a faint noise overlay. The top-right of the image area has a small, white rectangular label with black text: “CASE STUDY // 2024”.

Below the hero, the main column continues. Directly beneath the hero, in the off-white background, is a narrow, full-width strip of text acting like a tagline banner, with a thin 1px black border on top and bottom only. Inside, center-aligned monospaced text in black reads: “THIS IS NOT A REAL PRODUCT. THIS IS A PORTFOLIO PIECE ABOUT DESIGNING ONE.” On either side of this text are tiny solid black squares (5x5px) acting like bullet anchors.

Next section: “SECTION 01 — CONCEPT” heading in all caps, black, very bold, left-aligned, about 24px, with a thin neon green underline that extends past the text into the right margin, intentionally overshooting. Under the heading is a two-column feeling area, but still primarily vertical: on the left, taking about 60% width of the main column, is a dense block of body text describing the concept, written as actual visible text in monospaced font, about 14px. The text reads: “LOUDPASS is an experimental subscription that bundles independent music festivals into one yearly ‘pass’. Users pay monthly and unlock rotating access to micro-festivals, warehouse shows, and rooftop sets curated across multiple cities. This portfolio explores the UX, visual system, and data storytelling behind the idea.” Below it, another paragraph: “The aesthetic direction is brutalist: loud color blocking, unapologetic grid breaking, stark typography, and an intentional tension between order and chaos. The following case study demonstrates flows, touchpoints, and a speculative analytics layer for our imaginary subscribers.” On the right of this body text, stacked vertically but narrower (about 40% of width), are three small brutalist cards, each a simple box with harsh outlines. Each card is a solid background color (one neon green, one harsh yellow, one plain black); inside each is white text in uppercase: Card 1 (neon green): “01 / FESTIVAL DISCOVERY”. Card 2 (yellow): “02 / SUBSCRIPTION UX”. Card 3 (black background with white border): “03 / DATA & RETENTION”. They each have 2px black or white borders respectively, and each is separated by 10px. The cards are vertically aligned but slightly offset horizontally by 10-15px in a staggered fashion, giving a sense of misalignment.

Next, a full-width block for “Festival Discovery” appears. The heading is left-aligned: “FESTIVAL DISCOVERY SCREENS” in 22px uppercase black text, with a thin, jagged underline that looks like a hand-drawn line (but still vector), in bright red (#ff2b2b). Below it, a single tall mockup image of a mobile screen is centered within the column but set on a solid light gray (#e5e5e5) rectangle with a 3px dashed black border, making it look as if taped onto the page. The mobile screen image itself is a flat, high-contrast UI mockup: vertical smartphone with no bezel details, just a rounded rectangle screen shape with 1px black border. The UI shows a brutalist list of festivals: background off-white, huge black text “TONIGHT: WAREHOUSE ECHOES” at the top, under a black bar with the LOUDPASS wordmark in white. Below the headline, there are blocky festival tiles: each tile is a rectangle with thick borders, harsh colors (one neon green, one magenta, one cyan), with overlaid text like “NOISE GARDEN // 12 OCT // BERLIN”, “ROOFTOP SIRENS // 19 NOV // LISBON”, all in uppercase with uneven spacing. The spacing between elements is cramped but deliberate; there is minimal iconography, just an occasional square icon that looks like a tiny speaker symbol (a small square with four smaller squares inside). The lighting in the mockup image is flat; no device shadows, no gradient backgrounds; it floats starkly against the gray tape background. Beneath the image, a narrow caption in monospaced text reads: “Primary mobile flow focusing on immediacy and hierarchy of sound over polish.”

Following this, the subscription UX section appears as a solid block of black background spanning the main column edge-to-edge, visually disrupting the off-white. Inside this black block, there is generous padding. At the top-left in white uppercase letters: “SUBSCRIPTION FLOWS”. Underneath is a set of horizontally stacked, brutalist “steps” but still arranged in a vertical reading order because of the single-column constraint. Each step is inside a white rectangle with very thick black borders (4px), arranged one per line, stacked top-to-bottom with small gaps. Step 1 card title in large uppercase: “STEP 1 — CHOOSE A CHAOS LEVEL” with a short description below in monospaced text: “User picks between ‘LOCAL LINEUP’, ‘CITY HOPPER’, or ‘INFINITE NOISE’ subscription tiers.” Step 2 card: “STEP 2 — LOCK IN YOUR MONTHLY RITUAL”, description: “User commits to a minimum of one festival per quarter, with an aggressive opt-out prompt that feels more like a zine than a corporate form.” Step 3 card: “STEP 3 — RECEIVE YOUR LOUDPASS CODE”, description: “A rotating alphanumeric code unlocks specific festival entries, paired with dynamic QR visuals.” Each step card has a small brutalist UI detail: a horizontally-stretched black rectangle button at the bottom left labeled “VIEW WIREFRAMES” in uppercase neon green text.

Below the subscription flows, still inside the black block but near the bottom, there is a simple textual “pseudo-button” in white uppercase monospaced font, centered: “[ SCROLL FOR DATA VISUALIZATION ]”, with two thin vertical white lines above and below it, like a minimalist elevator shaft.

The final major section is “DATA & RETENTION”, returning to the off-white background. The heading is oversized, all caps black: “SECTION 03 — DATA & RETENTION”. Below the heading, a subtitle in smaller uppercase gray text: “IMAGINED ANALYTICS DASHBOARD FOR LOUDPASS SUBSCRIBERS”. The main content here is a wide, horizontal data visualization: a brutalist bar chart representing “Monthly Active Subscribers by Tier”. This chart is rendered as a flat, high-contrast graphic centered within the column, inside a white rectangle with a 3px solid black border and zero rounding. The chart background is plain white; axes are thick black lines. The Y-axis (left) has black text labels: “0”, “100”, “200”, “300”, “400”, “500” stacked vertically, with gridlines as dashed black lines stretching across the chart. The X-axis, along the bottom, has three category labels in all caps: “LOCAL LINEUP”, “CITY HOPPER”, “INFINITE NOISE”, spaced equally. Above the chart, a centered title in uppercase black reads: “MONTHLY ACTIVE SUBSCRIBERS BY TIER (IMAGINED)”. The three bars are wide rectangles with no gradient, each a different harsh color: Local Lineup is neon green (#39ff14), City Hopper is electric blue (#1f5fff), Infinite Noise is hot magenta (#ff1fbf). Their heights represent explicit values: Local Lineup reaches up to the 400 mark, City Hopper to 260, and Infinite Noise to 120. Each bar has a black outline, and above each bar, floating slightly above, is a small, black pill-shaped label (but still with sharp corners) with white text stating the value: “400”, “260”, “120” respectively. A minimal legend is placed above the top-right corner of the chart block: three tiny colored squares with monospaced labels stacked vertically: “LOCAL LINEUP = ENTRY-LEVEL MONTHLY”, “CITY HOPPER = MULTI-CITY PASS”, “INFINITE NOISE = UNLIMITED ACCESS”. To the right of the chart, still in the same section but below it in the flow (not a true side column, just next in vertical order), there is a dense block of explanatory monospaced text that reads: “In this speculative dataset, the ‘LOCAL LINEUP’ tier dominates adoption, reflecting a low-commitment entry point for curious listeners. ‘CITY HOPPER’ captures a smaller but highly engaged audience willing to travel, while ‘INFINITE NOISE’ remains niche, appealing to power users and scene die-hards. The brutalist visual language intentionally exaggerates differences—thick outlines, loud colors—to reflect the intensity of festival culture.”

Beneath the bar chart section is a small additional data note displayed like a brutally stamped label: a rectangular box with no fill and a 2px black border, containing monospaced text: “NOTE: NUMBERS ARE ENTIRELY FICTIONAL AND USED TO DEMONSTRATE INTERACTION BETWEEN UX AND DATA STORYTELLING.” This label is slightly tilted (visually rotated a few degrees) to break the strict grid, with the text itself still horizontal (the rotation is implied in composition).

Continuing downward, there is a simple “ABOUT THE DESIGNER” section. The heading in uppercase black: “ABOUT THE DESIGNER”. Below it, a single photo-style image placeholder rendered as a brutalist portrait card: a grayscale, high-contrast, almost photocopied-looking realistic portrait of a person viewed from the shoulders up, centered within a harsh black rectangle. The portrait shows a person facing forward, wearing over-ear headphones, in a dimly lit studio or backstage environment. The lighting is stark: a single overhead light casts sharp shadows on their face; the background is blurred but shows faint outlines of stage lights and metal truss structures, all in grayscale. The style is realistic but with visible grain and high contrast, like a xeroxed gig flyer photo. Over the bottom part of the image, a semi-transparent white strip with black uppercase text reads: “ART DIRECTION, UX & VISUAL SYSTEM BY [YOUR NAME HERE]”. Below the portrait, there is a text block in monospaced font: “I design digital experiences that feel like late-night shows: a little messy, a little loud, and unapologetically specific. This LOUDPASS study is an exploration of how subscription products can break away from sterile minimalism and lean into a visual language that reflects their culture.” Below this, a small, left-aligned line of text acts as a contact link: “EMAIL: hello@loudpass.studio” in uppercase electric blue, with a thin underlined baseline that extends further than the text.

Finally, at the bottom of the page, a narrow footer bar runs full width using a solid harsh yellow background (#ffe600). Inside, aligned to the main column, the left side shows tiny monospaced black text: “© 2024 LOUDPASS — BRUTALIST PORTFOLIO STUDY”. On the right side of the footer, also in monospaced black text and uppercase, are two minimal links separated by a vertical bar: “INSTAGRAM” | “GITHUB”. There are no hover animations described; everything remains static and typography-driven. Throughout the page, spacing is intentional but sometimes cramped; there are visible misalignments, overshooting underlines, and occasional use of 1px grid lines (thin black vertical lines running from top to near-bottom in the background, faintly, aligning with major content edges) to reinforce the brutalist, print-poster-inspired aesthetic. No videos, motion, or animated elements appear anywhere—every element is static, with the sense of energy coming purely from color, layout tension, and bold type.

### Dataset
| Tier            | MonthlyActiveSubscribers |
|-----------------|--------------------------|
| LOCAL LINEUP    | 400                      |
| CITY HOPPER     | 260                      |
| INFINITE NOISE  | 120                      |

instruction: Planning the generation details for the following website:
input:
Design a research-focused homepage for an online language learning platform, presented with a restrained editorial magazine aesthetic and a dominant two-column layout that occasionally breaks into creative asymmetry. The overall color palette is muted and intellectual: a warm off-white background (#F8F3EC) like uncoated book paper, with charcoal text (#1F1F1F), subtle sepia accents (#C9A574), and a soft desaturated blue (#9BB3C8) for highlights and charts. The entire page sits centered with generous margins on larger screens, constrained to a max width of 1200px, framed by wide off-white gutters on the left and right.

At the top, a fixed, thin header bar runs full width across the page, in the same off-white as the background but with a subtle 1px bottom border in a faint grey (#DDDDDD). On the left side of the header is a narrow, serif logotype set in a small caps style: “LINGUA RESEARCH LAB” in dark charcoal, using a high-contrast modern serif, recalling an academic journal masthead. To its left is a tiny square icon: a minimalist, flat-design line drawing of an open book and speech bubble overlapping, drawn in thin charcoal lines with a small sepia dot accent where the bubble tail meets the book. On the right side of the header is a horizontally aligned navigation set in uppercase, tightly spaced letters using a light-weight sans-serif: “ABOUT”, “RESEARCH”, “INSIGHTS”, “METHOD”, and “SIGN IN”. The nav labels are evenly spaced, charcoal by default, with a thin bottom border appearing in sepia when hovered. The current page (“RESEARCH”) has its label in bold charcoal with the sepia underline permanently visible.

Beneath the fixed header, the hero section fills the first full viewport height minus the header, organized into a two-column layout with a slight asymmetry where the left column is about 60% width and the right 40%, separated by a wide vertical column of white space (~64px) that reinforces the editorial feel. The background remains off-white. In the left column, there is a large featured headline aligned to the left: “How Adults Actually Learn Languages.” It is set in a large, elegant serif, around 44px, with generous line-height, in charcoal. Below the headline is a thinner subheading in all caps sans-serif, small, letterspaced: “A field guide for designers of language learning systems.” The subheading is in a soft gray (#676767). A horizontal hairline rule in sepia runs beneath the subheading, spanning about one-third of the column width. Under that line is a brief intro paragraph in serif body text, 16px, dark charcoal, describing the site: “We synthesize peer‑reviewed research, learner data, and design experiments to answer one question: What makes language learning actually stick?” The text is left-aligned, with ample leading and a lightly justified feel—but actual alignment remains left-ragged for a literary look.

Beneath this text, still in the left column, is a small pill-shaped label that reads “FEATURED STUDY” in tiny uppercase sans-serif in white, on a sepia background pill. Immediately underneath is a “Read the overview” call-to-action, styled as a subtle text link, lowercase and understated, with a tiny right-pointing arrow, all in charcoal with an underline appearing on hover. No button rectangles: everything is typographically anchored, like an article.

On the right column of the hero is a prominent static data visualization card that looks like it could belong in an academic magazine. It has a white card background with a very slight drop shadow and a thin gray border. The card title, at the top-left of this card in small uppercase sans-serif, reads “LEARNER RETENTION BY PRACTICE TYPE (12 WEEKS)”. Beneath it, occupying most of the card, is a vertical bar chart. The chart sits on a clean white background with no gridlines, only minimal axes. The x-axis features four labels in small sans-serif: “Translation”, “Grammar drills”, “Spaced recall”, and “Immersive dialogues”. The y-axis is labeled on the left in a vertical orientation: “% of learners active”. Four bars, each a different muted tone from the site palette, rise from the baseline: the “Translation” bar is pale gray (#D0D0D0) and reaches up to a “34%” tick; “Grammar drills” is a slightly darker grayish blue (#A7B4C2) reaching “41%”; “Spaced recall” is a warm sepia (#C9A574) reaching “68%”; and “Immersive dialogues” is a soft deep blue (#4A6A8C) rising highest to “76%”. Each bar has its exact percentage printed in small serif numerals above it in charcoal. At the bottom of the card is a caption in small italic serif: “Cohort of 1,200 adult learners over 12 weeks, normalized for time-on-task.” Below the caption, aligned to the right, is a faint text link: “View full dataset →” in a small sans-serif. The chart is static; no animation is implied.

Below the hero section, a thin horizontal divider line in light gray spans the content width, with generous whitespace above and below. The next block is a two-column section titled “Current Research Streams”. The title appears at the top of the left column, left-aligned, serif, about 26px, in charcoal. Under it is a short explanatory sentence in smaller serif text: “Each series combines literature reviews, learner interviews, and controlled experiments.” The right column at this section’s top is largely blank except for a small right-aligned filter label: “Filter: [All] [Adults] [Teens] [Heritage learners]”, presented as tiny uppercase sans-serif text, with the active filter (“All”) in sepia and the others in gray. Below, the section content adopts an asymmetrical arrangement: in the left column, two stacked research stream cards; in the right column, one full-height card that is taller, creating a visually interesting imbalance.

Each card is like a magazine article preview: a white rectangle with a thin border, generous padding, and a small header line. For example, the top-left card: at the top, small uppercase label “SERIES 01 — RETRIEVAL PRACTICE” in gray sans-serif. Below is the title in serif: “Designing for Forgetting and Re-remembering”, in bold charcoal. The subtitle underneath, in smaller serif, reads: “Why spacing, variation, and effortful recall outpace binge study.” To the right of the subtitle, a tiny vertical list shows metadata stacked: “Phase: Pilot”, “Participants: 180”, “Languages: Spanish, Japanese”. Each card’s lower area has a subtle three-column layout of bullet-like phrases separated by thin vertical rules; for this card they read “method notes”, “activity prototypes”, “learner transcripts”. These are in lowercase sans-serif, small, gray, suggesting links. The card in the right column mirrors this pattern but with a different header and longer subtitle, giving the page a dense, editorial texture.

Below the research streams, the layout breaks into a more dramatically asymmetrical section called “Field Notes from Real Learners”. This section has a soft pale blue background band spanning full width of the content area, with top and bottom padding. The title sits top-left in serif: “Field Notes from Real Learners”. Below, the area is split into two columns, but now the right column is wider (~60%), and the left is narrower (~40%) for variety. In the narrow left column, there is an image presented like a printed photo pinned to a magazine spread.
