The image is a static, realistic but slightly desaturated photo-style illustration: a mid-shot of a young adult in their late 20s sitting at a wooden desk in a small apartment at dusk, wearing headphones, speaking toward a laptop screen; a notebook lies open with handwritten vocabulary, a mug of tea steams gently, and in the background a bookshelf with a few language textbooks and a potted plant blurred. The lighting is soft, warm from a desk lamp, casting gentle shadows; the style is photorealistic but with a subtle grain texture, as if printed in a magazine. The photo has a thin white border and a slight drop shadow, and a small caption under it in tiny serif italics: “Learner interview, Tokyo — weekly conversation sessions, Week 6”.

In the wider right column, text is arranged like an annotated interview excerpt. At the top is a slender horizontal bar with two small tabs: “Interview Excerpts” (active, highlighted with a sepia underline) and “Session Logs” (inactive, gray). Below, the main content shows a large serif pull-quote in charcoal: ““I finally started thinking in Spanish when I stopped translating in my head.”” Beneath this, left-aligned, is a structured transcript snippet: lines of dialogue labeled “Learner:” and “Researcher:” in small caps gray, with the spoken lines in standard serif. Margins between lines are generous; entire block reads like it’s typeset in a journal. Along the right edge of this column runs a narrow vertical strip with tiny, rotated text in uppercase: “DATA-DRIVEN QUALITATIVE NOTES”, giving a subtle experimental-journal vibe.

Continuing downward, another horizontal rule in gray introduces a more data-focused section titled “What Sticks After 4 Weeks?”. This section retains the two-column grid but uses it to juxtapose narrative text and another data visualization. On the left, the title is in serif, 26px: “What Sticks After 4 Weeks?” followed by a serif paragraph describing a retention study: “We tracked comprehension and production ability across four activity designs over the first month of learning. Below: average gains across 320 learners.” Below this paragraph is a small list in sans-serif, small, showing legend entries for the upcoming chart, each with a tiny colored square and label: “Passive exposure”, “Vocabulary lists”, “Spaced recall prompts”, “Guided dialogues”.

On the right column, there is a compact, static grouped bar chart or combination chart in a white card. Title at the top reads “Average Skill Gain (0–100 scale) after 4 Weeks”. Beneath, the x-axis has three category labels: “Listening”, “Reading”, “Speaking”. For each category, four adjacent narrow bars compare methods. The “Passive exposure” bars are light gray (#D0D0D0), “Vocabulary lists” in a cool gray-blue (#A7B4C2), “Spaced recall prompts” in sepia (#C9A574), and “Guided dialogues” in deep blue (#4A6A8C). The y-axis marks 0, 20, 40, 60, 80, 100, with subtle gray tick labels. For example, under “Listening”, the heights are roughly: Passive exposure at 38, Vocabulary lists at 26, Spaced recall at 54, Guided dialogues at 62. Under “Reading”: Passive exposure 32, Vocabulary lists 47, Spaced recall 58, Guided dialogues 51. Under “Speaking”: Passive exposure 21, Vocabulary lists 29, Spaced recall 49, Guided dialogues 71. Each bar has a tiny serif numeral at its top indicating its precise value. A small note below the chart in tiny serif reads: “Scores are normalized composite proficiency indices (n=320).”. There is no animation; the chart is a static visual printed on the page.

Below the data section, the layout continues into a long, editorial-style “Methods & Instruments” segment which spans the full content width but internally retains a two-column grid. The left column holds multi-paragraph text; the right column features small, labeled modules. The heading, centered at the top of this segment but aligned slightly more to the left column, reads “Methods & Instruments” in a classic serif, 30px, with a thin sepia rule beneath extending only under the words. The left column is a dense but readable block of serif prose, justified left, explaining methodologies: subheadings like “Sampling & Cohorts”, “Measurement Scales”, and “Ethics & Privacy” appear in slightly larger serif with all-caps labels, and paragraphs use small initial caps on the first line, furthering the literary tone. The right column contains three stacked cards, each with a subtle gray background and thin border, titled “Task Library”, “Survey Items”, and “Open Datasets”. Inside “Task Library”, a small table-like layout lists task names in bold sans-serif on the left (e.g., “Dialogue shadowing”, “Picture description”, “Story re-telling”), and on the right, in tiny serif, is a one-line methodological note (e.g., “calibrated to CEFR A2–B1 range”). “Open Datasets” has a minimalist list of dataset names as text-only links (e.g., “LinguaLab A1 Cohort Retention (2024).csv”), each preceded by a tiny square icon of a document outline in gray.

After this dense middle, the page breathes again with a lighter, more visual section titled “Design Patterns for Practice”. The background returns to plain off-white. Here, the two-column structure is still visible but loosened: in the left column, a vertical sequence of three “pattern cards” that look like magazine spreads; on the right, margin notes and marginalia-like elements. Each pattern card has a title in serif (“Pattern 03: Gentle Failure Loops”), a short deck (“Let learners slip without penalty before testing them.”), and a set of three labeled annotations aligned along its right edge connected by hairline rules. The right column in this section has tiny, right-aligned sans-serif text rotated slightly or aligned flush right, reading like editorial notes: “See also: Error-tolerant UI microcopy” or “Align with weekly reflection prompts”. These right-column notes sit inside a faint vertical band of very light gray, echoing the margin scribbles of a printproof. No images here, only text, rules, and negative space.

The footer of the page restores a clear division. A horizontal rule separates it from the main content. The footer background is a slightly darker cream (#EDE2D3), giving a gently grounded base. In the footer, the two-column layout reappears: the left column contains the site’s name again, “LINGUA RESEARCH LAB”, in small caps serif, with a brief statement in small serif text: “An independent lab documenting how people actually learn new languages, in partnership with educators, designers, and learners worldwide.” The right column is split into two narrower subcolumns: one lists links under a small uppercase heading “Sections” (“Research”, “Insights”, “Methods”, “Newsletter”); the other lists under “Collaborate” (“Propose a study”, “Share your data”, “Volunteer as a learner”). At the very bottom, centered across the whole width in tiny, faint gray sans-serif, is a line: “All content is for informational purposes and does not replace formal instruction. © 2025 Lingua Research Lab.”

Throughout the page, typography is consistent: headlines and section titles in a refined serif reminiscent of literary journals; body copy in the same serif at smaller sizes; meta information, labels, and navigation in a clean, modern sans-serif. There are no videos, animations, or moving elements; everything is static, calm, and printlike in spirit, as if one is scrolling through a thoughtfully designed digital journal issue focused on the research behind how adults learn languages.

### Dataset

```csv
Chart,Category,Method,Value
Retention,Overall,Translation,34
Retention,Overall,Grammar drills,41
Retention,Overall,Spaced recall,68
Retention,Overall,Immersive dialogues,76
SkillGain,Listening,Passive exposure,38
SkillGain,Listening,Vocabulary lists,26
SkillGain,Listening,Spaced recall prompts,54
SkillGain,Listening,Guided dialogues,62
SkillGain,Reading,Passive exposure,32
SkillGain,Reading,Vocabulary lists,47
SkillGain,Reading,Spaced recall prompts,58
SkillGain,Reading,Guided dialogues,51
SkillGain,Speaking,Passive exposure,21
SkillGain,Speaking,Vocabulary lists,29
SkillGain,Speaking,Spaced recall prompts,49
SkillGain,Speaking,Guided dialogues,71
```

instruction: Planning the generation details for the following website:
input:
Design a single-page research site with an editorial, print-magazine aesthetic inspired by an independent bookstore and literary culture, using a mostly single-column layout that occasionally breaks into gentle asymmetry. The color palette is minimal and sophisticated: off-white (#F8F5F0) as the main background, deep charcoal (#202124) for primary text, muted ink blue (#2E4057) for accents, and a soft desaturated terracotta (#C48A6A) for subtle highlights like rules and small tags. Typography feels like a literary journal: the main serif is a high-contrast typeface similar to “Didot” or “Libre Baskerville” for headings and pull quotes, while body text uses a clean, legible humanist sans-serif like “Source Sans Pro” in a light weight, with generous line spacing. All content is centered within a narrow column (max-width about 760px) on larger screens, with abundant whitespace around it; occasionally, key elements gently offset left or right to create an editorial, asymmetric feel while still preserving the sense of a dominated single vertical reading flow.

At the very top, a slim, understated header bar spans full width, with a transparent background that lets the off-white page color show through, giving the sense of no hard edge at the top. The site title is centered within this bar: in small caps serif text, it reads “MARGIN NOTES RESEARCH” in deep charcoal, letter-spaced slightly. Below that, in a lighter gray sans-serif, centered and in smaller font, is the tagline: “Field Notes from Independent Book Culture.” There are no navigation links in this top header, just this quiet identity. A single, thin terracotta horizontal rule runs beneath the tagline, centered and only half the width of the page, evoking a printed magazine section divider.

Below the header, the hero section begins. It is dominated by a large, horizontally-oriented hero image that nearly spans the content width but leaves subtle margins on left and right. The hero image is a photorealistic, warmly lit, slightly desaturated photograph depicting the interior of a small independent bookstore in early evening. The point of view is from waist height, looking down a narrow aisle of bookshelves that recede into the background. On the left side of the image, tall wooden shelves in a dark walnut tone are filled with books of varying sizes and colors, their spines forming a subtle mosaic of muted reds, blues, greens, and creams; some books are stacked horizontally on top of vertical rows. On the right, a shorter shelf holds stacks of small literary journals and zines, their covers in subdued pastel tones with minimalist typography. In the midground slightly off-center, a wooden ladder leans casually against a shelf, with a soft shadow stretching across the floor. The floor is a pale, worn wooden plank with visible texture and grain. In the background, the store opens into a small reading nook with a single armchair upholstered in a faded dark green fabric; behind it, a large window diffuses warm golden natural light, casting gentle shadows and soft highlights on floating dust motes. The overall lighting is warm and inviting, with no harsh contrasts, as though shot on film. The photograph should be composed with leading lines along the shelves guiding the eye toward the distant reading chair, reinforcing the sense of depth and exploration. On hover (though no animation is visible in this description), the image remains static; there is no overlay text on the image itself.

Directly beneath the hero image, aligned to the left edge of the image but still within the main column, is the main title of the page in large serif text, in ink blue: “The Independent Bookstore Field Report”. It uses a high-contrast serif, tight leading, and is broken into three lines to create a blocky editorial feel. Beneath it, in smaller, uppercase sans-serif, letter-spaced, is a subtitle in terracotta: “AN ONGOING RESEARCH PROJECT”. Under that, a short introductory paragraph in charcoal sans-serif, left-aligned, explains the site’s purpose, reading: “This research site documents the micro-ecologies of independent bookstores—their readers, their shelves, and the everyday rituals that keep literary communities alive. Part editorial, part archive, ‘Margin Notes Research’ gathers interviews, observations, and small datasets from bookshops around the world.” The paragraph is about four lines long on desktop, with comfortable line spacing and slightly narrower measure than the title. A subtle, barely visible thin gray vertical line appears to the left of this introductory paragraph, running the height of the text; it feels like a margin note bar, reinforcing the research-notebook motif.

Scrolling down, the page transitions into a “Current Study Overview” section, still single-column but with a slight asymmetry. A small sans-serif section label appears in uppercase terracotta, left-aligned: “CURRENT STUDY”. Below, a medium-sized serif heading in charcoal reads: “How Readers Discover Books in Independent Stores”. The section breaks into two areas: on the left two-thirds of the column sits a block of body text, and on the right one-third, slightly indented and pushed down, is a framed data visualization. The left text is a series of two paragraphs in sans-serif, charcoal, describing the study. The first paragraph begins: “In 2024, we surveyed visitors in 12 independent bookstores across 6 cities, asking one simple question: ‘How did you find the book you’re buying today?’” The paragraph continues with concise explanation, moving into a second paragraph: “The responses reveal a delicate balance between serendipity and structure—handwritten shelf notes, staff recommendations, and meandering browsing all play a role alongside deliberate search. The chart on the right breaks down the primary discovery paths we documented.” The right-side visualization is set within a subtle off-white card with a thin ink blue border and a faint drop shadow (very soft, blurred, barely perceptible), creating the feel of an inset plate in a magazine.

The data visualization itself is a clean, minimal vertical bar chart, titled at the top in small, uppercase sans-serif: “DISCOVERY PATHS IN INDEPENDENT BOOKSTORES (N = 240)”. Directly under the title, in lighter gray sans-serif, is a note: “Percentage of surveyed purchases by primary discovery method.” The chart has a white background with light gray horizontal gridlines. The x-axis (horizontal) lists categories in small, rotated sans-serif labels: “Browsing Shelves”, “Staff Recommendation”, “Front Table Display”, “Planned Search (Specific Title)”, “Friend Recommendation”, and “Event or Reading”. The y-axis on the left is labeled “Share of Purchases (%)” in small vertical text, with ticks at 0, 10, 20, 30, 40, 50. Each bar is a different shade within the site’s palette, but kept muted and cohesive: “Browsing Shelves” is ink blue; “Staff Recommendation” is terracotta; “Front Table Display” is a muted moss green (#6C7A52); “Planned Search (Specific Title)” is charcoal; “Friend Recommendation” is a soft dull gold (#B79A5B); “Event or Reading” is a dusty plum (#7A506F). The bar heights correspond exactly to the following percentages: Browsing Shelves 38%, Staff Recommendation 22%, Front Table Display 15%, Planned Search (Specific Title) 12%, Friend Recommendation 8%, Event or Reading 5%. Each bar has the percentage value in small sans-serif text above it in charcoal. The overall style of the chart is flat, clear, and print-like, without gradients or 3D effects. Beneath the chart within the card, aligned right, is a small footnote in light gray: “Data: Margin Notes Field Survey, Spring 2024.”

Below this overview, a dividing section uses a generous amount of white space and a stylized pull quote to shift the tone back to editorial. A large serif pull quote is centered in the column, reading: ““Every independent bookstore is a hypothesis about what reading can be.”” The quotation marks use a slightly oversized, decorative style. Beneath the quote, right-aligned, in small caps sans-serif, it says: “Field Note, Lisbon, 2024”. A very thin, full-column-width horizontal rule in pale gray sits above and below this block, giving the sense of a feature box in a magazine.

The next section, titled “Field Notes from the Stacks”, introduces three feature “articles” stacked vertically, each presented as a research vignette. The section title is left-aligned, serif, ink blue, slightly smaller than the main page title. Underneath, there is a short description in sans-serif: “Each visit begins with a walk through the shelves. Below are selected notes from our ongoing bookstore visits.” Each vignette follows a consistent layout: a small, square image on the left and text on the right, but the entire component remains within the single main column. The layout subtly alternates the image alignment to create asymmetry: the first vignette has the image flush left with text wrapping to the right; the second vignette has the image flush right with text on its left; the third returns the image to the left.

For the first vignette, the square image is 220x220 pixels, depicting a close-up, top-down perspective of a wooden table in a bookstore. On the table are three stacks of books arranged in a loose triangle. The covers are mostly in muted blues and creams, with minimalist designs and serif titles; one book has a small line drawing of a city map on its cover, another has a bold title set in black letters. Next to the stacks is a porcelain coffee cup with a saucer, half-filled with black coffee, casting a soft shadow; there are faint coffee rings on the table nearby. A handwritten note card, slightly out of focus at the bottom edge, has crooked blue-ink script, hinting at staff recommendations but not legible in detail. The background is softly blurred wooden floorboards and a hint of a chair leg. The lighting is soft and natural, coming from the top-left, emphasizing the grain of the wood and the subtle textures of paper and ceramic. To the right of this image, aligned to its top, the vignette title appears in serif: “Vignette 01: The Front Table as Editorial Space.” Below, a 3–4 line paragraph in sans-serif explains: “In many stores, the front table functions less as a display and more as a thesis—a curated, rotating spread that announces what the store thinks matters now. Here, we document how often titles move, how quickly new authors appear, and what kinds of stories are given the most visible space.” Beneath the paragraph is a small, underlined text link in ink blue reading: “Read field protocol →”.

The second vignette’s image, aligned to the right, shows a photorealistic, slightly grainy scene: a low-angle view of a narrow bookstore aisle at night, focusing on a row of handwritten shelf labels. The camera is angled upward slightly, emphasizing the labels taped to the lower edge of a shelf where paperbacks are lined up. Each label is on a small cream card with uneven edges, written in various handwritings with black and dark blue ink: snippets like “quietly devastating”, “unexpectedly funny”, “for lovers of long sentences”, although these are hinted at rather than perfectly legible. A warm, overhead pendant light casts a soft cone of light, creating a gradient from bright amber near the labels to dim shadows at the far ends of the aisle. Some books protrude slightly from the shelf, casting thin, crisp shadows. The feel is intimate and analog. To the left of this image, the text block features a serif title: “Vignette 02: Handwriting as Interface.” The paragraph beneath reads: “Shelf talkers—those small, handwritten notes pinned beneath books—often convert ambient browsing into an encounter. We’re studying how language, handwriting style, and card placement shape whether a reader reaches for a book.” Again, a small link beneath says: “See sample annotations →”.

The third vignette’s image, again on the left, is a soft, slightly desaturated photograph taken from behind a seated reader in a bookstore’s reading corner. The focus is on the reader’s hands holding an open paperback; the cover is folded back slightly, and the text on the page is blurred to avoid legibility. The reader wears a dark wool sweater, and you can see the back of their head but not their face. Surrounding them are bookshelves that curve around to create a nook, with spines in earth tones and subtle color pops. A small table to the side hosts a single, warm-shaded lamp casting a gentle pool of light, while the rest of the store fades into soft shadow. The composition is intimate, with shallow depth of field. To the right, the title reads: “Vignette 03: Time Spent Sitting Down.” The paragraph explains: “We clock how long visitors linger—when they sit, what they pick up, and whether they stay long enough to forget the time. These micro-durations tell us how well a store invites deep attention.” The link below reads: “Read observational notes →”.

After the vignette section, a narrow, centered “Methods & Metadata” segment appears, resembling the appendix of a research paper but styled with editorial sensitivity. A small terracotta label at the top reads: “METHODOLOGY”. Directly under it, a medium serif heading in charcoal: “How We Collect and Classify Our Notes”. The content is a single paragraph of sans-serif text, center-aligned, explaining: “Our fieldwork blends ethnographic observation, short-form surveys, and spatial mapping of bookstores. Each visit is documented through handwritten notes, photographs, and structured coding of discovery paths, dwell times, and display practices. All visual materials are anonymized and composited to protect the identity of stores and readers.” Below the paragraph, there are three small, pill-shaped tags aligned horizontally and centered, each with a thin ink blue outline and tiny uppercase sans-serif text inside: “ETHNOGRAPHY”, “SURVEY DATA”, “SPATIAL ANALYSIS”. They sit with ample space between them, looking like refined category chips.

Towards the bottom of the page, a final section invites participation. It is titled in serif: “Contribute a Field Note”. The layout returns to a simple, single-column form. Beneath the title is a short line of text: “If you run, work in, or frequently visit an independent bookstore, you can submit your own observations to this ongoing research.” Below, there is a vertically stacked, minimalist form with three labeled fields. Each label is left-aligned, small uppercase sans-serif in charcoal. The first label: “Name (optional)”. Below it, a single-line input field, represented visually as a white rectangle with rounded corners and a thin pale gray border; the placeholder text in light gray reads: “e.g., ‘Alex, bookseller in Chicago’”. The second label: “Bookstore Context”. Its input is a single-line box with placeholder “Describe the store (city, size, focus)…”. The third label: “Observation or Field Note”. This is a larger, multi-line text area with more height, and placeholder text: “Tell us about a moment, a display, or a reader you noticed…”. Under the form fields, aligned left, is a primary call-to-action button in ink blue with white uppercase sans-serif text: “SUBMIT FIELD NOTE”. The button has slightly rounded corners and subtle inner padding, no heavy shadow. To the right of the button, in tiny sans-serif gray text, is a disclaimer: “By submitting, you agree that your note may be quoted anonymously in future reports.”

The footer is simple and quiet, echoing the header’s minimalism. A thin terracotta rule sits above it. The footer content is center-aligned, in small sans-serif gray text. The first line reads: “Margin Notes Research · An independent project on bookshop ecologies.” The second line, smaller and lighter, reads: “Contact: hello@marginnotesresearch.org · Archive: 2022–2024”. There are no social icons or extra decoration, keeping the sense of calm and focus. Throughout the page, the overall aesthetic mirrors a thoughtfully designed literary journal: generous margins, restrained color, careful type hierarchy, and images that feel like still, contemplative photographs. All elements remain static with no animation or video; the interactive feel comes only from potential hover states on links and form inputs, but these are not visually described as changing, maintaining a timeless, printed-page impression adapted to the web.

### Dataset
| Discovery Path                      | Percentage |
|-------------------------------------|------------|
| Browsing Shelves                    | 38         |
| Staff Recommendation                | 22         |
| Front Table Display                 | 15         |
| Planned Search (Specific Title)     | 12         |
| Friend Recommendation               | 8          |
| Event or Reading                    | 5          |

instruction: Planning the generation details for the following website:
input:
Imagine a whimsical, playful yet polished dashboard-style homepage for a “virtual reality wellness arcade” called “Cloud Lagoon”, blending the luxury spa resort vibe with a VR game tutorial overlay. The page has a two-column responsive layout with creative asymmetry: a wider left column for navigation, hero info, and interactive cards, and a narrower right column stacked with stats, schedules, and a data-driven visualization. The background is a soft vertical gradient, fading from a pale lavender at the top to a gentle mint green at the bottom, with faint, semi-transparent pastel shapes (rounded blobs and thin wavy lines) drifting diagonally across like clouds. The overall aesthetic is fun and dreamy, but carefully structured, with rounded corners, subtle drop shadows, and a playful sans-serif font like “Baloo” or “Poppins” for headings and a clean geometric sans-serif like “Nunito” for body text.  

At the very top, a full-width header bar spans the page with a height of about 80px, background in a semi-translucent white with a slight blur effect, giving a frosted glass look. On the left of this bar sits the logo: a rounded pill-shaped badge with a gradient from turquoise to sky blue, containing a minimal flat icon of a VR headset with a stylized wave inside the visor, and to the right of the icon the text “Cloud Lagoon” in a bold, playful font, in deep indigo. Under “Cloud Lagoon” in tiny caps is the tagline “VR Wellness Arcade” in soft gray. In the center of the header is a set of playful navigation links spaced evenly: “Dashboard”, “Experiences”, “Guided Sessions”, “My Progress”, “Support”, each in medium-weight text, dark indigo, with a pill-shaped hover state shown on “Dashboard” in pastel pink. On the far right of the header, there is a compact user profile area: a circular avatar image (flat illustration of a person wearing a floating VR headset, smiling, in pastel colors) with a soft white border, next to the text “Welcome back, Luna” in purple, and below that in small caps “LEVEL 7 · AURA EXPLORER” in teal. To the right of the name is a small bell icon (notification) with a subtle dot in coral orange indicating “3” alerts when hovered, and a small downward caret icon implying a dropdown.  

Below the header the page splits into the two main columns. The left column takes about 60% of the width; the right column about 40%. The left begins with a large hero card near the top, with generous margin from the header (about 24px). This hero card has a rounded rectangle shape, soft shadow, and a diagonal gradient background from light aqua to pastel pink, with faint dotted patterns in the corner. On the left side of this card is a detailed illustration: a stylized, semi-cartoonish, semi-flat scene of a person reclining on a floating lounge chair in a surreal VR lagoon. The figure wears a sleek white VR headset with an iridescent visor; their body is in a relaxed pose, floating above a pool of glowing water formed by smooth concentric ripples. The water is turquoise with subtle white reflections. Around them are floating translucent bubbles containing mini island scenes: one bubble shows a tiny waterfall flowing into mist; another bubble shows a bonsai tree on a rock; another shows a tiny mountain with a glowing pink sun. In the background of this illustration, there are curved, translucent arches, like futuristic spa cabanas, and a faint horizon of stylized mountains in a lavender haze. The lighting is soft and diffused, with gentle highlights on the headset and water ripples, and a dreamy, slightly foggy atmosphere.  

On the right side of the hero card, text is stacked. At the top, a small pill-shaped label with a subtle border reads “TODAY’S FLOW” in uppercase teal letters on a white background. Below it, the main heading in large playful font: “Sink into your next VR escape.”, in deep indigo, left-aligned. Under the heading, a subheading in medium gray text: “Choose a wellness scenario, follow your guided tutorial, and let your senses drift into calm.” Beneath the text is a horizontal row of two primary action buttons and one secondary text link. The left button is a large rounded pill, filled with a vibrant gradient from bright coral to magenta, with white text “Start Quick Session”, slightly larger than body text, and a subtle drop shadow. The right button is an outlined pill with a mint border and transparent interior, text in teal reading “Browse Experiences”. Below and slightly to the right, a small text link in muted gray with a tiny right-arrow icon reads “View tutorial tips”.  

Directly under the hero card, still in the left column, the layout transitions into a grid of three wide, rounded “Experience Cards” arranged in two rows: the top row has two cards side-by-side, the bottom row has a single, slightly taller card spanning the full left-column width. Each card uses a gentle pastel background and includes a small illustrative thumbnail, a title, a difficulty tag, and a progress bar. The top-left card is titled “Lagoon Float Ritual”. Its background is a soft gradient from pale aqua to white. On the left of this card, a small rectangular thumbnail illustration shows a top-down view of a tranquil VR lagoon: circular platforms made of smooth stone float on calm water, with glowing lily pads and a subtle mist; the style is clean, semi-flat, with soft gradients and gentle highlights. To the right of the thumbnail, the card title “Lagoon Float Ritual” is in bold indigo, with a smaller line beneath: “Session length: 15 min” in gray. Below this text, a small tag appears as a tiny pill reading “Beginner” in white on a lime-green background. Below all this, a progress bar spans the card width: a thin, rounded track in light lavender with a filled region in gradient teal showing “65% Completed” with the percentage label inside the filled part in white. The top-right card is “Crystal Cavern Breathwork” with a pale lilac-to-white background, thumbnail showing a glowing crystal cave with suspended polygonal crystals emitting gentle purple light, a silhouetted figure in VR headset sitting cross-legged on a platform. The difficulty tag reads “Intermediate” on a soft amber background, and the progress bar shows “25% Completed” in amber gradient. The single wide card beneath is “Nebula Sky Swim”, background gradient from dusky blue to pastel pink, thumbnail showing a floating swimmer figure in a stylized sky full of floating clouds and stars, wearing a VR headset, with pastel nebula clouds swirling around; the difficulty tag reads “Advanced” with a deep pink background, and a progress bar showing “5% Completed” with a small lock icon at the far right, hinting at upcoming unlocks.  

Below the experience cards, still in the left column, is a section titled “Tutorial Checklist” with a small, playful heading in indigo, and a faint dashed underline. This section appears as a vertical check-list card with a white background and light shadow. Inside, four steps are listed with playful icons on the left. The title at the top of the card is “Before you drift away, remember…”. Each item is in a horizontal row with a pastel circular icon, a title in bold text, and a smaller description. Item 1: a teal circle with a white headset icon, title “Check your headset fit”, description “Adjust straps so your headset feels snug but cloud-light.”, with a checkbox on the right that is filled with a checkmark and highlighted mint. Item 2: a coral circle with white wifi symbol, title “Run connection test”, description “Ensure a stable connection to reduce motion glitches.”, checkbox filled as completed. Item 3: a lavender circle with a small couch icon, title “Choose a comfy spot”, description “Sit or lie down where you can fully relax.”, checkbox in outlined state (not yet completed). Item 4: a pale yellow circle with a water droplet icon, title “Hydrate & breathe”, description “Sip water and take three slow breaths before starting.”, checkbox also outlined. The checkboxes have subtle hover glow indicated by a thin colored border in mock state. At the bottom of the checklist card, there is a small line of text in gray: “Tip: You can reorder steps in Settings.” with “Settings” underlined in teal.  

At the very bottom of the left column, there is a narrow horizontal strip card, like a playful footer widget, with a soft peach background and rounded edges, containing a small illustrated icon of a sleeping moon with a VR headset tilted up, and the text “Need a break? Try ‘Micro-Nap Oasis’ for a 3-minute reset.” with a tiny chevron icon to the right.  

On the right column, top section, there is a “Today at a Glance” panel with a white background, rounded corners, and a shadow giving the impression of floating above the pastel gradient. The panel heading, aligned left, reads “Today at a Glance” in indigo, with a small subtle calendar icon next to it. Inside, four compact stat tiles are arranged in a 2x2 grid with slight gaps. Each tile is a rectangle with softly colored background. Top-left tile: background pale mint, title in small uppercase gray “TOTAL MINUTES IN VR”, big number “42 min” in bold teal. Top-right tile: background pale coral, title “CALM SESSIONS COMPLETED”, big number “3” in bold orange. Bottom-left tile: background pale lavender, title “FOCUS SCORE”, number “87” with a tiny up-arrow in green and a percentage “+12% vs. yesterday” in small text below. Bottom-right tile: background pale blue, title “RESTFULNESS INDEX”, number “7.9 / 10” with a tiny smiling face icon in soft yellow.  

Directly below this “Today at a Glance” panel is a data visualization panel titled “Weekly Serenity Curve”, designed as a card with a white background and faint gridlines, occupying a substantial vertical chunk of the right column. The title “Weekly Serenity Curve” is centered at the top of the card, in indigo, with a tiny info icon (a small circle with “i” in it) on the right. Under the title, there is a horizontal legend with two small pill labels: one filled with a teal gradient reading “Serenity Score”, and another with a dotted lavender outline reading “Target Range (60-80)”. The chart itself is a smooth line chart over seven days (Mon–Sun), representing a numeric “Serenity Score” from 0 to 100. The horizontal axis has labels “Mon”, “Tue”, “Wed”, “Thu”, “Fri”, “Sat”, “Sun” in small gray text. The vertical axis shows markers at 0, 20, 40, 60, 80, 100 along the left, also in light gray. A softly curved, thick line in teal with gentle glow indicates the user’s score throughout the week. Each data point is indicated by a small circular node in a brighter teal. The scores are: Monday 55, Tuesday 62, Wednesday 70, Thursday 68, Friday 74, Saturday 80, Sunday 77. Behind the line, a semi-transparent lavender band fills the area between 60 and 80, stretching across the entire chart width, representing the “Target Range (60-80)”. On Tuesday, Wednesday, Friday, Saturday, and Sunday sections, the teal line dips into or rises into this band. Above the Saturday data point (80), a tiny speech bubble tooltip is shown, with white background, shadow, and text: “Sat: 80 · ‘Nebula Sky Swim’ unlocked peak calm.”. At the bottom right corner of the chart, a small caption in gray reads “Based on your completed sessions this week.”.  

Below the chart, further down the right column, there is a “Session Timeline” panel that looks like a simplified schedule view. The heading “Session Timeline” is left-aligned in indigo, with a small right-aligned toggle that says “Today · Week” as two pill segments; “Today” is filled in teal, “Week” is outlined. The panel itself shows a vertical timeline for the current day with colored blocks. The timeline runs from “08:00” to “22:00” along the left edge, with small gray ticks for every hour. There are three session blocks on this timeline, each a pill-shaped horizontal bar spanning across a narrow area. The 09:00–09:15 block is in a mint gradient with the text “Lagoon Float Ritual”, and beneath in smaller type “Completed · 15 min”; a subtle checkmark icon appears at its right edge. The 14:00–14:20 block is in a soft purple gradient labeled “Crystal Cavern Breathwork”, with text “Completed · 20 min”. The 21:00–21:05 slot at the bottom is in a pale coral gradient labeled “Nebula Sky Swim (scheduled)”, with a tiny clock icon on the right side. Vertical dotted lines show the current time between 14:00 and 21:00, with a small glowing dot at the top of the line and a label in tiny text “Now”. At the bottom of the panel, a muted gray link reads “View full schedule”, with a tiny angle-right icon.  

Finally, at the bottom of the right column, there is a compact “Support & Mood” card, background white with soft rounded corners and diagonal stripes in very faint opacity. The card is split horizontally into two parts. The top half has the heading “How are you feeling?” in indigo, and below it a row of five rounded emoji-style face icons, each a simple flat circle with expressive eyes and mouth: from left to right, a very relaxed face with closed eyes and a tiny smile in soft blue (labeled underneath “Deeply Calm”), a smiling face in green (“Good”), a neutral face in yellow (“Okay”), a slightly stressed face in orange (“Tense”), and a frazzled face with squiggly mouth in pink (“Overwhelmed”). The “Good” face is shown as selected with a thicker outline and faint glow. The bottom half of the card includes a small link “Talk to a Flow Guide” in teal, accompanied by a chat bubble icon, and a subtext line in gray: “Available from 8am–10pm in your local time zone.”.  

Overall, the spacing between sections is consistent, with 16–24px padding inside cards and about 24px vertical gaps between stacked elements. Borders are minimal; instead, the visual hierarchy relies on color, shadow, and typography. There are no videos or animations; all illustrations are static but rich in detail, with consistent semi-flat, slightly 3D shading, and soft pastel color palettes. Hover states and interactions are hinted visually but not animated. The entire page feels like a game-inspired control panel for peaceful VR escapes, playful yet luxurious, inviting the user to explore without any overwhelming motion.  

### Dataset
| Day | Serenity Score |
|-----|----------------|
| Mon | 55             |
| Tue | 62             |
| Wed | 70             |
| Thu | 68             |
| Fri | 74             |
| Sat | 80             |
| Sun | 77             |

instruction: Planning the generation details for the following website:
input:
Design a single-page "Linguava Studio" online language course subscription dashboard in a neo-skeuomorphic aesthetic, where the entire interface feels like a polished physical control console laid neatly across a multi-column grid on a dim, textured background. The page has a full-viewport width but is centered within generous side margins on a very dark, soft gradient backdrop: from charcoal (#111217) at the top fading into a slightly warmer near-black (#17171c) toward the bottom, with a faint, almost imperceptible linen-like texture and a subtle vignette effect darkening the corners. The primary content rests on a wide, rounded-rectangle dashboard panel that floats above the background with a soft, diffuse shadow and a slightly beveled edge, making it feel like a matte glass surface embedded into a high-end device. This main panel is a deep slate blue-gray (#181c24) with a delicate inner glow around its border and minute specular highlights at the top edge, giving the illusion that a soft light is shining down from above. The entire typography uses a sleek, modern sans-serif like “SF Pro Display” or “Inter.” Headings are medium-weight and slightly rounded, while smaller text is crisp; primary accent color is an electric cyan (#46d6ff) used sparingly for highlights and data; secondary accent is a muted violet (#7d68ff).

At the very top of the dashboard is a fixed-height header lane that visually resembles a slightly raised metal strip inset into the main panel, spanning edge to edge with rounded corners that match the main panel. The header background is a very dark navy (#11141b) with a brushed metal texture faintly visible on close inspection. On the left, aligned with generous padding, sits the brand wordmark “Linguava Studio” in a semi-bold, slightly condensed font, colored in a soft off-white (#f6f7ff). Just to its left is a small circular logo emblem: a flat, neo-skeuomorphic badge with a shallow embossed ring and a center icon of a minimal globe (longitudinal and latitudinal lines) glowing softly in cyan and violet on a nearly black circular button. On the right side of the header is a compact user control area arranged horizontally. There’s a pill-shaped notification button with an inset, tactile look: dark surface, faint inner shadow, and a tiny bell icon engraved in off-white with a small cyan dot in its top-right to indicate “3” notifications; next to it is a rounded rectangle showing the text “Current Plan: Pro” in small, light text, with the word “Pro” in cyan, and finally a user profile capsule: a small circular avatar image followed by the name “Alex Rivera” in off-white, and a subtle down-chevron icon etched into the capsule indicating a dropdown. The avatar is a photographic-style portrait of a person in a softly lit studio, but rendered in a slightly desaturated, stylized photorealism to match the UI’s muted tones, framed by an outer ring with a faint metallic sheen.

Below this header, the dashboard content is divided into a three-column grid, with the left and middle columns slightly wider and the right column narrower, creating a visually balanced yet subtly asymmetrical layout. The grid is not rigid rectangles: each section appears as its own physical “module card,” slightly raised from the main panel surface with its own inner shadows, rounded corners, and subtle gloss along the top edges, like analog instrumentation tiles on a mixing console. Between cards there is generous spacing and thin, tiny lines of cyan and violet light that sometimes trace along the gaps, resembling faint circuit traces etched into the surface.

At the top of the left column, taking the full width of that column, is the "Learning Overview" module, the largest and most prominent panel. It is a wide, horizontally oriented card with a slightly lighter slate tone (#1f2430) and a smooth, semi-matte finish, with a tiny bevel suggesting a sunken control bay. At its top-left corner is the heading “Learning Overview” in off-white, medium weight, and at the top-right corner is a small capsule button that looks like a physical toggle with the label “This Week” in cyan text on a dark background. Just under the heading is a line of subtext that reads: “Your progress across all active language tracks.” Alignment is left with comfortable padding. Below this, occupying the central portion of the card, is a horizontal bar chart-like visualization embedded directly into the panel as if it’s an illuminated screen under glass. The chart background is a darker inset rectangle with softly rounded corners, inferior brightness, and a subtle faint grid. Across the horizontal axis are days labeled “Mon, Tue, Wed, Thu, Fri, Sat, Sun” in tiny pale gray text. Three smooth, neon-like lines chart progress over time: one cyan line labeled “Study Minutes”, one violet line labeled “Vocabulary Learned”, and a warm amber line (#ffb454) labeled “Speaking Practice.” Each line glows softly and has small, subtle circular nodes at data points, slightly raised like tiny glass beads. Below the chart, three small tactile legend pills appear, each with a colored dot in its respective line color and label text: “Study Minutes”, “Vocabulary”, “Speaking.” In the bottom area of this module, there are three small summary gauges arranged side by side: each gauge is a circular, watch-like display with a brushed metal ring, tiny engraved tick marks, and a softly glowing progress arc. The first gauge reads “Weekly Goal” at the top, with a bright cyan arc filled to about 80%, and in the center bold text “12 / 15 hrs”. The second gauge reads “New Words” with a violet arc filled to roughly 70% and center text “84 / 120”. The third reads “Streak” with an amber arc filled fully and center text “21 days”. The gauges cast tiny shadows, as if they’re small dials set into the surface.

Directly below this large overview module in the left column is a “Today’s Tasks” panel that resembles a small notepad with perforated top edge engraved subtly into the card. The card background is a slightly warmer slate-gray with a faint paper texture, and faint vertical grain, still very dark to fit the overall look. The heading at the top-left in small caps reads “Today’s Tasks,” and beneath it, slightly smaller text says “3 of 5 completed”. Below, five task rows appear like tactile switches. Each row is a rounded pill bar, slightly elevated with a soft shadow, with a circular checkbox on the left that appears like a physical push-button, and task text aligned to the left. Three of them are marked completed with the circular button filled in cyan and a tiny, etched checkmark; the text of those completed tasks is in a softened gray and slightly italicized: “Complete 20 min Listening in Spanish,” “Review 30 Japanese Kanji,” “French Speaking Drill: Daily Challenge.” The remaining two tasks have empty, embossed circular buttons with a faint inner shadow and brighter rim, and their labels are bright off-white: “German Grammar: Cases Practice” and “Add 10 new Italian phrases.” Each row has a subtle dividing line between them that looks like a hairline groove carved into the surface.

The middle column starts under the header aligned with the left column’s top, but contains a vertically stacked set of two prominent modules. At the top is a “Courses & Levels” panel that looks like a compact control board with three large, horizontal sliders representing different language tracks. The card’s top area contains the heading “Courses & Levels” in off-white left-aligned, with a small label at the top-right reading “Manage” in cyan underlined text that feels like an engraved button. Below are three segment blocks, each labeled with a language. The first block is “Spanish · Upper Intermediate (B2)” with the label text engraved into the surface near the top of the block. Below it, a chunky, neo-skeuomorphic slider runs from left to right: the track is a long bar with a subtle recessed channel; the progress fill is cyan, ending at about 65% of the track. The handle is a small circular knob with a polished metallic rim catching a highlight on its top-left edge and a subtle, darker shadow on its bottom-right, suggesting a top-front light source. Above the slider, aligned to the right, tiny text reads “65% Complete.” Under this Spanish slider, there is tiny subtext: “Next milestone: Conversation mastery test.” The second block is for “Japanese · Beginner (A1)” with a violet fill slider only at around 25%, showing “25% Complete” and subtext “Next milestone: Hiragana review.” The third block is “French · Advanced (C1)” with an amber fill at “82% Complete” and subtext “Next milestone: Debate prep module.” Each slider responds visually with subtle gloss and edge highlights, as if they can be physically dragged.

Beneath “Courses & Levels” in the middle column is the “Subscription & Usage” module, a slightly shorter card with a more metallic sheen, suggesting a device status panel. At the top-left it shows the heading “Subscription & Usage,” and top-right, a pill button engraved with the text “Upgrade Plan” in cyan, with a faint glow around the text as if backlit. Inside, the left half of this module has a radial usage dial that resembles a high-end analog meter. It is a large circular gauge with concentric rings: an outer ring with engraved tick marks showing increments of 0 to 100, a mid ring with a cyan gradient arc filled to 70%, and an inner dark core with the bold text in the center: “Usage: 70%” and beneath it in smaller text “10.5 / 15 hr weekly limit.” A small, semi-transparent pointer needle extends from the center toward the 70% mark, colored subtly in cyan with a metallic hub. The right half of this card has three horizontal bars that appear like tiny glass tubes filled with light. Each bar shows an aspect of the subscription: “Devices Linked: 2 / 3”, “Download Slots: 8 / 10”, and “Tutor Credits: 3 / 5.” Each is a rounded rectangular tube slightly recessed, with a cyan, violet, or amber glow filling it proportionally, and tiny engraved labels above each bar. At the bottom edge of the card, centered, there is a subtle line of small text in gray that says: “Plan: Pro · Renews on Oct 28, 2025.”

The rightmost column is narrower and is divided into three stacked panels. At the top is a “Daily Phrase Deck” card styled like a small, glossy flip-card box. The heading at the top-left reads “Daily Phrase Deck,” with a tiny language picker pill on the right that says “Spanish” in small cyan text. The main content of this card is a slightly raised, centered pseudo-card with strong neo-skeuomorphic shading: a rounded rectangle with beveled edges, a soft internal gradient from deep navy at the top to slightly lighter slate at the bottom, and a very subtle reflection highlight near the top edge. On this card, in large, elegant, serif-like type (distinct from the rest of the dashboard), the phrase is displayed: “¿Podrías hablar más despacio, por favor?” Below it, in smaller sans-serif text, is the translation: “Could you speak more slowly, please?” The text is centered and crisp. At the bottom of the pseudo-card, there are two embossed buttons side by side: left one labeled “Reveal Tips” and right one labeled “Mark Learned.” The letterforms of the buttons are slightly indented into the surface, and each button has a faint inner shadow and an edge highlight, making them feel pressable. Just under the card, within the panel, there is a tiny engraved progress indicator: a row of five small circular dots, with three filled in cyan and two just outlined, and tiny text: “Card 3 of 5.”

Below the phrase deck in the right column sits a “Streak & Achievements” card that feels like a glossy plaque. The card surface is a deep, slightly reflective navy, with a faint, soft reflection of imaginary overhead lights. At the top-left, heading “Streak & Achievements” appears in medium-weight. On the left side of this card, a large “21” number is displayed in bold, glowing cyan digits, as though on a segmented digital display, with small text below: “day streak.” Under the number, in smaller text, it says: “Keep going! 3 days to next badge.” On the right side, three badge icons appear horizontally spaced: each is a metallic coin-like medallion with engraved icons and subtle shadows. The first is labeled underneath “Consistency I” and shows a laurel wreath encircling a small flame icon; it is fully colored in warm gold. The second is “Vocabulary Sprinter” with a lightning bolt icon, rendered in silver with a cyan rim. The third is “Marathon Listener” with a small headphone icon, but this medallion is only partially “lit,” with parts of it in a slightly darker, matte metal to show it’s in progress. Each badge sits on a faintly raised platform, with a tiny soft shadow on its lower edge.

At the bottom of the right column is a compact “Support & Tips” card that mimics a small help console panel. The heading reads “Support & Tips,” and below are two embossed button rows with a tactile look: one labeled “Open Help Center” and another “Chat with Tutor (Beta).” Each has a tiny icon engraved to its left: a small question mark in a circle for the help center, and a minimalist headset for the tutor chat. Under these buttons, in tiny italicized text, appears a tip line: “Tip: Set a daily reminder window to protect your streak.”

Throughout the dashboard, faint micro-details reinforce the neo-skeuomorphic theme: subtle tiny screws etched near the corners of some panels (just tiny circular marks with a cross indentation), extremely delicate hairline seams where cards “fit” into the main chassis, and a general lighting model that suggests a single soft light source above and slightly to the left, giving top-left edges of components a subtle highlight and bottom-right edges a gentle shadow. Text fields are all non-editable on this page, so there are no obvious input boxes, keeping the look cohesive.

Near the bottom-left of the main dashboard, beneath “Today’s Tasks,” there is a slim, wide card spanning the left and middle columns, slightly shorter than the others, titled “Activity Breakdown”. This panel acts as a data-driven visualization and looks like an inlaid glass chart with rounded corners and a border glow. The heading at the top-left is “Activity Breakdown (Past 7 Days).” Below the heading, centered, is a stacked horizontal bar chart that compares study time across languages. The bar chart is set on a very dark inset background with vertical markers and tiny labels. Each language is a separate horizontal bar row: “Spanish,” “Japanese,” and “French,” arranged vertically with labels on the left in off-white. Each bar is segmented by daily contributions, with each day’s segment represented by a slightly different shade of cyan or violet but all within a cohesive neon palette; subtle 3D shading makes each segment appear as a slightly raised bar within the larger bar. To the right of each bar, at the bar’s end, is a numeric label such as “6.0 hrs” for Spanish. Underneath the chart is a small legend with tiny colored squares labeled “Mon,” “Tue,” “Wed,” “Thu,” “Fri,” “Sat,” “Sun,” going from dark to lighter shades. At the bottom-right of this card, there is tiny gray text: “Click a bar to adjust next week’s goals” (even though in this static description, it just visually appears as engraved instructional text).

At the very bottom of the entire dashboard, aligned with the width of the main panel, is a thin footer strip that looks like a solid bar etched into the chassis, in the same dark navy as the top header but with reversed lighting (a slight top border highlight to indicate it’s inset). On the left, in tiny gray text, it says: “© 2025 Linguava Studio. All rights reserved.” On the right, small capsule-like links appear with engraved text: “Privacy,” “Terms,” and “Status,” separated by tiny vertical grooves that resemble subtle machined slots.

The whole page has no motion or animation; every visual effect is expressed as static lighting, shadows, and textures reminiscent of high-end physical devices, creating a consistent neo-skeuomorphic language-learning dashboard that feels like a luxurious, tactile control panel.

### Dataset

```csv
Language,Mon_hours,Tue_hours,Wed_hours,Thu_hours,Fri_hours,Sat_hours,Sun_hours,Total_hours
Spanish,1.0,0.5,1.2,0.8,1.5,0.6,0.4,6.0
Japanese,0.5,0.3,0.4,0.6,0.7,0.2,0.3,3.0
French,0.8,0.6,0.7,0.9,0.4,0.3,0.3,4.0
```

instruction: Planning the generation details for the following website:
input:
Design a single-page editorial-style dashboard that feels like a refined travel magazine spread translated into a data-rich homepage for a fictional travel blog called “Roam Editorial”. The overall layout has a hierarchical, overlapping collage aesthetic, with natural asymmetries and layered panels that feel like torn pages and pinned postcards, but every element is structured like a modern web dashboard. The color palette mixes warm off-white (#F7F3EC) for the background, charcoal black (#222222) for text, and accents of muted terracotta (#C96A4A), pale sky blue (#B6D5E1), and dusty olive (#8A8F6A). Use two main typefaces: a tall, elegant serif (like “Playfair Display”) for headlines and section titles, and a clean geometric sans-serif (like “Montserrat”) for navigation, labels, and data. Headings are slightly oversized and tightly spaced, with a print-like feel; body text uses generous line height, and all text is crisp and high-contrast. The entire viewport is framed with a subtle, thin border in a lighter charcoal (#333333), like the edges of a printed page.

At the very top, spanning edge-to-edge, is a narrow masthead strip about 80px tall with a white background. On the left, aligned with generous left margin, the logo reads “Roam Editorial” in all caps serif: “ROAM” in terracotta and “EDITORIAL” in charcoal, with slightly condensed letter spacing. To the right of the logo, spaced horizontally like magazine section tabs, are the navigation links: “Dashboard”, “Trips”, “Journal”, “Gallery”, “Settings”. Each is uppercase sans-serif, small but bold, with active page “Dashboard” underlined in a thin terracotta line. On the far right sits a compact user profile pill: a small circular avatar image (a flat illustration of a traveler with a backpack in muted colors, side profile, simple flat design) followed by the text “Emma Hart · Editor” in sans-serif, and a tiny downward caret icon. The masthead casts a very soft drop shadow on the main content below, emphasizing hierarchy.

Below the masthead, the main layout is divided into two primary columns with overlapping panels that break the grid. The left column is wider (about 65% width) and begins with a large hero card whose design resembles the cover of a travel magazine issue. This hero card has a lightly textured off-white background with a large editorial photo occupying the right two-thirds of the card: a photorealistic, high-resolution image shot from slightly above eye-level of an open road cutting through golden desert plains at sunset, fading into hazy mountains. The sky is vast, with pastel pink and lavender clouds; soft warm light and long shadows create depth, and the road’s center lines form leading lines into the distance. The photo has rounded corners and a thin white border, and it slightly overlaps the card’s boundaries on the right, creating an intentional layered effect. On the left third of the hero card, vertically centered, is the main heading: “This Month’s Journeys” in large serif, left aligned. Below it, in smaller sans-serif all caps, is the subheading “CURATED ITINERARIES & FIELD NOTES”. Just under the subheading is a slimmer horizontal pill containing three compact metrics in a row, separated by faint vertical dividers: the first metric shows “New Trips: 7”, the second “Journal Entries: 24”, and the third “Countries: 4”. Each metric label is uppercase sans-serif in muted charcoal, with the number displayed slightly larger in terracotta. At the bottom left of this hero card, overlaying the photo edge, there is a subtle button that looks like a magazine pull quote: a terracotta rectangle with rounded corners and the white text “View Trip Overview →” in uppercase sans-serif.

Partially overlapping the bottom edge of this hero card, and slightly shifted to the right like a taped note, is a smaller vertical card titled “Today’s Travel Checklist”. This card is about 40% of the hero card’s width, with an off-white background and a faint grey shadow. The title “Today’s Travel Checklist” is in small serif italics at the top, and beneath it is a vertical list of four check items, each in sans-serif with a small square checkbox icon: “Finalize Lisbon photo selects”, “Confirm Tokyo flight details”, “Draft Patagonia gear checklist”, and “Sync Marrakech field notes”. Two items show a filled terracotta checkbox, indicating completion, while the others are outlined. At the top-right corner of this card is a tiny circular plus icon with a thin terracotta border and a plus sign, indicating “Add Task” (label shown as a very small grey caption beneath).

In the right column (about 35% width), floating slightly higher than the hero card’s top, is a tall, narrower card that looks like a sidebar but is styled like an editorial sidebar feature. Its background is a very pale blue. At the top is the heading “Next Departure” in serif, below which there is a horizontal band showing a small postcard-like photo of a city at night: a photorealistic aerial view of Tokyo with neon signs and dense buildings, slight film grain effect. Under the photo, bold sans-serif text reads “Tokyo, Japan” and a smaller muted line below says “In 5 days · 8-day city exploration”. Beneath this, a minimalist horizontal progress bar shows “Preparation Progress” with the label left aligned and a thin line underneath: 70% of the line is filled in terracotta, the rest is soft grey, with the numeric “70% ready” in tiny sans-serif text to the right. Further down in this sidebar, there is a micro list of preparation tasks with timeline-like dots: each line has a small circular dot in olive color, then “Book ryokan stay · Done”, “Plan Shibuya photo walk · In Progress”, and “Reserve museum tickets · Pending”, using subtle color cues (checkmarks in olive for done, dash icon for pending).

Below the hero card on the left, the layout becomes more dashboard-like with a data visualization area styled as an editorial infographic spread. This is a large horizontal panel titled “Travel Time Allocation — Last 12 Months” in serif, anchored to the left, with a faint terracotta underline that doesn’t span the full width, leaving a deliberate asymmetry. The panel background is off-white, and the panel has internal padding like a magazine infographic. Dominating the center is a horizontal stacked bar chart spanning three-quarters of the panel width, with months along the vertical axis and total days along the horizontal axis, but visually simplified. The chart covers 6 months, each rendered as a horizontal bar divided into three color segments: terracotta for “On The Road”, pale sky blue for “Planning & Research”, and dusty olive for “Editing & Writing”. Next to the chart on the right, in a narrow column, there is a small color legend with three horizontal swatches and labels in sans-serif. The chart itself is minimal but clearly annotated: each bar is labeled on the left with the month in uppercase (“JUL”, “AUG”, “SEP”, “OCT”, “NOV”, “DEC”), and inside each colored segment is a tiny white text label with the number of days for that activity. Above the chart, aligned to the right, there is a small dropdown control in sans-serif reading “View: Days” with a tiny downward caret, suggesting other metrics (but not interactive here). Below the chart, in a fine sans-serif caption, text reads “Based on logged trip days, planning sessions, and editorial time.”

To the right of this chart, within the same horizontal band but separated by a very thin vertical grey line, is a summary card with a slightly darker cream background that overlaps the line by a few pixels to maintain the collage feel. The summary card is titled “This Season’s Focus” and contains three key metrics displayed in large serif numerals with smaller sans-serif labels beneath: “42 days · In Transit”, “26 days · Field Notes”, and “18 days · Post-Production”. These metrics are stacked vertically with ample white space and thin dividing lines between them. At the bottom of this summary card is a muted italic note that reads: “Longest trip: 14 days in Patagonia”.

Underneath this data band, the left column continues with a multi-card layout titled “Current Stories in Progress” in serif, centered over the left column content. A thin horizontal line extends from either side of the title, fading out midway across the page, echoing editorial section separators. Below, three story cards are arranged in an uneven grid: two cards on the top row and one wide card beneath them, all slightly overlapping each other’s corners with subtle shadows. The top-left card, “Lisbon Light Chaser”, features a large horizontal image on its upper half: a photorealistic street-level photograph of a narrow Lisbon alley at golden hour, cobblestone street, pastel buildings with laundry hanging, and tram cables slicing across the sky. Warm light casts long shadows and a soft gradient lights up the sky. Below the image, text reads “Status: Drafting · 12 days on location” in sans-serif, and a thin progress bar shows about 40% filled in terracotta. The top-right card, “Patagonia Windlines”, has a tall vertical image of a hiker’s silhouette on a ridge with dramatic snow-capped mountains and sweeping clouds in the background, low-angle lighting from the side, cool color temperature. Its status line reads “Status: Editing · 14 days on location”, with a progress bar at 70%. The bottom card is full-width and titled “Under Neon Skies: Tokyo After Dark”. Its image spans the entire top: a photorealistic street-level night scene of Shibuya Crossing, wet pavement reflecting neon signs, blurred motion of pedestrians, cinematic contrast. Beneath, the status line reads “Status: Researching · 0 days on location (upcoming)”, with a progress bar at 10%.

The right column, beneath the “Next Departure” card, transitions into a more list-focused sidebar called “Snapshot Feed” that resembles the side column of a print layout. This section title is in small caps serif. Inside, there are three stacked mini cards, each like a clipped Polaroid note. Each mini card has a 1:1 thumbnail image aligned left and text to the right. The first image shows a photorealistic overhead shot of a cappuccino on a café table with a paper map, a camera, and a boarding pass; warm soft lighting, shallow depth of field, muted colors. To its right: “Morning in Porto” as bold text, and below in smaller grey text: “New journal entry · 3 min read”. The second mini card image shows a close-up of a passport and vintage train ticket atop a textured wooden surface, side-lit with soft shadows; its text reads “Rail Lines & Old Cities” and below “Gallery update · 24 photos”. The third image captures a pair of worn leather boots at the edge of a cliff, with ocean waves far below; slightly desaturated, high contrast, and dramatic lighting; text reads “Edge of the Atlantic”, and “Journal draft · 70% complete”. Small, faint time stamps (e.g., “2h ago”, “1d ago”) appear beneath each.

Lower in the right column is a thin, horizontally oriented card that visually overlaps both columns slightly on small screens (but here it’s mostly in the right column) and is styled like an editorial pull-quote about data: it has a pale terracotta-tinted background and contains, in large serif italics, the quote: “Travel is the only context where statistics feel like memories.” Beneath it, in tiny uppercase sans-serif, aligned to the right, is the attribution “— Editorial Analytics”.

At the very bottom of the page, spanning full width, is a footer-style meta bar that feels like a closing masthead of a magazine issue but also serves as dashboard controls. The background is slightly darker cream (#E9E1D4). On the left, small uppercase sans-serif text reads “ISSUE: WINTER 2025 · VOLUME 08”. In the center are tiny filters rendered as pill-shaped buttons with thin borders and minimal fill: “All Trips”, “Europe”, “Asia”, “Americas”, “Remote”. “All Trips” has a filled terracotta background and white text; the others are white with grey borders. On the right side of the footer bar is a subtle icon row: a tiny outline of a globe, a bookmark icon, and a settings gear, all in thin charcoal strokes, evenly spaced. Beneath the footer bar is a slim line of text in micro-sized sans-serif, centered, reading: “Dashboard view optimized for editorial review · Data refreshed 10 minutes ago.”

Throughout the layout, maintain generous margins and breathing room; cards should feel like floating sheets of paper, with overlapping corners and shadows that never become too heavy. Lines are hairline-thin, all corners are slightly rounded, and no element is animated or moving; the whole page is static, like an exquisitely designed magazine dashboard captured in a single moment. The overall impression is a refined, narrative-driven travel dashboard where statistics and journeys are presented with the quiet elegance of a print editorial.

### Dataset

```csv
Month,On The Road (days),Planning & Research (days),Editing & Writing (days)
JUL,10,5,3
AUG,8,7,4
SEP,12,4,5
OCT,6,6,7
NOV,4,8,6
DEC,2,9,8
```

instruction: Planning the generation details for the following website:
input:
Design a minimal, single-column-leaning event landing page for an “indie mountain film & gear tutorial subscription night” called “Summit Stories Live”. The page has a clean, breathable aesthetic with plenty of white space, soft grayscale tones, and a single accent color: a muted alpine green (#2F6F4E). The background is pure white across the entire viewport, with subtle, very light gray (#F5F5F5) horizontal bands used sparingly to differentiate major sections. The layout is mostly single-column and centered, but occasionally allows asymmetric elements such as an image slightly offset to the left or right within the column width. Use a modern sans-serif font like “Inter” or “SF Pro Display” for all text. Headings are medium weight, body text is regular weight. The page is vertically stacked: a simple, almost invisible header, a hero section, event details, a schedule/program description, a minimalist subscription and pricing breakdown with one data visualization, speaker highlights, partner logos, and a footer. There are no videos or animations anywhere—everything is static and calmly composed.

At the very top, a narrow header bar spans the full width on a white background. Centered within the page’s 960px content column, the header contains a small logotype on the left and a very minimal navigation on the right, set on a single horizontal line. The logotype is simply the word “SUMMIT” in uppercase, letterspaced, in dark charcoal (#222222), with “stories” in lowercase, lighter gray (#777777) directly beneath it in a smaller size, stacked. There is no icon, just this typographic logo. On the right, aligned to the right edge of the content column, are three tiny navigation links in lowercase: “event”, “program”, “subscribe”. The navigation text is in a light gray (#999999) and changes to alpine green if hovered (this is just described, not animated). The type size is small (around 12–13px) to keep the header light and unobtrusive. A hairline gray divider (#E5E5E5) sits directly below the header, stretching full width.

Below the divider, the hero section occupies the full width of the page’s content column and extends vertically for about one and a half screen heights on laptops, giving it room to breathe. The hero background remains white. At the top of the hero, centered, is a small, pill-shaped badge with a thin 1px alpine green border and white fill, containing the text “Film Night • Live Tutorials • In-Person & Online” in tiny uppercase letters, letterspaced and colored alpine green. Beneath this, the main event title is a clean, large, left-aligned heading that starts slightly to the left of the centerline: “Summit Stories Live: Indie Mountain Films & Gear Tutorials”. The title uses bold weight, dark charcoal (#111111), and spans two or three lines on desktops, with generous line height and space above and below. Under the title, there is a single-sentence subtitle in smaller, regular-weight text: “An evening of independent mountain cinema, quiet gear know-how, and a subscription to keep the peaks in reach all year.” The subtitle is medium gray (#555555) and center-aligned, creating a subtle asymmetric tension between left-aligned title and center subtitle.

To the right of the title block, but still within the same overall column width, is a tall, narrow hero image that begins roughly aligned with the subtitle’s baseline and extends downward more than the text, creating a vertical asymmetry. The image is offset slightly to the right so that on larger screens it appears to float at the edge of the content area. The hero image is a static, photorealistic, desaturated photograph of a lone climber standing on a rocky ridge at dawn, facing away from the viewer. The camera angle is slightly above and behind the climber, looking out over overlapping mountain silhouettes fading into soft haze. The color palette is muted: cool grays, soft blue-greens, and a hint of pale peach on the horizon. The climber wears a dark jacket and carries a minimal alpine pack with neat straps and a coiled rope. The sky is clear with soft gradient light; shadows are long but gentle, and the overall exposure is calm, with no harsh contrast. The composition leans to the left, leaving negative space on the right side of the image that visually blends into the white page background.

Under the subtitle and slightly overlapping visually with the bottom edge of the hero image (but without actual layering), there is the main call-to-action area, centered horizontally. It contains two simple buttons side by side, with ample space around them. The left button is filled alpine green with white text that reads “Reserve your seat”. It has a subtle 4px corner radius and thin white border. The right button has a white fill and a 1px alpine green border with alpine green text that reads “View subscription plans”. Both buttons use small caps, medium weight, and generous horizontal padding, with a bit more top and bottom white space to feel airy. Below the buttons, in tiny, light gray text, centered, is the line: “Saturday • April 19, 2025 • 6:00 PM — 11:30 PM • Boulder, CO & Livestream”.

Directly below the hero, the “Event details” section appears on a very light gray background band (#FAFAFA) that spans full width but still keeps content within the central 960px column. At the top left of this band, in small uppercase pale gray, is a label: “about the night”. Beneath it, left-aligned, is a mid-sized heading in dark charcoal: “An indie festival, distilled down to a quiet alpine evening.” This heading is followed by a concise paragraph in medium gray, explaining the concept: “Summit Stories Live gathers four independent mountain films, a live gear teardown workshop, and a year-long tutorial subscription into one minimal, focused evening. No red carpets, no noise—just stories from high places and the tools that make them possible.” The text is left-aligned, with generous line spacing. To break the single-column slightly, on the right half of this section—but still within the same vertical zone—is a simple, monochrome single-color line illustration: a thin outline drawing of three stacked, simplified mountain peaks in a continuous line, with a small, outlined carabiner hanging from one of the lines. The illustration is in the same alpine green, super minimal, with no shading, and floats to the right with ample white space around it, not touching text. Below the paragraph, on the left, a simple two-column detail grid appears, but each “column” is very narrow and minimal. On the left column, in small uppercase dark gray, it says “where”, with the line below reading “The Stillwater Microcinema, Boulder, CO” and a second line “+ Private livestream access”. On the right column, in matching style, it says “what’s included” and below lists three short lines separated by subtle dot dividers: “Screening of 4 award-winning indie mountain films • Live gear tutorial and Q&A • 12-month access to Summit Stories tutorial library”.

Scrolling down, a “Program” section returns to a pure white background. The content is centered but the textual elements are left-aligned within the column for readability. At the top, small gray uppercase label: “program”. Then a larger, regular-weight heading in charcoal: “Films and gear, hour by hour.” Below, a vertical list of time blocks, arranged like a single-column timeline, with times on the left and descriptions on the right but kept minimal and linear, no lines or bullets. Each time is in a fixed-width, small uppercase font, light gray, e.g. “6:00 PM”, followed by a single em dash and the description, e.g. “Doors, quiet gear table, pour-over coffee”. The schedule includes: “6:00 PM — Doors, quiet gear table, pour-over coffee”, “6:30 PM — Opening short: ‘Edge of the Moraine’ (18 min)”, “7:00 PM — Feature: ‘Thin Air, Quiet Steps’ (62 min)”, “8:15 PM — Live gear teardown: alpine pack layering & minimalist rack setup”, “9:15 PM — Intermission & micro-exhibits from indie gear makers”, “9:45 PM — Feature: ‘Lines in the Mist’ (54 min)”, “10:50 PM — Closing short: ‘Last Light Over Granite’ (12 min)”, “11:10 PM — Q&A, subscription walk-through, slow exit”. Each description is in medium gray, normal weight, with a bit of vertical space between entries. There are no icons, only clean text.

Below this, a small “Why subscribe” section expands on the subscription component. It stays on white background, but a subtle vertical gray line (a pale #E0E0E0, 1px wide) is placed slightly left of center, extending down through the section as a quiet visual anchor. To the right of the line, a heading in charcoal reads: “Stay with the mountains all year.” Underneath, a short paragraph explains: “Every ticket includes one year of Summit Stories: monthly micro-tutorials on alpine movement, gear care, and slow, safe mountain days—recorded in the same quiet tone as the films we love.” Under this paragraph are three simple, horizontally aligned cards (but still within the single-column width) that describe the subscription features. Each card has a thin border (#EEEEEE), slightly rounded corners, and is filled white. The cards are separated by equal gaps and do not touch the vertical line. The left card title in small bold text says “Monthly micro-lessons”, with a line beneath that reads “10–15 minute sessions focused on a single technique.” The middle card title is “Gear deep-dives”, description “From ice axes to trail shoes, one piece at a time.” The right card title is “Community notes”, description “Quiet forum threads, trip notes, and field-tested checklists.” All card titles are dark charcoal; body text is medium gray.

Next comes a minimal “Subscriptions & capacity” section on another very light gray band (#FAFAFA), where a simple, static bar chart-like visualization is presented. At the top left, the section label reads “subscriptions & capacity” in small gray uppercase. The main heading beneath it reads: “A small room, a small stream.” in charcoal. A two-sentence explanatory paragraph states: “We keep this night intentionally small. There are only 70 seats in the microcinema, and streaming access is capped so that Q&A and chat stay human.” Below, centered within the column, is a very simple, static horizontal bar chart with two bars, described visually. The chart has a transparent background with a faint bounding box, and no axes labels except the category labels and capacity numbers.
