The chart title above the bars, in tiny uppercase centred text, reads “Capacity overview”. There are two horizontal bars stacked vertically, each left-aligned. The top bar represents “In-person seats” and is shorter; the bottom bar represents “Livestream passes” and is longer. Each bar is a filled, solid alpine green rectangle with a subtle soft shadow beneath to give slight depth. At the left of each bar, in small dark gray text, is the label (“In-person seats”, “Livestream passes”). At the right end of each bar, in white text over the green fill, is the numeric ratio: “56 / 70 reserved” for in-person, and “92 / 150 reserved” for livestream. Beneath the bars, a faint gray footnote in tiny text reads: “Reservations update hourly, but we never oversell.” No animation, just static depiction of the current status.

Following this, the “Speakers & makers” section returns to a white background. The layout remains vertically stacked but introduces quiet asymmetry in how the speaker blocks are aligned. The section label at top is “voices & tools” in small uppercase light gray, centered. The heading underneath, centered, reads: “People behind the stories and the gear.” Beneath the heading, content flows in a vertical sequence of speaker cards, each offset slightly left or right relative to the column’s centerline. Each speaker card consists of a small, square, black-and-white portrait image on one side and text on the other. Portrait images are static, high-contrast photographs but remain gentle and not overly dramatic. For example, the first speaker card aligns the portrait to the left edge of the content column: a black-and-white close-up of a filmmaker in their 30s, wearing a beanie and simple jacket, shot in soft window light, faint blurred mountain photos pinned behind them. To the right of this image, aligned vertically center with it, the text block begins with the name “Mara Levin” in bold dark charcoal, a small line below in pale gray “Director, ‘Thin Air, Quiet Steps’”, and a two-line description: “Works with analog cameras in high places, telling low-volume stories about risk, care, and returning home.” The second card alternates: portrait on the right, text on the left. The second portrait shows a gear maker, “Jae Ortega”, standing in a workshop with shelves of minimal, neatly hung climbing hardware blurred behind them, shot in monochrome with soft overhead light. Their blurb reads: “Founder of Northline Atelier. Designs small-batch alpine packs and racks with deliberate restraint.” A third card features “Samir Kulkarni”, described as “Guide & tutorial host”, with a calm portrait of them in a windbreaker, leaning on trekking poles on a ridgeline, distant mountains behind. Under each speaker card, there is a hairline divider (#EFEFEF) that spans only about 60% of the width and is centered, maintaining the minimal look.

After speakers, a short “Partners” strip appears with a white background and a single thin top border. Centered text reads “Quiet partners” in tiny uppercase gray, followed by a horizontal row of three or four monochrome, ultra-minimal logo placeholders: each just a light gray rounded rectangle with the partner names in uppercase thin text inside, like “RIDGELINE CINEMA”, “NORTHLINE ATELIER”, “SILENT ROPE COLLECTIVE”. These are evenly spaced and aligned center.

Near the bottom, the primary subscription and RSVP block repeats the call-to-action in a slightly more condensed form. This block sits on a light gray band (#FAFAFA) and is strictly single-column and centered. At the top, a heading reads: “Reserve your evening.” Below is a line of text: “Choose how you’ll join, then we’ll send a quiet confirmation—no marketing blasts, ever.” Below this, two minimal pricing cards are stacked vertically with a slim gap between them (rather than side by side, to honor the single-column tendency). Each card is a white rectangle with thin gray border and slightly rounded corners. The first card is titled “In-person seat” with the price below in larger dark text: “$48 per person”. Under that, three tiny checkmark lines in soft gray text: “Includes cinema screening”, “Includes live gear workshop”, “Includes 12-month subscription”. The second card is “Livestream pass” with “$36 per person” and similar checkmarks: “Live HD stream of all films & Q&A”, “Access to recorded tutorials”, “12-month subscription”. At the bottom of each card is a small, full-width button in alpine green with white text that says “Reserve in-person” on the first and “Reserve livestream” on the second. Between these two cards, a subtle line of text in tiny gray appears, centered: “Or, join the waitlist if seats fill up.” This is followed by a short, thin button with transparent background and alpine green border reading “Join waitlist”.

Finally, the footer is kept extremely minimal. It sits on a white background with a faint top border line in light gray. Content is centered within the 960px column. On the left side of the footer (within that centered area), there is tiny text: “© 2025 Summit Stories Collective.” and below it, in even smaller text, “Designed to be read in quiet rooms.” On the right side of the footer, aligned right, two subtle text links in light gray read “Contact” and “Accessibility notes”, separated by a small dot. The entire page uses consistent spacing, plenty of white space between sections, and no decorative flourishes beyond the single accent color and the minimal mountain line illustration. There are no videos, no animated elements, and no hover behaviors beyond simple color changes described. The whole visual impression is that of a quiet, considered, nearly monochrome event page that merges an indie mountain film festival with a mountain gear tutorial subscription, all within a restrained, single-column-forward layout.

### Dataset
| Type              | Category           | Reserved | Capacity |
|-------------------|--------------------|----------|----------|
| Seating           | In-person seats    | 56       | 70       |
| Streaming access  | Livestream passes  | 92       | 150      |

instruction: Planning the generation details for the following website:
input:
Design a single long-scroll event landing page in a brutalist aesthetic for a one-day coffee brewing deep-dive called “BRUTAL BREW // HANDS-ON COFFEE GEAR LAB”. The page should feel like a physical flyer slammed onto a screen: harsh contrasts, bold typography, overlapping blocks, deliberate “ugliness,” and a layered, almost collage-like hierarchy. The background of the entire page is a flat, slightly off-white #F7F4EC with very subtle, almost imperceptible gray noise texture, as if printed on cheap paper. Text is primarily in black #000000 with some sections in high-contrast color blocks: neon yellow #F9FF00, dirty cyan #00C2C7, and muted red #C43B2D. Typography is aggressively brutalist: the main heading font is a condensed, uppercase grotesk with tight tracking, like “Impact / Condensed Sans” style; body text uses a monospace font that looks like terminal output, with fixed-width characters. All text is left-aligned unless otherwise specified. Elements often overlap slightly, with drop shadows that look like cut-and-paste pieces: hard-edged shadows in solid #000000 at 6–8px offset.

At the very top, there is a fixed, horizontal navigation strip that looks like a black sticker stuck onto the page. It spans full width, about 72px tall, background #000000, with no transparency. On the far left is a brutalist wordmark reading “BRUTAL BREW LAB” in uppercase neon yellow #F9FF00, set in a tall condensed sans, around 26px, slightly vertically misaligned so it is a bit too close to the top edge, emphasizing deliberate imperfection. Immediately to its right is a tiny, thin vertical white line divider, 1px. The navigation links are aligned to the right, cramped together with irregular spacing, each in uppercase monospace white text, 14px. The visible nav items in order are: “EVENT”, “SCHEDULE”, “GEAR LAB”, “TICKETS”, “FAQ”. Each is separated by a thin vertical gray #666666 line. When hovered (describe visually but no animation), the link appears as if it’s been highlighted with a neon yellow highlighter: a rectangular background in #F9FF00 with the text turning black. At the far right of the nav, overlapped slightly on top of the black strip (extending beyond its bottom edge by about 6px) there is a hard-edged rectangular button reading “REGISTER // $89” in uppercase, background #F9FF00, black border 2px, black text in the same condensed grotesk, 14px, all squished tightly.

Below this fixed nav, the hero section occupies the first visible viewport height, intentionally cluttered. The hero is split into two primary overlapping columns: the left two-thirds is a stacked block of text, while the right third is dominated by a huge collage-like hero image of coffee equipment. The left side begins with a harsh neon yellow banner that looks like someone taped a strip across the page. It is a wide rectangle about 120px tall and 75% of the content width. On it, in misaligned black uppercase condensed sans, 18px, is the text aligned left: “ONE-DAY COFFEE EQUIPMENT INTENSIVE // OAKRIDGE WAREHOUSE // JUNE 28”. The neon banner has jagged, imperfect edges, as if torn. Overlapping the bottom right corner of this banner is the main title in massive black condensed uppercase letters, 72px, with super tight line spacing: “BRUTAL BREW // HANDS-ON COFFEE GEAR LAB”. The “//” is in neon yellow, slightly offset vertically, breaking the baseline. Below the title, in smaller monospace body text, 16px, black, left-aligned, is a block of copy reading: “No latte art. No lifestyle. Just uncompromising hardware and the physics of extraction. A full-day event for coffee nerds, baristas, and equipment-obsessed humans who want to strip brewing down to metal, water, and grind.” The edges of this text block do not align perfectly with the title; it is indented a few pixels too far to the right, adding to the brutalist feel. Under this paragraph, still on the left, there’s a slim, horizontal black rule spanning about 60% width. Beneath it, there is a small, three-column brutalist “meta info” strip with rigid borders: each column has a thin black border, slight gap between them, background #EDE6D6. The three columns read, in uppercase monospace, 12px: first column: “DATE: JUNE 28, 2025”; second: “LOCATION: OAKRIDGE INDUSTRIAL WAREHOUSE // DENVER, CO”; third: “CAP: 60 HUMANS // ZERO LIVESTREAMS”. The text inside is close to the borders, almost cramped.

The right hero column showcases a large, flat-design illustration of an aggressive coffee gear still life, highly detailed. The illustration is contained in a rectangle that overlaps both the hero background and marginally the nav bar. The background of the illustration is a flat concrete gray with faint, vertical hairline cracks. On a slightly skewed perspective table surface that extends from left to right, various coffee tools are arranged in a harsh, geometric composition: a tall, brushed stainless-steel kettle with a pronounced gooseneck spout sits in the foreground slightly right of center, rendered in semi-realistic flat shading with sharp highlights along its edges and a matte black handle; to its left is a chunky, industrial-looking grinder with a square body, visible hopper filled with illustrated coffee beans (each bean drawn as small, dark ovals with opposing highlights), and a calibration dial with bold numeric ticks; behind these, a manual espresso lever machine with exaggerated brass levers and a thick, black base, simplified but clearly mechanical, looms. The lighting in the illustration comes from the top left, casting matte, hard-edged shadows rightward. The color palette is restricted: metals in cool grays and steel blues, shadows in deep charcoal, accents in neon yellow on indicator lights and knobs. In the background, faint blocky silhouettes of shelving and boxes suggest a warehouse. The style is semi-flat with strong outlines and minimal gradients. In the top right corner of this illustration rectangle, overlapping it, is a small, pasted-like caption block in neon yellow with black monospace text, 11px, that reads: “NOT A COFFEE SHOP. A GEAR LAB.”

Below the hero, an irregular overlapping layout introduces the event’s core sections. First, a wide, left-aligned section with the heading “WHY THIS EXISTS” in uppercase black condensed sans, 28px, appears slightly overlapping the lower edge of the hero image by a few pixels. The heading is underlined with a thick, hand-drawn style black line that overshoots the text width. Underneath, two different blocks are arranged: on the left, a chunk of body copy; on the right, a vertical list of harsh bullet points. The body text reads, in 16px monospace: “Traditional coffee classes teach recipes. We teach systems. This day is about reverse-engineering every variable in your brew stack — from burr geometry to thermal mass — so you can stop guessing and start operating like a lab tech.” Below that: “We’ll smash through marketing myths, dissect gear, and give you repeatable frameworks for dialing in any setup, from travel rigs to overbuilt home bars.” On the right, in a narrow column with a pale gray #EFEFEF background and a 2px black border, there are four brutal bullet statements, each preceded by a small black square. Text exactly: “NO BRAND SPONSORS ON STAGE.”, “NO PRETTY LATTE SWANS.”, “NO SLIDESHows. ONLY GEAR AND WHITEBOARDS.”, “NO ‘SECRET’ RECIPES. ONLY TRANSPARENT DATA.”

Immediately below and slightly overlapping the “WHY THIS EXISTS” section, a brutalist schedule section appears with a full-width, dirty cyan banner. The banner background is #00C2C7, full width, about 200px tall, stretching edge-to-edge horizontally. At its left, in extremely large uppercase condensed black letters, 40px, slightly rotated 1–2 degrees counterclockwise, the heading reads: “SCHEDULE // JUNE 28”. To the right, within this cyan block, there’s a sharply bordered white box that looks “pasted” on top with a thick 3px black border and slight drop shadow. Inside, a vertical timeline list uses a grid-like structure with times in a fixed-width column. Each entry is monospace, 14px, black. The schedule entries appear as rows separated by hairline black rules. Each row shows: “09:00 — GATE OPENS // WEIGH-IN & WELCOME ESPRESSO”, “10:00 — EXTRACTION PHYSICS 101 (NO MATH DEGREE REQUIRED)”, “11:30 — GRINDER TEARDOWN: BURR GEOMETRY & ALIGNMENT”, “13:00 — BRUTAL LUNCH BREAK // FILTER COFFEE + BREAD”, “14:00 — HANDS-ON: DIALING POUR-OVER, AEROPRESS, AND IMMERSION”, “16:00 — TEMPERATURE, FLOW RATE, AND REAL-TIME TDS TESTING”, “18:00 — OPEN GEAR LAB: BRING YOUR OWN RIG”, “20:00 — WRAP-UP // TAKE-HOME PLAYBOOK & Q&A”. The rows are packed, almost touching.

Below the schedule, there’s a “GEAR LAB ZONES” section with overlapping cards. The heading “GEAR LAB ZONES” in uppercase condensed black, 28px, is right-aligned this time, with the text block positioned slightly into the right margin. The main content consists of three large brutalist cards arranged in a staggered, overlapping layout: the left card partially overlaps the middle, which slightly overlaps the right. Each card has a different background color: left is pale beige #EEE1C5, middle is light gray #E0E0E0, right is neon yellow #F9FF00, all with 2px solid black borders and stark drop shadows.

The left card is titled “ZONE 01 // FILTER” in uppercase 18px black condensed sans, top-left. Below, in a grid-like layout, there’s a medium-sized flat illustration of a V60 dripper, a Kalita-style flat-bottom dripper, and a scale. The drippers are drawn in thick outlined flat style: the V60 is a white cone with exaggerated ridges, the Kalita is a flat-bottom gray dripper with three visible holes shown in cross-section. The scale beneath is a black rectangle with a minimalist digital display reading “20.0g” in green digits. The objects sit on a flat horizontal line suggesting a table, with harsh shadows underneath in dark gray. Under the illustration is monospace text: “Dial-in lab with drippers, kettles, and scales. Expect to measure everything: bloom mass, flow rates, and bed depth.”

The middle card reads “ZONE 02 // ESPRESSO” with a flat illustration of a monstrous, dual-group chrome espresso machine with angular lines, shrunk down but detailed: visible pressure gauges, portafilters with black handles, and a bottomless portafilter shot pouring into a tiny demitasse cup, the stream drawn as a thick golden line with steam indicated by three straight gray lines above. A tamp, puck screen, and small distribution tool are arranged nearby. The background of the illustration is flat white. Below: “We’ll tear down baskets, discuss puck prep as engineering, and watch live pressure curves.”

The right card, neon yellow, reads “ZONE 03 // WILD CARDS” with body text: “Weird rigs welcome. Travel setups, manual lever machines, zero-electric brewers. Bring your own oddities — we have measurement gear to test them.” A small monochrome icon row sits at the bottom: a tiny outline of a travel hand grinder, a collapsible filter, and a camping stove, all drawn with near-stick-figure simplicity.

Directly beneath the “GEAR LAB ZONES” cards, use a brutalist, data-driven section called “EXTRACTION OUTCOMES (WHAT YOU’LL IMPROVE)” that includes a simple bar chart visualization with explicit values. The heading is uppercase black condensed, 24px, left-aligned. Under it, on the left, textual explanation: “We ran this event with a closed beta group of 24 attendees. On average, they reduced brew variance and improved target extraction consistency within a week.” On the right, a rectangular white chart area with a 2px black border and no gridlines, looking hand-drawn. Inside is a vertical bar chart of three metrics, each bar a solid rectangular block with harsh edges. At the bottom, three labels in uppercase monospace, 12px, angled slightly: “BREW-TO-BREW VARIANCE”, “HIT TARGET TDS RANGE”, “DIAL-IN TIME PER NEW COFFEE”. Each metric has two bars side by side: “BEFORE” (light gray #C4C4C4) and “AFTER” (neon yellow #F9FF00) with thin black outlines. Above each pair of bars is a small monospace annotation like “↓ from 8.2% to 3.1% variance” or “↑ from 42% to 78% target hits”; use exact values from the dataset below. A tiny legend in the top-right corner of the chart shows two small swatches with labels “BEFORE” and “AFTER” in 10px monospace.

Below the data section, the “WHO THIS IS FOR” block is a wide horizontal strip using inverted colors: background pure black #000000, text in off-white #F7F4EC. The heading is centered, uppercase, 24px, reading: “WHO THIS IS FOR (AND NOT FOR)”. Beneath, content is arranged in two columns. The left column title: “YES, YOU:” in neon yellow, 14px, uppercase monospace. Under it, a list of simple lines: “Home baristas with overbuilt setups”, “Café staff who want more than dial-in scripts”, “Engineers, scientists, and data nerds who also drink coffee”, “Existing pros who are tired of vague ‘notes of stonefruit’ talk”. The right column title: “PROBABLY NOT YOU:” in muted red #C43B2D. Under it: “You want latte art classes”, “You’re here for brand freebies”, “You’re offended by the word ‘nerd’”, “You don’t want to weigh or measure anything”. The columns are separated by a thin vertical neon yellow line.

Next, a brutalist “TICKETS” section with overlapping pricing cards appears. The heading at left is “TICKETS // LIMITED TO 60” in uppercase, 30px condensed black, over a faint diagonal pattern of light gray lines, as if photocopied. Three ticket tier cards are displayed horizontally, each a simple rectangle with 2px black border and rough-feeling spacing. They overlap slightly. The first card, slightly offset upward, has a background #EDE6D6 and reads: “EARLY METAL” in uppercase condensed at top, small monospace below: “$69 // FIRST 20 SEATS”. An underline text: “Includes full-day access + printed lab workbook”. The second card, slightly behind the first and bigger, is the primary tier with a neon yellow background that almost vibrates. It reads: “STANDARD HUMAN” at top, “$89 // NEXT 30 SEATS”, with subtext: “Full-day access + printed lab workbook + data pack downloads”. A thin black border around the price rectangle makes it pop. The third card, a muted red #C43B2D background with white text, reads: “PRO CREW” and “$129 // 10 SEATS” with subtext: “Bring your café team (up to 3 baristas) and access post-event Q&A document.” Overlapping the bottom edges of all three cards is a wide, full-width call-to-action strip in stark black with huge neon yellow text centered: “REGISTER NOW // ZERO LIVESTREAM. ZERO REPLAYS.” Under this CTA, there is a brutalist form: fields are simple rectangular boxes with 1px black borders, all full width on mobile but shown here as a stacked vertical list with minimal padding. The labels are uppercase monospace 12px above each field: “EMAIL”, “NAME”, “WHICH RIG DO YOU USE MOST?” (input in a single-line text box) and a simple checkbox with label “I ACCEPT THAT THIS WILL BE NERDY.” The checkbox is drawn as a 14px square with a hand-drawn black X inside when checked.

Following the tickets, an “ABOUT THE LAB HOSTS” section appears, with portraits presented in a rough, high-contrast style. The heading on the left: “HUMANS BEHIND THE METAL” in uppercase, 24px, black. Under it, a short paragraph: “We’re not influencers. We are ex-baristas, engineers, and repair techs who have broken, rebuilt, and abused more coffee gear than we’d like to admit.” To the right, three small, square, black-and-white portraits of the team in a halftone, photocopied style: each is a stylized, high-contrast headshot with thick black outlines, simplified features, as if screen printed. Beneath each portrait is a name and role in monospace: “KAI // EX-CAFÉ LEAD + MECHANICAL ENGINEER”, “REYES // FIELD TECH FOR ESPRESSO MACHINES”, “MIRA // DATA NERD & WATER CHEMISTRY ENTHUSIAST”. The portraits slightly overlap one another like scattered polaroids, each with an outlined frame and dropshadow.

Near the bottom, a brutally honest FAQ section appears with a grid-like layout. The heading reads: “FAQ // READ THIS BEFORE EMAIL” in uppercase black, 26px. Below, questions are presented in two columns, each Q in bold monospace uppercase about 14px, with answers in regular monospace 14px, black. For example: “Q: DO I NEED TO BRING ANYTHING?” with answer “A: OPTIONAL: YOUR OWN GRINDER OR BREWER. REQUIRED: A NOTEBOOK, AND A WILLINGNESS TO MEASURE STUFF.”; “Q: WILL THERE BE COFFEE?” with “A: YES. LOTS. BUT THIS IS NOT A ‘TASTING’. WE BREW TO LEARN, NOT TO SAVOR.”; “Q: IS THIS BEGINNER FRIENDLY?” with “A: IF YOU CAN MAKE BASIC COFFEE AND AREN’T AFRAID OF NUMBERS, YOU’RE GOOD.”; “Q: IS THIS RECORDED?” with “A: NO. YOU HAVE TO BE THERE IN PERSON.” Each Q&A pair is separated by a thin black horizontal rule that doesn’t span the full width, emphasizing the chopped, modular feel.

Finally, at the very bottom, a brutalist footer spans full width with a muted gray #D3CEC3 background and a thick top border in black. On the left, small uppercase monospace text: “BRUTAL BREW LAB // OAKRIDGE WAREHOUSE, DENVER, CO // JUNE 28, 2025”. Beneath that, a tiny disclaimer: “We are not affiliated with any coffee gear manufacturers. Bring your own critical thinking.” On the right of the footer, there is a column labeled “CONTACT” in uppercase 12px. Under it, simple text lines for an email (“EMAIL: hi@brutalbrewlab.com”) and an inline, underlined text link “VIEW WAREHOUSE MAP (PDF)” that looks like a plain black underline. The entire page maintains consistent brutalist styling: minimal rounded corners, no soft gradients, no animation, no video. Overlaps are slightly aggressive, shadows hard-edged, and the visual hierarchy is built through color blocks, intense typography, and deliberate misalignment.

### Dataset
| Metric                        | State   | Value |
|-------------------------------|---------|-------|
| Brew-to-brew variance (%)     | Before  | 8.2   |
| Brew-to-brew variance (%)     | After   | 3.1   |
| Hit target TDS range (%)      | Before  | 42    |
| Hit target TDS range (%)      | After   | 78    |
| Dial-in time per new coffee (min) | Before  | 18    |
| Dial-in time per new coffee (min) | After   | 9     |

instruction: Planning the generation details for the following website:
input:
Design a single-page dashboard-style homepage for a fictional film festival organizer platform called “FrameGrid Festival Console”, using a rigorous Swiss-style typography aesthetic with a hierarchical, asymmetric, and slightly overlapping layout. The overall background is a matte, off-white (#F5F5F5) with subtle, almost imperceptible vertical grid lines in a very pale gray (#E8E8E8) that suggest a modular grid without being distracting. Margins are generous and consistent, with a large left margin acting like a Swiss poster gutter. The primary color accents are a vivid tomato red (#E34234) and deep charcoal black (#111111), with occasional use of a steel gray (#666666) for secondary text. The primary typeface is a grotesk sans-serif reminiscent of Helvetica or Suisse Int’l; all text is in English, with a mix of uppercase and lowercase but with key labels in ALL CAPS. The structure feels like a modern poster grid: strong horizontal and vertical alignment, bold typographic blocks, and careful use of white space, while allowing some panels to slightly overlap or break the grid to create tension. At the very top, a fixed-height horizontal navigation bar runs edge-to-edge, about 80px tall, but visually divided into two columns. On the far left, aligned with the left grid, the brand mark is a simple text logo: “FRAMEGRID” in uppercase, bold, tight tracking, in black. Directly below in smaller, light gray uppercase, is the subtitle “FESTIVAL CONSOLE”. The logo column background is pure white with a 1px vertical divider line in light gray separating it from the rest of the nav. To the right of the logo, occupying the majority of the bar, is a horizontally split area: the top half contains a minimal nav with three items aligned to the right in medium weight, uppercase, small font: “DASHBOARD”, “FILMS”, “SCHEDULE”, evenly spaced with large letter spacing; the currently active “DASHBOARD” is underlined with a thin red line that aligns perfectly with the grid. Beneath the nav items, still within the header bar, is a subtle horizontal ticker-like text in a small, condensed font on the left side reading “Next deadline: Short Film Submissions — 18 SEP 2025, 23:59 CET”. The ticker text is in charcoal, with the date in red. The rightmost corner of the nav bar includes a circular user avatar placeholder (just a gray circle with initials “LM” in white) and to its left a minimal icon-only bell notification (outlined, simple geometry, black stroke) with a small red dot indicating alerts. Beneath the fixed nav, the page content is framed in a wide central column, but elements occasionally stretch full width. The main hero area spans the top third of the viewport and is divided into two overlapping layers: on the left, a large typographic block; on the right, a tall, narrow video panel that slightly overlaps the left block and extends a bit above and below it, breaking the strict grid. The left hero block sits on a white rectangle with a subtle drop shadow that lifts it from the off-white background. At the top left of this block, in very small uppercase, thin weight, gray text is the label “OVERVIEW / 2025 EDITION”. Below this, in large, bold black text that dominates the page, is the heading “Your festival at a glance.” set in sentence case, left-aligned, with careful leading and optical kerning. Directly under the heading is a smaller, lighter gray line of copy: “Track submissions, curate screenings, and keep your film festival running on time.” Beneath that, aligned with the left edge, is a horizontal row of two pill-like buttons. The primary button is a flat tomato red rounded rectangle with the label “Create New Screening” in white uppercase, medium weight. Next to it with 16px gap is an outlined button with a 1px black stroke and white fill, with the label “Invite Jurors” in black uppercase. Both buttons are strictly aligned to the underlying grid, and their baseline lines up with subtle elements in the right panel. The right hero panel is a tall vertical rectangle with slightly rounded corners, extending from near the top of the hero to just into the next section. It has a black background and contains a short looping video. The video depicts an abstract, high-contrast montage of film festival imagery in a flat yet cinematic style: stylized silhouettes of people seated in a dark theater, viewed from behind, with rows of heads in soft gradients; bright projector beams cutting through floating dust particles; minimalist festival laurel icons in white; and close-ups of ticket stubs and clapperboards. The style is semi-photorealistic but simplified, with sharp geometric shapes and strong contrast. The color palette is mostly deep blacks and dark blues, with bright red highlights that match the brand color and occasional white flashes that sync with the beat. The movement is subtle and looped over ~8 seconds: a projector beam slowly sweeps across the frame, a film reel turns in slow motion, light pulses gently to create a sense of breathing. There is no text overlay on the video, only a very minimal set of controls: a tiny white play/pause icon at the bottom left corner and a very thin progress bar that becomes a red line when hovered. The hero block and video overlap so that the right edge of the white hero block sits behind the left edge of the video, with a soft, layered effect. Below the hero, the page content is organized into an asymmetric two-column layout, with the left column wider (about 60%) and the right column narrower (40%), but each column contains multiple vertically stacked panels with different heights. The spacing between panels is constant and governed by the grid. Immediately under the hero on the left, there is a section titled “Today’s control panel” in uppercase, black, small size, aligned left. Below that title is a white rectangular card with subtle shadow, containing three horizontal metric tiles, each separated by 1px gray dividers. Each tile uses a Swiss-style layout: on the left a small label, on the right a large number, with supporting detail beneath. Tile 1 reads “Submissions” on the top-left in gray uppercase, and on the right, in bold large black numerals: “284”. Below the number, in smaller gray text: “+36 since yesterday”, with “+36” in green (#1AA04A). Tile 2 reads “Screenings” with “47” on the right, and below: “12 venues · 7 cities” in gray. Tile 3 reads “Jury decisions” with “61%” on the right, and under: “pending reviews: 112” in muted red text. Each tile aligns its text and numbers perfectly to a baseline grid. Beneath this card, still on the left column, is an overlapping panel arrangement: a wide schedule timeline card that subtly overlaps the bottom of the metrics card, with its top edge sliding up behind it. This schedule panel is titled on its top-left “Today’s schedule / 18 SEP 2025” in small uppercase gray. The card shows a horizontal timeline of screenings as a minimalist, data-driven visualization. A simple time axis runs from “10:00” to “22:00” with tick marks and thin vertical grid lines. Colored bars represent scheduled slots, arranged in a single row. Each bar is a flat rectangle with square corners, colored differently by programme: a red bar labeled “OPENING — 10:30” (white text inside the red bar), a dark gray bar labeled “DOCS BLOCK A — 13:00”, a muted blue bar labeled “SHORTS COMPETITION — 16:00”, and a muted green bar labeled “MIDNIGHT THRILLERS — 20:30” in white text. Hover states (to be described) would intensify the bar color and show a thin outline. The timeline is flat and clean, with no chart junk: light gray axis, simple labels beneath. To reinforce that this is data-driven, a tiny legend in the top-right corner of the card uses small color swatches with labels: “Premiere”, “Competition”, “Retrospective”, “Special”. Overlapping slightly with the bottom right of this schedule card, but visually controlled, is a small floating label-like panel in white with a tiny shadow, rotated by 1–2 degrees for subtle tension, containing the text “Next: ‘Short Form Dreams’ at 16:00” in black sentence case. The right column begins just below the hero video, and contains two main stacked cards. The top-right card is titled “Submission funnel” in uppercase, small black text. Inside is a compact bar chart dashboard-like visualization emphasizing Swiss clarity: a white background, no 3D, a thin black border. On the left side of the card, vertically aligned, are four horizontal bars representing stages: “Submitted”, “In review”, “Selected”, “Rejected”. Each label is aligned flush left in a small uppercase font with gray text. The bars extend to the right with lengths proportional to the underlying data values: “Submitted” is the longest, in a very light gray fill with a thin black outline; “In review” slightly shorter in a mid-gray; “Selected” smaller in tomato red (solid fill); “Rejected” a slightly darker gray than “Submitted”. To the far right, at the end of each bar, is the numeric value in small black text: “320”, “210”, “96”, “48” respectively. A faint vertical grid line behind them helps judge length. Above the chart, aligned right, is a tiny dropdown label: “This week ▼” in small black text. Below the chart, aligned left, a caption reads “Conversion to selection: 30%” with “30%” in red. The bottom-right card is a list-style panel titled “Active festivals” in uppercase. It lists three active festival projects as stacked rows, each row separated by a thin 1px line. Each row has: on the left, in bold black text, the festival name; on the right, in small uppercase gray, a status; and below that, a thin progress bar in red showing overall planning completion. Row 1: Festival name “FRAMEGRID SHORTS 2025” with status tag “PROGRAMMING” in a lozenge outlined in black, and a progress bar filled to about 70%. Row 2: “NEW WAVE DOCS WEEK” with status “SUBMISSIONS OPEN” in a red- outlined tag, and a bar at ~40%. Row 3: “URBAN FRAMES NIGHT” with status “IN REVIEW” in a gray-outlined tag, and a bar at ~55%. On hover or selection (described visually only), a row background would change to a very pale gray to maintain the minimalist style. The midsection of the page includes a subtle motion element in the form of a background transition behind a specific strip: between the metric cards and schedule on the left and the charts on the right, there is a narrow full-width horizontal band about 120px tall. This band has a slightly darker gray background (#EEEEEE) and displays a minimalist animated abstract pattern that moves extremely slowly: thin diagonal lines (hairline thickness) in slightly darker gray drift horizontally, like a long exposure of city lights, giving a sensation of film strip movement. The animation is subtle and slow enough not to distract, and the lines loop seamlessly. Over this band, aligned to the left, sits a small text block in black: “LIVE STATUS” in small uppercase above the phrase “3 screenings running · 2 venues idle” in medium weight, while a tiny pulsating red dot to the left indicates live activity; the dot gently scales up and down with a smooth easing animation. Toward the lower part of the page, below the main dashboard sections, the layout becomes more editorial. The left column now hosts a wider white card titled “Programming notes” in uppercase, small black text. Inside, the content is arranged in two columns of text like a magazine, using a slightly smaller font size. The left text column begins with a bold subheading in black: “Prioritize emerging voices” followed by a paragraph in gray body text: “Stay ahead of the curve by tracking diversity, debut features, and regional representation across your programme. Use this space to brief your team and pin key decisions.” The right text column has a subheading: “Balance formats and runtimes” with its own paragraph. The body text is justified, evoking Swiss editorial design, with consistent line length and generous leading. A thin red vertical rule separates the two columns. In the right column at this lower section, a vertical stack of two smaller cards appears. The top card is “Team activity” with a list of recent events: “09:12 — Jury member Ana L. submitted 12 new notes” in black, “08:47 — Venue ‘Cinema Lumière’ updated seating capacity” in gray, etc., each entry on its own line with a small square bullet in red. The card’s background is white and the typography is small and tightly set. Beneath it, a smaller card titled “Pinned deadlines” is a simple list with each deadline in bold black and the date in uppercase gray: e.g., “Final programme lock — 02 OCT 2025”, “Print materials to press — 10 OCT 2025”. The very bottom of the page features a narrow footer, with a thin black top border. On the left, small gray text reads “FRAMEGRID FESTIVAL CONSOLE — v2.5.1” and below that “© 2025 FrameGrid Collective”. On the right, aligned to the baseline, small uppercase links in gray: “Support”, “Status”, “Changelog”, spaced evenly. Throughout, all icons (like the bell, small chevron for dropdowns, etc.) are drawn in a very minimal line style, with strokes matching the font weight of small text, adhering to the Swiss fidelity to line quality. The entire page responds to hover interactions with micro-changes in color and subtle shadows, never using exaggerated effects. All charts and data-driven elements should be rendered with precise alignment and exact values as indicated below. The aesthetic must remain strictly grid-driven and typographic, with occasional overlaps (hero video over the hero text card, the schedule panel overlapping the metrics card edge, the floating “Next” label slightly rotated) to introduce visual hierarchy and dynamism while maintaining harmony.

### Dataset
| Metric                    | Value |
|---------------------------|-------|
| Submissions (today total) | 284   |
| Submissions (yesterday)   | 248   |
| Screenings                | 47    |
| Jury decisions complete % | 61    |
| Pending reviews           | 112   |

| Submission Stage | Count |
|------------------|-------|
| Submitted        | 320   |
| In review        | 210   |
| Selected         | 96    |
| Rejected         | 48    |

| Today Time Slot           | Start | End  |
|---------------------------|-------|------|
| OPENING                   | 10:30 | 12:00|
| DOCS BLOCK A              | 13:00 | 15:00|
| SHORTS COMPETITION        | 16:00 | 18:30|
| MIDNIGHT THRILLERS        | 20:30 | 22:00|

| Active Festival          | Status            | Progress (%) |
|--------------------------|-------------------|--------------|
| FRAMEGRID SHORTS 2025    | PROGRAMMING       | 70           |
| NEW WAVE DOCS WEEK       | SUBMISSIONS OPEN  | 40           |
| URBAN FRAMES NIGHT       | IN REVIEW         | 55           |

instruction: Planning the generation details for the following website:
input:
Design a brutally minimal, high-contrast landing page for a “FilmCraft Brutalist” film production tutorial subscription homepage, using a rigid but slightly broken multi-column grid that spans the full browser width. The background is pure #f8f8f8 off-white with no gradients, and the entire page relies on stark black (#000000) and an acid neon yellow (#EFFF00) as accent, with occasional flat gray lines. Fonts are entirely sans-serif and system-like: all headings use a heavy, condensed grotesk style that looks similar to “Impact” or “Anton,” all caps; body text uses a monospaced font similar to “Menlo” or “Courier New,” giving it a utilitarian code-manual feel. The page has harsh, uncompromising spacing: big gutters, visible grid lines implied by alignment, and strong horizontal and vertical edges; most elements sit inside rectangles with 1px black borders or no border at all. The entire layout feels like a film production zine from the 1980s that has been digitized.

At the very top, a thin, full-width horizontal bar (about 60px tall) acts as the header. It has a neon yellow background and black text. On the left, aligned to a strict left margin, is the logo: a simple rectangular block of text, all caps, bold condensed: “FILMCRAFT BRUTALIST”. The words “FILMCRAFT” and “BRUTALIST” are stacked in two lines, left-aligned, with “FILMCRAFT” slightly larger. To the right, occupying roughly the middle third of the header, is a navigation cluster in uppercase monospaced text: “COURSES”, “LIVE LABS”, “PRICING”, “ABOUT”, “LOGIN”. Each item is spaced by vertical black bars “|” instead of big padding, giving it a command-line vibe. On the far right, there’s a flat black button with no rounded corners, labeled in neon yellow monospace text: “START 7-DAY TRIAL”. When hovered in the imagined interaction, the button inverts: neon yellow background, black text. The header has a fixed position at the top, so as users scroll it remains anchored like a rigid strip.

Below the header, the hero section consumes the rest of the first viewport height and is divided into a three-column grid with uneven column widths: 40% / 35% / 25%. The entire hero has a slightly textured, pale gray (#eeeeee) background with no gradient, just a faint noise texture to keep it from feeling too clean. In the left column, there is a bold block of text, aligned flush left with huge typographic hierarchy. At the top of this column, in small uppercase monospace, black: “ONLINE FILM PRODUCTION LAB”. Below it, large all-caps condensed heading, consuming two lines: “LEARN TO SHOOT FILMS LIKE YOU MEAN IT.” The period at the end is emphasized with a neon yellow square instead of a dot — a tiny filled square aligned with the baseline of the last letter. Under that, a short paragraph in smaller monospaced text: “Raw, unpolished, industry-grade tutorials on directing, cinematography, sound, and post. No fluff. Just what you actually use on set.” The paragraph is left aligned, with uneven line breaks that give it a slightly printed zine feel. Below this text, there is a rough horizontal black line (1px, slightly offset, drawn intentionally a bit too long) acting as a separator. Under the line, two horizontally aligned call-to-action buttons: on the left, a large neon yellow rectangle with black monospace text: “SUBSCRIBE NOW”; on the right, outlined with a 1px black border and transparent inside, black text: “BROWSE COURSES”. The buttons are flat and rectangular, with no rounded corners and square edges. The line height is tight so they feel bold and boxy.

The middle column of the hero section holds a tall brutalist-styled video frame showing a short looping video. The video frame is a rigid rectangle with a 2px black border. At the top left of the frame, overlayed in a small black label box with neon yellow monospace text, is “LOOPING DEMO REEL”. Within the video: a high-contrast, black-and-white, grainy montage of film production shots, cut in quick, 2–3 second loops. The first shot: overhead view of a camera rig on a tripod, set in an empty studio with visible cables on the floor, harsh top lighting casting hard shadows, static camera. Then it cuts to a close-up of a clapperboard snapping shut, the white clapstick creating a strong diagonal. Next, a handheld shot gliding past an actor under a bright key light, their face blown out in high contrast, background lost in deep shadows. Then a slow dolly shot of a light stand silhouette against a textured concrete wall. The video loops seamlessly, with jittery jump cuts; the colors remain monochrome, but occasional neon yellow overlays appear: a thick yellow “REC” text in the corner, a blinking square representing a record indicator. The video is mute by default; at the bottom right of the frame is a tiny minimalist square icon, black with a neon yellow outline, resembling a speaker mute symbol created with three sharp lines.

The right column is a narrow vertical strip, designed like a printed sidebar. It has a white background and a thin black border. At the top, in small uppercase monospace text aligned center: “NEXT LIVE SESSION”. Below this, large bold heading: “DIRECTING ON A MICRO-BUDGET”. Underneath, smaller text: “SAT · APR 26 · 19:00 UTC (LIVE Q&A)”. There is a horizontal black dividing line. Under this line, a block of text: “Reserve a spot and get access to the replay, shot breakdown PDF, and lighting diagrams.” Beneath this description is a narrow neon yellow button spanning the column width labeled “RESERVE MY SPOT”. Below the button, the bottom third of this sidebar column contains a micro-schedule list with each item on its own line, monospaced and aligned like a terminal: “04/22 — ‘Handheld Chaos: Controlled Camera Shake’”, “05/03 — ‘No-Budget Lighting for Night Exteriors’”, “05/17 — ‘Sound You Don’t Have to Fix in Post’”.

As users scroll down, the hero content moves but the header stays fixed. The next major section starts right below the hero and spans the full width. It is subdivided into a broken three-column grid (two columns top row, three misaligned columns bottom row) showcasing the course categories. The background flips to white again. At the top left of the section, in small uppercase monospace text, is the label “COURSE MODULES”. Next to it on the right, there’s a vertical neon yellow rectangle, like a highlight bar, running from the label to halfway down the section, emphasizing the left edge. In the top row, there are two oversized rectangular tiles side by side. The left tile is slightly wider than the right and has a black background with white text. The heading in all caps: “DIRECTING & STORY”. Below it, a bullet-less list in monospaced font with line breaks: “Blocking actors in tiny spaces”, “Directing non-actors”, “Visual storytelling with zero set design”. On the right side of this tile, there is a flat, high-contrast static image: a black-and-white stylized illustration of a director’s chair, seen straight-on, with jagged halftone textures and a neon yellow rectangle behind it. The illustration is flat vector art, sharp edges, no gradients. The right tile has an off-white background and a 1px black outline. Its heading: “CAMERA & LENSES”. Inside, there is a flat static image of a camera viewed in stark front view: a rectangular body with a exaggeratedly large lens circle, the lens filled partly with neon yellow. Around the image are three words in small uppercase text placed at odd angles like annotations: “FOCAL LENGTH”, “MOVEMENT”, “PRACTICALITY”. Beneath the illustration is a paragraph: “Learn to choose the right lens, move the camera with purpose, and build coverage fast when the clock is killing you.”

Below, in the second row, there are three smaller square tiles, each misaligned vertically so they feel like cut-and-paste blocks. The left tile: a neon yellow background, black text “SOUND & MUSIC” and an illustration of a shotgun microphone drawn as a simple horizontal black rectangle with a gridded texture, angled slightly; below: “Capture clean audio with basic gear & mix in a cramped bedroom.” The middle tile: white background with border, heading “EDIT & POST”, body text: “Cut fast, keep rhythm, and grade logic instead of trend.” Its image: an abstract flat design of a timeline bar — a long horizontal black bar with randomly spaced vertical neon yellow rectangles like clips. The right tile: black background, neon yellow heading “PRODUCTION REALITIES”, white text: “Budgets, permits, contracts, ego, and the art of actually finishing.” No image, just text, so it feels more severe and blunt.

Scrolling further, the next section is a brutalist pricing + plan comparison area on a light gray background with a subtle grid overlay: thin, faint gray vertical lines run down the page every fixed distance, defining columns visually. At the top center, in all caps condensed big heading: “SUBSCRIPTION PLANS”. Under it, smaller subtitle in monospace: “ONE PRICE IF YOU’RE SERIOUS. ANOTHER IF YOU’RE OBSESSED.” The layout uses three columns: two plan cards on the left and center, and an empty or nearly empty column on the right used for marginalia. The left card is labeled “MONTHLY” in a small uppercase block at the top edge. Below, huge bold price: “$29” with smaller “/month” next to it. Under the price is a list of features in monospace, each preceded by square brackets: “[x] All core courses”, “[x] Live labs (watch live or replay)”, “[x] Downloadable shot diagrams”, “[x] Members-only feedback threads”. The center card is slightly taller and has a neon yellow background with black text. It is labeled at the top: “ANNUAL” with a small “MOST POPULAR” tag in a tiny outlined rectangle next to it. The price reads: “$240” with “/year” smaller, and below in monospace: “You save 31% and get a free 30-min session with a mentor DP.” Its feature list includes everything from the monthly plus: “[x] Priority critique on your scenes”, “[x] Access to raw footage to re-edit”, “[x] Private Discord channel.” Under the features in the annual card, a blunt line of text: “If you’re not shooting at least one film this year, don’t choose this.” Each card has a flat “START TRIAL” button at the bottom, rectangular with no rounding; on the monthly card it’s outlined, on the annual card it’s filled black with neon yellow text. On the rightmost third, occupying the open column, there’s a vertical block of monospaced copy functioning as small-print philosophy, aligned to the top: “No hidden tiers. Cancel online in 2 clicks. We don’t do fake countdown timers.” Under it, a small box with a dotted border containing text: “Student / low-income scholarships available. Email: support@filmcraftbrutalist.com”.

Continue downward into a section that features a data-driven visualization of member progress and satisfaction. This section sits on pure white background with a stark, almost scientific feel. On the left quarter of the width, a heading: “IS THIS ACTUALLY WORKING FOR PEOPLE?” followed by a smaller line: “Membership stats from the last 6 months”. The right three-quarters contain a flat, brutalist bar chart. The chart is housed in a black rectangular frame with axes drawn as thick black lines. The y-axis runs along the left edge with numeric labels in monospace from “0%” at the bottom to “100%” at the top, in 20% increments. The x-axis has four category labels: “COMPLETE AT LEAST 1 COURSE”, “SHOOT A NEW SHORT FILM”, “SUBMIT TO A FESTIVAL”, “RECOMMEND TO A FRIEND”. Each is written in stacked, vertically oriented uppercase text, rotated 90 degrees, looking cramped and intense. Bars are simple rectangles, each with a different fill pattern: the first bar is filled with solid neon yellow and reaches 82% on the y-scale; the second bar is black with thin white horizontal stripes and reaches 57%; the third bar is white with a black outline and diagonal stripes reaching 24%; the fourth bar is neon yellow with sparse black vertical stripes reaching 91%. Above each bar is a small text label inside a tiny rectangle: “82%”, “57%”, “24%”, “91%”. To the right of the chart, in the same data block, there’s a small legend placed in a stacked manner with each pattern label: “Neon Solid — Completed course”, “Striped Black — Shot a film”, “Striped White — Submitted”, “Vertical Neon — Recommend”. Below the chart, in small, almost footnote-like text, is a line: “Sample size: 500 active members · Survey period: OCT — MAR”. The chart looks like it was printed on paper then scanned — the lines are clean but the aesthetic is harsh and analog.

Scrolling again, the next section showcases real tutorial modules with preview thumbnails in a four-column grid that intentionally misaligns on smaller screens but is described as desktop here. The section has a light gray background. At the top left, small heading: “WATCH A LESSON TODAY”. Under it, a thin horizontal black .75px line spans half the width. Below, four cards in a row, each occupying roughly a quarter of the width. Each card is a vertical rectangle, with a thumbnail image on top and text below. All thumbnails are highly stylized, high-contrast frames that look like stills from the video lessons. The first card: title “SHOT BREAKDOWN: ALLEYWAY CHASE”, with an image showing a narrow alley at night, wet pavement reflecting a harsh overhead streetlight; a person is mid-run, motion blur visible, camera angle low, looking up slightly; the colors: almost monochrome but with neon yellow splashes where a sign glows and on a painted door; heavy shadows, deep blacks. Below, text: “Inside the blocking, lens choices, and sound design of a 90-second chase made with one light and one actor.” A small label in a tiny black box with white monospace text says “27 MIN”. The second card: title “DIY LIGHTING: BATHROOM MIRROR”, image of a cramped bathroom, camera looking into a mirror reflecting a person’s face half-lit by a naked bulb clamped to the mirror; background is cluttered with toiletries; the neon yellow accent shows up as a sticky note on the mirror. The style is gritty, almost documentary-like. Text: “Turn a boring bathroom into a noir-practical lighting test with a clamp light and aluminum foil.” Label: “18 MIN”. The third card: “SOUND IN CHAOS: STREET INTERVIEWS”. Thumbnail: a street scene with a subject wearing over-ear headphones and holding a shotgun mic on a boom pole, busy city blur in the background; the frame is high contrast with neon yellow outlines drawn around the boom mic and the recorder with a graphic overlay. Text: “How to capture intelligible dialogue in noisy outdoor environments without expensive gear.” Label: “35 MIN”. The fourth card: “EDITING UNDER PRESSURE: 24-HOUR CUT”. Thumbnail: a close-up of an editing timeline on a computer screen captured from an oblique angle, with neon yellow bars highlighting selected clips; room is dark except for screen glow. Text: “Compress days into minutes, keep pace, and stay sane in a marathon edit session.” Label: “42 MIN”. Hover imagined behavior: thumbnails invert their colors or overlay a neon yellow “PLAY” label in big uppercase text.

Between sections, there are occasional full-width brutalist separators: a single thick black horizontal line stretched across, or a neon yellow strip with black text that reads “SCROLL ↓” in a tall, condensed font, reinforcing vertical movement.

Further down, a testimonial / community section is arranged as a two-column layout with intentional asymmetry. Left two-thirds: a large block quote in oversized condensed all-caps: “THIS FELT MORE LIKE BEING ON SET THAN BEING IN SCHOOL.” The quote text is black on white and is enclosed by a thin black rectangle that doesn’t fully close at the bottom right corner, leaving a deliberate gap. Under the quote, in small monospace: “— KIRA J., DOCUMENTARY DIRECTOR”. On the right third, there’s a vertical stack of three smaller testimonials, each in its own box with a thin black border and a subtle background shift (one white, one light gray, one with diagonal pale gray stripes). Each includes a single sentence, e.g., “The live labs are chaotic in a good way.”, “I finished my short because the weekly check-ins wouldn’t let me drop it.”, “I stopped obsessing over camera gear and started actually making things.”, followed by a smaller name line.

Near the bottom, a minimal “About” style block is presented as a multi-column text wall that mimics a printed brochure. Three columns of dense monospace text, justified left. Column one is titled “WHAT THIS IS”, describing: “FilmCraft Brutalist is a subscription-based tutorial platform for filmmakers who prefer concrete over glossy inspiration, with all content delivered in raw, practical modules recorded on real sets and real locations, not studios.” Column two is “WHO IT’S FOR”, listing types: “Directors, cinematographers, and editors working with micro-budgets; film students who don’t want another lecture; experienced crew shifting roles.” Column three is “WHAT IT ISN’T”, with blunt statements: “Not a gear review channel. Not a vibe class. Not a ‘how I got famous’ autobiography.” All headings are uppercase, slightly larger than the body text.

The footer is a stark, bold band across the bottom. Background black, text in neon yellow and white. It is divided into a four-column layout. The first column shows the logo again, but inverted: neon yellow text “FILMCRAFT BRUTALIST” and underneath, a final line in tiny uppercase: “BUILT SOMEWHERE BETWEEN SET AND POST.” The second column lists quick links in monospace: “FAQ”, “Support”, “Scholarships”, “Press Kit”. The third column contains legal text: “Terms”, “Privacy”, “Cookie Settings”. The fourth column has a simple newsletter signup: a single-line text input box with black border on a neon yellow background for email, with placeholder text “enter email for brutal film notes” in small, lowercase monospace; to the right, a black rectangle button with neon yellow text: “SUBMIT”. Below all columns is a sharply aligned bottom-most line in very small text: “© 2025 FilmCraft Brutalist. No AI-generated scripts were harmed in the making of these tutorials.” Above the footer, a tiny “BACK TO TOP ↑” text link in uppercase sits aligned to the right, behaving as a simple anchor link.

Throughout the page, any subtle motion is restrained but noticeable: the hero video loops continuously and slightly jitters at the loop point, evoking analog tape; when you hover over buttons, they instantly invert colors with no animation easing, reflecting the brutalist style; the data bar chart subtly pulses its neon bars up by 2px on hover; some section labels like “ONLINE FILM PRODUCTION LAB” have a very slow, barely perceptible flicker as if on an old CRT, simulated by subtle opacity shifts. No soft shadows or gradients are used; the entire site relies on solid blocks, sharp lines, and harsh typographic hierarchy, creating a stark, uncompromising brutalist film tutorial homepage that feels like a cross between a set call sheet and a punk zine.

### Dataset
| Metric                         | Percentage |
|-------------------------------|-----------:|
| Complete at least 1 course    |        82 |
| Shoot a new short film        |        57 |
| Submit to a festival          |        24 |
| Recommend to a friend         |        91 |

instruction: Planning the generation details for the following website:
input:
Design a minimal, airy, three-column dashboard homepage for a fictional startup incubator called “Summit Foundry”, with a visual theme that subtly references mountain climbing and elevation without feeling like an outdoor brand site. The page should have a soft, almost brutalist minimalism: lots of white and off-white, precise grid alignment with occasional intentional misalignment, thin lines, and sparse color highlights in a muted palette of charcoal #1E1E1E, soft stone #F5F5F7, pale glacier blue #E4F1FF, and a single accent of warm orange #FF7A3C. The entire page sits within a centered max-width container, 1440px wide, with 64px left and right padding and 48px top and bottom padding; outside the container, the background is pure white #FFFFFF. The primary font is a modern grotesk sans-serif (like “Inter” or “Suisse Intl”) with slightly tighter letter spacing; headings are medium-weight, body text regular. The whole page is divided into a three-column CSS-grid-like layout with columns of equal width separated by 24px gutters. The grid is visible in the layout but not as explicit lines, except for subtle dividing rules between certain modules.

At the very top is a slim horizontal strip spanning the full width of the container: on the left, a small logo lockup that reads “Summit Foundry” with “Summit” in bold uppercase and “Foundry” in light uppercase, both in charcoal. Left of the text is a very minimal icon: a thin, outline-only mountain peak made of three short diagonal lines forming a stylized triangular peak, in charcoal, with a tiny orange dot hovering slightly above the peak to symbolize a summit marker. On the right of this strip, aligned to the far edge, are three navigation links in small uppercase, 12px font: “PORTFOLIO”, “PROGRAM”, “INSIGHTS”, spaced evenly with 32px gaps, in medium charcoal at 60% opacity, turning full charcoal on hover (describe as a state, not visible). A minimalist icon-only user avatar circle, 28px diameter, with a very faint gray outline, sits to the right of the nav links; inside is a simple gray silhouette of a head and shoulders in ultra-minimal outline.

Below this header, occupying the top left and center columns, is a hero-like dashboard summary module, framed by a soft stone (#F5F5F7) panel with 24px corner radius and 32px internal padding. Its background is almost flat but with a barely perceptible vertical gradient from white at the top to stone at the bottom. At the top-left of this panel is a small label in 11px uppercase letters, tracking widened, reading “TODAY’S ALTITUDE” in light charcoal. Below, a large main headline in 32px, medium weight, left-aligned reads: “Welcome back, Alex.” Below that, in a 14px lighter weight, is an explanatory subheadline: “Here’s how your cohort is climbing this week.” On the right side of this same panel, aligned to the top-right, there is a subtle sparkline-style mini-chart: a thin, hand-drawn-feeling line in glacier blue plotting a gentle upward trend across seven points, each point indicated by a tiny filled circle; the last circle is orange and slightly larger. Under this sparkline is a small caption in 11px text reading “Portfolio traction, last 7 days”. Beneath the subheadline, across the full width of the panel, are three horizontally aligned metric cards (each effectively one grid cell wide within the two-column hero, but still inside the panel). Each metric card is a small, pill-shaped rectangle with a white background, soft drop shadow (very faint, 0 8px 20px rgba(0,0,0,0.03)), and 16px padding. The first displays the label “Active startups” in 11px uppercase, and below it a large number “24” in 24px bold, with a tiny green upward arrow and “+3 vs last cycle” in 11px to the right of the number. The second reads “Avg. runway” with the number “18.4 mo” and a neutral gray dot and “Stable” text. The third reads “Mentor sessions” with “42” and a small orange arrow and “+12 this week”. The metric labels are light charcoal, numbers are full charcoal, and accent arrows/dots in green #18A572 or orange #FF7A3C. At the bottom-right corner inside this hero panel is a small, almost ghosted button with a subtle border: text “View full analytics” in 12px, with a right-pointing thin arrow icon.

On the far right of the hero row, occupying only the third column and spanning the same height, is a vertically oriented “Next Milestone” card. It has a white background with a subtle border in #E0E0E5 and 20px padding. At its top, aligned left, the label “NEXT MILESTONE” appears in 10px uppercase. Below is a slightly larger title “Demo Day: Batch 07” in 16px, medium weight. To the right of the title is a tiny rounded badge with orange background and white text “21 days”. Beneath, a minimal vertical timeline with three steps: each step is represented by a small circle (first two filled glacier blue, the third outlined), connected by a faint vertical line. Next to each, left-aligned text: “Pitch polish sprints”, “Investor rehearsal”, “Final deck freeze”. The first two step labels are medium charcoal; the last is lighter gray indicating upcoming. The bottom of this card includes a thin, horizontal progress bar: a 2px-high line with 60% of its length filled with a gradient from glacier blue to orange, and below it tiny text “63% prep completed”.

Immediately below this top row, the layout shifts into a more clearly defined three-column dashboard grid with subtle asymmetries. The leftmost column contains a tall navigation and filter stack; the center column holds primary data content (including a key data visualization); and the right column functions as an activity and media sidebar featuring a subtle looping video.

In the left column, below the hero panel, a vertically stacked in-card navigation occupies most of the column’s height. This card has a white background with a thin border and a title at the top: “Workspace” in 14px medium weight. Below the title, a vertical list of navigation items, each a row of 32px height: “Overview”, “Startups”, “Mentors”, “Capital”, “Resources”, “Settings”. “Overview” is currently active, indicated by a slim 3px-wide vertical orange bar on its left edge and a pale glacier blue pill background spanning the row; the text for the active item is charcoal and bold, whereas the inactive items are medium gray and regular. Each row has a tiny minimal icon to the left: for example, “Startups” uses a minimal outline of three small squares forming a cluster; “Mentors” uses two overlapping outline circles; “Capital” shows a simple line chart icon; “Resources” a folder outline; “Settings” a tiny gear. All icons are thin-stroke, charcoal at reduced opacity. At the bottom of this column’s nav card, separated by a soft divider line, is a tiny compact filter toggle row with the label “Stage” and three pill toggles: “Pre-seed”, “Seed”, “Series A”. “Seed” is highlighted in orange with white text; others appear as outlined pills.

In the center column, beneath the hero, a larger analytical panel dominates. This panel is titled “Cohort performance” in 18px medium weight, left-aligned, with a small subtitle below in 12px reading “Funding and runway across active startups”. At the top-right corner of this panel are two minimal segmented controls: one text button reading “6 months” and another “12 months”; “6 months” appears active with an orange underline. Below the title is a responsive, interactive-looking chart canvas area with a very clean, minimal style. The chart is a dual-axis combination of a line and bars, plotted on a soft off-white background, with no heavy gridlines—only very faint horizontal guides in #EFEFF3. The x-axis displays six labeled months in 11px: “Jul”, “Aug”, “Sep”, “Oct”, “Nov”, “Dec”. On the left y-axis, there are labels like “0”, “5”, “10”, “15”, “20” in 11px, indicating “Startups with > 12 mo runway”, while the right y-axis remains unlabeled but implied to represent funding amounts. The data visualization should show: a glacier blue line with soft round joints representing “Avg runway (months)” and translucent orange bars behind it representing “Total raised (M$)”. For each month, the chart clearly coordinates: for example, in July the bar rises to “3.2” (million) and the blue line sits at “14.1”; in August bar at “4.5”, line at “15.0”; in September bar at “6.0”, line at “16.3”; in October bar at “5.4”, line at “17.0”; in November bar at “7.2”, line at “18.2”; in December bar at “8.1”, line at “18.4”. Each bar is a soft rect with slight rounded top corners and subtle drop shadow. The blue line runs smoothly across, slightly above the bars, with circular points at each month. Under the chart, there is a small legend: two tiny 8px color swatches with labels: a blue dot followed by “Avg runway (months)” and an orange bar rectangle followed by “Total raised (M$)”. To keep the layout minimal, all chart labels and tick marks are small and light, allowing the graphic elements to stand out. Beneath the chart, taking the full width of the same center column panel, there is a compact, borderless table with three rows highlighting top performers. Each row is 40px high and separated by a light horizontal line. Columns include: “Startup”, “Stage”, “Runway”, “Raised”, “Focus”. Data might include rows like: “Northwind Labs”, “Seed”, “22 mo”, “$3.8M”, “Dev-tools”; “Alpenglow”, “Pre-seed”, “19 mo”, “$1.4M”, “Climate”; “Granite Loop”, “Seed”, “21 mo”, “$2.3M”, “Fintech”. Startup names are bold; the “Stage” column uses tiny colored circles preceding text (pale green for Pre-seed, glacier blue for Seed). Hover state descriptions can be implied (highlight row background on hover).

Below the main chart panel in the center column, a shorter card displays “Mentor bandwidth” as a simple horizontal bar chart or stacked horizontal pills: a title in 14px “Mentor bandwidth” and smaller text “Sessions scheduled next 7 days”. Below, three rows: “Product”, “Go-to-market”, “Fundraising”. Each row has a bar representing number of sessions: “Product” with a long glacier blue bar labeled “18 sessions”, “Go-to-market” with a shorter bar and “11 sessions”, “Fundraising” with “7 sessions”. The bars are aligned flush left, with subtle rounded corners; numbers appear in 11px at the right end of each bar.

In the right column, aligned under the “Next Milestone” card, the first module is an “Activity” stream with very minimal styling: title “Activity” in 14px medium, then a vertical list of recent events. Each activity line is composed of a tiny dot (color-coded by event type) on the left, a bold entity name, and lighter event description. Example entries: “SummitFlow closed a $1.1M seed round.”, “New mentor added: Lena Ortiz (Growth)”, “Red Ridge submitted updated pitch deck.” Each event has a timestamp in 11px light gray aligned to the right of the row like “2h ago”, “5h ago”, “Yesterday”. The list is confined to 4–5 items with a subtle scroll indicator hint (for instance, a faint gradient at the bottom of the card suggesting more content).

Directly beneath the Activity card, still in the right column, there is a slightly taller media card that incorporates a subtle looping video. The card title, aligned left at the top, reads “Founder briefing” in 14px medium. Beneath the title and spanning almost the full card width is a 16:9 video thumbnail with rounded corners and a very thin border. The video content is an ultra-minimal, abstract, looped animation meant to play automatically without audio: viewed from a straight-on flat perspective, against a white background, two or three flat, monochrome shapes representing abstract “mountain ranges” (angled trapezoids and triangles in pale gray and glacier blue) slowly shift and slide up and down, subtly rearranging. A thin orange line resembling a route or path traces over these shapes, gently animating from left to right like a path being drawn, then fading and starting again. The animation is soft and slow, evoking calm progress and elevation, with very subtle shadow gradients to imply depth. Within the thumbnail, at the bottom-left overlay, small white text reads “Weekly ascent briefing · 03:12”. In the center of the video thumbnail, a minimal circular play button is overlaid: a hollow white circle with an outlined triangle, no heavy shadow. Around the video card, the rest of the module has a short description paragraph in 12px: “This 3-minute overview walks your team through priorities for the coming sprint.” and beneath that a tiny link-like text “Open in Insights” with a right-pointing arrow.

Across all columns, the alignment respects an invisible baseline grid: headings have a consistent top margin (24px from preceding elements), paragraphs 8–12px spacing, and cards separated vertically by 24px. Dividing lines are always a soft gray (#E4E4EB), 1px thick, and never full-bleed—they have 16px inset from card edges. Corners are consistent (16–24px radius) and shadows are minimal to keep the aesthetic crisp. Buttons are rare; instead, key actions are subtle text links or understated pill buttons with thin outlines in charcoal, filling with orange on hover (only described, not visible). There are no heavy gradients, no photographs of people, and no complex textures; the only imagery is the abstract, geometric, mountain-like animation and very simplified icons. On hover over datapoints (implied, not visually visible), tooltips appear with exact values (e.g., “Oct · Avg runway: 17.0 mo · Total raised: $5.4M”). The entire page feels like a calm, analytical command center—quiet, bright, with a disciplined grid, letting whitespace and gentle color distinctions define the hierarchy.

### Dataset
```csv
Month,Avg_Runway_Months,Total_Raised_Millions
Jul,14.1,3.2
Aug,15.0,4.5
Sep,16.3,6.0
Oct,17.0,5.4
Nov,18.2,7.2
Dec,18.4,8.1
```

instruction: Planning the generation details for the following website:
input:
Design a minimal, multi-column dashboard-style homepage for a “Comic Panel Review Club” subscription service, where users track and discover weekly comic book review digests. The overall aesthetic is ultra-clean and spacious, with a light background, subtle shadows, and a restrained palette of off-white, charcoal, and a single accent color: a muted electric blue. The page should feel like a control center for curated comic reviews, using a grid layout aligned to a 12-column system with asymmetric variations: some cards span 4 columns, others 6 or 8, with staggered heights to create visual interest while remaining orderly. The background color for the entire page is a very light neutral, almost white but slightly warm: hex #F7F7F8. All main text uses a modern sans-serif font, like “Inter” or a similar geometric sans-serif, with weights from 300 to 700; headings are set in a slightly heavier weight, body text in regular weight, and labels and tags in all caps with letter-spacing. The entire layout is centered with generous side padding (about 80px on desktop), and plenty of white space between blocks (24–40px). At the very top, a fixed-height header bar spans the full width with a white (#FFFFFF) background and a 1px bottom border line in a very light gray (#E1E1E4). Inside the header, content is horizontally aligned and vertically centered. On the far left is a simple text logo: “Comic Panel Review Club” in a medium weight, dark charcoal (#22232A), with “Comic Panel” on the first line and “Review Club” on the second line in slightly smaller size; both lines are left-aligned and sit close together, giving a compact stacked logo effect. To the right of the logo, centered in the remaining space, is a very minimal navigation text row with three links: “Dashboard”, “Library”, and “Settings”, each in a lighter charcoal (#555667) and small caps; “Dashboard” is bold and has a thin electric blue underline indicating it is the current page. On the far right of the header, there is a circular profile avatar placeholder (a pale gray circle with stylized initials “JD” in dark gray) and next to it, a small chevron-down icon to suggest an account dropdown. Below the header, the main dashboard area begins with a hero strip that spans the full width of the content area but is only about 220px tall, giving a low-profile, dashboard-like feel. This hero section has a white card background with slightly rounded corners (8px radius) and a soft, diffuse drop shadow (blurred, very subtle). Inside, the content is split into two zones: on the left (about 7 columns wide), text content; on the right (about 5 columns), a looping, muted video thumbnail that feels like a living preview of the subscription content. On the left, at the top, a small label in all caps and electric blue reads “WEEKLY DIGEST OVERVIEW”; below it, the main heading: “Your Comic Review Briefing” in dark charcoal, large but not overwhelming. Under the heading, a one-line description in muted gray: “Explore the latest curated reviews from across superhero, indie, and manga universes — all in one clean dashboard.” Under this text, there is a compact horizontal row of two minimalist buttons. The primary button is filled electric blue with white text: “Start this week’s tour”, with slightly rounded corners and a subtle drop shadow; the secondary button is text-only, dark gray with a faint bottom border that appears only on hover. On the right of this hero card is a 16:9 video area with a thin outline in light gray; it contains a short looping video (muted) of stylized comic panels sliding horizontally across a flat, minimal background. The video animation shows flat-design comic panels in a grid, each with bold blocks of color (reds, blues, yellows) and speech bubble shapes, gently sliding from right to left. The motion is subtle and slow, with a micro parallax effect where foreground panels move slightly faster than background ones. Every 5 seconds, the loop resets seamlessly. A translucent white play icon sits centered over the video, indicating it can be expanded, but on this static dashboard view, it remains a looping ambient motion background.

Below the hero, the content is arranged in a three-column grid (on a wide desktop) that adapts gently but preserves minimalism: a main left column (about 7 columns wide) and a right sidebar (about 5 columns). The columns are visually separated by whitespace rather than heavy lines. In the main left column, at the top, is a “This Week’s Highlights” section. A slim label in all caps, light gray text reads “THIS WEEK’S HIGHLIGHTS”. Just under it, a slightly larger heading in dark charcoal: “Featured Review Collections”. Beneath this heading are three horizontally scrollable cards arranged side-by-side on desktop (each card about 4 columns wide) with a gentle snap scroll. Each card is a white rectangle with 8px rounded corners, a tiny drop shadow, and a minimal border in #ECECF0. The left card is titled “Issue #142: Multiverse Mayhem”. On the top half of this card is a static flat illustration: a stylized comic page seen from a slightly angled overhead perspective. The page is divided into four rectangular panels with thick black borders; each panel contains abstract silhouettes of caped figures, speech bubbles with zig-zag tails, and blocks of primary colors. Background is light beige, panels alternate between navy, crimson, and mustard yellow; the style is clean vector art with no gradients. The bottom half of the card contains a short description in small text: “Deep-dive into a tangled multiverse arc, with panel-by-panel breakdowns and pacing notes.” Below that, a thin pill-shaped tag in very light blue with electric blue text reads “Superhero • 26 reviews”. A small, right-aligned, ghost-style button reads “Open collection”. The second card is titled “Indie Spotlight: Quiet Stories” and features a static illustration of muted pastel panels, with lots of negative space and simple, slice-of-life scenes (a coffee cup, a window with rain streaks); below it, a tag reads “Indie • 14 reviews”. The third card is titled “Manga Marathon: Shonen Surge” and its illustration features vertical manga-style panels in grayscale with a single red accent; tag reads “Manga • 18 reviews”.

Below the highlights section on the left, there is an “Activity & Metrics” area, which includes one main data visualization card and a compact stacked list below it. The visualization card is titled “Reading & Review Time (Last 7 Days)” with a small, subtle info icon to the right. Inside the card, there is a minimalist line chart with a white background, faint grid lines, and horizontal x-axis representing days “Mon”, “Tue”, “Wed”, “Thu”, “Fri”, “Sat”, “Sun” in light gray text. The y-axis represents “Minutes” from 0 to 60. The line itself is electric blue with rounded joint points at each day. Each data point has a very small, solid blue circle; hovering (if interactive) shows exact values. Below the line, a faint gradient fill (from translucent blue near the line to fully transparent at the bottom) softly suggests volume. At the top-left of the chart space, a small dropdown with text “Minutes read” indicates metric type. The dataset represented is: Mon 35, Tue 42, Wed 28, Thu 50, Fri 46, Sat 30, Sun 55.
