At the top left of this section, in small uppercase serif, is the label “TONIGHT’S LINEUP”, and directly below, spanning two columns, is a secondary headline in serif reading “Eight founders. Eight radically different journeys.” The section background remains off-white, but each founder feature is set in a card that resembles a mini magazine cover. Each card has a white background, a thin faint border in very light gray, and a slight drop shadow. The cards are laid out in a 3-column grid with uneven heights to create an asymmetrical mosaic. In each card’s top half there is a static, editorial-style portrait image of the founder: realistic but stylized photography with soft natural light, shallow depth of field, and a subtle color grade that leans neutral but with slightly emphasized amber highlights. Each founder is shot in a candid pose: one looking off-camera in a coworking space with blurred laptops in the background, one laughing in a brick-walled café, one standing on a rooftop with the city skyline softly out of focus, another leaning on a whiteboard covered in sticky notes. Clothing is modern and understated; colors are mostly neutral (black, white, gray, navy) so as not to clash with the site palette. Under each image, there’s a column of text: the founder’s name in bold sans-serif, the startup vertical in small uppercase (e.g. “CLIMATE FINTECH”, “FEMTECH”, “EDTECH”), and a one-sentence teaser in italic serif like: “A former aerospace engineer building financial tools for farmers facing climate uncertainty.” On hover, each card image shifts slightly upward by a few pixels and the background of the card warms slightly, while the teaser text underlines in amber, giving subtle motion. In the gap between the first and second rows of founder cards, slightly offset to the right, there's a narrow vertical strip like a pull-quote column in a magazine. It has a vertical amber line on the left and inside it a short quote in serif: “Pitch nights should feel like stories, not spreadsheets.” Below this quote is a small credit line in uppercase sans-serif: “– CURATOR-IN-CHIEF”. Continuing down, the next section spans nearly full width but with a strong left-right split, titled “How Pitchfolio Live Works”. On the left half, text is set in stacked vertical blocks, laid out like an editorial explainer. The section header in serif reads “How Pitchfolio Live Works” in large size, with a subheading in smaller sans-serif: “Built to showcase humans behind the decks and decks behind the humans.” Below, there are three numbered steps, “01”, “02”, and “03”, each number large and in a semi-transparent serif, aligned vertically. To the right of each number is the step title and description: “01 · Curated Founders” with copy describing a small, handpicked cohort; “02 · Live, Unsanitized Q&A” describing candid audience questions; “03 · Afterlight Sessions” explaining post-event one-on-ones. Each block has plenty of spacing, with thin horizontal rules between them, like magazine article separators. On the right half of this section is a collage-like composition that subtly animates. It consists of overlapping polaroid-style frames, each containing a monochrome photograph tinted in deep midnight blue: one shows a close-up of hands gesturing mid-pitch with a laptop in the foreground; another shows a judge panel seated at a long table, lit from the front, faces partially visible; another shows audience members leaning forward, taking notes. These polaroid frames are slightly rotated and layered, with drop shadows to create depth. Every 10 seconds, one of the frames slowly fades out and is replaced with another image of the same tinted style, maintaining the collage layout but gently shifting which moments are highlighted. The transitions are slow crossfades, avoiding any jarring motion. Slight grain and vignettes on the photos enhance the editorial aesthetic. A faint, animated line—like a hand-drawn marker stroke—occasionally draws itself between two edges of frames, suggesting connection between moments. Below this explainer section, there is a slender horizontal bar spanning the width, with a soft gray background slightly darker than the page, giving the impression of a section divider. Centered inside this bar is a scrolling marquee-like text in tiny uppercase sans-serif: “HEADLINE PITCHES · FIRESIDE INTERVIEWS · LIVE CRITIQUES · LATE-NIGHT NETWORKING · COFFEE AT DAWN”, repeating in a slow left-to-right loop. The text is very subtle, moving slowly, like a quiet ticker you notice only after a few seconds. Continuing down, the next major segment is a two-column asymmetrical layout titled “For Founders & For Investors”, where each side feels like its own magazine column. The section background is now pure white to create contrast, and the columns are separated by a thin vertical amber line running the full height of this section. On the left side, aligned flush left, is the heading “For Founders” in serif, with supporting text underneath in sans-serif listing benefits like “Crafted story arcs, live feedback, investor connections.” Below that, a stacked list with small check icons (minimal, circular outlines) marks three bullet-style blocks, each looking like a small pull-out text box with a soft blush background. Each box includes a short subheading such as “A stage that feels safe.” and a concise description. On the right side, aligned flush right, is “For Investors” with analogous content: focus on discovery, context beyond metrics, and curated matches. The right column’s bullet-style boxes use a very pale cool gray background instead of blush. Between and slightly overlapping into both columns near the lower middle is a small editorial feature box that cuts across the vertical divider. It has a subtle shadow and contains a black-and-white photograph of a notebook with handwritten notes and doodles (“Market size?”, “Founder-market fit?”) and a pen lying diagonally across it, shot from above with soft natural light. To the right of this image inside the box is a compact text block: “We send a one-page editorial memo ahead of each event. No data rooms, just narratives.” Beneath this dual-audience section is a full-width band called “The Venue” that features a large panoramic background image at moderate height (about 400–450px tall). This image is static but appears as if printed edge-to-edge, with no borders: a photorealistic wide shot of an industrial-chic venue with exposed brick, hanging Edison bulbs, and long rows of chairs facing a minimalist stage with a large screen and a simple lectern. The perspective is slightly elevated, as if shot from the balcony or back row, showing a sea of empty chairs and the quiet anticipation of an event not yet begun. The color grading is warm and somewhat desaturated to feel cinematic; the ambient light from the hanging bulbs casts soft, golden reflections on the polished concrete floor. Overlaid on the left side of this image is a semi-transparent box with a gradient from midnight blue to near-transparent, containing white text: “Next Edition: The Foundry Loft, San Francisco · April 24, 2025 · Doors 6:00 PM”. Below that, smaller copy says, “A converted foundry bathed in warm light and late-night momentum.” On the right of this overlay box, three small icon+label clusters sit along the bottom: a tiny line-art coffee cup icon next to “Third-wave espresso bar”; a small outline of headphones next to “Quiet corners for side conversations”; and a minimalist Wi-Fi symbol next to “No dead zones, promise.” As the user scrolls past this venue image, a subtle parallax effect is described: the background photo scrolls more slowly than the overlay text, creating a gentle sense of depth. After the venue section, there’s a narrower band called “Mentors & Curators”, laid out in a multi-column grid of monochrome portraits. The grid has four columns on desktop and two on tablet, each cell containing a circular portrait in grayscale with a slight sepia tone. Below each portrait, in small uppercase sans-serif, is the name and title, e.g. “JIA LEE · Partner, Northline Ventures” and “MARCOS RAMIREZ · Founder-in-Residence, Atlas Mobility”. Above the grid, a centered serif subheading reads “Mentors & Curators”, with a tiny subtitle: “The people behind what makes it onto the stage.” On hover, each portrait shifts from monochrome to a faint color wash, and the name’s letterspacing widens slightly, echoing magazine hover states. Below this, occupying a more intimate space that feels like the back page of a magazine, is the “Afterlight Journal” section. The layout switches to a two-column design where the left column shows a stylized illustration and the right column shows text. The illustration is a flat but textured drawing in an editorial style: a person sitting on a bench outside at night, viewed slightly from behind, with a city skyline of simplified geometric buildings in the distance. The sky is a deep midnight blue gradient sprinkled with tiny amber and white dots suggesting city lights and stars, and the person is lit softly from a smartphone screen in their hands, the glow tinting their face and hands in a warm amber hue. The bench and surroundings are minimal; the focus is on the moody, reflective atmosphere. The illustration uses clean vector shapes with subtle noise textures and soft shadows to feel both modern and tactile. To the right, the text column titled “Afterlight Journal” includes a paragraph explaining that after each event, a curated write-up is sent to attendees, focusing on founder stories, key quotes, and behind-the-scenes moments. Beneath is a small, slender email capture form with a single line input labeled “Enter your email for the next Journal” and a right-aligned arrow button with text “Subscribe”. The input has a thin border and a gentle focus state where the border glows in amber. At the bottom of the page, the footer feels like a masthead. It spans the full width, with a background of deep midnight blue and white text organized into three columns. The left column features the logo again (“Pitchfolio Live”) in white serif, plus a tiny tagline under it in small caps: “An editorial stage for early-stage stories.” The center column lists site links: “About”, “Partners”, “Press”, “Careers”, and “Privacy”, stacked vertically in sans-serif with generous line height. The right column contains social icons (minimal line icons for “X”, “LinkedIn”, and “Instagram”) aligned horizontally, and below them a small line of text: “© 2025 Pitchfolio Inc. All rights reserved.” At the very bottom, perfectly centered across the full footer, is a thin dividing line in a slightly lighter midnight blue, above which sits a tiny, italic serif text in a muted gray-blue: “Produced like a magazine. Lived like a night you remember.” Throughout the entire page, micro-interactions are subtle and refined: buttons gently scale, shadows deepen on hover, and text links show a thin amber underline that animates from left to right. Scroll transitions between major sections have a faint, almost imperceptible fade and vertical slide, inspired by the turning of pages in a magazine, but without any literal page-turn animation. The overall feeling is that of a carefully art-directed editorial feature that just happens to be interactive: a multi-column grid where images, text, and motion are choreographed to tell the story of a live startup pitch event as if it were the cover story of a quarterly print journal.

instruction: Planning the generation details for the following website:
input:
Design a whimsical, research-focused homepage called "OddCorners Lab" that feels like a playful mashup between a street photography journal and a digital art tutorial subscription site. The aesthetic should be bright, slightly chaotic but intelligently structured, with a hierarchical, overlapping layout and intentional asymmetries. The entire page sits on a soft off-white background (#FDF8F1) with faint, hand-drawn doodles of camera outlines, lightbulbs, arrows, and question marks scattered sparsely like a subtle wallpaper. At the very top, a thin, sticky navigation bar spans edge to edge, with a semi-transparent blurred glass effect over whatever scrolls beneath. The navbar background is a translucent white with a slight gradient from left to right, and a very soft shadow drop. On the left, the logo reads "OddCorners Lab" in a bold, slightly rounded sans-serif typeface reminiscent of Futura, with "OddCorners" in dark charcoal and "Lab" in a bright tomato red pill-shaped badge behind it. Immediately under the logo, overlapping into the navbar slightly, is a tiny, circular stamp-style icon of a cartoon camera with legs running, drawn in a flat, vector style in dark navy and pastel coral. To the right of the logo, spaced with generous gaps, are navigation links in small uppercase letters: "HOME", "FIELD NOTES", "METHODS", "LAB MEMBERS", "JOIN THE LAB". Hovering over each link underlines it with a hand-drawn squiggle line that animates in from left to right. On the far right, there is a rounded button labeled "SIGN IN" in dark charcoal outline with white fill, and next to it a slightly larger, filled button labeled "GET ACCESS" in tomato red with white text; both buttons have a subtle hover lift and shadow.

Just below the navbar, taking up about 70% of the viewport height, is the hero section with a creative overlapping composition. On the left two-thirds of the hero, there is a semi-opaque rectangular container with softly rounded corners, slightly tilted a few degrees counterclockwise, filled with a pale blush gradient. This container contains the main headline text aligned left: in large, bold, playful serif font, the title reads: "Research, but make it streetwise." Below it, in a medium-weight sans-serif, smaller but still prominent, is the subheading: "OddCorners Lab is a playful research hub where we study how people see cities, make art, and tell visual stories—through experiments you can actually try." The text is dark charcoal with a few key words in tomato red: "playful", "cities", "make art", "experiments". Beneath this, two horizontally aligned call-to-action buttons overlap the bottom of the text container. The first is a large, pill-shaped button reading "Explore Current Experiments" in white text on a gradient from tomato red to deep coral, with a soft glow around the edges. The second, slightly offset and overlapping the first, is a ghost-style pill with a thin navy border and the text "Watch 2-Minute Lab Tour" in navy; to its left, slightly overlapping the button, is a tiny animated play icon—a white triangle inside a small tomato red circle that gently pulses every three seconds, expanding and fading to suggest interactivity.

On the right one-third of the hero section is a tall, vertical card that looks like a stack of overlapping Polaroids and sticky notes pinned to an invisible board. The base is a slightly tilted off-white card with a thin, dark charcoal border and a soft shadow. On top of this base, there is a looping video occupying the central area of the card, framed like a Polaroid with a thick white border and a small caption area at the bottom. The video plays silently and loops seamlessly: it shows a first-person POV walking through a city street at dusk, with slightly desaturated, documentary-style colors. As the camera walks, subtle animated overlays appear: floating, hand-drawn outlines around people holding cameras, arrows pointing to interesting details like graffiti, reflections in puddles, and signage, along with tiny hand-lettered labels like "Observation #12", "Unexpected pattern", "Lighting test". The video is shot handheld, with natural slight movement, and the overlays animate in and fade out smoothly. At the bottom of the Polaroid frame, a caption in a handwritten, slightly messy script font reads: "Field Study: How people pause in between." Behind this main Polaroid, slightly offset to the upper right, is a secondary, smaller card that peeks out; it shows a static, stylized illustration of a city corner in flat, vivid colors—deep navy buildings with windows in pastel yellow, a lone orange streetlight casting a soft glow, and a single silhouetted figure taking a photo. A small digital sticky note overlaps the top right of the video card, in a pale yellow sticky-note texture, with the text "Hypothesis: Surprise lives in the edges." written in dark charcoal handwritten font. A tiny doodle of a lightbulb sits next to the text.

Underneath the hero section, there is a subtle transition where the background shifts to a slightly more solid creamy hue, offset with a large, irregular organic blob shape in pale turquoise on the left, partially tucked behind the hero and continuing downward. This introduces the "Current Lab Tracks" section. A small, all-caps label sits at the top left of this section in tomato red: "CURRENT LAB TRACKS". Below it, aligned left, a playful heading reads: "Pick a track and start experimenting." in mid-sized bold serif type. The content is arranged in a 3-column, slightly asymmetrical grid of overlapping cards, each representing a research track blending street photography and creative inquiry. Each card is a rounded rectangle with a slight drop shadow, a subtle paper texture, and a small tab sticking out at the top labeled as a "TRACK" number in tiny caps.

The first card, leftmost and slightly larger, is titled "Track 01 — Street Gaze". At the top of the card, an image spans edge to edge: a rich, high-contrast street photograph in realistic style, showing a person from behind wearing a bright yellow raincoat crossing a zebra-striped crosswalk in a rainy city scene. Reflections in puddles show neon signs in magenta and cyan; the image has strong diagonal lines and motion blur of other pedestrians. Under the image, in dark charcoal text, is a short description: "Weekly guided prompts to document how strangers move, pause, and share space in your city." Below this, three playful bullet-like tags appear as small pills: "Observation drills", "Photo tasks", "Micro-notes". At the bottom of the card, a subtle link in tomato red reads "View syllabus →".

The middle card is slightly overlapped by the left card at its top-left corner, creating depth. It is titled "Track 02 — Color Stories" with a top image that is a stylized digital collage: multiple smartphone screens floating diagonally, each showing a different monochrome city scene dominated by one color (all-blue alleyway, all-red market stall, all-green park bench). The collage is flat yet layered with soft shadows, giving a cut-paper feel. The description reads: "Experiment with color as data—map emotions, routines, and rhythms through curated palettes and street scenes." Tag pills read: "Palette labs", "Photo-walks", "Color diaries". A small, drawn-on piece of tape graphic appears at the top-left corner of the image, as if taping it to the card.

The third card, on the right, is slightly lower than the others to create a stepped, asymmetrical baseline. It’s titled "Track 03 — Quiet Corners". Its top image is a soft, slightly hazy photograph of an empty café window at night: warm interior light, a single empty table, reflections of the street outside, and raindrops on the glass. The style is cinematic and moody. The description reads: "Practice noticing the spaces between activity—document silence, anticipation, and stillness in public spaces." Tags: "Story sketches", "Audio notes", "Night walks". On the top-right corner of this card, a tiny circular badge in pale turquoise reads "New" in small, uppercase white text.

Below the tracks, an overlapping strip extends widthwise: a slightly darker creamy band with a hand-drawn zigzag at its top edge, resembling torn notebook paper. Centered in this band is a playful quote in italic serif font: "We study the city the way kids study clouds—by staring, sketching, and asking ‘what if?’". The band has a faint background pattern of lightly outlined clipboards and lenses.

Scrolling down, the page transitions into a "How the Lab Works" section with a two-column layout and a strong sense of hierarchy. On the left, there is a vertical timeline represented as a loose, hand-drawn line in tomato red that wiggles down the page, with three circular nodes. Each node corresponds to a step, and the circles are filled with a different pastel color (pale yellow, pale turquoise, pale lilac). Next to each node, slightly offset with overlapping index cards, are step explanations. Step 1 is labeled "1. Choose your track" in small uppercase sans-serif above a larger line reading: "Pick a lens: movement, color, or quiet." in bold serif. Below it, two lines of smaller text explain: "Each track contains 4–6 mini-experiments. You can complete them in order, or shuffle them like street scenes." Step 2, further down and slightly indented, is labeled "2. Run tiny experiments" with a description: "Receive weekly prompts in your inbox and inside your dashboard. Photograph, scribble, or record audio in the wild." Step 3, at the bottom, says "3. Share & compare" and explains: "Upload your findings to private critique rooms, get annotated feedback from mentors, and peek at others’ fieldwork." Small icons appear beside each: for Step 1, a minimalist icon of a rotating, multi-faceted prism; for Step 2, an icon of a camera with a small spark; for Step 3, an icon of two overlapping speech bubbles with a checkmark. These icons are all flat, monochrome dark navy line drawings with minimal filled areas.

On the right side of this section, there is an overlapping stack of three pieces: at the back, a faint, large outline of a city map made of curving lines; on top, a medium-sized, vertical tablet mockup with rounded corners showing a mock dashboard UI. The dashboard screen has a clean, card-based layout: at the top, a heading "Today in OddCorners Lab" with a tiny date underneath. Below, three small horizontal cards labeled "Prompt #07 — Watch for echoes", "Upload from yesterday", and "New critique received". Each card has a tiny thumbnail image: a brick wall with repeated posters, a blurred crowd crossing a street, and a close-up of a bus stop bench. The colors of the UI are very minimal: plenty of white, dark charcoal text, and tomato red accent buttons. Overlapping the bottom of the tablet is a smaller square card with a clipped-corner design, containing a simplified photo of people standing in a subway car, each outlined with a different colored halo, plus handwritten annotations like "waiting", "absorbed", "tired" hovering near them. The overall styling of these visuals is semi-realistic with hand-drawn overlays that reinforce the experimental, research-y vibe.

Further down, a section titled "Field Notes from the Lab" introduces a horizontally scrollable carousel of research stories or blog posts presented as long, narrow polaroid-style columns that partially overlap each other. At the left edge of the section, a small label says "FIELD NOTES" in uppercase tomato red, and below it the main heading reads: "Peeks into ongoing experiments." in bold serif. Under the heading is a short description in smaller sans-serif text: "No jargon, no paywalls—just real experiments with cameras, sketchbooks, and curiosity." The carousel displays three cards at a time, with hints of more cards fading off to the right. Each card is a vertical rectangle with a thick white border and subtle grain texture. The top two-thirds of each card is an image, and the bottom third is text. One card shows a wide-angle street shot of a market at golden hour, with colorful umbrellas and blurred figures in motion, titled "Counting pauses at the Saturday market", with a subtitle: "Why the busiest corners reveal the slowest stories." Another card features a high-contrast black-and-white street photograph of a person’s shadow elongated on a cracked sidewalk, titled "Shadows as field notes", with subtitle: "Using absence to map presence." A third card has a muted pastel illustration instead of a photograph: a stylized overhead view of a crossroads with tiny icon people walking in different directions, each path drawn with a different color line, titled "Tracing invisible routes", subtitle: "Draw the patterns of how strangers share space." At the bottom of each card, a tiny line in caps reads something like "EXPERIMENT LOG #19" in a monospaced font. Navigation arrows for the carousel are small, circular buttons that float just outside the carousel on left and right; they are transparent with dark navy outline and a subtle micro-interaction: when hovered, they wobble slightly, as if tugged by a rubber band.

The next section has a more human, community feel, with a blocky, overlapping layout that highlights testimonies from the "Lab Members". The section starts with a centered heading: "Who’s inside OddCorners Lab?" in bold, playful serif, followed by a line in smaller sans-serif: "Photographers, illustrators, UX researchers, teachers, and curious roamers—learning to see together." The content is arranged in a loose, offset grid of three testimonial cards, each card shaped like a cut-out piece of paper with slightly irregular edges and a drop shadow. Each card includes a circular avatar image that slightly overlaps the top edge of the card, as if pinned. The avatars are stylized portraits in a semi-realistic, painterly style: one shows a person with short, curly hair and glasses, holding a camera, with a blurry city background; another shows a person wearing headphones, looking at a sketchbook; the third shows someone leaning on a railing at night, city lights behind. Inside each card, the name is bold ("Miri, street photographer in Berlin"), the role in lighter text ("Lab Member, Track 01"), and a short quote in italics, like: "The ‘Street Gaze’ track made my everyday commute feel like a film set—I started noticing micro-scenes I used to ignore." or "I came for camera tips, stayed for the way this space turns experiments into games." On the background of this section, faintly visible, are sketchy line drawings of speech bubbles and exclamation marks.

Below the testimonials, there is a call-to-action stripe designed like a torn-off notebook strip with perforated holes along its bottom. The background is pale turquoise with a slight gradient, and across the middle, in large, bold, contrasting dark navy text, it says: "Ready to treat your curiosity like a research project?" Underneath, centered smaller text says: "Join our next 4-week cycle and get guided experiments, critique sessions, and surprise prompts." Beneath this, a prominent pill-shaped button in tomato red reads "Start a free 7-day lab pass", with white bold text. To the right of the button, slightly overlapping, a tiny line of text in monospaced type reads: "Next cycle begins: April 04". A small doodle of a ticking analog clock sits beside this text, with its hands slowly rotating in a gentle, barely noticeable loop animation.

Near the bottom of the page is a lightly bordered "FAQ & Methods" area, framed as an expandable notebook section. The section header on the left reads: "Our methods, minus the boring parts." in mid-sized serif, with a smaller subtitle: "Frequently asked questions—answered like a person, not a textbook." Beneath, there is an accordion-style list of questions in a vertical stack. Each question is inside a pill-shaped bar with a soft grey outline and light background, with the question in uppercase dark charcoal on the left and a tiny plus icon on the right. Sample questions include: "Do I need to be a ‘real’ photographer?", "How much time does each experiment take?", "Can I use my phone instead of a camera?", "Is this a class or a community?", "What kind of feedback will I get?". When a question is expanded, the pill stretches vertically, pushing down the others; the background inside becomes pale yellow, and text appears in friendly paragraphs underneath. For example, the answer to the first question might say: "We love beginners. If you can walk around and feel curious about what you see, you’re ready. You’ll get enough guidance to try new things without needing any past experience." The plus icon rotates to become a minus symbol, animated with a simple 180-degree spin. In the right column of this section, there is a hand-sketched infographic-style illustration (static, not data-driven) showing a playful, simplified diagram of the lab method: three overlapping circles labeled "Observe", "Experiment", "Share" with scribbled arrows connecting them in a loop, surrounded by doodles of a camera, a notebook, and a speech bubble.

The footer is deep navy, spanning full width, with ample vertical padding. At the top of the footer, centered, a small, monochrome, inverted version of the logo appears in white: "OddCorners Lab". Underneath, three columns of footer content are arranged horizontally, but slightly staggered so the middle column is a bit higher, emphasizing asymmetry. The left column is labeled "Explore" and lists links like "Field Notes", "Methods", "Lab Members", "Office Hours". The middle column is labeled "Follow our experiments" and displays small, circular social icons in white outline (for a photo-sharing platform, video channel, and newsletter), designed as generic icons and not referencing any specific real brand. Each icon is flat and minimal, and gently scales up on hover. The right column, "Stay in the loop", has a tiny email subscription form: a rounded, elongated input field with light background and placeholder text "Enter your email for one experiment a month", followed by a compact tomato red button labeled "Subscribe". Above this form is a line of text: "No spam, just one strange prompt per month." At the very bottom edge of the page, in a thin, small line of text, centered and in light grey, is the copyright notice: "© OddCorners Lab. Built with questions, cameras, and coffee." Behind the footer content, faint, almost invisible to the first glance, are repeated outlines of city skylines in a thin, lighter navy stroke, creating a subtle texture. Throughout the entire page, micro-interactions and subtle motion—like hovering shadows, gentle button lifts, and the looping hero video—bring the playful research aesthetic to life without overwhelming the user, and the hierarchical, overlapping layout ensures that the most important calls to action stand out amid the experimental, collage-like visual language.

instruction: Planning the generation details for the following website:
input:
Design a long, vertically scrolling single-column landing page for a brand called “ALPINE ROTATION,” a mountain biking gear subscription service, using a strict Swiss-style typography aesthetic: grid-based layout, strong alignment, generous white space, bold sans-serif type, and a restrained color palette dominated by white, black, and one accent color (a bright vermillion red). The entire page should feel like a modernist poster translated into a responsive webpage: minimal, precise, with carefully calibrated asymmetry and type-heavy sections. The background of the page is pure white (#FFFFFF), with text primarily in solid black (#000000) and accent elements in vermillion red (#E53935). Use a single typeface family throughout, such as a Helvetica-like grotesk: clean, neutral, and geometric. Headings are uppercase, tightly tracked, and left-aligned; body text is sentence case with generous leading. The layout is technically single-column but uses offset blocks, asymmetric margins, and occasional side-by-side elements that never break the underlying vertical rhythm and grid.

At the very top of the page is a fixed, ultra-thin header bar spanning full width, sitting on the white background with no drop shadow. Inside the header, content is constrained to a centered max-width column (around 1100px). The header bar has three main zones on a precise grid: on the far left, the logo; centered, a minimalist navigation; and on the right, a compact CTA and toggle. The logo is purely typographic: the word “ALPINE” in bold uppercase, followed by a thin vertical black rule, then “ROTATION” in regular uppercase. All are in black, aligned baseline, and the vertical rule is exactly the height of the letters. To the right of the logo, spaced by generous white space, is the navigation: four links in small uppercase with slightly increased letter-spacing, reading “GEAR”, “PLANS”, “HOW IT WORKS”, and “STORIES”. They sit on a single line, left-aligned within their region, in regular weight black text; on hover, they invert to vermillion red. On the right side of the header is a small inline cluster: a pill-shaped outlined button reading “LOG IN” in black outline with white fill and tiny uppercase text inside, followed by a filled rectangle button with vermillion background and white text reading “START SUBSCRIPTION”. To its immediate right is a minimal icon toggle: a small outlined circle with a diagonal slash that switches between “METRIC” and “IMPERIAL”; the current mode is indicated by a tiny red underline beneath the active label (“METRIC” or “IMPERIAL”) rendered in a micro uppercase font. The header remains subtly fixed as the user scrolls, without any animation, reinforcing the grid.

Directly below the header, the hero section occupies the full browser height minus the header, with a clean, poster-like composition. The main content is vertically centered within the page’s max-width column, but offset slightly to the left to create asymmetry: more white margin on the right. The left half of this hero column is dominated by a large typographic block. At the top of this block, in tiny uppercase, vermillion text aligned strictly to the left margin, is a label: “MOUNTAIN BIKE GEAR SUBSCRIPTION · SWISS PRECISION”. Below it, in very large uppercase black letters that break into three lines, is the main headline: “ROLL FASTER.\nPACK LIGHTER.\nRIDE EVERY MONTH.” Each line is left-aligned, with a tight vertical rhythm and no hyphenation. The words sit flush with the grid, with subtle gutters between lines. Under the headline, there is a short, two-line description in smaller, regular-weight black text: “Curated MTB essentials delivered monthly. Always tuned to your terrain, season, and pace—so your focus stays on the trail, not the checklist.” This copy is left-aligned, with generous leading and a comfortable paragraph width (~60 characters).

Below the description, there is a horizontal row of two call-to-action areas aligned to the left. The primary CTA is a wide, low-profile rectangular button with vermillion background and white uppercase text reading “BUILD YOUR KIT”. It is perfectly rectangular (no rounded corners), with a very thin white stroke and no shadows. To its right, separated by a fixed white gap, is a secondary CTA rendered as just an outlined rectangle with black stroke, white fill, and black uppercase text reading “VIEW SAMPLE BOXES”. Both buttons align to the same baseline and snap into the grid. Underneath the CTAs, a fine, hairline black horizontal rule runs from the left margin to the center of the column. Beneath this rule, three small metric snippets are displayed in one line but within the same single-column alignment: “3 TIERS”, “100+ BRANDS”, “CANCEL ANYTIME”. Each is in tiny uppercase, with thin black text separated by small vertical hairline rules. All of this text sits strictly on the vertical baseline and aligns left, reinforcing the Swiss-style discipline.

To the right of this type block, still within the hero region but visually separated, is a tall, narrow vertical video window that floats in the white space like a modernist image block. It is aligned to the top of the headline text’s x-height and aligned to a right-side guide of the grid. This video window represents a short looping video clip. The frame has a thin black border and a 3:4 vertical aspect ratio, slightly taller than the text block. The video content is a desaturated, semi-monochrome (black, white, and faint hints of red) cinematic loop of a rider descending a rugged alpine trail. The camera angle is slightly low and behind the rider, with the bike leaning into turns, dust and tiny pebbles flicking up in soft slow motion. The background reveals layered mountain silhouettes fading into atmospheric haze, with faint sunlight streaks. The rider’s helmet or gloves carry a subtle vermillion accent that matches the page’s accent color. The motion is smooth and slow, giving a sense of continuous forward movement without being distracting. When the user hovers the video, a minimal overlay appears: a thin black border thickens slightly, and at the bottom left corner, small uppercase white text on a semi-transparent black strip reads “TRAIL: PILATUS NORTH FACE · LENGTH: 12.4 KM”. To the bottom right, a minimalist play/pause icon (two vertical bars for pause, a triangle for play) is drawn in white lines. The video auto-loops and defaults to muted. Behind this video window, the background remains pure white—no gradients or shadows—so the video looks like a printed photograph on a poster.

As the user scrolls down, the next section appears separated from the hero by a generous block of white space and a subtle horizontal hairline rule that extends only partway across the page from the left margin. This section is titled “HOW IT WORKS” in uppercase black text, left-aligned, with a small vermillion square placed precisely to its left, acting as a bullet-like accent. The section remains single-column but introduces an asymmetric layout by pushing the text slightly to the right, leaving a wider white margin on the left. Beneath the title is a three-step process presented as stacked blocks rather than a horizontal row, maintaining the concept of a single column. Each step has a bold step number on the left (“01”, “02”, “03”) in large, light gray outline text (with no fill), overlapping subtly into the left margin. Next to each number, aligned to the main text column, is the step title in uppercase black text and a short description below in smaller regular text. Step 1: “TELL US WHERE YOU RIDE” with description: “Choose your primary terrain—alpine, forest, desert, or urban trail—and share how often you ride. We tune your kit to your conditions.” Step 2: “LOCK IN YOUR ROTATION” with description: “Pick a monthly, bi-monthly, or seasonal plan. We map out a 12-month gear rotation that avoids clutter and upgrades what matters.” Step 3: “UNBOX, ADJUST, RIDE” with description: “Receive tightly curated essentials: tires, tools, protection, and maintenance. Everything arrives trail-ready with no plastic filler.” Each step block is separated by substantial vertical white space and a thin horizontal line that extends only halfway across the column.

Further down, a “ROTATION TIERS” section presents three subscription levels. Instead of the usual three-column layout, the tiers appear as three full-width bands stacked vertically, each occupying their own block with clean edges and aligned to the same left margin. The section title “ROTATION TIERS” appears in uppercase black, with a tiny vermillion line drawn under just the word “ROTATION”. Under the title, a brief one-sentence explanation in smaller text reads: “Three precision-tuned kits. One consistent cadence.” Each tier block has a subtle background: the first (“BASELINE”) on white with a thin black border, the second (“ELEVATION”) on an extremely light gray (#F5F5F5), and the third (“APEX”) on white again but with a thicker black border on the left only, creating a simple vertical bar. Tier 1: “BASELINE” features a medium-sized uppercase label, a short description (“Minimalist essentials for frequent riders who prefer light kits.”), followed by a simple, column-aligned list of bullet points set with tiny vermillion square bullets and black text: “Trail-grade tubes & sealant”, “Core multi-tool”, “Seasonal socks & hydration”, “Quarterly surprise upgrade”. At the bottom right of this tier block, a small text link in uppercase black reads “VIEW SAMPLE BASELINE BOX”, aligned with the right margin of the main column. Tier 2: “ELEVATION” features a slightly larger title and a subtle vermillion horizontal bar across the top of the block, with description: “Balanced upgrades for riders who want a bit more performance each month.” Bullet points: “High-performance tires in seasonal rotation”, “Protection layers (gloves, pads) tuned to your climate”, “Maintenance and cleaning essentials”, “Occasional tech add-ons (lights, mounts)”. At the bottom, a vermillion-outlined button with white fill and black uppercase text reads “ELEVATION: START HERE”, slightly indented to the left. Tier 3: “APEX” has the title in bold uppercase and the left border in solid vermillion, description: “Aggressive component and protection upgrades for riders who live on black diamonds.” Bullet points: “Premium tire sets and inserts”, “Advanced protection (full-face, armor as available)”, “High-end maintenance tools”, “Priority support and trail consultation”. At the bottom left of this block, aligned with the left margin, a filled vermillion button with white uppercase text reads “APEX WAITLIST”. Each tier block is separated by generous vertical whitespace.

The next section is a visually calm “GEAR, REDUCED TO ESSENTIALS” section, which showcases sample gear items in a single, vertically scrolling column but arranged with Swiss-style asymmetry and strong grids. The section title “GEAR, REDUCED TO ESSENTIALS” appears left-aligned in uppercase black, with the comma hanging out into the left margin (slightly “hung punctuation” effect, making the title align visually with an invisible grid line). Below, a short description in smaller text: “Every item in your box passes a simple test: would a rider trust this on a remote descent? If not, it does not ship.” Beneath this, three sample gear “cards” are presented sequentially. Each card consists of a monochrome, flat-design illustration on one side and text on the other, but because we are sticking to a single-column flow, each card stacks its image and text vertically with a thin baseline grid separating them.

For the first gear card, the illustration fills a rectangular frame spanning the full column width. The style is flat and graphic, with crisp vector shapes and limited colors (black, white, gray, and touches of vermillion). It depicts a close-up of a mountain bike tire in profile, with pronounced knobby treads. The tire arcs diagonally from the lower left to the upper right. Behind it, an abstracted alpine landscape is suggested by overlapping geometric triangles as mountain peaks, rendered in light gray tones. A small vermillion diagonal stripe cuts across the top-right corner of the illustration, echoing the brand color. Below this image, the text area has a label “TIRE SYSTEMS” in small uppercase black, then a bold subheading in larger text: “Trail compounds that match your terrain, not a marketing campaign.” A paragraph beneath explains in smaller text: “From high-volume casings to winter-ready rubber, we rotate tires by season and region, not by warehouse surplus.” Under this is a tiny inline label “INCLUDED IN: ELEVATION · APEX” in uppercase micro text with vermillion dots separating the tiers. The second gear card’s illustration shows, in similar flat style, an exploded view of a minimalist multi-tool and a compact pump laid out on a grid. Each tool is represented as a simplified silhouette in dark gray against a white field, with faint gray grid lines forming a precise background. One element—a hex key or pump shaft—is highlighted in vermillion. Beneath it, text: label “TOOLS”, bold subheading “Only what you can carry on a real climb.” and a paragraph. The third card features an illustration of protective gear—gloves and a helmet—hung on a clean horizontal line against a white wall, shown in stark silhouette from the side, casting simple, sharp shadows that indicate a directional light source from the upper left. The gloves have a small vermillion stripe. The accompanying text discusses “PROTECTION LAYERS”.

As the user scrolls, there is a subtle motion element: between sections, the transition is marked by a horizontal band where a very faint, animated diagonal grid pattern moves slowly from left to right, like a barely perceptible shifting halftone. This band is about 60px tall, stretches full width, and uses ultra-light gray lines on white, so it doesn’t distract but suggests continuous motion. The lines are perfectly straight, at 45 degrees, and their movement is slow and linear, matching the Swiss aesthetic of order and consistency.

Next is a “TRAIL PROFILES” section that remains simple but introduces user voices without any chart-like visuals. The title “TRAIL PROFILES” appears in uppercase black, left-aligned, with a small black horizontal line placed just under the last word only. Below, three customer quotes are stacked vertically, each formatted as a clean block. Each profile has a thin black border around it, with slightly more padding on the left than the right, creating a sense of asymmetry. At the top left inside each profile block, a small label indicates trail type in vermillion uppercase, such as “ALPINE”, “FOREST”, “DESERT”. Under that, a larger bold line with the rider’s name and location: “LENA · CHAMONIX, FRANCE”, “DAI · WHISTLER, CANADA”, “AMIR · MOAB, USA”. Under each is a short, one- or two-sentence testimonial in regular-weight black text, left aligned, for example: “I stopped hoarding spares. Everything in the box goes straight onto the bike or into the pack.” The blocks have generous vertical spacing between them, and no avatars or photos, keeping the section typographically pure.

Following this, there is a “WHY SUBSCRIPTION?” section that uses a simple two-column feel within a single-column layout by shifting the text content to different horizontal positions while still stacking. The heading is bold uppercase, with “WHY” and “SUBSCRIPTION?” on separate lines. Below, three conceptual comparisons are shown. Each comparison appears as two short paragraphs: one labeled “BEFORE” and one labeled “WITH ROTATION”. These labels are tiny uppercase in gray. The “BEFORE” text is aligned slightly left; the “WITH ROTATION” text is indented slightly to the right, creating a zig-zag pattern down the page but still on the central column. The content contrasts clutter, decision fatigue, and inconsistent gear with consistency and curation. Thin vertical lines, in light gray, run next to the “WITH ROTATION” paragraphs, reinforcing the alignment.

Near the bottom of the page, a minimal FAQ section titled “CLARITY BEFORE COMMITMENT” appears. The title is uppercase black text, with a small vermillion dot at the end instead of a standard period. Below, four FAQ items are displayed as expanding/collapsing accordions but visually represented only through typographic cues. Each question is a single line of uppercase black text, aligned left and prefixed by a tiny plus/minus symbol in vermillion drawn as a simple cross or dash. Example questions: “CAN I PAUSE MY ROTATION?”, “WHAT IF I ALREADY HAVE SOME GEAR?”, “DO YOU SHIP INTERNATIONALLY?”, “HOW DO RETURNS WORK?”. When expanded (represented in the design), the answer text appears beneath in smaller, regular-weight sentence-case paragraphs, indented by one grid unit. The expansion is indicated by the plus icon turning into a minus and a very thin vertical vermillion line running along the left edge of the expanded text block.

At the very bottom of the main content, a strong, poster-like closing call-to-action section appears, sitting above a discrete footer. This section is framed by a thin black rectangle that encloses both the headline and the CTAs, giving the impression of a printed callout glued to the page. Inside this rectangle, centered vertically but aligned to the left, a large uppercase black headline reads “SET YOUR NEXT 12 RIDES ON AUTOPILOT.” Underneath, in smaller text, a simple phrase: “Start your rotation, ship your first kit, ride next weekend.” Below the text, aligned to the left, two elements appear on the same line: a large vermillion filled rectangle button with white uppercase text “START SUBSCRIPTION” and, to the right, a small inline text link in black uppercase with a thin underline reading “VIEW FIRST MONTH CONTENTS”. Behind this entire CTA block, in the background but not overlapping the text, is a full-width, extremely subtle, grayscale abstract contour map pattern, indicating terrain lines. The lines are very light gray, almost invisible, and do not interfere with readability.

The page concludes with a minimal footer on a white background. The footer content sits within the same central column and is divided vertically into two stacked rows. The top row contains the brand name “ALPINE ROTATION” in small uppercase black on the left, and on the right, a micro-navigation with simple text links “TERMS”, “PRIVACY”, “CONTACT” spaced apart. The bottom row, a smaller line in tiny gray text, reads “Designed for fewer, better rides.” centered in the column. At the very bottom, a thin horizontal black line spans the full width, aligning with the grid. Throughout the entire page, the Swiss-inspired aesthetic is preserved: strict typographic hierarchy, flush left alignment, generous use of negative space, precise grid alignment, minimal colors, and all subtle motion limited to the barely moving diagonal background bands and the looped hero video. No charts or data visualizations are present anywhere—only typography, image blocks, and minimal motion in service of a calm, modern, and highly structured landing page.

instruction: Planning the generation details for the following website:
input:
Design a dark-themed, data-dense “LumaHome Device Health Center” dashboard for monitoring a global fleet of smart home IoT devices, keeping a three-column layout with a dominant middle column and multiple stacked panels. The top-left panel is a vertical list of horizontal bar meters titled “Top Device Types by Online Rate,” showing device categories such as Smart Thermostats, Security Cameras, Door Locks, Light Hubs, Voice Assistants, Smart Plugs, Smoke Sensors, Water Leak Sensors, and Energy Meters, each with a colored bar indicating Online Rate (%) and a numeric count of Online Devices on the right. Directly below it, add another full-width left panel titled “Daily Alert Volume by Device Type,” showing a clustered column chart across nine device types on the x-axis and Alert Count on the y-axis, with three legend series representing Critical, Warning, and Info alerts. In the center column header, display the brand name and page title “LumaHome Device Fleet Health Overview,” with the current date-time in the top-right corner chrome. Under this header, the main center-top panel is a “Fleet Summary” block containing one large KPI showing “Total Active Devices” in big numerals and three circular mini-widgets to the right labeled “Healthy Devices,” “Devices Needing Attention,” and “Offline Devices,” each with a percentage and ring visualization. Immediately below, place a combined column-and-line chart titled “Annual Device Activity and Failure Rate,” with years on the x-axis (2019–2023), stacked colored columns for Monthly Active Devices and Average Daily Sessions (left axis), and an overlaid line for Hardware Failure Rate % (right axis). At the bottom of the center column, add a wide panel titled “Hourly Connectivity Stability,” showing a multi-line chart with time of day on the x-axis (0:00–23:00) and Connectivity Success Rate % on the y-axis, with separate lines for Wi‑Fi Devices and Ethernet Devices. In the right column’s upper half, include a panel titled “Firmware Adoption Trend,” using a column chart with years on the x-axis and Installed Devices on the left axis, plus an overlaid line for Latest Firmware Adoption % on the right axis. Beneath that, add a compact table panel titled “Regional Device Snapshot,” listing regions (North America, Europe, Asia Pacific, South America) with columns for Total Devices, Online Ratio %, Average Latency (ms), and Open Support Tickets. At the bottom-right, include a circular donut chart panel titled “Issue Category Breakdown,” with a central total label “Open Issues” and three segments labeled Connectivity Issues, Sensor Calibration Issues, and Power/Battery Issues, each segment color-coded and accompanied by numeric counts around the ring. Use vivid accent colors for different metrics, subtle gridlines, and hoverable tooltips implied in the design so the dashboard feels interactive and reusable for any smart home platform monitoring a large distributed device fleet.

### Dataset

| Device Type             | Online Rate % | Online Devices |
|-------------------------|---------------|----------------|
| Smart Thermostats       | 97            | 18,420         |
| Security Cameras        | 94            | 25,310         |
| Door Locks              | 92            | 12,880         |
| Light Hubs              | 89            | 9,540          |

instruction: Planning the generation details for the following website:
input:
Design a full-screen dark-themed “NovaForge Quality & Downtime Monitor” dashboard for a manufacturing plant, keeping a wide central KPI band, side charts, and data tables. At the very top, use a glowing header bar with the title and a small subtitle like “Real-Time Production Health for AlloyGear Product Line.” Directly beneath the header, centered across the page, place a prominent card titled “This Month’s Production Plan” that shows the main KPI pair “Units Produced / Planned Units” in large white type (e.g., 14,930 / 64,940), with a green label for “Completion Rate: 22.9%” and a red label for “Remaining Units: 52,367.” The left column of the dashboard should contain three stacked panels: at the top, a filled area chart titled “Hourly Defect Rate (Last 24 Hours)” with time along the x-axis and “Defects per 1,000 Units” on the y-axis, showing a smooth line that rises and falls over the day; beneath that, a radar chart titled “Defect Category Distribution” with axes such as “Surface,” “Assembly,” “Calibration,” “Packaging,” and “Labeling,” highlighting where the most issues occur; and at the bottom, a line chart titled “Monthly Quality Score Trend” showing months on the x-axis and “First-Pass Yield %” on the y-axis with points connected by a thin glowing line. Just to the right of the radar chart, centered vertically, place a bold numeric tile titled “Active Lines / Total Lines” that simply shows a large fraction like “18 / 24” to indicate how many production lines are currently running. The central lower section should hold a wide table titled “Line-Level Quality Summary,” listing for each production date and line: “Production Date,” “Line ID,” “Planned Units,” “Good Units,” “Defective Units,” and “First-Pass Yield %,” with the yield column color-coded in green when above target. On the right side, mirror the layout with a tall table at the top titled “Machine Downtime Status,” listing rows for “Machine Name,” “Status” (Normal, Warning, or Fault), and “Continuous Uptime” shown as a human-readable duration, coloring faulted rows in red and normal rows in green. At the bottom right, keep space for a smaller analytics table or compact chart if desired, but maintain the overall emphasis on production quality, defects, and downtime, using clean, reusable labels suitable for any manufacturing environment.

instruction: Planning the generation details for the following website:
input:
Design a full-screen dark-themed “NovaQuest Live Ops Control Center” dashboard for a mobile game studio, optimized for 1920x1080 displays, with a dense data layout. At the very top, place a bold centered title “NovaQuest Live Ops KPI Dashboard” with a small right-aligned timestamp showing the current server time and date. Directly under the title, in the upper left, include three compact KPI tiles summarizing key game scale metrics: “Daily Active Players” (show 2,000,000), “New Installs Today” (120,000), and “Concurrent Peak” (150,000), each with a small icon and subtle glow. Below these tiles on the left column, stack three horizontal stat cards for service health: “Matchmaking Success Rate”, “Purchase Success Rate”, and “Chat Message Delivery”, each with a numeric value (e.g., 98.4%), a slim progress bar, and a small right-aligned comparison label like “vs. yesterday +1.2%” in green or red depending on trend. Fill the lower left quadrant with a smooth line chart titled “Session Volume by Hour” showing total sessions across the last 24 hours on the x-axis (00:00 to 23:00) and session count on the y-axis, with a highlighted line and subtle dots on peaks; place a second nearly identical line chart directly to its right, titled “Average Session Length by Hour”, overlaying two lines for “Casual Mode” and “Ranked Mode”, both sharing the same hourly x-axis but with minutes on the y-axis. In the center top, create a large numeric highlight labeled “Total Revenue Today (USD)” showing a big, bright figure (e.g., 1,938,272) and directly to its right three circular mini-gauges for high-level monetization health: “ARPU”, “Payer Conversion”, and “Retention D1”, each showing a percentage ring with the current value in the center (e.g., 80%, 7.3%, 30%). Beneath the revenue highlight in the central area, add a vertical clustered bar chart titled “Monthly Gross Revenue by Region” with months from Jan to Dec on the x-axis and revenue in thousands on the y-axis, using grouped bars for three regions “Americas”, “Europe”, and “Asia-Pacific”. On the upper right, mirror the left KPI band with three smaller tiles focusing on live-ops content: “Live Events Running” (25), “Promotions Active” (8), and “Experiments Online” (15), each with contrasting accent colors. Under these tiles, place a radar (spider) chart titled “Game Health Profile” with five axes labeled “Engagement”, “Monetization Depth”, “Content Freshness”, “Technical Stability”, and “Community Activity”, overlaying two polygons for “Current Week” and “Previous Week” in different translucent colors. Finally, in the lower right corner, add a vertical ranking panel titled “Top Revenue-Generating Countries Today” displaying a horizontal bar chart-style list with ranks 1–5, colored badges, country names, and bar lengths scaled by “Revenue (USD)” so that the top country has the longest bar; show country values on the right end of each bar for quick comparison.

instruction: Planning the generation details for the following website:
input:
Design a dark-themed “LumaHome Fleet Health Center” dashboard for monitoring a global smart home IoT device fleet, with a three-column layout and a high-density data feel. The top left features a large KPI tile labeled “Total Online Devices” showing a prominent numeric counter, with a slim status bar above it indicating “Fleet Status: Stable.” Directly beneath, place a pie chart titled “Device Type Composition” breaking down the fleet into categories such as Smart Hubs, Cameras, Thermostats, Locks, and Sensors, using bold contrasting colors. Below the pie, include a scrolling table labeled “Recent Device Events” listing columns for Device ID, Location Label, Event Type, Source (App, Voice, Automation), and Timestamp, styled as a compact log. The central column header bar shows the product logo and title, and directly under it a time-and-environment widget displaying current time, date, and a small icon-based card labeled “Cloud Service Status” with indicators for API latency, message queue health, and storage usage. To the right of that widget, show a vertical list titled “Top Sites by Active Devices” with three ranked entries and bright numeric counts. The main panel in the center column is a large world map titled “Device Connectivity by Region,” with countries or regions shaded by average online rate, plus a highlighted tooltip example when hovering over a region (e.g., “Western Europe: 18,240 devices, 98.4% online”). The far-right column contains three stacked analytics charts: at the top, a donut chart called “Device Age Distribution (years in service)” with segments like <1, 1–2, 2–3, 3–4, >4; in the middle, a vertical bar chart titled “Support Tickets by Region (last 24h)” with regions on the x-axis and ticket counts on the y-axis; and at the bottom, a smooth line chart labeled “Commands Processed by Time of Day” with four time blocks along the x-axis (00:00–06:00, 06:00–12:00, 12:00–18:00, 18:00–24:00) and command volume on the y-axis, using dual lines for “Successful” vs “Failed” to emphasize operational health.

instruction: Planning the generation details for the following website:
input:
Design a dark, neon-accented “NovaRealm Live Ops Control” dashboard for a mobile game publisher, keeping a futuristic data-visualization style with a strong central focus. At the very top center, show the title “NovaRealm Live Ops Dashboard” with a small subtitle “Real-time mobile game performance overview.” Directly beneath, place a large circular globe-style graphic representing the global player base; inside the circle, display the primary KPI “Total Active Players” with a prominent value (e.g., 5,689,741) and a glowing percentage ring for “Daily Goal Completion 89%.” Radiating around this central orb are four curved connector lines that end in small circular badges: one at upper left for “Year-to-Date Revenue” with a numeric counter, one at upper right for “Monthly Revenue,” one at mid-right for “Weekly Revenue,” and one at lower right for “Today’s Revenue,” each using warm accent colors and small percentage change labels like “+12% vs last period.” Below the orb, integrate a compact progress bar labeled “Server Stability 99%” and a micro chart indicating “Match Completion Rate 89%.” On the far left column, create a vertical “Top 5 Live Games” card stack showing the leading titles in the NovaRealm portfolio, each row displaying a small game icon, game name, concurrent players, and a horizontal bar indicating “Engagement Score” as a percentage. Directly under this, add a “Game Mode Performance” horizontal bar chart comparing several key modes (Ranked, Casual, Co-op Raids, Events) with metrics for matches played and completion percentage, using segmented bars to convey progress. On the far right, mirror the layout with a slim analytics panel titled “Monetization Targets,” containing three stacked KPI tiles: “Projected Revenue,” “Actual Revenue,” and “In-App Purchase Total,” each showing a currency value, small subtitle text, and colored indicators to show on-target or off-target states. Beneath these tiles, keep a compact map-style panel labeled “Regional Player Distribution” with three pinned regions (Americas, Europe, Asia-Pacific) and bubbles sized by active players plus a tiny label for “ARPDAU” per region. At the bottom-right, include a simple line chart titled “Sessions per Minute (Today)” plotting session counts across the current hour, with a subtle grid and time markers along the x-axis. Along the very bottom center of the page, add a pill-shaped footer tab labeled “Data Stream Status” that can visually indicate connected or degraded states, tying the whole real-time live-ops story together.

Design a dark-mode, neon-accented “SkyLoop Travel Demand & Pricing Command Center” dashboard with a cinematic, sci‑fi HUD feel, where the background is a subtle blurred world map and circuitry texture and the interface elements glow in teal and magenta. At the very top center, place the product name and a slim header bar with pill-shaped indicators showing “Live”, “Last 24h”, and “Last 7d” modes. Directly beneath, the main focal point is a large circular KPI gauge that dominates the center of the screen, labeled “Live Trip Requests” with a bold numeric value and an animated radial progress arc, flanked by left and right chevron buttons suggesting time-step navigation and a vertical bar on the right side showing a scrollable timeline index. On the left side of the central module, add a compact vertical mini-gauge labeled “Search-to-Booking Conversion” with a circular dial and two stacked horizontal bars indicating “Mobile” and “Desktop” conversion rates; symmetrically on the right, mirror this with a “Instant vs Flexible Pricing Share” mini-gauge using the same style but comparing “Instant Deals” and “Flexible Fares”. Along the bottom edge, lay out three holographic-style KPI pods that appear to rise from circular platforms: the left pod titled “Daily Flight Demand” shows a large total requests number with two percentage breakdown labels, “Leisure” and “Business”; the center pod titled “Daily Hotel Demand” mirrors the layout and categories; the right pod titled “Daily Package Demand” again uses the same treatment but with “Short Breaks” and “Extended Trips” as segments, each pod including small up/down arrows to indicate day-over-day change. Above these pods, slightly off-center toward the top, add a semicircular world map widget in soft teal, labeled “Top Origin Regions (Requests)” where animated pins highlight three leading origin regions and a legend shows their shares; to the far left and right of this map, place tiny circular badges ranking the top three origin cities by booking volume, each badge labeled “#1”, “#2”, “#3” with a city name and a compact percentage ring around it. instruction: Planning the generation details for the following website:
input:
The dashboard is titled “NovaNest Smart Home Fleet Health Center” and uses a dark, neon-accented theme to monitor the status of a large distributed fleet of home IoT devices. At the top center, a compact summary strip lists major device families (Thermostats, Security Cams, Smart Locks, Light Hubs, Voice Assistants, Air Quality Sensors) in a horizontal table, with columns for “Devices Online,” “Devices Offline,” “Average Uptime %,” and “Active Alerts,” allowing operators to scan health by product line. Dominating the central area is a stylized regional map labeled “Household Distribution & Fault Hotspots,” showing a fictional country divided into zones (North Ridge, East Bay, Central Heights, South Grove, West Valley, Island Park); each region is shaded by total active devices, with glowing pins marking clusters of recent critical faults and a small legend indicating color ranges for “Device Density” and icon styles for “Minor,” “Major,” and “Critical” incidents. On the left side, the upper panel “Connectivity Mix by Device Type” contains a grouped vertical bar chart comparing connection methods (Wi‑Fi 2.4G, Wi‑Fi 5G, Zigbee, Z‑Wave, Thread, Ethernet) across the same key device families, showing counts of devices by connectivity technology. Directly beneath, a “Network Design” section is split into two donut charts side by side: the first, “Device Online Status Breakdown,” segments the fleet into Online Stable, Flapping, Offline Planned, and Offline Unexpected by percentage; the second, “Firmware Adoption Status,” shows the share of devices on Latest Release, Previous Stable, Legacy, and Beta. Below these donuts, a time-series line chart labeled “Daily Incident Volume by Severity” tracks the last 10 days on the x-axis, with separate lines for Info, Warning, Major, and Critical incidents, plus a faded area under the total incident count, enabling quick recognition of trend spikes. Along the bottom-left, a compact table titled “Hardware Model Inventory” lists popular hardware models (e.g., TH-100, TH-200, CAM-X1, CAM-X2, LOCK-Pro, HUB-Lite) with columns for “Total Units,” “Avg Age (months),” and “Warranty Expiring (30d).” On the right side, the upper panel “Live Health Indicators” shows six radial gauge charts arranged in two rows, displaying key KPIs: “Fleet Uptime,” “Secure Connection Rate,” “Successful Command Rate,” “Cloud Sync Success,” “Average CPU Utilization,” and “Average Memory Utilization,” each with numeric values and colored ranges for Poor, Acceptable, and Optimal. Directly below these gauges, a slim horizontal column chart titled “Alert Volume by Category (Last 24h)” shows counts for Connectivity, Power, Sensor Drift, Security, Firmware, and Overheat. The lower-right section is a dense “Performance Benchmark Matrix” rendered as a table grouped by transport layer (Wi‑Fi, Zigbee, Z‑Wave, Thread, Cellular Backup) with metrics such as “Avg Latency (ms),” “Packet Loss %,” “Reconnect Rate %,” and “Encrypted Traffic %,” plus an overall “Health Score” column, allowing power users to compare network behaviors across the fleet. The entire layout is designed to be reusable for any large smart home brand while showcasing multi-source telemetry, regional behavior, and network quality in a visually cohesive, data-rich experience.

instruction: Planning the generation details for the following website:
input:
Design a dark-themed “FitFlux Weekly Activity Overview” dashboard for a consumer fitness app, using a high-contrast neon accent palette and a data-dense layout. The top-left hero panel spans the width of the first column and shows “Today’s Activity Summary” with a large flip-style counter labeled “Total Active Minutes Today” (e.g., 389) and a row of smaller tiles beneath it for key categories: Cardio Minutes, Strength Minutes, Steps Taken, Calories Burned, and Active Hours, each with brightly colored numeric values and subtle trend arrows. To the right, a tall bar chart titled “Top Workouts This Week” ranks workout types by total minutes, using horizontal bars with labels such as Running, Cycling, Strength Training, Yoga, HIIT, Walking, Rowing, and Pilates; a toggle in the corner switches between “Day” and “Week” views. Next to that, another vertical stacked bar chart titled “Goal Completion by Day” displays each day of the current week on the x-axis and three stacked segments per bar for Move Goal, Exercise Goal, and Stand Goal, showing how close the user came to each daily target. Along the bottom row, the left panel is a multi-series line chart titled “Weekly Trends by Activity Type,” with months along the x-axis and total minutes per month on the y-axis, showing separate lines for Running, Cycling, Strength Training, Yoga, and Walking to highlight seasonal and long-term patterns; a day/week/month toggle in the header controls aggregation. The middle-bottom panel is a donut chart titled “Manual vs. Auto-Tracked Sessions,” showing the proportion of workout sessions logged manually versus those captured automatically by the app’s sensors, with a bold percentage label in the center and two contrasting ring segments. The bottom-right panel is a pie chart titled “Activity Split by Category,” with slices for Cardio, Strength, Flexibility, Mindfulness, and Other, each labeled with percentage of total weekly active minutes. Finally, a narrow stacked column chart to the far right titled “Workout Type Distribution by Intensity” shows Low, Medium, and High intensity segments stacked for key workout types, enabling a quick view of how hard the user typically trains. All charts share a modern card layout with subtle glows, clear legends, and responsive tooltips that reveal exact values and dates when hovered.

instruction: Planning the generation details for the following website:
input:
Design a full-screen dark-themed “StreamFlow Engagement Overview” dashboard for a fictional video streaming platform, using a cosmic/tech background and neon cyan, magenta, and amber accents. Centered at the top is the dashboard title with a slim horizontal divider line stretching left and right. On the upper-left, create a tall card labeled “Realtime Snapshot” containing four stacked KPI tiles: “Active Viewers Now” with a large number (e.g., 124,580), “Concurrent Streams” with a slightly smaller number, “New Sessions Today” with a count, and “Today’s Engagement Rate (%)” with a percentage; each tile uses an icon (eye, play button, plus sign, percentage) and a subtle gradient. Directly beneath this snapshot card, add a donut chart titled “Viewing by Content Category” showing the share of total watch time across categories like Drama, Comedy, Action, Documentary, and Kids, with a central label showing “Total Watch Time (hrs)”. Below that, add another donut chart titled “Viewing by Device Type” splitting usage among Smart TV, Mobile, Tablet, Desktop, and Game Console, with labels and percentages around the ring. In the center of the layout, show a prominent choropleth world map titled “Live Viewers by Region,” where regions are shaded based on current viewer counts and major markets display hover tooltips with city names and counts; on top of the map, overlay a huge, animated numeric readout labeled “Total Live Viewers” in bold cyan digits. Below the map, create a wide area chart titled “View Minutes Over 24 Hours” with a smooth line and filled area illustrating hourly total view minutes from 00:00 to 23:00, with peak periods highlighted by brighter points. On the right side, stack three panels: at the top, a radial multi-segment donut chart titled “Top 5 Subscription Plans (This Month)” showing share of total active subscriptions for five plan tiers (Basic, Standard, Premium, Family, Ultra) with each segment labeled and connected to external text markers. In the middle-right, place a vertical bar chart titled “Top 5 Genres by Daily Active Viewers” with genres along the x-axis and viewer counts on the y-axis, showing five bars with numeric labels. At the bottom-right, include a triangular area chart (stacked spikes) titled “Top 10 Titles by Stream Count” where each spike represents a show or movie ranked from 1 to 10, with the x-axis labeled by short fictional title IDs and the y-axis as “Streams (k)”; alternate spike colors to distinguish items. Use compact, legible typography, subtle gridlines, and hover tooltips in all charts to emphasize data density while maintaining clarity.

instruction: Planning the generation details for the following website:
input:
Design a dark-themed, full HD web dashboard for “NovaQuest Live Ops Center,” a real-time monitoring console for a mobile RPG. The top left panel is a large donut chart titled “Session Status Breakdown (Last 24h)” showing proportions of Active Sessions, Idle Sessions, and Disconnected Sessions, with a small text header summarizing “Total Sessions: 820, Snapshot: 2025-06-14 15:30.” In the center top, create a map panel titled “Regional Player Density & Server Health” using a world or continental map with glowing markers at key regions; to the left of the map, include a vertical ranked bar list titled “Top Regions by Concurrent Users” showing several regions and their percentage share, and directly beneath that a horizontal stacked bar labeled “Server State Mix” with colored segments for Online, High Load, Maintenance, and Offline. The bottom middle panel is a line chart titled “Peak Concurrency by Hour” plotting concurrent users across several time points in the last 24 hours. The bottom right is a horizontal stacked bar chart titled “Monetization Events by Month” comparing counts of different event types (In-App Purchase, Rewarded Ad, Subscription Start, Cosmetic Purchase) across recent months, and beside it (sharing the same card) add a compact radar chart titled “Player Behavior Profile (Core KPIs)” with axes such as Retention, Engagement, Social Activity, Spend Intensity, and PvP Participation, filled with a gradient polygon. instruction: Planning the generation details for the following website:
input:
Design a dark-themed “NovaChain Inventory & Fulfillment Command Center” dashboard for desktop, with a high-tech blue background and glowing accent colors. Across the very top, place a bold title bar with the product name and a subtle subtitle like “Real-time Supply Chain Performance”. On the upper left, show two large KPI numbers: “Total Inventory Value (USD)” and “Open Orders (Units)”, each in neon typography, with four circular gauge charts beneath them in a 2x2 grid; the left column gauges show “Warehouse Utilization Rate” and “On-time Replenishment Rate”, while the right column gauges show “Order Fill Rate” and “Perfect Shipment Rate”, each gauge containing a numeric value and percentage label. The central top panel is a wide grouped vertical bar chart titled “Monthly Inventory Turnover by Year” comparing two years (e.g., 2023 vs 2024) across the 12 months on the x-axis, with the y-axis showing “Turns per Month”; bars should be side-by-side by month with a small legend above the plot. To the upper right, stack two donut charts in a vertical column, both roughly square panels: the top one is titled “Inventory Distribution by Product Category” with segments for categories such as “Electronics”, “Home Goods”, “Apparel”, and “Accessories”; the bottom donut is titled “Open Orders by Fulfillment Channel” with segments for “Direct-to-Consumer”, “Retail Partners”, “Marketplaces”, and “Bulk Wholesale”, each chart including a legend with color keys. Along the bottom left, place a dense table titled “Priority Shipments Queue” listing the latest outbound orders with columns like “Order ID”, “Destination Region”, “SKU Count”, “Order Value (USD)”, “Requested Ship Date”, and “Current Status”; use multiple rows to convey a streaming effect. In the bottom center, include a smooth line chart titled “Daily Warehouse Throughput (Last 7 Days)” with the x-axis showing recent dates and the y-axis representing “Units Processed”, plotting a single yellow line that gently oscillates to reflect volume changes. On the bottom right, show a dual-line trend chart titled “Backorders vs In-Transit Stock” where the x-axis is months and the y-axis is “Units”, drawing one line for “Backordered Units” and another for “In-Transit Units”, with a small legend and a highlighted point on the latest month to emphasize recent performance. Maintain a cohesive visual style with thin grid lines, subtle glows around charts, and consistent typography so the whole page feels like one integrated real-time supply chain monitoring surface.
